seeds.t: A/B Testing: Peeking at Results and Stopping Early
seeds.w: Checking experiment results before the planned sample size is reached and stopping when p < 0.05 — peeking inflates Type I error rates far above the nominal 5%.
seeds.c: Pre-specify sample size using power analysis. Use sequential testing methods if you need to peek. Never stop an experiment based on interim p-values alone.
seeds.s: Experiment stopped at p = 0.04 after week 1; rolled out; effect disappears; was a false positive from peeking.

seeds.t: A/B Testing: Multiple Metrics Without a Pre-Specified Primary
seeds.w: Tracking 10 metrics in an experiment and celebrating the ones that are significant — with 10 metrics at α=0.05, expect 0.5 false positives by chance.
seeds.c: Pre-specify one primary metric before the experiment. Secondary metrics can be exploratory. Apply correction if multiple primary metrics are needed.
seeds.s: Experiment 'wins' on signup rate but loses on revenue; team picks signup as the win and ships; business metric worse.

seeds.t: A/B Testing: Novelty Effect Inflating Early Results
seeds.w: Shipping a new feature, seeing strong early results, and declaring success — users engage more with anything new; the effect decays as novelty wears off.
seeds.c: Run experiments long enough for novelty to wear off (typically 2-4 weeks for engagement features). Check if results are strongest for day-1 users and weaker for day-7+ users.
seeds.s: New UI ships to 50% of users, immediate 15% engagement lift; rolled out; engagement returns to baseline after 3 weeks.

seeds.t: A/B Testing: Undetected Sample Ratio Mismatch
seeds.w: Running an experiment where the assignment ratio doesn't match the observed ratio (50/50 plan, 52/48 reality) — indicates a bug in assignment that biases results.
seeds.c: Always check SRM before analyzing results: observed/expected ratio should be close to 1.0. Common causes: logging failures, bot traffic, cache effects.
seeds.s: Experiment shows significant lift; post-hoc SRM check reveals 42/58 split; result is biased and unreliable.

seeds.t: A/B Testing: Experiment Groups Contaminating Each Other
seeds.w: Running social or collaborative features where treated users interact with control users — the treatment effect 'leaks' into the control group, underestimating the true effect.
seeds.c: Use cluster randomization (randomize by group, not individual) for features with network effects. Measure spillover explicitly.
seeds.s: Notification feature tested by user; treated users notify control users; control engagement rises; effect looks smaller than it is.

seeds.t: A/B Testing: Winning Primary Metric but Ignoring Guardrail Metrics
seeds.w: Shipping an experiment that improves the primary metric but degrades latency, error rate, or another guardrail metric.
seeds.c: Define guardrail metrics before the experiment (latency p99, error rate, revenue). An experiment is only a win if the primary metric improves AND no guardrail metric degrades significantly.
seeds.s: Feature reduces bounce rate but increases page load time 20%; shipped without checking load time; user experience degrades.

seeds.t: A/B Testing: Running Experiment Without Capturing Weekly Seasonality
seeds.w: Running a 3-day experiment when behavior differs between weekdays and weekends — result is biased by when the experiment ran.
seeds.c: Run experiments for at least 1-2 full weeks to capture weekly cycles. Check day-of-week segmentation to identify patterns.
seeds.s: Experiment run Monday-Wednesday shows negative result; run Thursday-Sunday shows positive; true effect is neutral.

seeds.t: A/B Testing: Short-Term Metric Improvement With Long-Term Harm
seeds.w: Shipping a feature that improves a short-term metric at the cost of a long-term metric — the experiment window is too short to see the full effect.
seeds.c: Include lagging metrics (7-day retention, NPS, subscription renewal) alongside leading metrics. Run longer experiments or holdout groups for features with delayed effects.
seeds.s: More aggressive push notifications increase short-term opens but reduce 30-day retention; not visible in 2-week experiment.

seeds.t: Use Lifecycle-Aware Components Instead of Manual Lifecycle Calls
seeds.w: Starting and stopping observers, registrations, and callbacks manually in onResume/onPause — easy to miss symmetrical calls, causing leaks or missing unregistrations.
seeds.c: Use LifecycleObserver or DefaultLifecycleObserver to bind component lifecycle to the Activity/Fragment lifecycle automatically. Observers registered with lifecycle.addObserver() are automatically removed when the lifecycle is destroyed. Prefer lifecycle-aware versions of librari…
seeds.s: crash or leak from a callback firing after Activity is destroyed because onPause cleanup was forgotten.

seeds.t: Never Hold a View, Activity, or Context Reference in ViewModel
seeds.w: Passing an Activity or View reference into a ViewModel constructor, or storing context in a ViewModel field — ViewModels survive configuration changes (rotation); Activity references held by ViewModel will be leaked until the ViewModel is cleared.
seeds.c: Use Application context (via AndroidViewModel) if you need context in ViewModel. Communicate with the UI layer via LiveData, StateFlow, or events. Pass only data, not UI references. Use Hilt's @HiltViewModel for injection without context coupling.
seeds.s: memory leak detected by LeakCanary: Activity retained by ViewModel after rotation.

seeds.t: Launch Coroutines in the Correct Scope
seeds.w: Launching coroutines in GlobalScope from a Fragment or Activity — GlobalScope coroutines outlive the UI component and cause leaks and crashes when accessing destroyed views.
seeds.c: Use viewModelScope in ViewModel (cancelled when ViewModel is cleared), lifecycleScope in Activity/Fragment (cancelled when destroyed), and repeatOnLifecycle(Lifecycle.State.STARTED) for collecting flows that should pause in the background. Use viewLifecycleOwner.lifecycleScope i…
seeds.s: coroutine updating a destroyed Fragment's views, causing IllegalStateException or NullPointerException.

seeds.t: Never Query Room on the Main Thread
seeds.w: Calling Room DAO methods directly on the main thread — Room throws IllegalStateException by default (unless allowMainThreadQueries() is set, which is only for tests).
seeds.c: Use suspend functions in DAOs for coroutine-based queries (Room generates the dispatcher switch automatically). For Flow queries, collect in a coroutine scope with the appropriate lifecycle. Use .allowMainThreadQueries() only in tests.
seeds.s: 'Cannot access database on the main thread' IllegalStateException, or ANR from blocking the main thread with a slow query.

seeds.t: Prefer StateFlow Over LiveData for New Kotlin Code
seeds.w: Using LiveData in the ViewModel and mapping it through transformations for reactive state — LiveData is lifecycle-aware but requires the main thread and is less powerful than Kotlin's Flow.
seeds.c: Expose state as StateFlow (for current state) and SharedFlow (for one-shot events) from ViewModel. Collect in the UI layer using repeatOnLifecycle(STARTED) to automatically pause collection when the app is backgrounded. StateFlow integrates naturally with coroutines and can be u…
seeds.s: complex LiveData transformation chains that Flow operators (map, filter, combine) would express more clearly.

seeds.t: Use Hilt for Dependency Injection Instead of Manual Singletons
seeds.w: Using companion object singletons or Application subclass fields to share dependencies — global mutable state, hard to test, and tight coupling between components.
seeds.c: Use Hilt (@HiltAndroidApp on Application, @AndroidEntryPoint on Activity/Fragment, @HiltViewModel on ViewModel). Define modules with @Module and @Provides or @Binds. Hilt generates scoped component graphs automatically. Replace singleton manually managed objects with @Singleton-…
seeds.s: Application class holding 15 singleton references, or tests unable to replace real implementations with fakes.

seeds.t: Use WorkManager for Guaranteed Background Work
seeds.w: Using AsyncTask (deprecated), foreground services, or AlarmManager for simple deferrable background work — AsyncTask is removed in API 30+; services are killed by the OS; AlarmManager is complex and not guaranteed.
seeds.c: Use WorkManager for deferrable, guaranteed background work that must survive process death (sync, upload, cleanup). Define a Worker or CoroutineWorker, set Constraints (network, charging), and enqueue with OneTimeWorkRequest or PeriodicWorkRequest. WorkManager handles Doze mode,…
seeds.s: background sync failing on Android 10+ because foreground services are restricted and AlarmManager is killed in Doze mode.

seeds.t: Minimise Recomposition Scope With State Hoisting
seeds.w: Placing all state in a single top-level composable — any state change recomposes the entire tree.
seeds.c: Hoist state to the lowest common ancestor that needs it; pass it down as parameters to stateless child composables. Use remember { } for local stable state, rememberSaveable for state that survives recomposition and process death. Use derivedStateOf { } to derive state that shou…
seeds.s: typing in a text field causing the entire screen to recompose, visible in the Layout Inspector's recomposition highlights.

seeds.t: Use the Correct Compose Side-Effect API for Each Use Case
seeds.w: Running side effects directly in the composable body — side effects in composition run on every recomposition, causing repeated API calls, log spam, or resource initialisation on every frame.
seeds.c: LaunchedEffect(key) for coroutines that should run when key changes and cancel/relaunch on recomposition. SideEffect for synchronising Compose state to non-Compose objects. DisposableEffect(key) for effects that need cleanup (registering/unregistering listeners). rememberCorouti…
seeds.s: API being called on every recomposition because a LaunchedEffect was omitted.

seeds.t: Use Navigation Component for Fragment and Compose Navigation
seeds.w: Managing back stack manually with FragmentManager transactions — complex, error-prone, hard to handle deep links and process death restoration.
seeds.c: Use Jetpack Navigation Component with a nav_graph.xml (or NavHost in Compose). It handles back stack, deep links, safe args for type-safe argument passing, and animated transitions. Use NavController.navigate(action) and popBackStack() instead of direct FragmentTransactions. For…
seeds.s: back button behaviour inconsistent, deep links not working, or process death not restoring the correct screen.

seeds.t: Use ActivityResultLauncher for Runtime Permissions
seeds.w: Calling requestPermissions() and overriding onRequestPermissionsResult() — deprecated in favour of the Activity Result API; the old pattern requires matching request codes manually.
seeds.c: Register a launcher with registerForActivityResult(RequestPermission(), callback) before the Fragment/Activity is started. Call launcher.launch(Manifest.permission.CAMERA) to trigger the request. Handle DENIED and permanently denied (shouldShowRequestPermissionRationale returns …
seeds.s: permission request code mismatches or permission results delivered to the wrong callback.

seeds.t: Use DataStore Instead of SharedPreferences
seeds.w: Using SharedPreferences for storing app preferences — synchronous API that blocks the main thread (commit()), not safe for concurrent access, and lacks type safety.
seeds.c: Use Preferences DataStore (simple key-value) or Proto DataStore (typed schema with protobuf) from androidx.datastore. DataStore is fully asynchronous (coroutines/Flow), handles concurrent access safely, and is consistent across process restarts. Migrate with SharedPreferencesMig…
seeds.s: ANR from SharedPreferences.commit() on the main thread, or concurrent write corruption from multiple threads accessing SharedPreferences.

seeds.t: Use Parcelable or @Parcelize for Intent Extras
seeds.w: Passing complex objects via Intent extras using Java Serializable — Serializable uses reflection, is slow, and produces large byte arrays.
seeds.c: Implement Parcelable for classes passed between Activities/Fragments — Kotlin's @Parcelize annotation generates the implementation automatically. For Navigation Component, use Safe Args which generates type-safe argument classes. For large data, pass an ID and retrieve data from…
seeds.s: TransactionTooLargeException from large Serializable objects, or slow Intent extras parsing.

seeds.t: Never Hold Activity Context in Long-Lived Objects
seeds.w: Storing an Activity reference in a singleton, companion object, or static field — the Activity cannot be garbage collected as long as the reference is held, leaking the entire Activity's view hierarchy.
seeds.c: Use Application context for operations that don't need a UI context (database, shared prefs, WorkManager). Pass Activity context only to short-lived objects (dialogs, Toasts). Use WeakReference<Activity> if you genuinely need the Activity reference in a longer-lived object. Use …
seeds.s: memory growing with each screen rotation or navigation, OutOfMemoryError after extensive app use.

seeds.t: Use Coil or Glide for Bitmap Loading and Caching
seeds.w: Decoding bitmaps manually with BitmapFactory.decodeFile() and holding them in memory — easy to forget recycle(), no memory or disk caching, and no automatic downsampling to display size.
seeds.c: Use Coil (Kotlin-first, coroutines-native) or Glide for image loading — they handle memory and disk caching, automatically downsample to the ImageView size, respect lifecycle, and handle transformations. Use inSampleSize with BitmapFactory.Options only when loading bitmaps for p…
seeds.s: OutOfMemoryError from holding full-resolution bitmaps in memory, or images not garbage collected because Bitmaps were never recycled.

seeds.t: Add ProGuard Rules for Libraries That Use Reflection
seeds.w: Releasing a release build without testing it — ProGuard/R8 obfuscation removes class names and methods used only via reflection (Gson, Retrofit model classes, Firebase), causing runtime crashes in release builds that work fine in debug.
seeds.c: Test release builds regularly. Libraries like Retrofit and Gson require @Keep annotations or ProGuard rules to preserve model class names. Check the library's documentation for required consumer ProGuard rules. Use @Keep on classes accessed by name via reflection. Add -printmapp…
seeds.s: app crashes only in release builds with ClassNotFoundException or JsonSyntaxException on model classes.

seeds.t: Use Network Security Config for Certificate Pinning
seeds.w: Trusting all SSL certificates with a custom TrustManager for debugging — this code sometimes accidentally makes it into production.
seeds.c: Use Android's Network Security Configuration (res/xml/network_security_config.xml) to configure certificate pinning, trust anchors, and debug overrides separately per build type. Add the config reference in AndroidManifest.xml: android:networkSecurityConfig. Never disable certif…
seeds.s: SSL certificate validation disabled in production, making the app vulnerable to MITM attacks.

seeds.t: Use Build Types and Flavors for Environment Configuration
seeds.w: Using a single API_URL string in the source code and commenting/uncommenting for different environments — error-prone, pollutes version history, and risks deploying dev URLs to production.
seeds.c: Define environment-specific values in build.gradle using buildConfigField: buildTypes { release { buildConfigField 'String', 'API_URL', '"https://api.prod.com"' } debug { buildConfigField 'String', 'API_URL', '"https://api.dev.com"' } }. Access as BuildConfig.API_URL in code. Us…
seeds.s: development API URL shipped in a production release because manual editing was missed.

seeds.t: Write Explicit Migrations for Room Database Version Changes
seeds.w: Incrementing Room's database version without providing a Migration — Room throws IllegalStateException and falls back to destructive migration (wipes the database) if fallbackToDestructiveMigration() is set.
seeds.c: Write a Migration for every database version increment: Migration(1, 2) { db -> db.execSQL('ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN bio TEXT') }. Add it to the database builder. Test migrations with MigrationTestHelper. Use Room's autoMigrations for simple structural changes (Android 4.1+)…
seeds.s: user data wiped on app update because a migration was missing and destructive migration silently occurred.

seeds.t: Mark Types as @Stable or @Immutable for Compose Optimisation
seeds.w: Passing data classes or interfaces to composables and wondering why recomposition is excessive — Compose's compiler can't infer stability for classes with non-stable fields, so it always recomposes when the reference changes.
seeds.c: Mark classes as @Stable (if equality checks are reliable) or @Immutable (if content never changes after construction) so the Compose compiler can skip recomposition when the object reference is the same. Use the Compose compiler metrics (freeCompilerArgs += '-Pplugin:androidx.co…
seeds.s: composable with a data class parameter recomposing on every parent recomposition even when the data hasn't changed.

seeds.t: Use OnPush Change Detection to Avoid Full Tree Checks
seeds.w: Leaving the default ChangeDetectionStrategy.Default on all components — Angular checks the entire component tree on every browser event, timer, and HTTP response, even when nothing relevant changed.
seeds.c: Set changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush on components. Angular then only checks when an @Input reference changes, an event fires inside the component, or an async pipe emits. Pair with immutable data or Signals. Inject ChangeDetectorRef and call markForCheck() when t…
seeds.s: profiling showing hundreds of change detection cycles per second in a large app, causing frame drops.

seeds.t: Use async Pipe in Templates Instead of Manual subscribe()
seeds.w: Subscribing in the component class with this.data$ = obs.subscribe(v => this.data = v) and storing values in a component property — must manually unsubscribe or risk memory leaks.
seeds.c: Assign the Observable to a class property and use the async pipe in the template: {{ data$ | async }}. The async pipe subscribes on creation, updates the view on each emission, and unsubscribes automatically on component destroy. Works with *ngIf as let: *ngIf='data$ | async as …
seeds.s: memory leak from subscriptions not cleaned up when navigating away from a component.

seeds.t: Use takeUntilDestroyed to Auto-Unsubscribe
seeds.w: Implementing ngOnDestroy with a Subject and takeUntil pattern in every component — repetitive boilerplate that's easy to forget.
seeds.c: Use takeUntilDestroyed() from @angular/core/rxjs-interop (Angular 16+): this.service.data$.pipe(takeUntilDestroyed()).subscribe(). When called in the constructor or injection context, it hooks into the component's destroy lifecycle automatically. For use outside injection contex…
seeds.s: boilerplate Subject destroy$ in every component that takeUntilDestroyed eliminates.

seeds.t: Use Standalone Components Instead of NgModule Declarations
seeds.w: Creating NgModules to declare every component, directive, and pipe — NgModule is now optional and adds boilerplate with no benefit for most use cases.
seeds.c: Add standalone: true to the component decorator and import dependencies directly in the imports array: imports: [CommonModule, RouterLink, MatButtonModule]. Bootstrap with bootstrapApplication(AppComponent, appConfig) instead of an AppModule. Standalone is the default in Angular…
seeds.s: AppModule, SharedModule, and FeatureModule files that serve only as declaration registries without providing actual functionality.

seeds.t: Use Signals for Fine-Grained Reactive State
seeds.w: Using component properties with manual markForCheck() calls for local reactive state — verbose and error-prone.
seeds.c: Use Angular Signals (Angular 17+): count = signal(0); doubled = computed(() => this.count() * 2). Read signals by calling them: {{ count() }}. Update with set(), update(), or mutate(). Computed signals memoize and only recompute when dependencies change. Use toSignal() to conver…
seeds.s: manual change detection triggering for local state that computed Signals would track automatically.

seeds.t: Always Use trackBy in *ngFor for List Performance
seeds.w: Using *ngFor='let item of items' without trackBy — Angular destroys and recreates DOM nodes on every array reference change even if the data is the same.
seeds.c: Provide a trackBy function: *ngFor='let item of items; trackBy: trackById'. Define trackById(index: number, item: Item): number { return item.id }. Angular uses the returned key to match existing DOM nodes to updated data, only creating/destroying nodes that actually changed.
seeds.s: list flickers and loses focus/animation state on every data refresh because all nodes are recreated.

seeds.t: Lazy-Load Routes With loadComponent for Code Splitting
seeds.w: Importing all components eagerly in the root routing config — the entire application is bundled and downloaded on first load.
seeds.c: Use loadComponent for standalone components: { path: 'dashboard', loadComponent: () => import('./dashboard/dashboard.component').then(m => m.DashboardComponent) }. For module-based apps, use loadChildren. Angular's build system creates separate chunks. Use preloadingStrategy: Pr…
seeds.s: initial bundle over 2 MB because every feature's code is included in the main chunk.

seeds.t: Use InjectionToken for Optional Configuration Dependencies
seeds.w: Using a concrete class as a DI token for optional configuration — tree-shaking can't remove it and providing optional config requires awkward null-checking or subclassing.
seeds.c: Create typed InjectionToken: export const CONFIG = new InjectionToken<AppConfig>('app.config', { factory: () => defaultConfig }). Provide with: providers: [{ provide: CONFIG, useValue: customConfig }]. Inject with: private config = inject(CONFIG). Use the factory option for a tr…
seeds.s: library configuration requiring a concrete class that pollutes the consumer's DI tree even when using defaults.

seeds.t: Use Typed Reactive Forms for Compile-Time Safety
seeds.w: Using untyped FormGroup and FormControl (or FormGroup<any>) — no type inference on .value, .get(), or setValue().
seeds.c: Use strictly typed forms (Angular 14+): form = new FormGroup({ name: new FormControl<string>('', Validators.required), age: new FormControl<number | null>(null) }). form.value.name is typed as string | undefined; form.getRawValue() returns the full typed value. Use FormBuilder w…
seeds.s: runtime type errors from form.get('fieldName').value when the field name is misspelled or the type is wrong.

seeds.t: Use Renderer2 for DOM Manipulation, Not nativeElement Directly
seeds.w: Accessing ElementRef.nativeElement directly to manipulate the DOM — breaks server-side rendering (Angular Universal), web workers, and introduces XSS risks with innerHTML.
seeds.c: Inject Renderer2 and use its methods: renderer.setStyle(el, 'color', 'red'), renderer.addClass(el, 'active'), renderer.setAttribute(el, 'aria-label', 'x'). Renderer2 abstracts platform differences and works in all Angular environments. Use DomSanitizer for content that must be t…
seeds.s: application working in the browser but crashing in Angular Universal SSR because nativeElement is null in server rendering.

seeds.t: Use HTTP Interceptors for Auth Tokens and Error Handling
seeds.w: Adding Authorization headers in every service method and handling 401 errors in every component — duplicated code that gets missed in new services.
seeds.c: Provide a functional interceptor: export const authInterceptor: HttpInterceptorFn = (req, next) => { const token = inject(AuthService).token; return next(req.clone({ setHeaders: { Authorization: `Bearer ${token}` } })); }. Register with provideHttpClient(withInterceptors([authIn…
seeds.s: auth headers missing from some API calls because a new service didn't include the manual header setup.

seeds.t: Understand View Encapsulation Before Disabling It
seeds.w: Setting ViewEncapsulation.None on components to easily override child styles — this leaks all styles from the component globally, affecting the entire application.
seeds.c: Keep the default ViewEncapsulation.Emulated (adds attribute selectors to scope styles). To style child components from a parent, use ::ng-deep (deprecated but functional) with the component's host attribute for scoping, or prefer CSS custom properties for theming. Use ViewEncaps…
seeds.s: styles from one component unexpectedly changing the appearance of unrelated components after ViewEncapsulation.None was added.

seeds.t: Use ng-content for Flexible Component Composition
seeds.w: Accepting HTML template content as a string @Input and using innerHTML — XSS risk, no Angular binding, and no template compilation.
seeds.c: Use <ng-content> in the component template for transclusion. Multi-slot projection with select: <ng-content select='[header]'></ng-content> and <ng-content select='[footer]'></ng-content>. Use ngTemplateOutlet for programmatic template projection. With signals, use contentChild(…
seeds.s: passing raw HTML as a string input when ng-content composition would be safer and more capable.

seeds.t: Use Route Resolvers to Pre-Fetch Data Before Navigation
seeds.w: Fetching data in ngOnInit and showing a loading spinner while the component is active — the component mounts in an empty state and users see layout shifts.
seeds.c: Use a functional resolver on the route: resolve: { user: () => inject(UserService).getUser(id) }. The router waits for the resolved data before activating the component. Access with this.route.snapshot.data['user'] or inject(ActivatedRoute).data. Combine with loadComponent for l…
seeds.s: content popping in after navigation complete, causing layout shifts and flash of empty content.

seeds.t: Run Intensive Work Outside Angular's Zone
seeds.w: Running requestAnimationFrame loops, intensive polling, or WebSocket message handlers inside Angular's zone — Zone.js intercepts all async operations and triggers change detection on every iteration, tanking performance.
seeds.c: Inject NgZone and run intensive code outside: this.ngZone.runOutsideAngular(() => { setInterval(() => { /* heavy work */ }, 16); }). Re-enter the zone when DOM update is needed: this.ngZone.run(() => this.count = newValue). With Signals and OnPush, consider zoneless mode (experi…
seeds.s: canvas animation or WebSocket app running at 10 fps because change detection fires 60 times per second.

seeds.t: Migrate to Signal Inputs and Model Signals
seeds.w: Using @Input() decorator properties — not reactive, requires ngOnChanges to respond to changes, and doesn't integrate with computed or effect.
seeds.c: Use input() signal (Angular 17.1+): name = input<string>(); name = input.required<string>(). Read as a signal: this.name(). Use model() for two-way binding: count = model(0). Use output() for typed events: clicked = output<MouseEvent>(). Signal inputs integrate directly with com…
seeds.s: ngOnChanges with a SimpleChanges loop to detect which @Input changed, replaced by an effect() on the specific signal input.

seeds.t: Use inject() Function Instead of Constructor Injection
seeds.w: Adding constructor parameters for every dependency, leading to long constructor signatures that are hard to read and break when dependencies are added.
seeds.c: Use the inject() function (callable during construction): private userService = inject(UserService); private router = inject(Router). Works in components, directives, pipes, services, and functional guards/resolvers. Inject with options: inject(TOKEN, { optional: true, self: tru…
seeds.s: constructors with 8+ parameters making components hard to instantiate in tests.

seeds.t: Use @defer for Lazy-Loading Component Subtrees
seeds.w: Loading heavy below-the-fold components (rich text editors, charts, maps) eagerly with the rest of the bundle — users download code for components they may never see.
seeds.c:
Use Angular 17's @defer block: @defer (on viewport) { } @placeholder {
Loading chart...
}. Trigger conditions: on idle, on interaction, on viewport, on timer(500ms), when condition. The deferred block is lazy-loaded as a separate chunk automatically. Us…
seeds.s: main bundle including chart.js and complex editor libraries even for users who never scroll to those components.

seeds.t: Implement ControlValueAccessor for Custom Form Controls
seeds.w: Creating a component that wraps an input and uses @Input value and @Output valueChange for form integration — doesn't work with Angular's reactive forms or ngModel.
seeds.c: Implement ControlValueAccessor and provide NG_VALUE_ACCESSOR: provide: NG_VALUE_ACCESSOR, useExisting: forwardRef(() => MyControl), multi: true. Implement writeValue (receive value from form), registerOnChange (notify form of changes), registerOnTouched. For standalone, the host…
seeds.s: custom date picker or multi-select not working with formControl binding because ControlValueAccessor wasn't implemented.

seeds.t: Opt Into Zoneless Mode to Remove Zone.js Overhead
seeds.w: Keeping Zone.js in production Angular apps that have fully adopted Signals — Zone.js patches all async browser APIs and adds overhead to every async operation just to detect changes.
seeds.c: Angular 18+ supports zoneless mode experimentally: provideExperimentalZonelessChangeDetection() in appConfig providers. Remove zone.js from angular.json polyfills. Change detection now only runs when Signals change or markForCheck is called. Requires OnPush everywhere and Signal…
seeds.s: every setTimeout and Promise.then triggering change detection even for fully Signals-based components.

seeds.t: Tasks Must Be Idempotent — Safe to Run Repeatedly
seeds.w: Using the shell or command module for operations that have a dedicated Ansible module (creating directories, installing packages, editing files) — shell commands are not idempotent and re-running the playbook causes unintended side effects or errors.
seeds.c: Use the appropriate module for each task: file for directories, package for software, lineinfile for file edits, copy/template for file deployment. These modules check current state before acting. Reserve shell/command for operations with no module and add creates: or when: guar…
seeds.s: playbook re-run reports changed on tasks that should be no-op, or applies duplicate changes.

seeds.t: Use Handlers to Restart Services Only When Config Changes
seeds.w: Adding a service restart task directly after a config file task — the service restarts on every playbook run even when the config was unchanged.
seeds.c: Use notify: to trigger a handler: notify: Restart nginx in the config task, then define a handler (handlers: - name: Restart nginx; service: name=nginx state=restarted). Handlers run once at the end of the play, only if their task reported changed.
seeds.s: services restarting on every run causing unnecessary downtime, or multiple restarts when one would suffice.

seeds.t: Encrypt Secrets With ansible-vault, Never Commit Plaintext
seeds.w: Storing passwords, API keys, or private keys in plaintext in vars files or group_vars — they are visible in version control and logs.
seeds.c: Encrypt secret values with ansible-vault encrypt_string 'secret' --name 'var_name' and embed the encrypted value directly in vars files. Or encrypt entire files with ansible-vault encrypt secrets.yml. Run playbooks with --ask-vault-pass or set ANSIBLE_VAULT_PASSWORD_FILE. Use di…
seeds.s: credentials visible in git history or leaked through CI log output.

seeds.t: Organise Complex Playbooks Into Roles
seeds.w: Putting all tasks for every service into a single long playbook file — impossible to reuse, test, or share individual components.
seeds.c: Use ansible-galaxy init rolename to create a role with the standard directory structure: tasks/main.yml, handlers/main.yml, defaults/main.yml, templates/, files/, vars/, meta/main.yml. Include roles in playbooks with roles: [nginx, app, database]. Roles in defaults/ have the low…
seeds.s: playbook with 500+ tasks in one file, no component reuse across environments.

seeds.t: Use --check Mode to Preview Changes Before Applying
seeds.w: Running playbooks directly on production without previewing what will change — risk of unintended modifications.
seeds.c: Run ansible-playbook --check playbook.yml for a dry run that shows what would change without making changes. Combine with --diff to show file diffs. Add check_mode: yes to tasks that should only run in check mode (validation tasks). Note: some modules behave differently in check…
seeds.s: unexpected production changes because the playbook behaviour was not previewed.

seeds.t: Use loop Instead of Deprecated with_items
seeds.w: Using with_items: [a, b, c] to loop over a list — with_items is deprecated in favour of loop and has different behaviour for nested lists.
seeds.c: Use loop: [a, b, c] and reference the current item with {{ item }}. For dict iteration, use loop with dict2items: loop: '{{ my_dict | dict2items }}' and reference item.key and item.value. Use loop_control.label to customise the loop output label for readability.
seeds.s: deprecation warnings on with_items, or unexpected flattening of nested lists.

seeds.t: Use block/rescue/always for Task Error Handling
seeds.w: Using ignore_errors: yes on tasks to prevent playbook failure — this silently ignores all errors including critical ones and provides no recovery logic.
seeds.c: Use block/rescue/always for structured error handling: block contains tasks to try, rescue contains recovery tasks (runs if any block task fails), always contains cleanup tasks (always runs). This is Ansible's equivalent of try/catch/finally. Use ignore_errors only for truly non…
seeds.s: playbook succeeding despite critical failures because ignore_errors masked them.

seeds.t: Use when to Skip Tasks Conditionally
seeds.w: Using shell: if [[ condition ]]; then action; fi — mixing shell logic into Ansible obscures intent, breaks check mode, and bypasses Ansible's changed/ok tracking.
seeds.c: Use Ansible's when: condition on the task: when: ansible_os_family == 'Debian' or when: my_var is defined. Combine conditions with and/or. Use register to capture task output and when: result.rc == 0 to branch on it.
seeds.s: shell conditional tasks always reporting changed because Ansible can't introspect the condition.

seeds.t: Tag Tasks and Roles for Selective Execution
seeds.w: Running the entire playbook every time a single role needs to be updated — takes minutes when only seconds of targeted tasks are needed.
seeds.c: Add tags: [nginx, config] to tasks and roles. Run only tagged tasks with ansible-playbook --tags nginx or skip them with --skip-tags tests. Use always and never as special tags. Document the available tags in the playbook README.
seeds.s: full multi-minute playbook runs when updating only a config file for one service.

seeds.t: Use group_vars and host_vars for Variable Organisation
seeds.w: Defining all variables in the playbook or passing them all on the command line — no separation between environments and hard to maintain at scale.
seeds.c: Use the group_vars/ and host_vars/ directories relative to the inventory file. group_vars/all.yml applies to all hosts; group_vars/webservers.yml applies to the webservers group; host_vars/db01.yml applies to one host. Ansible merges variables from all applicable files in a defi…
seeds.s: production and staging using the same variable values because environment-specific vars weren't separated.

seeds.t: Use template for Dynamic Files, copy for Static Files
seeds.w: Using the copy module for config files that need variable substitution, or using template for binary files — copy does no variable expansion; template renders Jinja2 before writing.
seeds.c: Use template: src=nginx.conf.j2 dest=/etc/nginx/nginx.conf for any file containing {{ variables }} or {% logic %} blocks. Use copy: for binary files, static assets, or scripts that should be transferred verbatim. Both modules are idempotent using checksums.
seeds.s: config file contains literal {{ variable_name }} text instead of the resolved value because copy was used instead of template.

seeds.t: Register Task Output and Use debug to Inspect It
seeds.w: Running a shell command and guessing whether it succeeded or what it returned — no visibility into intermediate values.
seeds.c: Use register: result to capture a task's output, then debug: var=result to print it. Check result.stdout, result.stderr, result.rc for shell tasks. Use failed_when: result.rc != 0 to control failure conditions. Access registered variables in subsequent when: and other conditions.
seeds.s: tasks silently doing the wrong thing because the command output was never inspected.

seeds.t: Use become for Privilege Escalation, Not sudo in shell
seeds.w: Adding sudo to shell commands directly — bypasses Ansible's privilege escalation framework, breaks become_user, and doesn't work with become_method alternatives (su, pbrun, doas).
seeds.c: Set become: yes at the play, role, or task level to enable privilege escalation. Set become_user: postgres to escalate to a specific user. Configure become_method: sudo (or alternatives) in ansible.cfg. Use become: yes only on tasks that require it, not globally.
seeds.s: sudo inside shell commands failing in environments that require interactive password or different privilege methods.

seeds.t: Disable gather_facts When Speed Matters and Facts Aren't Needed
seeds.w: Leaving gather_facts enabled for playbooks that don't use any ansible_* variables — fact gathering connects to every host and takes 1–5 seconds per host at the start of every play.
seeds.c: Set gather_facts: false at the play level for playbooks that don't need host facts. For roles that need specific facts, use the setup module with filter: ansible_distribution to gather only required fact categories. On large inventories, this can save minutes per playbook run.
seeds.s: playbook taking 2 minutes on 30 hosts just in the Gathering Facts phase.

seeds.t: Use delegate_to for Tasks That Run on a Different Host
seeds.w: Using local_action or a separate play targeting localhost for tasks that logically belong with their host's context (registering a service in a load balancer, creating a DNS record).
seeds.c: Use delegate_to: localhost (or any other host) to run a task on a different host while keeping the host variables of the original target. Use run_once: true with delegate_to for tasks that should run once regardless of inventory size (updating a shared resource).
seeds.s: awkward playbook structure using a separate local play to perform actions that are logically part of the host's provisioning.

seeds.t: Use Jinja2 Filters for Variable Transformation
seeds.w: Using shell tasks or Python scripts to transform variables (converting to uppercase, joining lists, defaulting undefined vars) — adds unnecessary tasks and breaks check mode.
seeds.c: Use Jinja2 filters directly in variable expressions: {{ my_var | upper }}, {{ my_list | join(',') }}, {{ my_var | default('fallback') }}, {{ my_dict | combine(overrides) }}. Ansible adds many filters beyond Jinja2 defaults: selectattr, map, to_json, b64encode, hash. Use {{ my_va…
seeds.s: shell tasks for simple string transformations that a Jinja2 filter handles in one line.

seeds.t: Test Roles With Molecule Before Production Use
seeds.w: Testing Ansible roles by running them against development servers — slow feedback, risks misconfiguration, and no automated regression testing.
seeds.c: Use Molecule to test roles in containers or VMs: molecule init scenario creates test infrastructure. Write tests with testinfra or ansible-verify to assert the role's outcomes. Run molecule test to converge (apply role), verify (assert outcomes), and destroy (clean up) automatic…
seeds.s: role works in development but fails in production because untested edge cases weren't caught.

seeds.t: Use async for Long-Running Tasks to Avoid Timeouts
seeds.w: Running long operations (large package installs, database migrations) synchronously — SSH connections can timeout after 30 minutes, killing the task mid-run.
seeds.c: Run long tasks asynchronously with async: 3600 poll: 30 (start the task, check every 30 seconds, timeout after 3600 seconds). Use async_status to check the job later. For fire-and-forget tasks, use async: 300 poll: 0. Monitor with register + async_status in a separate task.
seeds.s: Ansible connection timed out during a long package install, leaving the host in an inconsistent state.

seeds.t: Use Fully Qualified Collection Names for Clarity
seeds.w: Using short module names (community.docker.docker_container becomes docker_container) — ambiguous when multiple collections provide modules with the same short name.
seeds.c: Use fully qualified collection names (FQCN) in tasks: ansible.builtin.copy, community.docker.docker_container, community.postgresql.postgresql_db. Set collections: [community.docker] at the play level as a shorthand. Install collections with ansible-galaxy collection install com…
seeds.s: module name collision where the wrong collection's module is used, or playbooks breaking after collection updates.

seeds.t: Run ansible-lint and --syntax-check Before Applying
seeds.w: Applying playbooks without linting — common mistakes (deprecated modules, missing names, bad variable quoting) cause runtime errors or unexpected behaviour.
seeds.c: Run ansible-lint playbook.yml to enforce best practices and catch common errors before execution. Run ansible-playbook --syntax-check playbook.yml for a fast syntax validation. Integrate both into CI/CD pipelines as a pre-merge check. Use a .ansible-lint config file to configure…
seeds.s: playbook failing mid-run on a syntax error or deprecated feature that linting would have caught before execution.

seeds.t: XOR Register With Itself to Zero It
seeds.w: Using mov eax, 0 to zero a register — this encodes as a 5-byte instruction and does not break false dependencies on AMD micro-architectures.
seeds.c: Use xor eax, eax; it encodes in 2 bytes, is recognised as a dependency-breaking idiom by all modern x86-64 CPUs, and also clears the upper 32 bits of RAX due to zero-extension. Recognised zeroing idioms: xor, sub reg,reg, pxor xmm,xmm.
seeds.s: unnecessary instruction bytes and stale dependency chains in tight loops.

seeds.t: Use LEA for Combined Multiply-Add Without Flags
seeds.w: Using a multiply instruction and an add to compute address-like expressions like 3*x+5 — ties up the multiply unit and sets flags.
seeds.c: Use LEA (Load Effective Address) to compute linear combinations without memory access: lea rax, [rdx + rdx*2 + 5] computes 3*rdx+5 in one instruction using the AGU, leaves flags unchanged, and has 1-cycle latency on most CPUs. Valid scale factors are 1, 2, 4, 8.
seeds.s: unnecessary flag-setting or longer dependency chains for index arithmetic.

seeds.t: CMOV Eliminates Unpredictable Branches
seeds.w: Using a conditional jump for a simple value selection when the branch is hard to predict — branch misprediction costs 15–20 cycles on modern CPUs.
seeds.c: Use CMOVcc (conditional move) for branchless selection: cmovg rax, rbx moves rbx into rax if the Greater flag is set, with no branch. Modern compilers often do this automatically; override with __builtin_expect or profile-guided optimisation.
seeds.s: hot code with branch misprediction penalties visible in perf stat or VTune.

seeds.t: Callee-Saved Registers Must Be Preserved
seeds.w: Using RBX, RBP, R12–R15 in a function without saving and restoring them — the caller's values in those registers are silently corrupted.
seeds.c: Under the System V AMD64 ABI, RBX, RBP, R12–R15 are callee-saved: push them at function entry and pop (in reverse order) before ret. Caller-saved (volatile) registers RAX, RCX, RDX, RSI, RDI, R8–R11, XMM0–XMM7 may be freely clobbered.
seeds.s: intermittent data corruption or crashes after calling external functions.

seeds.t: Maintain 16-Byte Stack Alignment Before call
seeds.w: Calling a function without ensuring the stack pointer is 16-byte aligned at the call site — SSE/AVX instructions in the callee that assume alignment (movaps, vmovaps) will fault with #GP.
seeds.c: The System V ABI requires RSP to be 16-byte aligned at the point of a call instruction (which pushes 8 bytes, making RSP mod 16 == 8 on entry). Adjust with sub rsp, 8 (plus any locals rounded to 16) before calls.
seeds.s: 'Segmentation fault' or #GP in library functions using SIMD on an otherwise correct address.

seeds.t: Call VZEROUPPER After AVX Code to Avoid SSE Penalty
seeds.w: Mixing AVX (256-bit YMM) instructions and legacy SSE instructions without clearing the upper YMM bits — on Intel CPUs, each SSE instruction that touches a dirty YMM register incurs a ~70-cycle state-transition penalty.
seeds.c: Call VZEROUPPER (or VZEROALL) before transitioning from AVX to SSE code, or at the end of functions that use YMM registers. Compilers insert this automatically; be explicit in hand-written SIMD.
seeds.s: dramatic unexplained slowdown in SSE code after a library using AVX has run.

seeds.t: Align SIMD Data to Its Natural Width
seeds.w: Loading 128-bit or 256-bit SIMD data from unaligned addresses with aligned load instructions (movaps, vmovaps) — causes a #GP fault on older CPUs and a performance penalty even on newer ones that permit it.
seeds.c: Align data buffers to at least 16 bytes for SSE and 32 bytes for AVX using alignas(32) in C, posix_memalign, or aligned_alloc. Use unaligned loads (movdqu, vmovdqu) only when alignment cannot be guaranteed and profile the impact.
seeds.s: SIGSEGV in SIMD code with aligned move instructions on unaligned heap allocations.

seeds.t: Pad Shared Data to Avoid False Cache Line Sharing
seeds.w: Placing per-thread or per-core data structures adjacent in memory — if two cores write to different variables on the same 64-byte cache line, each write invalidates the other core's cached line (false sharing), serialising otherwise independent writes.
seeds.c: Pad per-thread data structures to a full cache line (64 bytes) with alignas(64) or explicit padding arrays. Group frequently co-written fields together, frequently co-read fields together.
seeds.s: parallel code scaling poorly despite no logical data sharing between threads.

seeds.t: Prefetch Data Before It Is Needed to Hide Latency
seeds.w: Accessing memory sequentially in a loop without prefetching — main-memory latency (~100ns, ~300 cycles) stalls the CPU while data is fetched from DRAM.
seeds.c: Insert prefetchnta or prefetcht0 instructions several iterations ahead of the consuming instruction to hide latency: prefetchnta [rax + 256] followed by the load of [rax]. Use prefetchnta for streaming data to avoid polluting the cache. Profile cache miss rates with perf stat -e…
seeds.s: loop throughput limited by DRAM latency rather than compute.

seeds.t: Use Memory Barriers to Enforce Ordering Between Cores
seeds.w: Assuming that writes in one thread are immediately visible to another without explicit synchronisation — the CPU and compiler may reorder loads and stores.
seeds.c: Use MFENCE for a full store/load barrier, SFENCE after non-temporal stores to make them globally visible, and LFENCE before reading data expected from another core. In C/C++, prefer atomics with appropriate memory_order rather than raw barrier instructions.
seeds.s: lock-free data structures with rare but irreproducible corruption under load.

seeds.t: Align Hot Loop Tops to Cache/Decode Boundaries
seeds.w: Letting the assembler place a loop label at an arbitrary byte offset — if the first few instructions of a loop span two 16-byte fetch windows, the CPU decodes fewer instructions per cycle.
seeds.c: Align hot loop tops with .align 16 (NASM) or .p2align 4 (GAS); pad with multi-byte NOP instructions (0F 1F family) rather than single-byte NOPs to avoid decode stalls. Most compilers do this automatically with -O2.
seeds.s: loop throughput below theoretical maximum visible in CPU performance counters.

seeds.t: Optimise for Throughput Not Just Latency in Loops
seeds.w: Choosing instructions solely by latency and assuming lower-latency instructions are faster in a loop — CPUs execute multiple independent µops in parallel; the limiting factor is often throughput (reciprocal throughput), not latency.
seeds.c: Consult Agner Fog's instruction tables for both latency and reciprocal throughput. Unroll loops and interleave independent computations to keep execution units full. A 5-cycle-latency instruction with 0.5-cycle throughput outperforms a 1-cycle instruction with 1-cycle throughput…
seeds.s: loop performance not improving despite switching to lower-latency instructions.

seeds.t: Use Non-Temporal Stores for Write-Only Streaming
seeds.w: Using regular mov stores to write a large buffer that will not be read back soon — each store triggers a cache-line read-for-ownership, polluting the cache with data that displaces more useful content.
seeds.c: Use non-temporal store instructions (movntps, movntdq, vmovntps) for write-only streaming data that exceeds the cache size. Follow with SFENCE to make stores globally visible before signalling other threads.
seeds.s: memcpy or buffer initialisation slower than memory bandwidth should allow, cache thrashing visible in perf.

seeds.t: Serialise Before RDTSC for Accurate Timing
seeds.w: Using RDTSC alone to time a code segment — the CPU may speculatively execute instructions before or after RDTSC out of order, corrupting the measured interval.
seeds.c: Use CPUID (or LFENCE on AMD) before the start RDTSC to serialise the instruction stream, and RDTSCP (which serialises itself) at the end, then subtract start from end. Intel recommends the CPUID + RDTSC / RDTSCP + CPUID sequence for tight microbenchmarks.
seeds.s: benchmark results with impossible negative intervals or high variance from out-of-order execution.

seeds.t: Replace Integer Division With Reciprocal Multiplication
seeds.w: Using DIV or IDIV for division by a known compile-time constant — integer division is 20–90 cycles on modern CPUs.
seeds.c: Divide by a constant using multiply-by-magic-number + shift: the compiler does this automatically, but in hand-written assembly compute the magic constant and shift amount using a tool or the algorithm in Hacker's Delight. For power-of-two divisors, use SAR (arithmetic shift rig…
seeds.s: hot path spending many cycles in DIV instructions visible in profiler.

seeds.t: Use JMP Not CALL for Tail Calls to Avoid Stack Growth
seeds.w: Ending a function with CALL then RET to a tail-called function — each call pushes an 8-byte return address; chains of tail calls grow the stack unboundedly.
seeds.c: For tail calls, replace CALL/RET with a single JMP to the target: restore the caller's stack frame first, then jump. This is the basis of proper tail-call optimization for recursive routines.
seeds.s: stack overflow from deeply recursive functions that should use O(1) stack space.

seeds.t: Avoid Partial Register Writes That Cause Merge Stalls
seeds.w: Writing to AL or AX then reading RAX — on Intel CPUs before Ice Lake, writing a partial register inserts a merge µop when the full register is later read, adding a latency penalty.
seeds.c: Write the full 32-bit (or 64-bit) register when possible (movzx eax, byte ptr [mem] instead of mov al, byte ptr [mem]); zero-extending to 32 bits implicitly clears the upper 32 bits of the 64-bit register. Use movsx/movzx to extend partial values cleanly.
seeds.s: unexplained latency in code that reads a byte or word then uses the full register.

seeds.t: Unroll Loops to Amortise Branch Overhead
seeds.w: Leaving a tight loop with one iteration per branch — the loop counter update, compare, and branch consume execution resources and limit instruction-level parallelism.
seeds.c: Manually or compiler-unroll the loop body 4–8 times, adjusting the loop counter and bounds accordingly, then handle the remainder with a scalar tail loop. Use #pragma GCC unroll N or equivalent hints. Profile before and after — over-unrolling increases code size and may harm ins…
seeds.s: loop throughput limited by branch and counter overhead rather than compute.

seeds.t: Break Long Dependency Chains for ILP
seeds.w: Writing a reduction loop that chains all iterations (sum += a[i]) through a single accumulator — each addition waits on the previous, limiting throughput to one addition per latency cycles even with a superscalar CPU.
seeds.c: Use multiple independent accumulators (sum0, sum1, sum2, sum3) and combine them after the loop; this exposes instruction-level parallelism. The compiler does this with -O3 and -ffast-math for floating-point; for integers, unroll manually.
seeds.s: loop throughput far below the addition unit's reciprocal throughput.

seeds.t: Use Regular Loads for Data That Will Be Reused
seeds.w: Using non-temporal load hints (PREFETCHNTA, MOVNTDQA) for data that is accessed multiple times — non-temporal hints bypass or minimise cache allocation, so reused data must be re-fetched from DRAM on every access.
seeds.c: Use non-temporal hints only for true streaming data (single-pass reads or writes exceeding the LLC). For data accessed multiple times (filter weights, lookup tables), use regular loads so the hardware prefetcher and cache do their job.
seeds.s: repeated-access patterns running slower after adding non-temporal optimisation.

seeds.t: Always start scripts with set -euo pipefail
seeds.w: a bash script with no error handling — commands silently fail and the script continues.
seeds.c: start every script with set -euo pipefail. -e exits on any error, -u treats unset variables as errors, -o pipefail makes pipes fail if any command in the pipe fails. These three options catch the vast majority of silent failure modes. Add to the top line: #!/usr/bin/env bash the…
seeds.s: script continues after a failing command, producing incorrect results silently.

seeds.t: Always double-quote variables to prevent word splitting
seeds.w: cp $filename $destination — if filename contains spaces, it is split into multiple arguments.
seeds.c: cp "$filename" "$destination". Double-quote all variable expansions. Exception: inside [[ ]] test, variables do not need quoting for comparisons but still should be for consistency. Use "$@" not $@ to preserve argument grouping.
seeds.s: commands fail or behave incorrectly with filenames containing spaces.

seeds.t: Use [[ ]] not [ ] for conditionals in bash
seeds.w: [ $var == 'value' ] (single bracket) — requires careful quoting, does not support && or ||, and has inconsistent behaviour with empty strings.
seeds.c: [[ $var == 'value' ]] (double bracket) — handles empty variables gracefully, supports && ||, and allows pattern matching with ==. Use [ ] only for POSIX sh compatibility. For arithmetic, use (( )) instead: if (( count > 5 )).
seeds.s: unexpected behaviour with empty variables, or syntax error with && inside [ ].

seeds.t: Declare function variables with local to prevent global pollution
seeds.w: variables set inside functions are global by default in bash — they leak into the calling scope and persist after the function returns.
seeds.c: use local var_name='value' inside functions to limit scope. local can also declare without assignment: local result. The return value pattern: local result=$(my_command); echo "$result" returns the value without polluting globals.
seeds.s: variable from one function unexpectedly overwriting a same-named variable in another.

seeds.t: Variables set in subshells and pipes do not persist
seeds.w: command | while read line; do count=$((count+1)); done — the while loop runs in a subshell, and count changes do not propagate back.
seeds.c: use process substitution: while read line; do count=$((count+1)); done < <(command). Or use mapfile/readarray to read into an array. Alternatively, restructure to avoid needing the variable outside the subshell.
seeds.s: counter or variable appears unchanged after a pipeline loop.

seeds.t: Use $() for command substitution, not backticks
seeds.w: result=`command` — backtick syntax is hard to read, cannot be nested without escaping, and is a legacy form.
seeds.c: result=$(command). $() is nestable: result=$(outer $(inner)). Always quote: result="$(command)" to prevent word splitting on the output. For multi-line output, use an array: mapfile -t lines < <(command).
seeds.s: nested command substitution fails, or output split incorrectly on spaces.

seeds.t: Iterate arrays with ${arr[@]} not ${arr[*]}
seeds.w: for item in "${arr[*]}" — joins all elements into one string with IFS.
seeds.c: for item in "${arr[@]}" — expands each element as a separate word, preserving spaces within elements. To get the count: ${#arr[@]}. To append: arr+=(new_element). To pass array to a function: func "${arr[@]}" and receive with local items=("$@").
seeds.s: array elements with spaces merged into one item, or only first element processed.

seeds.t: Use trap for cleanup on exit or signals
seeds.w: creating temp files or resources without cleanup — if the script exits early due to an error, resources leak.
seeds.c: set up cleanup with trap: trap 'rm -f /tmp/myfile.$$' EXIT. The EXIT trap runs on any exit including errors. For multiple cleanup steps: trap 'cleanup_function' EXIT INT TERM. Create temp files with mktemp: tmpfile=$(mktemp).
seeds.s: temp files accumulate, or resources not released when script is interrupted.

seeds.t: Use (( )) for arithmetic, not expr or let
seeds.w: count=$(expr $count + 1) — expr is a separate process, slow for loops, and has confusing quoting.
seeds.c: ((count++)) or ((count = count + 1)) for arithmetic in bash. For assignment: count=$((count + 1)). Comparison: if ((count > 10)). Note: (( )) returns exit code 1 when result is 0, which triggers set -e — use (( count++ )) || true if incrementing from 0.
seeds.s: arithmetic slow in loops, or unexpected exit with set -e when result is zero.

seeds.t: Check for command existence with command -v not which
seeds.w: which curl — which is not available on all systems and may return an exit code of 0 even when the command is not found on some platforms.
seeds.c: command -v curl > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo 'curl required'; exit 1; }. Or: if ! command -v curl &> /dev/null; then echo 'curl not found'; fi. command -v is POSIX and reliable across all shells.
seeds.s: script continues despite missing dependency, or which behaves differently across Linux/macOS.

seeds.t: Use process substitution to avoid subshell variable loss
seeds.w: cmd | while read line; do count=$((count+1)); done — the while loop runs in a subshell so count is lost after the pipe.
seeds.c: use process substitution: while read line; do count=$((count+1)); done < <(cmd). This keeps the loop in the current shell so variable changes persist. Alternatively use lastpipe option in bash 4.2+: shopt -s lastpipe.
seeds.s: variable modified in a while-read loop is still 0 after the loop exits.

seeds.t: Heredoc with dash strips leading tabs for readability
seeds.w: outdenting heredoc content awkwardly to column 1 to avoid whitespace in the output.
seeds.c: use <<- (hyphen) instead of << to strip leading tab characters, allowing the heredoc body to be indented with tabs alongside the surrounding code: cat <<-EOF [TAB]indented content [TAB]EOF. Note: only tabs are stripped, not spaces — ensure your editor uses tabs for this block.
seeds.s: heredoc content has unexpected leading whitespace in output, or code looks poorly formatted.

seeds.t: Use getopts for robust option parsing, not manual $1 checks
seeds.w: manually checking $1 == '-v' with if/case — this breaks for combined flags (-vf), optional arguments, and error handling.
seeds.c: use getopts in a while loop: while getopts 'vf:' opt; do case $opt in v) verbose=1;; f) file=$OPTARG;; esac; done. getopts handles -vf combined flags, OPTARG for values, and invalid-option errors automatically. Shift remaining args with shift $((OPTIND - 1)).
seeds.s: script can't combine flags, breaks with unexpected options, or loses positional args.

seeds.t: Read files line by line with while read, not for
seeds.w: for line in $(cat file) — word-splits on spaces and glob-expands, breaking lines with spaces or special characters.
seeds.c: while IFS= read -r line; do echo "$line"; done < file. IFS= prevents leading/trailing whitespace trimming; -r prevents backslash interpretation. To process both stdin and files, use: while IFS= read -r line; do ...; done < "${1:-/dev/stdin}".
seeds.s: lines with spaces are split into separate iterations, backslashes disappear.

seeds.t: Use parameter expansion instead of forking sed or cut
seeds.w: var=$(echo $str | sed 's/foo/bar/') — forks two processes for simple string operations.
seeds.c: use bash parameter expansion: ${var#prefix} strips shortest prefix, ${var##prefix} strips longest, ${var%suffix} and ${var%%suffix} for suffixes, ${var/find/replace} for substitution. Example: strip extension: ${filename%.*}; get extension: ${filename##*.}. These run in the shel…
seeds.s: scripts slow down in tight loops due to excessive sed/cut/awk forks for string ops.

seeds.t: Check and propagate exit codes explicitly
seeds.w: ignoring the exit code of commands and proceeding regardless of failure, or using set -e without understanding its limitations (it doesn't catch failures in if conditions or pipes).
seeds.c: check $? explicitly for critical commands, or use || to handle failures: cmd || { echo 'cmd failed' >&2; exit 1; }. For pipelines, use set -o pipefail so a failure anywhere in a pipe is captured. Combine with set -euo pipefail at the top of every script.
seeds.s: script continues after a failed command and corrupts subsequent output.

seeds.t: Declare constants with readonly to prevent accidental reassignment
seeds.w: using plain variable assignment for values that should never change — any later code can overwrite them silently.
seeds.c: use readonly VAR=value or declare -r VAR=value to make a variable immutable. Attempting to reassign a readonly variable causes an error immediately, catching bugs early. Use uppercase names by convention for constants. Similarly, export -r for constants passed to subprocesses.
seeds.s: a constant is accidentally overwritten deep in a script, causing hard-to-trace bugs.

seeds.t: Functions return exit codes, not values — use echo or variables
seeds.w: return $result in a bash function and capturing it with result=$( myfunc ) when result is a number larger than 255 — return only accepts 0-255.
seeds.c: output values via echo or printf and capture with command substitution: result=$(myfunc); or write to a named variable (pass-by-name pattern): myfunc result_var. Use return only for success/failure status codes (0 = success).
seeds.s: function 'return 256' silently wraps to 0, or large numbers are truncated mod 256.

seeds.t: Set nullglob so unmatched globs don't become literals
seeds.w: for f in *.log; do process "$f"; done — if no .log files exist, bash passes the literal string '*.log' to process instead of nothing.
seeds.c: shopt -s nullglob before the loop so the glob expands to nothing when no files match, and the loop body is skipped entirely. For one-off checks, test with compgen -G '*.log'. Unset with shopt -u nullglob after if needed.
seeds.s: script processes a literal '*.log' filename when no files exist, causing errors.

seeds.t: Use mktemp for temporary files and clean up with trap
seeds.w: tmp=/tmp/myscript.tmp — predictable temp file names are a security risk (symlink attacks) and may conflict with concurrent runs.
seeds.c: tmp=$(mktemp) or tmp=$(mktemp -d) for a temp directory; immediately register cleanup with trap 'rm -rf "$tmp"' EXIT so it's removed on any exit, including errors and signals. Always quote $tmp in rm to handle paths with spaces.
seeds.s: temp files collide between concurrent script instances, or temp files persist after script failure.

seeds.t: Kelly Criterion: Size Resource Bets Proportionally to Confidence, Not Hope
seeds.w: Allocating maximum resources to an unproven approach because it looks promising — retrying a failing service at 100% rate, shipping 100% of traffic to a promising variant immediately, over-provisioning infrastructure because "more is safer."
seeds.c: Size the allocation to your estimated edge. Kelly criterion: f* = (b·p − q) / b where p = probability of success, b = gain if successful, q = 1−p. Use half-Kelly (0.5×f*) in practice to account for estimation error. Exponential backoff is fractional Kelly for retry: rate proport…
seeds.s: retrying at maximum rate during a degraded service makes the outage worse; canary deployment failure causes full rollout incident; 100% A/B traffic shift after early positive results fails in production; infrastructure over-provisioning doesn't prevent the failure it was meant t…

seeds.t: Systems Near Phase Transitions Collapse Discontinuously, Not Gradually
seeds.w: Adding load incrementally and expecting proportional degradation, then setting a single alert threshold based on linear extrapolation.
seeds.c: Systems have latent capacity — they absorb pressure without visible degradation until a phase transition, then collapse discontinuously. Hysteresis: the collapse threshold differs from the recovery threshold (you must reduce load further than the level that caused failure). Desi…
seeds.s: system appears healthy under increasing load until sudden catastrophic failure; recovery requires reducing load to well below the level that caused failure; performance degrades in a step function, not a curve; "it was fine and then all of a sudden it wasn't."

seeds.t: Failures in Complex Systems Are Correlated and Cascade Past a Critical Density
seeds.w: Estimating system reliability as the product of independent component SLAs, then being surprised when correlated failures cause cascades that the math didn't predict.
seeds.c: Failures are correlated — one failure increases the probability of related failures. There is a critical density: below it, failures are absorbed; above it, they cascade. This is the edge of chaos. Build circuit breakers at the transition point. Do not model failures as independ…
seeds.s: system reliability far worse than component SLAs predict; single component failure triggers disproportionate downstream failures; recovery requires taking multiple services offline; SLAs calculated from independence assumptions are systematically optimistic.

seeds.t: Iterative Processes Require a Contraction Mapping to Converge
seeds.w: Increasing step size or retry rate when an iterative process converges slowly, expecting to reach the solution faster.
seeds.c: Convergence requires a contraction mapping — each step must move strictly closer to the fixed point. Over-relaxation (steps too large) causes oscillation or divergence. Cycles indicate the step lands in a periodic orbit rather than converging. Reduce step size when oscillating; …
seeds.s: retry loop oscillates between two states rather than resolving; optimizer makes progress then reverses; increasing learning rate made training worse; recursive function does not terminate despite appearing to make progress; auto-scaler thrashes between over- and under-provisione…

seeds.t: Extreme Sensitivity to Small Inputs Is a Structural Property, Not a Bug
seeds.w: Treating extreme sensitivity to small input changes as a bug to eliminate through more guards, retries, or defensive coding.
seeds.c: This is a structural property — the system is operating near a stability boundary (Julia set boundary). Two nearby inputs can land in completely different attractor basins regardless of how clean the code is. The fix is to map the boundary and either: (a) redesign to operate wel…
seeds.s: tests are flaky in ways uncorrelated with code changes; function returns dramatically different outputs for inputs differing by a tiny amount; cannot reproduce bugs without exact input replication; behavior appears random despite deterministic code; small default changes cause l…

seeds.t: Concurrent Update Results Depend on the Ordering Convention, Not Just the Lock
seeds.w: Adding mutexes and atomic operations to fix race conditions, then being surprised when results still depend on execution order in ways that locks don't prevent.
seeds.c: When events are nearly simultaneous, the result depends on the mathematical convention for handling simultaneity — not just on synchronization. Ito and Stratonovich conventions give different results for the same system with the same locks. Define the ordering convention explici…
seeds.s: race conditions persist after adding synchronization; replay of events doesn't reproduce original state; financial calculations give different results depending on whether prices apply before or after position updates; test outcomes change based on thread scheduling even when "t…

seeds.t: Adding Features Past the Curse-of-Dimensionality Threshold Actively Hurts
seeds.w: Adding more features to improve model accuracy or more attributes to improve search quality, assuming monotonically better results.
seeds.c: In high dimensions, all points become equidistant — distance metrics lose meaning (curse of dimensionality). Adding features past the intrinsic dimensionality of the data increases variance without reducing bias. Feature selection and dimensionality reduction are load-bearing, n…
seeds.s: model performance degrades after adding more features; similarity search returns irrelevant results at scale; distance-based clustering becomes meaningless; training data requirements grow exponentially; adding attributes to search index makes relevance worse.

seeds.t: Feedback Loops Without Dissipation Cannot Reach a Stable Equilibrium
seeds.w: Building feedback loops expecting them to self-stabilize, without explicitly identifying the dissipative mechanism that removes energy from the loop.
seeds.c: Conservative systems (no energy loss) do not have attractors — they oscillate forever (Liouville's theorem). Stable systems require dissipation: a mechanism that removes energy. Without it, positive feedback diverges; negative feedback oscillates. Identify the dissipative mechan…
seeds.s: auto-scaling thrashes between over- and under-provisioned; recommendation system enters filter bubble and amplifies rather than corrects; feedback amplifies instead of settling; the fix causes more of the problem.

seeds.t: Time-Average User Experience Differs From Ensemble-Average Metrics
seeds.w: Validating system health using statistical sampling across users/requests, assuming these samples represent what an individual user experiences over time.
seeds.c: Ergodicity failure: the time-average a single user experiences can differ fundamentally from the ensemble average across users. Long sessions are over-represented in samples (inspection paradox). A system can appear healthy in aggregate while every individual user experiences de…
seeds.s: metrics look healthy but users complain about degradation; A/B test shows no significant difference but longitudinal users notice regression; p99 looks fine but long-session users experience compounding issues; performance tests pass but production feels slower after extended us…

seeds.t: Zero Gradient Means Stationary Point, Not Necessarily a Minimum
seeds.w: Stopping optimization when gradients reach near-zero, assuming convergence to a minimum.
seeds.c: Zero gradient identifies a stationary point — which may be a minimum, maximum, or saddle point. In high dimensions, saddle points vastly outnumber minima. The stationary action principle confirms: nature (and optimization algorithms) find stationary points, not necessarily minim…
seeds.s: optimizer reports convergence but solution quality is poor; different initializations produce different converged solutions; loss plateaus at a value worse than expected; model trains but validation performance is far below training performance; hyperparameter search always retu…

seeds.t: Improvement After an Extreme Event Is Likely Regression to Mean, Not Causation
seeds.w: Crediting a change made after unusually poor performance with causing the subsequent improvement.
seeds.c: Extreme measurements partly reflect random fluctuation. They are followed by less extreme measurements regardless of any intervention — regression to the mean. The emergency configuration change after an incident appears to "work" partly because the incident itself was an extrem…
seeds.s: every emergency fix appears to work when measured against incident-day metrics; performance improvements look larger than they are because measurement started after a bad period; A/B test results don't hold when the traffic mix returns to baseline; post-mortems consistently iden…

seeds.t: Usage Follows a Power Law: Optimize the Few That Dominate, Tolerate the Many
seeds.w: Optimizing uniformly across all code paths, queries, or users based on mean performance.
seeds.c: Usage almost always follows a power law — a few paths/users/queries consume the majority of resources. The Kolmogorov cascade: energy concentrates in the dominant few before cascading to the many. Optimizing based on mean-case analysis misses the dominant paths. Find the power l…
seeds.s: mean response time looks fine but the app feels slow to heavy users; caching strategy improves average but doesn't move the needle on perceived performance; optimization effort has diminishing returns because the target wasn't the dominant path; p99 is 10-100× p50.

seeds.t: High-Entropy Responses Provide Low Information — Reduce Uncertainty Explicitly
seeds.w: Returning all available information from a function or API on the assumption that consumers can filter what they need.
seeds.c: Shannon entropy measures how much a message reduces uncertainty. High-entropy responses (many equally-likely fields) provide little signal per byte — consumers must spend compute filtering noise. Design for low entropy: return only information that changes what the consumer does…
seeds.s: API consumers always filter down to the same 10% of a large response; adding more log fields doesn't improve debuggability; large payloads with low actionability; downstream services ignore most response fields; "we return everything and let them pick."

seeds.t: Systems in the Same Universality Class Behave Identically — Recognize the Class
seeds.w: Reimplementing a solution from scratch when encountering a problem in a new domain, because the surface vocabulary is different.
seeds.c: Systems in the same universality class behave identically near their critical point regardless of microscopic details. The same mathematical structure governs seemingly unrelated domains — only a few operators are relevant at the scale of interest (renormalization group). When a…
seeds.s: solving a problem that "feels like" something solved before but vocabulary is different; spending time on implementation details that turn out not to matter; discovering mid-implementation that the solution is identical to a known pattern; "we basically rebuilt X without knowing…

seeds.t: Codebase Entropy Increases Over Time — Fight It Explicitly or Accept Growing Change Cost
seeds.w: Treating the increasing cost of making changes as a people problem, a process problem, or a problem fixable by working harder or adding more engineers.
seeds.c: Entropy in a codebase increases over time unless work is done to reduce it — the second law applied to software. Every change that doesn't explicitly reduce entropy (refactoring, deletion, simplification) increases it. Disorder accumulates naturally. The system drifts toward max…
seeds.s: simple features take progressively longer; every fix introduces new bugs; new developers take months to be productive; test coverage grows but developer confidence decreases; "the codebase used to be much simpler."

seeds.t: Flat Loss Minima Generalise Better Than Sharp Ones — Quasispecies Dynamics in Neural Networks
seeds.w: Minimising training loss as the primary objective, treating models with lower training loss as better models.
seeds.c: Sharp minima in the loss landscape (narrow peaks) correspond to solutions that generalise poorly — a small perturbation in weights or inputs moves the solution off the peak. Flat minima (broad, low-curvature regions) are robust to perturbation and generalise better. SGD with sma…
seeds.s: lower training loss accompanies worse test performance; larger batch size improves training speed but hurts generalisation; model fails under small input perturbation despite low training loss; removing regularisation improves training metrics but hurts production performance.

seeds.t: Established Protocols Are Evolutionarily Stable — Superiority Alone Does Not Displace Them
seeds.w: Expecting a technically superior protocol, API, or file format to displace an established one based on performance or elegance alone.
seeds.c: An established protocol is an evolutionarily stable strategy: once adopted widely, no small group of adopters can improve by unilaterally switching to an alternative, regardless of the alternative's technical merit. The switching cost creates an invasion barrier proportional to …
seeds.s: technically superior protocol fails to gain adoption despite clear advantages; migration path exists but is never taken; the "better" solution is always used in new systems but never replaces old ones; "everyone agrees it's better but nobody switches."

seeds.t: Security Hardening Has No Permanent Advantage — Every Defence Is Eventually Bypassed
seeds.w: Treating security as a problem to be solved permanently — a state where the system is secure.
seeds.c: Security is a Red Queen arms race: every defence is an adaptive pressure on attackers, who evolve to bypass it. There is no permanent security; only a current relative position in an ongoing coevolutionary process. The appropriate mental model is not "securing a system" but "run…
seeds.s: security hardening is bypassed within months of deployment; patches address known vectors but new vectors emerge; "we fixed the vulnerability" followed by a related exploitation; security team constantly reactive despite investment; "we thought we were secure."

seeds.t: A/B Test Differences in Small Samples Are Often Drift, Not Selection — Require Large Effects
seeds.w: Shipping an A/B test winner based on a statistically significant result from a small sample, without verifying the effect size is large enough to survive production conditions.
seeds.c: Kimura's neutral theory applied to A/B testing: when the selection coefficient (effect size) is small relative to 1/√n, the observed difference is dominated by random drift, not genuine signal. A result is statistically significant but practically meaningless if the effect size …
seeds.s: A/B test winner shows no effect in production rollout; result replicates in some segments but not others; effect size shrinks from test to production; "it worked in the test but not at scale."

seeds.t: Feature Interactions Are Generically Non-Additive — Unit Tests Cannot Catch Epistatic Failures
seeds.w: Relying on unit tests and individual integration tests to catch all interaction failures when deploying multiple features simultaneously.
seeds.c: Epistasis in biology demonstrates that feature interaction effects are generically non-additive — the effect of A given B is present can be completely different from the effect of A alone, including sign reversal (two individually beneficial changes that together are harmful). T…
