seeds.s: elements overflow their container after padding is added, or layout breaks when borders are added.

seeds.t: CSS Grid Creates Implicit Tracks for Overflow Items
seeds.w: Defining grid-template-columns but not grid-template-rows, then being surprised when extra items are placed in unexpectedly thin rows — CSS Grid creates implicit row tracks sized to auto (content height) for items beyond the defined template.
seeds.c: Set grid-auto-rows to a useful value (minmax(100px, auto)) to control implicit track sizing. Use grid-auto-flow: dense to fill holes in the grid. Explicitly define rows when row sizing is critical to the layout.
seeds.s: rows after the first set are collapsed to content height rather than matching the defined row size.

seeds.t: Animate transform and opacity for GPU-Accelerated Transitions
seeds.w: Animating top, left, width, height, or margin — these trigger layout recalculation on every frame, causing jank.
seeds.c: Animate only transform (translate, scale, rotate) and opacity, which the browser can composite on the GPU without layout recalculation. Use will-change: transform sparingly on elements that will definitely animate to promote them to their own layer ahead of time.
seeds.s: animations stutter or drop frames on mid-range devices; Chrome DevTools shows purple 'Recalculate Style' or green 'Layout' frames.

seeds.t: Pseudo-Elements Require content Property to Render
seeds.w: Styling ::before or ::after without setting the content property — the pseudo-element does not render at all even with display, width, height, and background set.
seeds.c: Always include content: '' (empty string) at minimum on ::before and ::after pseudo-elements; without it the element does not exist in the rendering tree. Set display: block if you need dimensional properties.
seeds.s: ::before or ::after styling has no visible effect; inspecting in DevTools shows the pseudo-element is absent.

seeds.t: overflow: hidden Clips Absolute Children and Creates Stacking Context
seeds.w: Adding overflow: hidden to a container to contain floats or prevent scrollbars, not realising it also clips absolutely-positioned children and creates a new stacking context.
seeds.c: For containing floats, use display: flow-root instead. For scroll containment, set overflow: clip (CSS Overflow Level 4) which clips without creating a scroll container. Reserve overflow: hidden for intentional clipping.
seeds.s: dropdown menus or tooltips clipped by an ancestor's overflow:hidden.

seeds.t: Write Mobile-First CSS with min-width Queries
seeds.w: Writing desktop styles first and overriding with max-width queries — overrides compound and must be undone for each breakpoint, and adding a new breakpoint in the middle requires careful surgery.
seeds.c: Write the base styles for the smallest viewport, then progressively enhance with min-width media queries. Fewer overrides, easier to extend, matches how browsers and screen readers parse styles.
seeds.s: media query file growing with reset rules that undo desktop styles for mobile.

seeds.t: Use font-display: swap to Prevent Invisible Text
seeds.w: Loading web fonts without font-display — browsers may show invisible text (FOIT) for up to 3 seconds while the font loads, harming UX and Lighthouse scores.
seeds.c: Add font-display: swap to @font-face declarations so the browser shows the fallback font immediately and swaps to the custom font when it loads. Match the fallback font metrics with size-adjust and ascent-override to minimise layout shift on swap.
seeds.s: text invisible for several seconds on slow connections; CLS score hurt by layout shift on font load.

seeds.t: Use Logical Properties for RTL and Writing Mode Support
seeds.w: Using margin-left, padding-right, border-top etc. for layout spacing — physical properties break in RTL languages and vertical writing modes.
seeds.c: Use CSS logical properties: margin-inline-start instead of margin-left, padding-block-end instead of padding-bottom. They automatically flip for RTL (Arabic, Hebrew) and vertical writing modes. Modern browsers support all logical properties.
seeds.s: layout misaligned or asymmetric when switching the page to an RTL language.

seeds.t: Use clamp() for Fluid Typography and Spacing
seeds.w: Defining multiple font-size breakpoints in separate media queries for responsive type — verbose and creates discrete jumps rather than smooth scaling.
seeds.c: Use clamp(min, preferred, max) for fluid values that scale linearly between breakpoints: font-size: clamp(1rem, 2.5vw, 2rem). Combine with calc() for precise control of the preferred value slope. Also applicable to spacing, padding, and gap.
seeds.s: typography with jarring size jumps at breakpoints instead of smooth scaling.

seeds.t: Browsers Read CSS Selectors Right-to-Left
seeds.w: Writing deeply nested selectors like .page .sidebar .nav ul li a:hover assuming they are evaluated left-to-right — browsers match right-to-left, checking every <a> element on hover against all ancestor conditions.
seeds.c: Keep selectors shallow (2–3 levels max) and prefer class selectors close to the target element. The rightmost selector (the key selector) should be as specific as the target element needs to be. Avoid universal selectors (*) as the key selector in complex chains.
seeds.s: hover or focus state CSS causing frame drops in pages with many DOM elements.

seeds.t: Use Container Queries for Component-Level Responsiveness
seeds.w: Using viewport-based media queries to adapt a component that can appear in different-sized containers — the component responds to window size, not the space it actually has.
seeds.c: Declare container-type: inline-size on the parent and use @container (min-width: 400px) inside the component for styles that respond to the component's available width. Supported in all modern browsers since 2023.
seeds.s: a card component styled for a full-width layout breaking when placed in a narrow sidebar.

seeds.t: Use Cascade Layers to Manage Override Order
seeds.w: Fighting specificity wars between third-party CSS, utility classes, and component styles — without an explicit layer structure, the last rule wins unpredictably.
seeds.c: Use @layer to define explicit specificity layers: @layer reset, base, components, utilities. Rules in later layers always win over earlier layers regardless of selector specificity. Wrap third-party imports in a low-priority layer.
seeds.s: third-party library styles overriding your component styles despite lower specificity.

seeds.t: Use gap in Flex and Grid Instead of Margin for Spacing
seeds.w: Adding margin to flex or grid children for spacing — margin on the first or last child often needs negation or special-casing, and it doesn't respect justify-content spacing.
seeds.c: Use gap (row-gap and column-gap) on the flex or grid container to add gutters between items uniformly without affecting outer edges. gap works in both flex and grid containers in all modern browsers.
seeds.s: needing :last-child { margin: 0 } or negative margins on a flex container to compensate for unwanted spacing.

seeds.t: Data Visualization: Truncated Y-Axis Exaggerating Differences
seeds.w: Starting bar chart y-axis at a value other than zero, making small percentage differences appear dramatic.
seeds.c: Bar charts should start at zero — bars encode length; truncation makes a 2% difference look like 50%. Use line charts (which can start anywhere) for trends. Mark any axis break explicitly.
seeds.s: Bar chart showing Q4 revenue 'up 3%' appears to show a 200% increase due to axis starting at $95M.

seeds.t: Data Visualization: Dual Y-Axis Creating Spurious Correlation
seeds.w: Putting two unrelated metrics on a dual y-axis chart where the apparent correlation is an artifact of scale choices.
seeds.c: Show two metrics as separate panels. If overlap needed, normalize both to the same scale. Dual axes should only be used for metrics with a known direct relationship.
seeds.s: Chart 'shows' ice cream sales and drowning deaths track together; scales were chosen to make them overlap.

seeds.t: Data Visualization: Pie Chart for Comparisons Beyond 2-3 Categories
seeds.w: Using a pie chart with more than 3-4 slices, or to compare similar values — human perception cannot accurately judge arc lengths.
seeds.c: Use a sorted bar chart for proportions with more than 3 categories. Reserve pie charts for when one category clearly dominates. Never use 3D pie charts.
seeds.s: Pie chart with 8 slices; no one can tell which of 18% and 22% is larger.

seeds.t: Data Visualization: Bar Chart Sorted Alphabetically Instead of by Value
seeds.w: Sorting a bar chart alphabetically by category name when the insight is in the ranking order.
seeds.c: Sort bars by value (descending) unless order has intrinsic meaning (time, ordinal ranking). Alphabetical sorting rarely reveals insight.
seeds.s: Country bar chart sorted A-Z; reader can't identify top or bottom performers without scanning entire chart.

seeds.t: Data Visualization: Showing Average Without Distribution
seeds.w: Reporting only the mean without showing the distribution, variance, or percentiles — obscuring bimodal distributions, outliers, and heavy tails.
seeds.c: Show distributions: box plots, violin plots, or histograms. Report mean ± SD or median + IQR. A bimodal distribution with mean in the trough is described misleadingly by its average.
seeds.s: User satisfaction 7.2/10 average; reality is half rate 9-10 and half rate 1-3; entirely different problem from 'lukewarm 7s.'

seeds.t: Data Visualization: Logarithmic Scale Without Labeling
seeds.w: Using a logarithmic y-axis without clearly labeling it, causing readers to misread exponential growth as linear.
seeds.c: Label log axes clearly with 'log scale.' Show gridlines at powers of 10. Consider whether linear scale with a note is clearer for non-technical audiences.
seeds.s: COVID case chart on log scale appears to show slowing growth; readers miss that absolute cases are still doubling.

seeds.t: Data Visualization: Color Palette Inaccessible to Color-Blind Viewers
seeds.w: Using red/green to distinguish categories — 8% of men and 0.5% of women have red-green color blindness.
seeds.c: Use colorblind-safe palettes (Okabe-Ito, viridis, ColorBrewer). Encode redundantly: color + shape or color + label. Test with Coblis or Stark.
seeds.s: Chart distinguishing 'above target' (green) from 'below target' (red) appears monochrome to 8% of male stakeholders.

seeds.t: Data Visualization: Too Many Series on One Chart
seeds.w: Plotting 10+ data series on a single line chart — readers cannot track individual lines; the chart becomes 'spaghetti.'
seeds.c: Use small multiples (same chart per category), highlight one series while graying others, or split into focused sub-charts.
seeds.s: Line chart with 15 countries per year is unreadable; lines overlap with near-similar colors.

seeds.t: Data Visualization: Metric Shown Without Baseline or Comparison
seeds.w: Showing a single number or trend without the context needed to judge if it's good or bad.
seeds.c: Always provide comparison context: a target line, benchmark, same period last year, or control group. A metric without context is an anecdote, not data.
seeds.s: Dashboard shows 23% conversion rate — reader has no idea if that's excellent or catastrophic.

seeds.t: Data Visualization: Decorative Elements That Obscure Data
seeds.w: Adding grid lines, 3D effects, drop shadows, gradients, and decorative backgrounds that add visual noise without information value.
seeds.c: Maximize data-ink ratio: remove every element that doesn't carry information. Remove gridlines, borders, tick marks, and legends replaceable with direct labels.
seeds.s: Elaborate 3D bar chart with gradient fills; the bars are hard to read; decoration draws attention from the insight.

seeds.t: Data Visualization: Choropleth Map Using Raw Counts Instead of Rates
seeds.w: Coloring a geographic map by raw counts — larger areas tend to have more people, making the map reflect population density rather than the phenomenon.
seeds.c: Normalize by population, area, or another relevant denominator for choropleth maps. Raw count maps only work when the raw count itself is the point.
seeds.s: Disease outbreak map shows cities darkest because they have most cases; rural areas appear safest despite higher per-capita rates.

seeds.t: Database Schema: Entity-Attribute-Value Table
seeds.w: Storing arbitrary key-value pairs in a three-column table (entity_id, attribute_name, attribute_value) to allow flexible schemas — queries require multiple self-joins and lose all type safety.
seeds.c: Use JSONB columns for truly dynamic attributes with known query patterns. Use separate typed tables for structured optional attributes. Use a document database if the schema is genuinely schemaless.
seeds.s: Query for users where age > 30 requires multiple self-joins that take minutes; indexes are useless; type conversion errors everywhere.

seeds.t: Database Schema: Missing Index on Foreign Key Columns
seeds.w: Defining a foreign key constraint without creating an index on the referencing column — every JOIN on this relationship causes a full table scan on the child table.
seeds.c: Always index foreign key columns: CREATE INDEX idx_orders_user_id ON orders(user_id); Most databases don't auto-create FK indexes (PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB is an exception).
seeds.s: JOIN query between users and orders takes 10 seconds; EXPLAIN ANALYZE shows sequential scan on orders; adding index drops it to 5ms.

seeds.t: Database Schema: Storing Comma-Separated Values in a Column
seeds.w: Storing a list of values as a comma-separated string in a single column to avoid a junction table — loses all relational power and requires application-side parsing.
seeds.c: Create a junction/association table for many-to-many relationships. Use PostgreSQL ARRAY type if the list is simple and won't be queried individually. Never parse data out of a string in queries.
seeds.s: Querying for all orders with product X requires LIKE '%X%' — no index, slow, and breaks on product names containing commas.

seeds.t: Database Schema: Soft Delete Breaking Unique Constraints
seeds.w: Adding deleted_at TIMESTAMP NULL to every table for soft delete — unique constraints now allow multiple 'deleted' records with the same unique value; queries must always filter for WHERE deleted_at IS NULL.
seeds.c: Use partial unique indexes for soft-delete: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ON users(email) WHERE deleted_at IS NULL. Or use archive tables. Or truly delete and store audit events separately.
seeds.s: User re-registers with old email; gets unique constraint violation from their own deleted account; or second account silently allowed because constraint isn't respected.

seeds.t: Database Schema: Nullable Everything in Schema Design
seeds.w: Making every column nullable because 'we might not always have this data' — the optimizer loses the ability to guarantee non-null, and application code must null-check everywhere.
seeds.c: Start columns as NOT NULL with appropriate defaults or sentinel values. Add NOT NULL constraints from day one; relaxing them later is easier than adding them (requires backfilling).
seeds.s: Query results contain unexpected NULLs; aggregations silently exclude NULL rows; JOIN conditions behave differently with NULLs.

seeds.t: Database Schema: Polymorphic Associations Breaking Foreign Keys
seeds.w: Storing (entity_type VARCHAR, entity_id INT) as a 'belongs-to-any' pattern — you cannot define a foreign key constraint, losing referential integrity and the ability to JOIN efficiently.
seeds.c: Use separate tables for each relationship: comment_on_post(post_id FK, comment_id FK), comment_on_video(video_id FK, comment_id FK). Or use a common parent table with UUID IDs.
seeds.s: Orphaned comments pointing to deleted posts with no cascade; EXPLAIN shows no index usage on polymorphic JOIN; data integrity violations.

seeds.t: Database Schema: Wide Table With Many Nullable Optional Columns
seeds.w: Adding optional attributes directly to the main table as nullable columns instead of related tables — each row becomes very wide; most values are NULL; queries scan many bytes to find few values.
seeds.c: Extract optional attributes to a related table (users_settings, users_preferences). Use JSONB for truly sparse attributes. Keep the core table narrow with the most-queried, non-null columns.
seeds.s: SELECT * on users table is slow because each row is 500 bytes even though 80% is NULL; index scans read pages with mostly wasted space.

seeds.t: Database Schema: ENUM Type That Needs to Grow
seeds.w: Using database ENUM types for status fields that will add new values over time — adding an ENUM value requires an ALTER TABLE that may lock the table, and removed values are permanent.
seeds.c: Use a VARCHAR with a CHECK constraint, or a lookup/reference table with a foreign key. These allow adding values without DDL and allow documenting each status with metadata.
seeds.s: ALTER TABLE orders ADD VALUE 'pending_review' TO status_enum locks a high-traffic table for minutes; can never remove unused values.

seeds.t: Database Schema: Natural Key as Primary Key
seeds.w: Using a natural key (email, SSN, username, phone) as the primary key — natural keys change, can't be guaranteed unique forever, and appear in all foreign key relationships.
seeds.c: Use a surrogate primary key (SERIAL/BIGINT or UUID). Reference the natural key with a separate UNIQUE constraint. Changing a natural key then only requires updating one column instead of all FK references.
seeds.s: User changes email; every table with user_id as FK must be updated in a transaction; multi-table cascade update takes minutes.

seeds.t: Database Schema: Missing CHECK Constraints on Domain Rules
seeds.w: Allowing invalid data into the database because domain rules are only enforced at the application layer — any bug, migration, or direct SQL access can violate invariants.
seeds.c: Add CHECK constraints for domain rules: CHECK (price > 0), CHECK (status IN ('active','inactive')), CHECK (end_date > start_date). These are enforced by the database regardless of how data enters.
seeds.s: Direct SQL update sets price to -50; application doesn't catch it; negative prices appear in invoices and corrupt reports.

seeds.t: Distributed Systems: Non-Idempotent Webhook/Message Handler
seeds.w: Processing a webhook or message queue event in a way that causes side effects (charging, sending email, creating records) if delivered more than once — all distributed systems deliver at-least-once.
seeds.c: Use idempotency keys: check if this event ID was already processed before acting. Store processed IDs in Redis or a DB table. Design operations to be naturally idempotent (upsert vs insert).
seeds.s: Customer charged twice; duplicate order created; email sent multiple times when queue retries delivery.

seeds.t: Distributed Systems: Synchronous Call for Inherently Async Work
seeds.w: Making the caller wait synchronously for work that doesn't need an immediate result — inventory update, email send, report generation — coupling availability and latency.
seeds.c: Publish an event or enqueue a job and return 202 Accepted immediately. The caller gets a job ID to poll status. Use async communication between services that don't need real-time coupling.
seeds.s: API p99 latency is dominated by the slowest downstream service; email service outage causes checkout failures.

seeds.t: Distributed Systems: No Circuit Breaker for External Dependencies
seeds.w: Calling a downstream service or third-party API in a tight loop without a circuit breaker — when it degrades, threads pile up waiting, taking down the caller too.
seeds.c: Implement circuit breakers (Resilience4j, Polly, Hystrix). Set timeouts on ALL outbound calls. Return cached or degraded responses when the circuit is open. Track error rates, not just availability.
seeds.s: Payment processor slowdown causes all web workers to block on HTTP calls; entire application becomes unresponsive.

seeds.t: Distributed Systems: Assuming Clocks Are Synchronized
seeds.w: Using wall clock timestamps from different machines to determine order of events — clocks drift and NTP corrections can cause time to go backward.
seeds.c: Use logical clocks (Lamport timestamps, vector clocks) or distributed timestamps (Hybrid Logical Clocks, TrueTime). For ordering, use a single authoritative sequencer. Never use wall time for happened-before.
seeds.s: Event B appears to happen before event A despite A being submitted first; database replication conflicts from clock skew.

seeds.t: Distributed Systems: Shared Database Between Microservices
seeds.w: Two or more microservices reading and writing the same database tables — they can never be deployed or scaled independently; schema changes require coordinating all teams.
seeds.c: Each service owns its data store and exposes it only via API. Other services hold a copy of needed data (eventual consistency via events) or query through the owning service's API.
seeds.s: Deploying service A requires a database migration that also updates service B's schema; both must deploy together; independent deployability is lost.

seeds.t: Distributed Systems: Thundering Herd After Cache Expiry
seeds.w: A popular cached value expires and hundreds of requests simultaneously hit the database to recompute it, overwhelming the DB.
seeds.c: Use cache locking (only one request rebuilds, others wait or get stale), probabilistic early expiry, or staggered TTLs. Return stale-while-revalidate for read-heavy keys.
seeds.s: Cache miss storm takes down the database; happens at regular intervals matching cache TTL.

seeds.t: Distributed Systems: Dual Write Without Outbox Pattern
seeds.w: Writing to a database AND publishing an event in the same operation without atomicity — if the event publish fails after the DB write, the event is permanently lost.
seeds.c: Use the Outbox Pattern: write the event to an outbox table in the same DB transaction, then a separate process reliably publishes outbox entries to the message broker.
seeds.s: Database updated but downstream services never receive the event; data is inconsistent across the system with no alert.

seeds.t: Distributed Systems: Producer Faster Than Consumer Without Backpressure
seeds.w: Producing messages or requests faster than consumers can process them, with no mechanism to slow the producer — queues grow unboundedly, eventually causing OOM or message loss.
seeds.c: Implement backpressure: block or slow the producer when the queue depth exceeds a threshold. Use bounded queues with rejection policies. Monitor queue depth as a leading indicator.
seeds.s: Memory exhaustion as queue grows; message broker runs out of disk; consumers lag further and further behind.

seeds.t: Distributed Systems: No Dead Letter Queue for Failed Messages
seeds.w: Retrying failed messages indefinitely or discarding them on max retries without a dead letter queue — errors are invisible and messages are silently lost.
seeds.c: Configure a DLQ on every queue. Alert when messages land in the DLQ. Process DLQ messages manually or via automated remediation. Never silently drop messages.
seeds.s: Payment processing errors silently retry until max, then disappear; revenue lost with no alert.

seeds.t: Distributed Systems: N+1 Queries Across Service Boundaries
seeds.w: Fetching a list from service A, then calling service B once per item to enrich each one — O(N) network calls with serialized latency.
seeds.c: Design batch APIs: service B should accept a list of IDs and return results in bulk. Cache aggressively. Use GraphQL or BFF (Backend-for-Frontend) to aggregate in one call.
seeds.s: Listing 100 orders takes 100 sequential API calls to the inventory service; latency = 100 × round-trip time.

seeds.t: Distributed Systems: Using a Database Table as a Message Queue
seeds.w: Polling a database table for new work items (SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED in a loop) at scale — high polling overhead, lock contention, and no backpressure.
seeds.c: Use a dedicated message broker (SQS, RabbitMQ, Kafka) for task queuing. Databases work at small scale but break under load. Transition to outbox pattern for transactional events.
seeds.s: Database CPU spiked by constant polling; lock contention slows transactional writes; 'job queue' table is the database bottleneck.

seeds.t: Distributed Systems: Reading Own Write After Eventual Consistency
seeds.w: Reading data from a replica immediately after writing to the primary, expecting to see the write — replication lag means the read may return stale data.
seeds.c: Use read-your-writes consistency: route reads to primary for the same session/user, or add a monotonic read token. Cache the write result client-side until confirmed replicated.
seeds.s: User updates profile, refreshes, sees old data — appears as a bug but is replication lag.

seeds.t: Send Celery Tasks After DB Transaction Commits
seeds.w: Calling task.delay() inside a Django view or service before the database transaction commits — the Celery worker receives the task ID, queries the database, and finds the record doesn't exist yet because the transaction hasn't committed.
seeds.c: Use transaction.on_commit(lambda: task.delay(obj.id)) to delay task dispatch until the current transaction is committed. Or use django-celery-results with CELERY_TASK_ALWAYS_EAGER=False. This guarantees the worker can find the object when it runs.
seeds.s: Celery task failing with DoesNotExist immediately after creation because the task ran before the transaction that created the object committed.

seeds.t: Use @shared_task for Reusable Django App Tasks
seeds.w: Using @app.task in a reusable Django app where app is imported from the project's celery.py — creates a hard dependency between the reusable app and the specific project's Celery instance, breaking reuse across projects.
seeds.c: Use @shared_task in reusable apps: from celery import shared_task; @shared_task def send_confirmation(user_id): .... Django's INSTALLED_APPS order and the Celery autodiscovery (app.autodiscover_tasks()) picks up shared tasks automatically. The @shared_task proxy binds to whateve…
seeds.s: reusable Django app that cannot be installed in a different project because it imports the project-specific Celery app instance.

seeds.t: Use Django ORM Normally in Celery Tasks With django-celery-results
seeds.w: Calling django.setup() manually inside Celery tasks or forgetting that the worker needs Django configured to use ORM — tasks fail with 'Apps aren't loaded yet' or no database connection.
seeds.c: Configure DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE before Celery imports: in celery.py, call django.setup() and set os.environ.setdefault('DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE', 'myproject.settings'). Celery's Django integration (from celery.contrib.django.celeryd_init import fix_fake_datetime) is handled auto…
seeds.s: 'Apps aren't loaded yet' or 'No module named settings' errors in Celery workers missing Django setup.

seeds.t: Pass Primary Keys to Tasks, Not Django Model Instances
seeds.w: Passing a Django model instance as a task argument — the instance is pickled at dispatch time and deserialised in the worker with potentially stale data; if the default serialiser is JSON, it fails entirely because model instances aren't JSON-serialisable.
seeds.c: Pass the primary key: send_notification.delay(user_id=user.pk). Inside the task, fetch fresh: user = User.objects.get(pk=user_id). This ensures the worker operates on the current database state. Add prefetch_related and select_related in the task fetch for efficiency.
seeds.s: Celery task processing stale model data or failing with JSON serialisation errors because a model instance was passed directly.

seeds.t: Use django-celery-beat for Database-Driven Periodic Tasks
seeds.w: Hardcoding periodic task schedules in celery.py beat_schedule — requires code changes and redeployment to adjust task schedules; no admin interface for operations teams.
seeds.c: Install django-celery-beat and add django_celery_beat to INSTALLED_APPS. Run migrations to create PeriodicTask, IntervalSchedule, and CrontabSchedule models. Schedules are editable via Django Admin without code changes or restarts. Set CELERY_BEAT_SCHEDULER = 'django_celery_beat…
seeds.s: redeploying the application every time marketing wants to change the frequency of a newsletter task.

seeds.t: Use Redis as Both Celery Broker and Result Backend
seeds.w: Using the database result backend (django-celery-results with DB storage) for high-frequency tasks — every task completion writes to the Django database, creating significant write load and growing the results table without bounds.
seeds.c: Use Redis as both broker and result backend: CELERY_BROKER_URL = 'redis://localhost:6379/0'; CELERY_RESULT_BACKEND = 'redis://localhost:6379/0'. Set CELERY_RESULT_EXPIRES to auto-clean old results (default 24h). For tasks where results matter long-term, use the database backend …
seeds.s: django_celery_results table growing to millions of rows and slowing the database because all tasks stored results indefinitely.

seeds.t: Use Soft Time Limits to Gracefully Handle Long-Running Tasks
seeds.w: Using only task_time_limit (hard kill) for long-running Django tasks — SIGKILL doesn't allow database connections to close, Django ORM transactions to roll back, or any cleanup to happen.
seeds.c: Set CELERYD_TASK_SOFT_TIME_LIMIT = 25 and CELERYD_TASK_TIME_LIMIT = 30. Catch SoftTimeLimitExceeded in the task: from billiard.exceptions import SoftTimeLimitExceeded; except SoftTimeLimitExceeded: transaction.rollback(); log_partial_completion(). The soft limit raises an except…
seeds.s: Django database connections left open and transactions uncommitted because SIGKILL prevented ORM cleanup.

seeds.t: Route Tasks to Separate Queues for Django Workloads
seeds.w: Running all Django Celery tasks on one default queue — a burst of slow report generation tasks starves fast email sending tasks.
seeds.c: Define queues with routing: CELERY_TASK_ROUTES = {'myapp.tasks.send_email': {'queue': 'email'}, 'myapp.tasks.generate_report': {'queue': 'reports'}}. Start separate worker pools: celery -A myproject worker -Q email -c 8 and celery -A myproject worker -Q reports -c 2. Assign data…
seeds.s: users waiting 10 minutes for a password reset email because the queue was full of report generation jobs.

seeds.t: Use Celery Chord for Fan-Out Fan-In Django Workflows
seeds.w: Using a coordinating task that polls for subtask completion with time.sleep loops — wastes a worker for polling, is fragile if any subtask fails, and creates artificial delays.
seeds.c: Use Celery Canvas chord: from celery import chord; chord([process_chunk.s(chunk) for chunk in chunks])(aggregate_results.s()). The aggregate_results callback fires automatically when all process_chunk tasks complete. Celery handles the synchronisation. Use chord_error callback f…
seeds.s: a coordinating Django task sleeping in a loop waiting for worker tasks to finish instead of using chord.

seeds.t: Enable CELERY_TASK_ALWAYS_EAGER for Django Unit Tests
seeds.w: Running tests that exercise Celery tasks requiring a running broker and worker — slow, complex CI setup, and tests depend on external infrastructure.
seeds.c: Override settings in tests: from django.test import override_settings; @override_settings(CELERY_TASK_ALWAYS_EAGER=True, CELERY_TASK_EAGER_PROPAGATES=True). Tasks execute synchronously inline; exceptions propagate to the test. For integration tests that need true async behaviour…
seeds.s: slow tests requiring a Redis broker and Celery worker process for every test run when ALWAYS_EAGER would test the task logic synchronously.

seeds.t: Guard Against Duplicate Execution With Django Model Flags
seeds.w: Writing Django Celery tasks that modify database state without checking if they already ran — Celery's at-least-once delivery and automatic retries mean tasks may run more than once.
seeds.c: Use a database flag or unique constraint as the idempotency guard: check a processed boolean field or a completed_at timestamp at the start of the task; return early if already processed. Use select_for_update() with nowait=True to prevent concurrent execution of the same task: …
seeds.s: order processed twice when the task failed after processing but before marking as done, then was retried.

seeds.t: Use Django Signals Carefully With Celery Task Dispatch
seeds.w: Dispatching Celery tasks in Django post_save signals without wrapping in transaction.on_commit — signals fire inside the transaction, so the task may run before the save is committed.
seeds.c: In signal handlers, always dispatch via on_commit: transaction.on_commit(lambda: process_new_user.delay(instance.pk)). Be aware that post_save signals fire inside the transaction while the save is atomic. Use post_save sparingly for Celery dispatch — prefer explicit task dispatc…
seeds.s: Celery task receiving a DoesNotExist error because the post_save signal dispatched the task before the enclosing transaction finished.

seeds.t: Index ArrayField With GIN for Fast Containment Queries
seeds.w: Using ArrayField without an index and querying with __contains or __overlap — PostgreSQL performs a full table scan for every array containment check.
seeds.c: Add a GIN index on the ArrayField in the migration: from django.contrib.postgres.indexes import GinIndex; indexes = [GinIndex(fields=['tags'])]. Then __contains=[tag] and __overlap=[t1, t2] use the index efficiently. GIN indexes support @>, &&, and @< operators which Django's OR…
seeds.s: array containment queries slow on large tables despite the column being 'indexed' with a standard B-tree (which doesn't help for array operators).

seeds.t: Use PostgreSQL JSON Operators Through Django's JSONField
seeds.w: Fetching all objects and filtering JSON content in Python — bypasses PostgreSQL's native JSON operators and kills performance.
seeds.c: Django's JSONField (backed by PostgreSQL jsonb) supports ORM lookups: filter(data__status='active'), filter(data__tags__contains=['python']), filter(data__score__gte=80). Use __icontains for case-insensitive text within JSON. Add a GIN index: GinIndex(fields=['data']) for jsonb …
seeds.s: loading thousands of rows to filter a JSONB field that PostgreSQL's @> operator could filter in the index.

seeds.t: Use SearchVector and SearchQuery for Full-Text Search
seeds.w: Using __icontains for full-text search — does not rank results, doesn't understand word stems or language, and can't use full-text indexes.
seeds.c: Use django.contrib.postgres.search: annotate with SearchVector then filter with SearchQuery: qs.annotate(search=SearchVector('title', 'body')).filter(search=SearchQuery('django best practices')). Store a pre-computed tsvector column with SearchVectorField and a GIN index for pro…
seeds.s: icontains search missing 'running' when query is 'run', or results unordered by relevance.

seeds.t: Order Full-Text Results by SearchRank
seeds.w: Applying SearchVector/SearchQuery filtering but returning results in database insertion order — users get no relevance ranking.
seeds.c: Annotate with both SearchVector and SearchRank then order by rank: from django.contrib.postgres.search import SearchRank; qs = Post.objects.annotate(rank=SearchRank(vector, query)).filter(rank__gte=0.1).order_by('-rank'). Use weights (A–D) on SearchVector fields to boost title m…
seeds.s: full-text search results shown in arbitrary order with no relevance weighting.

seeds.t: Use TrigramSimilarity for Fuzzy Search and Typo Tolerance
seeds.w: Using __icontains for search and getting no results for 'Djangoo' or 'Posgtres' — exact substring match has no typo tolerance.
seeds.c: Enable pg_trgm extension in a migration (CreateExtension('pg_trgm')) then use TrigramSimilarity: from django.contrib.postgres.search import TrigramSimilarity; qs.annotate(similarity=TrigramSimilarity('name', query)).filter(similarity__gt=0.3).order_by('-similarity'). Add a GIN o…
seeds.s: search returning nothing for common typos or partial product names.

seeds.t: Use PostgreSQL Aggregate Functions for In-Query Grouping
seeds.w: Fetching related objects in Python to build comma-separated lists or arrays — causes N+1 and is slower than a single aggregating query.
seeds.c: Use django.contrib.postgres.aggregates: Post.objects.annotate(tag_list=ArrayAgg('tags__name'), author_names=StringAgg('authors__name', delimiter=', ')). These generate PostgreSQL's array_agg() and string_agg() functions, returning Python lists and strings directly. Use filter= p…
seeds.s: Python list comprehension joining related names that StringAgg could produce in one query.

seeds.t: Use UniqueConstraint With condition for Partial Unique Indexes
seeds.w: Adding a plain unique_together or UniqueConstraint to enforce uniqueness only over a subset of rows — a plain unique index covers all rows including ones where uniqueness shouldn't apply.
seeds.c: Use UniqueConstraint with a condition: UniqueConstraint(fields=['user', 'slug'], condition=Q(deleted_at__isnull=True), name='unique_active_user_slug'). PostgreSQL creates a partial index that only enforces uniqueness where deleted_at IS NULL, allowing multiple soft-deleted dupli…
seeds.s: unique constraint violations when trying to re-use a slug for a record that was previously soft-deleted.

seeds.t: Use SKIP LOCKED for Concurrent Task Queue Patterns
seeds.w: Using select_for_update() for a task queue where multiple workers compete for the next available task — workers block each other waiting for the lock to release, serialising what should be parallel processing.
seeds.c: Use select_for_update(skip_locked=True) so workers skip rows locked by other workers and take the next available one: Task.objects.select_for_update(skip_locked=True).filter(status='pending').first(). This requires a transaction. Ideal for building database-backed queues with Dj…
seeds.s: multiple workers all blocking on the same queue row instead of each picking a different available task.

seeds.t: Use Range Fields for Interval and Period Data
seeds.w: Storing a time period as two separate DateField columns (start_date, end_date) and writing complex Q object filters for overlap detection — error-prone and doesn't use PostgreSQL's native range operators.
seeds.c: Use IntegerRangeField, DateRangeField, or DateTimeRangeField from django.contrib.postgres.fields. Query with __contains, __overlap, __contained_by, __fully_lt. PostgreSQL's && (overlap) and @> (contains) operators are much more expressive. Add GiST index for range queries.
seeds.s: complex three-condition Q expression to detect date range overlaps that PostgreSQL's range overlap operator handles natively.

seeds.t: Install PostgreSQL Extensions in Migrations
seeds.w: Manually running CREATE EXTENSION in the database and not including it in migrations — other environments and CI databases won't have the extension, causing import errors or missing functions.
seeds.c: Add a migration using CreateExtension from django.contrib.postgres.operations: operations = [CreateExtension('pg_trgm'), CreateExtension('unaccent')]. This is idempotent — it uses CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS. Run it in a migration before any migration that uses the extension.
seeds.s: TrigramSimilarity or unaccent working locally but failing in CI or production because the extension wasn't installed.

seeds.t: Use unaccent Extension for Accent-Insensitive Search
seeds.w: Normalising text to ASCII in Python before storing, losing diacritic information — users searching 'cafe' don't find 'café' and vice versa.
seeds.c: Install unaccent extension via migration, then use the unaccent database function: from django.contrib.postgres.search import TrigramSimilarity; or apply it in a custom lookup. Use SearchVector with config='english_unaccent' if you configure a custom text search config. Or use .…
seeds.s: search for 'Muller' failing to find 'Müller' or vice versa.

seeds.t: Choose Between GIN and GiST Indexes for PostgreSQL Fields
seeds.w: Using a default B-tree index for full-text search vectors, JSONB containment, or array fields — B-tree doesn't support these operators at all.
seeds.c: GIN (Generalised Inverted): best for static or infrequently updated data; faster queries, slower writes. Use for JSONB containment (@>), full-text search (tsvector), and array operations. GiST (Generalised Search Tree): better for frequently updated data; slower queries, faster …
seeds.s: full-text or JSONB query not using the index even though one was created, because the wrong index type was used.

seeds.t: Use PostgreSQL Advisory Locks for Distributed Mutual Exclusion
seeds.w: Using Django's cache-based locking (cache.add) for distributed locks — cache backends (Memcached, Redis) provide no transaction guarantees and locks can be lost on crash.
seeds.c: Use PostgreSQL advisory locks via Django's connection: from django.db import connection; with connection.cursor() as cursor: cursor.execute('SELECT pg_try_advisory_lock(%s)', [lock_id]). Advisory locks are released automatically when the session ends or on pg_advisory_unlock. Us…
seeds.s: distributed lock acquired via cache not being released when a worker crashes, blocking other workers indefinitely.

seeds.t: Use HStoreField for Flat Key-Value Metadata
seeds.w: Using JSONField for simple flat key-value metadata where all values are strings — JSONField's flexibility is unnecessary overhead for flat string maps.
seeds.c: Use HStoreField from django.contrib.postgres.fields for string-to-string key-value pairs (like product attributes or user preferences). Supports ORM lookups: filter(attributes__color='red'), filter(attributes__has_key='size'), filter(attributes__keys__contains=['color', 'size'])…
seeds.s: JSONB column with only string values where HStore's simpler operators and equal semantics would suffice.

seeds.t: Use update() With Annotations to Avoid Row-by-Row Python Loops
seeds.w: Fetching objects, computing a value in Python, and saving each back individually — N round trips for N rows.
seeds.c: Push the computation into PostgreSQL using F() expressions, annotations with database functions, and bulk update(): from django.db.models.functions import Upper; MyModel.objects.update(slug=Lower('title')). Use Case/When expressions for conditional updates. For complex cross-tab…
seeds.s: Python loop calling obj.save() for each of 10,000 rows when a single .update() with a database expression would suffice.

seeds.t: Use select_related and prefetch_related to Avoid N+1
seeds.w: Iterating over a queryset and accessing a related object inside the loop (for post in Post.objects.all(): print(post.author.name)) — each iteration fires a new SQL query.
seeds.c: Use select_related for ForeignKey/OneToOne (SQL JOIN): Post.objects.select_related('author'). Use prefetch_related for ManyToMany and reverse ForeignKey (separate query + Python join): Post.objects.prefetch_related('tags'). Use django-debug-toolbar to see query counts.
seeds.s: 100 posts causing 101 SQL queries visible in debug toolbar.

seeds.t: Querysets Are Lazy — They Hit the DB at Evaluation
seeds.w: Assuming queryset = MyModel.objects.filter(active=True) immediately queries the database — it does not.
seeds.c: Querysets are evaluated (and the SQL runs) when you: iterate, slice with a step, call len(), bool(), list(), or repr(). Chain .filter(), .exclude(), .annotate() freely without DB cost; the query is built up and sent once. Cache the result in a variable if you need to iterate mul…
seeds.s: unexpected duplicate queries from iterating the same queryset twice.

seeds.t: Use bulk_create and bulk_update for Batch Operations
seeds.w: Calling Model.objects.create() or instance.save() inside a loop — one INSERT or UPDATE per iteration, O(n) round trips.
seeds.c: Accumulate objects and call Model.objects.bulk_create(objects, batch_size=1000) for inserts, and Model.objects.bulk_update(objects, fields=['status'], batch_size=1000) for updates. Note: bulk_create does not call save() or fire post_save signals.
seeds.s: importing 10,000 records takes minutes due to individual INSERT statements.

seeds.t: Use F() Expressions for Atomic Field Updates
seeds.w: Reading a field value then writing back an increment (obj.views += 1; obj.save()) — two requests can read the same value, both increment, and the second write overwrites the first.
seeds.c: Use F() expressions to perform the update in a single SQL statement: MyModel.objects.filter(pk=pk).update(views=F('views') + 1). This is atomic at the database level. Also use F() in annotations and filters to compare field values without loading them into Python.
seeds.s: counter values not incrementing correctly under concurrent load.

seeds.t: Avoid Business Logic in Signals
seeds.w: Putting critical business logic (sending emails, charging payments, updating caches) in post_save signals — signals are hard to test, fire from bulk_update bypassing them, and create invisible action-at-a-distance.
seeds.c: Keep signals for infrastructure concerns (invalidating caches, updating search indexes). Put business logic in service functions, model methods, or explicit calls in views. If you must use signals, make them idempotent and test them directly.
seeds.s: bulk_create silently skipping post_save logic, or tests missing side effects because signals aren't connected.

seeds.t: Split Settings Into Base, Dev, and Production Files
seeds.w: Using a single settings.py with if DEBUG: branches — secrets end up in version control, and differences between environments are hard to audit.
seeds.c: Use a settings/ package with base.py, development.py, and production.py that import from base. Use environment variables (python-decouple or django-environ) for secrets; never hardcode credentials. Point to the right settings with DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE.
seeds.s: DEBUG=True accidentally deployed to production, or database credentials committed to git.

seeds.t: Make Migrations Safe for Zero-Downtime Deploys
seeds.w: Adding a NOT NULL column with no default in a migration and deploying while the old code is still running — the migration locks the table and old code fails to INSERT without the new column.
seeds.c: Add columns in two phases: first add as nullable (or with a database default), deploy the new code, then backfill and add the NOT NULL constraint in a follow-up migration. Use django-pg-zero-downtime-migrations for PostgreSQL-safe operations.
seeds.s: table locks or app errors during deployments when adding columns to high-traffic tables.

seeds.t: Use Custom Managers and Querysets for Reusable Filters
seeds.w: Repeating MyModel.objects.filter(active=True, deleted_at__isnull=True) throughout the codebase — any change to the active definition must be updated everywhere.
seeds.c: Define a custom QuerySet with named methods (def active(self): return self.filter(active=True, deleted_at__isnull=True)) and expose it via a custom Manager. Chain custom methods with standard ORM methods. Use as_manager() for the simplest case.
seeds.s: the same filter chain repeated in 20 views, breaking when the active definition changes.

seeds.t: Use select_for_update Inside Transactions for Locking
seeds.w: Reading an object, checking a condition, then updating it without a lock — two concurrent requests both pass the check and both update, causing double-processing (e.g., double-charging).
seeds.c: Use MyModel.objects.select_for_update().get(pk=pk) inside an atomic() block to acquire a row-level lock; concurrent requests wait until the first transaction commits. Use select_for_update(nowait=True) or of=('self',) for advanced cases.
seeds.s: idempotent operations processing twice under concurrent load.

seeds.t: Push Aggregation to the Database With annotate()
seeds.w: Fetching all related objects into Python and computing counts or sums in a loop — moves aggregation work from the database to the application server.
seeds.c: Use annotate() with Count, Sum, Avg etc. to compute aggregations in SQL: Author.objects.annotate(post_count=Count('posts')). Use aggregate() for a single value over the whole queryset. Prefer DB-level grouping to fetching thousands of rows.
seeds.s: view loading all child records just to display a count next to each parent.

seeds.t: Use get_object_or_404 to Handle Missing Objects Correctly
seeds.w: Using Model.objects.get(pk=pk) in a view without catching DoesNotExist — uncaught exception returns a 500 instead of a 404.
seeds.c: Use get_object_or_404(Model, pk=pk) which raises Http404 on missing objects, returning a proper 404 response. For custom querysets, pass the queryset as the first argument: get_object_or_404(Model.objects.filter(active=True), pk=pk).
seeds.s: users seeing a 500 error page when navigating to a deleted or non-existent object URL.

seeds.t: Always Use form.cleaned_data After is_valid()
seeds.w: Accessing form.data['field'] directly after form validation — this bypasses type coercion and sanitisation, returning raw strings instead of validated Python types.
seeds.c: After form.is_valid(), use form.cleaned_data['field'] which returns the validated and coerced value (e.g., a date object for DateField, an integer for IntegerField). Never use request.POST data directly when a form is available.
seeds.s: type errors or XSS vulnerabilities from using raw POST data instead of cleaned form values.

seeds.t: Cache at the Right Granularity
seeds.w: Caching entire page views with @cache_page when only one slow section exists, or not caching at all — either stale pages for all users or repeated expensive queries.
seeds.c: Use cache.get/set for fine-grained caching of expensive computations (cache_key = f'user_stats:{user_id}'); use template fragment caching {% cache 300 sidebar request.user.id %} for partial page caching; use @cache_page only for public pages with no user-specific content.
seeds.s: logged-in users seeing other users' data from a per-view cache, or cache invalidation invalidating too broadly.

seeds.t: Use only() and defer() to Load Fewer Columns
seeds.w: Fetching all columns from a wide table when only two fields are needed — SELECT * transfers unnecessary data and inflates memory usage.
seeds.c: Use .only('id', 'name') to fetch only specified fields, or .defer('large_text_field') to exclude heavy fields. Accessing a deferred field triggers an extra query per object. For read-only data, use .values() or .values_list() which return dicts/tuples without model instantiation…
seeds.s: queryset hitting a table with 30 columns but only displaying 2, causing unnecessary data transfer.

seeds.t: Use TestCase Not TransactionTestCase Unless You Need It
seeds.w: Using TransactionTestCase for all tests because it seems more 'real' — it is 10–100x slower than TestCase because it truncates tables after each test instead of rolling back.
seeds.c: Use django.test.TestCase (which wraps each test in a transaction and rolls back) for the vast majority of tests. Use TransactionTestCase only when testing transaction-specific behaviour (ON COMMIT hooks, LISTEN/NOTIFY).
seeds.s: test suite taking 5+ minutes when it could run in 30 seconds with proper TestCase usage.

seeds.t: Django Middleware Order Is Significant
seeds.w: Adding middleware in arbitrary order — SecurityMiddleware must be first, SessionMiddleware before AuthenticationMiddleware, and CORS middleware before anything that reads the request.
seeds.c: Follow Django's recommended middleware order in MIDDLEWARE: SecurityMiddleware first, then SessionMiddleware, CommonMiddleware, CsrfViewMiddleware, AuthenticationMiddleware, MessageMiddleware, XFrameOptionsMiddleware. Middleware processes requests top-to-bottom and responses bot…
seeds.s: CSRF errors, missing session data, or CORS headers absent from responses.

seeds.t: Django ORM Is Synchronous — Use sync_to_async in Async Views
seeds.w: Calling ORM queries directly inside an async view — Django's ORM is synchronous and calling it from an async context raises SynchronousOnlyOperation.
seeds.c: Wrap ORM calls with sync_to_async: from asgiref.sync import sync_to_async; users = await sync_to_async(list)(User.objects.filter(active=True)). Or use Django 4.1+ async ORM methods where available: await MyModel.objects.filter(...).acount().
seeds.s: SynchronousOnlyOperation error in async views, or deadlocks under async load.

seeds.t: Always Define __str__ on Models
seeds.w: Omitting __str__ from model definitions — the Django admin, shell, and error messages display 'MyModel object (1)' instead of meaningful identifiers.
seeds.c: Define __str__ to return a human-readable string: def __str__(self): return self.title or self.email or f'{self.first_name} {self.last_name}'. This affects admin list views, inline representations, and select dropdowns.
seeds.s: Django admin showing 'Post object (42)' in dropdowns and list views.

seeds.t: Set related_name to Avoid Reverse Accessor Clashes
seeds.w: Defining two ForeignKey fields pointing to the same model (or using abstract models with ForeignKeys) without setting related_name — Django raises a system check error for clashing reverse accessors.
seeds.c: Set related_name='%(class)s_set' or a specific name on ForeignKey fields that may clash: author = models.ForeignKey(User, on_delete=models.CASCADE, related_name='authored_posts'). In abstract models, use related_name='%(app_label)s_%(class)s_set'.
seeds.s: 'ERRORS: reverse accessor clashes' system check preventing startup.

seeds.t: Use values_list(flat=True) for a List of Single Values
seeds.w: Fetching full model instances or tuples just to extract a list of IDs: [obj.id for obj in MyModel.objects.all()] — instantiates unnecessary model objects.
seeds.c: Use MyModel.objects.values_list('id', flat=True) for a QuerySet of scalar values, or values_list('id', 'name') for tuples. This skips model instantiation entirely and is much faster for large result sets. Use list() or set() to force evaluation.
seeds.s: collecting a list of PKs for an __in filter by loading full model instances.

seeds.t: Order Dockerfile instructions from least to most frequently changed
seeds.w: COPY . . before RUN npm install — any source file change invalidates the install layer, re-downloading all dependencies.
seeds.c: COPY package*.json ./ then RUN npm install then COPY . . This way dependency installation is only re-run when package.json changes, not on every code change.
seeds.s: builds re-install all dependencies on every code change, dramatically slowing the build.

seeds.t: Never put secrets in ENV or ARG — they appear in image history
seeds.w: ENV API_KEY=mysecret or ARG SECRET=value in a Dockerfile — both are visible in docker image history and any image layer, even if later deleted.
seeds.c: pass secrets at runtime via docker run -e API_KEY=$API_KEY or Docker Secrets (Swarm) or bind-mount a secrets file. For build-time secrets, use BuildKit's --secret flag: RUN --mount=type=secret,id=mykey cat /run/secrets/mykey.
seeds.s: secrets exposed in CI logs, image registries, or to anyone with docker image inspect access.

seeds.t: Run containers as non-root for security
seeds.w: running application processes as root inside a container — if the container is compromised, root access inside can be exploited.
seeds.c: create a non-root user in the Dockerfile: RUN addgroup -S app && adduser -S app -G app then USER app before CMD. Many base images (node, python) already provide a non-root user. Check with: docker run myimage whoami.
seeds.s: container runs as root, security scan flagged, or deployment blocked by policy.

seeds.t: Use exec form for CMD and ENTRYPOINT, not shell form
seeds.w: CMD npm start (shell form) — runs as /bin/sh -c 'npm start', so npm is not PID 1 and does not receive signals properly.
seeds.c: CMD ['node', 'server.js'] (exec form, JSON array) — the process runs as PID 1 and receives SIGTERM for graceful shutdown. Verify with docker stop — if it takes 10 seconds, the process is not receiving the signal.
seeds.s: container takes 10s to stop (hitting kill timeout), or graceful shutdown code never runs.

seeds.t: Use multi-stage builds to keep production images small
seeds.w: building and shipping a single image with build tools, compilers, and dev dependencies included.
seeds.c: use multi-stage builds — FROM node:20 AS builder for the build stage, then FROM node:20-alpine AS production and COPY --from=builder only the built artifacts. Final images can be 10x smaller. Key: only install production dependencies in the final stage.
seeds.s: image hundreds of MB larger than necessary, slow pulls, larger attack surface.

seeds.t: Container localhost is not the host's localhost
seeds.w: connecting to localhost:5432 from inside a container to reach a database on the host — localhost inside a container refers to the container itself, not the host.
seeds.c: use host.docker.internal (Docker Desktop) or the host's IP address. For services in the same docker-compose file, use the service name: postgres://db:5432. For external services: docker run --network host (Linux only) or host.docker.internal.
seeds.s: connection refused when container tries to reach host services.

seeds.t: Volume mount permissions must match container user
seeds.w: mounting a host directory into a container running as a non-root user without matching the UID — the container process cannot write to the mounted volume.
seeds.c: ensure the host directory is owned by the same UID as the container user (often 1000), or use named volumes which Docker manages. Check with: docker exec container id. Fix ownership: chown -R 1000:1000 ./data or set user: '1000:1000' in docker-compose.yml.
seeds.s: permission denied when container writes to mounted volume.

seeds.t: Pin base image versions for reproducible builds
seeds.w: FROM node:latest or FROM node:20 — these tags can change, causing builds to use different base images over time and introducing subtle breaking changes.
seeds.c: pin to a specific digest or minor version: FROM node:20.11.1-alpine3.19 or FROM node@sha256:... for maximum reproducibility. Update base images deliberately and test the update.
seeds.s: build worked yesterday, fails today with no code changes, or subtle runtime differences between environments.

seeds.t: depends_on doesn't wait for app readiness — use condition
seeds.w: depends_on: [db] in docker-compose — this only waits for the container to start, not for the database to be ready to accept connections.
seeds.c: use depends_on with condition: service_healthy and add a HEALTHCHECK to the dependency service. Alternatively, add retry logic in the application startup or use a wait-for-it.sh script.
seeds.s: application crashes on startup with 'connection refused' because it starts before the database is ready.

seeds.t: Separate .env files by environment in docker-compose
seeds.w: hardcoding environment variables in docker-compose.yml and committing them — secrets end up in version control.
seeds.c: use the env_file key to load variables from a file (env_file: .env.local) and add .env.local to .gitignore. Provide a .env.example with placeholder values committed to the repo. docker-compose also automatically reads a .env file in the project root for variable substitution in …
seeds.s: database passwords committed to git, or different environments share the same credentials.

seeds.t: Use BuildKit cache mounts to persist package manager caches
seeds.w: re-downloading npm or pip packages from the internet on every build even though they rarely change.
seeds.c: use BuildKit's RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.npm npm ci to persist the package manager cache between builds. Enable BuildKit with DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 or set buildkit: true in daemon.json. This keeps the cache local regardless of whether package.json changed, dramatically sp…
seeds.s: builds download hundreds of packages from scratch even on minor code changes.

seeds.t: Set restart policy so containers recover from crashes
seeds.w: running containers with no restart policy — if the process crashes, the container stays down until manually restarted.
seeds.c: use --restart unless-stopped (docker run) or restart: unless-stopped in docker-compose for services that should always run. Use restart: on-failure:5 to limit retries for transient failures. Avoid restart: always in development — it can mask crash loops.
seeds.s: container crashes and stays down until an operator notices and restarts it manually.

seeds.t: Use --init or tini to handle zombie processes as PID 1
seeds.w: running a shell script as PID 1 that does not reap zombie child processes — over time zombie processes accumulate and can exhaust the process table.
seeds.c: use docker run --init to inject tini as PID 1, or add ENTRYPOINT ["/sbin/tini", "--"] in your Dockerfile after installing tini. tini forwards signals correctly and reaps orphaned children. Critical for Node.js and Python apps that spawn child processes.
seeds.s: container accumulates zombie processes, or SIGTERM is not forwarded to the application.

seeds.t: Set memory and CPU limits to prevent resource starvation
seeds.w: running containers without memory or CPU limits — a single runaway container can starve other containers or the host OS.
seeds.c: set deploy.resources.limits.memory and cpus in docker-compose, or use --memory 512m --cpus 1.0 with docker run. Set both limits and reservations: limits prevent starvation, reservations help the scheduler. Monitor with docker stats.
seeds.s: one container causes OOM kills on other services or makes the host unresponsive.

seeds.t: Use docker-compose.override.yml for local development settings
seeds.w: adding development-only settings (volume mounts, debug ports, hot-reload) directly to docker-compose.yml — they get deployed to production.
seeds.c: put production-safe config in docker-compose.yml and local overrides in docker-compose.override.yml. Docker Compose automatically merges the override file in development. For CI or production, use docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.prod.yml to exclude develop…
seeds.s: production containers mount source code or expose debug ports unintentionally.

seeds.t: Use tmpfs mounts for ephemeral high-speed storage
seeds.w: writing temporary files or test databases to a bind mount or volume — disk I/O can be the bottleneck for write-heavy workloads like test suites.
seeds.c: use --tmpfs /tmp or tmpfs: in docker-compose for paths that need fast, ephemeral storage. tmpfs lives in RAM, is wiped on container stop, and is much faster than any disk-backed volume. Great for SQLite test databases, temp uploads, or large in-memory caches.
seeds.s: test suite runs much slower in Docker than locally due to bind mount I/O overhead.

seeds.t: Build multi-platform images with docker buildx
seeds.w: building an image on an ARM Mac and pushing it to a registry — other platforms (linux/amd64) pull an incompatible image and fail with 'exec format error'.
seeds.c: use docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 -t image:tag --push to build multi-platform images in one command. Ensure your base image supports all target platforms. Create a builder with driver=docker-container: docker buildx create --use.
seeds.s: container fails to start on CI or production with 'exec format error' because it was built on a different architecture.

seeds.t: Configure log drivers to avoid unbounded log growth
seeds.w: using the default json-file log driver with no size limits — container logs grow indefinitely and can fill the host disk.
seeds.c: configure log rotation in daemon.json: {"log-driver": "json-file", "log-opts": {"max-size": "10m", "max-file": "3"}}. For production, forward logs to a centralised system using the syslog, fluentd, or awslogs driver so logs survive container restarts.
seeds.s: disk fills up overnight due to verbose application logs with no rotation in place.

seeds.t: A Nash Equilibrium Is Stable, Not Necessarily Optimal — The Prisoner's Dilemma Is the Canonical Example
seeds.w: Assuming Nash equilibrium = best outcome — NE means no unilateral deviation improves payoff (stability), not that the outcome is Pareto optimal. In prisoner's dilemma, the NE (Defect, Defect) is strictly worse for both than (Cooperate, Cooperate).
seeds.c: NE: no player benefits from unilateral deviation. Not necessarily Pareto optimal. Prisoner's dilemma NE = (D,D) payoff (1,1); cooperative (C,C) = (3,3) — stable ≠ efficient. Mechanism design closes this gap.
seeds.s: a mechanism that achieves a NE but produces poor social outcomes because the NE is a prisoner's dilemma; an auction algorithm that finds a NE in bids but where all agents would prefer a cooperative outcome.

seeds.t: A Dominant Strategy Is Optimal Regardless of Others' Play; Nash Equilibrium Is Optimal Given Others' Play
seeds.w: Treating dominant strategy equilibrium and Nash equilibrium as equivalent — dominant strategy is optimal for ALL opponent profiles (very strong); NE is optimal only given the specific equilibrium strategies of others (weaker).
seeds.c: Dominant strategy: best regardless of others' play → always a NE. NE: best given others' equilibrium play → not necessarily dominant. DSIC > BIC in robustness: DSIC needs no beliefs about others' types.
seeds.s: designing a mechanism and claiming it is 'incentive compatible' without specifying whether this is dominant-strategy or Bayes-Nash IC — these have very different robustness properties; an agent simulation where agents play a NE but would deviate if the equilibrium shifted, unlik…

seeds.t: In a Vickrey (Second-Price) Auction Bidding True Value Is a Dominant Strategy — Not So in First-Price
seeds.w: Assuming truthful bidding is rational in all auctions — it is a dominant strategy only in second-price (Vickrey) auctions. In first-price auctions, optimal bids are shaded below true value by a factor depending on n and the value distribution.
seeds.c: Second-price (Vickrey): bid = true value vᵢ is dominant strategy (paying 2nd-highest bid). First-price: optimal bid = vᵢ(n−1)/n under symmetric uniform IPV (shade down). Revenue equivalence: both formats yield equal expected seller revenue under IPV.
seeds.s: a first-price auction platform assuming truthful bids for value estimation; a second-price auction implementation that charges the winner their own bid (a common implementation bug).

seeds.t: VCG Maximises Social Welfare but Generally Does Not Maximise Revenue — They Are Different Objectives
seeds.w: Expecting VCG to maximise revenue — VCG maximises social welfare (Σ vᵢ) and achieves DSIC, but can yield zero revenue. Revenue-optimal mechanisms (Myerson) sacrifice efficiency by withholding allocation when virtual values are negative.
seeds.c: VCG: socially efficient (max Σvᵢ), DSIC, but revenue can be low. Myerson: max expected revenue via virtual values ψᵢ(v) = v − (1−F(v))/f(v), allocate only when ψᵢ > 0. Efficiency and revenue are generally in tension.
seeds.s: deploying VCG for an ad auction expecting maximum revenue; computing 'optimal mechanism' and getting VCG back (they are not the same unless valuations are already symmetric and efficient).

seeds.t: The Revelation Principle Says Every Mechanism Has a Truthful Equivalent — Not That Truth-Telling Is Always Optimal
seeds.w: Using the revelation principle to justify agents reporting true types in any mechanism — it says every mechanism has a truthful equivalent, not that a given mechanism induces truth-telling or that direct mechanisms are always preferable.
seeds.c: Revelation principle: every BNE of any mechanism M has a DSIC/BIC direct-mechanism equivalent. Reduces design space to IC direct mechanisms analytically. Does NOT mean agents truth-tell in practice, nor that direct > indirect mechanisms.
seeds.s: a mechanism design algorithm that assumes agents report true types without verifying that the mechanism is incentive-compatible; a multi-agent simulation that invokes the revelation principle to justify setting agent strategies to truth-telling.

seeds.t: In Common-Value Auctions the Winner Likely Overpaid — Rational Bids Must Be Shaded for the Conditioning Effect
seeds.w: Bidding the naive estimate E[V|xᵢ] in a common-value auction — the winner's curse means winning is informative that your signal was an overestimate. Rational bid = E[V | xᵢ, you win], which is strictly less than E[V|xᵢ].
seeds.c: Rational bid in common-value auction: E[V | signal=xᵢ, xᵢ = max]. Shade below raw estimate; shading increases with number of bidders. Winner's curse: winning reveals upward bias in your signal. Critical in oil-tract, spectrum, and M&A auctions.
seeds.s: an automated bidding agent that uses its raw valuation signal without correcting for the information content of winning; an M&A valuation model that does not adjust for competitive bidding dynamics.

seeds.t: Arrow's Impossibility Theorem: No Rank-Order Voting Rule Satisfies All Three Fairness Conditions
seeds.w: Designing a rank-order voting system expecting to satisfy Pareto efficiency, IIA, and non-dictatorship simultaneously — Arrow's theorem proves this is impossible for ≥ 3 alternatives. All rank-order rules must violate at least one condition.
seeds.c: Arrow impossibility: no social welfare function on ≥ 3 alternatives satisfies Pareto + IIA + non-dictatorship. Plurality violates IIA; Borda violates IIA. Escapes: restrict domain, use cardinal utility, or allow randomisation.
seeds.s: a recommendation aggregation algorithm that claims to satisfy IIA and Pareto simultaneously with a non-dictatorial rule over 3+ item types; a rank-aggregation system that adds a new option and gets unexpected reversals (IIA violation).

seeds.t: Gibbard-Satterthwaite: No Non-Dictatorial Deterministic Voting Rule with ≥ 3 Outcomes Is Strategy-Proof
seeds.w: Building a deterministic, non-dictatorial, onto social choice function over ≥ 3 outcomes and expecting strategy-proofness — Gibbard-Satterthwaite proves this is impossible. All such rules are manipulable.
seeds.c: GS theorem: deterministic + onto + SP + ≥3 outcomes ⟹ dictatorial. All standard voting rules (plurality, Borda, IRV) are strategically manipulable. Escapes: randomisation, domain restriction (single-peaked → median voter), or approximate SP.
seeds.s: a platform voting/ranking system claimed to be manipulation-proof that is deterministic and supports ≥ 3 outcomes; a peer-review scoring system where reviewers have incentives to strategically misreport scores.

seeds.t: Adverse Selection Is Pre-Contractual Hidden Information; Moral Hazard Is Post-Contractual Hidden Action
seeds.w: Using 'adverse selection' and 'moral hazard' interchangeably — adverse selection is pre-contractual hidden information (who participates); moral hazard is post-contractual hidden action (how they behave). Different problems, different fixes.
seeds.c: Adverse selection: hidden type before contracting → screening/signalling (Akerlof lemons). Moral hazard: hidden action after contracting → incentive alignment, monitoring, deductibles (principal-agent). Timing and information type differ.
seeds.s: applying a monitoring solution (moral hazard fix) to an adverse selection problem; designing a deductible (moral hazard fix) when the core issue is that high-risk users are self-selecting into the platform.

seeds.t: Price Elasticity of Demand Is a Percentage Ratio — Not the Slope of the Demand Curve
seeds.w: Using slope ΔQ/ΔP as price elasticity — slope is unit-dependent and constant on a linear curve; elasticity = (∂Q/∂P)(P/Q) is unit-free and varies along any demand curve, even a linear one.
seeds.c: ε = (∂Q/∂P)(P/Q) = %ΔQ/%ΔP. For linear Q = a−bP: slope = −b (constant) but ε = −bP/(a−bP) (varies). Revenue maximised at |ε| = 1. Elastic: |ε| > 1, lower P raises revenue. Inelastic: |ε| < 1, higher P raises revenue.
seeds.s: a pricing optimiser that uses a constant slope parameter and produces the same 'elasticity' across all price levels; a demand model that predicts revenue-maximising price as the linear midpoint (correct for slope-based reasoning, wrong for elasticity-based reasoning).

seeds.t: Deadweight Loss Arises from Any Market Distortion — Not Only Taxes
seeds.w: Computing deadweight loss only for taxes — DWL arises from any distortion preventing mutually beneficial trades: monopoly (P > MC), price floors/ceilings, externalities, and information asymmetry all generate DWL.
seeds.c: DWL = foregone surplus from trades blocked by the distortion. Triangle area = ½ × distortion × ΔQ. Sources: tax, monopoly markup, price controls, externalities. DWL ≈ ½|ε|(distortion)²Q/P — grows with elasticity and distortion squared.
seeds.s: a welfare calculator that subtracts only the tax rectangle from total surplus and misses the DWL triangle; a policy analysis model that computes government tax revenue without the corresponding DWL.

seeds.t: Pareto Efficiency Makes No Statement About Fairness — A Highly Unequal Allocation Can Be Pareto Optimal
seeds.w: Treating Pareto efficiency as a complete welfare criterion — a Pareto optimal allocation can be extremely unequal. Pareto efficiency says only that no waste exists; it makes no statement about fairness or distribution.
seeds.c: Pareto optimal: no feasible improvement making everyone weakly better and someone strictly better. Compatible with extreme inequality. Separate from equity: Kaldor-Hicks adds compensation principle. Second welfare theorem: redistribution + markets can achieve any Pareto optimum.
seeds.s: a market algorithm that reports an outcome as 'optimal' because it is Pareto efficient, without acknowledging distributional or equity concerns; a welfare function that maximises Pareto efficiency without any equity weighting.

seeds.t: The First Welfare Theorem Requires Perfect Competition, No Externalities, and Complete Markets
seeds.w: Applying the first welfare theorem without checking its conditions — perfect competition, no externalities, complete markets are all required. Monopoly, pollution, and missing markets each independently violate it.
seeds.c: First WT: competitive equilibrium is Pareto optimal IFF price-taking + no externalities + complete markets. When any condition fails, markets overproduce (neg. externality), underproduce (pub. goods), or misallocate (market power).
seeds.s: a marketplace algorithm assuming user welfare is maximised by any transaction that both parties agree to (ignores externalities and market power); a platform fee structure that creates effective market power for the platform, violating the price-taker assumption.

seeds.t: Sunk Costs Are Irrelevant to Forward-Looking Decisions — Only Marginal Costs and Benefits Matter
seeds.w: Factoring in irrecoverable past costs when making forward-looking decisions — sunk costs are irrelevant to any decision. Only expected future costs and benefits (marginal analysis) determine the optimal choice.
seeds.c: Rational rule: continue iff E[future marginal benefit] > E[future marginal cost]. Sunk costs are irretrievable regardless of decision — they drop out of the analysis. Escalation of commitment is the cognitive bias of over-weighting sunk costs.
seeds.s: a project continuation recommendation justified by 'we've already spent $X' without a forward-looking marginal analysis; a stop-loss algorithm that fails to exit a position because the entry price was high.

seeds.t: Comparative Advantage Drives Trade — Even If One Party Has Absolute Advantage in Everything
seeds.w: Concluding that the party with absolute advantage in all goods gains nothing from trade — comparative advantage (lower opportunity cost, not lower absolute cost) determines the gains from trade and specialisation.
seeds.c: Comparative advantage: produce the good with lower opportunity cost. Even if A is absolutely better at everything, if B's opp. cost of Y is lower, A should specialise in X and trade. Gains from trade exist whenever opportunity costs differ.
seeds.s: a task assignment algorithm that gives all tasks to the most capable agent (violates comparative advantage; leaves gains from trade unexploited); a trade analysis model that computes who should export what based on absolute productivity rather than opportunity costs.

seeds.t: A Credible Signal Must Be Costly Enough That Only the High Type Finds It Worthwhile
seeds.w: Treating any observable action as a credible signal — cheap talk (costless claims) is not credible with misaligned interests. A credible signal requires cost asymmetry (single-crossing): only high types find the signal cost < benefit.
seeds.c: Spence signalling: separating equilibrium exists iff single-crossing holds — c_H(e) < c_L(e). High type signals e*, low type opts out. Signal credibility requires the signal to be genuinely costly enough to deter mimicry.
seeds.s: a reputation system that allows self-certification without any costly verification process; a credential system where the signal cost is the same for high and low types (no single-crossing, signal not credible).

seeds.t: Net Present Value Requires Discounting Future Cash Flows — Nominal Comparisons Across Time Are Meaningless
seeds.w: Comparing nominal (undiscounted) cash flows across time — $1 today ≠ $1 in 10 years. NPV = Σ Cₜ/(1+r)ᵗ; discount rate r reflects opportunity cost of capital + risk. Mixing nominal/real flows with real/nominal rates produces wrong NPV.
seeds.c: NPV = Σ Cₜ/(1+r)ᵗ; invest when NPV > 0. Use consistent real/nominal framing: real cash flows + real rate, OR nominal flows + nominal rate. IRR = r making NPV = 0; multiple IRRs possible with sign-changing cash flows.
seeds.s: a capital budgeting model that adds up undiscounted future cash flows and compares to initial investment; an NPV calculation that uses nominal cash flows with a real discount rate (or vice versa), producing inflation-distorted results.

seeds.t: The Coase Theorem Holds Only Under Zero Transaction Costs and Well-Defined Property Rights
seeds.w: Applying the Coase theorem to argue private bargaining solves externalities in general — the theorem requires zero transaction costs and well-defined property rights. Both conditions fail for most large-scale externalities (pollution, public goods).
seeds.c: Coase theorem: zero transaction costs + well-defined property rights → bargaining achieves efficiency regardless of initial rights assignment. With positive transaction costs or unclear rights, Pigouvian taxes/regulation are necessary.
seeds.s: a platform policy design that assumes users will negotiate efficient norms among themselves without considering transaction costs and information asymmetries; a policy recommendation against carbon taxes based on the Coase theorem without checking the scale of bargaining costs.

seeds.t: Use filter Context for Non-Scoring Queries
seeds.w: Putting all conditions inside a must clause — must scores every document even for binary yes/no conditions like status=active, wasting CPU on relevance calculation.
seeds.c: Put exact-match, range, and exists conditions inside a filter clause within a bool query; they skip scoring and are automatically cached. Reserve must for full-text search clauses where relevance scoring matters. Use should for boost conditions and minimum_should_match for OR lo…
seeds.s: slow queries with high CPU because every status=active filter is computing and caching relevance scores.

seeds.t: Map String Fields as keyword for Exact Match, text for Full-Text
seeds.w: Mapping all string fields as text (the default) — text fields are analysed (tokenised and lowercased), making exact match, sorting, and aggregations fail or produce wrong results.
