seeds.c: Use keyword for fields you filter exactly, sort, or aggregate on (status, country_code, user_id). Use text for fields you search with full-text queries (title, description). Dual-map with fields: {raw: {type: keyword}} when you need both. Keyword fields cannot be full-text searc…
seeds.s: terms aggregation on a text field returning individual tokens instead of whole values, or sort on a text field failing.

seeds.t: Use search_after for Deep Pagination, Not from+size
seeds.w: Using from: 10000, size: 10 to page deep into results — Elasticsearch fetches and discards the first 10,000 documents from every shard, becoming progressively slower and hitting the index.max_result_window limit (default 10,000).
seeds.c: Use search_after with a sort value from the last document of the previous page: sort: [{_score: 'desc'}, {_id: 'asc'}], search_after: [lastScore, lastId]. For stable snapshot pagination, use a Point in Time (PIT) with search_after. For export, use the Scroll API.
seeds.s: paginated search timing out or hitting max_result_window error beyond page 1000.

seeds.t: Use the Bulk API for High-Throughput Indexing
seeds.w: Sending one document per index request in a loop — each request is a full HTTP round trip with overhead, making bulk indexing orders of magnitude slower than it should be.
seeds.c: Use the Bulk API to batch 500–5000 documents per request: alternating action metadata lines and document source lines in NDJSON format. Monitor rejected_requests in the response for backpressure signals. Use the bulk processor abstraction in client libraries for automatic batchi…
seeds.s: indexing 100,000 documents taking 30 minutes that should take under a minute with bulk indexing.

seeds.t: Use Index Aliases for Zero-Downtime Reindexing
seeds.w: Pointing your application directly at an index name — when you need to reindex (mapping change, analyser update), you must take downtime or do a dangerous in-place change.
seeds.c: Always write to and read from an alias (e.g. products) that points to the real index (products_v1). To reindex: create products_v2 with the new mapping, reindex from v1 to v2, atomically swap the alias: POST _aliases with remove v1 and add v2 in one operation. Zero downtime.
seeds.s: hours of downtime during reindexing because the app pointed directly at the index name.

seeds.t: Aim for 10–50 GB Per Shard to Avoid Over-Sharding
seeds.w: Creating an index with 50 shards for a 1 GB dataset, or creating one with 1 shard for a 500 GB dataset — too many shards (over-sharding) wastes heap and slows searches; too few prevents horizontal scaling.
seeds.c: Target 10–50 GB per shard. For a 100 GB dataset with 5 nodes: 3–5 primary shards with 1 replica each. Start small (1–2 shards) and use Shrink API or rollover to adjust. Use _cat/shards to audit shard sizes.
seeds.s: cluster heap pressure from thousands of small shards, or a single shard that can't fit on one node.

seeds.t: Use nested Type for Arrays of Objects With Cross-Field Queries
seeds.w: Mapping an array of objects with the default object type and querying across fields within one object — Elasticsearch flattens object arrays, mixing values across objects so cross-field conditions match incorrectly.
seeds.c: Use type: nested for arrays where you need to query multiple fields of the same array element together. Query with nested: { path: 'comments', query: { bool: { must: [{match: {'comments.author': 'Alice'}}, {match: {'comments.text': 'great'}}] } } }. Nested documents are stored a…
seeds.s: query matching a document because field A matches one array element and field B matches a different one.

seeds.t: Increase refresh_interval During Bulk Indexing
seeds.w: Bulk indexing with the default 1-second refresh interval — Elasticsearch creates a new segment on every refresh, producing thousands of tiny segments that must be merged, slowing indexing significantly.
seeds.c: Before bulk indexing, set index.refresh_interval: -1 (disable auto-refresh) and index.number_of_replicas: 0 to maximise indexing throughput. After indexing completes, restore both settings and call POST /index/_refresh. For ongoing indexing, 30s–60s intervals balance freshness w…
seeds.s: bulk indexing 3x slower than expected because segment creation and merging overhead dominates.

seeds.t: Structure bool Queries With must/should/filter/must_not
seeds.w: Nesting multiple match queries at the top level or using only match for all conditions — match queries always score, and multiple top-level queries don't combine logically.
seeds.c: Use bool as the top-level query and place conditions in the appropriate clause: must (required, scored), filter (required, not scored, cached), should (optional, boosts score), must_not (exclusion, not scored). Combine multiple conditions in must or filter as arrays. Set minimum…
seeds.s: search returning irrelevant results because conditions that should be required are in should, or poor performance from unfiltered scorable queries.

seeds.t: Disable Dynamic Mapping to Prevent Mapping Explosion
seeds.w: Allowing dynamic mapping on indices that receive user-generated JSON — each new field creates a mapping entry; thousands of unique keys (e.g. user-defined form fields) bloat the cluster state and can crash it.
seeds.c: Set dynamic: false to silently ignore unmapped fields (they're stored but not indexed), or dynamic: strict to reject documents with unmapped fields. Explicitly map all fields you need to query. Use dynamic: true only for indices where field growth is bounded and controlled.
seeds.s: cluster state bloating with 100,000+ field mappings from user-generated keys, causing master node instability.

seeds.t: Cap Terms Aggregations on High-Cardinality Fields
seeds.w: Running a terms aggregation on a field with millions of unique values (user_id, session_id, UUID) — Elasticsearch must sort and return the top-N from every shard, consuming significant heap.
seeds.c: Use terms aggregations on fields with bounded cardinality (< ~10,000 unique values). For counting unique values use cardinality aggregation (approximate HyperLogLog). For high-cardinality grouping, use composite aggregation with pagination. Avoid terms aggregations on text field…
seeds.s: search node heap pressure or circuit breaker trips from a terms aggregation on a UUID field.

seeds.t: Use ILM to Automatically Manage Time-Series Indices
seeds.w: Manually managing log or time-series indices by periodically deleting old ones — error-prone, no gradual rollover, and requires cron jobs or scripts.
seeds.c: Configure an ILM policy with hot/warm/cold/delete phases. Attach it to an index template so new indices automatically follow the lifecycle. In the hot phase: rollover when the index reaches 50 GB or 30 days. In delete phase: delete indices older than 90 days. Use Kibana's Index …
seeds.s: log indices growing unbounded, or manual deletion scripts failing and leaving stale data.

seeds.t: Use _explain to Debug Unexpected Relevance Scores
seeds.w: Guessing why a document is ranked higher or lower than expected and adjusting queries blindly.
seeds.c: Use GET /index/_explain/doc_id with your query body to get a detailed breakdown of the score computation: BM25 term frequency, inverse document frequency, and field-length norm for each matching term. Use match_phrase for phrase queries, function_score for custom boosting by rec…
seeds.s: search results in an unexpected order with no visibility into why a less-relevant document ranks higher.

seeds.t: Choose the Right multi_match type for Cross-Field Search
seeds.w: Using default multi_match (best_fields) when searching across title and body where a document mentioning terms in different fields should rank higher.
seeds.c: best_fields (default): score from the best single matching field — good when one field should dominate. most_fields: sum scores across all matching fields — good for boosting documents that match in multiple fields. cross_fields: treats all fields as one big field — good for ent…
seeds.s: poor ranking because the wrong multi_match type doesn't match the content structure.

seeds.t: Use _source Filtering to Reduce Response Size
seeds.w: Fetching full documents when only a few fields are needed — large _source fields (embedded HTML, long text) inflate network transfer and parsing overhead.
seeds.c: Use _source: ['title', 'price', 'category'] in the request to include only needed fields, or _source: { excludes: ['large_blob_field'] } to exclude specific large fields. Use stored_fields for fields you always want separately from _source. Use docvalue_fields to retrieve value …
seeds.s: search response 10x larger than needed because full documents are returned when only IDs and titles were displayed.

seeds.t: Understand Cluster Health: Green, Yellow, Red
seeds.w: Ignoring a yellow cluster health status in production, or panicking at yellow thinking data is lost.
seeds.c: Green: all primary and replica shards are assigned. Yellow: all primary shards are assigned but some replicas are unassigned (data is safe, but reduced redundancy). Red: at least one primary shard is unassigned (data for that shard is unavailable). Yellow is expected on a single…
seeds.s: misinterpreting yellow as data loss, or ignoring red thinking it's a cosmetic issue.

seeds.t: Enable fielddata Only When Absolutely Necessary
seeds.w: Enabling fielddata: true on a text field for sorting or aggregations — fielddata loads the entire uninverted index into heap, consuming gigabytes for large text fields and triggering circuit breakers.
seeds.c: For sorting and aggregations, use keyword fields instead of enabling fielddata on text fields. If you need both full-text search and aggregation on the same field, use a multi-field mapping: { type: text, fields: { raw: { type: keyword } } }. Only enable fielddata for text field…
seeds.s: OutOfMemoryError or circuit breaker trips after enabling fielddata for a terms aggregation on a text field.

seeds.t: Configure highlight to Show Matching Snippets
seeds.w: Returning full document text and highlighting matches client-side with string search — expensive in the browser and doesn't respect query analysis.
seeds.c: Use the highlight object in the query: highlight: { fields: { body: { fragment_size: 150, number_of_fragments: 3 } } }. Elasticsearch returns <em>-wrapped snippets that match the query terms, respecting the same analyser used at index time. Use type: unified for the best highlig…
seeds.s: displaying full document bodies in search results instead of relevant snippets, or highlights not matching analysed query terms.

seeds.t: Use Composable Index Templates for Consistent Index Settings
seeds.w: Manually configuring settings and mappings for each new index — inconsistent shard counts, analysers, and field types across indices in the same data tier.
seeds.c: Define composable index templates (PUT _index_template/logs) with index_patterns: ['logs-*'] that automatically apply settings and mappings to matching new indices. Use component templates for reusable mapping fragments (timestamps, geo, metadata). Attach an ILM policy in the te…
seeds.s: new log indices created with default settings (1 shard, no ILM) because no template was configured.

seeds.t: Use the Reindex API to Change Mappings on Existing Indices
seeds.w: Trying to change an existing field's type with PUT /index/_mapping — Elasticsearch does not allow changing field mappings after index creation (except for some additions).
seeds.c: Create a new index with the updated mapping, then reindex: POST _reindex { source: { index: 'old_index' }, dest: { index: 'new_index' } }. For large indices, reindex with slicing for parallelism and use the wait_for_completion=false parameter. Update aliases to point to the new …
seeds.s: 'mapper_parsing_exception: cannot change type of field' error when trying to update an existing field mapping.

seeds.t: Use multiple function clauses instead of if/else branches
seeds.w: a single function body with nested if/case/cond for all input shapes — hard to read and extend.
seeds.c: write multiple function clauses with pattern matching in the head: def greet(%User{role: :admin}), do: 'Hello, admin'; def greet(%User{name: name}), do: 'Hello, #{name}'. Elixir tries clauses top to bottom; the first matching head wins. Put more specific patterns before general …
seeds.s: function with deeply nested if/else that multiple clauses would express more readably and extensibly.

seeds.t: Variables are immutable — rebinding creates a new binding, not mutation
seeds.w: assuming x = x + 1 mutates x the way it would in an OOP language — in Elixir this creates a new binding for x in the current scope.
seeds.c: understand that all data structures are immutable; operations return new values. To 'update' a map: user = %{user | name: 'Alice'}. In processes, state is passed as function arguments in recursive tail calls, not stored as mutable fields.
seeds.s: trying to 'update' a value inside a passed-in data structure and being confused when the original is unchanged.

seeds.t: Pipe operator passes the result as the first argument
seeds.w: nesting function calls: Enum.map(Enum.filter(users, &(&1.active)), &(&1.name)) — hard to read the data flow.
seeds.c: use the pipe operator: users |> Enum.filter(& &1.active) |> Enum.map(& &1.name). Each function in a pipe receives the previous result as its first argument. Design your own functions with the primary data argument first to make them pipe-friendly. Avoid mixing piped and non-pipe…
seeds.s: deeply nested function calls where a pipe chain would read as a clear left-to-right data transformation.

seeds.t: GenServer callbacks must return quickly — offload slow work
seeds.w: performing blocking I/O or long computations inside handle_call or handle_cast — the GenServer process is single-threaded; blocking it prevents other messages from being processed.
seeds.c: for slow work, spawn a Task: Task.async(fn -> do_slow_work() end) inside handle_cast and handle the result in handle_info. Or use handle_continue to break setup into non-blocking steps. handle_call should only be used when the caller must wait for the response; prefer handle_cas…
seeds.s: GenServer becomes unresponsive under load because a handle_call blocks for 2 seconds on a database query.

seeds.t: Choose the right child restart strategy for your supervisor
seeds.w: using :permanent restart for workers that should not be restarted (e.g. a one-off migration task), or :temporary for critical services that must stay alive.
seeds.c: :permanent (default) — always restart; :temporary — never restart; :transient — restart only on abnormal exit. Choose supervisor strategy: :one_for_one restarts only the failed child; :one_for_all restarts all siblings (use when children are interdependent); :rest_for_one restar…
seeds.s: a one-time startup task restarts in a loop after completing because it was declared :permanent.

seeds.t: Use with for sequential pattern matching with early exit on failure
seeds.w: nesting case expressions for multiple operations that can each fail — deep nesting with the same failure handling repeated.
seeds.c: with {:ok, user} <- Users.get(id), {:ok, token} <- Auth.generate(user), do: {:ok, token}, else: {:error, reason} -> {:error, reason}. If any pattern fails to match, the else clause handles it. The happy path flows left to right without nesting. Each step in with can have a diffe…
seeds.s: three nested case {:ok, _} <- ... expressions that with would collapse into a flat chain.

seeds.t: Changesets separate data validation from persistence concerns
seeds.w: validating data in the controller before passing it to Ecto, or calling Repo.insert directly without a changeset.
seeds.c: define a changeset function on the schema: def changeset(user, attrs) { user |> cast(attrs, [:name, :email]) |> validate_required([:name, :email]) |> validate_format(:email, ~r/@/) |> unique_constraint(:email) }. Changesets accumulate errors without raising; inspect with changes…
seeds.s: validation mixed into controller actions making it impossible to reuse for background jobs or seeds.

seeds.t: Preload associations explicitly — Ecto never lazy-loads
seeds.w: accessing user.posts after fetching a User without preloading — Ecto returns %Ecto.Association.NotLoaded{}, not nil.
seeds.c: preload in the query: Repo.get(User, id) |> Repo.preload(:posts), or inline: from u in User, preload: [:posts], select: u. For nested associations: preload: [posts: :comments]. Ecto's explicit preloading prevents N+1 queries by design — there is no lazy loading. Use Repo.preload…
seeds.s: %Ecto.Association.NotLoaded{} appears at runtime because the association wasn't preloaded before access.

seeds.t: Never create atoms dynamically from user input — the atom table is finite
seeds.w: String.to_atom(user_input) — atoms are never garbage collected; the atom table is limited (default 1,048,576). An attacker sending unique strings can exhaust atoms and crash the BEAM VM.
seeds.c: use String.to_existing_atom/1 when you know the atom must already exist, or keep data as strings and use Map.get with string keys. For JSON, use Jason with keys: :strings option. Only create atoms from known, bounded sets.
seeds.s: BEAM VM crash with 'system_limit' after processing many unique strings converted to atoms.

seeds.t: Use Task.async and Task.await for parallel work with bounded wait
seeds.w: sequential Enum.map(items, &slow_fetch/1) — processes items one at a time.
seeds.c: Task.async_stream(items, &slow_fetch/1, max_concurrency: 10, timeout: 5000) for controlled parallelism with back-pressure. For a fixed set of parallel tasks: tasks = Enum.map(ids, &Task.async(fn -> fetch(&1) end)); results = Enum.map(tasks, &Task.await(&1, 5000)). Task.await has…
seeds.s: fetching 100 items sequentially where Task.async_stream would reduce wall-clock time by 10x.

seeds.t: Use Registry for named process lookup instead of atoms
seeds.w: registering processes with a dynamic atom (e.g. {:via, :global, :'user_#{id}'}) — creates unbounded atoms.
seeds.c: use Registry: {:ok, _} = Registry.start_link(keys: :unique, name: MyRegistry). Register: {:via, Registry, {MyRegistry, user_id}}. Look up: Registry.lookup(MyRegistry, user_id). Registry handles concurrent registration, duplicate detection, and process monitoring. For partitioned…
seeds.s: dynamic process naming using atom interpolation in a long-running app slowly fills the atom table.

seeds.t: Phoenix Contexts are the boundaries between domain areas
seeds.w: calling Repo directly from controllers and views, or importing Ecto schemas across domain boundaries — couples the web layer to the database.
seeds.c: group related schemas and functions into a context module: defmodule MyApp.Accounts do; def get_user!(id) ...; def create_user(attrs) ... end. Controllers call context functions only. Contexts enforce that Accounts doesn't call Billing internals directly. Phoenix generators crea…
seeds.s: controller calling Repo.get, Repo.insert, and Changeset functions directly — bypassing the domain boundary that contexts provide.

seeds.t: Only update changed assigns to minimise LiveView diffs
seeds.w: calling assign(socket, all_data: fetch_everything()) on every event — sends the entire data structure to the client as a diff even when only one field changed.
seeds.c: use assign(socket, :specific_field, new_value) to update only what changed. Use stream for large lists — streams track changes with stream_insert and stream_delete without diffing the whole list. Use assign_async for async data loading. LiveView's diff algorithm compares assigns…
seeds.s: LiveView sends large diffs on every keypress because the whole socket assigns are rebuilt each event.

seeds.t: Stream creates lazy enumerables that avoid intermediate list allocation
seeds.w: Enum.map |> Enum.filter |> Enum.take — each step allocates a full intermediate list.
seeds.c: Stream.map |> Stream.filter |> Enum.take(10) — computation is lazy; only enough elements to produce 10 results are processed. Use Stream for large collections, file reading (File.stream!), and infinite sequences (Stream.iterate, Stream.cycle). Enum materialises results; Stream.r…
seeds.s: processing a 1M-row file loads the entire file into memory as a list when File.stream! and Stream would process it lazily.

seeds.t: Use @enforce_keys to require fields at struct creation time
seeds.w: defining a struct with defstruct [:name, :email] and accessing name without it being set — name defaults to nil, causing silent bugs downstream.
seeds.c: use @enforce_keys [:name] before defstruct to make those fields required at creation: %User{name: 'Alice'} succeeds; %User{} raises ArgumentError at compile time. Keep enforce_keys for truly required fields. Combine with typespecs for documentation.
seeds.s: struct field assumed to be present is nil at runtime because it was optional and not provided at creation.

seeds.t: Guards only support a limited set of expressions — use pattern matching for the rest
seeds.w: calling a custom function in a guard: def valid?(x) when my_validator(x) — only BIF (built-in functions) and a limited set of operators are allowed in guards.
seeds.c: allowed guard expressions: comparison operators, type checks (is_integer, is_list), arithmetic, boolean operators, elem, hd, tl, map_size, byte_size, length (with caveats), and a few others. For complex conditions, add a guard for the type check and move business logic into the …
seeds.s: 'invalid expression in guard' compiler error when calling a custom function in a when clause.

seeds.t: Macros are hygienic by default — use var! to escape hygiene
seeds.w: expecting a variable defined inside a macro to be accessible in the caller's scope — Elixir macros are hygienic and variables are scoped to the macro.
seeds.c: variables defined inside a macro do not leak into the caller. To intentionally inject a variable into the caller's context, use var!(x) inside the macro. Prefer functions over macros whenever possible — macros make code harder to understand and debug. Use macros only when you ge…
seeds.s: variable defined in a macro is undefined in the caller even though it 'should be' in scope.

seeds.t: Use Ecto.Multi for composable multi-step database transactions
seeds.w: wrapping multiple Repo operations in a plain transaction and handling rollback manually — the transaction block becomes large and difficult to test.
seeds.c: use Ecto.Multi: Multi.new() |> Multi.insert(:user, user_changeset) |> Multi.insert(:account, fn %{user: u} -> Account.changeset(u) end) |> Repo.transaction(). On failure, Multi returns {:error, failed_step, changeset, changes_so_far}. Steps can depend on previous results. Multi.…
seeds.s: large transaction block with many Repo calls where Multi would provide named steps, error identification, and composability.

seeds.t: Emit Telemetry events for observability instead of custom metrics
seeds.w: writing custom metrics emission code directly in business logic — duplicates work and doesn't integrate with the Elixir observability ecosystem.
seeds.c: use :telemetry.execute([:myapp, :order, :created], %{count: 1}, %{user_id: user.id}). Attach handlers with :telemetry.attach. Libraries like Phoenix, Ecto, and Oban emit Telemetry events natively. Use TelemetryMetrics + a reporter (Prometheus, StatsD) to aggregate. Keep event na…
seeds.s: custom metrics code mixed into business logic instead of Telemetry events that ecosystem tools can consume.

seeds.t: Elixir strings are UTF-8 binaries — byte and character counts may differ
seeds.w: using byte_size(str) to count characters — for multi-byte UTF-8 characters, byte_size returns the byte count, not the grapheme count.
seeds.c: use String.length(str) for grapheme count, byte_size for byte length. String.at(str, n) returns the nth grapheme; binary access with :binary.at returns the nth byte. Use String.graphemes for iteration. For performance on large binaries where character boundaries don't matter, bi…
seeds.s: truncating a username at 20 'characters' cuts mid-emoji because byte_size was used instead of String.length.

seeds.t: Evolutionary Optimisation Climbs to Nearby Peaks, Not the Global Maximum
seeds.w: Treating any convergence in an evolutionary or optimisation process as success, without verifying you are at the global rather than a local optimum.
seeds.c: In Wright's fitness landscape, selection climbs toward the nearest peak, not the highest. Local optima are stable against small perturbations — the system needs a large stochastic jump (genetic drift, mutation, perturbation, or simulated annealing) to escape. Rugged landscapes h…
seeds.s: genetic algorithm or gradient-based search converges to different solutions across runs; solution quality varies with initialisation; plateau reached early with no further improvement; hyperparameter search repeatedly returns similar suboptimal configurations.

seeds.t: Evolutionary Stable Strategies Are Stable Against Invasion, Not Necessarily Optimal
seeds.w: Designing a system toward an "optimal" strategy, expecting it to remain optimal once competitors adapt.
seeds.c: An evolutionarily stable strategy (ESS) is a strategy that cannot be invaded by any alternative once established — stable against perturbation, not necessarily optimal in isolation. ESS is a refinement of Nash equilibrium: every ESS is a Nash equilibrium, but not vice versa. Sub…
seeds.s: technically superior solution fails to displace an established one; "obviously better" protocol is not adopted; optimal algorithm loses to a good-enough incumbent with network effects; performance lead evaporates as competitors adapt.

seeds.t: In Small Populations, Random Drift Dominates Selection Below Threshold 1/(2N)
seeds.w: Attributing every observed difference between variants, cohorts, or model versions to selection (genuine performance difference), even in small populations or samples.
seeds.c: Kimura's neutral theory: selection dominates drift only when the selection coefficient satisfies |s| >> 1/(2N), where N is the effective population size. Below this threshold, allele frequencies wander randomly regardless of fitness. In A/B testing: with small n, observed differ…
seeds.s: A/B test results with small n show large differences that vanish in production; model A "beats" model B inconsistently across evaluation runs; benchmark differences disappear when evaluation is randomised; reported improvements fail to replicate.

seeds.t: In Coevolving Systems, Improvement Maintains Relative Position — Running to Stand Still
seeds.w: Measuring success in coevolving systems against an absolute performance standard, then being surprised when improvements are immediately countered.
seeds.c: Red Queen effect: in systems with mutual adaptation (security, competition, evolutionary arms races), fitness improvements by one party are matched by adaptations in others. Absolute fitness increases are countered; only relative position is maintained. This is not failure — it …
seeds.s: security hardening is bypassed as quickly as deployed; competitive feature lead evaporates when competitors update; constant investment required just to maintain position; "we're always just barely ahead no matter how much we improve."

seeds.t: Complex Adaptive Systems Show Long Stasis Interrupted by Rapid Discontinuous Change
seeds.w: Expecting continuous incremental improvement in complex adaptive systems, and planning roadmaps based on smooth extrapolation of current progress.
seeds.c: Gould and Eldredge's punctuated equilibrium: complex adaptive systems show long periods of stasis (the current solution is a local ESS — stable against small perturbations) interrupted by rapid bursts of change when the landscape shifts and the old equilibrium becomes unstable. …
seeds.s: legacy system stable for years despite accumulating technical debt, then suddenly requires complete rewrite; incremental improvement plateaus and only complete redesign helps; architectural change resisted for years happens in weeks when conditions shift.

seeds.t: Evolution Selects for Mutational Robustness, Not Peak Fitness — Flat Optima Beat Sharp Peaks
seeds.w: Optimising for the highest-scoring solution without considering the fitness landscape around it.
seeds.c: Quasispecies theory (Eigen): in the presence of mutation, selection acts on the entire cloud of related variants around a peak, not just the peak itself. A high but narrow peak (sharp optimum) surrounded by low-fitness variants is selected against in favour of a lower but broade…
seeds.s: model with lower training loss performs worse on held-out data; increasing batch size improves training speed but degrades generalisation; model performs well in-distribution but fails under distribution shift; removing regularisation improves training metrics but hurts producti…

seeds.t: Cooperation Evolves Only When Relatedness × Benefit Exceeds Cost — Hamilton's Rule
seeds.w: Designing modules or services to cooperate closely without accounting for the coordination cost relative to the benefit.
seeds.c: Hamilton's rule: altruism (cooperation) evolves when r × b > c, where r = genetic relatedness (shared information / shared context), b = benefit to the recipient, c = cost to the actor. Tightly coupled modules (high r) can afford expensive cooperation; loosely coupled modules (l…
seeds.s: microservice coordination overhead eliminates the performance benefit of splitting; distributed transactions cost more than the original monolith; services designed to cooperate closely end up tightly coupled despite architectural intention.

seeds.t: Independently Valid Changes Can Be Incompatible When Combined — Dobzhansky-Muller Effect
seeds.w: Testing components only in isolation and concluding integration will succeed if all individual tests pass.
seeds.c: Dobzhansky-Muller incompatibility: two mutations that each evolve successfully in separate lineages can be incompatible when combined, because fitness is not additive — it depends on genetic background (epistasis). Each change is viable in its own context but the combination is …
seeds.s: all unit and integration tests pass individually but deployment fails; feature flags that each work enable a failure when both active; library upgrades that each pass their own tests cause failures when deployed together; "everything was fine in isolation."

seeds.t: Without Recombination, Defect Accumulation Is Irreversible — Muller's Ratchet
seeds.w: Relying on incremental improvement alone to remove accumulated defects from a codebase without periodic structural reorganisation.
seeds.c: Muller's ratchet: in asexual populations (no recombination), the least-mutated class of individuals is occasionally lost by random drift and cannot be recovered — fitness irreversibly declines one step at a time. Recombination (sexual reproduction) allows restoration of low-muta…
seeds.s: each release introduces more problems than it fixes despite active engineering; incremental improvement does not reverse accumulated technical debt; codebase quality slowly trends downward over time; "we keep fixing bugs but the backlog never shrinks."

seeds.t: Above a Critical Mutation Rate, Genetic Information Is Lost Faster Than Selection Maintains It
seeds.w: Assuming that higher mutation rate (or learning rate, or temperature, or exploration parameter) always improves adaptability.
seeds.c: Eigen's error catastrophe: there exists a critical mutation rate μ_c above which genetic information is lost faster than selection can maintain it — the quasispecies melts into random sequence. Below μ_c: ordered phase, heritable information. Above μ_c: disordered phase, informa…
seeds.s: high learning rate or exploration parameter causes divergence rather than improved exploration; simulated annealing cooling schedule too slow → solution never stabilises; genetic algorithm with high mutation rate produces random solutions indistinguishable from noise; "cranking …

seeds.t: Fitness Effects of Changes Depend on Genetic Background — Epistasis Makes Effects Non-Additive
seeds.w: Measuring the effect of changes individually and expecting the combined effect to equal the sum of individual effects.
seeds.c: Epistasis: the fitness contribution of mutation A depends on whether mutation B is present. Effects are generically non-additive. This is the rule in biological systems, not the exception — most pairs of mutations interact. In software: the value of feature A depends on whether …
seeds.s: A/B tests show feature A beneficial and feature B beneficial, but both together degrades the product; optimisation benchmark shows 20% improvement, production shows 2%; changes that seemed safe individually cause incidents when combined; feature interaction bugs that unit tests …

seeds.t: Evolution Maintains Diversity to Balance Exploration and Exploitation — Monoculture Is Brittle
seeds.w: Eliminating variation to improve average performance, then being surprised when the system cannot adapt to environmental change.
seeds.c: Evolution maintains population diversity: most individuals exploit current knowledge; rare variants explore new strategies. The optimal mutation rate is not zero (pure exploitation) but calibrated to the rate of environmental change — this is Kelly-optimal resource allocation ac…
seeds.s: converging on a single implementation strategy makes the system brittle to changed requirements; "cleaning up" all feature flags reduces adaptive capacity; monoculture deployment fails uniformly when conditions change; "we optimised everything and now we can't respond to the com…

seeds.t: Individual-Level Optimisation Can Conflict with Group-Level Fitness — Price Equation
seeds.w: Optimising each component's individual metric and expecting system-level performance to improve.
seeds.c: Price equation: total evolutionary change = within-group selection + between-group selection. These can directly oppose each other: individual-level selection can reduce group fitness. Formally: Δz̄ = Cov(w_i, z_i)/w̄ + E(w_i Δz_i)/w̄. In software: each service hitting its SLA c…
seeds.s: all services meet their SLA but end-to-end latency degrades; each team hits their targets but the product gets slower; optimising the reported bottleneck doesn't improve user experience; individual contributors are high performers but team output is low.

seeds.t: Fisher's Runaway: Small Initial Bias Sets Up Positive Feedback That Amplifies Without Bound
seeds.w: Implementing positive feedback loops (recommender, viral loop, network effect) without an explicit stabilising mechanism that removes energy from the loop.
seeds.c: Fisher's runaway selection: a small initial bias toward a trait sets up a positive feedback loop between preference and expression that amplifies without bound until a physical constraint intervenes. The mathematics: the variance in the population grows over time, not stabilises…
seeds.s: recommendation system amplifies initial biases into filter bubbles; viral content loop causes quality degradation; rich-get-richer dynamics concentrate engagement in a few items; feedback mechanism amplifies rather than corrects; "the algorithm keeps showing the same things."

seeds.t: Key Innovations Open Niches That Are Rapidly and Discontinuously Colonised
seeds.w: Predicting smooth, proportional expansion into a new technological domain or market, and planning entry timing based on linear extrapolation.
seeds.c: Adaptive radiation: when a key innovation opens a new ecological niche (or a mass extinction clears existing niches), diversification is explosive and discontinuous — not gradual. The Cambrian explosion, the diversification of mammals after the KT extinction, and the rapid emerg…
seeds.s: new technology platform generates hundreds of competing products in months; early movers capture disproportionate market position; entering after initial diversification requires differentiation on multiple dimensions simultaneously; the window that seemed large closed quickly.

seeds.t: Severe Population Reduction Causes Random Capability Loss That Selection Cannot Recover
seeds.w: Treating severe team reduction or project scope cuts as a simple scaling event with proportional capability reduction.
seeds.c: Population bottleneck: severe reduction in population size causes random allele fixation regardless of fitness — survivors are not the fittest, just the survivors of a random process. Genetic diversity is permanently reduced, constraining future adaptive capacity. The key insigh…
seeds.s: post-restructuring team cannot recover domain expertise despite hiring new engineers; codebase areas lose maintainability for domains whose engineers left; platform capabilities atrophy even when the code still compiles; new engineers cannot reconstruct the intent of preserved s…

seeds.t: Systems Modify Their Own Selective Environment — Evolution and Environment Co-Evolve
seeds.w: Treating the requirements environment as fixed when designing and deploying a system.
seeds.c: Niche construction: organisms modify their own selective environment, changing the fitness landscape for themselves and for other organisms. Evolution and environment co-evolve — the act of adaptation changes what "fitness" means. In software: deploying a product changes user be…
seeds.s: deployed system makes its original design obsolete by changing user behaviour; product requirements shift faster than the product can track; users use the system in ways not designed for, revealing new requirements; "we solved the problem they had and created a different problem…

seeds.t: Rare Strategies Have Higher Fitness When Rare — Frequency-Dependent Selection Maintains Diversity
seeds.w: Optimising exclusively for the majority use case, assuming rare use cases have proportionally rare value.
seeds.c: Negative frequency-dependent selection: some phenotypes have higher fitness precisely because they are rare. When a strategy is common, competition is intense; when rare, it faces less competition. At equilibrium, multiple strategies coexist — rarity itself confers advantage in …
seeds.s: niche product for rare use case outcompetes the general solution within that niche; underserved minority segment grows disproportionately when targeted; "why does a startup with 0.1% of our use cases grow faster than we do in that segment?"

seeds.t: Buffering Mechanisms Hide Genetic Variation Until Stress — Revealing Hidden Adaptive Capacity
seeds.w: Treating system reliability under normal conditions as evidence of reliability under stress, and treating stress failures as purely negative events.
seeds.c: Evolvability buffering (Hsp90 as capacitor): molecular chaperones buffer phenotypic expression of genetic variation under normal conditions. When stress overwhelms the buffer, previously hidden variation is expressed — revealing both hidden fragility AND hidden adaptive capacity…
seeds.s: load testing reveals unexpected behaviours not seen at normal scale; near-capacity conditions reveal both failures and unexpected optimisations; "we only discover what the system can actually do when something breaks or overloads."

seeds.t: Individual-Level Fitness Maximisation Can Reduce Collective Fitness — Tragedy of the Commons
seeds.w: Designing incentive systems based on individual optimisation and expecting collective optimisation to emerge.
seeds.c: Group selection operates at a different level from individual selection and the two can directly conflict. Individually rational behaviour is collectively destructive when shared resources are involved (tragedy of the commons). Cooperation requires special conditions: kin select…
seeds.s: team of individually high-performing engineers produces mediocre collective output; optimising each component's local metric degrades system-level performance; each team hits their targets but the overall product gets worse; engineers optimise for the thing that is measured, not…

seeds.t: Wrap Async Route Handlers to Forward Errors
seeds.w: Using async route handlers without try/catch or a wrapper — unhandled promise rejections in Express 4 do not reach error middleware, silently hanging the request.
seeds.c: Wrap async handlers with a utility: const wrap = fn => (req, res, next) => fn(req, res, next).catch(next); then use app.get('/path', wrap(async (req, res) => {})). In Express 5 (currently in beta), async errors are forwarded automatically. Alternatively, use express-async-errors.
seeds.s: server never responding (hanging request) when an async route throws.

seeds.t: Error-Handling Middleware Must Have Exactly Four Arguments
seeds.w: Defining an error handler with three arguments (err, req, res) — Express identifies error-handling middleware by the arity of exactly four parameters; three-arg functions are treated as regular middleware.
seeds.c: Always define error handlers as (err, req, res, next) — all four parameters must be present even if next is unused. Register error middleware after all routes and other middleware.
seeds.s: errors passed to next(err) not being handled, reaching the default Express error handler instead.

seeds.t: Middleware Executes in Registration Order
seeds.w: Registering authentication middleware after the routes it should protect, or placing body parsing middleware after routes that need parsed bodies.
seeds.c: Register middleware in the order it should execute: security headers → body parsers → session → authentication → routes → error handler. Use app.use() for global middleware and router-level middleware for groups of routes.
seeds.s: req.body undefined in routes because body parser was registered after the route, or unauthenticated requests reaching protected routes.

seeds.t: Use helmet to Set Security Headers
seeds.w: Sending responses without security headers — Express by default exposes X-Powered-By: Express and lacks CSP, HSTS, and X-Frame-Options headers.
seeds.c: Add app.use(helmet()) early in the middleware stack; helmet sets 11 security headers including Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, and X-Content-Type-Options. Configure CSP explicitly for your content sources. Also add app.disable('x-powered-by') if not using hel…
seeds.s: security audit flagging missing headers, or browser refusing to load resources due to absent CSP.

seeds.t: Set a Body Size Limit to Prevent Memory Exhaustion
seeds.w: Using express.json() or express.urlencoded() without a size limit — attackers can send multi-gigabyte bodies, exhausting server memory.
seeds.c: Set a limit: app.use(express.json({limit: '1mb'})) and app.use(express.urlencoded({extended: true, limit: '1mb'})). For file uploads, use multer with limits.fileSize. Return 413 Payload Too Large for oversized bodies.
seeds.s: server OOM errors from clients sending large request bodies, or no error for requests with multi-MB JSON payloads.

seeds.t: Use express.Router() for Modular Route Organisation
seeds.w: Defining all routes directly on the app object in a single file — hundreds of routes become unmaintainable.
seeds.c: Create separate router files per feature: const router = express.Router(); router.get('/', handler); module.exports = router; then app.use('/users', usersRouter). Routers support their own middleware that only applies to their routes. Nest routers for sub-resources.
seeds.s: single app.js file with 300+ route definitions and unmanageable middleware chains.

seeds.t: Use res.json() Not res.send() for JSON Responses
seeds.w: Using res.send({data: 'value'}) for JSON — while it serialises objects, it does not reliably set Content-Type: application/json in all cases.
seeds.c: Use res.json({data: 'value'}) which always sets the correct Content-Type header, calls JSON.stringify with the app's json replacer setting, and handles null correctly. Use res.status(201).json({id: newId}) to set status and body together.
seeds.s: API clients receiving responses with text/html content type, causing JSON parse failures.

seeds.t: Configure CORS Explicitly With the cors Package
seeds.w: Setting Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * for all routes manually, or using wildcard for authenticated APIs — wildcard doesn't work with credentials and is too permissive for production.
seeds.c: Use the cors package: app.use(cors({origin: ['https://app.example.com'], credentials: true})) for specific origins, or a dynamic origin function for allowlisted domains. For pre-flight requests, cors handles OPTIONS automatically.
seeds.s: CORS errors in the browser for authenticated requests, or wildcard CORS blocking cookies.

seeds.t: Add Rate Limiting to Prevent Abuse
seeds.w: Deploying an Express API without rate limiting — endpoints are vulnerable to brute-force, credential stuffing, and denial-of-service attacks.
seeds.c: Use express-rate-limit: const limiter = rateLimit({windowMs: 15 * 60 * 1000, max: 100}); apply globally or to sensitive routes like /login with stricter limits. Store rate limit state in Redis (rate-limit-redis) for multi-process deployments. Return Retry-After headers.
seeds.s: authentication endpoints hammered with thousands of requests per minute, or API costs inflated by automated abuse.

seeds.t: Load Configuration From Environment Variables
seeds.w: Hardcoding database URLs, API keys, and port numbers directly in the source code or a committed config file.
seeds.c: Use dotenv (require('dotenv').config() at the top of the entry point) to load .env files in development; read values with process.env.DATABASE_URL. Add .env to .gitignore and provide a .env.example. In production, inject environment variables via the platform (Heroku, ECS, Kuber…
seeds.s: credentials committed to git, or app failing to start in a new environment because config is hardcoded.

seeds.t: Implement Graceful Shutdown on SIGTERM
seeds.w: Not handling process termination signals — the server is killed immediately on deploy or container stop, dropping in-flight requests.
seeds.c: Listen for SIGTERM and SIGINT: process.on('SIGTERM', () => { server.close(() => { db.close(); process.exit(0); }); }). server.close() stops accepting new connections and waits for existing ones to finish. Set a timeout to force exit if shutdown takes too long.
seeds.s: 502 errors from the load balancer during rolling deploys as requests are dropped mid-flight.

seeds.t: Use morgan for Structured Request Logging
seeds.w: Using console.log in every route to log requests — inconsistent format, missing status codes and response times, and no log level control.
seeds.c: Add morgan middleware early: app.use(morgan('combined')) for Apache-style logs in production or 'dev' for colourised development output. Pipe morgan output to a structured logger (winston, pino) with morgan's stream option. Skip health check endpoints to reduce log noise.
seeds.s: no visibility into request volume and response times, or cluttered logs from manual console.logs.

seeds.t: Set Cache Headers for Static Files in Production
seeds.w: Serving static files with express.static without cache headers — browsers re-download assets on every page load, wasting bandwidth and increasing load times.
seeds.c: Set maxAge in express.static options: app.use('/static', express.static('public', {maxAge: '1y', etag: false})) for immutable assets with content-hashed filenames. Use maxAge: 0 or no-cache for files that change without a name change. In production, serve static files via a CDN …
seeds.s: browser DevTools showing large assets downloaded on every navigation without 304 responses.

seeds.t: Enable Response Compression for Text Responses
seeds.w: Sending JSON and HTML responses without compression — large payloads increase transfer time and bandwidth costs.
seeds.c: Add compression middleware: app.use(compression()) to gzip/brotli responses above a threshold (default 1 KB). Place it before static file serving and route handlers. In production with a reverse proxy (nginx), delegate compression to the proxy for better performance.
seeds.s: large JSON API responses taking seconds to download that would be 5x smaller with gzip.

seeds.t: Call next() or Send a Response in Every Middleware
seeds.w: Writing middleware that neither calls next() nor sends a response in some code path — requests hang indefinitely for that path with no error.
seeds.c: Every middleware function must either call next() (or next(err)) to pass control, or terminate the request with res.json(), res.send(), res.end(), etc. Check all branches including error paths and early-return conditions.
seeds.s: requests to certain routes or with certain inputs hanging until the client times out.

seeds.t: Set trust proxy When Behind a Reverse Proxy
seeds.w: Reading req.ip or req.protocol without setting app.set('trust proxy', 1) when Express runs behind nginx or a load balancer — req.ip returns the proxy's IP, and req.secure is always false even for HTTPS connections.
seeds.c: Set app.set('trust proxy', 1) to trust the first hop's X-Forwarded-For and X-Forwarded-Proto headers. For multiple proxy hops, set the count or a specific subnet. Rate limiters also need trust proxy to use the real client IP.
seeds.s: rate limiting applied to the proxy's IP instead of the client, or HTTPS redirects looping because req.secure is false.

seeds.t: Use a Strong, Environment-Specific Session Secret
seeds.w: Using a hardcoded or weak session secret like 'secret' or 'keyboard cat' — sessions can be forged by anyone who knows the secret, and sharing a secret across environments exposes production sessions.
seeds.c: Generate a cryptographically random secret (node -e "console.log(require('crypto').randomBytes(64).toString('hex'))") and load it from an environment variable. Use separate secrets per environment. Rotate secrets with rolling secret arrays: secret: [newSecret, oldSecret].
seeds.s: session cookies valid in production using the development secret, or hardcoded secret visible in the repository.

seeds.t: Validate and Sanitise Query and Path Parameters
seeds.w: Using req.params.id or req.query.page directly in database queries — no type checking, no length limits, and SQL/NoSQL injection risk.
seeds.c: Validate with Joi, Zod, or express-validator: check that id is a valid UUID or integer, page is a positive integer within bounds. Use a validation middleware that returns 400 Bad Request on invalid input before reaching the route handler.
seeds.s: database errors from unexpected string values where integers are expected, or 500 errors from malformed query parameters.

seeds.t: Cluster Across CPU Cores for Production Throughput
seeds.w: Running a single Node.js process in production — Node is single-threaded; a single process cannot use more than one CPU core, leaving most server capacity idle.
seeds.c: Use Node's cluster module or PM2 (pm2 start app.js -i max) to spawn one worker per CPU core, sharing the same port via the OS. Use a stateless architecture (sessions in Redis, not in-process) so any worker can handle any request.
seeds.s: high CPU on one core and idle CPUs on a multi-core server under load.

seeds.t: Use router.param() for Reusable Route Parameter Loading
seeds.w: Loading a resource by ID at the start of every route handler that uses it (const user = await User.findById(req.params.userId)) — duplicated across GET, PUT, PATCH, DELETE handlers.
seeds.c: Define router.param('userId', async (req, res, next, id) => { req.targetUser = await User.findById(id); next(); }) to run once whenever the :userId param appears in a matched route. This centralises not-found handling and DB loading.
seeds.s: resource-loading code copy-pasted into 5 route handlers for the same resource.

seeds.t: Use def Not async def for Blocking IO Operations
seeds.w: Defining a route with async def but calling a blocking library inside (requests.get(), synchronous SQLAlchemy, time.sleep()) — blocking calls in an async function freeze the entire event loop, halting all other requests.
seeds.c: Either use a truly async library (httpx, asyncpg, aiofiles) inside async def, or define the route as a plain def — FastAPI runs plain def routes in a thread pool automatically, preventing event loop blocking.
seeds.s: one slow endpoint making all other endpoints unresponsive under load.

seeds.t: Use Depends() for Shared Logic Across Routes
seeds.w: Duplicating authentication, database session, and rate-limiting logic directly in every route function — fragile, inconsistent, and hard to test.
seeds.c: Extract shared logic into dependency functions and inject them with Depends(): async def get_current_user(token: str = Header(...)) -> User. FastAPI calls dependencies automatically, caches them per request, and surfaces them in the OpenAPI docs. Chain dependencies by accepting …
seeds.s: authentication code copy-pasted into 30 route functions.

seeds.t: Use lifespan for Startup and Shutdown Logic
seeds.w: Using the deprecated @app.on_event('startup') and @app.on_event('shutdown') decorators — these are deprecated since FastAPI 0.93.
seeds.c: Use the lifespan context manager: define an async generator decorated with @asynccontextmanager that yields after setup and cleans up after the yield, then pass it to FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan). This ensures setup and teardown are symmetric and testable.
seeds.s: deprecation warnings in startup logs, or startup/shutdown logic not running in tests.

seeds.t: Use response_model to Filter and Validate Responses
seeds.w: Returning ORM objects or internal dicts directly from routes, exposing sensitive fields like passwords and internal IDs.
seeds.c: Set response_model=UserPublic on route decorators; FastAPI validates and filters the response to include only fields defined in the Pydantic model, excluding sensitive data automatically. Use response_model_exclude_unset=True to omit fields not set in partial responses.
seeds.s: password hashes or internal metadata accidentally included in API responses.

seeds.t: Pydantic v2 Uses model_config Not Class Config
seeds.w: Using class Config inside a Pydantic model for configuration (orm_mode = True, schema_extra = ...) — this is Pydantic v1 syntax that Pydantic v2 ignores silently or raises warnings.
seeds.c: Use model_config = ConfigDict(from_attributes=True) at the class level with Pydantic v2 (FastAPI 0.100+). orm_mode is now from_attributes; schema_extra is now json_schema_extra; validators use @field_validator with mode='before'.
seeds.s: ORM objects not serialising or validation not applying because Config is silently ignored.

seeds.t: BackgroundTasks for Lightweight Fire-and-Forget Only
seeds.w: Using FastAPI's BackgroundTasks for long-running jobs (email sending with retries, video processing) — background tasks run in the same process and have no retry, persistence, or failure handling.
seeds.c: Use BackgroundTasks only for sub-second non-critical tasks (logging, cache warming) where loss is acceptable. For reliable background work, use a proper task queue: Celery + Redis, ARQ, or Dramatiq.
seeds.s: background tasks silently lost on server restart or crashing the worker without retry.

seeds.t: Organise Routes With APIRouter and Prefixes
seeds.w: Defining all routes directly on the app object in a single file — the file becomes thousands of lines with no logical organisation.
seeds.c: Create APIRouter instances per feature (users_router = APIRouter(prefix='/users', tags=['users'])) and include them: app.include_router(users_router). This groups related routes, applies shared dependencies via router-level Depends, and organises OpenAPI docs by tag.
seeds.s: single main.py with hundreds of route definitions, impossible to navigate.

seeds.t: Register Exception Handlers for Consistent Error Responses
seeds.w: Raising HTTPException everywhere with varying status codes and detail formats — inconsistent error responses across routes, hard for clients to handle programmatically.
seeds.c: Define custom exception classes and register app.add_exception_handler(MyError, my_error_handler) to produce consistent JSON error responses. Use RequestValidationError handler to customise the default 422 validation error format.
seeds.s: API clients needing different parsing logic for errors from different endpoints.

seeds.t: Create a New Database Session Per Request via Depends
seeds.w: Using a global database session object shared across all requests — sessions are not thread/coroutine safe and accumulate state across requests.
seeds.c: Create a session per request using a dependency: def get_db(): db = SessionLocal(); try: yield db; finally: db.close(). Inject with Depends(get_db) in route signatures. With async SQLAlchemy, use AsyncSession and async generators.
seeds.s: data bleeding between requests, 'session is already closed' errors under concurrent load.

seeds.t: Path Params Are Required; Query Params Can Be Optional
seeds.w: Declaring an optional parameter in the path (/items/{item_id?}) — FastAPI does not support optional path parameters; the route won't match.
seeds.c: Required values belong in the path (/items/{item_id}); optional filters belong as query parameters with a default (q: Optional[str] = None). Use Query(default=None, alias='search') for validation and aliasing.
seeds.s: route not matching URLs when an expected path parameter is absent.

seeds.t: Add CORSMiddleware Before Other Middleware
seeds.w: Adding CORSMiddleware after other middleware in the stack — middleware executes in reverse order of registration; CORS headers may not be added to responses from inner middleware.
seeds.c: Add CORSMiddleware first (innermost) using app.add_middleware(CORSMiddleware, allow_origins=[...], allow_methods=['*'], allow_headers=['*']). For authenticated endpoints, include the Authorization header in allow_headers.
seeds.s: CORS preflight OPTIONS requests returning 4xx or missing Access-Control headers.

seeds.t: Use TestClient for Integration Tests Without a Running Server
seeds.w: Spinning up a real server with uvicorn in tests and hitting it with requests — slow, requires port management, and complicates test isolation.
seeds.c: Use from fastapi.testclient import TestClient; client = TestClient(app) — it runs the ASGI app in-process synchronously without a network. Override dependencies with app.dependency_overrides[get_db] = get_test_db for test isolation. Use AsyncClient from httpx for async tests.
seeds.s: integration tests that are slow or flaky due to real server startup/teardown.

seeds.t: Use OAuth2PasswordBearer for Token Authentication
seeds.w: Manually parsing the Authorization header and duplicating auth logic in every protected route.
seeds.c: Use FastAPI's built-in security: oauth2_scheme = OAuth2PasswordBearer(tokenUrl='token') then inject with token: str = Depends(oauth2_scheme). This adds the lock icon to OpenAPI docs and standardises token extraction. Verify JWTs with python-jose or python-jwt in a get_current_us…
seeds.s: inconsistent auth header parsing across routes, no auth UI in Swagger docs.

seeds.t: Use UploadFile Not bytes for File Uploads
seeds.w: Declaring a file parameter as bytes — this reads the entire file into memory before the route function runs, causing OOM for large files.
seeds.c: Use UploadFile which provides a SpooledTemporaryFile with async read support: contents = await file.read(). Stream large files with file.read(chunk_size) in a loop. Use List[UploadFile] for multiple files. Validate file type from file.content_type.
seeds.s: server OOM errors when users upload large files, or timeouts on multi-file uploads.

seeds.t: Use pydantic-settings for Type-Safe Configuration
seeds.w: Reading os.environ['DATABASE_URL'] directly throughout the codebase — no type coercion, no validation, and missing variables crash at runtime instead of startup.
seeds.c: Create a Settings class inheriting from pydantic_settings.BaseSettings; declare fields with types and defaults; instantiate once as a module-level singleton. BaseSettings reads from environment variables and .env files automatically, validates types, and raises clear errors at s…
seeds.s: KeyError for missing env vars deep in request handling instead of at app startup.

seeds.t: Annotate Routes With summary and description for Clean Docs
seeds.w: Leaving route functions with no docstrings or metadata — the auto-generated OpenAPI docs show meaningless function names and no descriptions.
seeds.c: Add summary='Create user', description='Creates a new user and sends a verification email' to route decorators, or write a docstring in the route function (FastAPI uses the first line as summary, the rest as description). Use tags=['users'] for grouping.
seeds.s: Swagger UI with routes named 'create_user_users_post' and no descriptions.

seeds.t: Manage WebSocket Connections With a ConnectionManager
seeds.w: Storing active WebSocket connections in a simple list without handling disconnections — iterating a list with a closed connection raises WebSocketDisconnect and leaves stale entries.
seeds.c: Implement a ConnectionManager class with connect, disconnect, and broadcast methods; wrap sends in try/except WebSocketDisconnect and remove the connection on disconnect. For multi-worker deployments, use Redis pub/sub to broadcast across workers.
seeds.s: broadcast loop crashing when any client disconnects, or stale connections accumulating in memory.

seeds.t: Use StreamingResponse for Large or Progressive Responses
seeds.w: Building a large string or bytes object in memory and returning it as a plain Response — holds the full payload in memory and delays first byte until generation is complete.
seeds.c: Use StreamingResponse with a generator or async generator that yields chunks: StreamingResponse(generate_csv(), media_type='text/csv'). Set Content-Disposition header for file downloads. For server-sent events, yield newline-terminated JSON with text/event-stream media type.
seeds.s: large file downloads causing memory spikes or users waiting for the full file before download starts.

seeds.t: Use Discriminated Unions for Polymorphic Request Bodies
seeds.w: Accepting a generic dict or Any for a request body that can be multiple types — no validation, no docs, and complex if/else parsing in the route.
seeds.c: Use Pydantic discriminated unions with a Literal type discriminator field: Annotated[Union[CatModel, DogModel], Field(discriminator='pet_type')]. FastAPI validates and deserialises to the correct type based on the discriminator, with full OpenAPI schema generation.
seeds.s: large if/else blocks parsing an Any body, no schema in Swagger for the endpoint.

seeds.t: Use Alembic for Database Migrations With SQLAlchemy
seeds.w: Calling Base.metadata.create_all(engine) at application startup to manage the schema — this only creates missing tables and never handles column changes, renames, or drops.
seeds.c: Use Alembic for incremental schema migrations: alembic revision --autogenerate -m 'add users table' generates a migration from model differences; alembic upgrade head applies it. Run migrations in a separate step from app startup, not inside the app process.
seeds.s: column changes or index additions not applied to existing databases after deploy.

seeds.t: Use the Application Factory Pattern
seeds.w: Creating the Flask app at module level as a global: app = Flask(__name__) and importing it everywhere — makes testing difficult (can't create multiple app instances with different configs) and causes circular import issues.
seeds.c: Define create_app(config=None): app = Flask(__name__); app.config.from_object(config); db.init_app(app); return app. Call in the entry point: app = create_app(). Extensions are initialised with init_app(app) inside the factory. Tests call create_app('testing') for an isolated in…
seeds.s: tests sharing the same global app state, or circular imports because extensions import the global app object.

seeds.t: Never Use flask run or app.run() in Production
seeds.w: Starting Flask with flask run or app.run(debug=True) in production — the built-in Werkzeug server is single-threaded, not production-hardened, and debug mode exposes an interactive debugger to anyone who can cause an exception.
seeds.c: Use a production WSGI server: gunicorn -w 4 myapp:app or uwsgi. For async Flask (2.0+), use an ASGI server like uvicorn with gunicorn -k uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker. Set debug=False and use environment variables to configure.
seeds.s: Flask app serving one request at a time in production, or the debugger PIN exposed to external users.

seeds.t: Organise Routes With Blueprints
seeds.w: Registering all routes directly on the app object in a single file — the file grows to thousands of lines with no logical separation.
seeds.c: Create Blueprint objects per feature: auth_bp = Blueprint('auth', __name__, url_prefix='/auth'). Define routes on the blueprint: @auth_bp.route('/login'). Register on the app: app.register_blueprint(auth_bp). Use url_for('auth.login') to reference blueprint endpoints. Blueprints…
seeds.s: a single routes.py with hundreds of route functions, no separation between authentication, API, and admin code.

seeds.t: Push an Application Context for Code Outside Requests
seeds.w: Accessing current_app, g, or request globals outside of a request context (in a CLI script, background thread, or test) — raises RuntimeError: 'Working outside of application context'.
seeds.c: Push an app context manually: with app.app_context(): db.session.query(User).all(). For tests, use the app.test_request_context() context manager when request globals are needed. Background threads spawned from within a request must push their own context. The factory pattern ma…
seeds.s: RuntimeError when trying to access db or current_app from a management script or background task.

seeds.t: Use flask.g for Request-Scoped Shared Data
seeds.w: Storing request-scoped data (current user, database connection) in a module-level global variable — shared across concurrent requests, causing data leakage between users.
seeds.c: Use flask.g for data that should live only for the duration of a single request: in before_request, set g.current_user = get_user_from_token(). Access it throughout the request's handlers and templates. g is reset at the end of each request. Use teardown_request to clean up reso…
seeds.s: one user's authenticated data leaking into another user's response because a module-level variable was used instead of g.

seeds.t: Use Config Classes for Environment-Specific Settings
seeds.w: Using if os.environ.get('FLASK_ENV') == 'production' branches throughout configuration code.
seeds.c: Define a Config hierarchy: class Config: SECRET_KEY = os.environ.get('SECRET_KEY'); class DevelopmentConfig(Config): DEBUG = True; class ProductionConfig(Config): DEBUG = False. Load with app.config.from_object('myapp.config.ProductionConfig') or via the FLASK_ENV/APP_SETTINGS e…
seeds.s: configuration spread across multiple if/else branches with hardcoded values that end up in version control.

seeds.t: Use url_for() Instead of Hardcoded URL Strings
seeds.w: Hardcoding URL paths in templates and redirect calls: return redirect('/dashboard') — breaks when the route is renamed or the URL prefix changes.
seeds.c: Use url_for('blueprint.view_function') in Python and {{ url_for('auth.login') }} in Jinja2 templates. Pass route parameters as keyword arguments: url_for('posts.detail', post_id=42). Use _external=True for absolute URLs in emails. url_for raises BuildError immediately if the end…
seeds.s: 404 after renaming a route because hardcoded URL strings weren't updated throughout the codebase.

seeds.t: Register Error Handlers for Clean Error Responses
seeds.w: Letting Flask return its default HTML error pages for API endpoints — JSON clients receive HTML they can't parse when a 404 or 500 occurs.
seeds.c: Register error handlers on the app or blueprint: @app.errorhandler(404) def not_found(e): return jsonify(error=str(e)), 404. Register a generic 500 handler for unhandled exceptions. Use abort(404, description='User not found') to trigger handlers with context. Blueprints can hav…
seeds.s: API clients receiving HTML error pages that break JSON parsing in error handling code.

seeds.t: Initialise Extensions With init_app() Not the App Directly
seeds.w: Passing the app to extensions at creation time: db = SQLAlchemy(app) — requires the app to exist before the extension, preventing the factory pattern and making testing harder.
seeds.c: Create extensions without the app: db = SQLAlchemy(). Initialise in the factory: db.init_app(app). This allows extensions to be defined at module level and used across blueprints, while the actual binding to an app happens in create_app(). Test with different app configurations …
seeds.s: circular import error because db = SQLAlchemy(app) requires importing the global app object from a different module.

seeds.t: Use Scoped Sessions and Close After Each Request
seeds.w: Creating a new SQLAlchemy session manually per function and forgetting to close it — session accumulates unconsumed results and holds database connections.
seeds.c: Flask-SQLAlchemy provides a request-scoped session via db.session — it's automatically removed (and the connection returned to the pool) at the end of each request via teardown_appcontext. Never call db.session.close() manually in most cases; call db.session.remove() in teardown…
seeds.s: database connection pool exhausted because sessions were not closed after requests.

seeds.t: Use before_request and teardown_request for Cross-Cutting Concerns
seeds.w: Duplicating authentication checks, database connection setup, and request logging at the top of every view function.
seeds.c: Use @app.before_request to run code before every request (auth checks, setting g.user). Use @app.after_request to modify responses (adding CORS headers, logging). Use @app.teardown_request for cleanup that must run even if an exception occurred (closing db connections). Blueprin…
seeds.s: auth check copied into every protected route function when before_request would apply it once globally.

seeds.t: Test Flask Apps With the Test Client
seeds.w: Spinning up a real server and using requests.get() to test Flask routes — slow, requires port management, and can't inspect internal state.
seeds.c: Use app.test_client(): with app.test_client() as client: response = client.get('/api/users'). Set testing=True config to propagate exceptions. Use client.post('/login', json={'email': 'a@b.com'}) for JSON requests. Access response.json, response.status_code, response.headers. Us…
seeds.s: integration tests making real HTTP calls that the test client would handle in-process without network overhead.

seeds.t: Use Jinja2 Autoescape and Markup for XSS Safety
seeds.w: Rendering user-supplied content with {{ user_html | safe }} without sanitisation — allows arbitrary HTML/JavaScript injection (XSS).
seeds.c: Jinja2 autoescapes all variables in .html templates by default — this is the safe default. Only use | safe for content you have explicitly sanitised with bleach or similar. Use Markup('literal html') in Python code to mark trusted strings. For user-supplied rich text, always san…
seeds.s: XSS vulnerability from user-supplied content rendered with | safe without server-side sanitisation.

seeds.t: Use ProxyFix When Deployed Behind a Reverse Proxy
seeds.w: Deploying Flask behind nginx or a load balancer without ProxyFix — request.url shows http:// even for HTTPS requests, and request.remote_addr shows the proxy IP instead of the client IP.
seeds.c: Wrap the app with ProxyFix: from werkzeug.middleware.proxy_fix import ProxyFix; app.wsgi_app = ProxyFix(app.wsgi_app, x_for=1, x_proto=1, x_host=1). Set x_for to the number of trusted proxy hops. This correctly reads X-Forwarded-For, X-Forwarded-Proto, and X-Forwarded-Host heade…
seeds.s: url_for generating http:// links in an HTTPS application, or rate limiting applying to the load balancer's IP instead of the client.

seeds.t: Use async def Views for Concurrent I/O in Flask 2.0+
seeds.w: Blocking the worker thread with sequential synchronous I/O calls when multiple external services need to be called per request.
seeds.c: Flask 2.0+ supports async view functions: @app.get('/data') async def get_data(): results = await asyncio.gather(fetch_users(), fetch_orders()). Requires installing flask[async] and running under an ASGI server (hypercorn, uvicorn). Each async view runs in an event loop; don't m…
seeds.s: slow API endpoint making three sequential external API calls that async/gather would make concurrently.

seeds.t: Register Custom CLI Commands With Flask's Click Integration
seeds.w: Writing standalone scripts with manual app bootstrapping for database migrations, seeding, and maintenance tasks.
seeds.c: Register CLI commands in create_app or with @app.cli.command(): @app.cli.command('seed-db') def seed_db(): db.session.add(User(email='admin@example.com')); db.session.commit(). Run with flask seed-db. Commands have full access to the app context. Group related commands: admin_cl…
seeds.s: separate shell scripts running python scripts that manually call create_app() when Flask CLI commands would integrate cleanly.

seeds.t: Return Explicit Response Objects for Headers and Status Codes
seeds.w: Returning a tuple (body, status_code) from views and forgetting to set Content-Type or other necessary headers — inconsistent response format across endpoints.
seeds.c: Use make_response() for explicit control: resp = make_response(jsonify(data), 201); resp.headers['Location'] = url_for('posts.detail', id=new_id); return resp. Or return jsonify(data), 201, {'Location': url} as a three-tuple. Use flask.Response for streaming: return Response(gen…
seeds.s: API returning 200 for resource creation that should be 201, or missing Location header that RFC 7231 requires.

seeds.t: Generate a Strong SECRET_KEY and Load From Environment
seeds.w: Setting app.secret_key = 'dev' or hardcoding any key in source code — session cookies are signed with this key; anyone who knows it can forge sessions.
seeds.c: Generate a cryptographically random key: python -c 'import secrets; print(secrets.token_hex())'. Load from environment: app.config['SECRET_KEY'] = os.environ.get('SECRET_KEY') and raise an error at startup if not set in production. Use different keys per environment. Change the …
seeds.s: secret key committed to git, allowing attackers to sign arbitrary session cookies and bypass authentication.

seeds.t: Set Cache Headers Explicitly for API Responses
seeds.w: Returning API responses with no cache headers — browsers and proxies cache JSON responses unpredictably, causing stale data to be displayed.
seeds.c: For dynamic API data: after_request handler adds Cache-Control: no-store. For static-ish data: use cache_control(max_age=300, public=True) decorator or set in the response object. Use ETags for conditional requests. Flask-Caching provides @cache.cached(timeout=300) for memoising…
seeds.s: users seeing stale API data because an intermediate proxy cached a no-cache-header-free response.

seeds.t: Configure Logging Properly Instead of Using print()
seeds.w: Using print() for debug output and relying on Flask's default logging — print doesn't support log levels, formatting, or routing to different handlers (file, syslog, Sentry).
seeds.c: Configure Python's logging module in create_app(): logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format='%(asctime)s %(levelname)s %(name)s: %(message)s'). Access via app.logger or module-level logger = logging.getLogger(__name__). In production, output JSON logs for structured log ag…
seeds.s: print() debug statements accidentally shipped to production with no way to disable them by log level.

seeds.t: Cancel Firestore Listeners in dispose() to Prevent Leaks
seeds.w: Setting up a Firestore real-time listener with stream.listen() or StreamBuilder without cancelling it when the widget is disposed — the listener keeps the widget alive and fires callbacks on a destroyed widget, causing memory leaks and 'setState called after dispose' errors.
seeds.c: Store the StreamSubscription and cancel it in dispose(): _subscription = FirebaseFirestore.instance.collection('items').snapshots().listen(handler); then in dispose(): _subscription?.cancel(). For StreamBuilder, Flutter manages the lifecycle automatically — prefer StreamBuilder …
seeds.s: 'setState called after dispose' errors appearing in the log as users navigate between screens with Firestore listeners.

seeds.t: Use StreamBuilder With Firestore for Reactive UI
seeds.w: Calling .get() in initState and storing the result in state — the UI doesn't update when the Firestore data changes.
seeds.c: Use StreamBuilder with Firestore's real-time stream: StreamBuilder<QuerySnapshot>(stream: FirebaseFirestore.instance.collection('messages').snapshots(), builder: (context, snapshot) { if (snapshot.hasError) return ErrorWidget(snapshot.error!); if (!snapshot.hasData) return Circu…
seeds.s: list showing stale data because .get() was called once at init instead of streaming real-time updates.

seeds.t: Enforce Access Control in Firestore Security Rules, Not Client Code
seeds.w: Filtering Firestore reads in Flutter code to show only the current user's data — the client code is bypassed by anyone using the Firestore REST API directly with the user's credentials.
seeds.c: Write Firestore Security Rules to enforce access at the database level: allow read: if request.auth != null && request.auth.uid == resource.data.userId. Test rules with the Firebase Rules Playground or firebase emulators:start. Client-side filtering is for UX only; security must…
seeds.s: users able to read other users' data by making direct Firestore API calls, bypassing the app's client-side filter.

seeds.t: Create Composite Indexes for Multi-Field Firestore Queries
seeds.w: Querying Firestore with multiple where() clauses and an orderBy() without creating a composite index — Firestore throws a FirebaseException with a direct link to create the missing index.
seeds.c: When you combine where() on one field with orderBy() on a different field, or use multiple inequality filters (>, <, !=), Firestore requires a composite index. Click the URL in the error message to create it in the Firebase Console, or define indexes in firestore.indexes.json an…
seeds.s: 'The query requires an index' FirebaseException at runtime for a query that worked with a single clause.

seeds.t: Use authStateChanges Stream for Authentication State
seeds.w: Checking FirebaseAuth.instance.currentUser at app start and assuming it's stable — currentUser is null during the initial auth state resolution, causing a brief unauthenticated state flash.
seeds.c: Listen to authStateChanges() stream: StreamBuilder<User?>(stream: FirebaseAuth.instance.authStateChanges(), builder: (ctx, snapshot) { if (snapshot.connectionState == ConnectionState.waiting) return LoadingScreen(); return snapshot.data != null ? HomeScreen() : LoginScreen(); })…
seeds.s: app briefly showing the login screen on every cold start before recognising the already-authenticated user.

seeds.t: Enable Firestore Offline Persistence for Resilient Apps
seeds.w: Not configuring offline persistence — Firestore reads fail entirely when the device is offline, showing error states to the user.
seeds.c: Enable offline persistence (enabled by default on mobile, must be enabled explicitly on web): FirebaseFirestore.instance.settings = const Settings(persistenceEnabled: true, cacheSizeBytes: Settings.CACHE_SIZE_UNLIMITED). Firestore caches data locally; reads serve from cache when…
seeds.s: app completely broken without internet connection when offline persistence would serve cached data seamlessly.

seeds.t: Use Batch for Multi-Document Writes, Transaction for Read-Then-Write
