seeds.w: Using a transaction for bulk writes that don't depend on reading current values — transactions have read overhead and can retry multiple times unnecessarily.
seeds.c: Use WriteBatch for atomic multi-document writes that don't need to read first: final batch = db.batch(); batch.set(doc1, data1); batch.update(doc2, data2); await batch.commit(). Use runTransaction only when the write depends on reading the current value: db.runTransaction((tx) a…
seeds.s: unnecessary read overhead and retry risk from using transactions for pure multi-document writes that WriteBatch handles more efficiently.

seeds.t: Minimise Cold Start Latency in Firebase Cloud Functions
seeds.w: Writing large Cloud Functions that import many dependencies — the Node.js runtime must load all imports on cold start, causing multi-second latency for the first invocation.
seeds.c: Keep functions small and import dependencies lazily (inside the function) when possible. Deploy functions in the region closest to users and Firestore. Use minimum instances to keep functions warm for latency-sensitive paths: functions.runWith({minInstances: 1}). Consider Cloud …
seeds.s: Firebase Cloud Function taking 3–5 seconds on first invocation due to cold start from a large bundle.

seeds.t: Handle FCM Token Refresh for Reliable Push Notifications
seeds.w: Saving the FCM token once at registration and never updating it — tokens change when the app is reinstalled, restored from backup, or the user clears app data; old tokens result in failed deliveries.
seeds.c: Listen to token refresh events: FirebaseMessaging.instance.onTokenRefresh.listen((token) => saveTokenToServer(token)). Also retrieve the initial token on every app start in case it changed since last launch: final token = await FirebaseMessaging.instance.getToken(); saveIfChange…
seeds.s: push notifications failing for a portion of users because their FCM tokens changed after app reinstall.

seeds.t: Choose Firestore Over Realtime Database for New Projects
seeds.w: Choosing Firebase Realtime Database for a new project because it's simpler — Realtime Database has limited querying, no subcollections, and charges per connection at scale.
seeds.c: Use Cloud Firestore for new projects: richer querying (compound queries, collection group queries), hierarchical data model with subcollections, per-document reads pricing, and stronger offline support. Use Realtime Database only for use cases requiring extremely low latency (< …
seeds.s: hitting Realtime Database's query limitations mid-project and needing a costly migration to Firestore.

seeds.t: Use Firebase Emulator Suite for Local Development
seeds.w: Developing against the production Firebase project — test data pollutes production, costs money, and changes to security rules affect live users.
seeds.c: Run firebase emulators:start to start local emulators for Auth, Firestore, Storage, Functions, and Hosting. Connect Flutter to emulators: FirebaseFirestore.instance.useFirestoreEmulator('localhost', 8080); FirebaseAuth.instance.useAuthEmulator('localhost', 9099). In tests, use t…
seeds.s: test users appearing in production Firestore or security rule experiments breaking production auth.

seeds.t: Model Firestore Data Around Query Patterns, Not Normalisation
seeds.w: Normalising Firestore data like a relational database with IDs to look up — Firestore has no joins; multiple round trips for related data degrade UX.
seeds.c: Denormalise for your query patterns: embed frequently co-read data (embed author name and avatar in each post document to avoid a separate lookup). Accept data duplication — update all copies when the source changes (Cloud Functions trigger on document updates). Use subcollectio…
seeds.s: app making 3 Firestore requests to display one screen because data was normalised across collections.

seeds.t: Don't Use BuildContext Across Async Gaps
seeds.w: Using context after an await call inside a widget method — the widget may have been disposed between the await and the context use, causing 'use of context after async gap' or a crash.
seeds.c: Check if mounted before using context after any await: if (!mounted) return; Then use context synchronously. In Flutter 3.7+, the linter warns about this automatically. Store Navigator, ScaffoldMessenger etc. in local variables before the await if needed.
seeds.s: 'Looking up a deactivated widget's ancestor is unsafe' crash after an async operation completes.

seeds.t: Use const Constructors to Prevent Unnecessary Rebuilds
seeds.w: Creating widget instances without const when they are compile-time constants — Flutter rebuilds them on every parent rebuild even though they haven't changed.
seeds.c: Mark widget constructors const wherever possible and use const when constructing them: const Text('Hello'), const SizedBox(height: 16). Enable the prefer_const_constructors and prefer_const_literals_to_create_immutables lints. Const widgets are canonicalised and skipped during r…
seeds.s: profiling showing large subtrees being rebuilt on every state change when they contain only static content.

seeds.t: Call setState on the Smallest Possible Widget
seeds.w: Calling setState at the top of a large widget tree to update a small part of the UI — rebuilds the entire subtree including expensive child widgets.
seeds.c: Extract the stateful part into a small child widget with its own State; setState there rebuilds only that subtree. Alternatively, use a state management solution (Provider, Riverpod, BLoC) to rebuild only consumers of the changed value.
seeds.s: frame drops on simple state changes because a large widget tree is being rebuilt.

seeds.t: Store Futures in State, Not in build()
seeds.w: Creating a Future inside the build() method and passing it to FutureBuilder — build() is called on every rebuild, creating a new Future each time and causing repeated data fetches.
seeds.c: Create the Future in initState() or a state variable and reference that variable in FutureBuilder. For more complex cases, use a state management library that caches async results (Riverpod's FutureProvider, flutter_bloc).
seeds.s: API being called on every parent rebuild, multiple requests for the same data.

seeds.t: Use ListView.builder for Long or Dynamic Lists
seeds.w: Using ListView(children: items.map((e) => ItemWidget(e)).toList()) for long lists — builds all item widgets upfront regardless of screen visibility, causing jank and high memory use.
seeds.c: Use ListView.builder(itemCount: items.length, itemBuilder: (ctx, i) => ItemWidget(items[i])) which lazily builds only visible items. For variable-height items with more complex scroll behaviour, use CustomScrollView with SliverList.
seeds.s: long list causing slow initial render or scroll jank from building hundreds of off-screen widgets.

seeds.t: Use Keys When Widget Identity Must Be Preserved
seeds.w: Not using keys when reordering, adding, or removing widgets of the same type in a list — Flutter reconciles by type and position, so state from one widget can bleed into another position after a reorder.
seeds.c: Provide a Key (ValueKey, UniqueKey, GlobalKey) to uniquely identify stateful widgets in dynamic lists. Use ValueKey(item.id) for stable identity across rebuilds. GlobalKey is expensive and should be rare.
seeds.s: text field content or animation state jumping to the wrong list item after a reorder.

seeds.t: Use Named Routes or go_router for Navigation
seeds.w: Using Navigator.push(context, MaterialPageRoute(builder: (_) => DetailsPage())) scattered throughout the codebase — couples screens tightly, breaks deep linking, and is hard to test.
seeds.c: Define routes with go_router (or Navigator named routes) for declarative, URL-based navigation. go_router supports deep links, web URLs, and nested navigation. Pass data through route parameters or query strings rather than constructor injection for externally linkable routes.
seeds.s: deep linking not working, or back button behaviour inconsistent on web.

seeds.t: Always Dispose Controllers and Streams in dispose()
seeds.w: Creating AnimationController, TextEditingController, ScrollController, or StreamSubscription in a State without overriding dispose() — controllers are never cleaned up, leaking memory and causing 'a AnimationController was disposed with listeners still attached' errors.
seeds.c: Override dispose() and call controller.dispose(), subscription.cancel() for every resource created in the State. Use late final for controllers to enforce single assignment.
seeds.s: 'listener still registered' exceptions after navigating away, or memory growing with each navigation.

seeds.t: Use Platform Checks for Platform-Specific Behaviour
seeds.w: Hardcoding Android-specific or iOS-specific logic throughout widget code without abstractions — tightly couples UI to platform and breaks on other targets (web, desktop).
seeds.c: Use Platform.isAndroid / Platform.isIOS (dart:io, not available on web) or Theme.of(context).platform for UI-safe checks. For feature detection, use try/catch with PlatformException. For major platform differences, create separate widget implementations behind an interface.
seeds.s: dart:io import crashing on Flutter Web, or UI looking wrong on one platform.

seeds.t: Use cached_network_image for Network Images
seeds.w: Using Image.network() for remote images — re-fetches from the network on every widget rebuild with no disk cache, causing flickers and excess bandwidth.
seeds.c: Use the cached_network_image package: CachedNetworkImage(imageUrl: url, placeholder: (ctx, url) => CircularProgressIndicator()) which caches images to disk and memory. For images in lists, set memCacheWidth/memCacheHeight to the display size to reduce memory.
seeds.s: network images flickering on every rebuild or scroll, high bandwidth usage from repeated image downloads.

seeds.t: Wrap App in ProviderScope for Riverpod
seeds.w: Adding Riverpod providers without wrapping the app in ProviderScope, or creating providers inside widget build methods — providers created inside build are recreated on every rebuild.
seeds.c: Wrap the root of the app in ProviderScope(child: MyApp()); define providers at the top level of Dart files as final globals (final counterProvider = StateProvider((ref) => 0)). Use ConsumerWidget or Consumer for widgets that read providers.
seeds.s: 'No ProviderScope found' exception, or provider state being reset unexpectedly.

seeds.t: Use RepaintBoundary to Isolate Animated Subtrees
seeds.w: Placing an animation directly inside a frequently-painted parent — the animation causes the parent and all its siblings to repaint on every frame.
seeds.c: Wrap animated subtrees in RepaintBoundary to give them their own compositing layer; only the bounded subtree repaints during animation. Use the Flutter DevTools 'Highlight Repaints' mode to identify unnecessarily repainting widgets.
seeds.s: static UI elements repainting on every animation frame, high GPU usage visible in DevTools.

seeds.t: Customise ErrorWidget for Production Error Display
seeds.w: Leaving the default red error screen in production — the red screen exposes internal error details and crashes UX.
seeds.c: Set ErrorWidget.builder = (details) => MyErrorWidget(details) in main() before runApp to replace the red screen with a branded error UI. Use FlutterError.onError to log errors to Crashlytics or Sentry. In release mode, Flutter removes some debug assertions but ErrorWidget.builde…
seeds.s: users seeing a red error screen with a stack trace in the production app.

seeds.t: Run Heavy Computation in an Isolate
seeds.w: Parsing a large JSON file, running image processing, or computing heavy algorithms on the main isolate — all UI rendering runs on the main isolate; blocking it causes jank.
seeds.c: Use compute(function, arg) for simple off-main-thread work, or Isolate.spawn for more control. Use the worker_manager package for an isolate pool. Only plain data (no Flutter objects) can be passed across isolate boundaries via SendPort.
seeds.s: UI freezing for hundreds of milliseconds when processing large data on the main thread.

seeds.t: Use Theme TextStyles Instead of Hardcoded Styles
seeds.w: Writing Text('Hello', style: TextStyle(fontSize: 24, fontWeight: FontWeight.bold)) everywhere — hardcoded styles ignore the app theme, break dark mode adaptation, and require global find-and-replace for design changes.
seeds.c: Use Theme.of(context).textTheme.headlineMedium (or the appropriate semantic style) for consistent typography. Define your design system in ThemeData.textTheme. Use copyWith for small variations.
seeds.s: dark mode causing text to be unreadable because hardcoded colours don't adapt.

seeds.t: Use SizedBox for Whitespace, Not Empty Container
seeds.w: Using Container() with only width/height for spacing, or Container() with no decoration or child — Container is heavier than necessary for simple sizing.
seeds.c: Use SizedBox(height: 16) for fixed-size spacing, SizedBox.expand() to fill available space, and SizedBox.shrink() for zero-size placeholders. Use Padding for adding space around a child. Reserve Container for when you need decoration, transform, or alignment combined.
seeds.s: unnecessary use of Container for simple spacing, slightly heavier widget tree than needed.

seeds.t: Use Form and GlobalKey for Coordinated Validation
seeds.w: Manually tracking validation state for each TextFormField with separate boolean variables — duplicated logic and hard to coordinate across many fields.
seeds.c: Wrap fields in a Form widget, assign a GlobalKey<FormState>, and call _formKey.currentState!.validate() to trigger all validators at once. Use TextFormField's validator parameter for inline validation. Call _formKey.currentState!.save() to collect cleaned values.
seeds.s: validation logic scattered across multiple state variables that fall out of sync.

seeds.t: Use MediaQuery and LayoutBuilder for Responsive Layouts
seeds.w: Hardcoding pixel dimensions for different phone sizes — layouts break on tablets, foldables, and desktop targets.
seeds.c: Use MediaQuery.of(context).size.width for viewport-relative sizing, and LayoutBuilder(builder: (ctx, constraints) => ...) for component-level responsive layout based on available space. Use the Flex and Expanded widgets for proportional layout. On Flutter Web/Desktop, handle win…
seeds.s: UI overflowing or text unreadable on tablet or larger screen sizes.

seeds.t: Handle Platform Channel Calls on the Platform Thread
seeds.w: Performing long blocking operations on the platform channel handler thread (Android main thread, iOS main thread) — blocks UI and can cause ANR or watchdog termination.
seeds.c: On Android, use MethodChannel with a background thread or coroutine: channel.setMethodCallHandler on a background executor. On iOS, dispatch expensive work to a background queue. Return results asynchronously via result callback.
seeds.s: ANR dialogs on Android or app killed by iOS watchdog when platform channel operations take too long.

seeds.t: Use AppLifecycleListener to Respond to App State Changes
seeds.w: Ignoring app lifecycle events — resources not paused when the app backgrounds, or data not refreshed when returning to foreground.
seeds.c: Mix in WidgetsBindingObserver and override didChangeAppLifecycleState: pause audio/camera when AppLifecycleState.paused, resume when AppLifecycleState.resumed, clean up on detached. In Flutter 3.13+, use AppLifecycleListener for the newer callback API. Remember to call WidgetsBi…
seeds.s: audio playing while the app is backgrounded, or stale data displayed after returning from background.

seeds.t: Multiply movement by delta time for frame-rate independence
seeds.w: position += speed * direction each frame — movement speed depends on frame rate. At 30 FPS an object moves twice as far per second as at 60 FPS.
seeds.c: position += speed * direction * delta where delta is the time since the last frame (in seconds). This makes all time-based behaviour consistent regardless of frame rate.
seeds.s: game runs faster on high-end machines, slower on low-end ones.

seeds.t: Use a state machine for character and game state — not boolean flags
seeds.w: managing character state with multiple boolean flags (isRunning, isJumping, isAttacking) — flag combinations produce invalid states and complex conditionals.
seeds.c: use an enum or state machine: enum State { Idle, Running, Jumping, Attacking }. Only one state is active at a time, transitions are explicit, and new states add a new enum value not a new flag.
seeds.s: impossible state combinations (isRunning && isJumping && isAttacking), or unreachable code from flag combinations.

seeds.t: Pool frequently spawned objects — do not create and destroy every frame
seeds.w: instantiating and destroying bullets, particles, or enemies every time they are needed — each allocation and garbage collection causes frame spikes.
seeds.c: create a pool of pre-allocated objects at game start. When an object is 'spawned', activate it from the pool and reset its state. When it 'dies', deactivate and return it to the pool. Pool size should be the maximum expected simultaneously active count.
seeds.s: frame rate spikes when many objects spawn or die at once.

seeds.t: Use lerp for smooth camera follow — not direct assignment
seeds.w: camera.position = player.position each frame — camera snaps instantly, revealing clipping and feeling unpolished.
seeds.c: camera.position = lerp(camera.position, player.position, speed * delta). Lerp fraction should be scaled by delta to remain frame-rate independent. For more responsive feel, use exponential smoothing: camera.position = lerp(camera.position, target, 1 - exp(-speed * delta)).
seeds.s: camera movement is jerky or feels physically ungrounded.

seeds.t: Separate save data from runtime game state
seeds.w: serialising the entire game state (including runtime objects, references, computed values) to disk — fragile, large, breaks on version changes.
seeds.c: define a minimal SaveData struct containing only the persistent player choices and progress. Reconstruct all runtime state from SaveData on load. Version your save format and handle migration. Test save/load as part of core gameplay loop.
seeds.s: save files break on game updates, or loading takes too long because too much is serialised.

seeds.t: Buffer player inputs for responsive controls
seeds.w: only checking if a button is pressed in the exact frame an action window opens — players who press slightly early or late miss the window and feel the game is unresponsive.
seeds.c: store the timestamp of the last input and allow it to trigger if the action window opens within a buffer window (typically 100-200ms). Example: if jump_pressed_at > Time.now - buffer_window: do_jump().
seeds.s: controls feel unresponsive, players complain about missed inputs.

seeds.t: Use collision layers and masks to control what collides with what
seeds.w: using a single collision layer for everything and filtering in code — all physics objects check collision against all others, wasting CPU.
seeds.c: assign objects to layers by category (Player=1, Enemy=2, Projectile=3, Environment=4). Set collision masks so each object only checks against layers it should interact with. Halving the number of collision checks roughly halves physics CPU time.
seeds.s: physics is expensive with many objects, or player collides with their own projectiles.

seeds.t: Seed your random number generator for reproducible gameplay
seeds.w: using the system RNG with a time-based seed and discarding it — procedural generation, AI decisions, and loot drops cannot be replicated or debugged.
seeds.c: store the seed used for each game session or procedural generation pass. Use a seeded RNG (not the global one) for gameplay-affecting decisions. Display or log the seed so players can reproduce runs.
seeds.s: bug reports that cannot be reproduced, or procedural content that cannot be shared between players.

seeds.t: Draw debug shapes in development to visualise invisible logic
seeds.w: debugging collision boxes, pathfinding, AI vision cones, and spawn ranges by inference from behaviour alone — invisible systems are hard to debug.
seeds.c: draw debug shapes during development: lines for raycast paths, wireframe spheres for hitboxes, cones for AI sight, coloured boxes for trigger zones. Gate all debug drawing behind a flag or build configuration. Most engines have a debug draw API (Godot's draw_line, Unity's Debug.…
seeds.s: collision or AI bugs take hours to diagnose because the invisible system cannot be seen.

seeds.t: Use an event/signal system to decouple game systems
seeds.w: direct references between game systems (UI directly calling combat methods, audio directly reading player health) — creates tight coupling that makes systems hard to change or test.
seeds.c: use an event bus or signal system. Combat fires 'player_took_damage' event; UI, audio, and achievement systems each listen independently. Systems do not know about each other. In Godot: signals. In Unity: C# events or UnityEvent.
seeds.s: changing one system requires changes to five others, or circular dependencies between systems.

seeds.t: Profile before optimising — bottlenecks are rarely where you expect
seeds.w: optimising based on intuition — developers routinely optimise the wrong thing, wasting time on code that is not actually slow.
seeds.c: use the engine's built-in profiler (Godot's profiler, Unity's Profiler, Unreal's Insights) to identify actual bottlenecks before writing a single optimisation. The real bottleneck is often rendering (draw calls, overdraw), not game logic. Fix the identified bottleneck, measure a…
seeds.s: significant engineering effort spent on optimisation that produces no measurable improvement.

seeds.t: Coyote time and jump buffering make platformers feel fair
seeds.w: only allowing a jump when the player is exactly on the ground at the moment of input — this feels unresponsive and punishes slightly early or late presses.
seeds.c: implement coyote time (allow jumping for ~100ms after walking off a ledge) and jump buffering (queue a jump input for ~100ms before landing). Both are invisible to players but dramatically improve perceived responsiveness. These techniques are industry standard and used in virtu…
seeds.s: jumping feels unresponsive or unfair — players 'miss' jumps they expected to land.

seeds.t: Separate hitboxes and hurtboxes for precise combat feel
seeds.w: using the same collision shape for both dealing and receiving damage — causes self-hits, incorrect hit detection, and inflexible combat design.
seeds.c: give every combatant a hurtbox (where they can be hurt) and a hitbox (where their attack deals damage) as separate collision areas on different layers. The hitbox is only active during attack frames; the hurtbox is always active. This separation allows hit-stun, invincibility fr…
seeds.s: attack hits the attacker themselves, or hit detection feels wrong because one shape serves both purposes.

seeds.t: Implement screen shake via camera offset, not scene root movement
seeds.w: shaking the scene's root node or physics bodies to simulate camera shake — this affects physics calculations and causes jitter on all objects.
seeds.c: apply shake as an offset or rotation on the Camera node only, independent of the scene hierarchy. Use a random walk or Perlin noise for naturalistic shake: each frame add a random offset that decays over time. Trauma-squared model: trauma (0-1) is added by events, shake magnitud…
seeds.s: screen shake causes visible physics jitter or affects character movement because it modifies the wrong node.

seeds.t: Store game data in external files, not hardcoded in game logic
seeds.w: hardcoding enemy stats, item properties, or level parameters directly in scripts — changing any value requires a code edit and recompile.
seeds.c: store data in JSON, CSV, or engine-native resource files and load at runtime. Define a data schema (struct, class, or resource) and deserialise into it. This enables designers to iterate without programmer involvement, supports modding, and makes balancing much faster. Separate …
seeds.s: adjusting a single enemy health value requires modifying a script and waiting for a rebuild.

seeds.t: Route audio through buses for independent volume control
seeds.w: controlling audio volume by setting volume on each individual AudioStreamPlayer — you can't adjust music vs SFX independently, and saving volume settings requires tracking every player.
seeds.c: create named audio buses (Master, Music, SFX, UI) in the audio bus layout. Route each AudioStreamPlayer to the appropriate bus. Control volume with a single AudioServer.SetBusVolumeDb('Music', linear_to_db(volume)). Save bus volumes to player preferences.
seeds.s: player can't independently adjust music and sound effects volume because audio isn't routed through buses.

seeds.t: Use a scene manager for transitions instead of direct scene changes
seeds.w: calling SceneTree.ChangeSceneToFile() directly from gameplay code — no loading screen, no transition animation, and abrupt cuts between scenes.
seeds.c: route all scene transitions through a scene manager (autoload/singleton) that plays a fade-out animation, loads the new scene (possibly with background loading), then fades in. The manager can also handle passing data between scenes. Centralising transitions prevents duplicate t…
seeds.s: scene transitions are instant cuts with no animation because each script calls ChangeSceneToFile directly.

seeds.t: Design explicit positive feedback loops for engagement
seeds.w: building systems where all rewards are equal regardless of player skill or investment — no escalating returns means no sense of progression or investment.
seeds.c: intentionally design positive feedback loops: better equipment → easier combat → more gold → better equipment. Balance with catch-up mechanics to prevent runaway leaders. In RPGs, the XP curve is a feedback loop. In city builders, population growth enables more production. Name …
seeds.s: player engagement drops mid-game because there's no escalating sense of power or progression.

seeds.t: Affordances teach players mechanics without explicit tutorials
seeds.w: requiring players to read a tutorial popup to understand how to interact with an object — most players skip text tutorials and remain confused.
seeds.c: design visual and audio affordances: a chest with a glowing outline, a breakable wall that looks visually different from solid walls, a button on the floor in front of a door. Let level design guide players through mechanics experientially before challenging them. Use the 'Ninte…
seeds.s: players don't understand a core mechanic despite a text tutorial because it wasn't taught through play.

seeds.t: Every GDExtension class must be registered in register_types
seeds.w: defining a class that extends Godot types without registering it — it will not appear in the editor or be instantiable from GDScript.
seeds.c: call ClassDB::register_class<MyClass>() inside the register_types function that is exported from your extension. For abstract base classes use ClassDB::register_abstract_class<MyClass>().
seeds.s: class not found in editor, or GDScript cannot instantiate the type.

seeds.t: _bind_methods must register every method and property GDScript will use
seeds.w: defining a C++ method and calling it from GDScript without binding — GDScript has no knowledge of unbound methods.
seeds.c: inside static void _bind_methods(), call ClassDB::bind_method(D_METHOD('method_name', 'arg1'), &MyClass::method_name) for each exposed method. Properties need ADD_PROPERTY with getter and setter bound first.
seeds.s: 'Method not found' error in GDScript when calling the C++ method.

seeds.t: Godot objects must use memnew/memdelete, not new/delete
seeds.w: using C++ new/delete for Godot Object subclasses — Godot's memory manager will not track them, causing leaks or double-free crashes.
seeds.c: use memnew(MyClass) and memdelete(obj) for Object subclasses. For RefCounted subclasses, use Ref<MyClass> smart pointers — they handle lifetime automatically. Plain C++ structs not inheriting from Object can use normal new/delete or stack allocation.
seeds.s: memory leaks, crash on exit, or Godot warning about leaked instances.

seeds.t: Use Ref<T> for RefCounted objects, never raw pointers
seeds.w: storing a raw pointer to a RefCounted subclass — the reference count is not incremented, and the object may be freed while you hold the pointer.
seeds.c: always use Ref<MyRefCounted> which automatically manages the reference count via RAII. To create: Ref<MyClass> obj = memnew(MyClass) or Ref<MyClass>(memnew(MyClass)). Pass by Ref<T> in function signatures to transfer ownership safely.
seeds.s: use-after-free crash or object freed unexpectedly.

seeds.t: Variant is Godot's universal type — use it at API boundaries
seeds.w: using std::vector, std::map, or custom structs directly in bound method signatures.
seeds.c: convert to Godot equivalents (Array, Dictionary) at the boundary.
seeds.s: compile error or crash when GDScript calls the method.

seeds.t: gdextension file must list every class and method entry point
seeds.w: omitting the entry_symbol or listing the wrong library path — Godot silently fails to load the extension.
seeds.c: entry_symbol must match the exported function name exactly (typically gdextension_init). Library paths use platform-specific keys: [libraries] windows.debug.x86_64 = 'res://bin/my_ext.dll'.
seeds.s: extension does not load, no error shown, classes unavailable in editor.

seeds.t: GDExtensions can hot-reload in editor — design for it
seeds.w: using global static state or OS-level resources that cannot be cleanly re-initialised — hot-reload calls your initialize and deinitialize functions on every reload.
seeds.c: ensure initialize sets up all state from scratch and deinitialize cleans up everything. Use the GDEXTENSION_INITIALIZATION_SCENE level for most game code.
seeds.s: editor crashes or behaves incorrectly after extension reload.

seeds.t: Declare signals with ADD_SIGNAL in _bind_methods
seeds.w: emitting a signal from C++ without declaring it — GDScript cannot connect to undeclared signals.
seeds.c: in _bind_methods, declare: ADD_SIGNAL(MethodInfo('signal_name', PropertyInfo(Variant::INT, 'arg_name'))). Emit with: emit_signal('signal_name', value). Signal names must match exactly between declaration and emission.
seeds.s: 'Signal not found' error when GDScript tries to connect.

seeds.t: Use the official godot-cpp SConstruct as your build base
seeds.w: writing a custom CMake or Makefile from scratch — Godot's binding generation and platform flags are complex.
seeds.c: start from the godot-cpp template repository which provides a working SConstruct. Key flags: target=template_debug/template_release, platform=windows/linux/macos/android. Always run 'scons generate_bindings=yes' after updating godot-cpp.
seeds.s: linker errors about missing Godot symbols, or bindings out of date with engine version.

seeds.t: Godot scene tree is not thread-safe — use call_deferred from threads
seeds.w: modifying the scene tree or calling most Object methods from a background thread — Godot's scene tree is single-threaded.
seeds.c: use call_deferred('method_name', args) or callable.call_deferred() to queue scene tree modifications to run on the main thread. WorkerThreadPool is safe for pure computation.
seeds.s: random crashes, assertion failures, or corrupted state when multithreading.

seeds.t: Create Callables from C++ lambdas using Callable::create
seeds.w: trying to pass a raw C++ function pointer or lambda directly where Godot expects a Callable — the API requires a Callable object.
seeds.c: use Callable::create(object, StringName('method_name')) for object methods, or wrap lambdas by binding them as methods via a helper class. For signal connections from C++: node->connect('signal', Callable(this, 'handler_method')). The handler must be a registered method with BIN…
seeds.s: compiler error or crash when passing C++ callbacks to Godot signal connections expecting a Callable.

seeds.t: Use TypedArray<T> instead of Array for type-safe collections
seeds.w: using Godot's untyped Array for collections of nodes or resources — requires casting every element and loses type information in the editor.
seeds.c: use TypedArray<Node3D> to create typed arrays that are type-safe in C++ and display correctly in the Godot editor inspector. Declare exported properties: GDPROPERTY_TYPED_ARRAY(TypedArray<Node3D>, nodes). TypedArray<T> is backed by Array but enforces element type at runtime.
seeds.s: Array of nodes requires unsafe cast on every element access when TypedArray would enforce the type.

seeds.t: Register engine singletons via register_types, not _ready
seeds.w: creating a singleton node in _ready() or expecting autoload-style singletons to be available via C++ constructor — extension singletons need special registration.
seeds.c: register a singleton in the initialize function of your GDExtension: Engine::get_singleton()->register_singleton('MySingleton', memnew(MySingleton)). Unregister in deinitialize. Access from GDScript with MySingleton.method(). Mark the class with GDCLASS and implement _bind_metho…
seeds.s: GDScript cannot access a C++ singleton via class name because it wasn't registered through the Engine singleton API.

seeds.t: Use ADD_GROUP and ADD_SUBGROUP to organise inspector properties
seeds.w: registering many exported properties without grouping — the inspector becomes a flat list that is hard to navigate.
seeds.c: use ADD_GROUP('Movement', 'move_') in _bind_methods() to group all properties prefixed with move_ under a collapsible 'Movement' header in the inspector. ADD_SUBGROUP creates nested sections. Properties without a matching prefix still appear in the group until the next ADD_GROUP…
seeds.s: component with 15 exported properties showing as an undifferentiated flat list in the inspector.

seeds.t: Annotate with TOOL_ENABLED to run extension code in the editor
seeds.w: expecting C++ GDExtension code to execute in the editor at design time — by default, extension classes only run in game mode.
seeds.c: set GDCLASS(MyTool, Node) and override _get_configuration_warnings() for editor feedback, but to execute arbitrary logic in the editor add ClassDB::set_class_enabled_in_editor('MyTool', true) or set the GDEXTENSION_TOOL flag in your registration. Useful for custom gizmos, proced…
seeds.s: extension _ready runs at runtime but not when the scene is open in the Godot editor.

seeds.t: Use PhysicsDirectBodyState3D in _integrate_forces for direct physics control
seeds.w: modifying a RigidBody3D's linear_velocity directly in _process or _physics_process — this bypasses the physics simulation and can cause instability or jitter.
seeds.c: override _integrate_forces(PhysicsDirectBodyState3D *state) and modify state->set_linear_velocity(), state->apply_impulse() etc. within the callback. This runs inside the physics step and is the safe way to apply custom physics logic to rigid bodies.
seeds.s: RigidBody moves erratically because velocity is set directly each frame instead of through _integrate_forces.

seeds.t: Extend Resource to create data containers saveable as .tres files
seeds.w: storing game configuration or item data in plain C++ structs or JSON — not editable in the Godot inspector and not saveable as Godot resource files.
seeds.c: extend Resource: class ItemData : public Resource { GDCLASS(ItemData, Resource) }. Add GDPROPERTY fields for all data. Instances can be saved as .tres files, edited in the inspector, dragged into exported properties, and loaded with ResourceLoader::load(). This enables data-driv…
seeds.s: game data stored in hardcoded structs when a Resource subclass would make it inspector-editable and asset-pipeline friendly.

seeds.t: Check Error return codes from Godot API calls — don't ignore them
seeds.w: calling Godot API functions that return Error and discarding the result — failures are silent and cause downstream crashes or incorrect behaviour.
seeds.c: check every Error return: Error err = node->connect('signal', callable); ERR_FAIL_COND_MSG(err != OK, 'Failed to connect signal: ' + itos(err)). Use ERR_FAIL_COND, ERR_FAIL_NULL, CRASH_COND macros for common patterns. Godot's error codes are defined in error_macros.hpp.
seeds.s: signal connection or resource load fails silently, causing a NullPointerException later in code that assumed success.

seeds.t: .back() returns Variant — must cast
seeds.w: var last := arr.back() then last.member — .back() returns Variant, member access fails.
seeds.c: var last: Dictionary = arr.back() as Dictionary, then last.get('key', '').
seeds.s: Invalid get index on base Nil.

seeds.t: static func cannot access instance members
seeds.w: using self or instance variables inside a static func — static functions belong to the class, not an instance, and have no self.
seeds.c: pass required data as parameters, or convert to a regular func.
seeds.s: 'self' is not defined in static function, or member variable not accessible.

seeds.t: Typed arrays require Array[Type] syntax
seeds.w: var items: Array = [] with assumed type — untyped arrays lose type safety and autocomplete.
seeds.c: var items: Array[MyClass] = [] for typed arrays in GDScript 4. For dictionaries: var data: Dictionary = {} is the best available (Dictionary is not generically typed).
seeds.s: no autocomplete on array elements, or type errors at runtime.

seeds.t: @onready vars are null before _ready() runs
seeds.w: accessing an @onready variable in _init() or at class level — @onready assignments run at the start of _ready(), not at object construction.
seeds.c: all @onready access must be inside _ready() or functions called from it.
seeds.s: 'Cannot call method on null value' when accessing a node reference.

seeds.t: Use queue_free() not free() for nodes in the scene tree
seeds.w: calling .free() on a node that is still in the scene tree — this can crash if signals fire or the node is accessed after freeing during the same frame.
seeds.c: use queue_free() which defers deletion to the end of the frame after all processing is done. Only use free() on objects not in the scene tree (like Resources created in code)
seeds.s: Invalid get index on base Nil when accessing last element of array

seeds.t: @export requires explicit type annotation
seeds.w: @export var speed = 5.0 — without a type annotation the Inspector shows a generic Variant field.
seeds.c: @export var speed: float = 5.0. Type annotations unlock proper Inspector widgets (sliders for floats, colour pickers for Color, etc.) and enable export hints like @export_range(0, 100).
seeds.s: Inspector shows wrong widget type or no widget at all.

seeds.t: preload() is compile-time, load() is runtime
seeds.w: preload(some_variable) — variables are not allowed.
seeds.c: use preload() for known static assets, load() for dynamic or user-generated paths.
seeds.s: 'Expected constant expression' error with preload.

seeds.t: Accumulate delta for time-based logic, don't assume frame rate
seeds.w: using a frame counter for timing (frame_count += 1; if frame_count > 60).
seeds.c: accumulate delta time — time += delta; if time >= 1.0: time = 0.0; do_thing(). Frame rate is not guaranteed; delta-based timing works correctly at any FPS.
seeds.s: behaviour runs faster on high-end machines and slower on low-end ones.

seeds.t: Normalise direction vectors before applying speed
seeds.w: velocity = Vector2(input_x, input_y) * speed — diagonal movement is faster than cardinal (magnitude is √2 ≈ 1.41 instead of 1).
seeds.c: velocity = Vector2(input_x, input_y).normalized() * speed. Always check for zero vector before normalising: if input != Vector2.ZERO: velocity = input.normalized() * speed.
seeds.s: character moves faster diagonally.

seeds.t: Use push_error() and push_warning() for structured errors
seeds.w: print('ERROR: something went wrong') — only appears in output, not caught by error handlers.
seeds.c: push_error('description') for errors and push_warning('description') for warnings. These appear in the Debugger panel, can be caught by error handlers, and show the correct stack trace. Use assert(condition, 'message') for debug-only invariant checks that are stripped in release…
seeds.s: AudioStreamPlayer does not play on first call without a prior user gesture in HTML export

seeds.t: for i in range() is inclusive of start, exclusive of end
seeds.w: for i in range(1, count+1) when you meant 0-indexed.
seeds.c: for i in range(count) for 0-indexed, for i in range(1, count+1) for 1-indexed.
seeds.s: off-by-one errors in loops.

seeds.t: Use $NodeName shorthand or @onready, not get_node() strings
seeds.w: get_node('UI/Panel/Label') throughout code — brittle string paths break silently when scene structure changes.
seeds.c: use @onready var _label: Label = $UI/Panel/Label at the top of the script to cache the reference once. $NodeName is syntactic sugar for get_node('NodeName').
seeds.s: 'Node not found' errors after scene refactoring.

seeds.t: Use Callable.bind() to pass arguments to signal callbacks
seeds.w: connecting a signal to a method that needs arguments by calling the method directly — signals call callbacks with the signal's own parameters only.
seeds.c: use .bind() to pre-bind extra arguments: button.pressed.connect(on_pressed.bind(item_data)). The bound arguments are appended after the signal's own arguments.
seeds.s: callback receives wrong number of arguments, or extra data is unavailable in the handler.

seeds.t: class_name makes a class globally accessible — name carefully
seeds.w: generic names like Manager, Controller, Data — these collide easily and can shadow built-in types.
seeds.c: use project-specific prefixes or descriptive names. class_name is only needed if the class must be referenced by name elsewhere; omit it for internal implementation scripts.
seeds.s: 'Class name already defined' error, or wrong class used silently.

seeds.t: Use is_instance_valid() before accessing potentially freed objects
seeds.w: if obj != null: obj.do_thing() — a freed object is not null in GDScript; accessing it crashes.
seeds.c: if is_instance_valid(obj): obj.do_thing(). Use this whenever an object might have been freed between when the reference was captured and when it is used, especially in async code or after timers.
seeds.s: 'Attempt to call function on a previously freed instance'.

seeds.t: Integer division truncates in GDScript — cast to float explicitly
seeds.w: var result = 5 / 2 — both operands are int, result is 2 (truncated).
seeds.c: var result = 5.0 / 2 or float(5) / 2 to get 2.5. GDScript infers the type of literals — 5 is int, 5.0 is float. This is a common source of subtle bugs in percentage calculations and normalisation.
seeds.s: unexpected integer result from division that should produce a decimal.

seeds.t: Never Force-Push to a Shared Branch
seeds.w: Running git push --force on main, develop, or any branch others are working on — this rewrites remote history, orphaning teammates' commits and requiring manual intervention to recover.
seeds.c: Use git push --force-with-lease instead, which only force-pushes if no one else has pushed since your last fetch, preventing accidental overwrites. Reserve force-push for your own unreviewed feature branches.
seeds.s: teammate's local branch diverges from remote with 'histories have diverged' after your push.

seeds.t: Rebase Feature Branches; Merge Into Shared Branches
seeds.w: Merging main into a feature branch repeatedly creating a tangled merge-commit history, or rebasing after a PR has been reviewed, changing commit SHAs unexpectedly.
seeds.c: Rebase your feature branch onto main/develop before opening a PR to keep history linear (git rebase main). Once a branch is shared or reviewed, use merge to integrate. Use git pull --rebase instead of git pull to avoid spurious merge commits when pulling updates.
seeds.s: git log showing dozens of 'Merge branch main into feature/x' commits that add no information.

seeds.t: reflog Recovers Any Seemingly Lost Commit
seeds.w: Panicking and assuming commits are permanently lost after a bad reset, rebase, or accidental branch deletion — Git keeps all commits for 90 days by default.
seeds.c: Run git reflog to see a timestamped history of every HEAD movement; find the SHA of the lost commit and git checkout <sha> or git cherry-pick <sha> to recover it. For a deleted branch, git checkout -b recovered-branch <sha> recreates it.
seeds.s: 'I lost all my work after a rebase gone wrong' — almost always recoverable via reflog.

seeds.t: Name Your Stashes for Later Identification
seeds.w: Running git stash without a message and accumulating a stack of unnamed stashes (stash@{0}, stash@{1}) — impossible to identify which stash contains what work.
seeds.c: Use git stash push -m 'WIP: payment form validation' to name the stash. List with git stash list; apply a specific one with git stash apply stash@{2}. Use git stash pop to apply and drop, git stash drop to discard.
seeds.s: running git stash list and seeing five stashes with no idea which contains the relevant work.

seeds.t: Use Interactive Rebase to Clean Up Before Merging
seeds.w: Merging a PR with 23 commits like 'fix', 'fix again', 'typo', 'actually fix it' — pollutes project history and makes git bisect and blame useless.
seeds.c: Before opening a PR, run git rebase -i main to squash, fixup, and reorder commits into logical, atomic units. Use fixup! commits (git commit --fixup=<sha>) and git rebase -i --autosquash to automate squashing. Aim for each commit to represent one self-contained change.
seeds.s: git blame on a line shows 'fix' with no context about why the change was made.

seeds.t: gitignore Does Not Affect Already-Tracked Files
seeds.w: Adding a file pattern to .gitignore and being confused that the file still appears in git status — .gitignore only prevents untracked files from being tracked; it does not remove files already in the index.
seeds.c: To stop tracking a file that is already committed: git rm --cached <file>, then commit the removal, then add to .gitignore. For directories: git rm -r --cached <dir>.
seeds.s: .env or node_modules showing as modified in git status after adding them to .gitignore.

seeds.t: Use git bisect to Find the Commit That Introduced a Bug
seeds.w: Manually checking out commits one-by-one to find when a bug was introduced — slow and error-prone.
seeds.c: Use git bisect start, then git bisect bad (current commit) and git bisect good <sha> (last known good); Git performs a binary search through history, checking out commits for you to test. Mark each as good or bad; Git finds the first bad commit in O(log n) steps. Automate with g…
seeds.s: 'this bug appeared some time in the last 200 commits' — bisect finds it in 8 steps.

seeds.t: Cherry-Pick Specific Commits Rather Than Porting Changes Manually
seeds.w: Manually copying changes from one branch to another by re-writing the code — error-prone, loses authorship, and diverges from the original.
seeds.c: Use git cherry-pick <sha> to apply a specific commit from any branch onto the current branch. Cherry-pick preserves the commit message and authorship (adding a cherry-picked-from note). For a range: git cherry-pick A..B. Use --no-commit to stage the changes for review before com…
seeds.s: copy-pasted fixes between branches diverging over time.

seeds.t: Use git worktree for Parallel Branch Work Without Stashing
seeds.w: Stashing uncommitted work every time you need to switch branches to fix a hotfix — disruptive and error-prone for large uncommitted changesets.
seeds.c: Use git worktree add ../project-hotfix hotfix/issue-42 to check out a different branch in a separate directory simultaneously. Both worktrees share the same repository; changes in one are immediately available in the other. Remove when done: git worktree remove ../project-hotfix.
seeds.s: frequent stash/unstash cycles to switch between feature and hotfix work.

seeds.t: Follow Conventional Commits for Meaningful History
seeds.w: Writing commit messages like 'fix', 'update', 'changes', or 'WIP' — useless for code review, changelog generation, and future blame.
seeds.c: Follow the Conventional Commits spec: type(scope): short description with a blank line before the body. Types: feat, fix, docs, style, refactor, test, chore. The subject line should complete 'if applied, this commit will...'. Tools like commitlint enforce this in CI.
seeds.s: CHANGELOG generation is useless because all commits are 'fix' or 'update'.

seeds.t: Understand the Three git reset Modes
seeds.w: Using git reset --hard without understanding what it does — this discards both the commit and all working directory changes, with no undo except reflog.
seeds.c: --soft resets the branch pointer only (changes remain staged); --mixed (default) unstages changes but keeps them in the working tree; --hard discards everything. For undoing a pushed commit safely, prefer git revert which creates a new commit that undoes the change.
seeds.s: accidentally discarding hours of uncommitted work with --hard.

seeds.t: Stage Partial Changes With git add -p
seeds.w: Committing all changes in a file together even when they represent different logical concerns — creates noisy commits that are hard to review and revert selectively.
seeds.c: Use git add -p (patch mode) to interactively stage individual hunks within a file, splitting unrelated changes into separate commits. Use git add -i for a fuller interactive interface. Each commit should represent exactly one logical change.
seeds.s: git blame shows a commit that changed 15 unrelated things, making it impossible to revert just one.

seeds.t: Submodules Require Explicit Update After Cloning
seeds.w: Cloning a repository with submodules and finding empty subdirectories — git clone does not initialise submodules by default.
seeds.c: Clone with git clone --recurse-submodules, or run git submodule update --init --recursive after cloning. Update submodules to their latest tracked commit with git submodule update --remote. Understand that submodule pointers must be committed when the submodule is updated.
seeds.s: empty submodule directories, build failures because dependency code is missing.

seeds.t: Ignore Formatting Commits in git blame
seeds.w: Running git blame on a file and seeing that every line was last changed by a formatting/linting commit, obscuring who actually wrote the logic.
seeds.c: Create a .git-blame-ignore-revs file listing bulk-formatting commit SHAs and configure it: git config blame.ignoreRevsFile .git-blame-ignore-revs. GitHub reads this file automatically. Commit the file to the repository so all team members benefit.
seeds.s: git blame useless because a Prettier commit last touched every line.

seeds.t: Use Aliases for Readable git log Output
seeds.w: Running git log and getting a verbose paragraph format for each commit that makes history hard to scan.
seeds.c: Add a graph alias: git config --global alias.lg 'log --oneline --graph --decorate --all'. The --graph flag shows branch/merge topology; --oneline compresses to one line per commit; --decorate shows branch and tag labels. Save as a shell alias or git alias to use across all repos…
seeds.s: git log output impossible to navigate for anything more than a handful of commits.

seeds.t: Use Git Hooks to Enforce Quality at Commit Time
seeds.w: Relying only on CI to catch lint errors and test failures — developers push broken code and wait minutes for CI feedback before knowing there's an issue.
seeds.c: Configure pre-commit hooks with husky + lint-staged (Node) or pre-commit (Python) to run linters and formatters only on staged files before each commit. Use commit-msg hooks to enforce commit message format. Keep hooks fast (under 10 seconds) to avoid friction.
seeds.s: CI fails on trivial lint errors that a pre-commit hook would have caught instantly.

seeds.t: Use Sparse Checkout for Large Monorepo Performance
seeds.w: Cloning a large monorepo entirely when working on only one service — cloning terabytes of history and files for one subdirectory is slow and wasteful.
seeds.c: Use git sparse-checkout init --cone and git sparse-checkout set services/payments to check out only the relevant directories. Combine with --filter=blob:none (blobless clone) or --depth=1 (shallow clone) for maximum speed. Ideal for CI jobs that build one service.
seeds.s: multi-minute clone times in CI pipelines that only need one service's files.

seeds.t: Tag Releases With Annotated Tags Not Lightweight Tags
seeds.w: Using lightweight tags (git tag v1.2.0) for releases — lightweight tags are just pointers with no metadata; they do not carry a tagger, date, or message, and git describe behaviour differs.
seeds.c: Use annotated tags: git tag -a v1.2.0 -m 'Release 1.2.0'. Annotated tags are full Git objects with a tagger identity and timestamp; they are pushed separately with git push --follow-tags or git push origin v1.2.0.
seeds.s: git describe output inconsistent, or release tags missing author and date information in the repository.

seeds.t: Use git filter-repo Not filter-branch for History Rewriting
seeds.w: Using git filter-branch to remove a secret or large file from history — filter-branch is extremely slow, has dangerous defaults, and is officially deprecated.
seeds.c: Use git filter-repo (install separately) to rewrite history: git filter-repo --path secret.key --invert-paths removes a file from all commits. Then force-push all branches and require all collaborators to re-clone. Immediately rotate any exposed credentials.
seeds.s: needing to purge a committed .env file or a binary blob from all history.

seeds.t: Use .gitattributes to Normalise Line Endings
seeds.w: Sharing a repository across Windows and Unix developers without a .gitattributes file — Windows commits CRLF line endings that Unix developers see as changes, and vice versa, causing perpetual diffs.
seeds.c: Add a .gitattributes file with * text=auto to let Git normalise line endings on checkout per platform. Mark binary files explicitly: *.png binary. Commit the file early; run git add --renormalize . to fix existing files.
seeds.s: entire files showing as changed in git diff because of invisible line-ending differences.

seeds.t: Undo the last commit without losing your changes
seeds.w: git reset --hard HEAD~1 — this discards your changes permanently.
seeds.c: git reset --soft HEAD~1 keeps your changes staged and ready to re-commit. git reset HEAD~1 (no flag) keeps changes unstaged but present. Only use --hard if you are certain you want to discard the changes entirely.
seeds.s: accidentally committed the wrong files, or want to rewrite the commit message.

seeds.t: Adding a file to .gitignore does not untrack it if already committed
seeds.w: adding a file to .gitignore and expecting it to disappear from the repo — .gitignore only prevents untracked files from being added. If the file was already committed, git still tracks it.
seeds.c: run git rm --cached filename to stop tracking without deleting the local file, then commit the removal. After that, .gitignore prevents it from being re-added.
seeds.s: secrets or large files remain in the repo despite being in .gitignore.

seeds.t: Always pull before pushing to avoid rejected pushes
seeds.w: pushing changes when the remote has commits you don't have locally — git rejects the push with 'failed to push, tip of your current branch is behind'.
seeds.c: run git pull --rebase origin main before pushing to incorporate remote changes. --rebase keeps history linear by replaying your commits on top of the remote changes rather than creating a merge commit.
seeds.s: 'rejected, non-fast-forward' error on git push.

seeds.t: Write commit messages in imperative present tense
seeds.w: 'Fixed the bug' or 'Fixing scroll layout' — past or present participle tense is inconsistent with git's own generated messages.
seeds.c: 'Fix scroll layout', 'Add seed file for React', 'Update deploy workflow'. The convention is imperative mood: reads as 'This commit will [message]'. Keep the first line under 72 characters. Add a blank line and more detail below if needed.
seeds.s: inconsistent history that is harder to read and search.

seeds.t: Secrets committed to git history are compromised — rotate immediately
seeds.w: committing an API key, password, or token and then deleting it in the next commit — the secret is still in the git history and visible to anyone with repo access.
seeds.c: rotate the secret immediately (invalidate the old one, generate a new one). Use git filter-repo or BFG Repo Cleaner to scrub history. For prevention: use environment variables, .env files listed in .gitignore, and GitHub Actions secrets for CI.
seeds.s: accidental commit of credentials, .env file, or private key.

seeds.t: GitHub Pages URLs are case-sensitive
seeds.w: linking to docs/Overview.html when the file is named docs/overview.html — works locally on Mac, 404 on GitHub Pages.
seeds.c: use consistent lowercase filenames for all web assets, and match the case exactly in all links and imports.
seeds.s: page works locally but returns 404 on GitHub Pages.

seeds.t: Merge preserves history, rebase keeps it linear
seeds.w: rebasing commits that have already been pushed and shared — it rewrites history and forces others to reconcile.
seeds.c: use merge for feature branches going into main (clear record of the feature); use rebase for pulling in upstream changes to your working branch (keeps your changes on top without noise).
seeds.s: confusing history, or 'refusing to merge unrelated histories'.

seeds.t: Interactive rebase cleans up commits before merging
seeds.w: merging a branch full of 'WIP', 'fix typo', and 'oops' commits into main — pollutes the log and makes git log and git bisect harder.
seeds.c: before opening a PR, run git rebase -i HEAD~N (where N is the number of commits to clean). Use squash (s) to combine into the previous commit, reword (r) to rename a commit message, and fixup (f) to squash discarding the message. Force-push the cleaned branch: git push --force-w…
seeds.s: main branch history has dozens of 'fix' commits per feature that interactive rebase would have squashed.

seeds.t: cherry-pick applies a single commit to another branch
seeds.w: merging an entire branch just to get one bug fix commit onto another branch — pulls in unwanted work-in-progress commits.
seeds.c: git cherry-pick <commit-hash> applies only that commit to the current branch. Cherry-pick multiple commits: git cherry-pick A^..B for a range. If there's a conflict, resolve and git cherry-pick --continue. Use --no-commit to stage the changes without committing: inspect first, t…
seeds.s: merging an entire feature branch just to backport a single fix to a release branch.

seeds.t: Reusable workflows eliminate copy-paste across repositories
seeds.w: copying the same CI workflow YAML into every repository in an organisation — when the standard pipeline changes, every copy must be updated manually.
seeds.c: create a reusable workflow in a central repo with on: workflow_call and inputs/secrets parameters. Call it from other repos: uses: myorg/workflows/.github/workflows/ci.yml@main with: node-version: '20'. The calling workflow can pass inputs and secrets. Centralising the pipeline …
seeds.s: 20 repositories each with a copy of the same CI YAML that needs to be updated one by one.

seeds.t: Cache dependencies in Actions to dramatically speed up builds
seeds.w: running npm install or pip install from scratch on every CI run — downloading and installing all dependencies is often the slowest part of a build.
seeds.c: use actions/cache@v4 keyed on the lockfile hash: key: ${{ runner.os }}-npm-${{ hashFiles('**/package-lock.json') }}. The cache is restored on cache hit, skipping installation; on miss the workflow installs and saves the cache. For Node.js, actions/setup-node has built-in caching…
seeds.s: CI pipeline takes 4 minutes where 3 minutes is npm install that could be cached.

seeds.t: Protect main branch with required reviews and status checks
seeds.w: allowing direct pushes to main — a single typo or untested change can break production for the whole team.
seeds.c: enable branch protection rules: require at least 1 pull request review, require status checks (CI) to pass before merging, and enable 'Require branches to be up to date'. Optionally require signed commits. Enable 'Dismiss stale reviews' so approved PRs aren't merged after new ch…
seeds.s: someone pushes directly to main breaking the build, or a PR is merged after CI fails.

seeds.t: CODEOWNERS auto-requests reviews from the right team
seeds.w: manually remembering to tag the right person for review based on which files changed — easy to forget and causes PRs to sit without the right reviewer.
seeds.c: create .github/CODEOWNERS with patterns: /frontend/ @org/frontend-team, *.go @alice @bob. GitHub automatically adds the matching owners as required reviewers when those paths are changed in a PR. Combine with branch protection 'Require review from Code Owners'. Owner patterns ar…
seeds.s: security-sensitive files get reviewed by the wrong team because CODEOWNERS isn't configured.

seeds.t: Enable Dependabot to keep dependencies automatically updated
seeds.w: manually reviewing and updating dependencies infrequently — dependencies drift, security vulnerabilities accumulate, and major version upgrades become large painful projects.
seeds.c: add .github/dependabot.yml: package-ecosystem: npm, directory: '/', schedule: interval: weekly. Dependabot opens PRs for each dependency update; merge the ones that pass CI. Enable security updates separately under Settings > Security. Group minor/patch updates to reduce PR nois…
seeds.s: project running packages with known CVEs because no automated process flags updates.

seeds.t: Use Environments to scope secrets and require deployment approvals
seeds.w: storing production secrets as repository-level secrets accessible to every branch and every workflow run — any PR could exfiltrate prod credentials.
seeds.c: create Environments (Settings > Environments) named production, staging. Add environment secrets accessible only to deployments targeting that environment. Configure required reviewers so deployments to production require manual approval. Reference in workflows: environment: pro…
seeds.s: production database URL stored as a repo secret accessible in every PR's workflow run.

seeds.t: shader_type declaration is mandatory in Godot shaders
seeds.w: omitting shader_type or using GLSL without it — Godot rejects the shader entirely.
seeds.c: shader_type canvas_item; as the first line for 2D shaders. The shader_type determines which built-in variables (UV, COLOR, VERTEX, etc.) are available.
seeds.s: 'Shader compilation failed: shader_type not found'.

seeds.t: GLSL requires float literals to have a decimal point
seeds.w: float x = 1 / 2 — integer division returns 0 in GLSL, not 0.5.
seeds.c: float x = 1.0 / 2.0. All float literals must include a decimal point. This differs from GDScript where 1.0 and 1 are both valid in float context.
seeds.s: unexpected 0.0 or integer results from arithmetic that should produce a decimal.

seeds.t: Uniforms are set from code, varyings pass data between vertex and fragment
seeds.w: trying to pass per-vertex data to the fragment shader via a uniform — uniforms are constant per draw call.
seeds.c: declare varying vec2 my_uv and assign in vertex(), read in fragment().
seeds.s: per-vertex data appears constant across the surface.

seeds.t: UV origin is top-left in Godot canvas_item shaders
seeds.w: assuming UV (0,0) is bottom-left as in OpenGL — effects like gradients or texture lookups will be vertically flipped.
seeds.c: to flip Y for effects that expect bottom-origin coordinates: vec2 flipped_uv = vec2(UV.x, 1.0 - UV.y).
seeds.s: textures or gradients appear vertically mirrored.

seeds.t: Use texture() not texture2D() in Godot shaders
seeds.w: texture2D(my_texture, UV) — this is GLSL 1.2 syntax, not supported in Godot's GLSL ES 3.0 shaders.
seeds.c: texture(my_texture, UV) is the correct function in Godot shaders. Declare the sampler as uniform sampler2D my_texture : source_color. The source_color hint tells Godot the texture is in sRGB space and should be converted to linear.
seeds.s: 'Identifier not found: texture2D' shader compilation error.

seeds.t: render_mode controls blending and culling behaviour
seeds.w: reimplementing additive blending manually in the fragment shader — it's slower and may not compositing correctly.
seeds.c: render_mode blend_add; at the top of the shader. Multiple modes can be combined: render_mode unshaded, blend_add;
seeds.s: shader produces black output on mobile despite working correctly in editor

seeds.t: Use TIME built-in for animation — do not pass time as a uniform
seeds.w: passing time from GDScript as a uniform every frame to animate shaders — this requires a _process call and uniform update every frame.
seeds.c: use the built-in TIME variable which Godot updates automatically. TIME is a float in seconds since the game started. Use sin(TIME * speed) for oscillating effects.
seeds.s: shader animation requires GDScript scaffolding that could be eliminated.

seeds.t: Use mediump not highp for mobile shader performance
seeds.w: using highp throughout mobile-targeted shaders — causes unnecessary GPU load.
seeds.c: declare precision mediump float; at the top of canvas_item shaders targeting mobile. Use highp only for values requiring full precision (world-space positions, large coordinate ranges).
seeds.s: poor shader performance on mobile despite simple effects.

seeds.t: Pass data from vertex to fragment using varying variables
seeds.w: trying to compute a value in the vertex function and directly use it in fragment without a varying — vertex and fragment functions do not share scope.
seeds.c: declare varying vec2 my_uv; at shader top level (outside both functions), assign in vertex: my_uv = UV; read in fragment: color = texture(my_texture, my_uv). In Godot's canvas_item shaders, varyings are also used for world-space positions or custom data interpolation.
seeds.s: value computed in vertex() is undefined when accessed in fragment().

seeds.t: Reconstruct world position in fragment from depth and INV_VIEW_MATRIX
seeds.w: passing world position as a varying from vertex — this works but interpolates in clip space incorrectly for perspective-correct effects.
seeds.c: in a spatial fragment shader, reconstruct world position from depth: vec4 ndc = vec4(FRAGCOORD.xy / VIEWPORT_SIZE * 2.0 - 1.0, FRAGCOORD.z * 2.0 - 1.0, 1.0); vec4 world = INV_PROJECTION_MATRIX * ndc; world /= world.w; world = INV_VIEW_MATRIX * world. Use VERTEX (varying) for sim…
seeds.s: world-space effect has visible seams at triangle edges because position was interpolated in screen space.

seeds.t: Use EMISSION for unlit colours; ALBEDO is multiplied by light
seeds.w: setting ALBEDO for elements that should glow or be unaffected by scene lighting — ALBEDO is modulated by light; in dark areas it appears dark.
seeds.c: set EMISSION = colour to add an additive, lighting-independent colour contribution. For a pure flat colour ignoring all lighting, use render_mode unshaded and set ALBEDO. For bloom-contributing glow, set both ALBEDO and EMISSION. EMISSION_ENERGY scales the emission intensity for…
seeds.s: a glowing UI element or energy beam darkens in shadow because ALBEDO was used instead of EMISSION.

seeds.t: NORMAL is in view space in spatial shaders — transform for world space
seeds.w: using NORMAL directly for world-space lighting calculations — NORMAL in Godot spatial fragment shaders is in view space, not world space.
seeds.c: to get world-space normal, transform with: vec3 world_normal = normalize((INV_VIEW_MATRIX * vec4(NORMAL, 0.0)).xyz). For normal maps, use NORMAL_MAP to assign the sampled normal map texture; Godot handles the TBN transform automatically when NORMAL_MAP is assigned.
seeds.s: lighting calculation appears to rotate with the camera because NORMAL was used in world-space math without transformation.

seeds.t: Disable depth_draw for transparent objects to avoid z-fighting
seeds.w: using default depth writing for transparent or alpha-blended materials — they write to the depth buffer and occlude geometry drawn after them even where fully transparent.
seeds.c: set render_mode blend_mix, depth_draw_opaque or depth_draw_never for transparent materials. For particles and foliage, depth_draw_opaque writes depth only for opaque pixels. For sorted transparency, depth_draw_never and ensure draw order is correct in the scene.
seeds.s: transparent mesh cuts a hole in geometry behind it because it writes to the depth buffer.

seeds.t: Use instance uniforms for per-instance variation without new materials
seeds.w: creating a separate ShaderMaterial per object instance to vary a colour or value — causes a draw call per material and high memory use.
seeds.c: declare instance uniform vec4 instance_color : source_color = vec4(1.0); in the shader. Set per-instance values in GDScript: mesh_instance.set_instance_shader_parameter('instance_color', Color(1,0,0)). Instance uniforms allow one material shared by thousands of instances with pe…
seeds.s: hundreds of enemies each with their own ShaderMaterial varying only by colour when instance uniforms would allow one shared material.

seeds.t: Use smoothstep and mix for smooth transitions instead of branching
seeds.w: using if/else or step() to create hard transitions in shaders — if/else causes branch divergence across GPU threads, and step() produces hard edges.
seeds.c: use smoothstep(edge0, edge1, x) for a smooth S-curve transition between two values, and mix(a, b, t) for linear interpolation. Combine: float edge = smoothstep(0.4, 0.6, dist); ALBEDO = mix(inner_color, outer_color, edge). GPU hardware executes mix and smoothstep in a single cyc…
seeds.s: visible aliased hard edge in a shader effect that smoothstep would smooth, or an if/else branch causing warp divergence.

seeds.t: COLOR in canvas_item fragment is pre-multiplied by node modulate
seeds.w: setting ALBEDO or ignoring COLOR in canvas_item shaders — the canvas_item shader type uses COLOR for output, not ALBEDO, and COLOR is initialised with the node's modulate colour.
seeds.c: in canvas_item fragment: COLOR = texture(TEXTURE, UV) * COLOR. This preserves the node's CanvasItem modulate tint. If you want to ignore modulate, assign a fixed colour directly: COLOR = vec4(1.0). Discarding COLOR.a discards the node's alpha modulate, including Tween alpha anim…
seeds.s: shader ignores the node's modulate colour or alpha animations because COLOR was overwritten instead of multiplied.

seeds.t: Use SCREEN_TEXTURE carefully — it captures state before transparent objects
seeds.w: sampling SCREEN_TEXTURE in a transparent spatial shader and expecting to see other transparent objects behind it — SCREEN_TEXTURE is captured before the transparent pass, so other transparent objects are not included.
seeds.c: SCREEN_TEXTURE provides the opaque scene colour. For glass or lens effects, this is correct — it refacts the opaque scene. For effects requiring fully composited transparency, use a SubViewport with custom render layering. Require hint_screen_texture on the uniform: uniform samp…
seeds.s: glass material doesn't show other transparent objects because SCREEN_TEXTURE only captures opaque geometry.

seeds.t: The light() function runs per-light — don't do heavy work there
seeds.w: performing expensive texture samples or complex math inside the light() function of a spatial shader — light() is invoked once per visible light affecting the object, multiplying GPU cost.
seeds.c: do expensive pre-computation in fragment() and store in varying or global variables accessible via global uniform. In light(), only do the per-light accumulation: DIFFUSE_LIGHT += clamp(dot(NORMAL, LIGHT), 0.0, 1.0) * ATTENUATION * precomputed_value.
seeds.s: shader is very fast with no lights but slows significantly with 4+ lights because heavy work runs inside light().

seeds.t: Goroutines leak if not given a way to exit
seeds.w: starting a goroutine that blocks on a channel read or infinite loop with no cancellation path — it lives for the lifetime of the program.
seeds.c: pass a context.Context and select on ctx.Done() to exit cleanly. Use defer cancel() immediately after context.WithCancel().
seeds.s: memory grows unboundedly, goroutine count climbs under load.

seeds.t: Wrap errors with %w to preserve the chain
seeds.w: returning errors unchanged or concatenated as strings — callers lose the ability to inspect the chain.
seeds.c: fmt.Errorf('operation failed: %w', err). Use errors.Is() to check for specific errors, errors.As() to extract a type. The %w verb (not %v) enables unwrapping.
seeds.s: errors.Is() always returns false.

seeds.t: A nil concrete value inside an interface is not nil
seeds.w: returning a nil pointer of a concrete type as an interface and checking if err != nil — an interface holds both type and value; a nil pointer stored in an interface is not a nil interface.
seeds.c: return nil directly as the interface type.
seeds.s: if err != nil passes even though the underlying value is nil.

seeds.t: Loop variable capture in goroutines — Go 1.21 and earlier
seeds.w: launching goroutines inside a for loop referencing the loop variable — all goroutines share the same variable and see its final value (Go 1.21 and earlier).
seeds.c: pass the loop variable as an argument: go func(v item) { ... }(v). Go 1.22+ creates a new variable per iteration.
seeds.s: all goroutines process the same last item.

seeds.t: defer in a loop defers until function return
seeds.w: using defer inside a for loop expecting cleanup at end of each iteration — defer runs when the enclosing function returns, holding resources for the entire loop.
seeds.c: wrap the loop body in an anonymous function: func() { defer f.Close(); ... }().
seeds.s: file handles or locks exhaust before the loop finishes.

seeds.t: Maps are not safe for concurrent access
seeds.w: reading and writing a map from multiple goroutines without synchronisation — Go maps are not thread-safe and concurrent access causes a fatal runtime panic.
seeds.c: use sync.Mutex or sync.RWMutex, or use sync.Map for concurrent cases.
seeds.s: 'concurrent map read and map write' fatal error under load.

seeds.t: append may or may not create a new underlying array
seeds.w: assuming append always returns the same slice — if capacity is insufficient, append allocates a new array and the original variable is unaffected.
seeds.c: always assign the result back: s = append(s, item). Two slices sharing a backing array will interfere after append if capacity is not exceeded.
seeds.s: items lost after append, or unexpected mutations between slices.

seeds.t: context.Context is always the first function argument
seeds.w: storing context in a struct field and passing it implicitly.
seeds.c: pass ctx explicitly through the call chain. This makes cancellation propagation visible and auditable.
seeds.s: cancellation does not propagate, or context.TODO() scattered throughout production code.

seeds.t: Struct embedding promotes methods but not constructors
seeds.w: embedding a type that requires explicit setup (e.g. sync.Mutex) and forgetting to initialise it — a zero-value sync.Mutex is valid, but embedded types with non-zero defaults need explicit init.
seeds.c: always initialise embedded types in the constructor.
seeds.s: embedded type panics or misbehaves because it was not properly set up.

seeds.t: Design types so the zero value is useful
seeds.w: requiring a constructor call before any method can be used safely — callers will forget.
seeds.c: ensure zero value is a valid, safe state. Document if a constructor is required.
seeds.s: nil pointer panics when callers use the type without calling New().

seeds.t: Name return values only for documentation, not for naked returns
seeds.w: using named returns with naked return statements in long functions — it makes control flow opaque and is a common source of bugs.
seeds.c: name returns for clarity or defer patterns; always use explicit return values.
seeds.s: functions return wrong values silently after refactoring.

seeds.t: Declare channel direction in function signatures
seeds.w: passing chan T to functions that should only send or only receive — any accidental operation in the wrong direction is silently allowed.
seeds.c: use directional channels: chan<- T for send-only, <-chan T for receive-only. The compiler enforces the direction, preventing accidental misuse.
seeds.s: goroutine accidentally reads from a channel it should only write to, causing a deadlock.

seeds.t: init() runs before main and cannot be controlled — use sparingly
seeds.w: using init() for setup that can fail, has side effects, or needs to be tested — it cannot return errors and cannot be called directly in tests.
seeds.c: use explicit initialisation functions called from main().
seeds.s: subtle ordering bugs, initialisation errors that cannot be handled, or tests that cannot control setup.

seeds.t: Never copy a sync.Mutex after first use
seeds.w: passing sync.Mutex by value, assigning a struct containing a Mutex to another variable, or returning a struct with a Mutex by value — the copy creates a separate, unlocked mutex that does not protect the same data.
seeds.c: use a pointer to the struct containing the Mutex: func NewThing() *Thing. go vet detects this with the 'copylock' analyser.
seeds.s: data race not caught by the mutex, or deadlock because lock and data are in different copies.

seeds.t: Use strings.Builder for string concatenation in loops
seeds.w: str += piece inside a loop — each concatenation allocates a new string, making the operation O(n²) in total allocations.
seeds.c: use strings.Builder: var b strings.Builder; b.WriteString(piece); result := b.String(). For known size, call b.Grow(n) upfront to avoid reallocations.
seeds.s: string building is slow, profiler shows excessive allocations in a loop.

seeds.t: Declare sentinel errors as package-level vars
seeds.w: returning fmt.Errorf('not found') each time — callers cannot reliably compare against it.
seeds.c: declare once at package level, return the same value, wrap with %w when adding context. Export sentinels (ErrXxx) for callers to check against.
seeds.s: errors.Is(err, ErrNotFound) always returns false despite the error message matching.

seeds.t: Call WaitGroup.Add() before launching the goroutine
seeds.w: calling wg.Add(1) inside the goroutine — there is a race between launching the goroutine and calling wg.Wait() where Wait may return before Add is called.
seeds.c: call wg.Add(1) in the goroutine's parent, immediately before go func() { defer wg.Done(); ... }().
seeds.s: wg.Wait() returns before all goroutines finish, causing data races or incomplete work.

seeds.t: Panics in goroutines crash the whole program if not recovered
seeds.w: launching goroutines without panic recovery — a panic in any goroutine that is not recovered terminates the entire process.
seeds.c: add defer func() { if r := recover(); r != nil { log.Printf('goroutine panic: %v', r) } }() at the top of long-lived goroutines. For worker pools, add recovery in the worker function.
seeds.s: server crashes entirely due to a panic in one request handler.

seeds.t: Use override void _Ready() not constructors for node init
seeds.w: initialising node-dependent state in the C# constructor — the node is not yet in the scene tree at construction time.
