seeds.c: override _Ready() for all node-dependent initialisation. The constructor runs before the node is added to the tree; _Ready() runs after. C# constructors in Godot nodes should only set pure C# state with no Godot API calls.
seeds.s: NullReferenceException when accessing child nodes in the constructor.

seeds.t: [Export] attribute exposes properties to the Inspector
seeds.w: making a field public expecting it to appear in the Inspector — only fields and properties with [Export] are exposed.
seeds.c: [Export] public float Speed { get; set; } = 100.0f. For node references: [Export] public NodePath TargetPath { get; set; }. Supported types are the same as GDScript export types.
seeds.s: property not visible in Inspector despite being public.

seeds.t: Godot object null checks require IsInstanceValid()
seeds.w: if (obj != null) on a Godot Object that may have been freed — destroyed Godot objects are not C# null; they are invalid instances.
seeds.c: use Godot.Object.IsInstanceValid(obj) before accessing a potentially freed object. For C# nullable reference types, also check obj is not null first.
seeds.s: ObjectDisposedException or engine crash when accessing a destroyed node.

seeds.t: Use GetNode<T>() for typed node access
seeds.w: GetNode('Label') returning an untyped Node requiring a cast.
seeds.c: GetNode<Label>('Label') returns a typed Label directly. For @onready equivalent: [Export] public NodePath LabelPath; and in _Ready: _label = GetNode<Label>(LabelPath). Or use the $ shorthand via GetNode<Label>('%UniqueNamedNode').
seeds.s: InvalidCastException or loss of type safety when accessing child nodes.

seeds.t: Godot C# async must return to main thread for scene operations
seeds.w: performing scene tree operations after an await that resumed on a thread pool thread — Godot's scene tree is not thread-safe.
seeds.c: use await ToSignal(GetTree(), 'process_frame') instead of Task.Delay() to stay on the main thread, or explicitly marshal back with Callable.From(action).CallDeferred().
seeds.s: random crashes or corrupted state after async operations that touch the scene tree.

seeds.t: Implement IDisposable for C# resources held by Godot nodes
seeds.w: holding C# managed resources (file handles, HttpClient, timers) in a Godot node without cleanup — _ExitTree() or QueueFree() does not call IDisposable.Dispose() automatically.
seeds.c: override _ExitTree() to clean up managed resources, or implement the full IDisposable pattern. Alternatively: prefer Godot's own resource types which are managed by the engine.
seeds.s: resource leaks, file handles not released after scene changes.

seeds.t: Rebuild the C# solution after adding new classes
seeds.w: expecting new C# classes to appear in the editor without building — the editor only sees compiled assemblies.
seeds.c: after adding a new C# script or class, use Build → Build Solution (or press Ctrl+B) to compile. The editor hot-reloads the assembly automatically after a successful build. If IntelliSense is broken: close and reopen the solution in your IDE after Godot generates new bindings.
seeds.s: new script not appearing in 'Attach Script' dialog, or editor showing stale class information.

seeds.t: Godot uses float vectors — use Godot.Vector2 not System.Numerics.Vector2
seeds.w: importing System.Numerics and using its Vector2/Vector3 — these are incompatible with Godot's API which expects Godot.Vector2/Vector3.
seeds.c: use Godot.Vector2, Godot.Vector3, Godot.Color etc. directly. Add 'using Godot;' at the top. Note: Godot vectors use float precision (single), not double — be aware of precision limits.
seeds.s: 'Cannot convert System.Numerics.Vector2 to Godot.Vector2' type errors.

seeds.t: Await Tween.Finished to sequence animations without timers
seeds.w: using Timer nodes or manual delta accumulation to delay code after an animation — requires disconnecting signals and managing state.
seeds.c: create a tween and await its completion: var tween = CreateTween(); tween.TweenProperty(this, 'position:x', 100f, 0.5f); await ToSignal(tween, Tween.SignalName.Finished). Or use tween.Finished directly with async/await. This keeps animation sequencing in a readable linear flow w…
seeds.s: chains of timer callbacks for animation sequencing that async Tween awaiting would express linearly.

seeds.t: Cache PackedScene references at load time, not every instantiation
seeds.w: calling GD.Load<PackedScene>('res://Enemy.tscn') inside a loop or frequently called function — loads and parses the file each time, causing hitches.
seeds.c: load the scene once in _Ready or a static field: private static readonly PackedScene EnemyScene = GD.Load<PackedScene>('res://Enemy.tscn'), then call EnemyScene.Instantiate<Enemy>() each time. ResourceLoader.LoadThreadedRequest can pre-load in background for large resources.
seeds.s: visible stuttering when spawning objects because the scene file is loaded from disk each time.

seeds.t: Access Autoloads via GetNode from the scene tree root
seeds.w: using a C# static class or singleton pattern to share global state — it bypasses Godot's scene tree and doesn't participate in the node lifecycle.
seeds.c: declare an Autoload in Project Settings; access it from any node with GetNode<GameManager>('/root/GameManager') or use the typed shortcut if declared as a partial class. Define an extension or singleton accessor to avoid repeating the path string. This integrates with save/load,…
seeds.s: global state managed via C# static fields instead of Godot Autoloads, breaking save states and inspector visibility.

seeds.t: Use node groups for broadcasting messages to multiple nodes
seeds.w: keeping a manual List<Enemy> and iterating it to call methods on all enemies — requires careful add/remove management and breaks when nodes are freed.
seeds.c: add nodes to groups in the editor or with AddToGroup('enemies'); call them with GetTree().CallGroup('enemies', 'TakeDamage', 10f). Or iterate: foreach (Node3D enemy in GetTree().GetNodesInGroup('enemies')). Groups are automatically cleaned up when nodes are freed.
seeds.s: manual collection of node references that breaks with free'd nodes when Godot groups would handle it automatically.

seeds.t: Use named collision layers via bit-shift instead of hardcoded numbers
seeds.w: setting CollisionLayer = 4 or CollisionMask = 6 in code — magic numbers that break when layer order changes in Project Settings.
seeds.c: define constants for your layers: const uint LAYER_PLAYER = 1 << 0; const uint LAYER_ENEMY = 1 << 1; then use CollisionLayer = LAYER_PLAYER, CollisionMask = LAYER_ENEMY | LAYER_WORLD. Alternatively, read layer indices from project settings to stay in sync with editor names.
seeds.s: collision stops working after reordering physics layers in Project Settings because code used hardcoded integers.

seeds.t: Use Input.IsActionPressed over GetKey for remappable controls
seeds.w: Input.IsKeyPressed(Key.Space) hardcodes the physical key and bypasses the Godot input remapping system.
seeds.c: define actions in Project > Input Map and check them with Input.IsActionPressed('jump'), Input.IsActionJustPressed('attack'). This allows players to remap controls and supports controller/keyboard interchangeably. For custom actions in code, InputMap.AddAction('custom') + InputM…
seeds.s: game with keyboard controls that are hardcoded to specific keys and cannot be remapped by the player.

seeds.t: Use ResourceSaver to persist custom Resource subclasses
seeds.w: serialising game data to JSON or binary manually when the data lives in a Godot Resource subclass.
seeds.c: call ResourceSaver.Save(myResource, 'user://save_data.tres') to persist any Resource to disk; load it back with ResourceLoader.Load<MySaveData>('user://save_data.tres'). All [Export] properties on the Resource are automatically included. For sensitive data or binary efficiency, …
seeds.s: manual JSON serialisation for data that is a Resource subclass and could be persisted with ResourceSaver in one line.

seeds.t: Use CallDeferred when modifying the scene tree during physics callbacks
seeds.w: adding, removing, or freeing nodes directly inside _PhysicsProcess or collision callbacks — the scene tree is locked during physics and structural changes cause errors or crashes.
seeds.c: defer scene tree modifications with CallDeferred('add_child', newNode) or use the C# extension: this.CallDeferred(Node.MethodName.AddChild, newNode). QueueFree() is always safe. For lambda-style deferred calls, use Callable.From(() => AddChild(node)).CallDeferred().
seeds.s: 'Parent node is busy' error or crash when adding a node inside a physics or body_entered callback.

seeds.t: Set ShaderMaterial parameters via Set, not property strings
seeds.w: setting shader uniforms with (material as ShaderMaterial).Set('my_uniform', value) using raw strings — typos cause silent failures with no error.
seeds.c: cache the StringName: private static readonly StringName SpeedParam = new('speed'); then shaderMaterial.SetShaderParameter(SpeedParam, 2.5f). Caching StringName avoids repeated string->hash conversions per frame. For materials shared across instances, use a unique surface materi…
seeds.s: shader parameter change has no visible effect because the uniform name string was misspelled.

seeds.t: Use % prefix for scene-unique names to avoid fragile GetNode paths
seeds.w: GetNode<Label>('UI/HUD/ScorePanel/ScoreLabel') — this long path breaks whenever any intermediate node is renamed or restructured.
seeds.c: mark a node as 'Access as Scene Unique Name' in the Godot editor (right-click > Access as Scene Unique Name), then access it with GetNode<Label>('%ScoreLabel'). The % prefix resolves to the unique-named node anywhere in the scene tree regardless of hierarchy.
seeds.s: GetNode paths break silently when the scene hierarchy is refactored and a node is renamed or moved.

seeds.t: Use DataLoader to Batch N+1 Resolver Queries
seeds.w: Fetching a relation inside a field resolver without batching — each parent object triggers a separate DB query, causing N+1 queries for a list of N items.
seeds.c: Use DataLoader to batch and deduplicate per-request: collect IDs across all resolver calls in the same tick, then fire a single query for the batch. Libraries like graphql-dataloader-js or graphene-django's promise loader implement this pattern.
seeds.s: 100 items in a list triggering 101 database queries visible in query logs.

seeds.t: Resolvers Should Fetch Only Requested Fields
seeds.w: Fetching the entire database row in every resolver regardless of which fields the client requested — GraphQL's field selection is wasted if the resolver ignores it.
seeds.c: Inspect the info.fieldNodes or use a library (join-monster, Prisma's select inference) to translate the requested field set into a minimal SQL SELECT or projection.
seeds.s: large payloads and slow queries even when clients request only two fields.

seeds.t: Wrap Mutation Arguments in Input Types
seeds.w: Listing many scalar arguments directly on a mutation — adding fields later is a breaking change, and the signature becomes hard to read.
seeds.c: Wrap all mutation arguments in a single input type (input CreateUserInput { ... }); this makes the signature stable and allows adding optional fields without breaking clients. Use a consistent naming convention like <Action><Entity>Input.
seeds.s: mutation signatures with 10+ individual arguments.

seeds.t: Use Union Types for Domain Errors Not HTTP Codes
seeds.w: Returning null and putting error details only in the top-level errors array — clients can't distinguish domain errors (invalid input) from server errors and can't type-check error payloads.
seeds.c: Model expected errors as union return types (union CreateUserResult = User | ValidationError | DuplicateEmailError) so clients can pattern-match on __typename. Reserve the errors array for unexpected/server errors.
seeds.s: clients parsing unstructured error messages from the errors array for business logic.

seeds.t: Choose Schema-First or Code-First Consistently Per Project
seeds.w: Mixing schema-first SDL files with programmatic schema construction in the same codebase — the two sources of truth diverge and type generation breaks.
seeds.c: Commit to one approach: schema-first (write SDL, generate types) or code-first (write resolvers, derive SDL). Schema-first is better for API-first design across teams; code-first (TypeGraphQL, Pothos) is better for type-safe monorepos.
seeds.s: SDL and code-generated types out of sync, runtime type errors.

seeds.t: Limit Query Depth to Prevent Circular Query Attacks
seeds.w: Allowing clients to send arbitrarily deep queries — a malicious or accidental deeply nested query (user.friends.friends.friends...) can cause exponential resolver calls.
seeds.c: Add query depth limiting middleware (graphql-depth-limit or server built-ins) and set a reasonable maximum (typically 10-15 levels). Also implement query complexity analysis to cap expensive but shallow queries.
seeds.s: server slowdown or crash from a single deeply nested client query.

seeds.t: Disable Introspection in Production for Security
seeds.w: Leaving introspection enabled in production — attackers can enumerate the entire schema, finding types, queries, and mutations to probe.
seeds.c: Disable introspection in production environments via server config (Apollo Server's introspection: false); keep it enabled in development and staging. Provide schema access to trusted clients through a schema registry.
seeds.s: schema fully exposed to unauthenticated requests in production.

seeds.t: Clean Up Subscription Resources on Unsubscribe
seeds.w: Starting subscriptions (WebSocket connections, event listeners) without a cleanup function — connections accumulate and leak memory when clients disconnect.
seeds.c: Return a cleanup function from the subscription resolver (or use the async iterator protocol) that cancels event listeners, closes DB cursors, and removes the client from pub/sub channels on unsubscribe.
seeds.s: memory growing with subscription count, listeners not removed after client disconnect.

seeds.t: Use Non-Null Sparingly; Nullable Fields Allow Partial Data
seeds.w: Marking every field as non-null (!) for convenience — a single resolver error propagates nulls up through all non-null ancestors, potentially nulling out the entire response.
seeds.c: Mark fields non-null only when null is truly impossible; nullable fields allow partial responses where one field errors without destroying the whole query result. Errors bubble to the nearest nullable ancestor.
seeds.s: one failing field nulls the entire object or query response unexpectedly.

seeds.t: Use Persisted Queries to Reduce Payload and Attack Surface
seeds.w: Sending full query strings on every request — large payloads waste bandwidth and allow clients to send arbitrary queries to production.
seeds.c: Implement persisted queries (Automatic Persisted Queries or compile-time query IDs) so clients send a hash; the server looks up the pre-registered query text. This reduces payload size and prevents ad-hoc queries in production.
seeds.s: large query strings in every network request or arbitrary queries reaching production resolvers.

seeds.t: Use Relay Connections for Cursor-Based Pagination
seeds.w: Using page/limit offset pagination for large lists — skipping rows is O(offset) in most databases and the page shifts under concurrent mutations.
seeds.c: Implement the Relay Connection specification with edges/nodes, cursor, and pageInfo.hasNextPage. Cursor-based pagination is stable under concurrent inserts and scales to large datasets. Libraries like graphql-relay-js provide spec-compliant helpers.
seeds.s: missing or duplicated items during pagination of frequently-updated lists.

seeds.t: Build Context Fresh Per Request, Not Per Server
seeds.w: Putting mutable state (user session, DB transaction) in a shared server-level context object — requests share and corrupt each other's state.
seeds.c: Build a fresh context object per request in the context factory function; include the authenticated user, a per-request DataLoader instance, and the request-scoped DB connection.
seeds.s: data leaking between requests or DataLoader caches not being per-request.

seeds.t: Use Fragments for Reusable Field Selections on Client
seeds.w: Repeating the same field list in multiple queries on the client — when the type gains a new field, every query must be updated independently.
seeds.c: Define named fragments for common field selections and spread them into queries. Collocate fragments with the component that uses them (Relay-style). Code-generation tools produce typed hooks per fragment.
seeds.s: duplicated field lists across queries, missing fields after schema update.

seeds.t: Use Custom Scalars for Domain-Specific Value Types
seeds.w: Representing dates, URLs, UUIDs, and email addresses as plain String — no validation at the schema level, clients receive inconsistently formatted values.
seeds.c: Define custom scalar types (DateTime, URL, UUID, EmailAddress) with serialize, parseValue, and parseLiteral; use the graphql-scalars library for common ones. This enforces format at the schema boundary.
seeds.s: clients receiving inconsistent date formats or invalid values that pass through resolver layers.

seeds.t: Authorize at the Resolver Level Not Just Gateway
seeds.w: Checking auth only at the HTTP gateway level — internal resolver calls, subscriptions, or schema stitching bypass gateway auth checks.
seeds.c: Validate permissions inside each resolver or use a directive-based auth layer (graphql-shield, @auth directive) that runs on every field resolution regardless of entry point. Trust the context.user from a verified request, not raw headers.
seeds.s: internal GraphQL calls or federated resolvers bypassing authorization.

seeds.t: Define Entity Keys Correctly in Apollo Federation
seeds.w: Omitting @key directives or using unstable fields as federation keys — the gateway cannot resolve cross-service references and returns null for federated fields.
seeds.c: Mark entities with @key(fields: 'id') on a stable, globally unique identifier and implement the __resolveReference resolver to hydrate the entity from just the key. Stitch subgraphs through the gateway's query plan.
seeds.s: federated fields returning null or 'Cannot return null for non-nullable field' across service boundaries.

seeds.t: Deprecate Fields Before Removing Them
seeds.w: Removing a field from the schema immediately when it is no longer needed server-side — clients using that field break with cryptic errors.
seeds.c: Mark the field @deprecated(reason: 'Use newField instead') and leave it in the schema; monitor usage via field-level metrics; remove only after client traffic drops to zero. Use a schema registry to track breaking changes.
seeds.s: client applications breaking after a schema deploy due to removed fields.

seeds.t: Avoid Multiple Sequential Mutations in One Request
seeds.w: Sending a single request with five aliased mutations expecting them to be atomic — GraphQL executes top-level mutations sequentially but does not wrap them in a transaction.
seeds.c: If atomicity is required, create a single compound mutation that performs all operations within one transaction server-side. Or use a REST endpoint for the batch operation.
seeds.s: partial failures leaving data in an inconsistent state when one of several aliased mutations fails.

seeds.t: Generate Types from Schema for End-to-End Type Safety
seeds.w: Manually writing TypeScript/Flow types that mirror the GraphQL schema — they drift from the actual schema and produce runtime type mismatches.
seeds.c: Use GraphQL Code Generator (graphql-codegen) to auto-generate types and typed hooks from the SDL and operation documents as part of the build. Commit generated files or run generation in CI to catch mismatches.
seeds.s: TypeScript says a field exists but the schema doesn't, or vice versa.

seeds.t: Implement __resolveType for Interfaces and Unions
seeds.w: Defining a union or interface type without implementing __resolveType (or isTypeOf) — the GraphQL runtime cannot determine the concrete type of a resolved object and throws an abstract type error.
seeds.c: Implement __resolveType on the type resolver to return the type name string based on the resolved value's shape (check a __typename field or discriminant property). Without it, inline fragments on the client cannot match.
seeds.s: 'Abstract type X must resolve to an Object type at runtime' error.

seeds.t: Use Deadlines, Not Timeouts — They Propagate
seeds.w: Setting a timeout on just the outermost RPC call and not propagating it to downstream calls — a downstream service can run for minutes after the original caller has given up and moved on.
seeds.c: Use deadlines (absolute timestamps) rather than timeouts and propagate the context through the entire call chain. In gRPC, pass the context with its deadline to every downstream call: ctx, cancel := context.WithDeadline(parentCtx, time.Now().Add(5*time.Second)). Deadline propaga…
seeds.s: cascading slow responses as downstream services keep working after the upstream caller timed out.

seeds.t: Use gRPC Status Codes, Not HTTP Status Semantics
seeds.w: Mapping gRPC status codes to HTTP semantics (using INVALID_ARGUMENT for 400-like errors, NOT_FOUND for 404) — gRPC has its own richer status code vocabulary that should be used semantically.
seeds.c: Use gRPC-native codes: NOT_FOUND when the requested resource doesn't exist. INVALID_ARGUMENT for malformed input (vs FAILED_PRECONDITION for valid input in wrong state). UNAVAILABLE for retryable server errors. UNAUTHENTICATED (no credentials) vs PERMISSION_DENIED (insufficient …
seeds.s: clients unable to implement correct retry logic because status codes don't accurately reflect whether the error is retryable.

seeds.t: Choose the Right Streaming Pattern for the Use Case
seeds.w: Using unary RPC for operations that naturally produce or consume streams of data — requires buffering the entire response or sending many sequential RPCs.
seeds.c: Unary: single request, single response — simple queries. Server streaming: one request, stream of responses — large result sets, real-time updates, log tailing. Client streaming: stream of requests, one response — batch uploads, aggregations. Bidirectional streaming: both sides …
seeds.s: server buffering an entire large dataset into memory before sending it as a single unary response when server streaming would have allowed incremental delivery.

seeds.t: Reuse gRPC Channels — Never Create Per-Request
seeds.w: Creating a new grpc.Dial() connection for every RPC call — gRPC channels use HTTP/2 and are expensive to establish; creating them per-request negates HTTP/2's multiplexing benefits.
seeds.c: Create channels at startup and reuse them throughout the application lifetime: conn, _ = grpc.Dial(addr, grpc.WithTransportCredentials(creds)); client = pb.NewServiceClient(conn). In server code, inject clients as dependencies. The channel handles connection pooling and reconnec…
seeds.s: RPC latency dominated by TLS handshake overhead from creating a new channel per call instead of reusing an existing one.

seeds.t: Use Interceptors for Auth, Logging, and Metrics
seeds.w: Duplicating authentication token validation, request logging, and metric recording in every RPC handler method.
seeds.c: Implement gRPC interceptors (middleware) for cross-cutting concerns: UnaryServerInterceptor for unary RPCs, StreamServerInterceptor for streaming. Common interceptors: grpc-ecosystem/go-grpc-middleware provides auth, logging (zap, logrus), recovery (panic handling), and rate lim…
seeds.s: auth check copy-pasted into every handler that a single interceptor would apply automatically.

seeds.t: Field Numbers in Protobuf Are Permanent — Never Reuse
seeds.w: Removing a field from a proto message and later adding a new field with the same field number — the old and new fields have different types; existing serialised data will be misinterpreted by new code.
seeds.c: Field numbers are the actual wire format identifiers — names are just for readability. Deleted fields must be reserved: reserved 3; reserved 'old_field_name'. This prevents accidental reuse. Add new fields with new numbers. Use proto3's optional for fields that may or may not be…
seeds.s: deserialization errors or silently wrong values after a proto field was removed and a new field with the same number was added.

seeds.t: Follow Proto3 Compatibility Rules for Schema Evolution
seeds.w: Changing a field's type, making an optional field required, or renaming a field and assuming it's backwards compatible — binary incompatibility silently corrupts deserialization.
seeds.c: Safe changes: adding new optional fields (old clients ignore them, new clients see zero values for absent fields). Removing optional fields (mark as reserved). Renaming fields (only the number matters on the wire — safe if you don't rename in generated code simultaneously). Unsa…
seeds.s: deserialization silently returning zero values for all fields after a proto field number changed.

seeds.t: Implement gRPC Health Checking Protocol for Orchestration
seeds.w: Using an HTTP /health endpoint on a separate port for gRPC service health checks — Kubernetes probes and load balancers may not support gRPC natively, and the HTTP endpoint doesn't reflect gRPC server health.
seeds.c: Implement the standard gRPC Health Checking Protocol (grpc.health.v1). Register the health server and set service status: healthServer.SetServingStatus('', healthpb.HealthCheckResponse_SERVING). Kubernetes uses grpc liveness/readiness probes (requires Kubernetes 1.24+). For olde…
seeds.s: load balancer routing to a gRPC server that returned SERVING for the HTTP health check but was actually unable to serve RPCs.

seeds.t: Use Rich Error Details for Machine-Readable Error Information
seeds.w: Encoding error details only in the status message string — requires string parsing on the client and is fragile.
seeds.c: Use the google.rpc.Status proto with details field to attach structured error information: BadRequest with field violations, RetryInfo with retry delay, ErrorInfo with domain and reason. In Go: use status.New(codes.InvalidArgument, 'invalid').WithDetails(&errdetails.BadRequest{.…
seeds.s: clients parsing error message strings to extract field names for form validation when BadRequest.FieldViolation provides them structurally.

seeds.t: Register Server Reflection for Development Tooling
seeds.w: Requiring clients to have .proto files to inspect or test a gRPC service — no discovery mechanism for available methods and message types.
seeds.c: Register gRPC server reflection (disable in production): reflection.Register(server). This allows grpcurl, grpc-client-cli, and Postman to discover services without .proto files: grpcurl -plaintext localhost:50051 list. Use buf studio or BloomRPC as GUI clients. Generate a buf r…
seeds.s: developers unable to test the gRPC service without finding and configuring the correct .proto files, slowing integration work.

seeds.t: Use Client-Side Load Balancing for gRPC
seeds.w: Putting a standard L4 TCP load balancer in front of gRPC services — HTTP/2 long-lived connections mean all requests from one client go to one backend; the load balancer balances connections, not requests.
seeds.c: Use client-side load balancing with service discovery: configure grpc.Dial with a resolver that lists multiple backends and round-robin policy: grpc.WithDefaultServiceConfig('{"loadBalancingPolicy":"round_robin"}'). For Kubernetes, use headless services (clusterIP: None) so DNS …
seeds.s: one backend receiving all requests while others are idle because the L4 load balancer distributed long-lived HTTP/2 connections, not individual RPCs.

seeds.t: Use gRPC Metadata for Request Context Headers
seeds.w: Embedding authentication tokens, trace IDs, and tenant IDs inside the request proto message — pollutes the domain schema with infrastructure concerns.
seeds.c: Use gRPC metadata (analogous to HTTP headers) for request-level context: md := metadata.Pairs('authorization', 'Bearer '+token, 'x-trace-id', traceId); ctx = metadata.NewOutgoingContext(ctx, md). On the server, extract with metadata.FromIncomingContext(ctx). Metadata is separate…
seeds.s: proto messages with auth_token and correlation_id fields that belong in metadata, not the domain payload.

seeds.t: Use buf for Proto Linting and Breaking Change Detection
seeds.w: Manually managing .proto files, protoc invocations, and plugin versions — inconsistent generation, easy to introduce breaking changes, and complex CI setup.
seeds.c: Use buf.build for the proto toolchain: buf.yaml for module configuration, buf.gen.yaml for code generation plugins, buf lint for style enforcement, buf breaking --against .git#branch=main to detect backwards-incompatible changes in CI. buf push to the Buf Schema Registry for dep…
seeds.s: CI failing because a developer changed a field number without realising it was a breaking change, detected only after deployment.

seeds.t: Configure Retry Policy via Service Config, Not Application Code
seeds.w: Implementing retry logic in every gRPC client call with manual backoff code — duplicated, inconsistent retry parameters across services.
seeds.c: Configure retries declaratively via gRPC service config JSON: {"methodConfig": [{"name": [{"service": "MyService"}], "retryPolicy": {"maxAttempts": 4, "initialBackoff": "0.1s", "maxBackoff": "1s", "backoffMultiplier": 2, "retryableStatusCodes": ["UNAVAILABLE"]}}]}. Pass via grpc…
seeds.s: each team implementing their own retry logic with inconsistent backoff parameters when service config would centralise and standardise it.

seeds.t: Enable Compression for Large gRPC Payloads
seeds.w: Sending large proto messages over gRPC without compression — text-heavy fields (JSON blobs, descriptions) compress extremely well but are sent as-is.
seeds.c: Enable gzip compression on the client: grpc.UseCompressor(gzip.Name) per-call or as a default DialOption. Server must register the compressor: encoding.RegisterCompressor(encoding.GetCompressor('gzip')). Negotiate via Accept-Encoding. Use zstd for better ratios on large payloads…
seeds.s: gRPC streaming large text-heavy messages consuming 5x the bandwidth that gzip compression would reduce.

seeds.t: Lazy Evaluation Causes Space Leaks in Accumulator Patterns
seeds.w: Writing a left fold with foldl over a large list — lazy evaluation builds a chain of unevaluated thunks proportional to the list length before computing anything, consuming O(n) heap.
seeds.c: Use foldl' (strict left fold) from Data.List or Data.Foldable for accumulator patterns; the prime-suffixed version forces evaluation at each step. Use seq or $! to force evaluation in custom recursion. For summing a large list: foldl' (+) 0 list, not foldl (+) 0 list.
seeds.s: program running correctly on small inputs but running out of memory or crashing with a stack overflow on large lists.

seeds.t: Use Text or ByteString, Not String, for Real Data
seeds.w: Using Haskell's String type (a type alias for [Char]) for file contents, API responses, or user data — String is a linked list of characters, with O(n) indexing and extremely poor memory efficiency (dozens of bytes per character).
seeds.c: Use Data.Text for Unicode text data and Data.ByteString for binary/network data. Use Data.Text.Lazy and Data.ByteString.Lazy for streaming. Enable {-# LANGUAGE OverloadedStrings #-} to write text literals as Text or ByteString. Convert between types with encodeUtf8/decodeUtf8.
seeds.s: text processing code 10–100x slower than equivalent C or Python due to linked-list String representation.

seeds.t: Use Bang Patterns for Strict Fields in Data Types
seeds.w: Defining a data type with lazy fields that accumulate unevaluated thunks — a record updated many times will hold a chain of computations instead of the current value, causing a space leak.
seeds.c: Use bang patterns on fields that should be strict: data Counter = Counter { _count :: !Int, _total :: !Double }. The ! forces evaluation when the constructor is applied. Enable {-# LANGUAGE BangPatterns #-} for bang patterns in function arguments. Use the strict package or the S…
seeds.s: long-running accumulation logic using growing amounts of memory instead of constant memory.

seeds.t: Avoid Partial Functions head, tail, fromJust
seeds.w: Using head, tail, fromJust, read without handling the empty/Nothing case — these throw exceptions on invalid input with unhelpful error messages at runtime.
seeds.c: Use pattern matching: case list of { [] -> handle empty; (x:xs) -> use x and xs }. Use listToMaybe instead of head (returns Maybe). Use fromMaybe default instead of fromJust. Use readMaybe instead of read. The Safe package provides safeHead, safeTail etc.
seeds.s: 'Prelude.head: empty list' or 'Maybe.fromJust: Nothing' exception with no stack trace context.

seeds.t: do Notation Is Syntactic Sugar for >>= and >>
seeds.w: Thinking of do notation as imperative sequencing and being surprised when a non-IO monad (Maybe, Either, List) doesn't behave like a sequence of statements.
seeds.c: do { x <- action; body } desugars to action >>= \x -> body. For Maybe, a Nothing short-circuits the rest. For List, the do block is list comprehension. For Either Left, the rest is skipped. Use do notation for any monad, but understand the specific monad's semantics. Use let (no…
seeds.s: do block in a Maybe context 'skipping' the rest of the computation without explanation because a Nothing was encountered.

seeds.t: Use newtype for Zero-Cost Type Safety
seeds.w: Using bare String, Int, or Double for domain types (UserId, EmailAddress, Dollars) — no type safety, easy to confuse arguments of the same underlying type.
seeds.c: newtype UserId = UserId Int derives (Eq, Ord, Show). newtypes are erased at compile time — zero runtime overhead. Use GeneralizedNewtypeDeriving to inherit instances from the wrapped type. Distinguish between a newtype (single-field, single-constructor, zero cost) and data (gene…
seeds.s: swapping a UserId and an OrderId argument (both Ints) with no compile error.

seeds.t: Avoid Orphan Typeclass Instances
seeds.w: Defining a typeclass instance for a type in a module that owns neither the typeclass nor the type — orphan instances cause problems when multiple libraries define the same orphan, leading to conflicting instances and hard-to-debug errors.
seeds.c: Define instances either in the module that defines the type, or in the module that defines the typeclass. If you don't own either, create a newtype wrapper and define the instance on the wrapper. GHC warns about orphans with -Worphans. Use newtype deriving or coerce to avoid wri…
seeds.s: 'Duplicate instance declarations' error from two libraries both providing an orphan instance for the same type.

seeds.t: Use IORef for Mutable State in IO, STM for Concurrency
seeds.w: Trying to use mutable variables the way you would in an imperative language — Haskell's pure functions cannot mutate state; mutation requires explicit monadic context.
seeds.c: Use IORef for simple mutable state in IO: newIORef val, readIORef ref, writeIORef ref val, modifyIORef' ref f (strict version). For concurrent mutable state, use TVar in STM (Software Transactional Memory): atomically $ modifyTVar' counter (+1). STM composites atomically without…
seeds.s: attempting to use a global mutable variable and finding no way to update it from a pure function.

seeds.t: Use try and catch From Control.Exception for IO Errors
seeds.w: Letting IO exceptions propagate unhandled, or using error for expected failure cases in IO — error throws an imprecise exception (ErrorCall) that's difficult to catch and provides poor error messages.
seeds.c: Use try @SomeException action to catch all exceptions, or catch action handler for specific exception types. Throw with throwIO (safer than throw from pure code). Use bracket for resource acquisition and release even in the presence of exceptions: bracket (openFile path) hClose …
seeds.s: IO exception crashing the program that should have been caught and handled gracefully.

seeds.t: Use Data.Map.Strict for Key-Value Maps
seeds.w: Using association lists ([(k,v)]) for key-value lookups — lookup in a list is O(n); for any non-trivial size, a map is required.
seeds.c: Use Data.Map.Strict (strict values, O(log n) operations) or Data.HashMap.Strict from the unordered-containers package (O(1) average for hashable keys). Import qualified: import qualified Data.Map.Strict as Map. Use Map.fromList, Map.lookup (returns Maybe), Map.insertWith. Data.M…
seeds.s: O(n) lookup in a list being called repeatedly in an inner loop, causing quadratic runtime.

seeds.t: Use fmap/<$>, <*>, and >>= at the Right Abstraction Level
seeds.w: Using monadic bind (>>=) for operations that only need Functor or Applicative — needlessly requiring Monad prevents using the function with Applicative contexts that are not monadic.
seeds.c: Use fmap / <$> when applying a pure function to a single effectful value. Use <*> when applying an effectful function to an effectful argument (Applicative). Use >>= only when the next action depends on the previous result (Monad). Prefer the weakest constraint that suffices; Ap…
seeds.s: functions requiring Monad constraint that could work with Applicative, restricting reuse.

seeds.t: Use Record Update Syntax for Partial Record Modification
seeds.w: Constructing a new record by specifying every field just to change one: User { userId = userId u, userName = newName, userEmail = userEmail u, ... } — brittle, breaks when fields are added.
seeds.c: Use record update syntax: u { userName = newName }. Unspecified fields are copied from the original. This is the equivalent of Scala's .copy() or JavaScript's object spread. For deeply nested updates, consider the lens library (view, set, over from Control.Lens).
seeds.s: record construction that repeats all fields except one, breaking when a new field is added to the record.

seeds.t: Use where for Definitions, let for Expressions
seeds.w: Using let for all local definitions, even when where would be cleaner — let introduces bindings as an expression (can appear anywhere); where introduces bindings local to a definition's equation.
seeds.c: Use where at the end of a function definition for helper functions and named constants: f x = helper x where helper y = ... . Use let in in expressions, do blocks, and list comprehensions: let x = expr in body. Both introduce local scope; where can reference the function's argum…
seeds.s: deeply nested let-in expressions that where bindings at the definition level would flatten.

seeds.t: concatMap Is >>= for the List Monad
seeds.w: Writing map then concat separately when concatMap (or the list monad's >>=) handles both in one pass.
seeds.c: concatMap f xs == xs >>= f == concat (map f xs). In a list do-block: do { x <- xs; y <- f x; return (x, y) } is a list comprehension. Use concatMap for flat-mapping over lists. Similarly, mapM is sequence . map for traversing with effects. Use the Traversable typeclass (traverse…
seeds.s: map followed by concat where concatMap or >>= expresses the same thing more directly.

seeds.t: Enable Language Extensions Explicitly Per-Module
seeds.w: Adding all commonly used extensions to a global OPTIONS_GHC pragma or enabling them all in package.yaml defaultExtensions — extensions interact and some (OverlappingInstances, IncoherentInstances) are dangerous globally.
seeds.c: Enable extensions per-module with {-# LANGUAGE OverloadedStrings, TupleSections #-} at the top. Common safe extensions: OverloadedStrings, TupleSections, LambdaCase, MultiWayIf, DeriveFunctor. List extensions used in cabal/package.yaml for discoverability but prefer per-module f…
seeds.s: extension-specific syntax causing errors in modules where the extension wasn't expected to be active.

seeds.t: Use cabal or Stack Consistently Per Project
seeds.w: Mixing cabal commands and stack commands in the same project, or switching between them without understanding the different snapshot and resolver systems — they use different package databases and configurations.
seeds.c: Choose one tool per project: Stack uses a curated resolver (LTS Haskell snapshot) guaranteeing compatible package versions; cabal uses a solver and cabal.project.freeze for reproducibility. Stack generates stack.yaml; cabal uses cabal.project. Both can coexist in a repo but don'…
seeds.s: packages building with cabal but not stack (or vice versa) because resolver snapshots differ.

seeds.t: Use newtype for Single-Constructor Single-Field Wrappers
seeds.w: Using data for a type with exactly one constructor and one field: data Wrapper a = Wrapper a — data boxes the value at runtime whereas newtype is erased.
seeds.c: Use newtype when there is exactly one constructor with exactly one field: newtype Wrapper a = Wrapper { unwrap :: a }. The compiler guarantees newtype is zero-overhead — the wrapped type and the newtype have identical runtime representations. newtype cannot be recursive (use dat…
seeds.s: unnecessary heap allocation and indirection from using data for a simple wrapper type that newtype would handle at zero cost.

seeds.t: Compose STM Actions for Lock-Free Concurrency
seeds.w: Using locks (MVar with takeMVar/putMVar) for complex concurrent state that involves multiple variables — acquiring multiple MVars in different orders causes deadlock.
seeds.c: Use Software Transactional Memory (STM) from Control.Concurrent.STM. Operations on TVar, TQueue, TChan are composable — multiple reads and writes can be wrapped in a single atomically block that either all succeed or retry. STM retries automatically when a read variable changes,…
seeds.s: complex MVar lock ordering to prevent deadlock in code that accesses multiple shared variables.

seeds.t: Balance Point-Free Style With Readability
seeds.w: Writing maximally point-free expressions that are impossible to read: ((.) . (.)) or foldr (flip (:)) [] — point-free style removes variable names, which can obscure intent.
seeds.c: Use point-free style where it genuinely improves readability: map (+1) . filter even rather than \xs -> map (+1) (filter even xs). For complex compositions, use intermediate named values or let bindings to add clarity. Tools like pointfree.io can suggest point-free forms; use th…
seeds.s: code reviewed as 'unreadable' by teammates because point-free composition hides the data flow.

seeds.t: Don't Use Show/Read for Serialisation
seeds.w: Using show to serialise data to strings and read to deserialise — Show/Read are for debugging and REPL use; their format is not stable, not efficient, and not interoperable with other languages or tools.
seeds.c: Use aeson for JSON (ToJSON/FromJSON instances, auto-derivable), binary or cereal for binary serialisation, or cassava for CSV. These produce stable, documented formats with proper error handling. Show/Read format can change between GHC versions or library updates.
seeds.s: serialised data from one GHC version failing to parse with read on another version, or Show output not parseable by other languages.

seeds.t: Use hx-boost for SPA-Like Navigation Without JavaScript
seeds.w: Loading entire pages on every navigation in a traditional multi-page app, causing full page reloads with flash and scroll loss.
seeds.c: Add hx-boost='true' to the <body> or a container element — all anchor links and forms within are automatically enhanced to use AJAX, replacing only the <body> content while preserving the <head>. The browser history is updated normally. Combine with hx-push-url to control URL up…
seeds.s: full page flash and scroll-to-top on every navigation in a traditional server-rendered app that hx-boost eliminates in one attribute.

seeds.t: Control What Gets Swapped With hx-target and hx-swap
seeds.w: Returning a full HTML page from a partial HTMX request, or not specifying a target so the response replaces the triggering element.
seeds.c: Use hx-target to specify which element receives the response: hx-target='#results' targets by ID. Use hx-swap to control how it's inserted: innerHTML (default), outerHTML, beforeend (append), afterbegin (prepend), beforebegin, afterend, delete (no response needed), none. Use hx-…
seeds.s: entire page replaced when only a small section should update, or new content appended in the wrong position.

seeds.t: Use Out-of-Band Swaps to Update Multiple Page Sections
seeds.w: Making multiple separate HTMX requests to update different parts of the page after one user action — multiple round trips for logically one operation.
seeds.c: Include hx-swap-oob='true' on additional elements in the server response to update multiple page sections in one request. The primary response updates the hx-target; elements with hx-swap-oob update their corresponding DOM elements by ID. Use hx-swap-oob='beforeend:#notification…
seeds.s: two or three HTMX requests firing sequentially after a form submit to update the result, a counter, and a notification area.

seeds.t: Use hx-trigger to Control When Requests Fire
seeds.w: Only triggering HTMX requests on click, missing use cases like search-as-you-type, scroll-triggered loading, or periodic refresh.
seeds.c: hx-trigger='keyup changed delay:300ms' fires after typing pauses — ideal for search. hx-trigger='revealed' fires when the element scrolls into view — for infinite scroll. hx-trigger='every 5s' for polling. hx-trigger='click from:body' for event delegation. hx-trigger='custom-eve…
seeds.s: search input making an API call on every keydown instead of waiting for typing to pause.

seeds.t: Use HTTP Status Codes to Control HTMX Behaviour
seeds.w: Always returning 200 with empty body to indicate no-op — HTMX swaps the empty body, potentially clearing the target element.
seeds.c: Return 204 No Content to tell HTMX to do nothing (no swap). Return 286 to stop polling (used with hx-trigger='every 2s'). Return HX-Redirect response header to trigger a client-side redirect. Return HX-Refresh: true to trigger a full page refresh. Return HX-Trigger response head…
seeds.s: polling request clearing the target element when the server has nothing new to return, fixed by returning 204.

seeds.t: Include CSRF Tokens in HTMX Requests
seeds.w: Forgetting CSRF tokens for HTMX-triggered POST/PUT/DELETE requests — server-side CSRF protection rejects the request with 403.
seeds.c: Configure HTMX to automatically include the CSRF token: htmx.defineExtension('csrf', ...) or add it via htmx:configRequest event: document.addEventListener('htmx:configRequest', e => { e.detail.headers['X-CSRFToken'] = getCsrfToken(); }). For Django: use the {% csrf_token %} tem…
seeds.s: HTMX form submission returning 403 Forbidden from CSRF middleware that wasn't updated to handle AJAX CSRF headers.

seeds.t: Show Loading Indicators With htmx-indicator
seeds.w: No visual feedback during HTMX requests — users click buttons multiple times thinking nothing happened.
seeds.c: Add a loading indicator element with class htmx-indicator. HTMX automatically shows it (opacity: 1) while a request is in flight and hides it on completion: <button hx-post='/submit' hx-indicator='#spinner'>Submit</button><span id='spinner' class='htmx-indicator'>Loading...</spa…
seeds.s: users double-submitting forms because no indicator showed the first request was in progress.

seeds.t: Manage Browser History With hx-push-url
seeds.w: Updating page content with HTMX without updating the URL — users can't bookmark, share, or use the back button for HTMX-driven navigation.
seeds.c: Add hx-push-url='true' to push the request URL to the browser history, or hx-push-url='/custom/path' for a specific URL. HTMX handles popstate events to restore content on back-navigation by re-fetching. Use hx-replace-url for replacing the current history entry instead of pushi…
seeds.s: clicking the back button after HTMX navigation returning to the page before HTMX was triggered, not the previous HTMX state.

seeds.t: Use HTMX Events to Integrate With JavaScript
seeds.w: Trying to call JavaScript functions directly from hx-on attributes for complex interactions — limited expressiveness and mixes concerns.
seeds.c: Listen to HTMX lifecycle events to integrate JavaScript: htmx:afterSwap fires after content is swapped (re-initialise JS plugins), htmx:beforeRequest for request customisation, htmx:responseError for error handling. Dispatch custom events from JavaScript: htmx.trigger(element, '…
seeds.s: JavaScript plugins (datepickers, rich text editors) not initialising in HTMX-loaded content because DOMContentLoaded doesn't fire for partial swaps.

seeds.t: Use Alpine.js or _hyperscript for Client-Side Logic
seeds.w: Writing jQuery or vanilla JS alongside HTMX for simple client-side interactions (toggling classes, hiding elements, form validation) — introduces complexity that inline scripting tools handle more naturally with HTMX's philosophy.
seeds.c: Use Alpine.js (x-data, x-show, x-on) or _hyperscript (on click toggle .hidden on #sidebar) for lightweight client-side behaviour that doesn't require server round trips. Alpine integrates naturally with HTMX: Alpine handles local UI state; HTMX handles server interactions. Keep …
seeds.s: large JavaScript files managing toggle states and simple animations that Alpine directives would eliminate.

seeds.t: Return 422 With Error HTML for Server-Side Form Validation
seeds.w: Returning validation errors as JSON and writing JavaScript to render them — undermines HTMX's server-driven HTML approach.
seeds.c: On validation failure, return HTTP 422 Unprocessable Entity with the form HTML including inline error messages: Email is required. HTMX swaps the 422 response body just like a 200, so the form re-renders with errors in place. This keeps validation logi…
seeds.s: writing a JavaScript error renderer for HTMX form validation when the server can return the re-rendered form with errors directly.

seeds.t: Implement Infinite Scroll With hx-trigger revealed
seeds.w: Implementing infinite scroll with a scroll event listener and JavaScript — complex, requires debouncing, and doesn't degrade gracefully.
seeds.c:
Place a sentinel element at the bottom of the list:
Load more
. When the sentinel scrolls into view, HTMX fetches the next page and replaces the sentinel with the new items plus a new sentinel for the nex…
seeds.s: complex scroll-event JavaScript for infinite scroll that hx-trigger='revealed' implements declaratively.

seeds.t: Use hx-disinherit to Prevent Attribute Inheritance
seeds.w: Adding hx-headers or hx-target to a parent element expecting it to apply globally, then being surprised it applies to child elements you didn't intend.
seeds.c: HTMX attributes are inherited by children — a parent hx-target='#results' applies to all child HTMX requests unless overridden. Use hx-disinherit='hx-target' on child elements to opt out of specific inherited attributes. Use hx-inherit='hx-target' to explicitly opt in to attribu…
seeds.s: a child element's request updating the wrong target because it inherited the parent's hx-target unexpectedly.

seeds.t: Use the Preload Extension for Instant-Feeling Navigation
seeds.w: Waiting for the user to click before starting the HTMX request — click-to-response latency is perceptible for server-rendered content.
seeds.c: Install the htmx preload extension: <script src='preload.js'></script>. Add hx-ext='preload' to the body or specific links. On mouseenter (hover), the request starts before the click completes, so content is often ready by the time the click fires. For hx-boost navigation, this …
seeds.s: server-rendered navigation feeling slow compared to a SPA, fixed by starting the fetch on hover rather than click.

seeds.t: Use SSE Extension for Real-Time Server Push
seeds.w: Polling with hx-trigger='every 2s' for real-time updates — wasteful, introduces latency up to the poll interval, and hammers the server with requests even when nothing changed.
seeds.c: Use HTMX's SSE extension for server-sent events: <div hx-ext='sse' sse-connect='/events' sse-swap='message'>. The server sends text/event-stream responses; HTMX swaps the content on each event. Named events: sse-swap='notification' listens for event: notification in the stream. …
seeds.s: polling endpoint called every 2 seconds for real-time notifications that SSE pushes instantly with no polling overhead.

seeds.t: Incident Response: Changing Multiple Variables During Mitigation
seeds.w: Applying multiple fixes simultaneously during an incident — when the incident resolves, you don't know which change fixed it; when it doesn't, you've increased complexity.
seeds.c: Change one variable at a time and wait to observe the effect. Assign an incident commander to control the pace of changes. Document every change and its observed effect.
seeds.s: Incident resolved after applying 3 simultaneous changes; same incident recurs; no one knows which fix was responsible.

seeds.t: Incident Response: Not Updating Status Page During Outage
seeds.w: Experiencing an outage without updating a public status page — customers assume the worst, flood support, and trust erodes faster than with transparent communication.
seeds.c: Update the status page within 5 minutes of declaring an incident, even if the message is 'We are aware of an issue and investigating.' Update every 20-30 minutes thereafter.
seeds.s: 2-hour outage with no status update; support queue fills with 500 tickets; social media posts; trust damage exceeds technical impact.

seeds.t: Incident Response: Blameful Postmortem Focused on Individual Error
seeds.w: Postmortem that identifies 'person X made the wrong call' as the root cause — creates psychological safety problems that make future incidents worse.
seeds.c: Adopt blameless postmortems: the system failed, not the person. Find the conditions that made the error possible. Ask 'what would have prevented this?' not 'who caused this?'
seeds.s: Engineer who caused incident hides future problems; team stops raising concerns; next incident is larger and discovered later.

seeds.t: Incident Response: 5 Whys Stopping at 'Human Error'
seeds.w: Running a root cause analysis and stopping when you reach 'engineer did not follow procedure' — human error is a symptom, not a root cause.
seeds.c: Continue past human error: Why was the procedure not followed? Was it unclear? Conflicting with another goal? Under time pressure? The systemic conditions that make human error possible are the real root causes.
seeds.s: Postmortem concludes 'engineer deployed without testing'; next deployment same mistake happens; procedure wasn't followed because it was too slow.

seeds.t: Incident Response: Not Adding Monitoring After an Incident
seeds.w: Fixing an incident without adding alerts that would have caught it earlier — the same condition can recur without detection.
seeds.c: Every incident postmortem should produce at minimum one action item: 'add an alert for the condition that caused this incident.' Test that the alert would have fired before the incident reached customers.
seeds.s: Disk full caused 4-hour outage; fixed; no disk space alert added; same outage recurs 3 months later.

seeds.t: Incident Response: No Incident Commander Assigned
seeds.w: Responding to an incident with everyone on the call making suggestions and changes simultaneously — without a commander, changes are uncoordinated, communication chaotic, and recovery longer.
seeds.c: Assign an incident commander immediately. The IC controls who makes changes and when. Other responders make suggestions only; the IC decides. Rotate IC training.
seeds.s: 6 engineers on a bridge making simultaneous changes; changes conflict; outage extended 45 minutes from coordination failures.

seeds.t: Incident Response: Rolling Back Code When Data State Has Changed
seeds.w: Reverting a deployment without checking whether the new code wrote data in a format the old code can't read — the rollback causes a new failure.
seeds.c: Before rolling back: check whether the new code made schema migrations, wrote new data formats, or sent irreversible events. If yes, a rollback may cause data corruption. Consider a hotfix instead.
seeds.s: Rollback successful; old code reads new schema fields as NULL; data corruption detected 20 minutes later.

seeds.t: Incident Response: Optimizing MTTR While Ignoring MTTA
seeds.w: Tracking Mean Time to Recovery as the only reliability metric, ignoring Mean Time to Acknowledge — time from incident start to awareness is often larger than the fix time.
seeds.c: Track both MTTA and MTTR. Invest in alerting quality to reduce MTTA. A fast fix on a slowly-detected incident has high total customer impact.
seeds.s: Fast fix but slow detection; MTTR is 8 minutes; MTTA is 90 minutes; customer impact is 98 minutes; team celebrates MTTR.

seeds.t: iOS: Retain Cycle in Closure Capturing self
seeds.w: Capturing self strongly in a closure stored by an object that self also references (NotificationCenter handler, URLSession completion, view model closure) — creates a memory cycle.
seeds.c: Use [weak self] in the closure capture list: { [weak self] in guard let self = self else { return } }. Use [unowned self] only when self is guaranteed to outlive the closure.
seeds.s: ViewControllers never deallocated; memory grows unboundedly as user navigates; Instruments shows persistent VC leak.

seeds.t: iOS: UI Updates Called from Background Thread
seeds.w: Updating UILabel, UIImageView, UITableView, or any UIKit element from a URLSession completion handler or DispatchQueue.global() callback — UIKit is not thread-safe.
seeds.c: Always dispatch UI updates to the main thread: DispatchQueue.main.async { self.label.text = result } or use await MainActor.run {} in Swift concurrency.
seeds.s: Random crashes with BAD_ACCESS; UI glitches; intermittent visual corruption; occasionally works but not reliably.

seeds.t: iOS SwiftUI: @StateObject vs @ObservedObject Confusion
seeds.w: Using @ObservedObject for a view model created inside the view — the view model is recreated on every view refresh, losing all state.
seeds.c: Use @StateObject for view models owned and created by the view (only created once). Use @ObservedObject for view models passed in from a parent (owned elsewhere).
seeds.s: ViewModel resets every time the parent view rebuilds; user input or network state lost on trivial state changes.

seeds.t: iOS: Sensitive Data Stored in UserDefaults
seeds.w: Storing passwords, tokens, or private keys in UserDefaults — it's a plist stored unencrypted in the app's container, accessible to backup tools and debuggers.
seeds.c: Store sensitive data in the Keychain using SecItem APIs or a wrapper like KeychainAccess. UserDefaults is appropriate for preferences and settings, not secrets.
seeds.s: Auth token readable from unencrypted iTunes backup; access token exposed in device file system.

seeds.t: iOS SwiftUI: .task Modifier Not Cancelling Previous Task
seeds.w: Using .onAppear with an async task instead of the .task modifier — .onAppear doesn't automatically cancel in-flight async work when the view disappears, causing work to continue after the view is gone.
seeds.c: Use the .task modifier: it automatically cancels the task when the view disappears. For manual async work in .onAppear, store the Task in a @State and cancel it in .onDisappear.
seeds.s: Async work continues after navigating away; results write to a deallocated view model; memory not released.

seeds.t: iOS: Strong Delegate Reference Creating Retain Cycle
seeds.w: Declaring a delegate property as strong (the default): var delegate: MyDelegate — if the delegate also holds a reference to the delegating object, a retain cycle forms.
seeds.c: Declare delegate properties as weak: weak var delegate: MyDelegate? The protocol must be class-bound: protocol MyDelegate: AnyObject. Use weak for all delegate references.
seeds.s: View controller and its child component never deallocated because both hold strong references to each other.

seeds.t: iOS: Combine Publisher Not Stored in cancellables
seeds.w: Creating a Combine publisher subscription without storing the AnyCancellable in a Set<AnyCancellable> property — the subscription is immediately cancelled when the local AnyCancellable is deallocated.
seeds.c: Add .store(in: &cancellables) to all subscriptions. Declare var cancellables = Set<AnyCancellable>() as a property. Never let the AnyCancellable fall out of scope.
seeds.s: Publisher subscription silently cancelled immediately; network result, timer, or notification never received.

seeds.t: iOS: One-Time Setup in viewDidAppear Instead of viewDidLoad
seeds.w: Performing initialization that should happen once (network fetch, observer registration, expensive setup) in viewDidAppear — it's called every time the view becomes visible.
seeds.c: Put one-time setup in viewDidLoad (called once). Use viewWillAppear/viewDidAppear only for updates that should refresh on every appearance (analytics, resume animations).
seeds.s: Network request fires every time user navigates back to the screen; notification observer registered multiple times.

seeds.t: Checked Exceptions Force Callers to Handle
seeds.w: Declaring checked exceptions on every method forces callers to catch or rethrow them everywhere, creating boilerplate that swallows errors.
seeds.c: Reserve checked exceptions for recoverable conditions the caller can actually handle; use unchecked RuntimeException subclasses for programming errors. Wrapping with try-catch just to rethrow as RuntimeException is a code smell.
seeds.s: methods littered with 'throws Exception' or empty catch blocks.

seeds.t: Optional Is Not a Null Replacement Everywhere
seeds.w: Using Optional as a field type, method parameter, or in collections to avoid null checks everywhere.
seeds.c: Optional is designed as a return type for methods that may have no result; use it to signal absence at API boundaries, not internally. Optional fields waste memory and complicate serialization.
seeds.s: Optional<String> fields in entities or Optional parameters in method signatures.

seeds.t: String Concatenation in Loops Is Quadratic
seeds.w: Building a string with += inside a loop creates a new String object on every iteration, making it O(n²).
seeds.c: Use StringBuilder and call append() in the loop, then toString() once at the end. For joining collections, prefer String.join() or Collectors.joining() in streams.
seeds.s: noticeable slowdown with large lists or stack traces showing many String allocations.

seeds.t: Override hashCode Whenever You Override equals
seeds.w: Overriding equals() without overriding hashCode() breaks the contract that equal objects must have equal hash codes.
seeds.c: Always override both; use Objects.hash() for a concise implementation based on the same fields. Violating this contract causes objects to be lost in HashMaps and HashSets.
seeds.s: objects correctly equal by equals() cannot be found in hash-based collections.

seeds.t: Streams Cannot Be Reused After Terminal Operation
seeds.w: Storing a Stream in a variable and calling multiple terminal operations on it — the second call throws IllegalStateException.
seeds.c: Recreate the stream from the source each time, or collect results into a List first if multiple passes are needed. Streams are single-use pipelines.
seeds.s: 'stream has already been operated upon or closed' IllegalStateException.

seeds.t: Default Methods Create Diamond Problem in Interfaces
seeds.w: Implementing two interfaces that both define a default method with the same signature compiles but requires an explicit override.
seeds.c: Override the conflicting method in the implementing class and explicitly select the desired default with InterfaceA.super.method(). Failing to override causes a compile error.
seeds.s: 'types X and Y are incompatible; both define method()' compile error.

seeds.t: Generics Are Erased at Runtime
seeds.w: Checking instanceof against a generic type like 'obj instanceof List<String>' or creating generic arrays — both fail at runtime due to type erasure.
seeds.c: Check only the raw type with instanceof List<?>; use Array.newInstance(Class, size) for generic arrays. Capture the Class<T> token in constructors when runtime type info is needed.
seeds.s: unchecked cast warnings or ClassCastException at unexpected points.

seeds.t: Modifying Collection During Iteration Fails
seeds.w: Removing elements from a List or Map inside a for-each loop throws ConcurrentModificationException.
seeds.c: Use iterator.remove(), removeIf() predicate, or collect results to remove after the loop. For concurrent scenarios use CopyOnWriteArrayList or ConcurrentHashMap.
seeds.s: ConcurrentModificationException mid-loop even in single-threaded code.

seeds.t: Integer == Comparison Works Only for Small Values
seeds.w: Comparing Integer objects with == instead of equals() — this works accidentally for values -128 to 127 due to the Integer cache, then fails silently for larger numbers.
seeds.c: Always use .equals() for object comparisons; use == only for primitives. Autoboxing masks the bug by passing the small-range tests.
seeds.s: equality logic correct in tests with small values, broken in production with large IDs.

seeds.t: Single-Threaded Executor Hides Concurrency Bugs
seeds.w: Using Executors.newFixedThreadPool(1) in tests or Executors.newCachedThreadPool() in production without bounds — the cached pool creates unbounded threads under load.
seeds.c: Size thread pools based on workload type: CPU-bound tasks use Runtime.getRuntime().availableProcessors(); IO-bound tasks use a virtual thread executor in Java 21+ or a bounded pool.
seeds.s: production thread explosion and OOM vs. masked race conditions in tests.

seeds.t: Virtual Threads Enable Blocking IO at Scale
seeds.w: Wrapping blocking IO calls in CompletableFuture with a custom executor to avoid blocking platform threads — complex and error-prone.
seeds.c: In Java 21+, use virtual threads via Thread.ofVirtual().start() or Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor(); blocking calls are cheap on virtual threads. Avoid synchronized blocks holding locks across blocking calls on virtual threads — prefer ReentrantLock.
seeds.s: high complexity reactive code where simple blocking reads would suffice.

seeds.t: Records Provide Immutable Data Carriers
seeds.w: Writing boilerplate classes with final fields, constructor, equals, hashCode, and toString just to hold data.
seeds.c: Use records (Java 16+) for transparent, immutable data holders — the compiler generates all boilerplate. Add compact constructors for validation. Records cannot extend classes and all fields are final.
seeds.s: many-line value objects that could be replaced with a one-line record declaration.

seeds.t: Sealed Classes Enable Exhaustive Pattern Matching
seeds.w: Using instanceof chains or unconstrained inheritance when modeling a closed set of variants, missing new subtypes silently.
seeds.c: Declare sealed interfaces/classes (Java 17+) with permitted subtypes, then use pattern matching switch — the compiler enforces exhaustiveness. Add a default only when truly needed.
seeds.s: forgot-a-subtype bugs or verbose if-instanceof chains.

seeds.t: var Infers Type But Reduces Readability at Boundaries
seeds.w: Using var for all local variables including lambda parameters and public API return types — obscures intent for readers.
seeds.c: Use var (Java 10+) when the type is obvious from the right-hand side (e.g., var list = new ArrayList<String>()), but be explicit in method signatures and complex assignments. Avoid var where the inferred type is a supertype or wildcard.
seeds.s: 'what type is this?' confusion when reading code without an IDE.

seeds.t: groupingBy Preserves All Elements Including Nulls
seeds.w: Calling stream.collect(Collectors.groupingBy(fn)) when fn returns null for some elements — throws NullPointerException because the default HashMap disallows null keys.
seeds.c: Map nulls to a sentinel value before grouping, or use Collectors.groupingBy with a custom map supplier that allows nulls. Alternatively filter nulls upstream with filter().
seeds.s: NullPointerException in groupingBy with no obvious null in the data.

seeds.t: Static Field Initialization Order Is Top-Down
seeds.w: Referencing a static field before it is declared in the same class, or building circular static dependencies between classes — leads to null or zero values silently.
seeds.c: Declare static constants before any static fields that depend on them; move complex initialization into static {} blocks with explicit ordering. Avoid cross-class static initializer dependencies.
seeds.s: static field is unexpectedly null or 0 even though it is 'initialized'.

seeds.t: Try-With-Resources Closes AutoCloseables Safely
seeds.w: Calling resource.close() in a finally block — if an exception occurs in the try body and another in finally, the original is suppressed.
seeds.c: Declare AutoCloseable resources in try(Resource r = ...) and the JVM closes them in reverse order, adding suppressed exceptions to the primary. Multiple resources are comma-separated.
seeds.s: resource leaks when exceptions occur, or lost exception details from finally blocks.

seeds.t: computeIfAbsent Atomically Initialises Map Entries
seeds.w: Checking map.containsKey() then map.put() to initialise a default value — not atomic and verbose.
seeds.c: Use map.computeIfAbsent(key, k -> new ArrayList<>()) to initialise lazily and atomically (in ConcurrentHashMap). Prefer getOrDefault() for simple read-only defaults.
seeds.s: race conditions in concurrent map initialization or repeated containsKey/put patterns.

seeds.t: Pattern Matching instanceof Eliminates Cast
seeds.w: Testing instanceof then immediately casting to the same type on a separate line — redundant and error-prone.
seeds.c: Use pattern matching instanceof (Java 16+): 'if (obj instanceof String s)' declares and casts in one expression, scoped to the if block. Works in switch expressions with Java 21 pattern matching.
seeds.s: pairs of instanceof checks and casts throughout visitor or adapter code.

seeds.t: Self-Invocation Bypasses @Transactional Proxy
seeds.w: Calling a @Transactional method from another method within the same Spring bean — the call bypasses the AOP proxy so no transaction is started.
seeds.c: Inject the bean itself (or use ApplicationContext.getBean()) to go through the proxy, or restructure so transactional methods are called from different beans. Prefer structuring services so public transactional methods are the entry points.
seeds.s: transactions not rolling back on exception when called internally.

seeds.t: Know the Difference Between mockReset, mockClear, mockRestore
seeds.w: Using mockClear() expecting it to remove mock implementation and return values — mockClear only resets call history (calls, instances, results), not the implementation.
seeds.c: mockClear() resets call count and history only. mockReset() resets call history AND removes implementation/return values (mock returns undefined). mockRestore() fully restores the original implementation (only works on jest.spyOn mocks). Use afterEach(() => jest.clearAllMocks())…
seeds.s: mock returning undefined unexpectedly after mockClear, or call counts leaking between tests.

seeds.t: jest.mock Is Hoisted to the Top of the File
seeds.w: Placing jest.mock('module') below imports expecting it to apply after the import — Babel/Jest hoists jest.mock calls before imports, so variables referenced in the mock factory may not be initialised yet.
seeds.c: Use jest.mock at the top of the file (though it will be hoisted regardless). Access variables inside the factory via require() inside the factory, or use jest.mock with a manual mock in __mocks__. Factory variables must be prefixed with mock to opt out of the 'out of scope' vari…
seeds.s: 'The module factory of jest.mock() is not allowed to reference any out-of-scope variables' error.

seeds.t: Return or Await Promises in Async Tests
seeds.w: Calling an async function inside a test without await or return — Jest considers the test complete when the synchronous code finishes, before the promise resolves, making assertions never run (and tests pass falsely).
seeds.c: Either await the promise: await expect(asyncFn()).resolves.toBe('value'), or return it: return expect(asyncFn()).resolves.toBe('value'). Use async/await in the test function for readability. Add expect.assertions(1) to fail the test if no assertion runs.
seeds.s: async tests always passing even when assertions would fail because they never execute.

seeds.t: Use jest.spyOn Instead of Reassigning Module Exports
seeds.w: Directly reassigning module.method = jest.fn() to spy on a method — doesn't restore the original, breaks when modules are frozen (ESM), and can pollute other tests.
seeds.c: Use jest.spyOn(object, 'methodName') to create a spy that wraps the original method and can be restored: const spy = jest.spyOn(console, 'error').mockImplementation(() => {}); ... ; spy.mockRestore(). Call spy.mockRestore() in afterEach or use restoreMocks: true in jest config.
seeds.s: spied methods not restored between tests, affecting subsequent tests in the suite.

seeds.t: Use jest.useFakeTimers for Time-Dependent Code
seeds.w: Using real timers in tests with setTimeout or setInterval — tests become slow, flaky, and race-condition-prone; a 5-second timeout makes the test suite 5 seconds slower.
seeds.c: Call jest.useFakeTimers() (or configure in jest.config) to replace setTimeout, setInterval, Date, and related APIs with controllable fakes. Advance time manually: jest.advanceTimersByTime(1000) or jest.runAllTimers(). Use jest.useRealTimers() in afterEach. Prefer the modern impl…
seeds.s: test suite slow due to real timer waits, or flaky tests from timer race conditions.

seeds.t: Use test.each for Parameterised Test Cases
seeds.w: Writing nearly identical test functions differing only in input values — duplicated code that's easy to miss when adding new cases.
seeds.c: Use test.each([[input1, expected1], [input2, expected2]])('adds %i + %i = %i', (a, b, result) => { expect(add(a, b)).toBe(result); }). Or use a tagged template literal table for readability. Also available as describe.each for grouped parametrised tests.
seeds.s: test file with 10 near-identical tests that differ only in their values.

seeds.t: Snapshots Must Be Reviewed, Not Just Updated Blindly
seeds.w: Running jest --updateSnapshot to fix failing snapshot tests without reading the diff — snapshots become rubber stamps rather than meaningful regression guards.
seeds.c: Review snapshot diffs carefully before updating. Use inline snapshots (toMatchInlineSnapshot()) for small outputs so changes are visible in code review. Prefer explicit assertions for critical logic over snapshots. Add snapshot files to git to track changes over time. Limit snap…
seeds.s: snapshot tests always passing because developers update snapshots without reviewing them.

seeds.t: Use expect.assertions to Guard Async Test Paths
seeds.w: Writing async tests without expect.assertions — if the async code path is never reached (due to a bug), no assertions run and the test passes silently.
seeds.c: Add expect.assertions(2) at the top of tests with async assertions to declare the exact number expected. The test fails if fewer assertions run. Use expect.hasAssertions() as a looser check that at least one assertion ran. Particularly important in try/catch blocks where the cat…
seeds.s: test passes even though the code under test threw an exception and the assertions in the try block never ran.

seeds.t: Use mockResolvedValue for Async Function Mocks
seeds.w: Using mockReturnValue(Promise.resolve(data)) for mocking async functions — verbose and error-prone.
seeds.c: Use mockResolvedValue(data) as shorthand for mockReturnValue(Promise.resolve(data)), and mockRejectedValue(error) for rejected promises. Use mockResolvedValueOnce for sequential different values. Use mockImplementation(async () => { ... }) for complex async logic.
seeds.s: tests using the verbose Promise.resolve() wrapping pattern when jest provides dedicated async mock helpers.

seeds.t: Use setupFilesAfterFramework for Global Test Setup
seeds.w: Importing setup logic at the top of every test file or using setupFiles for setup that requires jest globals — setupFiles runs before the test framework is installed so jest.* APIs are not available there.
seeds.c: Use setupFilesAfterFramework in jest config for global setup that uses jest APIs: jest.setTimeout(10000), custom matchers via expect.extend(), global mocks. Use setupFiles for polyfills that must load before the framework (e.g. TextEncoder).
seeds.s: 'jest is not defined' in a setupFiles file because jest globals aren't available yet.

seeds.t: Set testEnvironment to Match Your Runtime Target
seeds.w: Leaving testEnvironment as the default jsdom for a Node.js backend test — jsdom emulates a browser DOM which is unnecessary overhead and may interfere with Node-specific APIs.
seeds.c: Set testEnvironment: 'node' in jest config for backend/Node tests (faster, no DOM). Use testEnvironment: 'jsdom' for React/browser tests (DOM APIs available). Override per-file with @jest-environment node docblock. Use jest-environment-miniflare for Cloudflare Workers.
seeds.s: backend tests failing on DOM-related globals, or unnecessary jsdom memory overhead in pure Node test suites.

seeds.t: Use __mocks__ Directory for Automatic Module Mocking
seeds.w: Calling jest.mock('fs') in every file that needs the fs mock — duplicated mock setup across test files.
seeds.c: Create a __mocks__/fs.js file adjacent to node_modules to automatically mock the fs module when jest.mock('fs') is called (or for automocking). For local modules, place __mocks__ next to the module file. Automatic mocking replaces all exports with jest.fn(). Import the manual mo…
seeds.s: identical mock implementations for the same module copy-pasted across dozens of test files.

seeds.t: Use --runInBand to Debug Tests Sequentially
seeds.w: Trying to use console.log or a debugger with tests running in parallel workers — output is interleaved and breakpoints may not work correctly across worker processes.
seeds.c: Use jest --runInBand to run all tests sequentially in the main process, enabling console.log debugging and node --inspect debugger attachment. Add a jest debug script: "test:debug": "node --inspect-brk node_modules/.bin/jest --runInBand". Use --testPathPattern to run a single fi…
seeds.s: console.log output missing or scrambled, breakpoints not hit when debugging jest tests.

seeds.t: Extend expect With Custom Matchers for Domain Assertions
seeds.w: Writing the same complex assertion chain in multiple tests: expect(response.status).toBe(200); expect(response.headers['content-type']).toContain('application/json') — duplicated and less readable.
seeds.c: Add custom matchers with expect.extend({ toBeJsonResponse(received) { ... } }) in setupFilesAfterFramework. Use them as: expect(response).toBeJsonResponse(). Custom matchers integrate with Jest's diff output on failure. Publish shared matchers as packages (jest-extended, jest-do…
seeds.s: complex repeated assertion patterns that a named custom matcher would express clearly.

seeds.t: Set Coverage Thresholds to Enforce Minimum Coverage
seeds.w: Measuring coverage without enforcing it — coverage reports become informational only and coverage silently drops over time.
seeds.c: Set coverage thresholds in jest config: coverageThreshold: { global: { branches: 80, functions: 85, lines: 85, statements: 85 } }. Jest fails the run if any threshold is not met. Set per-file thresholds for critical modules. Use coveragePathIgnorePatterns to exclude generated co…
