seeds.s: coverage metric declining sprint-over-sprint because there's no CI gate enforcing the minimum.

seeds.t: Use jest.isolateModules for Per-Test Module State
seeds.w: Expecting jest.resetModules() between tests to work when modules are already imported at the top of the file — top-level imports are cached for the entire file.
seeds.c: Use jest.isolateModules(() => { const module = require('./myModule'); }) to get a fresh module instance with a clean module registry for that scope. Use jest.resetModules() + require() inside beforeEach when you need different module state per test.
seeds.s: module singleton state bleeding between tests because the module cache was not cleared.

seeds.t: Use rejects.toThrow for Async Function Errors
seeds.w: Using expect(asyncFn()).toThrow() for async functions — this tests whether the Promise construction throws synchronously, not whether the promise rejects.
seeds.c: Use await expect(asyncFn()).rejects.toThrow('expected message') or await expect(asyncFn()).rejects.toBeInstanceOf(ErrorClass). For testing rejected values: await expect(promise).rejects.toMatchObject({ code: 'NOT_FOUND' }).
seeds.s: async functions that reject being tested with synchronous toThrow, causing tests to pass even when the rejection type is wrong.

seeds.t: Use beforeAll for Expensive Setup, beforeEach for State Reset
seeds.w: Using beforeEach to set up an expensive resource (database connection, server startup) that could be shared — re-creates it for every single test.
seeds.c: Use beforeAll for expensive, shareable setup (database connection, test server). Use beforeEach for resetting mutable state (clearing mock call counts, resetting test data) that must be fresh per test. Pair with afterAll/afterEach respectively for teardown. Tests inside one desc…
seeds.s: test suite taking 10x longer than necessary because a server is restarted for every test.

seeds.t: Use mockImplementationOnce for Sequential Different Behaviors
seeds.w: Overwriting mock.mockImplementation() mid-test to simulate different responses — brittle and order-dependent.
seeds.c: Chain mockImplementationOnce calls before the test to define the sequence of return values: mock.mockImplementationOnce(() => firstResponse).mockImplementationOnce(() => secondResponse). After all Once implementations are consumed, mockImplementation (the default) takes over. Us…
seeds.s: complex mock state management between calls within a single test.

seeds.t: Configure transform for TypeScript and ESM Support
seeds.w: Running Jest on TypeScript files without a transform configured — Jest can't parse TypeScript syntax and throws SyntaxError.
seeds.c: Use ts-jest (transform: { '^.+\.tsx?$': 'ts-jest' }) or @swc/jest for faster transpilation (transform: { '^.+\.[jt]sx?$': '@swc/jest' }). For native ESM, set transform: {} and --experimental-vm-modules in the Node invocation. Configure extensionsToTreatAsEsm for .ts files in ESM…
seeds.s: 'SyntaxError: Unexpected token import' or TypeScript type errors not caught because ts-jest is misconfigured.

seeds.t: JSON Disallows Trailing Commas
seeds.w: Leaving a trailing comma after the last item in an object or array — valid JavaScript but illegal JSON.
seeds.c: Remove trailing commas from all objects and arrays before serialization; use a linter like eslint-plugin-json or jsonlint to catch them. If you need trailing-comma support, use JSONC (JSON with Comments) with a compatible parser.
seeds.s: 'SyntaxError: Unexpected token }' or 'JSON parse error at position N'.

seeds.t: JSON Has No Comment Syntax
seeds.w: Adding // or /* */ comments to JSON files — JSON parsers reject them unconditionally.
seeds.c: Use JSONC (.jsonc extension, supported by VS Code and many toolchains) for config files that need comments. Strip comments with a pre-processor before passing to a standard parser. Alternatively, use YAML or TOML for comment-heavy config.
seeds.s: JSON parse error on a config file that looks valid in the editor.

seeds.t: Numbers Above 2^53 Lose Precision in JSON
seeds.w: Serializing 64-bit integer IDs or timestamps as JSON numbers — JavaScript's IEEE 754 doubles cannot exactly represent integers above 2^53, silently corrupting large IDs.
seeds.c: Serialize large integers as strings and parse them back with a BigInt-aware library or language-native 64-bit integer support. Document the type in your API schema.
seeds.s: database IDs returned from a server are subtly wrong on the client, e.g., last two digits become zero.

seeds.t: Floating-Point Numbers Are Not Exact in JSON
seeds.w: Storing currency amounts, measurements, or other exact decimals as JSON floating-point numbers — 0.1 + 0.2 is not 0.3 in IEEE 754.
seeds.c: Represent monetary values as integers (cents/pence) or as strings with a fixed decimal format; parse with a decimal library. For scientific data, document precision and acceptable rounding.
seeds.s: prices like 19.99 serialized as 19.990000000000002.

seeds.t: Always Wrap JSON.parse in Try/Catch
seeds.w: Calling JSON.parse(str) without error handling — any malformed input throws a SyntaxError that crashes the calling code.
seeds.c: Wrap JSON.parse in a try/catch (or use a safe-parse utility that returns a Result/Either type). Validate the parsed value against a schema (Zod, JSON Schema) before use. Never assume network or file JSON is valid.
seeds.s: uncaught SyntaxError crashing a request handler from a single malformed client payload.

seeds.t: null Value and Absent Key Have Different Semantics
seeds.w: Treating {"field": null} and {} (key absent) as equivalent in APIs and parsers — receivers can't distinguish 'explicitly unset' from 'not provided'.
seeds.c: Define a consistent API contract: use absent keys for optional fields not provided, and null for explicitly cleared values. In patch APIs (JSON Merge Patch, RFC 7396) null means delete, absent means unchanged.
seeds.s: PATCH operations unexpectedly deleting fields that the client just didn't include.

seeds.t: Use ISO 8601 Strings for All Date/Time Values
seeds.w: Serializing dates as Unix timestamps (integer seconds), locale-formatted strings ('01/12/2024'), or Date.toString() output — ambiguous timezone, locale, and epoch differences break cross-system compatibility.
seeds.c: Use ISO 8601 format with explicit timezone offset: '2024-01-12T15:30:00Z' or '2024-01-12T15:30:00+05:30'. Parse with a timezone-aware library on the receiving end.
seeds.s: dates off by hours in different timezones, or 'Invalid Date' from locale-specific strings.

seeds.t: JSON.stringify Throws on Circular References
seeds.w: Calling JSON.stringify on an object graph with circular references (e.g., DOM nodes, ORM models with back-references) — throws 'Converting circular structure to JSON'.
seeds.c: Use a replacer function to break cycles, or use a library like flatted or json-stringify-safe for circular-aware serialization. Alternatively, map to a plain DTO before serializing.
seeds.s: 'TypeError: Converting circular structure to JSON' at serialization time.

seeds.t: Stream Large JSON Files Instead of Loading Fully
seeds.w: Reading an entire multi-GB JSON file into memory with JSON.parse or json.load before processing — causes OOM errors or extreme GC pressure.
seeds.c: Use a streaming JSON parser (jsonstream in Node.js, ijson in Python, RxJava Jackson streaming API) to process records one at a time. For line-delimited data, use NDJSON format (one JSON object per line) which parsers can process line-by-line trivially.
seeds.s: process OOM or multi-second pauses loading large datasets.

seeds.t: Validate JSON Against a Schema, Not Manually
seeds.w: Writing hand-coded if/else checks to validate every field type, presence, and value — error-prone, incomplete, and tedious to maintain.
seeds.c: Define a JSON Schema (draft-07 or 2020-12) and validate with a compliant library (Ajv, jsonschema, Pydantic). In TypeScript, use Zod or Valibot to derive both the schema and the static type from one definition.
seeds.s: missing validations discovered in production, or inconsistent error messages across endpoints.

seeds.t: JSON Object Key Order Is Not Guaranteed
seeds.w: Relying on JSON key insertion order for logic, display, or canonical hashing — the JSON spec defines objects as unordered sets of key-value pairs.
seeds.c: Sort keys explicitly before serialization if order matters (e.g., for canonical signing): JSON.stringify(obj, Object.keys(obj).sort()). Use arrays of objects for ordered sequences.
seeds.s: JSON signatures mismatch across languages, or display order different between platforms.

seeds.t: Encode Binary Data as Base64 Strings in JSON
seeds.w: Embedding raw binary bytes directly in a JSON string — bytes outside ASCII are not valid JSON and corrupt on parse.
seeds.c: Encode binary data as a base64 string and decode it on the receiving end. Document the encoding in your schema. For large blobs, prefer a URL reference or multipart upload rather than base64 inline (base64 inflates size by ~33%).
seeds.s: binary fields corrupted or parse errors when images/files are included in JSON.

seeds.t: Use JSON Boolean Literals Not Strings
seeds.w: Representing boolean values as the strings 'true' or 'false' — receivers must parse the string manually and 'false' is truthy in most languages.
seeds.c: Use JSON's native boolean literals true and false (lowercase, no quotes). Audit APIs that return '1'/'0' or 'yes'/'no' booleans for consistency.
seeds.s: 'false' string evaluated as truthy in JavaScript, or extra parsing boilerplate for string-booleans.

seeds.t: Use NDJSON for Streaming or Log Line Formats
seeds.w: Writing logs or event streams as a JSON array in a file — the entire file must be loaded and parsed to read any record, and a write failure mid-stream corrupts the whole file.
seeds.c: Use Newline Delimited JSON (NDJSON / JSON Lines): one complete JSON object per line. Each line can be parsed independently; new records append safely. Tools like jq, grep, and most log processors handle NDJSON natively.
seeds.s: logs unreadable if the process crashes before closing the array.

seeds.t: Set Content-Type application/json for JSON Responses
seeds.w: Returning a JSON body with Content-Type: text/plain or omitting the header — clients may parse the body as text, and HTTP intermediaries won't cache or compress it correctly.
seeds.c: Always set Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8 on JSON responses. On the request side, set Accept: application/json and Content-Type: application/json for request bodies.
seeds.s: clients silently treating a JSON response as a string, or no automatic gzip compression on the response.

seeds.t: Use JSON.parse Reviver to Transform Values at Parse Time
seeds.w: Parsing JSON then walking the entire object tree to convert date strings to Date objects or BigInts — a second pass over the whole structure.
seeds.c: Pass a reviver function as the second argument to JSON.parse to transform values during parsing in one pass. Similarly, use a replacer function with JSON.stringify to control serialization (e.g., omitting undefined fields or converting BigInts to strings).
seeds.s: post-parse transformations that duplicate traversal work.

seeds.t: Object.assign and Spread Do Shallow Merge Only
seeds.w: Using Object.assign({}, base, overrides) or {...base, ...overrides} to merge nested JSON config objects — nested objects from base are replaced entirely, not merged.
seeds.c: Use a deep merge library (lodash.merge, deepmerge) or implement recursive merging for nested config. Be aware that arrays in deep merge have different semantics: replace vs concatenate vs union.
seeds.s: nested config keys from the base being silently dropped by a shallow spread.

seeds.t: Use JSON Patch for Partial Updates to JSON Documents
seeds.w: Sending the entire modified JSON document for a partial update, or constructing ad-hoc diff logic to compute what changed.
seeds.c: Use JSON Patch (RFC 6902) for structured partial updates: an array of operations (add, remove, replace, move, copy, test) applied atomically. Libraries like fast-json-patch (JS) or jsonpatch (Python) implement both applying and generating patches.
seeds.s: large PUT payloads for single-field changes, or custom diff code that misses edge cases.

seeds.t: undefined Values Are Silently Dropped by JSON.stringify
seeds.w: Relying on an object property set to undefined being serialized — JSON.stringify omits properties with undefined values silently, while null is kept.
seeds.c: Replace undefined with null before serialization if the field must be present, or omit the key intentionally and document the API contract. Use a schema validator after parse to detect absent required fields.
seeds.s: object fields silently absent in serialized JSON when set to undefined instead of null.

seeds.t: JSON Must Use \uXXXX for Non-ASCII Control Characters
seeds.w: Embedding raw control characters (ASCII 0–31) or unescaped U+2028/U+2029 line separators in JSON strings — strict parsers reject them and some JavaScript engines treat the latter as line terminators.
seeds.c: Escape non-printable characters as \uXXXX sequences; most serialization libraries do this automatically. Ensure your serializer handles the edge case of U+2028 and U+2029 inside string literals.
seeds.s: JSON parse errors from control characters in log messages or user-supplied text.

seeds.t: Consumer Groups Enable Parallel Processing Across Partitions
seeds.w: Running multiple consumers with the same group.id expecting all to receive every message — only one consumer per group receives each message; extra consumers above the partition count sit idle.
seeds.c: Partition count determines maximum parallelism within a group. Scale consumers up to the number of partitions. For broadcast (all consumers receive all messages), use different group IDs. For horizontal scaling, increase partition count first: kafka-topics.sh --alter --partition…
seeds.s: consumer instances above the partition count receiving no messages, wasting resources.

seeds.t: Disable Auto Offset Commit to Prevent Message Loss
seeds.w: Using enable.auto.commit=true (the default) — offsets are committed on a timer regardless of whether processing succeeded; if the consumer crashes after commit but before processing completes, messages are lost.
seeds.c: Set enable.auto.commit=false and commit offsets explicitly after successful processing: consumer.commitSync() after processing each batch, or commitAsync() with an error callback. Use at-least-once delivery (process then commit) and make processing idempotent. For exactly-once, …
seeds.s: messages silently dropped during consumer restarts because auto-commit fired before processing finished.

seeds.t: Set acks=all for Durable Message Production
seeds.w: Leaving acks=1 (default) — the leader broker acknowledges writes but replication to followers is asynchronous; if the leader fails before replication, messages are lost.
seeds.c: Set acks=all (or acks=-1) so the producer waits for all in-sync replicas to acknowledge. Combine with min.insync.replicas=2 on the topic to ensure at least 2 replicas confirm the write. Set retries=Integer.MAX_VALUE and enable.idempotence=true to handle transient failures withou…
seeds.s: messages lost during a broker failover because acks=1 allowed the leader to acknowledge before replication completed.

seeds.t: Partition Keys Determine Ordering and Load Distribution
seeds.w: Using a high-cardinality key (UUID, timestamp) that distributes messages evenly but breaks the ordering requirement, or using a constant key that puts all messages on one partition.
seeds.c: Choose partition keys based on your ordering requirement: use the entity ID (user_id, order_id) when messages for the same entity must be ordered. This guarantees ordering within a partition at the cost of potential skew if some entities produce far more messages. Use null key f…
seeds.s: messages for the same user arriving out of order because different partition keys were used, or one partition at 100% load while others are idle.

seeds.t: Enable Idempotent Producer to Prevent Duplicate Messages
seeds.w: Retrying on producer failure without idempotence — the broker may have received the message before the failure, and the retry creates a duplicate.
seeds.c: Set enable.idempotence=true on the producer. Kafka assigns a producer ID and sequence number to each message; the broker deduplicates retries within a session. This is now the default in Kafka 3.0+. For cross-session exactly-once (transactions), use transactional.id and initTran…
seeds.s: duplicate database records from Kafka messages produced during broker reconnection without idempotence enabled.

seeds.t: Use Schema Registry to Enforce and Evolve Message Contracts
seeds.w: Serialising Kafka messages as raw JSON strings — no schema enforcement, producers can send malformed messages that crash consumers, and schema changes break consumers silently.
seeds.c: Use Confluent Schema Registry with Avro, Protobuf, or JSON Schema. Producers register schemas and serialise with the schema ID embedded in the message. Consumers deserialise using the same registry. Schema Registry enforces compatibility rules: BACKWARD (consumers can read old m…
seeds.s: consumer crashing with deserialization errors because a producer added a required field without coordinating with consumers.

seeds.t: Route Unprocessable Messages to a Dead Letter Topic
seeds.w: Letting a consumer crash on a poison pill message (malformed, unexpected format) repeatedly — the consumer keeps restarting, processing the same bad message, blocking all subsequent messages.
seeds.c: Catch deserialization and processing exceptions; publish the failed message to a dead letter topic (e.g., original-topic.DLT) with metadata headers (original topic, error type, stack trace). Continue processing from the next offset. Monitor the DLT and implement a replay mechani…
seeds.s: consumer stuck in a restart loop from one malformed message, blocking all subsequent messages in that partition.

seeds.t: Choose Retention Policy Based on Topic Semantics
seeds.w: Using the default time-based delete retention for all topics — inappropriate for topics representing current state (like a user profile changelog) where old messages should be retained until superseded.
seeds.c: delete policy: retain messages for a time period or size limit — suitable for event streams and logs. compact policy: retain only the latest message per key — suitable for changelog topics, cache invalidation, and materialised views. Set cleanup.policy=compact,delete to compact …
seeds.s: consumer rebuilt from a compacted topic missing records because compaction removed messages that should have been the current state.

seeds.t: Monitor Consumer Lag as the Primary Health Metric
seeds.w: Monitoring only broker health and ignoring consumer lag — a healthy broker with slow consumers causes ever-growing lag until messages expire and are lost.
seeds.c: Monitor consumer group lag: kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe --group my-group shows LAG per partition. Set up alerts when lag exceeds a threshold (e.g., 10,000 messages for a real-time consumer). Use Burrow, Kafka Exporter (Prometheus), or Confluent Control Center for automat…
seeds.s: messages being lost because lag grew until old messages hit retention expiry, with no alert triggered.

seeds.t: Tune batch.size and linger.ms for Producer Throughput
seeds.w: Leaving default batch.size=16384 and linger.ms=0 for high-throughput producers — each message is sent immediately as a single-record batch, creating unnecessary network overhead.
seeds.c: Increase batch.size (bytes) to accumulate more messages per batch: 65536 or 131072 for high-volume topics. Set linger.ms=5–20 to wait briefly for more messages to accumulate before sending. Also set compression.type=lz4 or snappy to reduce network and storage cost. These setting…
seeds.s: producer throughput limited to a few thousand messages per second when tuned settings would enable millions.

seeds.t: Minimise Rebalance Frequency to Reduce Processing Pauses
seeds.w: Leaving short session.timeout.ms and heartbeat.interval.ms for consumer groups — frequent false-positive rebalances pause consumption as partitions are reassigned.
seeds.c: Set session.timeout.ms=30000–45000 (how long before the broker considers a consumer dead) and heartbeat.interval.ms=session.timeout.ms/3. Set max.poll.interval.ms high enough to accommodate the longest processing time. Use Static Group Membership (group.instance.id) to skip full…
seeds.s: consumer lag spikes every few minutes as rebalances pause all processing in the group.

seeds.t: Use Kafka Transactions for Exactly-Once Processing
seeds.w: Implementing exactly-once by manually deduplicating at the application level — complex, error-prone, and doesn't account for all failure scenarios.
seeds.c: For read-process-write patterns: use Kafka Streams (built-in EOS) or configure transactions manually: set transactional.id on the producer, call initTransactions(), beginTransaction(), process and produce within the transaction, commitTransaction() (or abortTransaction() on fail…
seeds.s: duplicate records in downstream systems from consumer restarts during the read-process-write cycle.

seeds.t: Set Replication Factor of 3 for High Availability
seeds.w: Creating topics with replication.factor=1 (no redundancy) in production — a single broker failure loses all data for that topic.
seeds.c: Set replication.factor=3 for production topics so data survives the loss of up to 2 brokers. Set min.insync.replicas=2 so the producer fails rather than accepting a write to only 1 replica. Set unclean.leader.election.enable=false to prevent data loss from electing an out-of-syn…
seeds.s: complete data loss for a topic when a single broker disk failed because replication factor was 1.

seeds.t: Use Kafka Streams for Stateful Stream Processing
seeds.w: Implementing stateful processing (aggregations, joins, windowing) in a plain consumer by managing state in an external database — complex coordination with Kafka offsets, fault tolerance is manual.
seeds.c: Use Kafka Streams (embedded JVM library) or ksqlDB for stateful operations: KStream.groupByKey().windowedBy(TimeWindows.ofSizeWithNoGrace(Duration.ofMinutes(5))).count(). State is stored in local RocksDB backed by a changelog Kafka topic — automatically replicated and restored o…
seeds.s: complex manual state management in a consumer that Kafka Streams windowed aggregations handle declaratively.

seeds.t: Use Message Headers for Metadata Without Polluting the Payload
seeds.w: Embedding metadata (correlation IDs, source service, event type, timestamps) directly in the message value schema — pollutes the domain schema, and metadata fields vary by infrastructure concern.
seeds.c: Use Kafka message headers for infrastructure metadata: headers.add('correlation-id', correlationId.getBytes()); headers.add('source-service', 'user-service'). Headers are accessible to consumers without deserialising the full payload. Useful for routing, tracing, and filtering w…
seeds.s: Avro schema containing correlation_id and timestamp fields that belong in headers, not the domain payload.

seeds.t: Establish a Topic Naming Convention at the Start
seeds.w: Creating topics with ad-hoc names (user-events, UserEvents, events_v2) — inconsistent naming makes discovery, ACLs, and monitoring difficult as the number of topics grows.
seeds.c: Adopt a naming convention early: {domain}.{entity}.{event-type} (e.g., ecommerce.orders.placed, ecommerce.orders.shipped). Or {env}.{team}.{topic} for environment and team scoping. Encode version in the name (ecommerce.orders.v2.placed) for incompatible schema changes. Apply nam…
seeds.s: 200 topics with no discernible pattern, making it impossible to find the right topic without asking the team who created it.

seeds.t: Use Kafka Connect for Source and Sink Integrations
seeds.w: Writing custom producer/consumer code to sync between Kafka and databases, S3, or Elasticsearch — high maintenance, requires reimplementing exactly-once delivery, error handling, and schema management.
seeds.c: Use Kafka Connect with community or Confluent connectors: Debezium for CDC (change data capture) from PostgreSQL/MySQL, S3 Sink connector for data lake, Elasticsearch Sink for search indexing. Connect handles offset management, parallelism via tasks, and failure recovery. Run as…
seeds.s: custom code syncing a database table to Kafka that Debezium's CDC connector would handle with one JSON configuration.

seeds.t: Enable SASL and TLS for Production Kafka Security
seeds.w: Running Kafka with no authentication or encryption in production — any client on the network can read and write to any topic.
seeds.c: Enable TLS (SSL listener) for in-transit encryption between clients and brokers and between brokers. Enable SASL for authentication: SASL/SCRAM-SHA-256 for username/password, SASL/OAUTHBEARER for token-based auth, or mTLS for certificate-based auth. Use Kafka ACLs or Confluent R…
seeds.s: internal Kafka cluster accessible from any service without authentication, enabling any team's code to read sensitive financial events.

seeds.t: Tune max.poll.records for Batch Processing Efficiency
seeds.w: Leaving max.poll.records=500 (default) for consumers that process records individually with slow operations — the consumer must process all 500 before calling poll() again, risking session timeout.
seeds.c: Set max.poll.records to match your processing capacity: lower (50–100) for slow per-record processing to stay within max.poll.interval.ms. Higher (1000–5000) for fast batch processing where you can handle many records quickly. Increase max.poll.interval.ms if bulk processing gen…
seeds.s: consumer session timing out because 500 records each took 200ms to process, exceeding the 5-minute max.poll.interval.

seeds.t: Configure Error Handling and DLQ in Kafka Connect
seeds.w: Leaving Kafka Connect with default error handling — a single bad record stops the entire connector task, blocking all subsequent records.
seeds.c: Configure per-connector error handling: errors.tolerance=all to skip bad records, errors.log.enable=true to log failures, errors.deadletterqueue.topic.name=my-topic.DLT to route failed records to a DLQ. Set errors.deadletterqueue.context.headers.enable=true to include error deta…
seeds.s: a single malformed database row stopping an entire Debezium connector, halting CDC for the entire database.

seeds.t: Kotlin null safety — prefer ?: and let over !! operator
seeds.w: using !! to assert non-null — this crashes with NullPointerException if the value is null.
seeds.c: use safe calls (?.), the Elvis operator (?:) for defaults, and let for null-conditional execution: myValue?.let { useIt(it) } ?: fallback(). Reserve !! only for values where null is genuinely a bug that should crash.
seeds.s: NullPointerException at the !! site in production.

seeds.t: Use copy() to create modified data class instances
seeds.w: modifying data class properties directly when they are val — val properties are immutable.
seeds.c: use the generated copy() function to create a new instance with selected properties changed: val updated = original.copy(name = 'new name'). Data classes also generate equals(), hashCode(), and toString() based on all constructor properties.
seeds.s: compile error when trying to assign to a val property, or incorrect equals() behaviour.

seeds.t: Use structured concurrency — launch coroutines in the right scope
seeds.w: launching coroutines in GlobalScope — they escape structured concurrency, live for the lifetime of the process, and cannot be cancelled.
seeds.c: use viewModelScope in ViewModels (Android), lifecycleScope in Activities/Fragments, or create a CoroutineScope tied to the component lifecycle. Scoped coroutines are automatically cancelled when the scope is cancelled.
seeds.s: coroutines continue running after the screen is closed, causing leaks or callbacks on destroyed UI.

seeds.t: Default coroutine dispatcher is not the main thread
seeds.w: assuming coroutines run on the main thread and accessing Android UI from a coroutine launched with Dispatchers.IO or Dispatchers.Default.
seeds.c: switch dispatchers explicitly: withContext(Dispatchers.Main) { updateUI() } for UI work, withContext(Dispatchers.IO) for blocking I/O. Use Dispatchers.Main.immediate when you are already on the main thread.
seeds.s: 'Only the original thread that created a view hierarchy can touch its views' exception.

seeds.t: Prefer Flow over LiveData for new code — collect safely in UI
seeds.w: collecting a Flow directly in a Fragment without lifecycle awareness — the collection continues after the view is destroyed.
seeds.c: use repeatOnLifecycle(Lifecycle.State.STARTED) or flowWithLifecycle(lifecycle) to collect only when the UI is visible. Example: lifecycleScope.launch { repeatOnLifecycle(STARTED) { myFlow.collect { ... } } }.
seeds.s: crashes after fragment is destroyed, or UI updated from background when invisible.

seeds.t: Use sealed classes for exhaustive when expressions
seeds.w: using open classes or interfaces for state/result types and missing cases.
seeds.c: sealed class Result<T> { data class Success<T>(val data: T): Result<T>() data class Error(val msg: String): Result<Nothing>() }. When used as a statement, when must be exhaustive — add else only when needed.
seeds.s: new state subclass added but unhandled case silently falls through.

seeds.t: Extension functions do not override member functions
seeds.w: expecting an extension function to override a class member of the same name — extension functions are resolved statically at compile time, not dynamically. If a class has a member function with the same signature as an extension, the member always wins.
seeds.c: use extension functions to add utility to types you cannot modify, not to override behaviour. Use interfaces or subclassing for polymorphism.
seeds.s: extension function is defined but the member function is called instead.

seeds.t: Use inline + reified to access generic type at runtime
seeds.w: trying to use T::class or is T inside a generic function — generic types are erased at runtime.
seeds.c: mark the function as inline and the type parameter as reified: inline fun <reified T> parseJson(json: String): T = gson.fromJson(json, T::class.java). reified only works with inline functions.
seeds.s: 'Cannot use T as a reified type parameter' compiler error, or ClassCastException at runtime.

seeds.t: Choose the right scope function: let, run, with, apply, also
seeds.w: using apply when you need a return value, or let when you want to configure the object and return it.
seeds.c: use apply to configure an object and return it (obj.apply { x = 1 }); use also for side effects while returning the object (obj.also { log(it) }); use let for null-safe operations on the result (nullable?.let { use(it) }); use run to operate on an object and return a result; use…
seeds.s: scope function chosen arbitrarily causing confusion about return value and receiver.

seeds.t: Use interface delegation to compose behaviour without inheritance
seeds.w: inheriting from a concrete class just to reuse a small portion of its behaviour — Kotlin classes are final by default, and deep inheritance hierarchies are fragile.
seeds.c: use interface delegation with the by keyword: class LoggingList<T>(inner: MutableList<T>) : MutableList<T> by inner { override fun add(e: T): Boolean { log(e); return inner.add(e) } }. The delegated interface methods are forwarded to the inner object automatically; override only…
seeds.s: class inheriting from a base just to reuse logging or caching behaviour that composition via delegation would express cleanly.

seeds.t: Value classes wrap primitives with type safety at zero runtime cost
seeds.w: using String or Long for domain identifiers (UserId, OrderId) — they're interchangeable at the type level, making it easy to pass the wrong ID.
seeds.c: @JvmInline value class UserId(val value: Long) — the compiler represents it as the underlying Long at runtime (no boxing), but the type system treats UserId and OrderId as distinct. Value classes can have methods and implement interfaces. Requires exactly one val property.
seeds.s: function accepting a userId and an orderId as Long — easy to transpose arguments silently.

seeds.t: Flow is cold; SharedFlow and StateFlow are hot
seeds.w: sharing a cold Flow between multiple collectors expecting them all to receive the same events — each collector restarts the flow from scratch, triggering the producer multiple times.
seeds.c: use shareIn(scope, SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed()) to convert a cold Flow to a hot SharedFlow that multicast to all collectors. Use stateIn to convert to StateFlow with a current-value cache. Cold Flow is fine for single-consumer data pipelines; hot flows for UI state and even…
seeds.s: an API call is made once per subscriber because a cold Flow is shared without shareIn.

seeds.t: Explicitly switch dispatchers instead of relying on the caller's context
seeds.w: calling withContext(Dispatchers.IO) from a function that already runs on the Main dispatcher and forgetting to switch back — or writing a suspend function that performs blocking IO on the Main thread.
seeds.c: library functions should switch to the appropriate dispatcher internally: suspend fun loadData() = withContext(Dispatchers.IO) { /* blocking IO */ }. Don't force callers to know which dispatcher a function needs. Dispatchers.Default for CPU-intensive work, Dispatchers.IO for blo…
seeds.s: UI freezes because a suspend function does blocking network or file IO on the Main dispatcher.

seeds.t: when on sealed classes is exhaustive — use it as an expression
seeds.w: using if/else chains or adding an else branch to a when statement on a sealed class — you lose the compiler's exhaustiveness guarantee, so new subclasses aren't caught at compile time.
seeds.c: use when(result) as an expression without else: val text = when(result) { is Success -> result.data; is Error -> result.message }. The compiler enforces that every subclass is handled. To force expression-form on a statement, use a .exhaustive extension property.
seeds.s: when on a sealed class has an else branch that silently swallows new subtypes added later.

seeds.t: Extension functions don't override member functions with same signature
seeds.w: writing an extension function with the same name and signature as an existing member function expecting it to override — member functions always win at call resolution.
seeds.c: extension functions are resolved statically based on the declared (not runtime) type of the receiver; they cannot override or be overridden. They can shadow member functions only if called on a supertype reference where the member isn't declared. Use extension functions for util…
seeds.s: extension function is defined but the member function of the same name is always called instead.

seeds.t: Use string templates with expressions for clean string building
seeds.w: string concatenation with + or StringBuilder for simple interpolation: "Hello, " + name + "! You have " + count + " messages.".
seeds.c: Kotlin string templates support full expressions: "Hello, $name! You have ${messages.count { !it.read }} unread messages.". Use ${} for any expression, $ for simple identifiers. For multi-line strings, use triple-quoted strings with trimIndent(). Reserve StringBuilder for perfor…
seeds.s: string concatenation with + where Kotlin templates would be cleaner and less error-prone.

seeds.t: Data classes support destructuring via componentN() functions
seeds.w: accessing data class properties individually: val name = user.name; val age = user.age — verbose when you need multiple fields.
seeds.c: use destructuring declarations: val (name, age) = user. The order matches the primary constructor parameter order. Use _ to skip components: val (_, age) = user. In lambda parameters: users.map { (name, age) -> "$name is $age" }. For maps, Map.Entry also destructures into (key, …
seeds.s: multiple property accesses on a data class where a single destructuring declaration would be cleaner.

seeds.t: Overload operators meaningfully — don't abuse operator conventions
seeds.w: overloading + to mean something unrelated to addition (e.g. database append) just because the syntax looks clean — it violates the principle of least surprise.
seeds.c: overload operators only when the semantics are universally clear: Vector + Vector for addition, Matrix * scalar for scaling, in for containment (contains), [] for index access (get/set). Use the operator keyword. For domain-specific operations, prefer named functions over obscur…
seeds.s: code that uses operator overloads in a way that confuses colleagues about the operation being performed.

seeds.t: Always Set Resource Requests and Limits
seeds.w: Deploying pods without resources.requests and resources.limits — the scheduler cannot place pods correctly and one runaway pod can starve the whole node.
seeds.c: Set requests (what the scheduler reserves) and limits (the hard cap) for both CPU and memory on every container. Use LimitRange objects to enforce defaults namespace-wide.
seeds.s: OOMKilled pods, noisy-neighbour CPU starvation, or scheduler Pending pods due to no room.

seeds.t: Liveness and Readiness Probes Have Different Jobs
seeds.w: Using the same probe for both liveness and readiness, or skipping probes entirely — unhealthy pods keep receiving traffic, and slow-starting pods get killed before they're ready.
seeds.c: Readiness controls traffic routing (remove from Service endpoints when failing); liveness triggers a pod restart (use only for deadlocks, not normal errors). Add a startupProbe for slow-starting containers to avoid premature liveness kills.
seeds.s: pods restarting in a crash loop or 502 errors during rolling deployments.

seeds.t: Never Use the latest Image Tag in Production
seeds.w: Specifying image: myapp:latest — Kubernetes may pull different image versions across nodes or restarts, making rollbacks impossible.
seeds.c: Always tag images with an immutable identifier (git SHA or semantic version) and set imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent to avoid unnecessary pulls. Pin the digest (image@sha256:...) for absolute immutability.
seeds.s: inconsistent pod versions across nodes or inability to roll back a deploy.

seeds.t: Set securityContext to Prevent Root Containers
seeds.w: Running containers as root (the default) — a container escape gives an attacker root on the node.
seeds.c: Set securityContext.runAsNonRoot: true and runAsUser: <non-zero UID> in the pod spec. Add readOnlyRootFilesystem: true and drop all capabilities, adding back only what is needed. Enforce via OPA/Gatekeeper or a Pod Security Standard.
seeds.s: security audit failures or compliance violations.

seeds.t: Single-Replica Deployments Have Zero Downtime Tolerance
seeds.w: Running replicas: 1 for any service that should be available during node maintenance or rolling updates — the pod is unavailable while it restarts.
seeds.c: Run at least 2 replicas and set a PodDisruptionBudget (minAvailable: 1) to prevent the cluster from evicting all pods simultaneously during drains. Use pod anti-affinity to spread replicas across nodes.
seeds.s: full downtime during kubectl drain or rolling deployment.

seeds.t: Mount Secrets as Files, Not Environment Variables
seeds.w: Injecting secrets via env.valueFrom.secretKeyRef — secret values appear in process listings, crash dumps, and logs more easily.
seeds.c: Mount secrets as volume files with mode 0400; the app reads them from the filesystem. For rotation, a volume mount reflects Secret updates (with some delay) while env vars require a pod restart.
seeds.s: secret values visible in kubectl describe pod or crash log output.

seeds.t: Namespaces Require NetworkPolicies for Real Isolation
seeds.w: Treating namespaces as security boundaries — by default all pods across all namespaces can reach each other.
seeds.c: Apply NetworkPolicy objects to restrict ingress and egress per namespace; deny all by default then allow required paths explicitly. Use RBAC to limit who can access each namespace's resources.
seeds.s: a compromised pod in namespace A reaches services in namespace B freely.

seeds.t: Use apply Not create for Declarative Resource Management
seeds.w: Using kubectl create for ongoing resource management — subsequent changes require delete then create, losing history.
seeds.c: Use kubectl apply -f to declaratively manage resources; it creates or patches based on the last-applied-configuration annotation. In GitOps workflows, use a controller (Argo CD, Flux) rather than direct kubectl.
seeds.s: 'already exists' errors when rerunning create, or stale resources not cleaned up.

seeds.t: HPA Requires Resource Requests to Function
seeds.w: Configuring HorizontalPodAutoscaler without setting resource requests on the target pods — HPA calculates utilization as a percentage of requests; without requests, it cannot compute a ratio.
seeds.c: Set CPU/memory requests, then create an HPA targeting a utilization percentage. Use custom metrics via the Metrics API for application-level autoscaling.
seeds.s: HPA shows 'unknown' current metrics and never scales.

seeds.t: Use Anti-Affinity to Spread Replicas Across Nodes
seeds.w: Not specifying affinity rules — all replicas of a Deployment may land on one node, so a single node failure takes down the whole service.
seeds.c: Add podAntiAffinity with preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution to spread replicas across nodes or zones. Use requiredDuring... only when strict placement is mandatory.
seeds.s: multiple replicas Pending after a node failure or poor availability during maintenance.

seeds.t: Tune maxSurge and maxUnavailable for Zero-Downtime Deploys
seeds.w: Leaving the default RollingUpdate strategy with maxUnavailable: 1 and maxSurge: 1 for a 1-replica deployment — it briefly drops to 0 available pods.
seeds.c: Set maxUnavailable: 0 and maxSurge: 1 to always bring up the new pod before removing the old, at the cost of briefly using extra capacity. Combine with a readiness probe to avoid routing traffic to unready new pods.
seeds.s: brief downtime windows during deployments.

seeds.t: Specify StorageClass Explicitly on PVCs
seeds.w: Omitting storageClassName on a PersistentVolumeClaim and relying on the cluster default — different clusters have different defaults, causing unexpected volume types in different environments.
seeds.c: Always specify storageClassName explicitly; use a fast SSD class for databases and a cheaper class for logs. Ensure the StorageClass supports the required accessMode (ReadWriteOnce vs ReadWriteMany).
seeds.s: PVC stuck in Pending or using a slow/wrong volume type in production.

seeds.t: Use Least-Privilege RBAC for Service Accounts
seeds.w: Giving all pods the default service account with cluster-admin — any compromised pod has full cluster access.
seeds.c: Create a dedicated ServiceAccount per workload, bind it to a Role (namespace-scoped) with only the required verbs and resources, never to ClusterRole cluster-admin. Audit bindings with kubectl auth can-i.
seeds.s: security audit flags broad permissions, or a compromised pod can delete cluster resources.

seeds.t: Set completions and parallelism for Batch Jobs
seeds.w: Running a batch Job without completions or parallelism — Kubernetes runs one pod once and marks the job complete, even for work that should fan out.
seeds.c: Set completions (total pods to succeed) and parallelism (concurrent pods) for work-queue or indexed jobs. Use restartPolicy: OnFailure and set backoffLimit to limit retries.
seeds.s: batch work not parallelized or job succeeds after processing only a fraction of items.

seeds.t: Use initContainers for Setup That Must Complete First
seeds.w: Adding startup logic to the main container with sleep loops waiting for a dependency — couples startup timing to the app and obscures failures.
seeds.c: Use initContainers to wait for dependencies (DB ready, secret populated) or run migrations; they complete sequentially before the main container starts and can use different images.
seeds.s: race condition on startup, or app container crashing until a retry hits the ready dependency.

seeds.t: Terminate TLS at the Ingress Not the Service
seeds.w: Handling TLS inside each application pod — certificate rotation requires redeploying every workload and each team manages certs separately.
seeds.c: Terminate TLS at the Ingress controller using cert-manager to provision and renew Let's Encrypt certificates automatically via tls.secretName. Internal pod-to-pod traffic uses plaintext within the cluster (or mTLS via a service mesh).
seeds.s: manual certificate rotation per-service or TLS misconfiguration under load.

seeds.t: Use nodeSelector or nodeAffinity on DaemonSets for Subsets
seeds.w: Deploying a DaemonSet that runs on every node in the cluster when it only needs to run on specific node pools (e.g., GPU nodes, spot nodes).
seeds.c: Add nodeSelector or nodeAffinity to restrict the DaemonSet to the intended node labels; this prevents wasted resources on irrelevant nodes. Use tolerations to schedule onto tainted nodes.
seeds.s: DaemonSet pods running on nodes where they serve no purpose, consuming CPU and memory.

seeds.t: Use Ephemeral Debug Containers Not exec in Prod
seeds.w: Using kubectl exec to run debugging tools inside a running production pod — mutates the running container, may not have tools installed, and the change is not audited.
seeds.c: Use kubectl debug to attach an ephemeral container (Kubernetes 1.23+) to a running pod with a debug image; it shares the process namespace without modifying the original container. Prefer distroless production images and a separate debug image.
seeds.s: production pods modified during incident investigation, lacking debugging tools.

seeds.t: Apply ResourceQuota to Prevent Namespace Overconsumption
seeds.w: Running multiple teams in a shared cluster with no quotas — one team's deployment can exhaust all cluster CPU and memory, starving others.
seeds.c: Create ResourceQuota objects per namespace to cap total requests, limits, and object counts. Pair with LimitRange to set default container limits. Communicate quotas to teams as part of namespace provisioning.
seeds.s: cluster-wide resource exhaustion caused by a single namespace's runaway workload.

seeds.t: Mark Stable ConfigMaps Immutable for Performance
seeds.w: Leaving all ConfigMaps mutable — the kubelet watches every ConfigMap for changes, adding API server load at scale.
seeds.c: Set immutable: true on ConfigMaps and Secrets that are not expected to change; Kubernetes stops watching them, reducing load significantly in clusters with thousands of pods. To update, create a new ConfigMap with a new name and roll the deployment.
seeds.s: API server load growing with ConfigMap count in large clusters.

seeds.t: Eager Load Relationships to Avoid N+1 Queries
seeds.w: Accessing a relationship inside a loop on a collection — Post::all() then $post->author->name inside foreach causes one query per post.
seeds.c: Eager load with with(): Post::with('author')->get(). For nested relationships: Post::with('author.profile')->get(). Use load() on an existing collection: $posts->load('comments'). Use withCount() for relationship counts. Enable the query log in development: DB::enableQueryLog().
seeds.s: 101 queries for 100 posts visible in Debugbar or query log.

seeds.t: Use Form Requests for Validation and Authorization
seeds.w: Validating requests inline in controller methods with $request->validate() — duplicated when the same validation is needed in multiple places, and mixes validation logic with controller logic.
seeds.c: Create dedicated Form Request classes: php artisan make:request StorePostRequest. Implement rules() for validation rules and authorize() for authorization checks. Type-hint the Form Request in the controller method — Laravel injects and validates automatically, returning 422 wit…
seeds.s: validation rules duplicated across multiple controller methods that FormRequest would centralise.

seeds.t: Bind Interfaces to Implementations in the Service Container
seeds.w: Type-hinting concrete classes throughout the application — tightly coupled code that's hard to test (can't swap in a fake) and change (must update every constructor).
seeds.c: Define interfaces for services and bind implementations in a ServiceProvider: $this->app->bind(PaymentGatewayInterface::class, StripeGateway::class). Type-hint the interface in controllers and services. For testing, swap with: $this->app->instance(PaymentGatewayInterface::class,…
seeds.s: every test making real Stripe API calls because the concrete class can't be swapped without changing the constructor type hint.

seeds.t: Make Queued Jobs Idempotent to Handle Retries Safely
seeds.w: Writing queue jobs that charge a payment, send an email, or modify a record without guarding against re-execution — jobs are retried on failure and may run multiple times.
seeds.c: Design jobs to be idempotent: check if the action was already performed before acting (lookup a completed_at flag). Use unique jobs with ShouldBeUnique to prevent duplicate dispatches. Use database transactions for state changes. Check the job's attempt number with $this->attemp…
seeds.s: customer charged twice when a payment job failed after charging but before marking complete, then was retried.

seeds.t: Use Policies for Model-Level Authorization
seeds.w: Scattering if ($user->id !== $post->user_id) abort(403) checks throughout controllers — duplicated, easy to miss, and hard to audit.
seeds.c: Create a policy: php artisan make:policy PostPolicy --model=Post. Implement view(), update(), delete() methods returning bool. Register in AuthServiceProvider or via #[Policy] attribute. Use $this->authorize('update', $post) in controllers, or @can('update', $post) in Blade, or …
seeds.s: authorization logic spread across controllers making it impossible to audit who can do what.

seeds.t: Use Query Scopes for Reusable Query Constraints
seeds.w: Repeating where('status', 'active')->where('published_at', '<=', now()) throughout multiple controllers and services.
seeds.c: Define local scopes on the model: public function scopePublished(Builder $query): void { $query->where('status', 'active')->where('published_at', '<=', now()); }. Call with Post::published()->paginate(). For global scopes that always apply (soft deletes, tenant scoping), impleme…
seeds.s: the same five-condition query constraint copied into six controller methods.

seeds.t: Use Laravel Collections for Expressive Data Manipulation
seeds.w: Using PHP array functions (array_map, array_filter, usort) on data that could be a Collection — less readable and chainable.
seeds.c: Use collect($data) to wrap arrays and Eloquent returns Collections automatically. Chain: collect($items)->filter(fn($i) => $i->active)->sortBy('name')->values()->map(fn($i) => $i->toArray()). Key methods: pluck(), groupBy(), keyBy(), chunk(), first(), each(), reduce(), zip(). La…
seeds.s: multi-step array transformation using four PHP array functions when three chained Collection methods would be clearer.

seeds.t: Wrap Multi-Step Operations in Database Transactions
seeds.w: Performing multiple database writes sequentially without a transaction — if the second write fails, the first is committed, leaving the database inconsistent.
seeds.c: Use DB::transaction(function() { /* all writes */ }) or manually manage with DB::beginTransaction(), DB::commit(), DB::rollBack() in a try/catch. Eloquent operations inside the transaction closure participate automatically. Use transaction(callback, attempts: 3) for automatic re…
seeds.s: order created but inventory not decremented when the inventory update failed after the order was committed.

seeds.t: Use Route Model Binding for Automatic Model Resolution
seeds.w: Manually fetching models in controllers: $post = Post::findOrFail($id) at the top of every method.
seeds.c: Type-hint the model in the route closure or controller method: Route::get('/posts/{post}', fn(Post $post) => ...). Laravel automatically resolves the {post} wildcard to a Post model by primary key, returning 404 if not found. For custom resolution: Route::get('/posts/{post:slug}…
seeds.s: $model = Model::findOrFail($request->route('id')) in every controller method when type-hinting eliminates it entirely.

seeds.t: Use cache()->remember() for Lazy Cache Population
seeds.w: Manually checking cache, computing if missing, then storing — three separate cache calls with a race condition window.
seeds.c: Use cache()->remember('key', $ttl, fn() => expensiveComputation()). The closure is only called on a cache miss; the result is automatically stored. Use rememberForever for data that only changes on explicit invalidation. Use cache()->tags(['users', 'posts'])->remember() for tag-…
seeds.s: cache-aside pattern manually written in multiple places with three lines that remember() handles in one.

seeds.t: Use Model Factories for Expressive Test Data Creation
seeds.w: Manually creating test data with Model::create(['field' => 'value', ...]) in every test — verbose, duplicated, and breaks when model requirements change.
seeds.c: Define factories in database/factories: $this->faker->name, $this->faker->email. In tests: User::factory()->create() for persisted models, User::factory()->make() for non-persisted, User::factory()->count(10)->create(). Override specific fields: User::factory()->create(['role' =…
seeds.s: 20-line test setups creating related models that factory chaining handles in 3 lines.

seeds.t: Use Broadcasting for Real-Time Event Delivery
seeds.w: Polling an API endpoint every few seconds from the frontend to check for updates — wasteful and slow.
seeds.c: Implement ShouldBroadcast on events: class OrderShipped implements ShouldBroadcast { public function broadcastOn(): Channel { return new Channel('orders'); } }. On the frontend, use Laravel Echo with Pusher or Soketi: Echo.channel('orders').listen('OrderShipped', callback). Use …
seeds.s: frontend polling every 2 seconds for order status updates that websocket broadcasting would push immediately.

seeds.t: Use Sanctum for SPA and Mobile API Authentication
seeds.w: Implementing custom JWT middleware or using Passport for simple SPA authentication — Passport adds OAuth complexity that most SPAs don't need.
seeds.c: Use Laravel Sanctum for SPA authentication (cookie-based session) and API tokens. For SPAs on the same domain: use stateful session authentication with CSRF tokens. For mobile/external clients: use Sanctum personal access tokens. Middleware: auth:sanctum on protected routes. Tok…
seeds.s: full OAuth server setup for an internal SPA that Sanctum's cookie-based auth handles with two lines of config.

seeds.t: Configure Rate Limiting With Named Limiters
seeds.w: Using throttle:60,1 middleware everywhere with the same fixed limit — can't customise per user type or route group.
seeds.c: Define named limiters in RouteServiceProvider: RateLimiter::for('api', fn(Request $r) => $r->user()?->isPremium() ? Limit::none() : Limit::perMinute(60)). Apply with middleware: ['throttle:api']. Return custom responses with Limit::perMinute(30)->response(fn() => response('Too m…
seeds.s: premium API users hitting the same rate limit as free users because limits are not user-aware.

seeds.t: Use Job Batching for Coordinated Parallel Processing
seeds.w: Dispatching many independent jobs and polling a flag in a separate job to check if all completed — manual coordination that's fragile and race-prone.
seeds.c: Use Bus::batch([new ProcessPart(1), new ProcessPart(2)])->then(fn(Batch $b) => finalise($b))->catch(fn($b, $e) => handleFail($b, $e))->finally(fn($b) => cleanup($b))->dispatch(). The then callback fires when all jobs in the batch complete successfully. Monitor progress with $bat…
seeds.s: complex flag-polling logic to detect when 100 parallel import jobs all finish, replaced by batch->then().

seeds.t: Use Blade Components for Reusable UI Fragments
seeds.w: Duplicating Blade template fragments across views with @include and relying on string-based template partials for all UI reuse.
seeds.c: Create class-based Blade components: php artisan make:component Button. Define props with public properties on the component class. Use in Blade: <x-button variant='primary' :label='$text'>. Use slots for flexible content: {{ $slot }}, {{ $header }}. Use anonymous components (re…
seeds.s: @include('partials.button', ['text' => 'Submit']) everywhere when <x-button> with typed props would provide better IDE support and validation.

seeds.t: Use Model Observers for Cross-Cutting Model Lifecycle Logic
seeds.w: Overriding boot() on models to add static::created, static::updated hooks — scattered across many model files, hard to find, and can't be disabled for seeding.
seeds.c: Create observers: php artisan make:observer UserObserver --model=User. Implement creating, created, updating, updated, deleting, deleted methods. Register in AppServiceProvider or via ObservedBy attribute. Temporarily disable with User::withoutObservers(fn() => ...) for imports/…
seeds.s: model boot() methods in 10 models with overlapping side-effect logic that observers would centralise per domain event.

seeds.t: Select Only Needed Columns to Reduce Memory and Query Cost
seeds.w: Using Post::all() or Post::where(...)->get() retrieving all columns including large text, JSON blobs, and binary fields when only id and title are displayed.
seeds.c: Use select(): Post::select('id', 'title', 'created_at')->where(...)->get(). For relationships: Post::with(['author:id,name'])->select('id', 'title', 'user_id'). Always include foreign keys when selecting with relationships. Use ->pluck('name') for a single column.
seeds.s: paginated list query returning full post body text (potentially MBs per row) when only titles are displayed.

seeds.t: Use Artisan Commands for Business Logic CLI Tasks
seeds.w: Writing standalone PHP scripts with require __DIR__.'/vendor/autoload.php' for maintenance tasks — no dependency injection, no scheduling integration, inconsistent error handling.
seeds.c: Create Artisan commands: php artisan make:command ImportProducts. Commands have access to the full service container, logging, and progress bars. Schedule in Console/Kernel.php: $schedule->command('import:products')->dailyAt('03:00')->emailOutputOnFailure($email). Use $this->lin…
seeds.s: cron job running a standalone PHP file that has to manually boot Laravel and handle its own error reporting.

seeds.t: Use chunk or lazy for Processing Large Eloquent Results
seeds.w: User::all()->each(fn($u) => process($u)) to process millions of users — loads all into memory at once, causing OOM.
seeds.c: Use User::chunk(1000, fn($users) => $users->each(fn($u) => process($u))) to process 1000 at a time. Use User::lazy() (PHP generators) for cleaner syntax: foreach (User::lazy() as $user) { process($user); }. Use chunkById() instead of chunk() when modifying records during iterati…
seeds.s: memory limit exceeded processing a large table that chunk() or lazy() would handle with constant memory.

seeds.t: Legal: Contractor Work Not Covered by IP Assignment Clause
seeds.w: Hiring a contractor without a written IP assignment agreement — under copyright law, work made by a contractor is owned by the contractor, not the client.
seeds.c: Include an IP assignment clause in all contractor agreements: 'Contractor assigns all IP created under this agreement to Client.' Sign before work begins. This is separate from an NDA.
seeds.s: Contractor completes the product, relationship sours, contractor claims ownership of the codebase.

seeds.t: Legal: Verbal Agreement Overriding Written Contract
seeds.w: Agreeing verbally to modify written contract terms — most written contracts include 'no oral modification' clauses; verbal changes may be unenforceable.
seeds.c: All modifications to written contracts should be in writing and signed by both parties. Send email confirming any verbal agreement. Use change orders or contract amendments.
seeds.s: Client verbally agrees to expand scope; later claims original scope applies; contractor did uncompensated work.

seeds.t: Legal: No Limitation of Liability Clause in SaaS Agreement
seeds.w: Offering software services without a limitation of liability clause — in the event of an outage, the provider may be liable for unlimited consequential damages.
seeds.c: Include a limitation of liability: cap at fees paid in the last 12 months or a fixed amount. Exclude consequential, incidental, and punitive damages. Have reviewed by a lawyer.
seeds.s: SaaS outage causes customer business loss; customer sues for $5M in lost revenue; contract has no liability cap.

seeds.t: Legal: Non-Compete So Broad It's Unenforceable — But Acting As If It Is
seeds.w: Signing or enforcing a non-compete with unlimited geographic scope, unlimited duration, or industry-wide restriction — often void, but employees act as if bound.
seeds.c: Non-compete enforceability varies dramatically by jurisdiction (California bans them almost entirely). Consult a lawyer before assuming you're restricted by any non-compete.
seeds.s: Developer avoids founding a startup for 2 years based on a non-compete that would have been void in their state.

seeds.t: Legal: SaaS Contract Missing Data Breach Notification Requirements
seeds.w: Handling customer data without contractual obligation to notify customers of data breaches within a specified timeframe — GDPR requires 72-hour notification.
seeds.c: Include breach notification requirements in your DPA: notification timeline (typically 72 hours), what must be reported, and who the contact is. Required for GDPR compliance.
seeds.s: Data breach occurs; company notifies customers 3 weeks later; customers file GDPR complaints; regulatory fines.

seeds.t: Legal: Equity Terms Accepted Without Negotiation
seeds.w: Accepting a job offer with equity compensation without negotiating vesting schedule, cliff, exercise window, and preference stack.
seeds.c: Before accepting: negotiate the cliff (≤1 year), vesting period (4 years is standard), post-termination exercise window (aim for ≥5 years, not 90 days), and understand the liquidation preference stack.
seeds.s: Engineer exercises options within 90-day window at termination; options expire before any liquidity event; others with 5-year windows profit.

seeds.t: Legal: Business Conducted Through Personal Accounts
seeds.w: Using personal bank accounts or email for business activity in an LLC — 'pierces the corporate veil,' potentially making you personally liable for business debts.
seeds.c: Maintain strict separation: business bank account, email, credit card, contracts signed in the company's name. Document this separation especially when you're the sole owner.
seeds.s: Business sued; plaintiff argues LLC is alter ego because personal and business finances were commingled; owner held personally liable.

seeds.t: Legal: GPL Code Included in Proprietary Product
seeds.w: Incorporating GPL-licensed code into a proprietary software product — GPL requires the entire combined work to be released under GPL.
seeds.c: Audit all dependencies for license compatibility before use. Tools: FOSSA, License Finder. Understand GPL vs LGPL vs MIT vs Apache distinctions. Prefer MIT/Apache for commercial flexibility.
seeds.s: Company ships product with GPL library; receives demand letter requiring open-sourcing the entire product.

seeds.t: Sanitise user input before inserting into prompts
seeds.w: directly interpolating user-provided text into a system prompt — users can inject instructions that override your intended behaviour.
seeds.c: clearly delimit user content with XML tags or explicit markers, instruct the model to treat delimited content as data not instructions. Never insert raw user input directly into privileged instruction positions.
seeds.s: model follows user instructions that override your system prompt.

seeds.t: Temperature 0 is not fully deterministic across API versions
seeds.w: assuming temperature=0 guarantees identical outputs — providers make infrastructure changes that affect outputs even at temperature 0.
seeds.c: do not rely on exact string matching for LLM outputs in tests. Test for structural correctness (valid JSON, expected fields present). Pin the model version to reduce variation.
seeds.s: tests fail intermittently despite temperature=0.

seeds.t: Request JSON output explicitly and validate the response
seeds.w: asking for structured data in natural language and parsing with regex — models produce inconsistent formatting.
seeds.c: instruct the model to respond only in JSON, specify the exact schema, and validate with a schema library. Many providers offer native structured output or function calling that enforces schemas at the API level.
seeds.s: JSON parsing errors, missing fields, or unexpected text surrounding the JSON.

seeds.t: Implement exponential backoff for rate limit errors
seeds.w: retrying immediately in a tight loop.
seeds.c: implement exponential backoff with jitter: wait 1s, 2s, 4s, up to a maximum. Use the Retry-After header if provided. Set a maximum retry count.
seeds.s: cascade of 429 errors, or requests that never complete during traffic spikes.

seeds.t: Buffer streaming responses before parsing structured data
seeds.w: attempting to parse JSON from a streaming response chunk by chunk — individual chunks are partial and not valid JSON.
seeds.c: accumulate all streaming chunks into a complete string before parsing. For UI responsiveness, stream text updates to the user while buffering separately for downstream processing.
seeds.s: JSON parse error on streaming chunks.

seeds.t: LLMs have no memory between API calls — send full history each time
seeds.w: assuming the model remembers previous API calls — each request is stateless.
seeds.c: maintain conversation history as an array of message objects and send the full array with every request. Implement a history management strategy (sliding window, summarisation) to stay within token limits.
seeds.s: model appears to forget context, ignores earlier messages.

seeds.t: AI task trackers may mark stub code as complete — verify actual implementation
seeds.w: treating a checked task as evidence of working functionality — the tool marks completion when code exists, not when it functions correctly.
seeds.c: verify each completed task by running the code, checking for placeholder returns, and confirming actual side effects (database writes, API calls, file creation). A function returning a string literal is not an implementation.
seeds.s: 100% task completion but no working features; stub functions returning 'endpoint name' strings.

seeds.t: Overly long system prompts reduce instruction-following accuracy
seeds.w: stuffing every rule, edge case, and example into the system prompt — models attend less reliably to instructions buried in very long prompts ('lost in the middle' effect).
seeds.c: keep system prompts focused on the most critical constraints. Put examples in the user turn if needed. Use structured formatting (numbered lists, headers) so key instructions are scannable. Test instruction-following explicitly when prompts exceed ~2000 tokens.
seeds.s: model ignores specific instructions that appear deep in a long system prompt.

seeds.t: Pin model versions in production — alias versions change behaviour
seeds.w: using alias versions like 'gpt-4o', 'claude-sonnet-latest' in production — providers update these aliases to point to newer model versions which may behave differently.
seeds.c: pin to a specific dated version string in production code. Test new versions explicitly before upgrading. Track model version in logs alongside outputs for debugging.
seeds.s: production behaviour changes without any code changes after a provider silently upgrades the alias.

seeds.t: Estimate costs before deploying — token pricing multiplies quickly
seeds.w: deploying LLM-powered features without cost modelling — a feature that costs $0.001 per call at 10,000 calls/day is $300/month, which surprises many teams.
seeds.c: calculate cost per call (input tokens × input price + output tokens × output price), multiply by expected call volume, and set up billing alerts. Use cheaper/faster models for high-volume low-complexity tasks. Cache responses for identical or near-identical inputs.
seeds.s: unexpected API bill at end of month.

seeds.t: Batch independent LLM calls in parallel, not sequentially
seeds.w: making multiple independent LLM API calls sequentially in a loop — total latency is the sum of all calls.
seeds.c: when calls are independent (e.g. summarising 10 documents), use Promise.all(), asyncio.gather(), or equivalent to fire them concurrently. Check rate limits — stay within requests-per-minute limits by chunking batches with a small delay.
seeds.s: feature is slow because 5 independent calls run sequentially, taking 5× longer than necessary.

seeds.t: Never trust LLM output directly in code execution or database writes
seeds.w: executing LLM-generated code, SQL queries, or shell commands without validation — even with safe-seeming prompts, models can generate malicious or incorrect operations.
seeds.c: parse and validate LLM output before use. For code generation: run in a sandbox. For SQL: use parameterised queries, never string interpolation. For structured data: validate schema before writing to a database.
seeds.s: SQL injection via LLM-generated queries, or code execution exploits.

seeds.t: Few-shot examples dramatically improve consistency — include them
seeds.w: relying on instructions alone for complex formatting or classification tasks — models follow examples more reliably than instructions.
seeds.c: include 2-5 examples of input→output pairs in the prompt. Examples should cover edge cases and the full range of expected outputs. For classification: include examples of each category. Format examples exactly as you want the output.
seeds.s: model understands the task but formats output inconsistently.

seeds.t: Ask the model to reason before answering for complex tasks
seeds.w: asking for a direct answer to a complex multi-step problem — the model answers from pattern matching without reasoning, producing errors.
seeds.c: ask the model to reason step-by-step before giving the final answer: 'Think through this step by step, then give your answer'. For structured output, separate the reasoning step: first prompt for reasoning in plain text, then prompt for the structured answer given the reasoning.
seeds.s: model gives wrong answers to problems it could solve correctly with explicit reasoning.

seeds.t: RAG chunk size affects both retrieval quality and context fit
seeds.w: using very large chunks (entire documents) — retrieval becomes imprecise and chunks waste context. WRONG: very small chunks (single sentences) — lose surrounding context needed for coherent answers.
seeds.c: chunk at natural semantic boundaries (paragraphs, sections) around 200-500 tokens. Include chunk overlap (10-20%) to avoid splitting context across boundaries. Test retrieval quality with representative questions.
seeds.s: retrieved chunks miss the relevant information, or answers lack context.

seeds.t: Always handle LLM API failures gracefully with fallbacks
seeds.w: treating LLM API calls as infallible — providers have outages, rate limits, and timeouts.
seeds.c: implement graceful degradation: catch API errors, serve cached responses where possible, fall back to rule-based logic for critical paths, and show appropriate user messaging. Use circuit breakers for high-traffic services. Set timeouts shorter than the API's own timeout.
seeds.s: entire feature unavailable during provider outages, or requests hang indefinitely.

seeds.t: Resize images before sending to vision APIs — size affects cost and latency
seeds.w: sending full-resolution images to vision APIs (8MP+ photos) — maximum detail mode on some providers costs significantly more and has much higher latency.
seeds.c: resize images to the minimum resolution that preserves necessary detail. Most vision tasks work well at 512-1024px on the longer edge. Check provider documentation for optimal sizes and pricing tiers (e.g. Anthropic's auto vs. exact pixel pricing).
seeds.s: vision API calls are slow and expensive with large images.

seeds.t: Build an eval set before deploying — do not rely on manual testing
seeds.w: testing LLM features manually with a few examples and calling it done — edge cases emerge in production and regressions are invisible when prompts change.
seeds.c: build a small eval set (50-200 representative inputs with expected outputs) before launch. Run evals on every prompt change. Use LLM-as-judge for open-ended outputs. Track metrics over time.
seeds.s: prompt change improves one case and silently breaks three others, discovered only via user complaints.

seeds.t: Every seed needs WRONG, CORRECT, Symptom
seeds.w: Writing chunk content as free prose without the three required section markers.
seeds.c: Always include WRONG:, CORRECT:, and Symptom: markers. The Symptom line is the primary retrieval hook for the index.
seeds.s: seed never surfaces when the error occurs; no matches in lookup.

seeds.t: Seed IDs must be snake_case
seeds.w: Using camelCase, PascalCase, or numeric-prefixed IDs.
seeds.c: IDs must be snake_case: all lowercase, underscores only, start with a letter.
seeds.s: seed fails to load; lint error "ID must be snake_case".

seeds.t: Symptom is the primary search key
seeds.w: Writing a vague or summary-level Symptom line.
seeds.c: Write the Symptom as the exact error message or specific observable failure a developer would paste into a search engine.
seeds.s: seed never surfaces when the error occurs; the model cannot find it by symptom lookup.

seeds.t: Verify your Lodestone is complete before each session
seeds.w: assuming the output is complete because the packaging or deploy step exited 0
seeds.c: run a manifest check against a known-good snapshot before every significant handoff or deploy
seeds.s: session behaves differently from last time despite no apparent changes; recipient reports missing patterns.

seeds.t: Capture bug fixes while they're fresh
seeds.w: solving a bug and moving on without saving the pattern — the next time the same bug appears you start from scratch.
seeds.c: immediately after confirming a fix works, call the capture_fix tool (or use the Lodestone interface) to save the WRONG pattern, CORRECT fix, and exact error Symptom. Your Lodestone grows by capturing what you actually encounter, not by guessing what you might encounter.
seeds.s: solving the same bug a second time with no memory of the first fix.

seeds.t: Seeds that work for you may not work for others
seeds.w: sharing a seed that references your project-specific variable names, internal APIs, or domain conventions as if it were general advice.
seeds.c: seeds shared with other Lodestones must use official API names and describe patterns that apply regardless of project. Personal project patterns belong in your personal seedbank (.distill/personal-patterns.json), not in shared seeds.
seeds.s: shared seed causes confusion because CORRECT references something the recipient has never heard of.

seeds.t: Capture fix then lint and review
seeds.w: capturing a fix and leaving it unreviewed.
seeds.c: after capture_fix, run lint-seeds to validate structure, then optionally contribute.
seeds.s: personal patterns accumulate without quality checks; same structural mistakes repeated across seeds.

seeds.t: Check session health before starting
seeds.w: starting a session without checking if seeds are valid, complete, or drifted.
seeds.c: verify completeness, lint, check drift, show last session, then run preflight.
seeds.s: session uses stale or invalid seeds; preflight injects wrong context.

seeds.t: Bulk graft seeds from a pack file
seeds.w: manually pasting seed JSON into stack files.
seeds.c: use the bulk graft workflow to validate, deduplicate, and merge a pack file cleanly.
seeds.s: duplicate IDs, malformed JSON, missing stack field after manual paste.

seeds.t: Detect stacks from workspace files
seeds.w: guessing which stacks to load before a session.
seeds.c: run active_stacks to auto-detect languages and frameworks from file extensions, import statements, and config files.
seeds.s: irrelevant seeds injected, relevant ones missed, wasted context window.

seeds.t: Summarise what this session injected and why
seeds.w: starting a continuation session with no memory of what was injected last time.
seeds.c: call session_brief to get a plain-English summary of injection decisions before continuing.
seeds.s: model re-explains patterns already covered; duplicate context injected.

seeds.t: Score a seed draft for quality before saving
seeds.w: saving a seed without checking if the symptom is specific enough to be retrievable.
seeds.c: run seed_quality before saving to get a rubric score and actionable improvement suggestions.
seeds.s: seeds never surface during lookup; vague symptoms cause zero matches.

seeds.t: Generate a stack-specific SKILL.md
seeds.w: generating a flat SKILL.md with all seeds at equal priority.
seeds.c: group by blast_radius (critical → high → medium → low), show explicit WRONG/CORRECT/Symptom blocks for high-blast seeds, include a symptom token index.
seeds.s: model treats a cosmetic warning the same as a data-loss pattern; misses the most important seeds.

seeds.t: Run npm run build after every seed change
seeds.w: Committing seed changes without rebuilding the derived outputs.
seeds.c: Always run 'npm run build' after changing any seed file.
seeds.s: symptom-index.json references stale chunk IDs; a seed has no .txt file; llms.txt shows wrong chunk counts.

seeds.t: Stack field must exactly match the filename
seeds.w: Using a different value in the stack field than the filename without .json.
seeds.c: The stack field must exactly match the filename: seeds/react.json means stack: 'react' in every chunk.
seeds.s: lint-seeds.mjs: 'stack is typescript but filename is javascript'.

seeds.t: Universal seed is for cross-language patterns only
seeds.w: Adding language-specific chunks to universal.json.
seeds.c: Only put truly cross-language patterns in universal.json. Stack-specific ones go in their own seed file.
seeds.s: misleading universal chunk injected for stacks where it is irrelevant or incorrect.

seeds.t: Use contribute.mjs to avoid duplicate chunks
seeds.w: Manually editing seed JSON files without checking for near-duplicate chunks.
seeds.c: Use 'npm run contribute' for guided contribution — it checks for near-duplicates before writing.
seeds.s: seed grows with near-identical chunks; PR rejected as redundant.

seeds.t: Seeds index and project manifest are different APIs
seeds.w: Querying api/manifest.json expecting to find seed stacks.
seeds.c: Use api/seeds/index.json to discover seed stacks; use api/manifest.json to find user-published project contexts.
seeds.s: 'no stacks found' when querying manifest.json for seed data.

seeds.t: Use --strict for validating new contributions
seeds.w: Running only 'npm run lint' before a PR instead of --strict mode.
seeds.c: Run 'npm run lint:strict' before any PR — strict mode enforces all quality rules, not just hard errors.
seeds.s: PR passes CI but reviewer requests style changes that --strict would have caught.

seeds.t: Never hand-edit generated .txt seed files
seeds.w: Manually editing .txt files in seeds/ — they are generated output, not source files.
seeds.c: Edit the .json source and run 'npm run build' to regenerate all .txt files.
