seeds.s: manual .txt edits disappear after the next deploy; content drifts between .json and .txt.

seeds.t: Confidence Feedback Loop Is a Closed Knot Not a Linear Pipeline
seeds.w: Waiting for more sessions to resolve a seed that repeatedly crosses the confidence threshold in both directions
seeds.c: Count threshold crossings (crossing number); if > 3, the trajectory is knotted — split the seed by the dimension along which it crosses (session depth, stack, domain)
seeds.s: seed oscillates repeatedly around the confidence threshold despite 15+ sessions; latent heat buffer shows no consistent direction; outcome-tracker --report shows sensitivity='falling' and 'rising' alternating.

seeds.t: Two-Phase Seed Obeys Gibbs Phase Rule — Split It, Don't Drain It
seeds.w: Waiting for more sessions when confidence is stuck near 0.5 with high clean_short and clean_long counts
seeds.c: Detect two-phase state via outcome-tracker --report; use split_two_phase_seed to create surface and deep variants
seeds.s: seed shows clean_short ≥ 4 and clean_long ≥ 4, both phases substantial; effective_confidence stuck within ±0.08 of 0.5 regardless of session count.

seeds.t: Extraneous Attractor Seeds Have High Confidence and Are Wrong
seeds.w: Treating high confidence as proof of quality without checking follow-on capture rate
seeds.c: Check extraneous_attractor flag in outcome-tracker --report; refine CORRECT to address root cause if flagged
seeds.s: developer records clean session, then opens another session in the same stack two days later for a related problem; follow-on capture rate > 40%.

seeds.t: Borromean Seed Triads Require Group-Level Relationships
seeds.w: Only running pairwise co-occurrence analysis; never checking for 3-way Borromean structure
seeds.c: Run npm run borromean after 10+ sessions; for score > 2.0 triads, mint a parent seed with mint_cascade_parent
seeds.s: compute-clusters.mjs shows no pairwise clusters for three seeds that frequently appear together in sessions; session outcome reports show all three consistently cited but no pairwise relationship exists.

seeds.t: harvest_docs Works for Any Domain With Documented Mistakes
seeds.w: Only passing code documentation URLs to harvest_docs
seeds.c: Pass --domain cooking|writing|legal|finance for any domain with documented mistakes; the extraction is domain-calibrated
seeds.s: seed corpus only covers code stacks despite the team using Lodestone for non-code knowledge management; harvest_docs has only ever been given code documentation URLs.

seeds.t: The Lodestone Mathematical Framework Closes Through Westinghouse
seeds.w: Treating the mathematical framework as decorative rather than structural — not reading docs/mathematical-framework.md
seeds.c: Read docs/mathematical-framework.md to understand why each system behaviour follows from mathematical necessity, not arbitrary design
seeds.s: using system features without understanding why they behave the way they do (why hysteresis at the drain threshold, why Aberth repulsion for diversity, why personal seeds get their own slot).

seeds.t: seed.stack From a Remote Henge Must Be Validated Before Use in a File Path
seeds.w: const fp = path.join(SEEDS_DIR, `${seed.stack}.json`) where seed is from a remote Henge A Henge under attacker control can serve seeds with stack: '../../../home/user/.ssh/authorized_keys' or stack: '../../../etc/cron.d/backdoor'. path.join resolves these traversal sequences. Th…
seeds.c: Validate stack name before any path operation: const SAFE_STACK = /^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$/; const stack = SAFE_STACK.test(seed.stack) ? seed.stack : 'universal'; Apply this check at every point where seed.stack from external data (Henge, vault pull, imported bundle) enters a path.join…
seeds.s: auto-seed.mjs, graftExternal, or vault_promote writes a seed file to an unexpected location; a community Henge causes file creation outside the seeds/ directory.

seeds.t: Filenames From GitHub API Directory Listings May Contain Path Traversal Sequences
seeds.w: for (const file of await ghListDir(token, owner, repo, 'vault')) { fs.writeFileSync(path.join(VAULT_DIR, file.name), content) } The GitHub contents API returns filenames from the remote repository. A vault repo under attacker control can contain a file named '../../.ssh/authoriz…
seeds.c: Validate file.name before path.join: const SAFE = /^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+\.json$/; for (const file of files.filter(f => f.name.endsWith('.json'))) { if (!SAFE.test(file.name)) { log.push(`Skipped unsafe: ${file.name}`); continue; } fs.writeFileSync(path.join(VAULT_DIR, file.name), cont…
seeds.s: vault_pull writes a file to a path outside .lodestone/vault/; a sync operation from a malicious vault repo creates or overwrites files in sensitive directories.

seeds.t: Lua Arrays Start at Index 1, Not 0
seeds.w: Starting loops at 0 or storing the first element at index 0 expecting standard array behaviour — Lua tables have no inherent start index, but convention and all standard library functions use 1-based indexing.
seeds.c: Start arrays at index 1: t = {10, 20, 30}; t[1] == 10. Use for i = 1, #t do for iteration. The ipairs() iterator also starts at 1 and stops at the first nil. Tables with a value at index 0 are valid but excluded from # and ipairs.
seeds.s: off-by-one errors and ipairs skipping elements when migrating C or Python indexing conventions to Lua.

seeds.t: Always Declare Variables With local
seeds.w: Assigning x = 10 without local — in Lua, unqualified assignments create or modify global variables, polluting the global environment and causing hard-to-trace bugs in large programs.
seeds.c: Declare every variable with local: local x = 10. Local variables are faster to access (VM uses registers vs hash lookup) and scoped to their block. Use local function f() ... end for local functions. Enable luac -l or use a linter (luacheck) to detect accidental globals.
seeds.s: variable unexpectedly modified from a different scope, or module-level state leaking between calls.

seeds.t: Only nil and false Are Falsy — 0 and Empty String Are Truthy
seeds.w: Writing if not count then expecting it to be true when count is 0 — in Lua, 0 is truthy; only nil and false are falsy.
seeds.c: Explicitly check for nil: if count == nil then. For zero checks: if count == 0 then. This is different from JavaScript and Python where 0 and '' are falsy. The pattern if x then is only reliable when x is either a boolean or a value that is nil when absent.
seeds.s: conditional failing to trigger for 0 or empty string values that should be treated as 'unset'.

seeds.t: Tables Serve as Arrays, Dicts, Objects, and Namespaces
seeds.w: Looking for separate array, dictionary, or set types — Lua has only one data structure, the table, which serves all of these roles.
seeds.c: Use tables for arrays ({1, 2, 3}), dictionaries ({name='Alice', age=30}), sets ({[value]=true}), objects (with metatables), and modules (returned tables). Mix integer and string keys freely. Understand that a Lua table has both an 'array part' (integer keys from 1) and a 'hash p…
seeds.s: searching for a list or dict type in Lua documentation when tables already provide all needed functionality.

seeds.t: Use Colon Syntax for Methods to Pass self Automatically
seeds.w: Defining a method with a dot (function Obj.method(self) ... end) and calling it with a colon (obj:method()) — the colon syntax passes the object as the first argument; mixing the two causes wrong self references.
seeds.c: Define methods with colon syntax (function Obj:method() ... end) which implicitly adds a self parameter. Call them with colon syntax (obj:method()). Use dot syntax only for functions that don't need self. Obj:method() is syntactic sugar for Obj.method(Obj).
seeds.s: 'attempt to index a nil value (local self)' because method was called with the wrong syntax.

seeds.t: The # Operator Is Undefined for Tables With Holes
seeds.w: Using # on a table with nil values in the middle (a 'sequence with holes') expecting it to return the last non-nil index — the result is undefined per the Lua reference manual.
seeds.c: Only use # on proper sequences (integer keys from 1 to n with no gaps). To track the actual count of sparse tables, maintain a separate count variable. Use table.pack() which stores n in the resulting table. For safe length of sparse arrays, iterate and count manually.
seeds.s: #t returning 3 for a table with 5 elements where elements 2 and 4 are nil.

seeds.t: Use pcall and xpcall for Protected Function Calls
seeds.w: Calling functions that may error without protection — an uncaught Lua error propagates up and terminates the coroutine or the whole program.
seeds.c: Use ok, result = pcall(func, arg1, arg2) to call func in protected mode; ok is false and result is the error message if an error occurs. Use xpcall(func, handler, ...) to specify a message handler (useful for adding stack traces). For OOP patterns, use pcall(obj.method, obj, arg…
seeds.s: one erroring function call crashing the entire Lua state in a game or embedded script.

seeds.t: Implement OOP With Metatables and __index
seeds.w: Duplicating methods in every object table instead of sharing them through prototypal inheritance.
seeds.c: Create a class table, set its __index to itself, and use it as the metatable for instances: Animal = {}; Animal.__index = Animal; function Animal.new(name) return setmetatable({name=name}, Animal) end; function Animal:speak() print(self.name) end. Each instance delegates method …
seeds.s: every object having its own copy of every method, wasting memory in games with many instances.

seeds.t: Build Strings With table.concat, Not .. in Loops
seeds.w: Concatenating strings with .. inside a loop: result = result .. part — each concatenation allocates a new string, making string building O(n²) in the number of parts.
seeds.c: Collect parts in a table and join once: local parts = {}; for ...: parts[#parts+1] = part; end; local result = table.concat(parts). This is O(n) and is the idiomatic Lua pattern for string building.
seeds.s: string building function noticeably slow for large numbers of parts.

seeds.t: Lua Functions Can Return Multiple Values Natively
seeds.w: Returning a table to pass multiple values from a function — adds a table allocation and access overhead.
seeds.c: Return multiple values directly: return x, y, z. Receive them with local a, b, c = myFunc(). Extra return values are silently discarded; missing ones become nil. This is used pervasively in Lua: string.find returns start, end positions; pcall returns ok, result. Wrap in parenthe…
seeds.s: needlessly returning a single-use table when multiple return values would be simpler.

seeds.t: require Caches Modules — It Does Not Reload on Re-Call
seeds.w: Calling require('module') in a loop or multiple times expecting it to reload the module on each call — require caches modules in package.loaded after the first call and returns the cached value on subsequent calls.
seeds.c: Call require once and store the result: local mymod = require('mymod'). If you genuinely need to reload a module (rare, usually only in development tools), set package.loaded['mymod'] = nil before the next require.
seeds.s: module 'reload' code not working because require returns the cached version.

seeds.t: ipairs Stops at the First nil; pairs Visits All Keys
seeds.w: Using ipairs to iterate a mixed or sparse table expecting all keys to be visited — ipairs stops at the first integer key for which the value is nil.
seeds.c: Use ipairs for sequences (arrays from 1 to n with no gaps). Use pairs for tables with arbitrary string or mixed keys. For a table with keys 1, 2, nil, 4: ipairs stops at 2; pairs visits 1, 2, and 4. The iteration order of pairs is not guaranteed for non-integer keys.
seeds.s: ipairs loop not visiting elements after a gap in a sparse array.

seeds.t: Coroutines Enable Cooperative Multitasking in Lua
seeds.w: Using busy-wait loops or global state to simulate async operations in Lua — blocks other code from running.
seeds.c: Create coroutines with coroutine.create(fn) or coroutine.wrap(fn). Resume with coroutine.resume(co, val); the coroutine runs until coroutine.yield(val). Coroutines are cooperative — only one runs at a time. Used extensively in game scripting (Roblox, LÖVE) for sequencing events …
seeds.s: using complex callback chains for sequential async game logic that coroutines express linearly.

seeds.t: Return a Table From Modules for a Clean Public API
seeds.w: Setting globals inside a module file (MyModule = {}) instead of using the standard module pattern — pollutes the global namespace and creates implicit dependencies.
seeds.c: Define a local table at the top, add functions to it, and return it: local M = {}; function M.greet(name) ... end; return M. The caller stores the return value: local MyModule = require('mymodule'). This keeps the module's internals private and the public API explicit.
seeds.s: modules polluting _G (the global table) with their internal functions and state.

seeds.t: Lua Uses Its Own Pattern Language, Not POSIX Regex
seeds.w: Writing POSIX regular expressions with \d, \w, \s, |, +? etc. in string.match or string.find — Lua patterns are not POSIX regex; \d is not valid (use %d for digits).
seeds.c: Use Lua's pattern language: %d (digit), %a (letter), %w (alphanumeric), %s (space), . (any char). Use * (0+), + (1+), - (0+ lazy), ? (0 or 1). Anchor with ^ and $. Use %b() for balanced matches. For full regex, use the lrexlib binding or PCRE2 via a C extension.
seeds.s: Lua pattern match silently failing or returning wrong results because POSIX regex syntax was used.

seeds.t: Use table.insert and table.remove for Sequence Mutations
seeds.w: Setting t[#t] = nil to remove the last element or t[#t+1] = val to append — direct index assignment works for appending but bypasses Lua's sequence conventions; t[#t] = nil doesn't update the cached length in all Lua versions.
seeds.c: Use table.insert(t, val) to append, table.insert(t, pos, val) to insert at a position, and table.remove(t, pos) (or table.remove(t) for the last element) to remove. These correctly update the sequence and return the removed value.
seeds.s: length operator returning unexpected values after manual nil assignment to remove elements.

seeds.t: Closures Capture Variables by Reference, Not Value
seeds.w: Creating closures in a loop expecting each to capture the loop variable's value at creation time — all closures share the same upvalue variable, which holds the final loop value.
seeds.c: Create a new local in each iteration to force a per-iteration binding: for i = 1, 5 do local j = i; callbacks[i] = function() return j end end. This is the same closure-in-loop problem as JavaScript (var vs let).
seeds.s: all closures created in a loop returning the same value (the last iteration's) instead of each iteration's value.

seeds.t: Lua Does Not Implicitly Coerce Between Strings and Numbers in All Contexts
seeds.w: Expecting '10' + 5 to fail or to produce '105' — Lua coerces strings to numbers in arithmetic (so '10' + 5 == 15), but not in comparisons ('10' ~= 10).
seeds.c: Use tonumber() and tostring() explicitly when converting between types to avoid unexpected coercion. Concatenation with .. requires string or number operands. Comparisons between different types always return false without an error.
seeds.s: '10' == 10 evaluating to false when arithmetic coercion suggested strings and numbers are interchangeable.

seeds.t: Seed math.random Before Use for Non-Deterministic Results
seeds.w: Using math.random() without calling math.randomseed() — the default seed is deterministic (often 1), producing the same sequence on every run.
seeds.c: Call math.randomseed(os.time()) at program startup to seed with the current time. In Lua 5.4, math.random() generates a random seed automatically on first call, making explicit seeding optional. For cryptographic randomness, use an OS-level source.
seeds.s: game producing the same 'random' outcomes every run because the default seed is always 1.

seeds.t: Use Weak Tables to Allow Garbage Collection of Cached Values
seeds.w: Storing references to objects in a cache table indefinitely — the cache prevents garbage collection of objects that are no longer used by the rest of the program, causing memory leaks.
seeds.c: Create a weak-value table with setmetatable(cache, {__mode='v'}); values in the table can be garbage collected when no other strong references exist. Use __mode='k' for weak keys (useful for object-keyed metadata). After GC, accessing a collected weak-value entry returns nil.
seeds.s: game's asset cache growing without bound as levels are loaded and unloaded.

seeds.t: Fit Scalers and Encoders on Training Data Only — Never on the Full Dataset Before Splitting
seeds.w: Fitting preprocessing (StandardScaler, imputer, PCA, tokeniser) on the full dataset before splitting — this leaks test/val distribution into the transformer and produces optimistically biased generalisation estimates.
seeds.c: Split first; fit all transforms on train only; transform (never fit_transform) val/test. Use sklearn Pipeline to enforce this in cross-validation. Every fold must fit its own scaler on that fold's training partition only.
seeds.s: model performs dramatically worse in production than in cross-validation; test-set performance inexplicably close to training-set performance; removing a feature causes no drop despite it being theoretically predictive.

seeds.t: Hyperparameter Tuning Requires a Validation Set Separate From the Test Set
seeds.w: Tuning hyperparameters by evaluating against the test set — each test-set evaluation leaks information; after n iterations the test set has been implicitly overfit and no longer measures generalisation.
seeds.c: Three splits: train (fit), validation (tune/select), test (single final report). Use k-fold CV on train if data is scarce. Nested CV for unbiased estimates. Count test-set evaluations — more than 1 = contamination.
seeds.s: model performs significantly worse on a new held-out sample than reported test performance; test accuracy improves with each architecture iteration even when validation accuracy does not.

seeds.t: Random Splits on Imbalanced Datasets Can Produce Folds With No Minority Class Examples
seeds.w: Using random KFold on imbalanced data — a rare class (e.g., 1% of samples) can be absent from some training folds. Use StratifiedKFold / stratify=y in train_test_split to preserve class ratios in every fold.
seeds.c: StratifiedKFold preserves class ratio per fold. Apply oversampling (SMOTE) only inside training folds, never to val/test. Use pos_weight or class_weight='balanced' for loss weighting. Stratify by bins for regression targets.
seeds.s: cross-validation fold where the minority class appears only in the test fold, producing undefined precision; AUROC of 0.5 on some folds because the model never learned the minority class.

seeds.t: Distance-Based and Gradient-Based Models Require Feature Scaling — Tree-Based Models Do Not
seeds.w: Omitting feature scaling for distance-based or gradient-based models — KNN, SVM-RBF, K-means, neural nets, and regularised linear models are all sensitive to feature magnitude. Tree-based models (RF, XGBoost) are the exception; they do not require scaling.
seeds.c: Scale for: KNN, SVM, K-means, PCA, neural nets, regularised linear models. Do NOT scale for: decision trees, random forests, gradient boosting. Use StandardScaler by default; RobustScaler for outliers; MinMaxScaler for bounded needs.
seeds.s: SVM with RBF kernel that performs like a random classifier because one feature has range [0, 10000] and another has range [0, 1]; Lasso regression that zeroes out all small-valued features regardless of predictive power.

seeds.t: He Initialisation for ReLU, Xavier/Glorot for Tanh/Sigmoid — Wrong Init Causes Vanishing or Exploding Gradients
seeds.w: Using zero initialisation or an unscaled normal distribution for network weights — zeros cause symmetry failure; unscaled variance causes vanishing (too small) or exploding (too large) activations through deep networks.
seeds.c: ReLU → He init: Var(W) = 2/n_in. Tanh/Sigmoid → Xavier: Var(W) = 2/(n_in+n_out). SELU → LeCun: 1/n_in. Biases = 0. GPT-style: scale output projections by 1/√(2L). NaN loss at step 1 = exploding init.
seeds.s: training loss is NaN from step 1 (exploding init); training loss decreases for 0 steps and then plateaus at chance level (vanishing init); all neurons learning the same feature (zero / constant init symmetry failure).

seeds.t: Gradient Clipping by Norm Preserves Direction; Clipping by Value Does Not
seeds.w: Using clip_by_value instead of clip_by_norm — value clipping independently caps each gradient element, changing the update direction. Norm clipping rescales the whole gradient vector, preserving direction while limiting step size.
seeds.c: torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(params, max_norm) — rescales the entire gradient by max_norm/‖∇‖ if ‖∇‖ > max_norm; direction preserved. With AMP: call scaler.unscale_(optimizer) before clipping. Typical max_norm: 1.0 (Transformers), 5.0 (RNNs).
seeds.s: training with clip_by_value where some gradient components are consistently at the clip boundary, causing the effective update direction to be unrelated to the true gradient; AMP training where clip_grad_norm is called before unscale, operating on artificially scaled gradients.

seeds.t: Learning Rate Warmup Is Not the Same as Using a Small Learning Rate — It Prevents Early Instability
seeds.w: Using a uniformly small LR instead of warmup — warmup addresses early-step instability (noisy gradients, uninitialised batch stats, Adam denominator bias) specifically; a globally small LR slows convergence uniformly without solving these problems.
seeds.c: Warmup: LR from ~0 → target over first 4–10% of steps (linear or cosine). Addresses: noisy early gradients, uninitialised BN stats, Adam v_t ≈ 0 at t=0. Use cosine-with-warmup as the default schedule for Transformers.
seeds.s: transformer training diverges in the first 100 steps with target LR but is stable if LR is manually set to 1e-7; model trained without warmup achieves lower final performance than the same model with warmup despite identical total compute.

seeds.t: Early Stopping Monitors Validation Loss — Not Training Loss
seeds.w: Monitoring training loss for early stopping — training loss decreases monotonically and provides no signal about overfitting. Monitor validation loss; stop when it fails to improve for patience N steps; restore the best-validation checkpoint.
seeds.c: Early stopping: monitor val_loss; save best checkpoint; stop after patience steps without improvement; restore best. Typical patience: 5–20 epochs. ReduceLROnPlateau must also monitor val_loss. Deploy the best-validation checkpoint, not the final one.
seeds.s: a training loop that stops after validation loss has been increasing for 50 epochs because training loss was still decreasing; a model deployed at the final epoch that performs worse than the checkpoint from 30 epochs earlier.

seeds.t: Inverted Dropout Scales Activations at Training Time — Not at Inference
seeds.w: Scaling activations by keep_prob at inference time — modern frameworks use inverted dropout (divide by keep_prob during training). model.eval() disables dropout entirely with no additional scaling. Multiplying by p at inference double-scales.
seeds.c: Inverted dropout: train → keep unit with prob p, scale by 1/p. Eval → all units active, no scaling. In PyTorch: model.train() enables dropout; model.eval() disables it. MCDropout: use model.train() at inference deliberately for uncertainty sampling.
seeds.s: model prediction magnitudes at inference are ~p times smaller than expected (forgot model.eval()); stochastic outputs with different values on each forward pass (dropout left active in eval mode); MCDropout that mistakenly uses model.eval() and loses the stochasticity.

seeds.t: Accuracy Is a Misleading Metric for Imbalanced Classification — A Model Predicting Only the Majority Class Can Score 99%
seeds.w: Using accuracy for imbalanced classification — a constant-majority-class predictor scores high accuracy while providing zero utility. Use precision, recall, F1, AUROC, and Average Precision; choose based on the cost of false positives vs false negatives.
seeds.c: Imbalanced: use Precision, Recall, F1, AUROC, AUPRC. AUPRC > AUROC for severe imbalance (< 1% positive). F-beta: β > 1 weights recall; β < 1 weights precision. Always report class support counts. sklearn.metrics.classification_report.
seeds.s: classifier with 99% accuracy on a 1% positive-rate dataset; removing the minority class from training improves accuracy; model has recall of 0.0 for the class of interest.

seeds.t: AUROC Is Optimistic for Severely Imbalanced Classes — Average Precision (AUPRC) Is More Informative
seeds.w: Reporting only AUROC for severely imbalanced tasks — ROC curves are insensitive to class imbalance; a model with AUROC = 0.95 can have near-zero precision at any useful recall level. AUPRC (Average Precision) reflects precision-recall tradeoffs more faithfully.
seeds.c: AUROC: threshold-independent rank metric, insensitive to imbalance. AUPRC: area under PR curve, equals base rate for random classifier. Use AUPRC when positive rate < 10%; essential when < 1%. Report both in publications. sklearn.metrics.average_precision_score.
seeds.s: model reports AUROC = 0.95 on a 0.5% positive-rate dataset, but when deployed, 99% of flagged cases are false positives — AUPRC would have revealed this.

seeds.t: MSE Penalises Large Errors Quadratically; MAE Is Linear — The Choice Determines What the Model Learns to Minimise
seeds.w: Defaulting to MSE without considering target distribution — MSE trains the model to predict the conditional mean and is sensitive to outliers. MAE trains to the conditional median and is outlier-robust. Huber and quantile loss offer intermediate options.
seeds.c: MSE → conditional mean (sensitive to outliers). MAE → conditional median (robust). Huber: quadratic near 0, linear beyond δ. Quantile loss L_τ → τ-th conditional quantile. Choose based on whether outliers are signal or noise and what quantity the business needs.
seeds.s: a price prediction model trained with MSE that systematically overpredicts median prices because of rare high-value transactions; a demand-forecasting model where MAE dramatically outperforms MSE on business metrics because the target distribution is heavy-tailed.

seeds.t: Residual Connections Allow Gradients to Flow Directly Through the Network — They Are Not Merely Adding the Input
seeds.w: Treating skip connections as a minor addition — residual connections create a gradient highway: ∂L/∂x = ∂L/∂H × (∂F/∂x + I). The identity term I allows gradients to bypass saturated layers entirely, enabling training of networks with 100+ layers.
seeds.c: H(x) = F(x) + x. Gradient: ∂L/∂x = ∂L/∂H × (I + ∂F/∂x). Identity term I = gradient highway, bypassing any vanishing in F. Dimension mismatch on skip path needs a projection. Remove skip connections from a Transformer → immediate divergence.
seeds.s: a deep network without skip connections where only the last few layers learn anything detectable; gradient norms that are 10⁻⁸ in early layers but 10⁰ in the final layer.

seeds.t: LayerNorm Normalises Over Features per Sample; BatchNorm Normalises Over the Batch — They Have Different Failure Modes
seeds.w: Using BatchNorm in a Transformer or any architecture where batch size is small or variable — BN requires large stable batches. LayerNorm normalises per-sample over features, works with any batch size, and is the correct default for sequence models.
seeds.c: BN: normalise over batch + spatial dims per channel; needs large batch; fails at B=1; has train/eval mode. LN: normalise over feature dims per token; batch-size independent; no running stats; standard for Transformers. GroupNorm: small-batch vision tasks.
seeds.s: Transformer trained with BatchNorm where batch size varies by sequence length — wildly unstable statistics; any model where batch size drops to 1 (online inference) and BatchNorm produces degenerate predictions.

seeds.t: Transformers Are Permutation-Invariant Without Positional Encoding — Token Order Is Not Implicit
seeds.w: Omitting positional encoding from a Transformer — self-attention is permutation-equivariant: QKᵀ scores depend only on content, not position. Without positional information the model cannot distinguish token order at all.
seeds.c: Add positional encoding to token embeddings before attention. Options: sinusoidal (fixed, generalises), learned absolute (BERT/GPT, limited to train length), relative bias (T5), RoPE (LLaMA — rotates Q/K, generalises well). Omitting PE = bag-of-words Transformer.
seeds.s: a sequence model where the first token is always the most attended regardless of content; a causal LM that generates valid tokens but in random order.

seeds.t: KV Cache Stores Past Keys and Values for Autoregressive Inference Only — It Is Not Used During Training
seeds.w: Enabling KV cache during training, or omitting it during autoregressive inference — KV cache is only valid for inference (one token at a time); training processes the full sequence in parallel with a causal mask and needs no cache.
seeds.c: Training: full sequence in parallel + causal mask; no cache. Inference: KV cache stores past K and V; appends new token's KV each step; reduces per-step cost from O(t) to O(1) attention. Memory: 2 × L × H × d_head × context_len × dtype_bytes.
seeds.s: training memory usage that grows with sequence length step-by-step (KV cache mistakenly enabled during training); inference that recomputes all previous K/V from scratch on every token (cache disabled, O(n²) instead of O(n) cost).

seeds.t: Fine-Tuning a Pretrained Model Requires a Much Lower Learning Rate Than Training From Scratch
seeds.w: Using the same LR for fine-tuning as for training from scratch (e.g., 1e-3) — a high LR during fine-tuning overwrites pretrained representations. Full fine-tuning: use 1e-5 to 5e-5 (Adam). LoRA/PEFT: 1e-4 to 3e-4. Always combine with warmup.
seeds.c: Fine-tuning LR: 10–100× lower than scratch training. Full FT: ~2e-5 (Adam). LoRA: ~1e-4. Use discriminative LR (lower for early layers). Always use warmup. Freeze lower layers early; unfreeze from top down. High LR → catastrophic forgetting.
seeds.s: fine-tuned model performs worse than the zero-shot pretrained baseline; training loss drops quickly but validation loss spikes; model generates incoherent outputs after one epoch of fine-tuning at a 1e-3 LR.

seeds.t: Label Smoothing Improves Generalisation but Hurts Probability Calibration — Do Not Use for Distillation Targets
seeds.w: Applying label smoothing to models used as teachers in knowledge distillation — label smoothing compresses the teacher's soft probability distribution, destroying the 'dark knowledge' that makes distillation effective. Train teachers without label smoothing.
seeds.c: Label smoothing: y' = (1−ε)y + ε/K; prevents overconfidence; improves standalone classifier accuracy. Hurts distillation: compresses teacher soft targets. For calibration post-training use temperature scaling (no training change needed).
seeds.s: a knowledge distillation pipeline where the teacher was trained with label smoothing and the student significantly underperforms a student trained from a non-smoothed teacher; a deployed classifier with label smoothing that returns max probabilities of 0.87 regardless of input, …

seeds.t: Double Descent: Test Error Can Decrease Again After the Interpolation Threshold as Model Size or Steps Increase
seeds.w: Applying the classical bias-variance U-curve and concluding that models above the interpolation threshold always overfit — double descent shows test error decreases again in the over-parameterised regime; the interpolation threshold peak is not the asymptote.
seeds.c: Double descent: bias-variance tradeoff holds below interpolation threshold; at the threshold, error peaks; above it, error decreases again (minimum-norm interpolation generalises). Epoch-wise double descent also exists. Classical U-curve is incomplete.
seeds.s: a tuning run where larger models slightly underperform smaller ones at a fixed epoch budget — the larger model may not have passed the double-descent peak yet.

seeds.t: A Single Training Run Is Not a Reliable Estimate of Model Performance — Report Mean and Std Over Multiple Seeds
seeds.w: Reporting a single training run's result as the model's performance — stochastic training produces high run-to-run variance. Report mean ± std over ≥ 3 seeds. Fix all random seeds for reproducibility. Use paired statistical tests for model comparisons.
seeds.c: Run ≥ 3 seeds; report mean ± std. Fix seeds: torch.manual_seed + np.random.seed + random.seed + CUDA seed. Use McNemar's test (classifiers) or paired t-test (regression) for comparison. Cherry-picking best seed biases reported results upward.
seeds.s: two models whose mean performance is identical but whose single-run comparison produces a clear 'winner'; a paper result that cannot be reproduced within 2% of the reported performance across 5 attempts.

seeds.t: Subtracting Nearly Equal Floats Destroys Precision
seeds.w: Subtracting two nearly equal floating-point numbers, losing all significant digits to cancellation.
seeds.c: Restructure the formula algebraically to avoid subtracting near-equal values; use log1p, expm1, and the stable quadratic root form.
seeds.s: result is many orders of magnitude less accurate than input precision suggests; relative error explodes at a specific input value (e.g., two nearly equal inputs).

seeds.t: Naive One-Pass Variance Formula Is Numerically Unstable
seeds.w: Using the textbook formula Var(X) = E[X²] − (E[X])² numerically — catastrophic cancellation for large-mean, small-variance data.
seeds.c: Use Welford's online algorithm, which accumulates squared deviations relative to a running mean and is stable regardless of mean magnitude.
seeds.s: variance computed as negative, NaN, or wildly inaccurate for a dataset with a large mean and small spread (e.g., Kelvin temperatures, GPS coordinates).

seeds.t: Solve Linear Systems Without Matrix Inversion
seeds.w: Calling inv(A) explicitly to solve Ax=b, tripling both error and computation cost compared to a direct solve.
seeds.c: Use numpy.linalg.solve(A, b) or an LU/Cholesky factorisation; never use the normal equations for least-squares.
seeds.s: solution x is wildly inaccurate despite A being theoretically invertible; residual ‖Ax − b‖ is large relative to ‖b‖.

seeds.t: High Condition Number Invalidates Linear Solve Results
seeds.w: Solving Ax=b without inspecting κ(A), getting an answer that looks plausible but is numerically meaningless.
seeds.c: Check np.linalg.cond(A) first; apply Tikhonov regularisation or SVD truncation if κ exceeds safe thresholds for the required precision.
seeds.s: small perturbations to b produce wildly different x; iterative refinement fails to converge; condition number warning printed by scipy.

seeds.t: Full Kelly Overbets: Apply Fractional Instead
seeds.w: Using the full Kelly fraction f* without discounting for probability estimation error, producing severe drawdown from edge overestimation.
seeds.c: Apply fractional Kelly (half or quarter) which retains most growth while dramatically reducing variance and sensitivity to estimation error.
seeds.s: geometric mean of wealth growing slower than expected despite correct edge; portfolio drawdowns far exceeding what return variance would predict under a fixed-fraction strategy.

seeds.t: Black-Scholes Assumes Constant Volatility — Markets Do Not
seeds.w: Using a single Black-Scholes IV for all strikes, systematically mispricing tails because the model ignores the volatility smile.
seeds.c: Calibrate to a full implied-volatility surface or use local/stochastic volatility models for cross-strike and cross-maturity consistency.
seeds.s: model prices match at-the-money options but are systematically mispriced for OTM puts and calls; vega hedging does not eliminate P&L for tail options.

seeds.t: Itô and Stratonovich Integrals Give Different Results
seeds.w: Applying the classical calculus chain rule to a stochastic process and omitting the (½)f''(X)(dX)² Itô correction term.
seeds.c: Apply Itô's lemma including the quadratic variation term (½)f''(X)dt; explicitly declare Itô or Stratonovich convention for every SDE.
seeds.s: simulated diffusion paths with mean systematically above or below the analytical expectation; Fokker-Planck equation derived incorrectly because chain rule was applied naively.

seeds.t: Ito Correction: GBM Drift Is Not Mu
seeds.w: Assuming the drift parameter μ equals the log-return growth rate, omitting the σ²/2 Itô correction (volatility drag).
seeds.c: Log-return growth rate = μ − σ²/2; account for this correction when fitting GBM or converting between arithmetic and geometric return expectations.
seeds.s: GBM Monte Carlo median far below E[S(T)] by factor e^(−σ²T/2), growing worse over long horizons; fitted drift systematically undershooting risk-neutral forward prices.

seeds.t: Chaos Requires Positive Lyapunov and Bounded Orbits
seeds.w: Declaring chaos from λ₁ > 0 alone without verifying bounded trajectories and examining the full Lyapunov spectrum.
seeds.c: Require both λ₁ > 0 and bounded orbits; compute the full Lyapunov spectrum and Kaplan-Yorke dimension to characterise the attractor.
seeds.s: system classified as chaotic despite trajectories diverging to infinity; or chaos misidentified in a stiff limit cycle with transient sensitivity.

seeds.t: Hausdorff and Box-Counting Dimensions Can Diverge
seeds.w: Interchanging Hausdorff and box-counting dimension, or reporting a 'fractal dimension' without specifying which definition was used.
seeds.c: Report which dimension is computed and the scale range; for self-similar sets use the Moran equation for the exact similarity dimension.
seeds.s: two algorithms reporting different 'fractal dimensions' for the same dataset — they measure different things.

seeds.t: Jensen's Inequality: E[f(X)] ≠ f(E[X]) for Nonlinear f
seeds.w: Substituting E[X] into a nonlinear function f to approximate E[f(X)], ignoring Jensen's inequality and the convexity correction.
seeds.c: Compute E[f(X)] directly or via the delta method: f(E[X]) + (f''(E[X])/2)Var(X) + O(Var²); for log-returns always subtract σ²/2.
seeds.s: strategy with positive expected return consistently losing money long-run; option priced at intrinsic value only, missing time value.

seeds.t: Time-Average Growth Rate ≠ Ensemble-Average Expected Value
seeds.w: Maximising expected wealth E[W] for a multiplicative process, choosing a strategy that harms the typical individual trajectory.
seeds.c: Maximise time-average growth rate g = E[log(1+r)] ≈ μ − σ²/2; use the geometric (not arithmetic) mean for any compounding process.
seeds.s: strategy with positive expected return consistently loses money; Monte Carlo shows mean growing but median trajectory declining.

seeds.t: High-Degree Polynomial Interpolation Oscillates at Boundaries
seeds.w: Using equidistant nodes for high-degree polynomial interpolation, producing boundary oscillations that grow with polynomial degree.
seeds.c: Use Chebyshev nodes to eliminate Runge's phenomenon; for n ≥ 10 prefer piecewise cubic splines over global high-degree polynomials.
seeds.s: interpolant with large oscillations near x = ±1 that worsen as more nodes are added.

seeds.t: Fourier Series Near Discontinuities Always Overshoots by ~9%
seeds.w: Expecting that adding more Fourier terms eliminates overshoot at a discontinuity — the Gibbs phenomenon is intrinsic, not a sampling artifact.
seeds.c: Apply Lanczos sigma factors to coefficients to suppress ringing; use Gegenbauer reconstruction or wavelets for piecewise-smooth signals with genuine jumps.
seeds.s: ringing artifacts in filtered audio or reconstructed images near sharp edges that persist at arbitrarily high bandwidth.

seeds.t: Cauchy Has No Mean and CLT Fails
seeds.w: Computing a sample mean or invoking the CLT for Cauchy or heavy-tailed data where the theoretical mean does not exist.
seeds.c: Estimate tail index α first; for Cauchy and α ≤ 1 distributions use median and IQR-based scale estimates rather than mean and variance.
seeds.s: sample mean of heavy-tailed data never stabilises across bootstrap resamples; confidence intervals for the mean widen rather than narrow with more data.

seeds.t: Multiple Tests Require Corrected p-Values or FDR Control
seeds.w: Testing m hypotheses at α = 0.05 each without correction, producing familywise false-positive rate of 1−(1−α)^m ≈ 1 for large m.
seeds.c: Use Bonferroni or Holm-Bonferroni for FWER control; use Benjamini-Hochberg for FDR control in exploratory multi-testing scenarios.
seeds.s: many 'significant' genes, variants, or A/B test segments in a large sweep, none of which replicate in a follow-up study.

seeds.t: Aggregated Trends Can Reverse When Data Is Disaggregated
seeds.w: Reporting pooled-group effects without checking if a confounder reverses the direction in every subgroup.
seeds.c: Disaggregate by all potential confounders; verify direction is consistent across subgroups; use Pearl's backdoor criterion for causal identification.
seeds.s: treatment benefit reverses direction or disappears entirely after adjusting for one covariate; subgroup-level effects all point one way while the overall effect points the other.

seeds.t: Gradient Descent Diverges Without a Lipschitz-Bounded Step
seeds.w: Choosing a fixed learning rate without bounding it by 1/L, causing divergence or oscillation when α > 2/L.
seeds.c: Set α ≤ 1/L (estimated via backtracking line search or power iteration on the Hessian); verify loss decreases monotonically for each step.
seeds.s: training loss oscillates or increases after each gradient step; NaN loss after a few iterations with large learning rate.

seeds.t: Bayes Theorem Requires an Explicit Prior
seeds.w: Interpreting test sensitivity as P(hypothesis|positive result), ignoring the prior probability (base rate).
seeds.c: Apply the full Bayes formula with an explicit prior; recalibrate when deployment prevalence differs substantially from training prevalence.
seeds.s: classifier with high precision on training data performing near-randomly on deployment with different prevalence; rare-event detector flooding with false positives.

seeds.t: Finite Difference Step h Has a Precision-Optimal Value
seeds.w: Making the finite-difference step h as small as possible, hitting the cancellation-error floor and increasing gradient noise.
seeds.c: Use h ≈ √ε for forward, h ≈ ε^(1/3) for central differences; or complex-step differentiation (Im[f(x+ih)]/h) for cancellation-free high accuracy.
seeds.s: numerical gradient improves with decreasing h until h ≈ 10⁻⁸, then suddenly becomes noisier; gradient check in a neural network failing only at very small h.

seeds.t: Natural Dataset First Digits Follow a Logarithmic Distribution
seeds.w: Assuming uniformly distributed leading digits in natural data, missing the log₁₀(1+1/d) distribution and its fraud-detection implications.
seeds.c: For scale-invariant datasets spanning ≥2 orders of magnitude, test first-digit distribution against P(d)=log₁₀(1+1/d) via chi-squared with 8 degrees of freedom.
seeds.s: manually-entered financial records showing suspiciously uniform first-digit distribution; generated test data failing Benford conformity that real data passes.

seeds.t: Power Law and Log-Normal Tails Are Visually Indistinguishable
seeds.w: Fitting a power law via OLS on a log-log plot, which is biased and cannot distinguish power laws from log-normals.
seeds.c: Use MLE for α with KS-minimised x_min (Clauset et al. 2009); formally test against log-normal and exponential alternatives via likelihood ratio test.
seeds.s: reported exponent α changes substantially depending on the data range chosen; the log-log plot looks linear but the KS statistic is poor; alternative distribution fits are not tested.

seeds.t: Lagrange Multipliers Require Constraint Qualification to Be Valid
seeds.w: Applying Lagrange multipliers without verifying a constraint qualification, potentially missing optima at degenerate constraint boundaries.
seeds.c: Check LICQ or Mangasarian-Fromovitz CQ at candidates; for convex programs verify Slater's condition, which makes KKT both necessary and sufficient.
seeds.s: KKT conditions yield no critical points despite the constrained minimum clearly existing; or KKT conditions give a point that is not the global minimum.

seeds.t: Tower Property: Marginalise Over Hidden Variables
seeds.w: Computing E[X] without using the tower property to marginalise over a conditioning variable Y, or computing E[X|Y=y] for one value of Y and treating it as the full expectation.
seeds.c: Apply E[X] = E[E[X|Y]] and Var(X) = E[Var(X|Y)] + Var(E[X|Y]) to decompose over hierarchical or latent structure.
seeds.s: Monte Carlo estimate with unexpectedly high variance because conditional structure was not exploited; closed-form calculation missing a constant factor from incorrect marginalisation.

seeds.t: DFT Output Bins Above N/2 Represent Negative Frequencies
seeds.w: Labelling DFT bin k as frequency k·Fs/N for all k, misinterpreting the negative-frequency bins N/2+1 through N−1.
seeds.c: Use np.fft.fftfreq(N, 1/Fs) for the correct signed frequency axis; use fftshift for DC-centred display; use rfft for real signals to avoid the redundant negative-frequency half.
seeds.s: spectrum appears to have a symmetric mirror above Fs/2; reconstructed signal is temporally shifted or aliased.

seeds.t: MCMC Thinning Wastes Computation — Run Longer Instead
seeds.w: Thinning MCMC chains to reduce autocorrelation, reducing ESS for the same compute budget without improving convergence.
seeds.c: Compute ESS = N/(1+2Σρ_k) and R̂; run chains longer rather than thinning; switch to NUTS/HMC for better mixing efficiency.
seeds.s: MCMC trace looks well-mixed visually but ESS is < 100; posterior quantile estimates unstable across runs of the same length.

seeds.t: Use lgamma Not Direct Factorial Computation
seeds.w: Computing n! or products of large factorials directly in floating point, overflowing at n=171 or underflowing probabilities to 0.
seeds.c: Use math.lgamma(n+1) for log(n!); work entirely in log-space; apply the log-sum-exp trick log(Σeˣᵢ) = max(x)+log(Σexp(xᵢ−max(x))).
seeds.s: RuntimeWarning: overflow encountered in double_scalars for combinatorial coefficients; NaN likelihood from log(0) due to probability underflowing to 0 before taking the log.

seeds.t: Kahan Summation Reduces Float Accumulation Error
seeds.w: Accumulating a float sum in a loop without compensation, losing O(n·ε) precision proportional to the term count.
seeds.c: Use Kahan compensated summation, math.fsum for exact results, or np.sum (pairwise by default) for stable float accumulation.
seeds.s: sum of many small values returning 0 or an incorrect order-of-magnitude result in float32; two different summation orders for the same data producing significantly different totals.

seeds.t: P-Value Is Not the Probability of the Hypothesis
seeds.w: Interpreting p = 0.05 as '5% probability the null hypothesis is true', conflating P(data|H₀) with P(H₀|data).
seeds.c: p-value = P(observed data or more extreme | H₀). For P(H₀|data), apply Bayes' theorem with an explicit prior on H₀.
seeds.s: paper concludes '96% confidence the effect exists' from p = 0.04; effect vanishes on replication with larger sample; meta-analysis shows consistent publication of just-significant results.

seeds.t: SVD Gives Numerical Rank Not Determinant
seeds.w: Using det(A) ≠ 0 to check matrix rank in floating point — near-singular matrices appear full-rank despite det ≈ ε.
seeds.c: Compute SVD and count singular values above τ = max(m,n)·σ_max·ε; use truncated SVD for rank-k approximation and pseudoinverse.
seeds.s: np.linalg.solve returning wildly wrong answer for a matrix that looks invertible; PCA retaining noise components because rank was not truncated.

seeds.t: Explicit ODE Solvers Explode on Stiff Systems
seeds.w: Applying Euler or RK4 to stiff ODEs, requiring h < 2/|λ_max| steps regardless of solution smoothness.
seeds.c: Use scipy.integrate.solve_ivp with method='Radau' or 'BDF' for stiff systems; detect stiffness via Jacobian eigenvalue ratio or solver step-rejection rate.
seeds.s: RK4 solution diverging to ±∞ or oscillating wildly; step size auto-halving repeatedly until it hits the minimum floor.

seeds.t: Extreme Values Regress Toward Mean Naturally
seeds.w: Attributing improvement in extreme scorers to an intervention without accounting for natural regression toward the mean.
seeds.c: Include a control group also selected at the extreme; quantify expected regression as z·(1−r) SDs toward the mean where r is test-retest correlation.
seeds.s: 'treatment' group shows dramatic improvement; the untreated matched-extreme control group improves nearly as much; effect sizes in the treated group shrink substantially toward the next measurement period.

seeds.t: KL Divergence Direction Determines Approximation Behavior
seeds.w: Treating D_KL(P‖Q) and D_KL(Q‖P) as equivalent — they produce mass-covering vs. mode-seeking approximations respectively.
seeds.c: Choose KL direction deliberately: forward KL for mass-covering, reverse KL (ELBO) for mode-seeking; use Jensen-Shannon for a symmetric bounded measure.
seeds.s: variational posterior ignoring multiple modes of the true posterior; GAN training instability from asymmetric divergence in non-standard objectives.

seeds.t: Prediction Error Decomposes Into Bias Variance Noise
seeds.w: Adding model capacity or tuning without decomposing whether test error is dominated by bias or variance.
seeds.c: Diagnose: both train+test high = bias; train low, test high = variance. Target regularisation (variance) or model capacity (bias) accordingly.
seeds.s: adding model capacity no longer improves test loss beyond a point; regularisation helps test loss but hurts train loss — variance was the dominant term.

seeds.t: Birthday Collision Probability Scales as Square Root
seeds.w: Expecting the first hash collision after O(N) samples — it occurs at O(√N) by the birthday paradox.
seeds.c: Plan for first collision at k* ≈ 1.25√N; double hash bit-width to halve the birthday-attack risk; use UUID7 or time-ordered IDs for temporal uniqueness.
seeds.s: hash table collision rate much higher than expected at 10% fill; distributed ID generation producing duplicate IDs far earlier than the 'total space' calculation suggested.

seeds.t: Confidence Intervals Are Not Probability Statements
seeds.w: Reading a 95% CI as '95% probability the parameter is inside it' — the parameter is fixed; the interval is the random quantity.
seeds.c: 95% CI means 95% of intervals from this procedure contain the true value; use a Bayesian credible interval for P(θ ∈ [a,b] | data) = 0.95.
seeds.s: A/B test CI of [+0.2%, +3.1%] interpreted as '95% certain the effect is positive'; post-hoc CI computation used to infer certainty about a specific observed result.

seeds.t: Put-Call Parity Is a Model-Free Constraint
seeds.w: Pricing calls and puts independently without enforcing C − P = S − Ke^(−rT), missing the model-free no-arbitrage constraint.
seeds.c: Enforce put-call parity as a hard constraint; use it to extract model-free forward prices and to detect borrow costs or liquidity premiums.
seeds.s: model-generated call and put prices each look reasonable individually but their difference deviates from S − Ke^(−rT); put prices in thinly traded markets deviating from parity because the stock is hard to borrow short.

seeds.t: Optional Stopping Cannot Profit From a Martingale
seeds.w: Stopping a sequential test the moment p < α is reached, exploiting optional stopping to inflate the Type I error rate far above nominal α.
seeds.c: Pre-specify sample size; or use SPRT, O'Brien-Fleming alpha-spending, or anytime-valid e-processes for valid sequential testing.
seeds.s: A/B test reaching significance at n = 500 that disappears by n = 2000; repeated peeking inflating false positive rate to 30–40% for nominal 5% tests.

seeds.t: High Dimensions Make All Pairwise Distances Equal
seeds.w: Applying Euclidean nearest-neighbour or distance-based methods in high dimensions where all pairwise distances concentrate to the same value.
seeds.c: Reduce to intrinsic dimensionality first (PCA, UMAP); use cosine similarity for embeddings; apply FAISS LSH for high-d approximate nearest-neighbour.
seeds.s: k-NN classifier accuracy peaking then declining as feature count grows; clustering producing near-identical intracluster and intercluster distances in high-d feature spaces.

seeds.t: Convolution via FFT Is O(n log n)
seeds.w: Computing convolution directly in the spatial domain at O(n²), when FFT reduces it to O(n log n) with the convolution theorem.
seeds.c: Use scipy.signal.fftconvolve for signals longer than ~50 elements; pad to length ≥ m+n−1 for linear (non-circular) convolution.
seeds.s: large-kernel image blur (σ = 50 px) taking minutes with scipy.ndimage.convolve that should take milliseconds with fftconvolve.

seeds.t: Identical Summary Statistics Can Hide Different Shapes
seeds.w: Reporting summary statistics or a regression fit without plotting the data, missing gross model assumption violations invisible to numbers alone.
seeds.c: Always plot raw data and residuals; use QQ-plots, residual-vs-fitted, and Cook's distance before trusting any statistical model.
seeds.s: regression R² = 0.68 reported confidently, but a residual plot shows a clear curve the linear model completely misses.

seeds.t: VaR Ignores Tail Severity — Use Expected Shortfall
seeds.w: Using VaR alone — it ignores the severity of losses beyond the threshold and is not subadditive, incentivising tail-risk concentration.
seeds.c: Use Expected Shortfall ES_α = E[loss | loss > VaR_α] = mean of the worst (1−α) fraction; it is coherent and captures tail severity.
seeds.s: portfolio optimised for minimum VaR showing catastrophic drawdowns; risk limits violated by loss far exceeding VaR despite 'within limits' reporting.

seeds.t: Classical Gram-Schmidt Is Numerically Unstable
seeds.w: Using classical Gram-Schmidt, which simultaneously subtracts all previous projections and accumulates errors that destroy orthogonality for ill-conditioned inputs.
seeds.c: Use modified Gram-Schmidt (sequential projections) or Householder QR via numpy.linalg.qr for numerically reliable orthogonalisation.
seeds.s: GMRES or CG iterative solver failing to converge because the Krylov basis is not truly orthogonal; CGS producing a Q where QᵀQ deviates from identity by 10⁻³ for a moderately ill-conditioned system.

seeds.t: Random Arrival Samples Longer Intervals More Often
seeds.w: Estimating average interval length from a random-time observer — length-biased sampling overweights longer intervals by factor μ(1 + CV²)/μ.
seeds.c: Account for length bias: E[observed] = μ + σ²/μ; correct by weighting by 1/observed_length or by sampling event start times rather than ongoing events.
seeds.s: monitoring tool reporting average task duration 2–3× higher than batch log statistics for the same workload.

seeds.t: Hilbert Curves Preserve Multidimensional Locality in 1D
seeds.w: Using row-major linearisation for spatial or multidimensional data, breaking locality between geometrically adjacent points at row boundaries.
seeds.c: Use Hilbert curve ordering for spatial indexes, matrix tiles, and multi-dimensional cache layouts to preserve adjacency in the 1D traversal.
seeds.s: spatial cache miss rate far higher than expected for nearby accesses; database 2D range queries scanning a multiple of the expected number of pages.

seeds.t: Reservoir Networks Peak at Edge of Chaos
seeds.w: Setting reservoir spectral radius far from 1 — ρ << 1 causes over-damping, ρ >> 1 causes chaos; both destroy useful temporal computation.
seeds.c: Set ρ(W) ≈ 1 for reservoir networks; use He initialisation (σ²_w = 2/fan_in) for deep networks to operate near the order/chaos critical point.
seeds.s: ESN with ρ = 0.3 unable to learn temporal patterns beyond a few steps; ESN with ρ = 1.8 producing explosive outputs; deep network with large weight variance showing exploding gradients in early layers.

seeds.t: IFS Contraction Mappings Converge to Unique Fractal
seeds.w: Applying an IFS without verifying all maps are strict contractions (Lip < 1), or storing self-similar structures point-by-point when a compact IFS description exists.
seeds.c: Verify contractivity (Lip(fᵢ) < 1 for all i); iterate any starting set to the unique attractor; use IFS for resolution-independent storage of self-similar structures.
seeds.s: IFS iteration not converging to the expected attractor — at least one map is not a strict contraction; each iteration producing a set that shifts rather than converges.

seeds.t: Newton Iteration Has Three Distinct Terminal States
seeds.w: Stopping when |z_{n+1} - z_n| < ε, assuming convergence means convergence to a root
seeds.c: After step-size convergence, verify f(z) ≈ 0; a converged step-size with f(z) >> ε indicates an extraneous attractor
seeds.s: rendered Newton fractal basin regions with no corresponding root — stable but uncoloured; iteration exits the convergence loop but f(z_final) >> ε.

seeds.t: Extraneous Attractor Basins in Newton Fractals Can Be Mandelbrot-Shaped
seeds.w: Treating Mandelbrot-shaped structures in Newton fractal plots as rendering artifacts or escaped points
seeds.c: Mandelbrot-shaped traps are genuine extraneous attractor basins from Douady-Hubbard universality; colour them as a distinct attractor class
seeds.s: parameter-space plots of Newton fractals show unexpected Mandelbrot-like structures that are not roots; escape-radius rendering leaves them as 'escaped' when they are actually trapped in stable cycles.

seeds.t: Mandelbrot Escape Radius Is Wrong Stopping Condition for Newton Fractals
seeds.w: Using |z| > 2 as basin escape condition copied from Mandelbrot renderer
seeds.c: Use root convergence |z - r_k| < ε as primary; |z| > 1e10 as overflow guard only, not basin classifier
seeds.s: Newton fractal renders with a large 'escaped' region that should be coloured by basin; coloured region boundary does not match the theoretical Julia set.

seeds.t: Newton Iteration Needs Cycle Detection for Extraneous Attractors
seeds.w: Newton loop has only residual check and maxIter cap; extraneous attractor pixels always hit maxIter
seeds.c: Add Floyd tortoise-hare cycle detection: if |z_fast - z_slow| < ε, mark as periodic attractor and exit
seeds.s: Newton fractal render time dominated by extraneous attractor basin pixels each running to maxIter; disabling max-iteration cap causes infinite loops on those pixels.

seeds.t: Fatou Set Is Stable Dynamics, Julia Set Is Chaotic — Not Both Unstable
seeds.w: Treating Fatou set as 'unstable chaos' and Julia set as just a boundary line
seeds.c: Fatou set = stable equicontinuity region (basins, Siegel disks, Herman rings); Julia set = sensitivity boundary of measure zero
seeds.s: rendering artefacts along basin boundaries that change with floating-point precision mode; anti-aliasing that samples 2×2 sub-pixels shows disagreeing basin colours for pixels near basin boundaries.

seeds.t: Smooth Mandelbrot Colouring Requires the Log-Log Continuous Escape Formula
seeds.w: Colouring by iteration count directly or iter/maxIter — discrete integer causes banding
seeds.c: μ = iter + 1 - log(log|z_n|)/log(degree) gives continuous escape time; map μ through a smooth palette
seeds.s: Mandelbrot render shows visible concentric rings ('bands') regardless of colour palette; increasing colour palette resolution does not remove the rings.

seeds.t: Float64 Loses All Mandelbrot Precision Beyond Zoom Level 1e14
seeds.w: Increasing max iterations when deep zoom produces uniform colour blobs
seeds.c: Beyond zoom 1e14, float64 is insufficient — use perturbation theory or arbitrary-precision centre coordinates
seeds.s: deep zoom render where all pixels have the same colour or form a uniform blurry blob; zooming past ~1e14 produces no new detail regardless of iteration count.

seeds.t: Julia Set Lives in the Dynamic Plane — Mandelbrot Set Lives in Parameter Space
seeds.w: Sweeping c to render a Julia set, or sweeping z₀ with c fixed to render the Mandelbrot set
seeds.c: Mandelbrot: fix z₀ = 0, sweep c. Julia set J_c: fix c, sweep z₀ over the plane
seeds.s: 'Julia set' render looks like a distorted Mandelbrot; 'Mandelbrot set' render shows a single Julia set computed at (0,0).

seeds.t: Nova Relaxation R Must Be Complex for Spiral Basin Structure
seeds.w: R = 0.8 (real scalar) — only scales step size, no rotation of Newton direction
seeds.c: R = 0.8 * exp(i * theta) (complex) — scales AND rotates the Newton step, producing spiral basin structure
seeds.s: Nova fractal render is structurally identical to a Newton fractal with different iteration counts; no spiral basin boundaries visible; rotating R in the UI has no visual effect on basin geometry.

seeds.t: Nova Mandelbrot and Nova Julia Sweep Different Variables
seeds.w: Sweeping z₀ and calling the result the Nova Mandelbrot set
seeds.c: Nova Mandelbrot: fix z₀ (e.g. 1.0+0i), sweep c. Nova Julia: fix c, sweep z₀ as pixel coordinate
seeds.s: 'Nova Mandelbrot' image looks like a slightly distorted Newton fractal rather than a Mandelbrot-like parameter space with bulb structure; changing c in a Julia-set viewer has no effect on the rendered image.

seeds.t: Nova Fixed Points Are Not Polynomial Roots When c≠0
seeds.w: if abs(z - precomputed_root) < eps: converged — fails for all c≠0 Nova iterations
seeds.c: Use cycle detection to find any fixed point; colour by attractor identity, not by proximity to polynomial roots
seeds.s: Nova Julia set renders entirely as the 'non-converging' colour with no basin regions; decreasing ε does not help; the image is structurally the same for any nonzero c value.

seeds.t: Over-Relaxation |R|>1 Can Escape a Basin That Standard Newton Converges In
seeds.w: Increasing |R| > 1 unconditionally to accelerate convergence
seeds.c: Check contractivity: |R·f''(r)/(2f'(r))·δ| < 1; use line-search to reduce |R| when residual increases step-to-step
seeds.s: iteration with R=1.5 converges for starting points near a root but diverges or finds wrong root for starting points that R=1 converges correctly; render shows missing basin regions compared to standard Newton.

seeds.t: Halley's Method Denominator Cancels Near Inflection Points
seeds.w: z -= f * f_prime / (f_prime**2 - f * f_double_prime / 2) — no guard for near-zero denominator
seeds.c: if abs(f * f_pp) < delta * abs(fp**2): use Newton step; elif abs(denom) < eps: use Newton; else: use Halley
seeds.s: Halley iteration produces NaN or Inf on specific starting points that Newton handles correctly; render shows black pixels (NaN colour) scattered through regions that Newton renders correctly.

seeds.t: Optimal Nova R Depends on Problem Conditioning, Not a Universal Constant
seeds.w: Fixed R = 0.5 or 1.5 regardless of polynomial; works for one case, fails silently for another
seeds.c: Estimate |f''|/|f'²| to gauge conditioning; use R near 1.0 for well-conditioned, R < 0.7 for ill-conditioned problems
seeds.s: fixed R works for one polynomial but produces incorrect basin assignments or non-convergence for another polynomial with roots at similar locations.

seeds.t: Steffensen's Method Achieves Quadratic Convergence Without f'
seeds.w: Using secant method (superlinear convergence) when f' is unavailable
seeds.c: z_{n+1} = z_n - f(z_n)² / [f(z_n + f(z_n)) - f(z_n)] — quadratic convergence, no f' required; guard: if |f(z_n)| < ε fall back to numerical Newton
seeds.s: Steffensen iteration stalls within 1e-6 of root rather than converging to machine precision; denominator underflow on final steps.

seeds.t: Burning Ship Is Non-Holomorphic — Smooth Colouring Fails Directly
seeds.w: Applying Mandelbrot smooth colouring formula directly to Burning Ship iteration
seeds.c: Smooth colouring is an approximation only; for distance estimation, account for sign reversals in dz at the fold lines Re(z)=0 and Im(z)=0
seeds.s: Burning Ship render shows smooth colouring bands that cut across the fold lines at Re=0 and Im=0 with visible discontinuities; boundary anti-aliasing samples disagree on either side of fold axes.

seeds.t: Burning Ship Formula Is Conventionally Rendered Upside-Down
seeds.w: Using c = x + iy directly and assuming screen Y-down convention makes the orientation correct
seeds.c: Explicitly negate Im(c) or flip the output image; document which convention the code uses rather than relying on coordinate accident
seeds.s: Burning Ship renders correctly in a canvas renderer (Y-down) but appears upside-down when the same code is adapted for a mathematical plot (Y-up); the 'ship' silhouette is mirrored vertically relative to reference images.

seeds.t: Buddhabrot Requires Orbit Storage — Cannot Reuse Escape-Time Code
seeds.w: Colouring c-pixels by escape time and calling the result a Buddhabrot
seeds.c: For each escaping c, store the full orbit and scatter-add to hit buffer; tone-map the density buffer; never use the Mandelbrot pixel-colour approach
seeds.s: 'Buddhabrot' render looks exactly like a standard Mandelbrot with different colours; no nebula-like orbit-density structure; changing max iterations has no visible effect on the image shape.

seeds.t: Buddhabrot Must Exclude Points Inside the Mandelbrot Set
seeds.w: Iterating all random c points and accumulating orbits for both escaping and bounded points
seeds.c: Pre-filter with cardioid/bulb check; only accumulate orbits for c values that escape within max_iter; use importance sampling near the boundary
seeds.s: Buddhabrot has a bright smear in the main cardioid region where the set interior should produce no orbit contributions; high-density regions near the set boundary are undersampled relative to the interior blob.

seeds.t: Distance Estimator Requires Iterating dz in the Main Loop Alongside z
seeds.w: Computing dz = (f(z + eps) - f(z)) / eps after the main iteration loop
seeds.c: In the main loop, before z = z*z + c: dz = 2*z*dz + 1. After escape: dist = abs(z)*log(abs(z))/abs(dz)
seeds.s: distance estimator values are noisy or discontinuous near fine filament structures; finite-difference approximation produces different boundary thickness at different zoom levels; NaN values appear when |z| is large.

seeds.t: Durand-Kerner Collapses When Initial Guesses Are Equal or Too Close
seeds.w: roots = [1, 1, 1, 1] or roots = [0, 0, 0, 0] as initial guess for Durand-Kerner
seeds.c: roots = [R * exp(2πik/n) for k in range(n)] where R > Cauchy bound; ensure initial guesses are distinct and well-separated
seeds.s: NaN or Inf on iteration 1 with equal-guess initialisation; extremely slow convergence (>1000 iterations) when initial guesses are real and clustered near the polynomial's real roots.

seeds.t: Orbit Trap Colouring Uses Minimum Distance Over All Iterates
seeds.w: min_dist = dist(z_final_before_escape, trap) — uses only the last iterate
seeds.c: min_dist = min(dist(z_k, trap) for all k in iteration loop) — update each step before z = z*z + c
seeds.s: orbit trap render shows a nearly uniform field with faint, blurry outlines instead of sharp trap-shaped structures; the 'trap' appears only near the centre of the image rather than distributed across the fractal.

seeds.t: Tricorn Is Antiholomorphic — Period-2 Cycles and Bulb Topology Differ
seeds.w: Using holomorphic period-detection f(f(z)) = z for the Tricorn — period-2 requires f(conj(f(conj(z)))) = z
seeds.c: Implement Tricorn as explicit real/imaginary operations (x,y) → (x,-y) then square; period-p detection uses alternating conjugation in the composition
seeds.s: period detection reports incorrect periods for Tricorn interior components; smooth colouring shows banding discontinuities at the boundary of the main body; bifurcation structures along the real axis appear with wrong topology compared to reference renders.

seeds.t: Aberth-Ehrlich Achieves Cubic Convergence via Electrostatic Repulsion
seeds.w: Using Durand-Kerner's product denominator ∏(z_k - z_j) — quadratic convergence
seeds.c: Aberth-Ehrlich: 1/E_j = p'/p - Σ_{j≠k} 1/(z_k-z_j) — cubic convergence, electrostatic repulsion prevents basin collapse
seeds.s: simultaneous root-finder is slower than expected for degree > 20; profiling shows most iterations spent in the inner loop with no convergence speedup compared to Newton.

seeds.t: Aberth-Ehrlich Overflows Float64 for High-Degree Polynomials
seeds.w: Direct Horner evaluation of p(z) for degree > 1000 — overflows float64 when |z| > 1
seeds.c: Evaluate log|p(z)| and arg(p(z)) separately; reconstruct ratio p/p' without materialising large intermediate values
seeds.s: Aberth iteration produces NaN or Inf on first step for degree > 100 polynomial with all positive coefficients; reducing degree to 10 makes NaN disappear; problem is coefficient magnitude not algorithm.

seeds.t: Multiple Roots Degrade Newton, Durand-Kerner, and Aberth to Linear Convergence
seeds.w: Running standard Aberth/Newton on polynomial with multiple roots, expecting fast convergence
seeds.c: Compute gcd(p, p'); divide out repeated factors; use modified Newton z' = z - m·f/f' when multiplicity m is known
seeds.s: Aberth iteration stalls with residual ~1e-6 for a polynomial with a known double root; iteration count hits maxIter without converging; residual oscillates rather than decreasing monotonically.

seeds.t: Polynomial Root-Finding from Coefficients Is Ill-Conditioned Even for Separated Roots
seeds.w: Assuming well-separated roots → well-conditioned problem in monomial coefficient form
seeds.c: Root-finding from monomial coefficients is generically ill-conditioned; use companion matrix eigenvalues or factored form directly
seeds.s: polynomial root-finder returns complex roots for a real polynomial known to have all real roots; roots cluster incorrectly when the polynomial was constructed as a product of linear factors; condition number of the coefficient-to-root map is 10^10 or larger for moderate-degree p…

seeds.t: Eigenvalues of the Companion Matrix Are More Stable Than Coefficient Root-Finding
seeds.w: Computing characteristic polynomial coefficients → finding roots of those coefficients
seeds.c: numpy.roots(coeffs) or scipy.linalg.eigvals(companion_matrix(coeffs)) — companion matrix eigenvalues bypass Wilkinson ill-conditioning
seeds.s: hand-rolled iterative root-finder returns wrong roots for degree-10 polynomial; numpy.roots() on the same coefficients returns correct roots because it uses the companion matrix internally.

seeds.t: Nebulabrot Requires Three Separate Hit Buffers with Different Iteration Depths
seeds.w: Single hit buffer for all orbit lengths, colour-mapped as a scalar
seeds.c: Three buffers with separate max_iter bounds (e.g. 500/5000/50000); orbit of length k only increments the buffer whose range contains k; sqrt-scale each buffer independently
seeds.s: Nebulabrot renders as uniform orange/grey rather than the characteristic blue-green-red nebula structure; all channels look identical; adjusting individual channel max_iter has no effect on the image.

seeds.t: Buddhabrot Hit Buffer Must Be Sqrt-Scaled Before Display
seeds.w: pixel = (hit_count / max_hit_count) * 255 — linear scale collapses the dynamic range
seeds.c: pixel = sqrt(hit_count / max_hit_count) * 255 — sqrt preserves gradient; scale each Nebulabrot channel independently before RGB combine
seeds.s: Buddhabrot render is almost entirely black with a few bright pixels; the characteristic nebula structure is invisible; adjusting brightness/contrast in an image editor partially recovers it, confirming the data is there but compressed.

seeds.t: Anti-Buddhabrot Traces Interior (Non-Escaping) Orbits — Opposite Algorithm to Buddhabrot
seeds.w: Inverting Buddhabrot colour map, or plotting non-escaping c values with escape-time colouring
seeds.c: For c inside M (reaches max_iter), store full orbit {z_0..z_{max_iter}} and scatter-add to hit buffer — identical pipeline to Buddhabrot but with the escape filter inverted
seeds.s: Anti-Buddhabrot render looks like a dim, blurry version of the regular Buddhabrot rather than a structurally different orbit-density image; interior structure is absent because escaping orbits are being plotted instead of bounded ones.

seeds.t: Chebyshev Basis Is Better Conditioned Than Monomial Basis for Polynomial Evaluation
seeds.w: Monomial basis Σ a_k x^k — catastrophic cancellation near roots, exponentially large coefficients
seeds.c: Chebyshev basis Σ c_k T_k(x) with Clenshaw evaluation — O(n) condition number, bounded coefficients, better companion matrix
seeds.s: polynomial evaluates to wrong sign near a known root using monomial Horner; same polynomial in Chebyshev basis evaluates correctly; condition number of Vandermonde system is 10^10+ while Chebyshev pseudo-Vandermonde is 10^3.

seeds.t: Phase Transitions Require Energy Flow Without Temperature Change
seeds.w: Detecting phase completion by temperature threshold — T stays constant during transition regardless of completion fraction
seeds.c: Track enthalpy or a phase-fraction variable φ; the transition is complete when L joules per kg have accumulated at constant T, not when T first reaches the transition point
seeds.s: simulation shows sharp temperature discontinuity instead of flat plateau at phase-change temperature; energy conservation violated near the transition; boiling point overshot before gas phase appears.

seeds.t: Hysteresis: Forward and Backward Transitions Have Different Thresholds
seeds.w: Single threshold for both forward and backward transitions — ignores remanence and metastable states
seeds.c: Two thresholds H_upper (forward) and H_lower < H_upper (reverse); state depends on history — implement as a stateful comparator with hysteresis gap
seeds.s: simulation of a magnetic relay chatters at the switching threshold; physical relay has separate pull-in and drop-out voltages but code uses one threshold for both directions; system oscillates when input is near threshold.

seeds.t: Supercooled Systems Are Metastable — Nucleation Triggers Abrupt Phase Change
seeds.w: Assuming threshold crossing immediately completes the phase transition — ignores metastability and nucleation barrier
seeds.c: Add nucleation probability per timestep: p_nucleate = 1 - exp(-J·Δt·V) where J is nucleation rate density (Arrhenius in supercooling depth)
seeds.s: phase-change simulation shows no transition even well below the transition temperature; adding a small perturbation causes instantaneous total phase change; the result depends on floating-point noise rather than physics.

seeds.t: Turbulent Energy Follows −5/3 Power Law Across Scales, Not Uniform Distribution
seeds.w: Expecting turbulent energy to be distributed uniformly across wavenumbers — requires same mesh resolution at all scales
seeds.c: E(k) ∝ k^{-5/3}: energy decays as a power law; mesh must span from integral scale to Kolmogorov scale; Re^{9/4} points required for DNS
seeds.s: CFD simulation produces unphysical large-scale oscillations because the mesh is too coarse to resolve the inertial subrange; adding more timesteps (not mesh refinement) does not fix it.

seeds.t: Maxwell-Boltzmann Gives Energy Distribution — Speed and Velocity Distributions Differ
seeds.w: Using the speed distribution f(v) to sample velocity vectors — f(v) is a scalar magnitude distribution, not a 3D vector distribution
seeds.c: Sample velocity as (N(0,kT/m), N(0,kT/m), N(0,kT/m)) — three independent Gaussian components; |v| then follows the Maxwell-Boltzmann speed distribution automatically
seeds.s: Monte Carlo gas simulation produces wrong escape rate because particles were sampled from f(v) treating v as a vector rather than a scalar speed.

seeds.t: Two-Phase Coexistence Is Not the Same as Measurement Uncertainty
seeds.w: Treating two-phase coexistence (constant T, energy still flowing) as measurement noise or numerical instability
seeds.c: Track phase fraction χ ∈ [0,1] as a separate variable; at equilibrium T is constrained by thermodynamics (Gibbs rule), not by measurement error
seeds.s: simulation treats the flat boiling plateau as a numerical instability and increases solver iterations unnecessarily; adding a regularisation term destabilises the phase boundary; the solver reports 'stiffness' at the transition because the Jacobian is near-singular (T doesn't re…

seeds.t: Computing log(Σ exp(xᵢ)) Overflows — Subtract the Maximum First
seeds.w: Computing log(Σ exp(xᵢ)) directly — exp(xᵢ) overflows for large inputs and underflows to 0 for very negative inputs, producing inf or NaN.
seeds.c: log(Σ exp(xᵢ)) = c + log(Σ exp(xᵢ−c)) where c = max(xᵢ). Use torch.logsumexp / jax.scipy.special.logsumexp; apply the same shift to softmax and log-softmax.
seeds.s: NaN or inf in a cross-entropy loss or attention score when input logits are large; log(0) when all but the max logit are very negative; numerically different results for logically equivalent softmax formulations.

seeds.t: Computing det(A) Then Taking log Overflows for n > ~100 — Use log-det Directly
seeds.w: Calling math.log(np.linalg.det(A)) — det overflows/underflows float64 for n ≳ 100 even when log(det(A)) is finite and moderate.
seeds.c: Use np.linalg.slogdet(A) → (sign, logabsdet) or Cholesky: log det(A) = 2 Σᵢ log Lᵢᵢ. Never materialise det(A) for large matrices.
seeds.s: log-likelihood returning NaN or −inf for large covariance matrices; det(Σ) = 0 for a covariance matrix that is clearly non-singular.

seeds.t: Real Eigendecomposition with Real Eigenvalues Requires a Real Symmetric Matrix
seeds.w: Using np.linalg.eig on a matrix believed to be symmetric, receiving complex eigenvalues from numerical noise, or expecting real eigenvalues from a non-symmetric matrix.
seeds.c: Use np.linalg.eigh for real symmetric / Hermitian matrices — guaranteed real eigenvalues and orthonormal eigenvectors. Symmetrise first if needed: A = (A + Aᵀ)/2.
seeds.s: eig returning complex eigenvalues with small imaginary parts for a matrix you believe is symmetric; eigh throwing an error because the matrix has accumulated asymmetry from floating-point operations.

seeds.t: Kronecker Product (⊗) and Hadamard Product (⊙) Are Different Operations With Different Shapes
seeds.w: Conflating the Kronecker product A ⊗ B (block structure, size mp×nq) with the Hadamard product A ⊙ B (element-wise, requires same shape).
seeds.c: Hadamard: (A ⊙ B)ᵢⱼ = AᵢⱼBᵢⱼ (same shape). Kronecker: (A ⊗ B) is a block matrix of shape mp×nq. Identity: vec(AXB) = (Bᵀ ⊗ A) vec(X).
seeds.s: shape mismatch when attempting element-wise multiplication on matrices of different sizes; incorrect covariance structure when using Kronecker-structured priors; LSTM gate computation producing wrong output shape.

seeds.t: tr(A) = Σ Eigenvalues and tr(AB) = tr(BA) — Misused in Matrix Gradient Derivations
seeds.w: Applying tr(AB) = tr(BA) to non-cyclic reorderings — tr(ABC) = tr(BCA) = tr(CAB), but tr(ABC) ≠ tr(BAC) in general.
seeds.c: Cyclic: tr(ABC) = tr(CAB) = tr(BCA). tr(A) = Σλᵢ. tr(AᵀA) = ‖A‖_F². Gradient: ∂tr(AᵀB)/∂A = B. Reordering is cyclic only, not commutative.
seeds.s: incorrect gradient derivations for loss functions involving log det or trace terms; wrong cyclic reordering producing a matrix of incorrect dimensions; computing ‖A‖_F² as tr(A²) instead of tr(AᵀA).

seeds.t: Moore-Penrose Pseudoinverse Gives the Minimum-Norm Least-Squares Solution — Not Any Least-Squares Solution
