seeds.w: Treating A⁺b as a general least-squares solver with regularisation — A⁺b gives the minimum-norm least-squares solution; ridge regression (A^ᵀA + λI)⁻¹Aᵀb gives a biased, regularised solution.
seeds.c: A⁺b minimises ‖Ax−b‖₂ with minimum ‖x‖₂. Via SVD: A⁺ = VΣ⁺Uᵀ. For regularisation, use (AᵀA + λI)⁻¹Aᵀ (ridge); for large λ the two diverge significantly.
seeds.s: using np.linalg.pinv for regularised regression (wrong — adds no regularisation, amplifies noise via small singular values); expecting pinv to solve an ill-conditioned system without understanding it will amplify directions with near-zero singular values.

seeds.t: Zero Correlation Does Not Imply Independence — Only for Jointly Gaussian Variables
seeds.w: Concluding X and Y are independent from ρ(X,Y) = 0 — correlation measures linear dependence only; Y = X² has ρ = 0 with X yet is completely determined by X.
seeds.c: ρ = 0 ↔ independence only for jointly Gaussian variables. For general independence testing use mutual information, distance correlation (dCor = 0 iff independent), HSIC, or copulas.
seeds.s: concluding two features are independent because their Pearson correlation is small; using ρ as a general independence test for non-Gaussian data; missing strong nonlinear dependencies in feature selection.

seeds.t: P(A|B) ≠ P(B|A) — Transposing the Conditioning Direction Is the Prosecutor's Fallacy
seeds.w: Assuming P(A|B) ≈ P(B|A) — the prosecutor's fallacy. The ratio equals P(B)/P(A), which diverges when base rates differ substantially.
seeds.c: P(B|A) = P(A|B)P(B)/P(A) (Bayes). Low P(evidence|innocent) does NOT mean low P(innocent|evidence). Always account for the prior P(B).
seeds.s: claiming a rare event is likely because the observed evidence under that hypothesis was improbable; using sensitivity as positive predictive value; reporting recall when precision is what the user cares about.

seeds.t: Central Limit Theorem Requires Finite Variance — Fails for Heavy-Tailed Distributions
seeds.w: Applying the CLT to any large sample, regardless of the underlying distribution — CLT requires finite variance; power-law tails with α ≤ 2 do not converge to a Gaussian.
seeds.c: CLT: i.i.d., finite variance σ² < ∞. Heavy tails P(X > x) ~ x^{−α}: if α ≤ 2, variance is infinite and the limit is an α-stable distribution, not Gaussian.
seeds.s: Gaussian-based confidence intervals that are too narrow for heavy-tailed data; sample mean estimates that don't stabilise even for large n; histogram of sample means showing non-Gaussian tails.

seeds.t: MAP Estimation with Gaussian Prior = L2 Regularisation; Laplace Prior = L1 (Lasso)
seeds.w: Treating MAP estimation as unrelated to regularised MLE — MAP with Gaussian prior = Ridge (L2 penalty); MAP with Laplace prior = Lasso (L1 penalty). The prior is the regulariser.
seeds.c: MAP = argmin −log p(data|θ) − log p(θ). Gaussian prior → L2 penalty; Laplace prior → L1 (sparsity). λ ∝ σ²/σ²_prior. Full Bayes integrates over θ; MAP is a point estimate.
seeds.s: implementing MAP without recognising the equivalent regularisation interpretation; choosing a regulariser type without understanding its implicit prior; reporting a MAP point estimate as if it were a full posterior.

seeds.t: Standard Bootstrap Assumes Exchangeability — Invalid for Time Series Without Block Resampling
seeds.w: Applying standard i.i.d. bootstrap to time series — destroys autocorrelation structure, producing overly narrow confidence intervals.
seeds.c: Use block bootstrap (block length l ~ n^{1/3}) or stationary bootstrap for time series. Standard bootstrap requires exchangeability, which autocorrelated data violates.
seeds.s: bootstrap confidence intervals for autocorrelated data that are substantially narrower than the correct ones; p-values from permutation tests on time series that reject too often.

seeds.t: The Hazard Rate h(t) = f(t)/S(t) Is a Rate, Not a Probability — It Can Exceed 1
seeds.w: Treating the continuous-time hazard rate h(t) as a probability bounded in [0,1] — h(t) = f(t)/S(t) is a rate with units 1/time, and can exceed 1.
seeds.c: h(t) = f(t)/S(t) is a rate (1/time). P(event in [t, t+dt) | alive at t) ≈ h(t)dt. S(t) = exp(−∫h). Only discrete-time hazards are bounded in [0,1].
seeds.s: clipping the output of a hazard model to [0,1] because it's 'a probability'; confusing the hazard with the density f(t); using a logistic output layer directly as a hazard without transforming to cumulative survival.

seeds.t: Swapping lim and ∫ Requires Dominated Convergence or Uniform Convergence
seeds.w: Swapping lim_{n→∞} and ∫ without justification — the exchange fails in general; a dominating integrable function or uniform convergence is required.
seeds.c: DCT: |fₙ| ≤ g ∈ L¹ and fₙ → f pointwise ⟹ lim ∫fₙ = ∫f. MCT: 0 ≤ fₙ ↑ f ⟹ same. Uniform convergence on a compact set also suffices.
seeds.s: gradient estimates that are biased due to a parameter-dependent domain of integration; a series that is summed term-by-term where the interchange with a limit is unjustified; NaN in a JAX gradient through an integral evaluated by quadrature.

seeds.t: Multivariate Chain Rule Requires Jacobian Matrix Multiplication, Not Scalar Product
seeds.w: Applying the scalar chain rule dz/dt = (dz/dx)(dx/dt) to vector functions — the correct form is Jacobian matrix multiplication: Jh = Jf · Jg (matrix product, not scalar product).
seeds.c: Chain rule: Jh(x) = Jf(g(x)) · Jg(x). For scalar loss: ∂L/∂x = Jg^ᵀ ∇_y L. Backpropagation computes VJPs (not full Jacobians) to avoid O(n²) cost.
seeds.s: shape errors in manual gradient implementations; wrong gradient for a layer that reshapes or permutes tensors; computing a full n×n Jacobian when only the VJP ∇L · J is needed.

seeds.t: Big-O Order Alone Does Not Determine Numerical Accuracy — The Remainder Constant Matters
seeds.w: Selecting a method purely by order p in O(hᵖ) without examining the prefactor C — error = Chᵖ; a smaller C can dominate a higher p over practical step sizes.
seeds.c: Lagrange remainder: error = hⁿ⁺¹ max|f⁽ⁿ⁺¹⁾|/(n+1)!. High order helps only when the function is smooth and h is in the asymptotic regime. Always verify on test cases.
seeds.s: claiming a high-order method is always better; not verifying whether the function being approximated is smooth enough for high-order accuracy; choosing h based on order without checking the actual error on a test case.

seeds.t: Laplace Transform Is for Causal/Unstable Signals; Fourier Requires Absolute Integrability
seeds.w: Applying the Fourier transform to causal or unstable signals — Fourier requires absolute integrability. Laplace (s = σ+iω, σ > σ_min) handles causal/unstable signals via the ROC.
seeds.c: Fourier: F{f}(ω) = L{f}(iω) only when ROC includes the imaginary axis (stable system). For causal signals or unstable systems, use Laplace. Frequency response = transfer function on jω axis.
seeds.s: attempting to Fourier-transform an unstable system's impulse response and getting non-convergent integrals; applying Fourier analysis to a causal system without checking stability; confusing frequency response (Fourier, for stable systems) with transfer function (Laplace, for an…

seeds.t: Only Convex Objective on a Convex Feasible Set Guarantees a Global Minimum
seeds.w: Assuming gradient-based optimisers find global minima for any objective — only convex objectives on convex domains guarantee that every local minimum is global.
seeds.c: Convex f, convex C: every local min = global min; ∇f = 0 is sufficient. Non-convex: local minima and saddle points may be suboptimal. Check convexity via ∇²f ⪰ 0.
seeds.s: reporting a local minimum as 'optimal'; stopping optimisation at a saddle point where gradient is zero but Hessian has negative eigenvalues; applying a convex solver to a non-convex problem.

seeds.t: In High-Dimensional Loss Landscapes Most Critical Points Are Saddle Points, Not Local Minima
seeds.w: Attributing training stalls to local minima — in high-dimensional networks, most ∇f = 0 points are saddle points with descent directions; local minima are rare and tend to have similar loss to the global minimum.
seeds.c: Most critical points in high-dim landscapes are saddles (∇²f has negative eigenvalues). Use gradient noise (SGD), momentum, or saddle-free Newton to escape. Confirm type via min eigenvalue of Hessian.
seeds.s: training stalling at high loss and assuming convergence to a poor local minimum; not recognising that gradient clipping or noise can help escape saddle regions.

seeds.t: Newton's Method Is Quadratically Convergent Only Near a Non-Degenerate Root — Can Diverge Globally
seeds.w: Expecting Newton's method to converge quickly from any starting point — quadratic convergence holds only in a neighbourhood of a non-degenerate root; globally it can diverge or cycle.
seeds.c: |xₙ₊₁ − x*| ≤ C|xₙ − x*|² only near x* with f'(x*) ≠ 0. Use damped Newton (line search) or trust-region for global convergence; L-BFGS for large-scale superlinear convergence.
seeds.s: Newton's method diverging from a good-looking initial guess; iteration cycling because f'(xₙ) ≈ 0; using Newton without a line search and getting step sizes >> 1.

seeds.t: L1 Regularisation Produces Sparsity via Non-Differentiability at Zero; L2 Does Not
seeds.w: Expecting L2 regularisation to zero out coefficients — L2 shrinks toward zero but never reaches it. L1 produces exact zeros via the soft-threshold operator and the sub-gradient at 0.
seeds.c: L2: gradient = 2λθ, shrinks exponentially but never zeros out. L1: soft-threshold S(θ, λ) = sign(θ)·max(|θ|−λ, 0) produces exact sparsity. Use proximal gradient or coord. descent for L1.
seeds.s: using L2 and wondering why no coefficients are exactly zero; applying gradient descent for L1 instead of proximal gradient or coordinate descent; ignoring that L1's subgradient requires proximal operators, not standard gradients.

seeds.t: Softmax Overflows Without Subtracting the Max — Use the Shift-Invariant Formulation
seeds.w: Computing exp(xᵢ)/Σ exp(xⱼ) directly — overflows float32 for xᵢ > 88 and float64 for xᵢ > 709, producing NaN even when the softmax is mathematically near a one-hot.
seeds.c: z = x − max(x); softmax = exp(z)/Σexp(z). Log-softmax = z − log(Σexp(z)). Shift-invariance guarantees same result. Framework softmax applies this; hand-rolled implementations often miss it.
seeds.s: NaN cross-entropy loss on the first training step with large random initialisations; inf in attention weights from large dot products before scaling.

seeds.t: Dot-Product Attention Without √dₖ Scaling Saturates the Softmax — Gradients Vanish
seeds.w: Omitting the √dₖ divisor in softmax(QKᵀ) — dot-product variance grows as dₖ, saturating softmax into near-one-hot distributions with near-zero gradients.
seeds.c: Attention = softmax(QKᵀ / √dₖ)V. Scaling removes the O(√dₖ) growth in dot-product variance, keeping softmax gradients non-negligible throughout training.
seeds.s: attention entropy collapsing to near zero after the first training step; gradient norms through the attention layer dropping to near zero; model failing to learn to attend to multiple positions.

seeds.t: Cross-Entropy H(p,q) = H(p) + KL(p‖q) — Minimising CE Minimises KL When p Is Fixed
seeds.w: Treating cross-entropy and KL divergence as equivalent — H(p,q) = H(p) + KL(p‖q). Minimising CE = minimising KL only when p (the true distribution) is fixed.
seeds.c: H(p,q) = H(p) + KL(p‖q). Fixed p (supervised): argmin CE = argmin KL(p‖q). Variable p: they differ. Forward KL is mean-seeking; reverse KL (used in VI) is mode-seeking.
seeds.s: conflating cross-entropy minimisation with KL minimisation when p is not fixed (e.g., in distribution matching); using CE loss for regression tasks where the distribution over targets is not categorical; assuming symmetric cross-entropy.

seeds.t: Batch Normalisation Uses Batch Statistics at Training but Running Statistics at Inference
seeds.w: Using batch statistics at inference, or running statistics at training — batch norm uses batch μ/σ² during training (and updates running estimates) but uses fixed running μ/σ² at inference.
seeds.c: Train: normalise with mini-batch μ_B, σ²_B; update running stats. Inference: normalise with frozen running μ_run, σ²_run. Call model.eval() in PyTorch; use small-batch-safe alternatives (LayerNorm, GroupNorm) for batch size < 8.
seeds.s: model accuracy drops significantly when switching from training to inference mode; accuracy differs between a single-sample inference and a batch inference; fine-tuned model performs worse than expected on the target domain.

seeds.t: Cramér-Rao Bound: Var(θ̂) ≥ 1/I(θ) — Fisher Information Quantifies How Sharply Data Constrains a Parameter
seeds.w: Ignoring the Cramér-Rao bound when evaluating estimator efficiency — Var(θ̂) ≥ 1/I(θ) for any unbiased estimator; without this lower bound you cannot know if your estimator is improvable.
seeds.c: I(θ) = E[(∂ log p/∂θ)²]. CR bound: Var(θ̂) ≥ 1/I(θ). MLE is asymptotically efficient (achieves the bound). Natural gradient descent uses I(θ) as the metric tensor.
seeds.s: reporting estimator variance without checking whether it is near the CR bound (i.e., whether there is room to improve); using standard gradient descent when natural gradient would converge faster; not recognising that some parameterisations have higher Fisher information than ot…

seeds.t: The Graph Laplacian's Second-Smallest Eigenvalue λ₂ Measures Graph Connectivity (Fiedler Value)
seeds.w: Treating connectivity as binary (connected vs not) and ignoring the Fiedler value λ₂ of the graph Laplacian, which quantifies the bottleneck strength and governs spectral clustering and random-walk mixing.
seeds.c: L = D − A. Zero eigenvalues count components. λ₂ (Fiedler) measures algebraic connectivity. Spectral clustering uses the Fiedler vector. Random-walk mixing time ∝ 1/λ₂.
seeds.s: checking only whether a graph is connected without measuring connectivity strength; using the adjacency matrix spectrum for spectral clustering (gives different cluster structure); confusing the graph Laplacian with the continuous Laplacian operator ∇².

seeds.t: Category Morphisms Need Not Be Functions and Objects Need Not Be Sets
seeds.w: Assuming category objects are sets and morphisms are functions — Set is one category; in a poset category morphisms encode ordering, in a monoid-as-category there is one object and morphisms are monoid elements.
seeds.c: A category is objects + Hom-sets + associative composition + identities. No elements or functions required. Posets, monoids, groups, topological spaces all form categories in multiple ways.
seeds.s: trying to 'look inside' an object to check a categorical claim; assuming a functor must send elements of a set to elements of another set; believing the only categories worth studying are concrete (where objects have underlying sets).

seeds.t: A Functor Must Preserve Both Composition and Identity — Either Alone Is Not Enough
seeds.w: Checking only composition preservation F(g∘f) = F(g)∘F(f) and omitting identity preservation F(id_A) = id_{F(A)} — both axioms are required; a map satisfying one but not the other is not a functor.
seeds.c: Functor = object map + morphism map satisfying F(id_A) = id_{F(A)} AND F(g∘f) = F(g)∘F(f). In Haskell: fmap id = id (identity law) and fmap (f.g) = fmap f . fmap g (composition law).
seeds.s: a Haskell fmap implementation that satisfies fmap (f.g) but not fmap id = id (e.g., fmap on a broken newtype wrapper); a functor implementation in Scala/Cats that violates the identity law for a particular ADT case.

seeds.t: Natural Transformations Require Every Naturality Square to Commute — Components Alone Are Not Enough
seeds.w: Collecting morphisms η_A: F(A) → G(A) for each object A without checking that η_B ∘ F(f) = G(f) ∘ η_A for every morphism f: A → B — components alone do not constitute a natural transformation.
seeds.c: η: F ⇒ G is natural iff for every f: A → B the square η_B ∘ F(f) = G(f) ∘ η_A commutes. Naturality = coherence with the action of morphisms. Parametric polymorphism in typed FP guarantees naturality (free theorem).
seeds.s: defining a polymorphic function in Haskell/Scala that has the right type at each type A but uses type-case (typecase / runtime type checks) — such functions are not natural (they are not parametrically polymorphic).

seeds.t: Yoneda Lemma: An Object Is Completely Determined by the Morphisms Into (or Out of) It
seeds.w: Describing objects by internal structure rather than by the morphisms into/out of them — Yoneda proves objects are fully determined by their hom-functor: A ≅ B iff Hom(A,−) ≅ Hom(B,−).
seeds.c: Yoneda: Nat(Hom(A,−), F) ≅ F(A), natural in A and F. The Yoneda embedding y: C → [C^op, Set] is fully faithful. Universal properties are representability: A×B represents Hom(−,A)×Hom(−,B).
seeds.s: defining objects concretely when a universal property would give a cleaner, more portable definition; missing that two functors are naturally isomorphic because their representing objects are isomorphic.

seeds.t: Adjoint Functors Are Defined by Unit and Counit Satisfying Triangle Identities — Not Just a Hom-Set Bijection
seeds.w: Defining L ⊣ R only as a hom-set bijection Hom(LA,B) ≅ Hom(A,RB) without the unit η: Id → RL and counit ε: LR → Id satisfying triangle identities — the unit/counit form is needed to derive the monad and verify functoriality.
seeds.c: L ⊣ R iff ∃ η: Id_C → RL, ε: LR → Id_D with triangle identities (εL)(Lη) = id_L and (Rε)(ηR) = id_R. Right adjoints preserve limits; left adjoints preserve colimits. Every adjunction generates a monad R∘L.
seeds.s: proving an adjunction by constructing the hom-set bijection but not identifying the unit and counit (which are needed for the monad structure); confusing left and right adjoint (free is left, forgetful is right — not the other way around).

seeds.t: A Monad Is a Monoid in the Category of Endofunctors — Not a 'Programmable Semicolon' or 'Burrito'
seeds.w: Defining monads only as containers or programming abstractions — a monad is a monoid (T, η: Id→T, μ: T²→T) in the monoidal category of endofunctors, with μ associative and η a two-sided unit.
seeds.c: Monad = (T, η, μ) with μ∘Tμ = μ∘μT and μ∘ηT = μ∘Tη = id. Kleisli composition is bind. Every adjunction L⊣R gives monad T=RL. Laws: left identity, right identity, associativity of >>=.
seeds.s: implementing bind (>>=) correctly but violating the monad laws (left identity, right identity, associativity); choosing a concrete monad when a free monad (purely syntactic) or a monad transformer stack would compose better.

seeds.t: Limits Are Terminal Cones and Colimits Are Initial Cocones — Not Just Tuple or Choice Constructions
seeds.w: Identifying a limit by its projection morphisms alone without the universal property (unique factorisation) — any object can have projection-like maps; only the limit has the terminal cone property.
seeds.c: Limit = terminal cone: ∀ cone (X, x_j) ∃! u: X → L with λ_j ∘ u = x_j. Products, equalisers, pullbacks are all limits over different diagram shapes J. Right adjoints preserve limits.
seeds.s: proving a construction is a limit by exhibiting projection maps without verifying the universal property (uniqueness of factorisation); forgetting that pullbacks, equalisers, and products are all limits — they can all be computed from each other given enough base limits.

seeds.t: Universal Properties Define Objects Up to Unique Isomorphism — Not Up to Equality
seeds.w: Expecting 'the' object satisfying a universal property to be canonical or unique-up-to-equality — universal properties give uniqueness only up to unique isomorphism; multiple non-equal objects can all satisfy the same universal property.
seeds.c: If P and P' both satisfy the same universal property, ∃! iso φ: P → P' respecting the structure. 'The product' means 'any representative'; uniqueness is of the isomorphism, not the object.
seeds.s: caching an object by identity rather than isomorphism class in a category-theoretic algorithm; failing to prove a functor respects a universal property by checking equality instead of the unique isomorphism.

seeds.t: Contravariant Functors Reverse Morphism Direction — They Are Covariant Functors from C^op
seeds.w: Treating a contravariant functor as a special definition separate from covariant functors — a contravariant functor C → D is a covariant functor C^op → D; arrow reversal is handled by the opposite category.
seeds.c: C^op reverses all morphisms. Contravariant F: C → D = covariant F: C^op → D, with F(f: A→B) = F(f): F(B)→F(A). Haskell: contramap :: (a→b) → f b → f a.
seeds.s: implementing contramap with the wrong arrow direction; confusing the covariant and contravariant hom-functors when applying Yoneda; forgetting that in a presheaf category the input category is C^op (arrows already reversed).

seeds.t: In a Cartesian Closed Category Currying Is the Natural Isomorphism Hom(A×B, C) ≅ Hom(A, C^B)
seeds.w: Treating currying as a mere programming convenience — in a CCC it is the natural bijection Hom(A×B,C) ≅ Hom(A, C^B) witnessing the universal property of the exponential object C^B.
seeds.c: CCC: finite products + exponentials C^B with Hom(A×B,C) ≅ Hom(A,C^B) natural in A,C. Curry = this iso. Curry-Howard-Lambek: types = objects, function types = exponentials, programs = morphisms.
seeds.s: manually threading function arguments instead of using curried style in a language where the type theory is CCC; confusion about why curry :: ((a,b) → c) → a → b → c is an isomorphism in Haskell but not in an impure language with side effects (where the CCC axioms fail).

seeds.t: Free Constructions Are Left Adjoints to Forgetful Functors — Free ≠ Initial Object
seeds.w: Confusing the free object F(X) (left adjoint to U: C→Set) with the initial object of C — they coincide only when X = ∅; in general the free object depends on the generator set X.
seeds.c: Free F(X) ⊣ Forgetful U: Hom_C(F(X), A) ≅ Hom_Set(X, U(A)). Map out of F(X) determined by images of generators in X. Free monoid = lists; free vector space = formal sums; free monad = syntax trees.
seeds.s: implementing a free monad by hand rather than deriving it from the adjunction; confusing the free algebra (left adjoint) with the terminal/initial algebra; not recognising that List in Haskell is the free monoid monad.

seeds.t: A Pullback Is a Limit Over a Cospan — Not a Product, Even Though It Has Two Projections
seeds.w: Treating the pullback A ×_C B as the same as the product A × B — the pullback adds the constraint f(a) = g(b) (compatibility over C); without it you get the product (the degenerate case C = 1).
seeds.c: Pullback = limit of cospan A →f C ←g B: object P with p₁: P→A, p₂: P→B, f∘p₁ = g∘p₂, universal. In Set: {(a,b) | f(a)=g(b)}. Reduces to product when C = 1. Dual = pushout.
seeds.s: computing A ×_C B as A × B in Set when the compatibility condition f(a) = g(b) is needed; implementing a database join as a Cartesian product followed by a filter (correct but computing A×B explicitly is O(|A||B|) rather than O(|A|+|B|) for indexed pullback).

seeds.t: Monoidal Categories Have Associativity and Unit Isomorphisms — Strictness Is the Exception, Not the Rule
seeds.w: Assuming ⊗ is strictly associative and unital on the nose — monoidal categories have natural isomorphisms α, λ, ρ (not equalities) satisfying pentagon and triangle equations. Strictness requires explicit proof.
seeds.c: Monoidal: (C,⊗,I,α,λ,ρ) with α: (A⊗B)⊗C ≅ A⊗(B⊗C), λ: I⊗A ≅ A, ρ: A⊗I ≅ A satisfying pentagon + triangle. Coherence: all canonical diagrams commute. Every monoidal category ≃ strict one.
seeds.s: forgetting to apply the associator when working with tensor products in code (e.g., applying a trilinear map to ((a⊗b)⊗c) when the map expects (a⊗(b⊗c))); assuming commutativity of ⊗ when only associativity (and possibly braiding) is given.

seeds.t: Left and Right Kan Extensions Are Different — The Left Is a Colimit, the Right Is a Limit
seeds.w: Confusing left Kan extension (Lan_K F, a colimit construction) with right Kan extension (Ran_K F, a limit construction) — they are adjoints on opposite sides of K* and compute different universal approximations.
seeds.c: Lan_K F ⊣ K* ⊣ Ran_K F. (Lan_K F)(d) = colim_{K↓d} F (left=colimit). (Ran_K F)(d) = lim_{d↓K} F (right=limit). Codensity monad = Ran_F F improves free monad O(n²) → O(n) in Haskell.
seeds.s: computing Lan when Ran is needed (or vice versa); attempting a pointwise Kan extension in a non-locally-small category where the required (co)limits don't exist; missing that the density comonad of the inclusion of finite sets gives the ultrafilter monad.

seeds.t: In the Functor Category [C, D] Objects Are Functors and Morphisms Are Natural Transformations
seeds.w: Treating functors and natural transformations as ad hoc constructions — they form the functor category [C,D] where objects=functors, morphisms=natural transformations, satisfying all categorical axioms.
seeds.c: [C,D]: objects = functors C→D, morphisms = natural transformations, composition = vertical composition. Yoneda embedding y: C^op → [C,Set]. Sheaves, group representations, semantics all live in functor categories.
seeds.s: working with functors and natural transformations procedurally without recognising that they themselves form a category, and therefore that categorical theorems apply to them at the meta-level.

seeds.t: Embed Documents for Read-Heavy, Reference for Write-Heavy
seeds.w: Reflexively referencing every related document like a relational database, causing $lookup joins on every read — MongoDB performs best when related data is read together and stored together.
seeds.c: Embed sub-documents when data is always read with the parent (order line items in an order), always updated together, and the array won't grow unboundedly. Use references (ObjectId) when the related data is large, updated independently, or shared across many parents.
seeds.s: $lookup on every query, defeating the purpose of a document database.

seeds.t: Avoid Unbounded Arrays in Documents
seeds.w: Storing an ever-growing list (all user activity events, all comments) inside a single document as an array — MongoDB documents have a 16 MB size limit, and large arrays degrade write performance as the document must be rewritten.
seeds.c: Cap arrays at a reasonable size using $slice on updates, or move high-cardinality data to a separate collection with a reference. Use the bucket pattern for time-series data: group events into fixed-time buckets.
seeds.s: document growth over time until hitting the 16 MB limit, or write latency increasing as documents grow.

seeds.t: Create Indexes on Every Queried Field
seeds.w: Running queries without indexes on large collections — MongoDB performs a full collection scan (COLLSCAN) examining every document.
seeds.c: Create indexes with db.collection.createIndex({field: 1}) for equality queries, compound indexes for multi-field queries in ESR order (Equality, Sort, Range), and text indexes for full-text search. Use explain('executionStats') to confirm IXSCAN. Set background: true for index b…
seeds.s: queries fast in development with small data, extremely slow in production with millions of documents.

seeds.t: Put $match and $limit Early in Aggregation Pipelines
seeds.w: Placing $match, $limit, or $sort after $lookup or $unwind stages — the pipeline processes every document before filtering, compounding the cost of expensive stages.
seeds.c: Place $match as early as possible to reduce document count before expensive operations; put $limit before $sort when fetching a small result set; place $project to reduce field count before $lookup. MongoDB's query planner optimises some cases automatically, but explicit orderin…
seeds.s: aggregation pipeline timing out or using excessive memory because filtering happens after expansion.

seeds.t: Think Carefully Before Overriding the Default _id
seeds.w: Using a custom _id (like a user-provided string or UUID) without considering write patterns — non-ObjectId _id values can cause hot spots in sharded clusters if they are monotonically increasing, and lose the embedded timestamp of ObjectId.
seeds.c: Keep the default ObjectId _id in most cases; it is time-sortable, shard-key friendly, and unique across replica sets. If you need a human-readable ID, store it as a separate indexed field. When using custom _ids in sharded collections, ensure they have good cardinality.
seeds.s: shard imbalance or hot-spot writes with sequential custom _id values.

seeds.t: Use Multi-Document Transactions for Atomic Cross-Collection Writes
seeds.w: Writing to two collections sequentially without a transaction and assuming both will succeed — if the second write fails, data is inconsistent with no automatic rollback.
seeds.c: Use multi-document ACID transactions (MongoDB 4.0+ replica sets, 4.2+ sharded): session = client.start_session(); with session.start_transaction(): followed by operations passing session=session. Note: transactions have a 60-second timeout and higher write latency — prefer singl…
seeds.s: partial writes leaving orders without corresponding inventory decrements.

seeds.t: You Cannot Mix Include and Exclude Projections
seeds.w: Writing a projection like {name: 1, password: 0} mixing 1s and 0s — MongoDB raises an error for mixed projections (except the _id field which can always be excluded).
seeds.c: Either include fields explicitly ({name: 1, email: 1, _id: 0}) or exclude fields ({password: 0, internalNotes: 0}). Use exclusion projections to strip sensitive fields; use inclusion projections to fetch only what's needed.
seeds.s: 'Projection cannot have a mix of inclusion and exclusion' error at query time.

seeds.t: Always Use Update Operators, Never Replace Accidentally
seeds.w: Calling collection.update_one({_id: id}, document) without an update operator like $set — this replaces the entire document with the provided object, deleting all other fields.
seeds.c: Always use update operators: $set to update specific fields, $inc to increment, $push to append to arrays, $pull to remove from arrays. Check your driver's method name: update_one with a filter and an update document, not replaceOne.
seeds.s: documents losing all fields except the ones in the update call.

seeds.t: Use Secondary Read Preference Only for Stale-OK Reads
seeds.w: Routing all reads to secondaries to reduce primary load without understanding replication lag — secondaries can be seconds or more behind the primary, returning stale data.
seeds.c: Use ReadPreference.SECONDARY or nearest only for analytics, reporting, or reads where eventual consistency is acceptable. Use ReadPreference.PRIMARY (default) for reads that must reflect recent writes. Use sessions with causal consistency if you need read-your-writes after switc…
seeds.s: users not seeing their own writes, or stale counts after an update.

seeds.t: Persist Change Stream Resume Tokens for Reliability
seeds.w: Opening a change stream without saving the resume token — if the consumer restarts, it misses all events that occurred during downtime.
seeds.c: Persist the _id (resume token) of the last successfully processed event to durable storage (another collection, Redis). On restart, open the change stream with resumeAfter: lastToken or startAfter: lastToken to replay from where processing stopped. Change stream tokens are valid…
seeds.s: events silently lost when the change stream consumer restarts.

seeds.t: Index High-Cardinality Fields First in Compound Indexes
seeds.w: Creating a compound index starting with a low-cardinality field like {status: 1, user_id: 1} when most queries filter on user_id — the index scan covers many documents with the same status before reaching the user_id filter.
seeds.c: For compound indexes used in equality queries, put higher-cardinality fields first unless the ESR rule requires otherwise. An index on {user_id: 1, status: 1} is more selective for user-specific queries. Use explain() to confirm the number of documents examined (nScanned) vs ret…
seeds.s: index exists but queries still examine many more documents than returned.

seeds.t: Create One MongoClient Per Process, Not Per Request
seeds.w: Instantiating a new MongoClient on every request — each client creates its own connection pool, exhausting available connections and adding latency.
seeds.c: Create a single MongoClient at application startup and reuse it across all requests; the client manages a connection pool internally. Pass the client or database reference via dependency injection. The default pool size is 100; tune with maxPoolSize for high-concurrency applicat…
seeds.s: 'Too many connections' errors under load, or high connection establishment latency per request.

seeds.t: Enforce Document Shape With JSON Schema Validation
seeds.w: Relying purely on application-level validation — a bug or direct database access can insert malformed documents that break downstream reads.
seeds.c: Add MongoDB JSON Schema validation to collections: db.createCollection('users', {validator: {$jsonSchema: {bsonType: 'object', required: ['email'], properties: {email: {bsonType: 'string'}}}}}) with validationLevel: 'strict'. This enforces shape at the database level as a safety…
seeds.s: malformed documents causing runtime errors in the application that are difficult to trace back to the write path.

seeds.t: Use an Indexed Filter Field for Upserts
seeds.w: Upserts with a filter on a non-indexed field — MongoDB must scan the collection to find a matching document, making the upsert O(n) even for a single-document operation.
seeds.c: Ensure the upsert filter uses indexed fields (typically _id or a unique indexed field). For idempotent data ingestion, use {$set: data} with a unique index as the natural key and upsert: true. Add a unique index on the natural key to prevent duplicate inserts under concurrent up…
seeds.s: upsert operations slow despite low document counts, or duplicate documents created under concurrent upserts.

seeds.t: Use Atlas Search for Production Full-Text Search
seeds.w: Using MongoDB text indexes for production full-text search — text indexes lack relevance ranking, language analysis, fuzzy matching, and faceted search.
seeds.c: For simple keyword matching, text indexes with $text are fine. For production search features (typeahead, facets, relevance scoring, synonyms), use Atlas Search (built on Lucene) or a dedicated search engine like Elasticsearch. Atlas Search indexes are defined declaratively and …
seeds.s: poor relevance, no fuzzy matching, or slow search with basic text indexes at scale.

seeds.t: Use $elemMatch for Multi-Condition Array Element Queries
seeds.w: Querying {scores: {$gt: 80, $lt: 90}} on an array field expecting to match elements where a single element is between 80 and 90 — MongoDB applies each condition to any element independently, matching documents where any element satisfies each condition separately.
seeds.c: Use $elemMatch: {scores: {$elemMatch: {$gt: 80, $lt: 90}}} to apply all conditions to a single array element.
seeds.s: query returning documents that appear not to match, because conditions are evaluated across different array elements.

seeds.t: Use explain('executionStats') to Diagnose Slow Queries
seeds.w: Guessing why a query is slow and adding random indexes without data — wasted index overhead and unresolved performance issues.
seeds.c: Run db.collection.find(query).explain('executionStats') and check: stage (COLLSCAN is bad, IXSCAN is good), nReturned vs totalDocsExamined (high ratio means poor selectivity), and executionTimeMillis. Use MongoDB Atlas's Query Profiler or set db.setProfilingLevel(1, {slowms: 100…
seeds.s: slow queries with no visibility into whether indexes are being used.

seeds.t: Use Pipeline Form of $lookup for Filtered Joins
seeds.w: Using the basic $lookup form and then $filtering the joined array in a later stage — fetches all matching foreign documents before filtering in memory.
seeds.c: Use the pipeline form of $lookup with a nested $match to push the filter into the join: {$lookup: {from: 'orders', let: {uid: '$_id'}, pipeline: [{$match: {$expr: {$eq: ['$userId', '$$uid']}, status: 'active'}}], as: 'orders'}}. This filters at the join stage and reduces data tr…
seeds.s: $lookup returning massive arrays that a subsequent $filter reduces to one or two elements.

seeds.t: Enable Retryable Writes for Network Error Resilience
seeds.w: Assuming a failed write means the write did not occur — a network timeout could happen after the server committed but before the client received the acknowledgement, causing a duplicate write on retry.
seeds.c: Enable retryWrites=true (default since MongoDB driver 4.0) in the connection string — the driver transparently retries once-failed writes with a server-side idempotency token, preventing duplicates. Ensure you are using a replica set or sharded cluster (standalone servers don't …
seeds.s: duplicate documents after network glitches, or write operations not retried after transient errors.

seeds.t: Use Time Series Collections for Metrics and Events
seeds.w: Storing time-series data (sensor readings, metrics, events) in a regular collection — each document stores one data point, causing massive collection size and poor query performance over time ranges.
seeds.c: Use MongoDB time series collections (5.0+): db.createCollection('metrics', {timeseries: {timeField: 'timestamp', metaField: 'sensorId', granularity: 'seconds'}}). Time series collections compress data automatically and optimise range queries on the time field. Use the bucket pat…
seeds.s: collection with billions of single-metric documents consuming excessive storage and slow on time-range queries.

seeds.t: Enharmonically Equivalent Pitches (C# / Db) Are the Same Frequency but Different Harmonic Functions
seeds.w: Treating C# and Db as interchangeable in harmonic contexts because they are the same MIDI note number — they differ in function, voice-leading obligation, and correct enharmonic spelling within a key.
seeds.c: Enharmonic equivalence = same Hz; functional equivalence = no. C# pulls upward; Db pulls downward. Store pitches as (letter, accidental, octave), not semitone-only, to preserve harmonic meaning.
seeds.s: music notation software displaying incorrect accidentals because pitches are stored only as semitone integers; a chord labelling algorithm calling the same chord both 'F#7' and 'Gb7' depending on input order; voice-leading analysis that misses an augmented sixth resolution becau…

seeds.t: Two Intervals Can Span the Same Semitones but Have Different Quality and Function
seeds.w: Identifying intervals by semitone count alone — augmented fourth and diminished fifth are both 6 semitones but are different intervals with different staff positions and opposite voice-leading resolutions.
seeds.c: Interval = number (letter-span) + quality (compare semitones to perfect/major standard). Store as (staff-steps, semitones). Aug4 resolves outward; dim5 resolves inward — opposite behaviour despite equal pitch.
seeds.s: a music analysis tool labelling C–F# and C–Gb identically as 'tritone' without distinguishing direction of resolution; a chord-spelling algorithm producing 'C–E–Gb' for a C major triad (should be C–E–G, the diminished fifth Gb is wrong even if it sounds the same).

seeds.t: Intervals Invert to Their Complement to 9 — Not to 12 or to 8
seeds.w: Inverting intervals by subtracting from 8 or from 12 — interval inversion is defined by interval number, and an interval plus its inversion always sums to 9 (not 8 or 12). Quality also inverts: major ↔ minor, Aug ↔ dim.
seeds.c: Inversion rule: number₁ + number₂ = 9. Quality: major↔minor, perfect↔perfect, aug↔dim. Major 3rd → minor 6th. Perfect 4th → perfect 5th. Applies to interval numbers (staff steps), not semitones.
seeds.s: a chord-voicing algorithm inverting a major third to a major sixth instead of a minor sixth; music analysis software reporting an inverted minor seventh as a major second instead of an augmented second; interval tables that subtract from 8 and produce off-by-one errors.

seeds.t: Transposing Instruments Sound a Different Pitch Than Written — Always Specify Concert vs Written Pitch
seeds.w: Treating written pitch and sounding pitch as identical for all instruments — transposing instruments (Bb clarinet, French horn, Eb alto sax) sound a fixed interval away from written pitch; always specify which domain.
seeds.c: Bb instruments sound M2 below written. Eb instruments sound M6 below written. F instruments (horn) sound P5 below written. Concert pitch = actual sound; written pitch = what the performer reads. Convert explicitly.
seeds.s: an algorithmic composition engine producing a Bb clarinet part in concert pitch that sounds a major second too high when the performer reads it; a transposing instrument MIDI track that sounds correct in a DAW (where MIDI is always concert) but is notated a step off in the music…

seeds.t: Modes Are Not Just 'The Major Scale Starting on a Different Note' — Each Has Its Own Tonal Center
seeds.w: Defining Dorian as 'start on the 2nd degree of the major scale' — each mode has its own tonic and characteristic interval pattern; the parent-scale framing obscures the mode's tonal identity.
seeds.c: Each mode is a distinct W/H pattern from its own root. Dorian: W-H-W-W-W-H-W (characteristic ♭3+♭7 but natural 6). Generate modes as fixed interval arrays, not offsets into a parent scale.
seeds.s: a modal melody generator that gravitates toward the relative major instead of the modal tonic; a scale library that stores all modes as offsets of C major, giving wrong accidentals when the tonic is not C.

seeds.t: Relative Keys Share a Key Signature; Parallel Keys Share a Tonic — They Are Different Relationships
seeds.w: Treating relative minor (same key signature, different tonic: C major / A minor) and parallel minor (same tonic, different key signature: C major / C minor) as the same relationship.
seeds.c: Relative: same key sig, different tonic (C major ↔ A minor). Parallel: same tonic, different key sig (C major ↔ C minor, +3 flats). Borrowed chords come from the parallel key, not the relative.
seeds.s: a key-modulation algorithm that confuses a borrowed iv chord (parallel minor) with a modulation to the relative minor; a transposition tool that moves to the relative minor instead of the parallel minor when asked to switch major↔minor.

seeds.t: Melodic Minor Ascends with Raised 6th and 7th, Descends as Natural Minor — Both Forms Are Correct
seeds.w: Using a single fixed array for melodic minor — melodic minor has different ascending (♮6, ♮7) and descending (♭6, ♭7 = natural minor) forms. Harmonic minor and melodic minor serve different purposes.
seeds.c: Natural minor: no alterations. Harmonic minor: raised 7th (leading tone, augmented 2nd between ♭6–♮7). Melodic minor ascending: raised 6th+7th. Melodic minor descending: same as natural minor.
seeds.s: a melody harmonization tool that builds scale-degree chords from harmonic minor for all purposes; a scale library that returns the same notes for melodic minor regardless of ascending/descending flag; an interval-analysis tool that reports an augmented second as an error in a ha…

seeds.t: Chord Inversion Is Defined by the Bass Note; Voicing Is the Arrangement of Upper Voices — They Are Orthogonal
seeds.w: Treating chord inversion and chord voicing as the same thing — inversion is solely determined by the bass note (which chord tone is lowest); voicing is the upper-voice arrangement and is independent.
seeds.c: Inversion: root/3rd/5th/(7th) in bass = root/1st/2nd/(3rd) inversion. Voicing: spacing of upper notes (close/open/drop-2). Orthogonal: same inversion can have many voicings. Figured bass encodes inversion only.
seeds.s: a chord-recognition algorithm that reports a first-inversion chord as a different chord entirely because the upper voices are rearranged; a voicing generator that accidentally changes inversion when intended to change only the spread.

seeds.t: A Major Seventh Chord Has a Major Triad + Major 7th; A Dominant Seventh Has a Major Triad + Minor 7th
seeds.w: Treating dominant seventh (V7 = major triad + minor 7th, contains tritone) and major seventh (Imaj7 = major triad + major 7th, stable) as equivalent — they differ by one semitone but have opposite harmonic functions.
seeds.c: Dom7 (C7): major triad + minor 7th (Bb) — tritone between 3rd and 7th, strong resolution to I. Maj7 (Cmaj7): major triad + major 7th (B) — stable, no tritone. Off by one semitone; opposite functions.
seeds.s: a chord symbol parser that treats 'C7' and 'Cmaj7' as equivalent; a chord-generation algorithm that builds a dominant seventh on every scale degree rather than only on scale degree 5.

seeds.t: An Add9 Chord Contains No 7th; A 9th Chord Includes the 7th — They Are Different Sonorities
seeds.w: Using 'add9' and '9th chord' interchangeably — Cadd9 (C–E–G–D) has no 7th; C9 (C–E–G–Bb–D) includes the 7th. The 7th is the distinguishing factor; its presence or absence changes the harmonic function.
seeds.c: Add9: triad + 9th, no 7th (Cadd9 = C–E–G–D). 9th chord: 7th + 9th (C9 = C–E–G–Bb–D). Extended chords include all thirds to the named degree. Check for the 7th before labelling a chord '9th'.
seeds.s: a chord parser reporting a Cadd9 (C–E–G–D) as 'C9' because it sees a ninth present; a chord-voicing generator building C9 without a 7th and mislabelling it.

seeds.t: A Secondary Dominant Temporarily Tonicizes a Scale Degree — It Is Not a Borrowed Chord
seeds.w: Confusing secondary dominants (V/V, V/IV — temporary tonicization via fifth-motion resolution) with borrowed chords (parallel-mode color without tonicization). Different mechanisms, different Roman numeral labels.
seeds.c: Secondary dominant: dominant 7th a P5 above a diatonic chord, resolves by fifth (V/V = D7 → G in C major). Borrowed chord: from parallel key, no required fifth-resolution (iv, ♭VII in C major). Diagnostic: does it resolve by fifth?
seeds.s: a harmonic analysis algorithm labelling all chromatic chords as 'borrowed' regardless of their resolution pattern; missing a V/V because the D7 chord resolves to G before the final cadence.

seeds.t: The Tritone Substitution Works Because V7 and ♭II7 Share the Same Tritone — Enharmonically Respelled
seeds.w: Applying 'replace V7 with ♭II7' mechanically without understanding that the substitution works because both chords share the same tritone (enharmonically spelled) between their 3rds and 7ths.
seeds.c: G7 tritone: B–F (3rd and 7th). Db7 tritone: F–Cb = F–B (same pitches). Tritone sub valid only for dominant 7th chords. Bass moves by semitone (Db→C) instead of 4th (G→C) — smoother voice-leading.
seeds.s: a chord substitution algorithm applying tritone substitution to major seventh chords; generating the correct substitute root (♭II) but with the wrong chord quality (not dominant seventh); missing the enharmonic respelling needed to correctly voice-lead the substituted chord.

seeds.t: The Leading Tone Resolves Up by Half Step; The Chordal Seventh Resolves Down by Step
seeds.w: Freely doubling or resolving the leading tone and chordal 7th without regard for their directional obligations — the leading tone (7̂) must resolve up by half step; the chordal 7th (4̂ in V7) must resolve down by step.
seeds.c: V7→I: leading tone (B, 3rd of G7) → up a half step to C (1̂). Chordal 7th (F) → down a step to E (3̂ of I). Never double the leading tone. 7th always resolves down. These opposing pulls define tonal resolution.
seeds.s: an auto-harmonizer that doubles the leading tone and produces parallel octaves on V7→I; a voice-leading engine that resolves the chordal 7th upward to the octave instead of downward to the 3rd of the tonic chord.

seeds.t: Parallel Perfect Fifths and Octaves Blur Voice Independence — They Are Not Just a Style Rule
seeds.w: Treating parallel P5/P8 avoidance as an optional style preference — parallel perfect consonances cause voice fusion, eliminating the independence that defines multi-voice counterpoint. Check all voice-pair combinations.
seeds.c: Parallel P5 and P8 fuse voices perceptually. Check all 6 voice pairs in SATB. Parallel thirds and sixths are fine. Preferred alternatives: contrary motion (opposite direction) or oblique motion (one voice held).
seeds.s: a chorale-harmonization algorithm that produces parallel fifths between tenor and bass on every chord change; a voice-leading engine that checks only adjacent voices and misses parallel octaves between soprano and alto.

seeds.t: 6/8 Is Compound Duple (2 Beats of 3) — Not Six Beats Per Measure
seeds.w: Reading 6/8 as 6 beats per measure or treating it as equivalent to 3/4 — 6/8 is compound duple: 2 beats each subdivided into 3 eighth notes; the beat unit is a dotted quarter. 3/4 has 3 beats each dividing into 2.
seeds.c: 6/8 = compound duple: 2 dotted-quarter beats, each divided into 3 eighth notes. Accent pattern: 1–2–3–4–5–6 with primary accents on 1 and 4. 3/4 accents 1, 3, 5. Use dotted-quarter grid for 6/8 in DAW.
seeds.s: a tempo-detection algorithm reporting 6/8 as 6 BPM instead of 2 (compound duple); a rhythm quantizer snapping 6/8 melodies to a quarter-note grid, obscuring the 3-subdivision feel.

seeds.t: Syncopation Shifts Accents Within a Meter; Hemiola Temporarily Superimposes a Different Grouping
seeds.w: Using 'syncopation' for any off-beat accent including hemiola — syncopation displaces accents within the established meter; hemiola replaces the metric grouping itself (3-against-2 across bars), creating a temporary meter change.
seeds.c: Syncopation: accent on weak beat within the meter (listener hears the displacement). Hemiola: metric reinterpretation across 2–3 bars (3/4 bars feel like 2/4 briefly). Hemiola spans multiple bars; syncopation operates within a bar.
seeds.s: a rhythm analysis tool labelling Baroque cadential hemiola as 'syncopation'; a generative rhythm engine producing syncopation patterns when hemiola was specified.

seeds.t: Swing Eighth Notes Are Not Exactly a 2:1 Triplet Ratio — The Ratio Varies with Tempo and Style
seeds.w: Implementing swing as an exact 2:1 (triplet) ratio — swing ratio varies from near-straight at fast tempos to well beyond 2:1 in slow blues. Use a continuous parameter, not a binary straight/triplet toggle.
seeds.c: Swing ratio (long:short) ≈ 1:1 at fast tempos, ~2:1 at medium, >2:1 at slow. Represent as a continuous percentage (50% = straight, 67% = triplet, scale freely). Genre and tempo both determine the target ratio.
seeds.s: a MIDI swing function that snaps all swing eighth notes to an exact triplet grid, sounding mechanical; a groove template that uses the same ratio at 80 BPM and 200 BPM, producing unplayable fast passages.

seeds.t: A Perfect Authentic Cadence Requires Root-Position V(7)→I with Soprano on Scale Degree 1
seeds.w: Labelling any V→I motion as a PAC — PAC requires: V or V7 in root position, I in root position, AND soprano on 1̂. Any deviation (inversion, soprano on 3̂ or 5̂, viiº substitution) produces an IAC.
seeds.c: PAC: V(7)/I both root position + soprano = 1̂. IAC: V→I with inversion or soprano ≠ 1̂. HC: ends on V. Plagal: IV→I. Deceptive: V→vi. Check three conditions for PAC: bass 5̂, bass 1̂, soprano 1̂.
seeds.s: a cadence detector labelling an IAC (soprano on 3̂) as a PAC; missing a half cadence because the algorithm only checks for V→I patterns.

seeds.t: Equal Temperament Makes Every Interval Except the Octave Slightly Out of Tune — By Design
seeds.w: Assuming equal temperament produces pure intervals — ET deliberately mistuned every interval except the octave (P5 is 2 cents flat, M3 is 14 cents sharp) to enable free modulation to all keys.
seeds.c: ET: 2^(n/12) per semitone. P5 = 700 cents (just = 702¢, 2¢ flat). M3 = 400¢ (just = 386¢, 14¢ sharp). Just intonation uses exact frequency ratios (5/4, 3/2) but produces wolf intervals in distant keys.
seeds.s: a synthesizer tuning algorithm that uses equal temperament ratios for a barbershop quartet or choral setting where just intonation is expected; a music theory tool reporting all perfect fifths as 'pure' when they are in ET.

seeds.t: Timbre Is Determined by the Relative Amplitudes of Harmonic Partials — Not by Waveform Shape Directly
seeds.w: Describing timbre as 'waveform shape' rather than as the distribution of harmonic partial amplitudes — waveform and spectrum are Fourier duals; for design purposes, partial amplitudes (frequency domain) are the actionable representation.
seeds.c: Timbre = relative amplitudes of harmonics (nf₀). Sawtooth: all harmonics 1/n. Square: odd harmonics 1/n. Triangle: odd harmonics 1/n². Inharmonic partials → metallic/noisy. Spectral envelope evolves over ADSR and shapes perceived timbre.
seeds.s: a synthesizer design that achieves a target timbre by adjusting waveform shape in the time domain without understanding the frequency-domain equivalent; a spectral analysis tool that reports 'inharmonic' for a string with slight stretching (which is physically expected and not a…

seeds.t: Phi (φ) Is Not a Musical Interval — Fibonacci Ratios Converge Toward φ but Never Reach It
seeds.w: Claiming φ ≈ 1.618 generates musical intervals or that the most consonant intervals equal φ — φ ≈ 833¢ matches no standard interval. Fibonacci ratios (8/5, 13/8) converge toward φ but are valued for their simplicity, not their proximity to φ.
seeds.c: φ ≈ 833¢, between m6 (800¢) and M6 (900¢), not equal to either. Fibonacci JI ratios (8/5, 13/8) are consonant because they are simple ratios, not because of φ. Bartók's Fibonacci usage is structural (form/rhythm), not intervallic.
seeds.s: a generative tuning system that uses phi as an interval ratio and produces a scale with no recognisable consonances; a music-theory app claiming the major sixth is 'the golden ratio interval' (it is ~49¢ away from φ).

seeds.t: Use utf8mb4 Not utf8 for Full Unicode Support
seeds.w: Creating tables or databases with CHARACTER SET utf8 — MySQL's utf8 is a broken 3-byte implementation that cannot store 4-byte characters (emoji, many CJK characters, mathematical symbols).
seeds.c: Always use CHARACTER SET utf8mb4 COLLATE utf8mb4_unicode_ci for full Unicode support. Set it at the database, table, column, and connection levels. Add character-set-server=utf8mb4 to my.cnf and use SET NAMES utf8mb4 on each connection.
seeds.s: emoji or certain Unicode characters silently truncated or causing 'Incorrect string value' errors.

seeds.t: Use EXPLAIN to Inspect Query Execution Plans
seeds.w: Adding indexes randomly without verifying they are used — unused indexes add write overhead with no read benefit.
seeds.c: Prefix any SELECT with EXPLAIN or EXPLAIN ANALYZE to see the execution plan: check type (ALL is a full scan; ref, eq_ref, const are good), key (which index was used), and rows (estimated rows examined). EXPLAIN FORMAT=JSON gives richer output. Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE (MySQL 8.0+) fo…
seeds.s: query slow despite having an index; EXPLAIN shows type=ALL meaning the index is ignored.

seeds.t: Always Use InnoDB, Never MyISAM
seeds.w: Creating tables with ENGINE=MyISAM for perceived performance — MyISAM has no transactions, no row-level locking, and no foreign key support, causing data corruption on crashes.
seeds.c: Use ENGINE=InnoDB (the default since MySQL 5.5). InnoDB provides ACID transactions, row-level locking, crash recovery, and foreign key constraints. Set default-storage-engine=InnoDB in my.cnf to prevent accidental MyISAM creation.
seeds.s: data inconsistency after a crash, table-level lock contention under concurrent writes.

seeds.t: DATETIME Stores What You Give; TIMESTAMP Converts to UTC
seeds.w: Using TIMESTAMP columns when your app runs across multiple timezones — TIMESTAMP stores in UTC and converts on read based on the session time_zone, so the same stored value returns different strings to clients in different zones.
seeds.c: Use DATETIME for values that must display the same regardless of time zone (event times, scheduled times); use TIMESTAMP for 'when this record was last modified' metadata where UTC is correct. Store your application's time zone explicitly if needed.
seeds.s: timestamps shifting by hours when the server's time zone is changed or different clients are in different zones.

seeds.t: SELECT Columns in GROUP BY Must Be Aggregated or Grouped
seeds.w: Selecting a non-aggregated column that is not in the GROUP BY clause — in MySQL's ONLY_FULL_GROUP_BY mode (default since 5.7), this raises an error; in older modes it returns an arbitrary value silently.
seeds.c: Include every selected column either in the GROUP BY clause or wrapped in an aggregate function (MAX, MIN, ANY_VALUE if non-determinism is acceptable). If you need a non-grouped column from the 'winning' row, use a subquery or window function.
seeds.s: 'Expression not in GROUP BY clause and contains nonaggregated column' error after a MySQL upgrade.

seeds.t: LIMIT Without ORDER BY Returns Non-Deterministic Rows
seeds.w: Using LIMIT to get a 'sample' or page of rows without an ORDER BY — MySQL may return any rows in any order; the result can change between executions on the same data.
seeds.c: Always pair LIMIT with ORDER BY on a unique column (typically the primary key) for deterministic pagination: ORDER BY id LIMIT 10 OFFSET 100. For keyset pagination, use WHERE id > last_id LIMIT 10 ORDER BY id to avoid the OFFSET performance cliff.
seeds.s: paginated results skipping or duplicating rows between pages.

seeds.t: LIKE Patterns Must Have a Prefix to Use an Index
seeds.w: Querying WHERE name LIKE '%smith' or WHERE name LIKE '%smith%' — a leading wildcard prevents index usage and forces a full table scan.
seeds.c: Only LIKE 'smith%' (trailing wildcard) can use a B-tree index. For contains/suffix search, use a FULLTEXT index with MATCH...AGAINST, or a dedicated search engine. For short lists, consider REVERSE(name) LIKE REVERSE('%smith') with an index on REVERSE(name) for suffix-only patte…
seeds.s: EXPLAIN showing type=ALL on a LIKE query despite having an index on the column.

seeds.t: Covering Indexes Serve Queries From the Index Alone
seeds.w: Creating single-column indexes and then fetching many non-indexed columns — MySQL must do a secondary lookup to the clustered index for each row (random IO).
seeds.c: Create a covering index that includes all columns needed by the query: CREATE INDEX idx_cover ON orders (user_id, status, created_at) covers SELECT status, created_at FROM orders WHERE user_id = ?. EXPLAIN shows 'Using index' in Extra when the covering index is used.
seeds.s: query fast for index scan but slow overall due to many random 'Using join buffer' or row lookups.

seeds.t: Understand REPEATABLE READ Phantom Row Behaviour
seeds.w: Assuming MySQL's default REPEATABLE READ completely prevents anomalies — phantom reads (new rows inserted by concurrent transactions) can still appear in certain scenarios, and SELECT...FOR UPDATE in REPEATABLE READ can still deadlock.
seeds.c: Use SELECT...FOR UPDATE for locking reads within a transaction. Use READ COMMITTED if you want to see committed changes from concurrent transactions immediately. Use SERIALIZABLE only when absolute correctness is required (high locking overhead).
seeds.s: unexpected deadlocks or phantom rows appearing inside a transaction.

seeds.t: Batch Inserts Into Single Multi-Row INSERT Statements
seeds.w: Executing one INSERT per row in a loop — each statement is a separate round trip and transaction commit, making bulk loads orders of magnitude slower than necessary.
seeds.c: Batch rows into multi-row INSERTs: INSERT INTO t (a, b) VALUES (1,2),(3,4),(5,6). Disable autocommit (START TRANSACTION) and commit in chunks of 1000–10000 rows. Use LOAD DATA INFILE for maximum speed. Disable indexes (ALTER TABLE ... DISABLE KEYS) before bulk load, re-enable af…
seeds.s: importing 100,000 rows taking minutes that should take seconds.

seeds.t: Enable the Slow Query Log to Find Performance Problems
seeds.w: Guessing which queries are slow without data — optimising the wrong queries wastes effort.
seeds.c: Enable with slow_query_log=1, slow_query_log_file=/var/log/mysql/slow.log, long_query_time=0.5 (seconds) in my.cnf or at runtime. Use log_queries_not_using_indexes=1 to catch full scans. Analyse with pt-query-digest (Percona) to aggregate and rank by total query time.
seeds.s: database occasionally slow with no visibility into which queries are responsible.

seeds.t: Re-Enable Foreign Key Checks After Data Loads
seeds.w: Disabling foreign key checks (SET FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS=0) for a data migration and forgetting to re-enable — subsequent inserts silently violate referential integrity with no error.
seeds.c: Wrap the disable/enable in a transaction or script that always re-enables: SET FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS=0; ... load ... ; SET FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS=1. Better yet, load data in referential order (parents before children) without disabling checks. Verify integrity with pt-table-checksum af…
seeds.s: orphaned child rows with no matching parent, application errors days later from broken joins.

seeds.t: ENUM Columns Are Costly to Alter
seeds.w: Using ENUM for any value that might need a new option later — adding a value to an ENUM requires an ALTER TABLE that rewrites the table in older MySQL versions.
seeds.c: Use a VARCHAR with a CHECK constraint (MySQL 8.0.16+) or a lookup table with a foreign key for values that may expand. Reserve ENUM only for truly static, closed sets (gender, currency_direction). Use pt-online-schema-change for zero-downtime ENUM alters on large tables.
seeds.s: ALTER TABLE taking hours on a large table to add one ENUM value, blocking all writes.

seeds.t: Use JSON Column Type With Generated Column Indexes
seeds.w: Storing JSON in a TEXT column without validation or indexing — no schema enforcement, and querying a specific JSON field requires full table scans.
seeds.c: Use the JSON column type (MySQL 5.7.8+) for automatic validation. Create virtual generated columns on frequently queried paths: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN status VARCHAR(50) AS (data->>'$.status') VIRTUAL, ADD INDEX (status). Use the -> and ->> operators for JSON path extraction.
seeds.s: WHERE JSON_EXTRACT(data, '$.status') = 'active' performing full table scans.

seeds.t: Size the Connection Pool to Match max_connections
seeds.w: Setting application connection pool max to 1000 per app instance when max_connections is 150 — each new connection beyond the limit gets 'Too many connections' and fails.
seeds.c: Set max_connections in MySQL based on available RAM (each connection uses ~1 MB). Total connections = pool_size × app_instances must stay under max_connections with headroom for admin connections. Use ProxySQL or PgBouncer for connection multiplexing at scale.
seeds.s: 'Too many connections' errors under load even though individual instances seem fine.

seeds.t: Type Mismatches in WHERE Clauses Prevent Index Use
seeds.w: Querying WHERE user_id = '42' when user_id is an INT — MySQL coerces the string to an integer, which can bypass index usage for the full column range and causes silent mismatch bugs with non-numeric strings.
seeds.c: Always match the value type to the column type in WHERE clauses. In application code, ensure integer IDs are passed as integers, not strings. Use CAST(val AS UNSIGNED) explicitly when coercion is required.
seeds.s: EXPLAIN showing type=ALL on an indexed integer column because the query value is a string.

seeds.t: Enable Binary Logging for Point-in-Time Recovery
seeds.w: Running MySQL without binary logging (binlog_format=ROW not set, log_bin disabled) — data loss from accidental DELETE or corruption can only be recovered from the last full backup.
seeds.c: Enable binary logging with log_bin=mysql-bin and binlog_format=ROW. Use mysqlbinlog to replay events between a backup and the failure point for PITR. Combine with a regular full backup schedule (mysqldump or xtrabackup).
seeds.s: accidental DELETE of a table recoverable only to the last nightly backup, hours of data lost.

seeds.t: Log and Analyse Deadlocks With InnoDB Status
seeds.w: Ignoring intermittent 'Deadlock found when trying to get lock; try restarting transaction' errors — deadlocks are often design issues that worsen under load.
seeds.c: Run SHOW ENGINE INNODB STATUS after a deadlock to see the last deadlock report including the transactions and locks involved. Enable innodb_print_all_deadlocks=ON to log all deadlocks to the error log. Resolve by: acquiring locks in consistent order, keeping transactions short, …
seeds.s: random deadlock errors in production that are hard to reproduce in testing.

seeds.t: AUTO_INCREMENT IDs Have Gaps and Are Not Reused
seeds.w: Relying on AUTO_INCREMENT IDs being sequential and gapless, or trying to reset them to fill gaps — gaps are normal (from rolled-back transactions, deletions) and reusing IDs can cause foreign key conflicts.
seeds.c: Treat AUTO_INCREMENT IDs as opaque unique identifiers, not sequential counts. To find the next ID, use LAST_INSERT_ID() after an INSERT, not SELECT MAX(id)+1 (race condition). For gapless sequences, use a separate counter table with SELECT...FOR UPDATE.
seeds.s: ID gaps causing confusion in auditing, or MAX(id)+1 logic causing duplicate key errors under concurrency.

seeds.t: MySQL 8.0 Removed the Query Cache
seeds.w: Configuring or relying on MySQL's query cache (query_cache_type, query_cache_size) in MySQL 8.0+ — the query cache was removed entirely in 8.0 and these settings cause startup errors.
seeds.c: Implement caching at the application layer (Redis, Memcached) for frequently-read, rarely-changed data. For MySQL 5.7, the query cache is available but deprecated and often hurts performance under write-heavy loads due to cache invalidation locking.
seeds.s: MySQL 8.0 refusing to start with 'Unknown system variable query_cache_type' error.

seeds.t: Negotiation: Entering Without a BATNA
seeds.w: Starting a negotiation without knowing your Best Alternative to a Negotiated Agreement — you have no basis for evaluating whether to accept or walk away.
seeds.c: Before any negotiation, write down your BATNA: what you'll do if this negotiation fails. Improve your BATNA before negotiating. The stronger your BATNA, the more leverage you have.
seeds.s: Candidate accepts below-market offer because they didn't have another offer lined up; stronger alternative would have produced better terms.

seeds.t: Negotiation: Making the First Offer Too Low Out of Politeness
seeds.w: Anchoring conservatively in a first offer out of fear of seeming greedy — the first offer anchors the negotiation, and a low anchor advantages the other party.
seeds.c: Research market rates and anchor high (for sellers/employees) or low (for buyers). A high anchor sets a favorable reference point. The other party will counter; the final number is influenced by your anchor.
seeds.s: Candidate asks for $120K when market is $140-160K; employer anchored at $120K, final offer $128K; would have been $145K with stronger anchor.

seeds.t: Negotiation: Negotiating Positions Instead of Interests
seeds.w: Treating negotiation as a battle over stated positions ('I want X', 'I'll give Y') rather than exploring underlying interests.
seeds.c: Ask 'why' and 'what matters most to you' to uncover interests. Two parties can want the same thing for different reasons and both can get what they need. Focus on creating value before dividing it.
seeds.s: Negotiation collapses over salary when candidate would have taken less cash for more equity — the real interest was never discussed.

seeds.t: Negotiation: Reactive Devaluation of Concessions
seeds.w: Viewing a concession less favorably simply because it came from the other party — if your counterpart offers something, you assume there must be a catch.
seeds.c: Evaluate concessions on their merits, not their source. Before the negotiation, list what concessions you'd want. If they offer those concessions, don't dismiss them reflexively.
seeds.s: The other party offers exactly what was on your wishlist; dismissed as 'too good to be true'; negotiation drags on unnecessarily.

seeds.t: Negotiation: Reducing Multi-Dimensional Negotiation to One Issue
seeds.w: Negotiating only on price when there are multiple issues that could create value — single-issue negotiations are inherently zero-sum.
seeds.c: Identify all negotiable issues before starting: salary, equity, start date, title, remote work, vacation. Package multiple issues together. Trades across issues create value for both parties.
seeds.s: Candidate negotiates only salary; leaves equity, start date, and title on the table; final package worse than possible.

seeds.t: Negotiation: Making Rapid Small Concessions
seeds.w: Conceding quickly under pressure — the other party learns that pressure produces concessions and keeps applying it.
seeds.c: Make concessions slowly and condition each one: 'I can move on X if you move on Y.' Each concession should be smaller than the last. Signal that you're approaching your limit.
seeds.s: Negotiation partner says nothing; uncomfortable silence causes immediate concession; pattern repeats; end far from opening position.

seeds.t: Negotiation: Not Asking for What You Want
seeds.w: Assuming the other party will offer what's fair, or that asking will seem rude — the other party optimizes for their own interest; what isn't asked for isn't offered.
seeds.c: Ask explicitly for what you want. The worst answer is 'no.' Most people fear rejection more than the actual cost of asking. Asking is the entire mechanism of negotiation.
seeds.s: Employee receives below-market raise and never asks for more; manager assumed employee was satisfied; conversation would have resulted in raise.

seeds.t: NEXT_PUBLIC_ Variables Are Baked In at docker build Time
seeds.w: Setting NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL as a runtime ENV in the Dockerfile and expecting it to be configurable at docker run time — Next.js inlines NEXT_PUBLIC_ variables into the JavaScript bundle at build time, not at runtime.
seeds.c: Pass NEXT_PUBLIC_ vars as Docker build arguments: ARG NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL; ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL=$NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL, then docker build --build-arg NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL=https://api.prod.com. For truly runtime-configurable public values, use a runtime config endpoint or inject …
seeds.s: changing the API URL via docker run -e NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL=... has no effect — the old URL is hard-coded in the bundle.

seeds.t: Use output: standalone for Minimal Production Images
seeds.w: Copying the entire project including node_modules into the final Docker image — Next.js full installs are 300 MB+; you're shipping dev dependencies and source files to production.
seeds.c: Add output: 'standalone' to next.config.js. Next.js traces only the files needed and writes them to .next/standalone. The multi-stage Dockerfile copies .next/standalone + .next/static + public to produce an image under 100 MB with no node_modules needed. This is Next.js's offici…
seeds.s: Docker image over 500 MB because the full node_modules is included, slowing push/pull and increasing attack surface.

seeds.t: Use a Three-Stage Dockerfile for Next.js
seeds.w: Building and running Next.js in a single Dockerfile stage — the build stage tools (devDependencies, build cache) end up in the production image.
seeds.c: Use three stages: (1) deps: install all dependencies; (2) builder: copy source, run next build; (3) runner: copy only .next/standalone, .next/static, and public from builder — no node_modules, no source. Base the runner on node:alpine or node:slim. Set NODE_ENV=production and ru…
seeds.s: production image containing source code, devDependencies, and build tooling that belong only in the build stage.

seeds.t: Mount .next/cache as a Volume to Speed Up Rebuilds
seeds.w: Discarding the Next.js build cache between CI runs — Next.js uses incremental compilation; without cache, every build recompiles everything from scratch.
seeds.c: In CI, persist .next/cache between runs as a cache artifact. In docker-compose for development, mount a named volume: volumes: [next-cache:/app/.next/cache]. Next.js stores per-page and module-level build artifacts in cache; a warm cache reduces rebuild time by 50–80%. Do not mo…
seeds.s: CI builds taking 3 minutes every run when a cached build would take 45 seconds.

seeds.t: Write a Next.js-Specific .dockerignore
seeds.w: Omitting .dockerignore or using a generic one that copies node_modules and .next into the build context — the Docker build context can be gigabytes, slowing every build.
seeds.c: Add to .dockerignore: node_modules, .next (except when explicitly copying), .git, .env*.local, coverage, .nyc_output, *.log, Dockerfile*, docker-compose*. This keeps the build context small and prevents local build artifacts from overwriting the Docker build's own .next output.
seeds.s: docker build taking 30 seconds just to send context before any layer builds because node_modules (hundreds of MB) is included.

seeds.t: Install sharp's Native Binaries for the Docker OS
seeds.w: Building node_modules with sharp on macOS and copying them into a Linux container — sharp bundles native binaries compiled for the build OS; Linux containers need Linux-compiled binaries.
seeds.c: Install dependencies inside the Docker build stage on the target Linux OS, not by copying from the host. For multi-platform builds (M1/M2 Mac building Linux images), the Node installer inside Docker uses the correct platform. Add --platform=linux/amd64 to FROM if building on ARM…
seeds.s: 'Cannot find module sharp' or 'Error: Module did not self-register' in the Linux container despite sharp being in node_modules.

seeds.t: Run the Next.js Server as a Non-Root User
seeds.w: Running the Next.js production server as root inside the container — root process in a container is a security risk; a container escape gives the attacker root access.
seeds.c: In the runner stage, create a system user and group: RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs; RUN adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs. Change ownership of .next to the nextjs user. Switch with USER nextjs before CMD. The standalone server is a plain node server.js — no special pe…
seeds.s: container security scan flagging root process, or compliance policy blocking deployment of root-running containers.

seeds.t: Server-Only Variables Are Available at Runtime via docker run -e
seeds.w: Treating all environment variables the same regardless of prefix — confusing NEXT_PUBLIC_ (build-time, client-exposed) with server-only variables (DATABASE_URL, SECRET_KEY).
seeds.c: Variables without NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix are server-side only and are read at runtime, not baked into the bundle. Pass them at runtime: docker run -e DATABASE_URL=postgres://... -e SECRET_KEY=... next-app. They're available in API routes, getServerSideProps, Server Components, and …
seeds.s: DATABASE_URL accidentally exposed in the client bundle because it was accidentally prefixed with NEXT_PUBLIC_.

seeds.t: Add a Dedicated Health Check Endpoint for Container Orchestration
seeds.w: Using HEALTHCHECK CMD curl http://localhost:3000 which hits the full Next.js page render — slow, can fail due to application logic, and doesn't isolate infrastructure from app health.
seeds.c: Create a lightweight API route at /api/health that returns 200 OK immediately. Add to Dockerfile: HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=3s --retries=3 CMD curl -f http://localhost:3000/api/health || exit 1. In Kubernetes, configure the readinessProbe and livenessProbe to use this…
seeds.s: container orchestrator killing healthy containers because / returns a redirect (301) rather than 200.

seeds.t: Respect the PORT Environment Variable in Production
seeds.w: Hardcoding EXPOSE 3000 and starting with node server.js without reading PORT — platforms like Railway, Render, and Cloud Run set PORT dynamically; hardcoding 3000 breaks deployment.
seeds.c: Next.js standalone's server.js reads process.env.PORT automatically. Pass PORT as a runtime env var: docker run -e PORT=8080 -p 8080:8080 next-app. In Kubernetes, set PORT in the container env and configure the Service to target the same port. Always EXPOSE the same port the app…
seeds.s: app deployed to a PaaS crashing on startup because PORT=8080 was set by the platform but the app hardcoded 3000.

seeds.t: Copy package.json Before Source for Optimal Layer Caching
seeds.w: Copying the entire project before running npm install — any source file change invalidates the npm install layer, making every build do a full install.
seeds.c: In the deps stage: COPY package.json package-lock.json ./; RUN npm ci. Then in the builder stage: COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules; COPY . .. This way, npm ci only re-runs when package.json or package-lock.json changes. npm ci is faster and reproducible compared…
seeds.s: docker build running npm install from scratch on every build because source files were copied before the install step.

seeds.t: Disable Next.js Telemetry in Docker Builds
seeds.w: Leaving Next.js telemetry enabled during Docker builds — Next.js attempts to send telemetry data during build, adding latency and generating noise in environments without outbound internet access.
seeds.c: Set ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1 in the Dockerfile builder stage. This is an environment variable (not a build arg) and disables the telemetry HTTP calls entirely. Also set it in CI environments. This is purely optional but good practice for build determinism.
seeds.s: next build printing 'Telemetry' messages, or timing out in air-gapped CI environments waiting for a telemetry endpoint.

seeds.t: 'use client' marks a component and all its imports as client-side
seeds.w: importing a large server-only library inside a client component — it ships to the browser.
seeds.c: keep 'use client' at the leaves of the component tree, as close to the interactive parts as possible. Use server components for data fetching and heavy computation; pass results down as props.
seeds.s: bundle size bloat, or 'this module cannot be used on the client' errors.

seeds.t: cookies() and headers() are now async in Next.js 15+
seeds.w: const cookieStore = cookies() — synchronous usage was deprecated in Next.js 15.
seeds.c: const cookieStore = await cookies() and const headersList = await headers(). This applies to both cookies and headers from 'next/headers'.
seeds.s: deprecation warning, or runtime error in Next.js 15+ projects.

seeds.t: Route params and searchParams are async in Next.js 15+
seeds.w: function Page({ params }) { const id = params.id } — synchronous params access is deprecated.
seeds.c: async function Page({ params }) { const { id } = await params; }. The same applies to searchParams in page components.
seeds.s: deprecation warning in Next.js 14, runtime error in Next.js 15+.

seeds.t: fetch() in server components is cached by default — opt out deliberately
seeds.w: forgetting that cached data is stale after mutation.
seeds.c: use fetch(url, { cache: 'no-store' }) for always-fresh data, or { next: { revalidate: N } } for time-based revalidation. Call revalidatePath() or revalidateTag() after server actions that mutate data.
seeds.s: page shows stale data after a form submission or database update.

seeds.t: Server actions must be defined with 'use server' directive
seeds.w: passing an async function directly as a form action without 'use server' — this is a client function, not a server action, and will not execute on the server.
seeds.c: add 'use server' as the first line of the async function, or define it in a separate file with 'use server' at the top. Server actions can be called from both server and client components.
seeds.s: action runs on the client instead of the server, or database calls fail in the browser.

seeds.t: Middleware runs on every request unless matcher is configured
