seeds.s: comparing potential values between simulations using different gauges and claiming disagreement; computing Aharonov-Bohm phase as zero because local B = 0; storing A without recording the gauge choice.

seeds.t: Only Accelerating Charges Radiate — Uniform Motion Does Not
seeds.w: Assuming motion implies radiation — radiated power ∝ a² (Larmor), not v². A uniformly moving charge carries its field without radiating.
seeds.c: P = q²a²/(6πε₀c³) (Larmor). Only acceleration generates EM radiation. Synchrotron, bremsstrahlung, and antenna emission all require a ≠ 0.
seeds.s: adding EM energy loss to a uniformly moving charge in simulation; missing synchrotron losses in a charged-particle tracker with circular orbits; omitting bremsstrahlung in collision cascades.

seeds.t: The Spacetime Interval Is Lorentz-Invariant; Space and Time Intervals Separately Are Not
seeds.w: Treating Δx or Δt as separately invariant under Lorentz boosts — only the spacetime interval Δs² = −c²Δt² + |Δx|² is invariant. Both space and time intervals transform.
seeds.c: Δs² = −c²Δt² + Δx² is Lorentz-invariant. Timelike (Δs² < 0): causal, defines proper time τ. Spacelike (Δs² > 0): defines proper length. Null (Δs² = 0): light cone.
seeds.s: computing time dilation without updating the spatial interval in the same problem; claiming the twin paradox is a contradiction without computing proper time along each worldline; confusing coordinate time with proper time.

seeds.t: Equivalence Principle Holds Only Locally — Tidal Forces Globally Distinguish Curved Spacetime
seeds.w: Applying the equivalence principle globally — it holds only locally (point-like lab). Tidal forces (geodesic deviation ∝ Riemann curvature) reveal curved spacetime globally and cannot be transformed away.
seeds.c: Equivalence principle is local: freely-falling frame ≈ Minkowski only at a point. Global curvature is revealed by tidal acceleration δa^μ = −R^μ_{νρσ} u^ν δx^ρ u^σ.
seeds.s: claiming a falling accelerometer proves 'zero gravity' everywhere in the field; computing satellite corrections with SR only; forgetting that tidal forces are globally detectable in any finite lab.

seeds.t: Gravitational Waves Require Changing Quadrupole Moment — Monopole and Dipole Vanish
seeds.w: Applying monopole or dipole radiation formulae to gravitational waves — mass conservation (monopole = 0) and momentum conservation (dipole = 0) eliminate both; the leading term is quadrupole.
seeds.c: GW power ∝ |d³Ī_{ij}/dt³|² (quadrupole formula). Spherically symmetric processes radiate no GW. GW frequency = 2f_orbital.
seeds.s: predicting GW emission from spherically symmetric collapse; computing GW frequency as the orbital frequency rather than twice it; applying an EM-like dipole formula to gravitational radiation.

seeds.t: SSB Produces dim(G)−dim(H) Goldstone Bosons; Gauged Goldstones Are Eaten by Gauge Fields
seeds.w: Saying the Hamiltonian breaks the symmetry, or counting dim(G) Goldstone bosons — SSB means G-symmetric Hamiltonian but H-symmetric ground state, giving dim(G)−dim(H) massless bosons.
seeds.c: SSB: G-symmetric Hamiltonian, H-symmetric vacuum → (dimG − dimH) Goldstone bosons. For gauged symmetry (Higgs mechanism) Goldstones are absorbed by gauge fields, which become massive.
seeds.s: applying Goldstone's theorem to a gauged symmetry without accounting for the Higgs mechanism; counting dim(G) Goldstones instead of dim(G)−dim(H).

seeds.t: RG Classifies Operators as Relevant, Marginal, or Irrelevant — Universality Follows From Irrelevance
seeds.w: Treating renormalisation as removing infinities rather than as systematic coarse-graining — RG identifies relevant (important at long distance), marginal, and irrelevant (exponentially suppressed) operators.
seeds.c: Under RG flow toward a fixed point: relevant couplings grow (drive phase structure), irrelevant couplings vanish (universality). Run couplings between scales using β functions.
seeds.s: keeping all operators in an EFT without identifying which are irrelevant at the scale of interest; applying high-energy coupling constants at low-energy scales without running them; treating every term in a Hamiltonian as equally important regardless of scaling dimension.

seeds.t: Shannon Entropy and Thermodynamic Entropy Are Identical Up to Boltzmann's Constant
seeds.w: Treating Shannon entropy H = −Σ pᵢ log₂ pᵢ and Gibbs entropy S = −kΣ pᵢ ln pᵢ as merely analogous — they are the same quantity; k and ln(2) are unit conversion factors.
seeds.c: S = k ln(2) × H. Landauer: erasing 1 bit dissipates ≥ kT ln 2. High-surprisal (low-probability) outcomes carry more information; this governs both thermodynamic free energy and retrieval signal.
seeds.s: mixing logarithm bases (bits vs nats) in the same entropy formula; treating Landauer's principle as speculative rather than experimentally verified; computing mutual information without the joint term H(X,Y).

seeds.t: Diffraction Limit Is Fundamental Fourier Physics — Not an Engineering Failure
seeds.w: Treating the diffraction limit Δx ≥ λ/(2NA) as an engineering limitation — it is fundamental Fourier wave physics. Superresolution (STED, STORM) uses nonlinearity or near-fields, not better optics.
seeds.c: Δx ≥ λ/(2NA) follows from Fourier uncertainty of the aperture. Superresolution requires near-field evanescent waves (k > k₀) or nonlinear fluorescence saturation — not improved far-field optics.
seeds.s: proposing a far-field linear-optics method to achieve sub-diffraction resolution; computing microscope resolution without specifying NA; expecting less-aberrated optics to deliver sub-diffraction images.

seeds.t: Playwright Auto-Waits — Never Use sleep or Fixed Delays
seeds.w: Adding await page.waitForTimeout(2000) or time.sleep(2) to wait for elements — arbitrary delays make tests slow and still flake when the page is slower than expected.
seeds.c: Playwright automatically waits for elements to be visible, enabled, and stable before interacting. page.click(), page.fill(), and other action methods auto-wait. For explicit assertions, use expect(locator).toBeVisible() which retries until the assertion passes or times out. Use…
seeds.s: tests with sleep() calls that still flake on slow CI machines and take 3x longer than necessary.

seeds.t: Use Locators for Lazy Element References
seeds.w: Using page.$(selector) or page.querySelector() which return ElementHandle at a point in time — if the element is re-rendered, the handle is stale.
seeds.c: Use page.locator(selector) to create a lazy reference that re-queries the DOM on each interaction: const btn = page.locator('button[name="submit"]'). Locators support auto-waiting, strict mode (fails if multiple match), and built-in assertions: await expect(btn).toBeEnabled(). C…
seeds.s: 'element is detached from the document' errors after a page re-render because ElementHandle cached a stale reference.

seeds.t: Use Role and Label Selectors for Test Stability
seeds.w: Selecting elements with CSS selectors like .btn-primary or #submit-form — these break when styles or IDs change for non-functional reasons.
seeds.c: Use semantic selectors that reflect user-visible properties: page.getByRole('button', {name: 'Submit'}), page.getByLabel('Email address'), page.getByText('Welcome back'), page.getByPlaceholder('Search...'). These selectors are resilient to refactoring and also validate accessibi…
seeds.s: tests failing after a CSS class rename that didn't change any functionality.

seeds.t: Use Separate Browser Contexts for Test Isolation
seeds.w: Sharing a single browser context across tests — cookies, localStorage, and session state leak between tests causing order-dependent failures.
seeds.c: Playwright's built-in test runner creates a fresh BrowserContext per test automatically. For API testing via context.request, use the same isolated context. Use browser.newContext() for manual context creation. Save authenticated state with context.storageState() and load it wit…
seeds.s: test passing in isolation but failing in the full suite because a previous test's session cookie was still active.

seeds.t: Intercept Network Requests to Control API Responses
seeds.w: Letting tests hit real APIs — slow, flaky, requires network, and can't easily test error states or edge cases.
seeds.c: Use page.route() to intercept and control network: await page.route('**/api/users', route => route.fulfill({ json: mockUsers })). Use route.abort() to simulate network failure. Use route.continue_() to pass through with modified request. Intercept by URL pattern, method, or head…
seeds.s: tests depending on a staging API that is sometimes down, making CI unreliable.

seeds.t: Save Authentication State to Skip Login in Tests
seeds.w: Logging in through the UI at the start of every test that requires authentication — slow and fragile, as UI changes to the login flow break all authenticated tests.
seeds.c: Perform login once in a global setup and save state: await context.storageState({path: 'auth.json'}). Reference it in playwright.config.ts: use: {storageState: 'auth.json'}. Log in via the API (faster): await request.post('/api/login', {data: creds}). Use multiple state files fo…
seeds.s: test suite with 100 tests each spending 3 seconds on UI login for 5 minutes of avoidable login overhead.

seeds.t: Use Soft Assertions to Collect Multiple Failures Per Test
seeds.w: Using hard assertions (expect()) that stop the test on the first failure — you only learn one thing is wrong per run, requiring multiple iterations to fix all issues.
seeds.c: Use expect.soft() to collect all failures before stopping: await expect.soft(page.getByRole('heading')).toHaveText('Dashboard'); await expect.soft(page.getByText('Welcome')).toBeVisible(). At the end of the test, Playwright reports all failures. Use for validating multiple eleme…
seeds.s: fixing one test failure at a time when the page has five broken elements that soft assertions would surface in one run.

seeds.t: Use Page Object Model to Encapsulate Page Interactions
seeds.w: Duplicating selector strings and interaction logic across test files — when the UI changes, every test file must be updated.
seeds.c: Create Page Object classes: class LoginPage { usernameInput = this.page.getByLabel('Username'); async login(user, pass) { await this.usernameInput.fill(user); await this.page.getByLabel('Password').fill(pass); await this.page.getByRole('button', {name: 'Login'}).click(); } }. Te…
seeds.s: 20 test files each containing the same login locator string that requires updating in all 20 when the label changes.

seeds.t: Use Trace Viewer to Debug Flaky and Failing Tests
seeds.w: Adding console.log() statements and running tests repeatedly to debug failures — slow iteration, and difficult to reproduce timing-related flakiness.
seeds.c: Enable tracing in playwright.config.ts: trace: 'on-first-retry'. Or trace specific tests: await context.tracing.start({screenshots: true, snapshots: true}). Open traces with npx playwright show-trace trace.zip. The viewer shows timeline, DOM snapshots at each action, network req…
seeds.s: spending hours adding console.log to reproduce a flaky test that the Trace Viewer would show immediately.

seeds.t: Run Tests in Parallel With Multiple Workers
seeds.w: Running tests serially (workers: 1) in CI — a large test suite takes 10 minutes when parallel execution could reduce it to 2 minutes.
seeds.c: Configure workers in playwright.config.ts: workers: process.env.CI ? 2 : undefined (undefined uses 50% of CPUs). Tests must be independent (isolated contexts) for parallel execution. Use test.describe.configure({mode: 'serial'}) for tests that must be ordered. Use --shard flag f…
seeds.s: 15-minute CI test run for a suite that completes in 3 minutes with appropriate parallelism.

seeds.t: Use globalSetup for Expensive One-Time Setup
seeds.w: Running expensive setup (database seeding, server startup, auth token generation) inside every test or beforeAll hook — repeated for each worker process in parallel mode.
seeds.c: Define globalSetup: './global-setup.ts' in playwright.config.ts. The globalSetup function runs once before all workers start. Store shared state (auth tokens, test data IDs) in environment variables or a temp file read by tests. Define globalTeardown for cleanup after all tests …
seeds.s: database seeded 8 times (once per worker) when a single global setup would suffice.

seeds.t: Configure Automatic Screenshots on Test Failure
seeds.w: Tests failing in CI with no visual evidence of what the page looked like at the point of failure — debugging requires reproducing locally.
seeds.c: Configure in playwright.config.ts: screenshot: 'only-on-failure', video: 'retain-on-failure'. Screenshots and videos are saved to the test-results directory. Upload as CI artifacts. Use await page.screenshot({path: 'debug.png'}) for manual screenshots at specific points. The HTM…
seeds.s: CI test failures with no visual context, requiring local reproduction to understand what broke.

seeds.t: Extend test Fixtures for Shared Setup and Helpers
seeds.w: Repeating beforeEach setup in every test file or using global variables for shared page objects — causes coupling and makes setup hard to customise per test.
seeds.c: Extend Playwright's test fixture: const test = base.extend({ loggedInPage: async ({page}, use) => { await page.goto('/login'); await page.fill('[name=email]', 'user@test.com'); await page.click('button[type=submit]'); await use(page); } }). Import the extended test in test files…
seeds.s: duplicated login setup in every test file's beforeEach that a fixture would centralise and make lazy.

seeds.t: Configure baseURL to Avoid Hardcoding in Tests
seeds.w: Hardcoding http://localhost:3000 in every page.goto() call — breaks when the port changes or when running against staging.
seeds.c: Set baseURL in playwright.config.ts: use: {baseURL: process.env.BASE_URL || 'http://localhost:3000'}. Then use relative paths: await page.goto('/dashboard'). Switch environments with BASE_URL=https://staging.example.com npx playwright test. Use different config files for differe…
seeds.s: changing the dev server port requiring updates to every test file's goto() calls.

seeds.t: Run Accessibility Checks With @axe-core/playwright
seeds.w: Only testing functionality without verifying accessibility — visual tests pass while keyboard navigation and screen reader support are broken.
seeds.c: Install @axe-core/playwright and run: const results = await new AxeBuilder({page}).analyze(); expect(results.violations).toEqual([]). Run on critical pages as part of the standard test suite. Use .include('#main-content') and .exclude('[aria-hidden=true]') to scope the analysis.…
seeds.s: application shipping with colour contrast and missing ARIA label violations that automated accessibility testing would catch.

seeds.t: Use toHaveScreenshot for Visual Regression Testing
seeds.w: Manually comparing screenshots or relying only on functional tests to catch visual regressions.
seeds.c: Use Playwright's built-in visual comparison: await expect(page).toHaveScreenshot('dashboard.png'). On first run, it creates the baseline. On subsequent runs, it compares against the baseline with a configurable pixel difference threshold. Update baselines with --update-snapshots…
seeds.s: CSS regression changing button colours or layout undetected because only functional tests existed.

seeds.t: Use Playwright Codegen to Record Initial Test Skeletons
seeds.w: Writing every selector and interaction from scratch by reading source code or inspecting the DOM manually — slow and error-prone.
seeds.c: Run npx playwright codegen https://example.com to open the browser with the inspector recording. Interact with the page; Playwright generates the test code in real time. Use the code as a starting point and refactor: replace generated CSS selectors with getByRole/getByLabel equi…
seeds.s: spending 30 minutes finding selectors for a complex form that codegen records interactively in 5 minutes.

seeds.t: Test Mobile Viewports and Touch Interactions
seeds.w: Only testing at desktop viewport — responsive design breaks, mobile menus may not be accessible, and touch-only interactions are untested.
seeds.c: Use device emulation: const iPhone = devices['iPhone 14']; const context = await browser.newContext({...iPhone}). Or configure in playwright.config.ts: projects: [{name: 'Mobile Chrome', use: {...devices['Pixel 7']}}]. Mobile devices have touch events, smaller viewports, and dif…
seeds.s: mobile users unable to navigate because the hamburger menu was never tested and its click handler was broken.

seeds.t: Use request Context for API-Level Test Setup
seeds.w: Setting up test data by driving the UI through admin forms — slow and fragile when admin UI changes.
seeds.c: Use Playwright's built-in API request context: const request = await playwright.request.newContext({baseURL, extraHTTPHeaders: {Authorization: `Bearer ${token}`}}); await request.post('/api/users', {data: userData}). The request context shares storage state with the browser cont…
seeds.s: test data setup taking 30 seconds through the UI when API calls would do it in 1 second.

seeds.t: Enable Strict Mode to Catch Ambiguous Selectors
seeds.w: Using a selector that matches multiple elements and only interacting with the first — silently tests the wrong element when the DOM changes.
seeds.c: Playwright's locator API is strict by default — if a selector matches multiple elements, the action throws 'strict mode violation'. Use .first(), .nth(0), or .last() explicitly when you intend to match multiple elements. Use .count() to assert the expected number of matches. Con…
seeds.s: test passing by interacting with the wrong button because the selector matched two elements and the first happened to be correct.

seeds.t: Use GENERATED AS IDENTITY not SERIAL for primary keys
seeds.w: id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY — SERIAL is a PostgreSQL shorthand that creates a sequence but has quirks: ownership is not properly tracked and it can cause issues with pg_dump and schema tools.
seeds.c: id INTEGER GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY (Postgres 10+). For UUID: id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(). GENERATED ALWAYS prevents accidental manual insertion of id values.
seeds.s: schema migration tools show unexpected sequences, or id conflicts when restoring from dump.

seeds.t: Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to see actual query execution, not just the plan
seeds.w: EXPLAIN without ANALYZE — shows the planner's estimated plan but not actual rows or timing.
seeds.c: EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT) SELECT ... to see actual rows, actual time, and buffer hits/misses. For production: EXPLAIN ANALYZE on a representative dataset in a test environment. Look for Seq Scan on large tables (missing index), high actual rows vs. estimated rows (…
seeds.s: slow query with no obvious reason when looking at just the SQL.

seeds.t: Use jsonb not json — jsonb is indexed and queryable
seeds.w: using json type for structured data you will query — json stores text verbatim and cannot be indexed.
seeds.c: use jsonb which stores a parsed binary representation: indexes work, operators like @> and ? are available, and storage is efficient. Create GIN index for full document search: CREATE INDEX ON table USING GIN (data). Use -> for object access, ->> for text output.
seeds.s: slow queries filtering on json fields, or inability to index json columns.

seeds.t: Use a connection pooler — PostgreSQL connections are expensive
seeds.w: opening a new PostgreSQL connection per request from a web application — each connection spawns a new process (~5-10MB RAM) and connection setup takes ~20-50ms.
seeds.c: use PgBouncer or pg_pool in transaction mode between your app and Postgres. Configure your application's connection pool (e.g. pg's pool, SQLAlchemy's pool_size). The typical Postgres server handles 100-300 simultaneous connections; applications need thousands of concurrent requ…
seeds.s: 'too many connections' error under load, high memory on Postgres server.

seeds.t: Use INSERT ... ON CONFLICT for atomic upsert
seeds.w: SELECT then INSERT or UPDATE separately — race condition between the select and insert causes duplicate key errors under concurrent load.
seeds.c: INSERT INTO table (col1, col2) VALUES (v1, v2) ON CONFLICT (unique_col) DO UPDATE SET col2 = EXCLUDED.col2. EXCLUDED refers to the row that was attempted. Use DO NOTHING if you want to silently skip duplicates. Requires a UNIQUE constraint or index on the conflict target.
seeds.s: duplicate key violations under concurrent writes, or lost updates.

seeds.t: Use full-text search with tsvector and tsquery for text search
seeds.w: LIKE '%search term%' for text search — slow, no relevance ranking, no stemming.
seeds.c: create a tsvector column: ALTER TABLE docs ADD COLUMN search_vector tsvector GENERATED ALWAYS AS (to_tsvector('english', title || ' ' || body)) STORED. Create GIN index: CREATE INDEX ON docs USING GIN(search_vector). Query: WHERE search_vector @@ plainto_tsquery('english', 'sear…
seeds.s: slow LIKE queries on text content, no stemming (search for 'run' doesn't find 'running').

seeds.t: Dead tuples cause table bloat — ensure autovacuum is running
seeds.w: disabling autovacuum to 'improve performance' — tables grow unboundedly and queries slow down.
seeds.c: monitor table bloat with pgstattuple. Tune autovacuum settings for high-churn tables: autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor = 0.01 for tables with millions of rows. Run VACUUM ANALYZE manually after large bulk operations.
seeds.s: table size grows despite stable row count, queries slow progressively.

seeds.t: Partial indexes cover only rows matching a condition — much smaller and faster
seeds.w: indexing an entire column when queries only ever filter on a subset.
seeds.c: partial index: CREATE INDEX ON orders (created_at) WHERE status = 'pending'. This index only includes pending orders — much smaller, faster to update, and faster to scan for the common query 'find pending orders by date'. Classic use: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ON users (email) WHERE d…
seeds.s: index on low-cardinality status column is large and not used by planner.

seeds.t: LATERAL JOIN lets subqueries reference outer query columns
seeds.w: using correlated subqueries that can only return a single value, forcing multiple separate queries to get top-N results per group.
seeds.c: use LATERAL JOIN to run a subquery for each row of the outer query: SELECT u.name, latest.* FROM users u JOIN LATERAL (SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id = u.id ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 3) latest ON true. LATERAL is PostgreSQL's way to express 'for each outer row, run this…
seeds.s: N+1 queries for 'top N per group' patterns that LATERAL collapses into one.

seeds.t: Use JSONB operators and indexes for efficient JSON querying
seeds.w: extracting all rows and filtering JSON in application code, or using json_each without an index.
seeds.c: store JSON as jsonb (not json), use ->> for text extraction, @> for containment: WHERE data @> '{"status": "active"}', and create GIN indexes: CREATE INDEX ON table USING gin(data). The @> containment operator uses the GIN index for fast lookup. For path access: data #>> '{a,b,c…
seeds.s: full-table scans on JSONB columns even with an index, or slow queries filtering inside JSON.

seeds.t: Generated columns auto-compute values from other columns
seeds.w: maintaining a denormalised computed column with triggers or application logic — it can get out of sync.
seeds.c: use a generated column: ALTER TABLE products ADD COLUMN search_name TEXT GENERATED ALWAYS AS (lower(name)) STORED. The database automatically recomputes the value on insert/update and it can be indexed. Generated columns cannot reference other generated columns or non-immutable …
seeds.s: a computed column maintained by a trigger that occasionally falls out of sync with its source columns.

seeds.t: Use advisory locks for application-level mutual exclusion
seeds.w: using a database row as a mutex with SELECT FOR UPDATE — this only works for rows that exist and requires a table.
seeds.c: use pg_try_advisory_lock(key) for non-blocking application-level locks keyed by an integer. They're session or transaction scoped: SELECT pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext('job:processor')) within a transaction auto-releases on commit. Great for preventing concurrent cron job runs …
seeds.s: duplicate processing from concurrent workers with no coordination mechanism.

seeds.t: LISTEN/NOTIFY enables lightweight pub/sub between PostgreSQL clients
seeds.w: polling a status table every second from multiple workers to detect changes — wasteful and adds unnecessary load.
seeds.c: use NOTIFY channel, payload in a trigger or after a mutation, and LISTEN channel in connected clients. PostgreSQL delivers the notification to all listeners on that channel asynchronously within the same transaction. psycopg2 exposes this via connection.notifies. Payload is limi…
seeds.s: polling loops checking for work that could be event-driven with LISTEN/NOTIFY.

seeds.t: max_connections is per-process — always use a connection pooler
seeds.w: opening a new database connection per HTTP request in a high-concurrency API — this exhausts max_connections (default 100) and causes 'FATAL: remaining connection slots are reserved' errors.
seeds.c: use PgBouncer or pgpool between the app and PostgreSQL. Set pool_mode = transaction for stateless apps. Even with pg_connection_pooling chunk in place, confirm the pooler is between the app and DB, not just configured.
seeds.s: database connections spike under load and exceed max_connections causing request failures.

seeds.t: Prefer declarative partitioning over table inheritance for partitioned data
seeds.w: using PostgreSQL table inheritance (INHERITS) for partitioning — queries don't automatically route to child tables efficiently, and many features (foreign keys, unique constraints) don't work across the hierarchy.
seeds.c: use declarative partitioning (PostgreSQL 10+): CREATE TABLE orders (...) PARTITION BY RANGE (created_at), then CREATE TABLE orders_2024 PARTITION OF orders FOR VALUES FROM ('2024-01-01') TO ('2025-01-01'). The planner automatically prunes partitions.
seeds.s: inherited tables don't benefit from partition pruning, making range queries scan all child tables.

seeds.t: Sequences have gaps by default — do not rely on them being contiguous
seeds.w: assuming SERIAL or IDENTITY columns produce gapless sequences — PostgreSQL pre-allocates sequence values in batches (cache) and does not reclaim values from rolled-back transactions.
seeds.c: use sequences only for unique identifiers, never for business logic that requires gap-free numbering (invoice numbers, receipt IDs). For gap-free sequences, use a dedicated counter table with SELECT ... FOR UPDATE or an advisory lock.
seeds.s: sequence jumps by hundreds after a server restart, or gaps appear after transaction rollbacks.

seeds.t: Set search_path explicitly to avoid schema ambiguity
seeds.w: relying on the default search_path (public) and creating objects in different schemas without qualifying names — queries resolve to the wrong table as schemas evolve.
seeds.c: set search_path explicitly per role: ALTER ROLE myapp SET search_path TO myapp, public; or at the session level: SET search_path = myapp, public. Always qualify table names in migrations to avoid ambiguity.
seeds.s: query works for one user but hits the wrong table for another due to differing search_path settings.

seeds.t: RETURNING clause fetches updated rows without a second query
seeds.w: running an UPDATE then a SELECT to fetch the updated row — this is two round-trips and creates a race condition if another writer modifies the row between them.
seeds.c: add RETURNING to get columns from the modified rows in a single statement: UPDATE users SET last_login = NOW() WHERE id = $1 RETURNING id, last_login, email. Works with INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE. Combine with CTEs for complex update-then-query patterns.
seeds.s: update-then-select pattern that should be a single atomic RETURNING statement.

seeds.t: Power Query: Query Folding Breaking Silently
seeds.w: Adding a transformation step that can't be pushed to the data source (e.g., adding a custom column with M code after a native query step) — subsequent steps no longer fold, and all data is loaded into memory for in-process transformation.
seeds.c: Check query folding: right-click a step and see if 'View Native Query' is available. Reorder steps to keep non-foldable operations at the end. Use Table.Buffer() before expensive non-foldable operations if the source data is large.
seeds.s: Query that took 2 seconds now takes 3 minutes; entire database table loaded into Excel before filtering.

seeds.t: Power Query: Table.TransformColumnTypes on Every Step
seeds.w: Changing column types in multiple steps throughout the query — each TransformColumnTypes call processes the entire table, and types set early are often overridden by later steps.
seeds.c: Set all column types once, at the end of the query after all transformations are complete. The auto-generated 'Changed Type' step after source connection can often be deleted.
seeds.s: Query takes 4× longer than necessary; Profiler shows multiple full-table type conversion passes.

seeds.t: Power Query: Hardcoded File Path
seeds.w: Embedding an absolute file path like C:\\Users\\john\\Downloads\\data.xlsx directly in the query source — the query breaks on any other machine or when the file moves.
seeds.c: Use a parameter for the file path, or store the path in a dedicated Parameters query. Use relative paths via File.Contents or configure the path as an Excel named range for user-editable settings.
seeds.s: Query works only on the author's machine; colleagues get DataSource.Error; breaks after moving file to SharePoint.

seeds.t: Power Query: Missing Table.Buffer Before Merge Causing Re-evaluation
seeds.w: Merging two queries where the right-hand table is itself a complex query — Power Query may re-evaluate the right-hand query once per row of the left table when folding is unavailable.
seeds.c: Wrap the right-hand query in Table.Buffer() before the merge: Table.Buffer(RightQuery). This forces evaluation once and stores results in memory before the merge begins.
seeds.s: Simple merge takes hours; CPU 100%; duplicate records in results from stochastic re-evaluation.

seeds.t: Power Query: Helper Queries Loaded to Data Model
seeds.w: Queries used only as intermediate steps (parameter tables, helper functions, staging transformations) are set to load into the Excel Data Model or Power BI dataset, bloating it unnecessarily.
seeds.c: Right-click the query > uncheck 'Enable Load' or set to 'Connection Only'. Only final output tables should load. In Power BI, disable load for all staging and helper queries.
seeds.s: Data Model size 10× larger than needed; refresh takes 5× longer; Excel file grows enormous; Tabular Editor shows dozens of staging tables.

seeds.t: Power Query: Formula Firewall Error from Mixed Privacy Levels
seeds.w: Combining data from sources with different privacy levels (e.g., a public API and a private database) without configuring privacy settings — Power Query blocks the query to prevent data leakage.
seeds.c: Set privacy levels explicitly for each data source via File > Options > Privacy. For trusted private environments, set all sources to 'Organizational' or 'Private'. Never set production sources to 'Public'.
seeds.s: Formula.Firewall: Query 'X' references other queries... error on queries that worked individually.

seeds.t: Power Query: List.Generate for Simple Transformations
seeds.w: Using List.Generate (Power Query's loop construct) to transform a list element by element — it's slow, hard to read, and almost always replaceable with List.Transform or Table.TransformColumns.
seeds.c: Use List.Transform for element-wise transformation: List.Transform(myList, each _ * 2). Use Table.TransformColumns for column transformations. List.Generate is for generating sequences, not transforming existing ones.
seeds.s: Transformation of 10,000 rows takes 30 seconds; List.Transform version takes 0.3 seconds.

seeds.t: Power Query: Expanding All Columns After Merge or Nest
seeds.w: Clicking 'Expand' after a merge and accepting all columns — adds unnecessary columns that slow refresh, increase model size, and require explicit removal later.
seeds.c: Explicitly select only the columns you need when expanding: click the expand icon, uncheck 'Use original column name as prefix', and select only required fields.
seeds.s: Data model contains 40 columns from a merged table when only 3 were needed; refresh time doubled; model unnecessarily large.

seeds.t: Power Query: Date Table Generated in M Instead of DAX
seeds.w: Building a date dimension table using List.Dates and M transformations in Power Query — it runs at refresh time against external sources, is slow, and can't use DAX time intelligence functions properly.
seeds.c: Create the date table in DAX using CALENDAR() or CALENDARAUTO(). DAX date tables integrate properly with time intelligence, are computed in the model engine (fast), and participate in auto date/time.
seeds.s: Time intelligence functions (TOTALYTD, SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR) don't work correctly; date table refresh is the slowest step.

seeds.t: Use select for Specific Fields, include for Relations
seeds.w: Using include to load a relation when only one field from that relation is needed — fetches the entire related object unnecessarily.
seeds.c: Use select to specify exactly which fields and relations to fetch: select: { id: true, name: true, posts: { select: { title: true } } }. select and include cannot be combined at the same level — choose one. Omitting both fetches all scalar fields but no relations.
seeds.s: API response taking twice as long because entire related objects are fetched when only their IDs were needed.

seeds.t: Use a Singleton PrismaClient to Avoid Connection Pool Exhaustion
seeds.w: Instantiating new PrismaClient() in every API route or request handler — Next.js hot reload and serverless cold starts create multiple instances, each opening its own connection pool and quickly exhausting the database's connection limit.
seeds.c: Create a singleton: export const prisma = global.prisma ?? new PrismaClient(); if (process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production') global.prisma = prisma. This reuses the single instance across hot reloads in development. In production serverless, use connection pooling middleware like P…
seeds.s: 'too many connections' database errors after a few minutes of development with Next.js hot reload.

seeds.t: Run prisma generate After Every Schema Change
seeds.w: Editing schema.prisma and using the Prisma Client immediately without regenerating — the generated client types are stale and don't reflect the schema changes, causing type errors or runtime failures.
seeds.c: Run npx prisma generate after every schema.prisma edit to regenerate the client. Add it as a postinstall script in package.json so it runs automatically after npm install. In CI, run generate before the TypeScript compile step.
seeds.s: TypeScript showing the correct schema in the file but the client types not reflecting new fields, or 'Field does not exist' at runtime.

seeds.t: Use migrate dev for Production, db push for Rapid Prototyping
seeds.w: Using prisma db push in production — it directly applies schema changes without generating a migration file, losing the audit trail and making rollbacks impossible.
seeds.c: Use npx prisma migrate dev during development to generate named, versioned migration SQL files that are committed to git. Use npx prisma migrate deploy in CI/CD to apply migrations in production. Reserve db push for rapid iteration on a local or throw-away database where migrati…
seeds.s: schema changes applied to production with no record of what changed or how to roll back.

seeds.t: Nested Includes Can Still Cause N+1 Queries
seeds.w: Assuming that include always batches related data efficiently — deeply nested includes with filtering at each level can still generate multiple queries.
seeds.c: Use Prisma's findMany with include for simple one-level or two-level relations, which Prisma batches efficiently. For complex nested queries, use a raw query or check the generated SQL with prisma.$on('query', console.log). Enable query logging in development to count actual que…
seeds.s: 10 users generating 10 separate author queries despite using include, visible in query logs.

seeds.t: Use Interactive Transactions for Complex Multi-Step Operations
seeds.w: Running multiple Prisma operations sequentially without a transaction — if the second operation fails, the first has already committed, leaving the database inconsistent.
seeds.c: Use prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => { const user = await tx.user.create({...}); await tx.account.create({...userId: user.id}); }) for multi-step operations that must be atomic. The tx client is the same as prisma but all operations share one transaction. On failure, all operat…
seeds.s: partial data (user created but no account) when an error occurs mid-sequence.

seeds.t: Use upsert for Create-or-Update Operations
seeds.w: Checking if a record exists with findUnique then conditionally calling create or update — not atomic, race condition between the check and the write.
seeds.c: Use prisma.model.upsert({ where: { email }, update: { name }, create: { email, name } }) which performs the check and write atomically at the database level. The where clause must use a unique field. Use createMany with skipDuplicates for bulk upsert-like inserts.
seeds.s: duplicate records created under concurrent requests because two threads both saw 'not found' and both created.

seeds.t: Use Cursor-Based Pagination for Stable Large Lists
seeds.w: Using skip/take (offset pagination) for large datasets — skip is O(offset) in most databases and results shift when records are inserted mid-page, causing duplicates or skips between pages.
seeds.c: Use cursor pagination: findMany({ take: 10, skip: 1, cursor: { id: lastId }, orderBy: { id: 'asc' } }). The skip: 1 skips the cursor record itself. Store the last record's ID as the next page cursor. Stable, O(log n) performance regardless of depth.
seeds.s: page 100 taking 10x longer than page 1, or items appearing twice or missing across paginated results.

seeds.t: Use $queryRaw Template Literals for Safe Raw Queries
seeds.w: Building raw SQL strings with string concatenation and passing them to $queryRawUnsafe — SQL injection vulnerability.
seeds.c: Use the tagged template literal form: prisma.$queryRaw`SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ${userId}`. Prisma automatically parameterises the interpolated values. Use Prisma.sql for composing fragments: Prisma.sql`WHERE id = ${id}`. Only use $queryRawUnsafe for dynamically constructe…
seeds.s: SQL injection vulnerability from user-controlled values interpolated into raw query strings.

seeds.t: Set relationMode prisma for Databases Without Foreign Keys
seeds.w: Using Prisma with PlanetScale, MongoDB, or other databases that don't support native foreign keys without setting relationMode — referential integrity is not enforced and Prisma relations may silently produce invalid references.
seeds.c: Add relationMode = "prisma" to the datasource block in schema.prisma for databases without native FK support. Prisma enforces referential integrity in the client layer (emulating ON DELETE CASCADE etc.). Add @@index decorators manually for relation fields since FK indexes are no…
seeds.s: orphaned child records after parent deletion, or Prisma errors about missing foreign key support.

seeds.t: Implement Soft Deletes With Prisma Middleware
seeds.w: Adding deletedAt checks to every query manually — easily forgotten, inconsistent across the codebase.
seeds.c: Register a Prisma middleware that automatically adds where: { deletedAt: null } to all findMany/findFirst queries and converts delete calls to update({ data: { deletedAt: new Date() } }). Prisma's newer extensions API (prisma.$extends) is the preferred approach in Prisma 4.16+. …
seeds.s: deleted records appearing in query results because a where: { deletedAt: null } filter was forgotten.

seeds.t: Prisma Enums Are Synced to Database Enum Types
seeds.w: Changing an enum value in schema.prisma and expecting the database to update automatically — enum changes require a migration, and renaming an enum value destroys and recreates the enum in PostgreSQL.
seeds.c: Adding a new enum value generates a safe ALTER TYPE ... ADD VALUE migration. Renaming or removing requires a multi-step migration: add new value, update data, remove old value. For frequently-changing status values, consider a VARCHAR with a CHECK constraint instead of a databas…
seeds.s: migration failing on enum rename, or existing data becoming invalid after an enum value is removed.

seeds.t: Use findUnique for Unique Fields, findFirst for Filtered Queries
seeds.w: Using findFirst({ where: { id } }) instead of findUnique({ where: { id } }) — findFirst can use any field and applies no uniqueness guarantee, while findUnique is restricted to unique/primary key fields and can be optimised differently.
seeds.c: Use findUnique when querying by a unique or primary key field — it communicates intent clearly and Prisma can optimise it. Use findFirst with orderBy when you need the first matching record of a non-unique query with a sort. Both return null when not found.
seeds.s: findFirst used everywhere including primary key lookups, missing the semantic clarity and potential optimisation of findUnique.

seeds.t: Use a Connection Pooler for Serverless Prisma Deployments
seeds.w: Connecting Prisma directly to PostgreSQL or MySQL from a serverless function — each function invocation opens connections without a warm pool, exhausting max_connections under load.
seeds.c: Use PgBouncer (transaction mode), RDS Proxy, or Prisma Accelerate as an external connection pooler between serverless functions and the database. Set connection_limit=1 in the DATABASE_URL for serverless when using an external pooler to prevent Prisma itself from opening multipl…
seeds.s: 'too many connections' errors under moderate load from a serverless API on Vercel or AWS Lambda.

seeds.t: Nullable Fields and Optional Fields Are Different in Prisma
seeds.w: Treating a nullable database column and an optional Prisma field as the same thing — nullable means the column can store NULL; optional in Prisma's generated types means the field can be omitted from create/update inputs.
seeds.c: In schema.prisma, ? after a type makes the field nullable in the database: email String?. Generated TypeScript types make nullable fields optional in inputs (can be omitted = undefined) and typed as string | null in outputs. Explicitly pass null to clear a nullable field; omit i…
seeds.s: sending undefined to clear a nullable field but the value remaining unchanged, or unexpected null type errors.

seeds.t: Enable Query Logging in Development to Detect Issues Early
seeds.w: Running Prisma in development without query logging and only discovering N+1 or missing-index issues in production under load.
seeds.c: Instantiate with logging enabled: new PrismaClient({ log: ['query', 'warn', 'error'] }). Subscribe to query events: prisma.$on('query', e => console.log(e.duration + 'ms', e.query)). Use this to count queries per request, spot full-table scans, and measure query duration during …
seeds.s: production performance issues from N+1 queries or missing indexes that were never visible in development.

seeds.t: Use @@unique and @@index for Multi-Field Constraints
seeds.w: Defining uniqueness constraints or indexes on individual fields when the constraint spans multiple fields — single-field @unique doesn't prevent duplicate (userId, postId) pairs.
seeds.c: Use @@unique([userId, postId]) at the model level for multi-field unique constraints, and @@index([createdAt, userId]) for composite indexes. These generate the correct database constraints and Prisma enforces them in the TypeScript types for where clauses. Name them explicitly:…
seeds.s: duplicate join-table records because a single-field unique was used instead of a composite unique.

seeds.t: Filter on JSON Fields Using Prisma's JSON Filter Operators
seeds.w: Fetching all records and filtering JSON fields in application code — full table scan transferred to the application layer.
seeds.c: Use Prisma's JSON filter operators for PostgreSQL/MySQL: where: { metadata: { path: ['$.status'], equals: 'active' } }. Use string_contains, array_contains for nested string and array matching. For complex JSON queries, fall back to $queryRaw with the database's native JSON func…
seeds.s: loading thousands of records with JSON fields to filter them in JavaScript when the database could do it.

seeds.t: Use Prisma Accelerate for Edge and Caching Scenarios
seeds.w: Connecting directly from edge runtimes (Cloudflare Workers, Vercel Edge) to a database — edge runtimes don't support TCP connections required by database drivers.
seeds.c: Use Prisma Accelerate as a connection pooler and query cache that works over HTTP, compatible with edge runtimes. Enable query-level caching with cacheStrategy: { swr: 60, ttl: 60 } in individual queries. Switch between direct and Accelerate URLs via environment variables per de…
seeds.s: 'TCP connections not supported in this environment' error when deploying Prisma to a Cloudflare Worker.

seeds.t: Use omit to Exclude Sensitive Fields From Query Results
seeds.w: Fetching all fields including password hashes and returning the full model object to the client — requires manual deletion of sensitive fields from query results.
seeds.c: Use the omit option (Prisma 5.13+): findMany({ omit: { password: true, internalNotes: true } }) to exclude sensitive fields at the query level. For consistent exclusion across the codebase, use Prisma Client extensions to define a model with sensitive fields always omitted. Alte…
seeds.s: password hashes or internal fields accidentally included in API responses.

seeds.t: Prompt Engineering: Zero-Shot for Tasks That Need Examples
seeds.w: Providing no examples in the prompt for tasks requiring a specific format, style, or judgment.
seeds.c: Add 2-5 representative input-output examples before the task prompt (few-shot). Choose examples covering the range of inputs and demonstrating the desired output quality and format.
seeds.s: Model returns inconsistent JSON schemas; formatting differs across runs; edge cases handled wrong despite instructions.

seeds.t: Prompt Engineering: Output Format Not Specified
seeds.w: Asking for structured output (JSON, table, list) without specifying the exact format — the model generates something plausible but inconsistent.
seeds.c: Specify the exact format: provide a JSON schema, template with fields, or an example of expected output. For JSON, include the exact keys.
seeds.s: Asking for 'a list of recommendations' returns prose paragraphs, bullet points, numbered lists, or JSON inconsistently.

seeds.t: Prompt Engineering: Skipping Chain-of-Thought for Reasoning Tasks
seeds.w: Asking for a direct answer on math, logic, or multi-step reasoning without asking the model to reason through the problem first.
seeds.c: Add 'Think step by step' or 'Let's reason through this:' before complex questions. For highest accuracy, use few-shot chain-of-thought examples.
seeds.s: Math word problem returns wrong answer; adding 'think step by step' returns correct answer on the same model.

seeds.t: Prompt Engineering: Role and Context Left Implicit
seeds.w: Prompting without establishing who the model is, who the user is, and what the goal is — model defaults to a generic assistant posture.
seeds.c: Open system prompts with explicit role, audience, and goal: 'You are a senior engineer reviewing code for a junior developer. Focus on correctness and teachability.'
seeds.s: Generic 'helpful assistant' response when domain expert review with specialized vocabulary was needed.

seeds.t: Prompt Engineering: Over-Constraining the Prompt
seeds.w: Adding so many constraints that the model spends reasoning on constraint satisfaction rather than the actual task — or generates refusals for benign content.
seeds.c: Specify what you want, not what you don't want. Use positive framing: 'Write in direct professional tone' rather than 20 prohibitions. Trust the model on unstated edge cases.
seeds.s: Model generates empty output or stilted text while trying to satisfy contradictory constraints.

seeds.t: Prompt Engineering: Assuming the Model Remembers Previous Conversations
seeds.w: Referring to 'what we discussed earlier' without including that context in the current prompt — each API call is stateless.
seeds.c: Include all relevant context in every prompt. For multi-turn systems, maintain conversation history and include it. Never assume the model recalls anything from previous API calls.
seeds.s: Model gives incorrect answer because it 'forgot' a constraint specified two messages ago that wasn't in context.

seeds.t: Prompt Engineering: Temperature Misconfigured for Task Type
seeds.w: Using high temperature for factual extraction or coding where consistency matters, or low temperature for creative tasks where variety is wanted.
seeds.c: Use temperature ≈ 0 for factual Q&A, structured extraction, and code generation. Use temperature 0.7-1.0 for creative writing, brainstorming, and ideation.
seeds.s: Code generation returns different implementations on every call making testing hard; brainstorming produces repetitive ideas.

seeds.t: Prompt Engineering: Single Prompt for Multi-Step Task
seeds.w: Asking a model to accomplish a complex multi-step task in a single prompt — errors in early steps compound through later steps.
seeds.c: Decompose complex tasks: use separate prompts for each step, chain outputs, or use structured multi-step prompting (plan → execute → verify). Check intermediate outputs before proceeding.
seeds.s: Single prompt to 'analyze, extract, verify, and write report' produces low-quality output mixing all steps.

seeds.t: Prompt Engineering: Not Asking the Model to Check Its Work
seeds.w: Accepting the model's first output without asking it to verify, review, or critique its own answer.
seeds.c: Add a verification step: 'Review your answer. Check that all facts are correct and requirements met.' Or use a separate review prompt: 'Review this output for errors.'
seeds.s: Model gives wrong calculation; asking it to 'verify your answer' causes it to catch and correct the error.

seeds.t: Prompt Engineering: Treating Context Window as Unlimited
seeds.w: Stuffing too much content into a single prompt — once the context window fills, earlier content is truncated or attention degrades, causing the model to 'forget' early instructions.
seeds.c: Keep prompts focused. Use retrieval (RAG) for long documents. Put the most important instructions at the beginning and end (primacy and recency effects). Monitor token counts.
seeds.s: System prompt instructions ignored in long conversations; model forgets the persona specified at the start.

seeds.t: Use Appropriate Fixture Scope to Avoid Redundant Setup
seeds.w: Defining an expensive fixture (database connection, API client, compiled binary) with function scope — it is recreated for every test, multiplying setup time by the number of tests.
seeds.c: Set scope='session' for fixtures that are safe to share across the entire test run, scope='module' for per-file sharing, scope='class' for class-based tests. Use scope='function' (default) only for fixtures that must be reset between tests.
seeds.s: test suite taking 5 minutes to spin up a Docker container once per test when a session-scoped fixture would spin it up once.

seeds.t: Use conftest.py for Shared Fixtures Across Test Files
seeds.w: Importing fixtures from test files into other test files, or duplicating fixtures across multiple test modules.
seeds.c: Place shared fixtures in conftest.py files — pytest discovers and makes them available automatically to all tests in the same directory and below without any imports. Nest conftest.py files for scope: top-level for global fixtures, subdirectory-level for sub-package fixtures.
seeds.s: ImportError trying to import fixtures from another test file, or identical fixtures copied across multiple test modules.

seeds.t: Use @pytest.mark.parametrize for Multiple Input Cases
seeds.w: Writing separate test functions for each input variant (test_add_positive, test_add_negative, test_add_zero) — duplicates test structure and creates a long list of nearly-identical functions.
seeds.c: Use @pytest.mark.parametrize('a,b,expected', [(1,2,3), (-1,-2,-3), (0,0,0)]) to run one test function against multiple inputs. Use ids=['positive','negative','zero'] for readable test names. Stack multiple parametrize decorators for a Cartesian product.
seeds.s: 20 nearly-identical test functions that differ only in the input values.

seeds.t: Use monkeypatch for Patching; unittest.mock for Assertions
seeds.w: Using unittest.mock.patch as a decorator for simple attribute patching, or using monkeypatch when you need to assert call arguments — each tool has its strength.
seeds.c: Use monkeypatch.setattr, monkeypatch.setenv, and monkeypatch.chdir for simple, auto-reverting attribute and environment substitution. Use unittest.mock.MagicMock and patch when you need to assert call counts, arguments, or return values. Both are valid in pytest — combine them f…
seeds.s: teardown forgetting to undo patches set without monkeypatch, or no way to assert mock was called without MagicMock.

seeds.t: Use tmp_path Fixture Not tmpdir for Temporary Files
seeds.w: Using the legacy tmpdir fixture (returns a py.path.local object) — it is deprecated and uses an older path API.
seeds.c: Use tmp_path which returns a pathlib.Path object, the standard modern Python path interface. tmp_path is function-scoped and unique per test. Use tmp_path_factory for session-scoped temporary directories. Operations: tmp_path / 'file.txt', (tmp_path / 'sub').mkdir().
seeds.s: deprecation warnings about tmpdir, or needing str() conversion to work with standard pathlib APIs.

seeds.t: Use pytest.raises as a Context Manager to Assert Exceptions
seeds.w: Wrapping code in try/except and manually failing if no exception is raised — verbose and misses the assertion that the correct exception type was raised.
seeds.c: Use with pytest.raises(ValueError) as exc_info: — the test fails if ValueError is not raised. Access exc_info.value for the exception instance and assert its message with match='regex pattern'. Use pytest.raises(Exception, match=r'expected message') for concise one-liners.
seeds.s: tests that pass when the wrong exception type is raised, or verbose manual try/except error-checking.

seeds.t: Register Custom Markers to Categorise and Filter Tests
seeds.w: Running the entire test suite when only integration tests or smoke tests are needed, or using ad-hoc string matching in test names to filter.
seeds.c: Define custom markers in pytest.ini or pyproject.toml: [tool.pytest.ini_options] markers = ['slow: marks tests as slow', 'integration: requires external services']. Apply with @pytest.mark.slow, then run subsets with pytest -m slow or pytest -m 'not slow'.
seeds.s: having to wait for a 10-minute test suite when only running quick unit tests, or PytestUnknownMarkWarning for undefined markers.

seeds.t: Use pytest-anyio or pytest-asyncio for Async Tests
seeds.w: Defining async test functions without an async test runner — pytest does not run coroutines by default and the test will silently pass without actually executing the body.
seeds.c: Install pytest-asyncio and mark tests with @pytest.mark.asyncio, or configure asyncio_mode = 'auto' in pytest.ini. For anyio-based code, use pytest-anyio. Verify async tests actually run by asserting something that would fail if the body were skipped.
seeds.s: async tests always passing even when they contain failing assertions, because the coroutine was never awaited.

seeds.t: Use capsys to Assert on stdout and stderr
seeds.w: Patching sys.stdout with a StringIO object manually to capture print output — verbose and doesn't integrate with pytest's capture infrastructure.
seeds.c: Inject the capsys fixture: out, err = capsys.readouterr() after the code under test. Use capfd for file descriptor level capture (useful for C extension output). Use capsys.disabled() context manager to temporarily show output during debugging.
seeds.s: complex stdout patching code replaced by a two-line capsys readouterr call.

seeds.t: Use yield in Fixtures for Setup and Teardown
seeds.w: Using request.addfinalizer for teardown code in fixtures — verbose and separates setup from teardown logic.
seeds.c: Use yield to split a fixture into setup (before yield) and teardown (after yield): set up the resource, yield it, then clean up. Teardown runs even if the test fails. Multiple yields are not allowed; use contextlib.ExitStack for complex teardown ordering.
seeds.s: teardown finalizers scattered across the fixture and hard to match with their corresponding setup.

seeds.t: Use xfail for Known Bugs, skip for Inapplicable Tests
seeds.w: Commenting out failing tests or using skip for tests that should fail — masks real failures and makes it unclear whether the failure is expected.
seeds.c: Use @pytest.mark.xfail(reason='Bug #123') for tests that are expected to fail due to a known bug; pytest marks them as xfail (expected failure) and alerts when they unexpectedly pass. Use @pytest.mark.skip or skipif for tests genuinely not applicable to the current environment.
seeds.s: failing test hidden with skip, then fixed but never re-enabled, test suite giving false confidence.

seeds.t: Use pytest.approx for Floating-Point Comparisons
seeds.w: Asserting assert result == 0.3 for floating-point arithmetic — IEEE 754 makes exact equality unreliable (0.1 + 0.2 != 0.3).
seeds.c: Use assert result == pytest.approx(0.3) which compares within a relative tolerance of 1e-6 by default. Set abs=1e-9 for absolute tolerance or rel=1e-3 for a different relative tolerance. Works with lists, dicts, and numpy arrays.
seeds.s: test intermittently failing on different platforms because floating-point rounding differs.

seeds.t: Use Factory Fixtures for Flexible Object Creation
seeds.w: Creating one rigid fixture that returns a specific object — tests that need a slightly different object must either duplicate the fixture or use complex parametrize.
seeds.c: Return a factory function from the fixture: @pytest.fixture def make_user(): def _make(name='Alice', role='user', **kwargs): return User(name=name, role=role, **kwargs); return _make. Tests call make_user(role='admin') for custom variants. Useful for database model factories alo…
seeds.s: dozens of near-identical fixtures (admin_user, inactive_user, verified_user) that could be one factory.

seeds.t: Use Plain assert — pytest Rewrites It for Rich Output
seeds.w: Using self.assertEqual, self.assertTrue, or custom assertion helpers to get useful failure messages — these were necessary for unittest but are not for pytest.
seeds.c: Write plain Python assert statements: assert result == expected, assert 'key' in response_data, assert isinstance(obj, MyClass). pytest's assertion rewriting introspects the AST of assert statements and produces detailed diffs showing actual vs expected values automatically.
seeds.s: writing helper functions to format assertion messages that pytest's assert rewriting provides for free.

seeds.t: Tests Must Not Depend on Execution Order
seeds.w: Tests that pass individually but fail when run together because they share mutable module-level state or a database that a previous test modified.
seeds.c: Each test must set up its own state via fixtures and clean up via yield teardown or database transactions that roll back. Use pytest-randomly to run tests in random order in CI and expose ordering dependencies. Use --forked (pytest-forked) to run in subprocess isolation for trul…
seeds.s: test suite passes with pytest tests/foo.py but fails with pytest (full suite).

seeds.t: Configure pytest in pyproject.toml Not Setup.cfg
seeds.w: Spreading pytest configuration across multiple files (setup.cfg, tox.ini, pytest.ini) or not configuring it at all — default settings cause warnings about unregistered markers and missing testpaths.
seeds.c: Centralise configuration in pyproject.toml under [tool.pytest.ini_options]: set testpaths = ['tests'], register markers, set asyncio_mode, and configure filterwarnings. A single source of truth for tooling configuration.
seeds.s: PytestUnknownMarkWarning on every test run, or test discovery finding files outside the tests directory.

seeds.t: Use side_effect for Exceptions and Dynamic Return Values
seeds.w: Trying to make a mock raise an exception by setting return_value to an Exception instance — return_value is returned as a value, not raised.
seeds.c: Set mock.side_effect = ValueError('not found') to make the mock raise when called. Use a list as side_effect to return different values on successive calls: [first_result, second_result, Exception('fail')]. Use a function as side_effect for dynamic responses based on arguments.
seeds.s: mock returning an Exception object instead of raising it, test passing when it should fail.

seeds.t: Run Doctests Alongside pytest Tests
seeds.w: Maintaining a separate doctest run script or ignoring doctests in library code — examples in docstrings drift from reality as code changes.
seeds.c: Add --doctest-modules to pytest options to collect and run doctests from all modules as part of the normal test run. Failing doctests are reported like failing tests. Use # doctest: +ELLIPSIS or # doctest: +NORMALIZE_WHITESPACE directives for flexible matching.
seeds.s: doctests in module files never run in CI, making code examples in docs silently incorrect.

seeds.t: Rerun Only Failed Tests With --lf During Development
seeds.w: Running the entire test suite after fixing a bug to confirm the fix — slow feedback loop when the suite takes minutes.
seeds.c: Use pytest --lf (last-failed) to run only the tests that failed in the previous run. Use --ff (failed-first) to run failed tests first then the rest. Use -x (exit on first failure) during active debugging. Combine: pytest --lf -x for rapid iteration.
seeds.s: waiting two minutes for a full suite run after every small code change during TDD.

seeds.t: Measure Branch Coverage Not Just Line Coverage
seeds.w: Targeting 100% line coverage and considering tests complete — a line with an if/else can be covered with one branch untested.
seeds.c: Run with pytest --cov=src --cov-branch --cov-report=html to measure branch coverage. A covered line with an uncovered branch is reported distinctly. Aim for high branch coverage on business logic; line coverage is a floor, not a ceiling. Configure omit patterns in .coveragerc to…
seeds.s: 95% line coverage but missing the else branch of a critical conditional.

seeds.t: Set PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 for Real-Time Log Output
seeds.w: Running a Python process in Docker without PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 — Python buffers stdout in non-TTY environments (which includes Docker containers), so log output may not appear for minutes or may be lost entirely if the container crashes.
seeds.c: Set ENV PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 in the Dockerfile, or pass -u to Python: python -u app.py. This forces unbuffered stdout and stderr, making logs appear immediately in docker logs and in the orchestrator's log stream.
seeds.s: container showing no log output during a long operation, then a flood of buffered logs just before it crashes.

seeds.t: Set PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 to Skip .pyc Files
seeds.w: Leaving Python to write .pyc bytecode cache files inside a Docker container — .pyc files are keyed to the source file's modification time and inode, which are meaningless in an immutable container image. They waste image space and cause confusing behaviour in mounted development…
seeds.c: Set ENV PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 in the Dockerfile to prevent Python from writing .pyc files. Also add *.pyc and __pycache__/ to .dockerignore. The small startup time advantage of .pyc is negligible for long-running server processes.
seeds.s: Docker image larger than expected from .pyc files, or stale bytecode being served from a development volume mount.

seeds.t: Copy requirements.txt Before Source for Layer Caching
seeds.w: Copying the entire project directory before running pip install — any change to any source file invalidates the pip install layer, causing a full reinstall on every build.
seeds.c: COPY requirements.txt .; RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt; COPY . . The pip install layer is cached as long as requirements.txt hasn't changed. Use pip install --no-cache-dir to avoid storing the pip download cache in the image layer. For poetry or pipenv, copy…
seeds.s: a one-line source code change triggering a 3-minute pip install because the layer order was wrong.

seeds.t: Do Not Create a Virtualenv Inside a Docker Container
seeds.w: Running python -m venv .venv inside a Docker build and installing into the virtualenv — the container filesystem is already isolated; a virtualenv adds a layer of indirection with no benefit.
seeds.c: Install packages directly into the system Python: pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt. If you need isolation within a multi-stage build (e.g. to prevent dev deps reaching the final image), use separate FROM stages rather than a virtualenv. The only exception: using a …
seeds.s: unnecessarily complex Dockerfiles activating virtualenvs inside containers that already provide isolation.

seeds.t: Prefer python:slim Over python:alpine for Most Apps
seeds.w: Using python:alpine to minimise image size — Alpine uses musl libc instead of glibc; many Python packages (numpy, pandas, Pillow, psycopg2) ship glibc-compiled wheels that don't work on musl and must be compiled from source, massively increasing build time and image size.
seeds.c: Use python:3.12-slim (Debian-slim, glibc) as the base for most Python apps — it's much smaller than the full image and compatible with all PyPI wheels. Use alpine only for pure-Python projects with no compiled extensions. Final image size is often smaller with slim because wheel…
seeds.s: pip install failing with 'no matching distribution' or taking 15 minutes to compile C extensions on Alpine.

seeds.t: Use Multi-Stage Builds to Separate Build and Runtime Dependencies
seeds.w: Installing build tools (gcc, python3-dev, libpq-dev) in the final image just to compile a Python extension — leaves compilers and headers in the production image, increasing attack surface and size.
seeds.c: In a builder stage: install build tools, compile extensions. In the final stage: copy only the installed packages from the builder: COPY --from=builder /usr/local/lib/python3.12/site-packages /usr/local/lib/python3.12/site-packages. The final image contains only the runtime and …
seeds.s: production image containing gcc and development headers because psycopg2 needed them to compile, adding 200 MB unnecessarily.

seeds.t: Use Gunicorn or Uvicorn as the Production Entry Point
seeds.w: Running Flask or FastAPI with their built-in development servers in Docker (flask run, uvicorn --reload) — development servers are single-threaded, not production-hardened, and reload on file changes is useless in a container.
seeds.c: For WSGI (Flask/Django): CMD ["gunicorn", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000", "--workers", "4", "myapp:app"]. For ASGI (FastAPI): CMD ["uvicorn", "myapp:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000", "--workers", "4"]. Or gunicorn with uvicorn worker: gunicorn -w 4 -k uvicorn.workers.Uvicor…
seeds.s: Docker container only handling one request at a time because Flask's development server is single-threaded.

seeds.t: Handle PID 1 Correctly for Graceful SIGTERM Shutdown
seeds.w: Running Python directly as PID 1 in the container without a signal forwarder — PID 1 doesn't receive SIGTERM from Docker by default (unless explicitly coded to handle it), so docker stop sends SIGKILL after the timeout, killing in-flight requests.
seeds.c: Use exec form CMD (not shell form): CMD ["gunicorn", "app:app"]. exec form makes gunicorn PID 1 which forwards signals correctly. Or add tini as an init process (--init flag or docker run --init) which properly forwards signals to child processes. Set gunicorn's --timeout and --…
seeds.s: docker stop taking 10 seconds to kill the container because SIGTERM wasn't received by the app.

seeds.t: Run Python Applications as a Non-Root User
seeds.w: Running the Python application as root inside the container — unnecessary privilege that becomes dangerous if combined with a volume mount or container escape.
seeds.c: Add a non-root user in the Dockerfile: RUN useradd --create-home appuser; USER appuser. Ensure application files are readable by appuser: COPY --chown=appuser:appuser . /app. For gunicorn, non-root is standard practice. The application should not need to bind to ports below 1024…
seeds.s: container security scanner reporting root-running process, or accidentally overwriting host files via a mis-configured bind mount as root.

seeds.t: Write a Python-Specific .dockerignore
seeds.w: Omitting .dockerignore for a Python project — Docker sends .venv (potentially gigabytes), __pycache__, .pytest_cache, .git, and local .env files in the build context, slowing the build and risking secret exposure.
seeds.c: Include in .dockerignore: .venv, __pycache__, *.pyc, *.pyo, .pytest_cache, .mypy_cache, .ruff_cache, dist, *.egg-info, .git, .env, .env.*, *.log, coverage.xml, .coverage. Keep it in version control. Check context size with docker build --no-cache 2>&1 | grep 'Sending build conte…
seeds.s: docker build printing 'Sending build context: 1.2GB' because the .venv directory is included.

seeds.t: Use psycopg2-binary in Docker, Not psycopg2
seeds.w: Installing psycopg2 (source package) in a Docker image based on python:slim without installing libpq-dev and gcc first — the C extension compilation fails.
seeds.c: Either: (a) use psycopg2-binary which bundles the libpq shared library and requires no system dependencies — ideal for most Docker use cases; or (b) install build dependencies in a builder stage, compile psycopg2 from source, and copy the .so to the final image. Do not use psyco…
seeds.s: pip install psycopg2 failing with 'pg_config not found' in a slim Docker image.

seeds.t: Add a Health Check Endpoint to Python Web Apps in Docker
seeds.w: Using HEALTHCHECK CMD curl localhost:8000 which hits application logic and can return 500 during startup — the orchestrator marks the container unhealthy before the app is ready.
seeds.c: Add a dedicated /health route that returns 200 immediately without database or external calls: it just confirms the process is running. HEALTHCHECK --interval=10s --start-period=30s --timeout=3s CMD curl -f http://localhost:8000/health || exit 1. Set --start-period to the expect…
seeds.s: Kubernetes marking pods as unhealthy and restarting them during the initial model load or migration phase.

seeds.t: Mutable default argument is shared across calls
seeds.w: def func(items=[]): items.append(x) — the default list is created once and shared.
seeds.c: def func(items=None): if items is None: items = [].
seeds.s: function accumulates values from previous calls.

seeds.t: Lambda closures in loops bind late
seeds.w: [lambda: i for i in range(5)] — all lambdas capture the variable i, returning its final value.
seeds.c: use a default argument: [lambda i=i: i for i in range(5)].
seeds.s: all lambdas return the same value.

seeds.t: is checks identity not equality
seeds.w: using 'is' to compare values — 'is' checks whether two names point to the exact same object.
seeds.c: use == for value comparison. Exception: use 'is None' and 'is not None' explicitly — None is a singleton.
seeds.s: comparison returns False for equal values that happen to be different objects.

seeds.t: Blocking calls inside async functions stall the event loop
seeds.w: calling time.sleep(), requests.get(), or any synchronous blocking I/O inside an async function — blocks the entire event loop.
seeds.c: use await asyncio.sleep(), aiohttp/httpx instead of requests, await asyncio.to_thread(func) for blocking code.
seeds.s: async server becomes unresponsive during long operations.

seeds.t: Generators are single-use and silently exhaust
seeds.w: iterating a generator twice expecting the same results — generators return nothing on subsequent iteration without raising an error.
seeds.c: convert to a list if multiple iterations are needed: data = list(generator()).
seeds.s: second loop over a generator produces zero items silently.

seeds.t: copy.copy() does not copy nested objects
seeds.w: using copy.copy() on a nested data structure expecting nested objects to be independent — shallow copy creates a new outer object but nested objects remain shared.
seeds.c: use copy.deepcopy() for a fully independent copy.
seeds.s: modifying a nested object in the 'copy' also changes the original.

seeds.t: Forward reference type hints must be strings or use annotations import
seeds.w: using a class as a type hint before it is defined — Python evaluates annotations at definition time and raises NameError.
seeds.c: use a string literal 'ClassName' or add 'from __future__ import annotations' at the top.
seeds.s: NameError on import for self-referential types.

seeds.t: Exception variable is deleted after except block
seeds.w: accessing the exception variable outside the except block — Python 3 deletes it at the end of the except clause to break reference cycles.
seeds.c: assign to a different variable inside the block: except Exception as e: err = e.
seeds.s: NameError when accessing the exception variable after the except block.

seeds.t: Cannot modify dict size while iterating
seeds.w: adding or removing keys from a dict inside a for loop iterating over it — raises RuntimeError.
seeds.c: iterate over a copy: for key in list(d.keys()), or collect keys to remove and delete after the loop.
seeds.s: RuntimeError mid-loop.

seeds.t: Walrus operator := assigns and returns in expressions
seeds.w: using a separate assignment line then referencing in the condition. Particularly useful in while loops, comprehensions, and if statements to avoid re-computation.
seeds.c: while chunk := f.read(8192): process(chunk). Or: if match := re.search(pattern, text): use(match).
seeds.s: verbose two-step assign-then-test patterns.

seeds.t: Use dataclasses for data-holding classes instead of manual __init__
seeds.w: writing __init__, __repr__, __eq__ manually for simple data classes.
seeds.c: from dataclasses import dataclass; @dataclass class Point: x: float; y: float. Generates __init__, __repr__, and __eq__ automatically. Use @dataclass(frozen=True) for immutable instances (also hashable). Use field(default_factory=list) for mutable defaults.
seeds.s: boilerplate __init__ methods that drift from the class attributes.

seeds.t: Use context managers for resource management — implement with __enter__/__exit__
seeds.w: manually calling cleanup in try/finally everywhere.
seeds.c: use 'with' for any resource that needs cleanup: files, locks, database connections, network sockets. To create a custom context manager: implement __enter__ and __exit__, or use @contextmanager from contextlib. __exit__ receives exception info — return True to suppress the excep…
seeds.s: resources not released on exception, or verbose try/finally patterns.

seeds.t: Use pathlib.Path instead of os.path for file operations
seeds.w: os.path.join(base_dir, 'subdir', 'file.txt') — verbose and platform-sensitive.
seeds.c: from pathlib import Path; path = Path(base_dir) / 'subdir' / 'file.txt'. Path objects support: path.read_text(), path.write_text(), path.exists(), path.stem, path.suffix, path.parent, path.glob('*.py').
seeds.s: platform-specific path separator bugs, verbose os.path chains.

seeds.t: Use logging not print for application output
seeds.w: print() for debug and diagnostic output in production code — cannot be disabled, lacks context, and has no severity levels.
seeds.c: import logging; logger = logging.getLogger(__name__). Use logger.debug(), .info(), .warning(), .error(). Configure level and handlers at application entry point. Libraries should never configure root logger — only add a NullHandler.
seeds.s: production applications flood stdout with debug prints, or no way to suppress output.

seeds.t: Python's GIL prevents true multi-core parallelism for CPU-bound code
seeds.w: using threading for CPU-bound parallel computation — threads compete for the GIL and run sequentially.
seeds.c: use multiprocessing for CPU-bound work (separate processes, no GIL). Use threading or asyncio for I/O-bound work where threads wait (GIL released during I/O). Python 3.13+ introduces experimental no-GIL builds.
seeds.s: multi-threaded CPU code is no faster than single-threaded.

seeds.t: Use enumerate() for index+value and zip() for parallel iteration
seeds.w: for i in range(len(items)): item = items[i] — verbose and error-prone.
seeds.c: for i, item in enumerate(items). For parallel lists: for a, b in zip(list_a, list_b). zip() stops at the shortest list — use itertools.zip_longest for equal-length enforcement. enumerate(items, start=1) for 1-based indexing.
seeds.s: off-by-one errors, IndexError from manual index management.

seeds.t: Wrap Inference in torch.no_grad() to Save Memory
seeds.w: Running model inference without torch.no_grad() — PyTorch builds a computation graph and stores intermediate activations for every forward pass, doubling memory usage unnecessarily.
seeds.c: Wrap evaluation and inference code with torch.no_grad(): or use the @torch.inference_mode() decorator (faster than no_grad in PyTorch 1.9+). This disables gradient tracking, reducing memory by 30–50% and speeding up inference.
seeds.s: GPU OOM during inference that doesn't happen during the same model's training, or slower-than-expected inference.

seeds.t: Call optimizer.zero_grad() Before Each Backward Pass
seeds.w: Omitting optimizer.zero_grad() in the training loop — PyTorch accumulates gradients by default; gradients from previous batches add to the current batch, causing incorrect parameter updates.
seeds.c: Call optimizer.zero_grad() (or zero_grad(set_to_none=True) for slightly better performance) before loss.backward() in every training step. The standard order is: zero_grad → forward → compute loss → backward → optimizer.step().
seeds.s: loss not converging or diverging due to accumulated gradient values from previous batches.

seeds.t: Switch Between model.eval() and model.train() Correctly
seeds.w: Forgetting to call model.eval() during validation/inference — layers like Dropout and BatchNorm behave differently in training and eval modes; Dropout randomly zeroes activations during training but is disabled in eval mode.
seeds.c: Call model.eval() before any validation loop or inference, and model.train() before the training loop. Use a context manager pattern: model.eval(); with torch.no_grad(): for inference.
seeds.s: validation metrics noisier than expected because Dropout is still active, or BatchNorm using batch statistics instead of running stats.

seeds.t: Move Tensors and Models to the Same Device Explicitly
seeds.w: Creating a model on GPU but loading input tensors on CPU, or hardcoding 'cuda' — operations fail if tensors are on different devices, and hardcoded device strings break CPU-only environments.
