seeds.c: Detect the device: device = torch.device('cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu'). Move the model: model.to(device). Move data in the training loop: inputs, labels = inputs.to(device), labels.to(device). Use .to(device) not .cuda() for portability.
seeds.s: 'Expected all tensors to be on the same device' RuntimeError when inputs and model are on different devices.

seeds.t: Set num_workers and pin_memory for DataLoader Performance
seeds.w: Using DataLoader with default num_workers=0 (synchronous data loading on the main process) — the GPU starves waiting for data to be loaded and preprocessed.
seeds.c: Set num_workers=4 (or number of CPU cores) to load data in parallel background workers. Set pin_memory=True when training on GPU to speed up CPU-to-GPU tensor transfers via pinned memory. Use persistent_workers=True to avoid worker restart overhead per epoch.
seeds.s: GPU utilisation below 50% because data loading is the bottleneck, visible in nvidia-smi.

seeds.t: Avoid In-Place Operations on Tensors With Gradients
seeds.w: Using in-place operations (tensor.add_(1), tensor[mask] = 0) on tensors that require gradients — modifies the tensor's data in-place, corrupting autograd's computation graph and causing incorrect or failing backward passes.
seeds.c: Use out-of-place operations: tensor = tensor + 1, result = tensor.clone(); result[mask] = 0. In-place ops are only safe on leaf tensors not in the computation graph, or on tensors explicitly detached with .detach().
seeds.s: 'RuntimeError: one of the variables needed for gradient computation has been modified by an inplace operation'.

seeds.t: Detach Loss From Graph to Prevent Memory Leaks
seeds.w: Appending loss to a list with losses.append(loss) — this retains the entire computation graph for every batch in memory, growing until OOM.
seeds.c: Detach the scalar value before storing: losses.append(loss.item()). Use loss.detach() when keeping tensor form is required. Similarly, any tensor accumulated across batches (for logging metrics) must be detached.
seeds.s: GPU memory growing continuously during training across epochs even though batch size is constant.

seeds.t: Save state_dict, Not the Entire Model Object
seeds.w: Saving the entire model with torch.save(model, 'model.pt') — serialises the class definition alongside weights, breaking when the model class is moved or refactored.
seeds.c: Save only the state dictionary: torch.save(model.state_dict(), 'model.pt'). Load by instantiating the model first, then calling model.load_state_dict(torch.load('model.pt', map_location=device)). Also save the optimizer state dict for resumable training.
seeds.s: model loading failing after refactoring or on a different machine because the class path changed.

seeds.t: Use Automatic Mixed Precision for Faster Training
seeds.w: Training entirely in float32 on modern GPUs — wastes memory bandwidth and compute on GPUs that have dedicated float16/bfloat16 tensor cores.
seeds.c: Use torch.cuda.amp: wrap the forward pass with torch.autocast(device_type='cuda', dtype=torch.float16) and use GradScaler to prevent float16 underflow in backward: scaler.scale(loss).backward(); scaler.step(optimizer); scaler.update(). Typically 1.5–3x speedup with no accuracy l…
seeds.s: training slower and using more VRAM than necessary on Ampere or newer NVIDIA GPUs.

seeds.t: Clip Gradients to Prevent Exploding Gradient Problems
seeds.w: Training RNNs, transformers, or deep networks without gradient clipping — large gradient values cause parameter updates to be enormous, destabilising training or producing NaN losses.
seeds.c: Add torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0) after loss.backward() and before optimizer.step(). The max_norm value (1.0 is common) clips the global L2 norm of all gradients. Monitor gradient norms during training to detect instability early.
seeds.s: loss going to NaN or inf after several steps, or training oscillating wildly.

seeds.t: Implement __len__ and __getitem__ for Custom Datasets
seeds.w: Not inheriting from torch.utils.data.Dataset, or implementing __getitem__ with slow Python operations (opening files per sample) inside a multi-worker DataLoader — each worker opens files independently, causing IO contention.
seeds.c: Subclass Dataset and implement __len__ and __getitem__. Pre-load all file paths (not data) in __init__; open and process files in __getitem__. Cache small datasets fully in __init__ with data already in tensors. Use memory-mapped files (np.memmap) for large datasets to avoid loa…
seeds.s: slow data loading with multiple workers despite having SSDs.

seeds.t: Call scheduler.step() After optimizer.step()
seeds.w: Calling scheduler.step() before optimizer.step() in the training loop — produces a 'detected call of lr_scheduler.step() before optimizer.step()' warning in PyTorch 1.1+ and updates the learning rate at the wrong point.
seeds.c: The correct order is: optimizer.zero_grad() → forward → loss.backward() → optimizer.step() → scheduler.step(). For epoch-based schedulers, call scheduler.step() once per epoch after the training loop.
seeds.s: deprecation warning about scheduler step order, or learning rate schedule not matching the expected schedule.

seeds.t: Detach and Move to CPU Before Converting to NumPy
seeds.w: Calling tensor.numpy() on a GPU tensor or a tensor with requires_grad=True — raises RuntimeError because numpy arrays can't represent CUDA memory or gradient tracking.
seeds.c: Chain: tensor.detach().cpu().numpy() to safely convert. Or use tensor.item() for scalar tensors. When converting back from numpy, use torch.from_numpy() which shares memory with the array (no copy) — be aware modifications to one affect the other.
seeds.s: 'Can't call numpy() on Tensor that requires grad' or 'can't convert a given Tensor to NumPy Array'.

seeds.t: Set All Seeds for Reproducible Training Runs
seeds.w: Not setting seeds, then being unable to reproduce a training run or debug a rare failure — randomness from Python, NumPy, PyTorch CPU and CUDA all contribute.
seeds.c: Set all relevant seeds: torch.manual_seed(42); torch.cuda.manual_seed_all(42); np.random.seed(42); random.seed(42). For full determinism, also set torch.backends.cudnn.deterministic=True and torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark=False (may slow training). Use torch.Generator for DataLo…
seeds.s: two runs with the same hyperparameters producing different results, making debugging impossible.

seeds.t: Call .contiguous() Before Operations Requiring Contiguous Memory
seeds.w: Performing view() or certain CUDA operations on a tensor that was transposed or sliced — non-contiguous tensors fail view() with 'non-contiguous tensor' error.
seeds.c: Call tensor.contiguous() before view() to create a contiguous copy if needed. Use reshape() instead of view() — reshape() calls contiguous() automatically when needed. Check with tensor.is_contiguous(). Transposing (tensor.T) creates non-contiguous tensors.
seeds.s: 'RuntimeError: view size is not compatible with input tensor's size and stride (at least one dimension spans across two contiguous subspaces)'.

seeds.t: Use Gradient Accumulation to Simulate Larger Batch Sizes
seeds.w: Reducing batch size to fit GPU memory without compensating — smaller batches have noisier gradients and often require learning rate tuning.
seeds.c: Accumulate gradients over N mini-batches before calling optimizer.step(): if (step + 1) % accumulation_steps == 0: optimizer.step(); optimizer.zero_grad(). Only call scheduler.step() at the same point. Scale the loss by 1/accumulation_steps to keep gradient magnitudes consistent…
seeds.s: GPU OOM with desired batch size, training with small batches producing worse results than larger batches.

seeds.t: BatchNorm Uses Running Stats in eval() Mode
seeds.w: Evaluating a model with very small batch sizes (1–4) while the model was trained with large batches — in train mode, BatchNorm normalises using the current mini-batch stats, which are noisy for small batches.
seeds.c: Call model.eval() for inference/validation — BatchNorm then uses its running mean and variance accumulated during training, giving stable results regardless of the evaluation batch size. For fine-tuning with small batches, consider freezing BatchNorm layers (layer.eval() for eac…
seeds.s: validation accuracy much lower than training accuracy with identical data, or noisy predictions on single-sample inference.

seeds.t: Use cuda.synchronize() for Accurate GPU Timing
seeds.w: Using Python's time.time() to measure GPU operation time — CUDA operations are asynchronous; the Python timer records only when the kernel was launched, not when it completed.
seeds.c: Wrap the timed region with torch.cuda.synchronize() before starting and after stopping the timer. Better: use torch.cuda.Event for GPU-native timing: start = torch.cuda.Event(enable_timing=True); end = torch.cuda.Event(enable_timing=True); start.record(); ... ; end.record(); tor…
seeds.s: GPU operations appearing to take microseconds because the timer measures only kernel launch time.

seeds.t: Use torch.compile for Easy Inference Speedup
seeds.w: Running a model in eager mode for inference when a compiled version would be significantly faster — torch.compile (PyTorch 2.0+) applies kernel fusion and other optimisations automatically.
seeds.c: Apply model = torch.compile(model) after model instantiation. The first run triggers compilation (slow); subsequent runs are faster. Use mode='reduce-overhead' for latency-sensitive inference. Use fullgraph=True to error if graph breaks occur. Not compatible with all model archi…
seeds.s: inference slower than necessary on PyTorch 2.0+ because torch.compile hasn't been adopted.

seeds.t: Use .detach() to Stop Gradient Flow, not no_grad
seeds.w: Using torch.no_grad() inside the forward pass to stop gradients from flowing through part of the network — no_grad disables gradient computation globally, not for a specific sub-graph.
seeds.c: Use tensor.detach() to stop gradient flow for a specific tensor or sub-network (e.g., stopping gradients into a frozen encoder in a two-part model): target = encoder(x).detach(). Use no_grad() for complete inference passes. In RL, detach target network outputs: target_q = target…
seeds.s: gradients flowing into a frozen encoder because detach() was forgotten, overwriting frozen weights.

seeds.t: Use includes or eager_load to prevent N+1 queries
seeds.w: User.all then user.posts in a loop — generates 1 query for users and N queries for posts.
seeds.c: User.includes(:posts).all loads posts in one additional query; User.eager_load(:posts) uses a LEFT JOIN. Use the Bullet gem in development to detect N+1s automatically. Use .joins for filtering (non-SELECT) and .includes for loading associated data for display.
seeds.s: page with 50 users makes 51 database queries where 2 would suffice.

seeds.t: Always use strong parameters — never pass params directly to model
seeds.w: User.create(params[:user]) — allows attackers to mass-assign any attribute, including admin: true.
seeds.c: define a private method returning permitted params: def user_params; params.require(:user).permit(:name, :email, :password); end, then User.create(user_params). Permit only the attributes a user should set. Use permit! only in admin controllers where you trust the source.
seeds.s: privilege escalation attack where a user sets admin: true by including it in the form POST.

seeds.t: Use after_commit for side effects, not after_save
seeds.w: triggering an email send or background job in after_save — if the transaction rolls back after the callback fires, the job runs for a record that doesn't exist.
seeds.c: use after_commit (or after_create_commit, after_update_commit) which fires only after the database transaction is committed. For jobs, use deliver_later in after_commit. Active Job's after_commit option handles this automatically.
seeds.s: email sent or job enqueued for a record that was subsequently rolled back because a validation failed later in the same transaction.

seeds.t: Define scopes as lambdas to evaluate conditions at call time
seeds.w: scope :recent, where('created_at > ?', 1.week.ago) — this evaluates 1.week.ago once when the class loads, not when the scope is called.
seeds.c: scope :recent, -> { where('created_at > ?', 1.week.ago) }. The lambda is evaluated each time the scope is called. This applies to any scope that uses dynamic values like Time.now, current_user, or random().
seeds.s: a scope using a relative time works correctly for the first request of the day but uses a stale timestamp from boot time for all subsequent requests.

seeds.t: Write reversible migrations using change or up/down
seeds.w: writing a migration with only a change method that performs an irreversible operation (drop_table, remove_column) — rails db:rollback fails with IrreversibleMigration error.
seeds.c: use the reversible block: reversible { |dir| dir.up { execute '...' }; dir.down { execute '...' } }. Or define explicit up and down methods. For destructive operations, always provide the down that recreates the structure. For column removal, specify the column type in the down:…
seeds.s: rollback fails mid-deployment because a migration has no down path.

seeds.t: Use counter_cache to avoid COUNT queries for associations
seeds.w: calling post.comments.count in a view — executes a COUNT(*) SQL query per post.
seeds.c: add a counter cache column: add_column :posts, :comments_count, :integer, default: 0, null: false. Declare on the association: belongs_to :post, counter_cache: true. Rails maintains comments_count automatically on create/destroy. Access with post.comments_count (no query). Run r…
seeds.s: page displaying 100 posts each with comment counts runs 101 queries.

seeds.t: Define enums with hash syntax to control database values explicitly
seeds.w: enum status: [:draft, :published, :archived] — positions are implicit integers; inserting a new status in the middle renumbers existing values in the database.
seeds.c: use hash syntax: enum status: { draft: 0, published: 1, archived: 2 }. Values are explicit and stable regardless of array position. Always add new enum values at the end or with an explicit unused integer. Prefix enum methods to avoid name clashes: enum status: { ... }, prefix: …
seeds.s: adding a new enum value in the middle silently corrupts existing rows because their integer values now map to different labels.

seeds.t: Extract complex business logic into service objects
seeds.w: putting complex multi-step business logic in controller actions or ActiveRecord callbacks — controllers become hundreds of lines, callbacks are hard to test in isolation.
seeds.c: create plain Ruby objects (POROs) in app/services/: class OrderProcessor; def initialize(order); def call; ... end. Call from the controller: result = OrderProcessor.new(order).call. Service objects are easy to unit test without the full Rails stack. Use a Result object or raise…
seeds.s: controller action with 50 lines of business logic that can't be unit tested without making HTTP requests.

seeds.t: Polymorphic associations require special handling in joins
seeds.w: Comment.joins(:commentable) where commentable is polymorphic — Rails raises an error because it doesn't know which table to join.
seeds.c: to query across a polymorphic association, use raw SQL in joins or query specific types: Comment.where(commentable_type: 'Post').joins('INNER JOIN posts ON posts.id = comments.commentable_id'). For searching all commentable types, use two queries and merge. Add a database index …
seeds.s: EagerLoadPolymorphicError or join failure when trying to join on a polymorphic belongs_to.

seeds.t: Wrap multi-step operations in transactions for atomicity
seeds.w: performing multiple related database mutations without a transaction — if the second operation fails, the first is committed, leaving data in an inconsistent state.
seeds.c: use ActiveRecord::Base.transaction { user.save!; account.debit!(amount); ledger.record!(amount) }. Any exception within the block rolls back all changes. Use save! and bang methods inside transactions to raise on failure. Nested transactions use savepoints by default.
seeds.s: a payment deducts from a balance but a subsequent ledger write fails, leaving the account balance wrong.

seeds.t: Configure retry and discard for idempotent background jobs
seeds.w: allowing jobs to fail silently or retry infinitely — transient failures are retried forever consuming worker capacity; non-retriable failures clog the queue.
seeds.c: declare retry_on NetworkError, wait: :exponentially_longer, attempts: 5 and discard_on PermanentError. Make jobs idempotent so retries don't cause duplicate side effects: check if the work is already done before doing it. Use a unique job ID to prevent duplicate enqueuing. Monit…
seeds.s: a flaky third-party API causes jobs to retry thousands of times, or a bad record causes a job to fail permanently and fill the queue.

seeds.t: Use select to fetch only needed columns from large tables
seeds.w: User.all when only user.name and user.email are used — selects all columns including large text, binary, or JSON columns, wasting memory and bandwidth.
seeds.c: User.select(:id, :name, :email) returns ActiveRecord objects with only those attributes populated. For read-only use, User.select(:name, :email).map { |u| [u.name, u.email] } or pluck(:name, :email) for raw arrays. pluck is fastest for simple scalar extraction.
seeds.s: large text or blob columns loaded for every user in a list view when only the name is displayed.

seeds.t: Use Turbo Streams to update DOM without full page reloads
seeds.w: responding to form submissions with JavaScript fetch and manual DOM manipulation — requires client-side templating logic duplicating server-side views.
seeds.c: respond to POST/PATCH with Turbo Stream format: respond_to { |f| f.turbo_stream { render turbo_stream: turbo_stream.replace('user_1', partial: 'user', locals: { user: @user }) } }. Turbo Streams support append, prepend, replace, update, remove, before, and after actions. Works w…
seeds.s: SPA-style partial updates implemented with custom JavaScript when Turbo Streams would achieve the same result server-rendered.

seeds.t: Use Concerns to extract shared model behaviour, not unrelated code
seeds.w: dumping unrelated methods into a Concern just to reduce a model's line count — the concern becomes a junk drawer.
seeds.c: Concerns should encapsulate a coherent, named capability: module Searchable, module Taggable, module Auditable. A model including three concerns should read like a sentence: 'this model is Searchable, Taggable, and Auditable'. Include in multiple models only if the behaviour gen…
seeds.s: a Concern named UserHelpers containing 20 unrelated methods.

seeds.t: Use Russian Doll caching to cache nested partials efficiently
seeds.w: cache-invalidating the outer partial whenever any nested record changes — cache busting propagates to large fragments unnecessarily.
seeds.c: cache each partial at every level of nesting with the record's cache_key_with_version: cache @post do; cache @post.comments do; ... end. Each level has its own cache entry keyed to its record version. When a comment changes, only the comment partial and the post partial (which i…
seeds.s: editing one comment invalidates the entire page cache when only the comment's fragment should expire.

seeds.t: has_many :through enables rich join models with extra attributes
seeds.w: has_and_belongs_to_many for many-to-many relationships that need extra attributes on the join — HABTM join tables cannot have extra columns accessible from Rails.
seeds.c: create an explicit join model: Membership with user_id, group_id, and role: string. Declare has_many :memberships, has_many :groups, through: :memberships on User. Access join attributes: user.memberships.find_by(group:).role. Use HABTM only for pure join tables with no extra at…
seeds.s: trying to store metadata on a HABTM join and discovering the join model is inaccessible.

seeds.t: CSRF authenticity token is required for all state-changing requests
seeds.w: making non-GET requests from JavaScript without including the CSRF token — Rails rejects them with ActionController::InvalidAuthenticityToken (HTTP 422).
seeds.c: include the token in fetch headers: headers: { 'X-CSRF-Token': document.querySelector('meta[name=csrf-token]').content }. In Axios, configure a default header. For API-only controllers, use protect_from_forgery with: null or skip_before_action :verify_authenticity_token for rout…
seeds.s: AJAX POST returns 422 Unprocessable Entity because the CSRF token wasn't included in the request headers.

seeds.t: File names must match class names exactly under Zeitwerk autoloading
seeds.w: naming a file app/services/process_order.rb with class ProcessOrder — Zeitwerk expects process_order.rb to define ProcessOrder (inflected), but naming mismatches or acronyms (HTTPClient vs. HttpClient) cause LoadError.
seeds.c: follow the one-file-one-class convention. For acronyms, configure the inflector: Rails.autoloaders.main.inflect { |inflector| inflector.acronym 'HTTP' }. For namespaced classes, mirror the module structure in directories: app/services/payments/processor.rb defines Payments::Proc…
seeds.s: uninitialized constant error in production because Zeitwerk couldn't find a class due to a naming mismatch.

seeds.t: Always add an index when adding a foreign key column
seeds.w: add_reference :comments, :post without add_index — foreign key columns without indexes make JOIN and WHERE queries do full table scans.
seeds.c: add_reference :comments, :post, foreign_key: true, index: true (index: true is the default in add_reference). For existing columns, add_index :comments, :post_id explicitly. Add composite indexes for common WHERE combinations: add_index :orders, [:user_id, :status].
seeds.s: queries filtering or joining on a foreign key column are slow because the column has no index.

seeds.t: Pass explicit locals to partials — avoid relying on instance variables
seeds.w: using @user inside a partial — this creates an implicit dependency on the controller's instance variable, making the partial impossible to reuse or test independently.
seeds.c: render the partial with explicit locals: render partial: 'user', locals: { user: @user } or the shorthand render 'user', user: @user. Inside the partial, use the local variable user. This makes partials self-contained, reusable, and testable in isolation.
seeds.s: a partial breaks when rendered from a different controller because the expected instance variable isn't set.

seeds.t: Apollo Cache Normalises by __typename + id
seeds.w: Expecting Apollo Client to automatically update all references to an object after a mutation — the cache only updates if the mutated object's __typename and id match a cached object exactly.
seeds.c: Ensure every query requests __typename and id (Apollo adds __typename automatically). When a mutation returns the updated object with the same id, Apollo updates all cached references automatically (automatic cache update). Always include the id field in mutation responses. Use …
seeds.s: mutation succeeding but the UI not reflecting the change because the returned object was missing id, preventing cache normalisation.

seeds.t: Update the Cache After Mutations That Add or Remove Items
seeds.w: Creating or deleting objects via mutation and expecting the list query to update automatically — cache normalisation updates individual objects but doesn't know to add/remove them from list queries.
seeds.c: Use the mutation's update option to manually update list queries: update(cache, {data}) { cache.modify({ fields: { todos(existing = []) { return [...existing, cache.writeFragment({data: newTodo, fragment})]; } } }) }. Or use refetchQueries: ['GetTodos'] to simply re-run the list…
seeds.s: newly created item not appearing in the list until page refresh despite the mutation succeeding.

seeds.t: Use optimisticResponse for Instant UI Updates
seeds.w: Waiting for the mutation network round trip before updating the UI — users see a delay between action and feedback.
seeds.c: Provide an optimisticResponse matching the expected mutation result shape: mutate({variables: {text}, optimisticResponse: {__typename: 'Mutation', createTodo: {__typename: 'Todo', id: 'temp-' + Date.now(), text, completed: false}}}). Apollo updates the cache immediately with the…
seeds.s: form submission showing a loading state for 500ms before the new item appears when optimistic response would make it instant.

seeds.t: Use skip Option to Conditionally Execute Queries
seeds.w: Conditionally calling useQuery inside an if statement — React hooks cannot be called conditionally, violating the rules of hooks.
seeds.c: Use the skip option: const {data} = useQuery(GET_USER, {skip: !userId, variables: {userId}}). When skip is true, the query is not executed and data is undefined. Use skipToken from Apollo Client 3.8+: useQuery(GET_USER, userId ? {variables: {userId}} : skipToken). For conditiona…
seeds.s: 'Rendered more hooks than during the previous render' error from calling useQuery inside a conditional branch.

seeds.t: Use Subscriptions for Real-Time, Polling for Periodic Refresh
seeds.w: Using pollInterval for updates that should be real-time, or subscriptions for data that just needs periodic refresh — subscriptions require WebSocket infrastructure and have more complexity overhead.
seeds.c: Use pollInterval for data that updates every few minutes and exact timing isn't critical: useQuery(QUERY, {pollInterval: 30000}). Pause polling when off-screen: stopPolling/startPolling or skip. Use useSubscription for truly real-time data (chat messages, live scores, collaborat…
seeds.s: WebSocket subscription set up for a dashboard that refreshes every 5 minutes, where polling is simpler and sufficient.

seeds.t: Distinguish Network Errors From GraphQL Errors
seeds.w: Checking only error from useQuery and missing GraphQL partial errors returned alongside data — a query can succeed at the network level but return both data and errors for different fields.
seeds.c: useQuery returns {data, loading, error, networkStatus}. error is set for network-level failures (status 4xx/5xx, connection failure). GraphQL field errors appear in data alongside partial data. Configure errorPolicy: 'all' to receive both partial data and errors. Use onError in …
seeds.s: component showing empty state because it only checked error (undefined) while actual field errors were in data.errors.

seeds.t: Use fetchMore for Cursor-Based Pagination
seeds.w: Re-running the query with a new page variable and replacing the results — loses previously fetched pages and causes layout shifts.
seeds.c: Use fetchMore to load additional pages: const {data, fetchMore} = useQuery(GET_ITEMS); fetchMore({variables: {cursor: data.items.pageInfo.endCursor}, updateQuery: (prev, {fetchMoreResult}) => ({...fetchMoreResult, items: {__typename: 'ItemConnection', edges: [...prev.items.edges…
seeds.s: 'Load more' replacing the current list with only the next page instead of appending to it.

seeds.t: Colocate Fragments With the Components That Use Them
seeds.w: Defining all query fields in the parent component's query and drilling props down to children — parent must know all fields children need, creating coupling.
seeds.c: Define a fragment in each component for the fields it needs: const USER_FIELDS = gql`fragment UserFields on User { avatar name email }`. Spread in the parent query: query { users { ...UserFields } }. Import the fragment: const USER_FIELDS = UserCard.fragments.user. Components de…
seeds.s: parent query listing 15 fields because child components need them, when fragment colocation would let each component declare its own requirements.

seeds.t: Use Reactive Variables for Local Client-Only State
seeds.w: Adding @client fields to the GraphQL schema and implementing local resolvers for simple UI state (selected filters, modal open/closed) — excessive boilerplate for local state.
seeds.c: Use reactive variables for local state that should update UI reactively: const filterVar = makeVar('all'). Read in queries: filterVar() or as a field policy. Update from events: filterVar('active'). Components that call filterVar() via useReactiveVar re-render when it changes. U…
seeds.s: Apollo local schema with @client and resolver functions for a simple isModalOpen boolean that a reactive variable handles in one line.

seeds.t: Use Apollo's SSR Support for Next.js Data Fetching
seeds.w: Running GraphQL queries client-side in a Next.js app and missing SSR benefits — pages show loading states before data, hurting Core Web Vitals and SEO.
seeds.c: Use @apollo/client's getDataFromTree or @apollo/experimental-nextjs-app-support for the App Router. In getStaticProps/getServerSideProps with Pages Router: create an Apollo client, run queries server-side, extract the cache with client.cache.extract(), pass as a prop, and hydrat…
seeds.s: Next.js pages showing loading spinners that SSR would have pre-populated with data.

seeds.t: Use Apollo DevTools to Inspect Cache and Queries
seeds.w: Debugging Apollo cache issues by adding console.log and running queries manually — slow iteration and poor visibility into cache state.
seeds.c: Install the Apollo Client DevTools browser extension. It provides: Cache Inspector (view the normalised cache, identify missing or stale data), Query Watcher (see all active queries, their variables, and results), Mutation Log (track mutations and optimistic responses), and Grap…
seeds.s: hours debugging a cache update issue that Apollo DevTools' Cache Inspector would have surfaced in minutes.

seeds.t: Apollo Deduplicates In-Flight Queries Automatically
seeds.w: Implementing manual deduplication logic for concurrent queries or worrying about multiple components triggering the same query simultaneously — Apollo handles this.
seeds.c: Apollo Client deduplicates identical in-flight queries by default — if two components mount simultaneously and run the same query with the same variables, only one network request is made; both receive the result. Disable with: new ApolloClient({queryDeduplication: false}) for c…
seeds.s: writing manual query deduplication logic that Apollo already provides, or being surprised to see only one network request for two identical concurrent queries.

seeds.t: Query by Role and Label, Not Test IDs
seeds.w: Adding data-testid='submit-button' to every element and querying with getByTestId — test IDs are invisible to users and screen readers; tests using them can pass while accessibility is broken.
seeds.c: Use getByRole('button', {name: /submit/i}) — this queries the accessible name that a screen reader would announce. Prefer: getByRole > getByLabelText > getByPlaceholderText > getByText > getByTestId. Role queries also verify the element is the correct semantic type. Reserve data…
seeds.s: tests passing but the button has no accessible name and is unusable with a screen reader.

seeds.t: Use userEvent Instead of fireEvent for Realistic Interactions
seeds.w: Using fireEvent.click(button) or fireEvent.change(input, {target: {value: 'text'}}) — fireEvent dispatches a single synthetic event; real user interactions trigger sequences (focus, keydown, input, keyup, change, blur).
seeds.c: Use import userEvent from '@testing-library/user-event'; const user = userEvent.setup(); await user.type(input, 'hello'); await user.click(button). userEvent v14 simulates the full sequence of events a real user produces, catching bugs that fireEvent misses. Always await userEve…
seeds.s: form validation tests passing with fireEvent even though the validation logic fires on blur, not on change.

seeds.t: Use findBy Queries for Elements That Appear Asynchronously
seeds.w: Using getByText('Welcome') immediately after triggering an async action (API call, state update) — getBy throws synchronously if the element isn't in the DOM yet.
seeds.c: Use findByText('Welcome') which returns a promise that resolves when the element appears (retries for up to 1000ms by default). findBy* = waitFor + getBy*. Use getAllBy*/findAllBy* for multiple elements. Use queryBy* only when asserting an element is absent: expect(queryByText('…
seeds.s: 'Unable to find element' error on a correct assertion because the component was still loading.

seeds.t: Create a Custom render That Wraps Providers
seeds.w: Wrapping every test manually with Redux Provider, Router, QueryClientProvider, ThemeProvider — verbose boilerplate repeated in every test file, and tests break when a new provider is added globally.
seeds.c: Create a custom render in a test-utils file: export function renderWithProviders(ui, options) { return render(ui, { wrapper: AllProviders, ...options }) }. Re-export everything from Testing Library and replace render with the custom version. Tests import from the test-utils file…
seeds.s: 15-line provider wrapper duplicated in every test file, or tests failing because a new global provider wasn't added to each one.

seeds.t: Resolve act() Warnings by Awaiting Async State Updates
seeds.w: Ignoring 'Warning: An update to X inside a test was not wrapped in act()' — this warning means state is being updated after the test assertion, potentially causing false positives or test pollution.
seeds.c: Wrap operations that cause state updates in act(). With userEvent and findBy queries, act() is handled automatically. For manual state triggers (timers, promises), use await act(async () => { ... }). Use waitFor(() => expect(...)) for assertions on asynchronously updated state. …
seeds.s: flaky tests that sometimes pass and sometimes fail based on timing, with act() warnings in the output.

seeds.t: Use within() to Scope Queries to a Specific Container
seeds.w: Using getByText('Edit') when multiple 'Edit' buttons exist in the document — getByText throws 'Found multiple elements' error.
seeds.c: Use within() to scope the query to a specific container: const row = within(screen.getByRole('row', {name: /alice/i})); row.getByRole('button', {name: /edit/i}). This is the Testing Library equivalent of querying within a specific DOM subtree. Combine with getAllByRole to get a …
seeds.s: 'Found multiple elements with the text: Edit' error that within() would resolve by scoping to the correct container.

seeds.t: Test Custom Hooks With renderHook
seeds.w: Testing a custom hook by creating a dummy component that uses it, then rendering that component — roundabout, produces coupling to the component's rendering.
seeds.c: Use renderHook from @testing-library/react: const { result } = renderHook(() => useCounter()); act(() => { result.current.increment(); }); expect(result.current.count).toBe(1). For hooks with context dependencies, pass a wrapper with providers. renderHook re-renders on prop chan…
seeds.s: custom hook tests tightly coupled to a throwaway component's rendering when renderHook would test the hook directly.

seeds.t: Use MSW to Mock API Calls at the Network Level
seeds.w: Mocking fetch or axios directly in tests with jest.spyOn or jest.mock — mocking at the module level couples tests to the implementation detail of which HTTP library is used.
seeds.c: Use Mock Service Worker (MSW) to intercept requests at the network level: server.use(http.get('/api/users', () => HttpResponse.json(mockUsers))). MSW works in both browser (Service Worker) and Node (msw/node) without changing production code. Handlers can be reset per-test to si…
seeds.s: tests breaking when switching from axios to fetch because mocks targeted the library import rather than the network request.

seeds.t: Test Behaviour, Not Implementation Details
seeds.w: Testing component state directly (wrapper.state()), testing internal methods, or asserting on CSS class names that encode internal state — these break when the implementation changes even if behaviour is preserved.
seeds.c: Test what the user sees and can interact with: does the error message appear? Does the button become disabled? Does the list update? Use Testing Library's accessible queries which reflect what a user perceives. Avoid enzyme's shallow rendering which exposes implementation detail…
seeds.s: tests failing after a refactor from useState to useReducer even though the component behaves identically to users.

seeds.t: Use waitFor for Assertions That Require Retrying
seeds.w: Adding arbitrary setTimeout delays in tests to wait for async updates — timing-dependent, slow, and flaky across different machines.
seeds.c: Use waitFor(() => expect(screen.getByText('Done')).toBeInTheDocument()) which polls the assertion until it passes or times out (default 1000ms). waitFor retries the entire callback on failure. Avoid putting multiple expectations in one waitFor — move all but the last outside. Us…
seeds.s: setTimeout(done, 500) in tests causing slow and intermittently failing test suites.

seeds.t: Use screen Queries Over Destructured render Queries
seeds.w: Destructuring query functions from render: const { getByText, getByRole } = render(<Component />) — if you need to add a query mid-test, you must destructure again or update the render call.
seeds.c: Use screen.getByText(), screen.getByRole() etc. — screen is globally available and always queries the latest rendered output. screen.debug() prints the current DOM state for debugging. screen makes it easy to search with Ctrl+F for the query name in the test file.
seeds.s: test maintenance friction from updating destructured query lists, or queries returning stale results from a prior render.

seeds.t: Override Context Values in Tests Without Mocking the Module
seeds.w: Mocking an entire context module with jest.mock to control the value in a test — replaces real context behaviour with a mock, potentially missing bugs in the provider.
seeds.c: Render the component inside a real provider with the desired test value: render(<ThemeContext.Provider value='dark'><Component /></ThemeContext.Provider>). For complex contexts, use the custom render wrapper approach with overridable defaults. Only mock context modules when the …
seeds.s: jest.mock of a context file replacing correct context behaviour with a hand-rolled stub that doesn't catch provider bugs.

seeds.t: Understand How Accessible Names Are Computed for getByRole
seeds.w: Using getByRole('button', {name: 'Icon'}) when the button contains only an SVG icon with aria-label='Close' — the accessible name comes from aria-label, not the element's text content.
seeds.c: Accessible names are computed from (in priority order): aria-labelledby → aria-label → element text content → title attribute. For icon buttons: add aria-label='Close' and query with {name: /close/i}. Use the browser's accessibility tree (Chrome DevTools → Accessibility panel) o…
seeds.s: getByRole('button', {name: /submit/i}) failing even though the button is visible, because the accessible name is computed differently.

seeds.t: Test Form Submission by Clicking the Submit Button
seeds.w: Testing form submission by calling fireEvent.submit(form) directly — bypasses the user interaction chain and can succeed even when the submit button is disabled or the form has validation errors.
seeds.c: Test forms the way users interact with them: fill fields with userEvent.type, then click the submit button with userEvent.click(screen.getByRole('button', {name: /submit/i})). This triggers field validation, the button's onClick handler, and the form's onSubmit in the correct or…
seeds.s: form submission test passing despite a broken onSubmit handler because fireEvent.submit bypassed the button's disabled state.

seeds.t: Wrap Components Using React Query in a Fresh QueryClient Per Test
seeds.w: Using a shared global QueryClient across tests — React Query caches data between tests, causing later tests to receive stale cached data from earlier tests.
seeds.c: Create a fresh QueryClient per test: const queryClient = new QueryClient({defaultOptions: {queries: {retry: false}}}); render(<QueryClientProvider client={queryClient}><Component /></QueryClientProvider>). Set retry: false to prevent React Query from retrying failed requests in …
seeds.s: tests passing in isolation but failing when run in sequence because cached API data from a previous test is used.

seeds.t: Stale closure in useEffect
seeds.w: reading state or props inside useEffect without including them in the dependency array — the effect captures a stale value from the first render.
seeds.c: include every value the effect reads in the deps array, or use useRef to hold a mutable reference.
seeds.s: effect always sees the initial value no matter how many times state updates.

seeds.t: Object or array in useEffect deps causes infinite loop
seeds.w: passing an object or array literal as a dependency — it creates a new reference every render, triggering the effect infinitely.
seeds.c: use a primitive value, useMemo to stabilise the reference, or move the object outside the component.
seeds.s: effect fires on every render, network calls loop, or 'Maximum update depth exceeded'.

seeds.t: useEffect callback cannot be async directly
seeds.w: async function passed directly as the useEffect callback — useEffect expects a cleanup function returned synchronously, not a Promise.
seeds.c: define an async function inside the effect and call it immediately: useEffect(() => { const run = async () => { await ... }; run(); }, []).
seeds.s: React warning about missing cleanup or unhandled Promise.

seeds.t: Using array index as key causes subtle bugs
seeds.w: using array index as the key prop in lists that can be reordered, filtered, or have items inserted or deleted.
seeds.c: use a stable unique identifier from the data (id, slug, uuid). Index keys cause React to reuse DOM nodes incorrectly, breaking input state, animations, and transitions.
seeds.s: input fields retain wrong values after list reorder, or animations play on wrong elements.

seeds.t: State reads are stale within the same event handler
seeds.w: reading state immediately after calling setState and expecting the new value — React batches updates and the state variable still holds the old value in the same synchronous block.
seeds.c: use the functional updater form setState(prev => prev + 1) when the new state depends on the current one.
seeds.s: counter increments by 1 instead of 2 when setState is called twice in one handler.

seeds.t: Undefined value prop switches input to uncontrolled
seeds.w: passing undefined or null as the value prop of an input — React treats this as uncontrolled, and later passing a defined value switches it to controlled, causing a React warning and unpredictable behaviour.
seeds.c: always initialise state with a defined value such as an empty string, not undefined.
seeds.s: 'A component is changing an uncontrolled input to be controlled'.

seeds.t: Cancel async operations in useEffect cleanup
seeds.w: starting a fetch or async operation in useEffect without cancelling it on cleanup — if the component unmounts before the operation completes, calling setState on an unmounted component causes a warning or stale update.
seeds.c: use an AbortController for fetch calls, or a boolean isMounted flag, and cancel in the cleanup function returned from useEffect
seeds.s: component re-renders on every parent render despite not receiving new props

seeds.t: Wrap lazy components in Suspense with fallback
seeds.w: using React.lazy() without wrapping the component in <Suspense> — React throws an error if a lazy component suspends with no boundary above it.
seeds.c: wrap <Suspense fallback={<Spinner />}> around any lazy-loaded or async component. Place boundaries at the right granularity — too high and the entire page shows a spinner; too low and you get a waterfall of small spinners.
seeds.s: 'A React component suspended while rendering, but no fallback UI was specified'.

seeds.t: Catch render errors with class-based Error Boundaries
seeds.w: expecting try/catch or useEffect to catch errors thrown during rendering — React render errors propagate up and unmount the entire tree unless an Error Boundary catches them.
seeds.c: create a class component implementing componentDidCatch and getDerivedStateFromError, wrap subtrees that may fail. Hooks cannot implement Error Boundaries; use a library like react-error-boundary for convenience.
seeds.s: entire app unmounts on a render error with no user-visible fallback.

seeds.t: StrictMode double-invokes effects in development only
seeds.w: assuming an effect runs exactly once on mount — in development it runs twice.
seeds.c: always write cleanup functions for effects; if the second run causes problems, the cleanup is missing. This only happens in development; production is unaffected.
seeds.s: fetch fires twice, subscriptions double-register, or counters increment by 2 in development.

seeds.t: Use forwardRef to expose DOM refs from custom components
seeds.w: passing a ref prop to a custom component and expecting it to reach the underlying DOM node — ref is not forwarded automatically and the component receives it as undefined.
seeds.c: wrap the component in React.forwardRef((props, ref) => ...) and attach the ref to the DOM element. In React 19, ref is passed as a regular prop and forwardRef is no longer needed.
seeds.s: ref.current is null when attaching a ref to a custom component.

seeds.t: Mark non-urgent state updates with startTransition
seeds.w: updating expensive-to-render state (large lists, heavy computations) in the same synchronous block as user input — the UI appears to freeze while React re-renders.
seeds.c: wrap the expensive state update in startTransition(() => setItems(filtered)), which marks it as interruptible and keeps input responsive. useTransition also provides an isPending flag to show loading states.
seeds.s: typing in a search box causes noticeable jank while a large list re-renders.

seeds.t: Server and client HTML must match during hydration
seeds.w: rendering content that differs between server and client — using Date, Math.random(), window checks, or browser-only APIs during the initial render.
seeds.c: guard browser-only code inside useEffect (which never runs on the server) or use the suppressHydrationWarning prop for intentionally dynamic content. For SSR frameworks, use dynamic() with ssr: false for purely client components.
seeds.s: 'Hydration failed because the initial UI does not match what was rendered on the server'.

seeds.t: React 19 use() reads promises and context in render
seeds.w: using useEffect + useState to load async data when a Suspense boundary is already in place.
seeds.c: pass the promise to use(promise) inside the component and wrap with <Suspense> to handle the loading state. Unlike hooks, use() can be called inside conditionals and loops.
seeds.s: boilerplate async state logic that could be replaced with use() and Suspense.

seeds.t: useReducer is better than multiple useState for related state
seeds.w: managing several related pieces of state with separate useState calls — updates become hard to coordinate and can result in partial state updates between renders.
seeds.c: group related state into a single useReducer with explicit action types. This makes state transitions predictable, testable, and easier to trace. Use useState for simple independent values; switch to useReducer when state updates are interdependent or involve complex conditions.
seeds.s: multiple setState calls in one handler, or state fields that must always be updated together.

seeds.t: Prefer TypeScript interfaces over PropTypes at runtime
seeds.w: using the prop-types package in a TypeScript project — it provides runtime validation but is redundant when TypeScript already enforces types at compile time and adds bundle weight.
seeds.c: define component props with TypeScript interfaces or type aliases; remove the prop-types dependency. Keep prop-types only for plain JavaScript projects where no build-time type checking exists.
seeds.s: duplicated type definitions maintained in both TypeScript and PropTypes, inconsistencies creeping in over time.

seeds.t: React attaches all events to the root — not individual nodes
seeds.w: calling event.stopPropagation() on a native DOM listener and expecting it to stop a React synthetic event — the events are on different levels.
seeds.c: handle cross-library event coordination at the React root level or use native addEventListener on the same element.
seeds.s: clicks propagate unexpectedly when mixing React and vanilla DOM event listeners.

seeds.t: Always Provide keyExtractor to FlatList
seeds.w: Omitting keyExtractor from FlatList — React Native falls back to the array index as the key, causing incorrect rendering, missed updates, and poor performance when items are reordered or removed.
seeds.c: Provide keyExtractor={(item) => item.id.toString()} using a stable, unique identifier from the item. Keys must be strings. Set removeClippedSubviews={true} on long lists for extra performance. Use getItemLayout when item height is fixed to skip measurement overhead.
seeds.s: list items showing stale data after updates, or items animating to wrong positions on reorder.

seeds.t: Use StyleSheet.create for Performance and Validation
seeds.w: Defining styles as inline objects: style={{flex: 1, color: 'red'}} — creates a new object on every render, causing unnecessary re-renders and skipping style validation.
seeds.c: Define styles with StyleSheet.create({container: {flex: 1}}) outside the component. StyleSheet.create validates prop names in development and sends style IDs to the native layer instead of the full object, reducing bridge traffic. Only use inline styles for dynamic values that c…
seeds.s: excessive re-renders in performance profiler, or typos in style props silently ignored.

seeds.t: Use .ios.js and .android.js for Platform-Specific Code
seeds.w: Using Platform.OS === 'ios' ? <IOSComponent /> : <AndroidComponent /> extensively inside components — couples platform logic into shared components, making both harder to maintain.
seeds.c: Create separate files Button.ios.js and Button.android.js; the Metro bundler automatically resolves the correct file for each platform. Use Platform.select({ios: value, android: value}) for simple value differences. Use Platform.OS checks only for small conditional values, not e…
seeds.s: component files with large Platform.OS conditional blocks that are better separated into platform files.

seeds.t: Use Reanimated 2+ for Complex or Gesture-Driven Animations
seeds.w: Using the built-in Animated API for complex gesture-driven animations — Animated runs on the JS thread and communicates across the bridge, causing jank on the 60fps requirement.
seeds.c: Use React Native Reanimated 2+ with useSharedValue and useAnimatedStyle for animations that run on the UI thread without bridge overhead. Use withSpring, withTiming, withDecay for smooth physics-based motion. Use Animated (with useNativeDriver: true) only for simple opacity/tran…
seeds.s: animations dropping frames during gestures, especially on lower-end Android devices.

seeds.t: Enable the Hermes JavaScript Engine for Better Performance
seeds.w: Running React Native with JavaScriptCore (the default before RN 0.70) — Hermes provides faster startup, lower memory use, and better performance through AOT bytecode compilation.
seeds.c: Hermes is enabled by default in React Native 0.70+. For older versions, enable in android/app/build.gradle: enableHermes: true. iOS: use_hermes! in Podfile. Verify with: console.log(typeof HermesInternal !== 'undefined'). Hermes debugger integrates with Chrome DevTools.
seeds.s: slow app startup time, high memory usage on older RN versions that haven't migrated to Hermes.

seeds.t: Use KeyboardAvoidingView to Handle the On-Screen Keyboard
seeds.w: Not handling the soft keyboard causing it to cover TextInput fields — users can't see what they're typing in forms.
seeds.c: Wrap forms with KeyboardAvoidingView behavior='padding' (iOS) or behavior='height' (Android). Use the keyboardVerticalOffset prop to account for headers. For scroll views with forms, use ScrollView's keyboardShouldPersistTaps='handled' to allow taps on buttons while keyboard is …
seeds.s: keyboard covering the active TextInput, or taps on buttons dismissed by the keyboard without triggering the action.

seeds.t: Use @react-native-async-storage/async-storage Not Core AsyncStorage
seeds.w: Importing AsyncStorage from 'react-native' — deprecated since RN 0.60 and will be removed; still works in some versions but shows deprecation warnings.
seeds.c: Install @react-native-async-storage/async-storage and import from that package. For sensitive data (tokens, credentials), use react-native-keychain (Keychain/Keystore backed). For large or relational data, use WatermelonDB or SQLite via expo-sqlite.
seeds.s: deprecation warnings for react-native AsyncStorage import, or confusion about where to store different types of app data.

seeds.t: Configure Deep Linking in React Navigation From the Start
seeds.w: Implementing navigation without deep link configuration and adding it later — retrofitting deep links into an existing navigation structure is complex and error-prone.
seeds.c: Configure linking in React Navigation's NavigationContainer: linking={{ prefixes: ['myapp://'], config: { screens: { Home: '', Profile: 'user/:id' } } }}. Configure URL schemes in Info.plist (iOS) and AndroidManifest.xml (Android). Test with Expo's URI scheme tester or npx uri-s…
seeds.s: app not opening to the correct screen from push notifications or marketing links.

seeds.t: Request Permissions at Runtime With react-native-permissions
seeds.w: Assuming permissions granted because they're in the manifest/Info.plist — iOS requires runtime permission requests; Android 6+ also requires runtime requests for dangerous permissions.
seeds.c: Use react-native-permissions to check and request permissions: check(PERMISSIONS.IOS.CAMERA) then request(PERMISSIONS.IOS.CAMERA). Request permissions contextually (when the feature is needed), not at app startup. Handle BLOCKED status with a prompt to open Settings.
seeds.s: camera or location feature failing silently because the runtime permission was never requested.

seeds.t: Clear Metro Cache When Facing Inexplicable Build Issues
seeds.w: Spending hours debugging a 'red screen' error that is actually a stale Metro bundler cache from a previous dependency change.
seeds.c: Run npx react-native start --reset-cache to clear Metro's cache. Also: watchman watch-del-all for Watchman cache, and rm -rf node_modules && npm install for dependency issues. For native build issues, run cd android && ./gradlew clean or cd ios && xcodebuild clean.
seeds.s: module not found errors after adding a package, or stale code executing after source changes.

seeds.t: Use react-native-fast-image for Performant Network Images
seeds.w: Using the built-in <Image> component for many network images — no disk caching, no prefetching API, and poor performance on lists with many remote images.
seeds.c: Use react-native-fast-image which uses SDWebImage (iOS) and Glide (Android) under the hood for aggressive disk and memory caching. Set priority={FastImage.priority.high} for above-the-fold images, preload with FastImage.preload(). Matches the performance of native image loaders.
seeds.s: network images reloading on every scroll, or slow image-heavy lists.

seeds.t: Use useWindowDimensions Hook, Not Dimensions.get
seeds.w: Calling Dimensions.get('window') at module level or in the component body — returns the dimensions at call time and doesn't update when the screen rotates or the window resizes (iPad Split View, foldables).
seeds.c: Use the useWindowDimensions() hook which re-renders the component on dimension changes. For class components or non-React contexts, add a Dimensions.addEventListener('change', handler). On tablets and iPad, design for dynamic dimensions.
seeds.s: layout not adapting to screen rotation or iPad Split View changes.

seeds.t: Prepare for the New Architecture With JSI and Turbo Modules
seeds.w: Relying heavily on third-party libraries that use the old bridge without checking New Architecture compatibility — React Native's New Architecture (JSI, Turbo Modules, Fabric) is the default in RN 0.74+.
seeds.c: Check that critical native modules support Turbo Modules (look for the TurboModuleRegistry API and codegen specs). Enable the New Architecture in your project and fix incompatible modules early. Use react-native-community libraries which typically have New Architecture support.
seeds.s: library incompatibilities when enabling New Architecture, or bridge-based modules performing poorly on the new renderer.

seeds.t: Use Pressable Instead of TouchableOpacity for New Code
seeds.w: Using TouchableOpacity, TouchableHighlight, or TouchableNativeFeedback for new touchable elements — these are legacy APIs that don't support the new Pressable API's press state styles and are harder to customise.
seeds.c: Use Pressable with the style prop as a function to apply press-state styles: <Pressable style={({pressed}) => [{opacity: pressed ? 0.5 : 1}]}>. Pressable supports hitSlop, onLongPress, and ripple effects via android_ripple.
seeds.s: using deprecated TouchableOpacity in new components when Pressable provides more flexibility.

seeds.t: Type Route Params With TypeScript in React Navigation
seeds.w: Accessing route.params.userId without TypeScript types — no autocomplete, easy to misspell param names, and runtime errors from missing params.
seeds.c: Define a ParamList type: type RootStackParamList = { Profile: { userId: string }; Home: undefined }. Use NavigationProp<RootStackParamList> and RouteProp<RootStackParamList, 'Profile'> in component props. This provides full type safety for navigate() calls and params access.
seeds.s: runtime crashes from misspelled or missing navigation params not caught at compile time.

seeds.t: Wrap App in GestureHandlerRootView for react-native-gesture-handler
seeds.w: Installing react-native-gesture-handler and importing it without wrapping the app in GestureHandlerRootView — gestures fail on Android or produce warnings about missing root view.
seeds.c: Import 'react-native-gesture-handler' at the top of index.js (before any other imports) and wrap the root component: <GestureHandlerRootView style={{flex: 1}}><App /></GestureHandlerRootView>. On iOS this is optional but required on Android for correct gesture handling.
seeds.s: swipe gestures not working on Android, or 'GestureHandlerRootView was not found in the view hierarchy' warning.

seeds.t: Use Expo Updates or CodePush for OTA JavaScript Updates
seeds.w: Submitting to app stores for every JavaScript-only change — review process takes days and users may not update immediately.
seeds.c: Use Expo Updates (EAS Update) or Microsoft CodePush to push JavaScript bundle changes over-the-air without a store submission. OTA updates are limited to JavaScript and assets only; native code changes still require a store update. Configure update checks on app foreground. Foll…
seeds.s: slow iteration cycle for bug fixes that only affect JavaScript, requiring full store review.

seeds.t: Memoize FlatList renderItem to Prevent Re-Renders
seeds.w: Defining renderItem inline in the FlatList component: renderItem={({item}) => <ItemCard item={item} />} — this creates a new function reference on every parent render, causing every list item to re-render.
seeds.c: Extract and memoize renderItem: const renderItem = useCallback(({item}) => <ItemCard item={item} />, []). Wrap ItemCard in React.memo to prevent re-renders when props haven't changed. Pass initialNumToRender and maxToRenderPerBatch tuned to the visible area.
seeds.s: scrolling through a list causing re-renders of all visible items on each frame.

seeds.t: Use SafeAreaView for Notch and Home Indicator Compatibility
seeds.w: Using a fixed top padding or not accounting for notches — content is hidden behind the iPhone notch/Dynamic Island or the Android status bar on edge-to-edge screens.
seeds.c: Wrap screen content in SafeAreaView from 'react-native-safe-area-context' (not the deprecated core one), or use the useSafeAreaInsets() hook for fine-grained control. Set edgeMode='all' for full edge protection. Configure the StatusBar component separately.
seeds.s: content clipped by notch or hidden behind system UI on newer iPhones or Android 15+ edge-to-edge.

seeds.t: Choose Expo Managed or Bare Workflow Based on Native Needs
seeds.w: Starting with the bare workflow for a simple app (needless complexity) or using Expo managed and then struggling to add a native module not in the Expo ecosystem.
seeds.c: Use Expo managed workflow (npx create-expo-app) if your dependencies are covered by Expo SDK and EAS — fastest development, no Xcode/Android Studio needed for most tasks. Eject to bare workflow (npx expo prebuild) when you need custom native code not available as an Expo module.…
seeds.s: mid-project decision to eject from managed workflow causing significant refactoring.

seeds.t: Cap Rate Is an Unlevered Property Yield — Not the Investor's Return on Equity
seeds.w: Using the cap rate as the investor's return on equity — cap rate = NOI / Value is an unlevered yield on the whole property. Leverage (positive or negative) changes the equity return above or below the cap rate.
seeds.c: Cap rate = NOI / Value (unlevered, pre-debt-service). Equity return depends on leverage: CoC = (NOI − debt service) / equity invested. Positive leverage: cap rate > loan rate → CoC > cap rate. Value = NOI / cap rate.
seeds.s: a valuation model that applies a target equity return as the cap rate without removing the effect of debt; an investment screener comparing cap rates to leveraged return hurdles.

seeds.t: NOI Excludes Debt Service and Capital Expenditures — It Is Not the Same as Cash Flow
seeds.w: Including debt service or capital expenditures in NOI, or treating NOI as the investor's cash flow — NOI = EGI − operating expenses only, before debt service, CapEx, or taxes. Cash flow = NOI − debt service.
seeds.c: NOI = EGI − operating expenses (taxes, insurance, mgmt, maintenance). Excludes debt service, CapEx, depreciation, income taxes. CFBT = NOI − debt service. Value = NOI / cap rate requires clean, financing-neutral NOI.
seeds.s: a proforma model that includes the mortgage payment as an operating expense and then applies a cap rate to the resulting understated 'NOI'; a comparison of two properties where one includes CapEx reserves in expenses and the other does not, making cap rates incomparable.

seeds.t: Cash-on-Cash Return Is a Single-Year Snapshot; IRR Accounts for All Cash Flows Including Sale
seeds.w: Treating cash-on-cash and IRR as equivalent return metrics — CoC is a single-year cash yield on equity (ignores appreciation and timing); IRR is the annualised return on the full investment including exit, accounting for the time value of all cash flows.
seeds.c: CoC = annual cash flow / equity (single-period, ignores exit). IRR = discount rate making NPV = 0 for all cash flows including sale proceeds (multi-period, time-value-aware). Use both: CoC for current yield, IRR for total return.
seeds.s: ranking investment opportunities by CoC alone and systematically ignoring value-add strategies with deferred income; a proforma that shows only CoC and omits the exit IRR.

seeds.t: Gross Rent Multiplier Ignores Vacancy, Expenses, and Debt — It Is a Screening Tool, Not a Valuation
seeds.w: Using GRM (Price / Gross Rent) as a valuation metric — GRM ignores vacancy, all operating expenses, and leverage. Two properties with equal GRM can have very different cap rates depending on their expense ratio.
seeds.c: GRM = Price / PGI — a rapid screening filter only. Always follow with full NOI analysis: NOI = EGI − operating expenses; Value = NOI / cap rate. GRM cannot substitute for expense-adjusted valuation.
seeds.s: a property screening tool that ranks opportunities by GRM and misses high-expense properties that appear attractively priced by GRM; a valuation model that multiplies gross rent by a market GRM to derive value without expense analysis.

seeds.t: In Real Estate DCF the Terminal Value Typically Represents 60–80% of Total Value — Exit Assumptions Dominate
seeds.w: Treating annual cash flow projections as the dominant DCF input — terminal value (NOI_exit / exit_cap_rate) typically represents 60–80% of total present value. A 1% exit cap rate change dominates any reasonable variation in annual cash flows.
seeds.c: PV = Σ(NOI_t / (1+r)^t) + (NOI_exit / exit_cap_rate) / (1+r)^T. Terminal value dominates. Always run two-way sensitivity: IRR vs exit cap rate and NOI growth. Exit cap rate ≈ entry cap rate + 25–50bps for hold-period uncertainty.
seeds.s: a proforma model with detailed year-by-year rent projections but a single hard-coded exit cap rate; an IRR sensitivity table that varies only the discount rate and holds the exit cap rate constant.

seeds.t: Physical Vacancy and Economic (Credit) Loss Are Distinct Line Items — Both Reduce Effective Gross Income
seeds.w: Using a single vacancy rate to capture all income loss — physical vacancy (empty units) and credit loss (non-paying tenants, concessions) are separate risks with different drivers. EGI = PGI − physical vacancy − credit loss − concessions.
seeds.c: EGI = PGI − vacancy loss − credit loss − concessions. Physical vacancy: unoccupied units. Credit loss: occupied but non-paying + loss-to-lease. Underwrite separately; stress-test each independently. Combined 5–10% is a shorthand, not a substitute.
seeds.s: a proforma that applies a single 5% vacancy factor and misses credit loss entirely; a market-rate multifamily underwritten with the same vacancy assumption as a Section 8 property.

seeds.t: Property Value Can Increase via Cap Rate Compression Even With Zero NOI Growth
seeds.w: Treating NOI growth as the only driver of property value appreciation — cap rate compression (lower market yields) independently increases value via Value = NOI / cap_rate, even with flat NOI. Both sources must be modelled separately.
seeds.c: Value = NOI / cap_rate. Cap rate compression alone: NOI flat, cap rate 7%→6% → value +16.7%. Approximate total return ≈ cap_rate + NOI_growth_rate − Δcap_rate. Decompose returns into income, NOI growth, and cap rate change.
seeds.s: a proforma that assumes constant exit cap rate equal to entry cap rate, inadvertently assuming zero cap rate change and systematically overstating return in rising-rate environments; a value-add model that attributes all appreciation to rent growth without isolating the cap rate…

seeds.t: LTV and DSCR Are Independent Loan Constraints — A Property Can Pass One and Fail the Other
seeds.w: Treating LTV as the only loan constraint — DSCR = NOI / debt service is an independent test. A low LTV property can fail DSCR (insufficient income); a high-DSCR property can fail LTV (over-leveraged). Both must be satisfied; take the min.
seeds.c: MaxLoan = min(Value × max_LTV, NOI / (DSCR_min × debt_constant)). LTV tests collateral; DSCR tests cash flow. Vacant land: good LTV, zero DSCR. Stabilised property at 80% LTV: may fail 1.25x DSCR if rates rise.
seeds.s: a loan sizing model that calculates only LTV and ignores cash flow coverage; an investment model where the buyer maximises loan amount from LTV without checking if the resulting debt service is covered by NOI.

seeds.t: Amortising Loans Build Equity via Principal Paydown; Interest-Only Loans Maximise Cash Flow but Build No Equity
seeds.w: Treating IO and amortising loans as equivalent except in monthly payment — IO maximises current cash flow and DSCR but builds no equity and creates balloon risk; amortising reduces cash flow but increases equity and reduces refinance risk.
seeds.c: IO payment = L × r. Amortising payment = L × r(1+r)^n/((1+r)^n−1). IO: higher CoC, no equity buildup, balloon risk. Amortising: lower CoC, declining balance, safer refinance. IO periods used in bridge/value-add to preserve cash flow during renovation.
seeds.s: a loan comparison that only shows monthly payment without projecting equity balance at exit; an IO underwriting that assumes permanent IO without modelling the balloon risk.

seeds.t: Non-Recourse Loans Limit Lender Recourse to the Property — Except for 'Bad Boy' Carve-Outs
seeds.w: Treating non-recourse loans as providing complete personal liability protection — bad boy carve-outs restore full personal recourse for fraud, misappropriation, voluntary bankruptcy, environmental violations, and physical waste.
seeds.c: Non-recourse: lender limited to collateral on default. Bad boy carve-outs: specific actions (fraud, misappropriation, voluntary BK, environmental) trigger full personal recourse. Always identify and flag carve-outs; non-recourse ≠ zero personal risk.
seeds.s: a loan term sheet parser that marks a loan as 'non-recourse' without flagging the bad boy carve-out provisions; a risk model that assigns zero personal liability to a non-recourse borrower who subsequently files voluntary bankruptcy.

seeds.t: Depreciation Recapture Is Taxed at 25% (Section 1250) — Separately From and In Addition to Capital Gains
seeds.w: Applying the long-term capital gains rate to the full gain on a real estate sale — accumulated depreciation is recaptured at up to 25% (Section 1250), separately from and in addition to capital gains tax on appreciation above original cost.
seeds.c: Gain split: Section 1250 recapture = accumulated depreciation × ≤25% rate. Section 1231 gain = appreciation above original cost × LTCG rate. After-tax proceeds require both calculations. Cost segregation increases current deductions but increases future recapture.
seeds.s: a disposition model that taxes the full gain at 20% LTCG rate and ignores the 25% recapture on accumulated depreciation; a cost segregation benefit analysis that shows current tax savings without quantifying the future recapture liability.

seeds.t: A 1031 Exchange Requires Property Identification Within 45 Days and Closing Within 180 Days — Missing Either Is Disqualifying
seeds.w: Treating 1031 exchange timelines as flexible — the IRS imposes hard deadlines: 45 calendar days to identify replacement property, 180 calendar days (or tax return due date) to close. Missing either is disqualifying and triggers full tax liability in the year of sale.
seeds.c: 45-day identification deadline from relinquished property closing (written notice to QI). 180-day closing deadline from same closing date. 3-property rule or 200% rule for identification. Constructive receipt of funds disqualifies exchange. No extensions.
seeds.s: a 1031 tracking system that starts the 45-day clock from the wrong event (e.g., contract signing rather than closing); exchange software that doesn't alert when only 10 days remain on the identification window.

seeds.t: A Lender's Title Policy Protects Only the Lender — the Owner Requires a Separate Owner's Policy
seeds.w: Assuming the lender's title policy at closing covers the owner — the lender's policy protects only the lender's loan balance. The owner needs a separate owner's title policy to protect their equity and ownership rights; it is not automatic.
seeds.c: Lender's title policy: protects lender up to loan balance, declines with amortisation, required by lenders. Owner's title policy: protects owner's full equity at purchase price, one-time premium, lasts indefinitely. Two separate policies, two separate premiums.
seeds.s: a mortgage closing cost calculator that shows only one title insurance line item; a real estate transaction management system that marks title insurance as 'complete' when only the lender's policy is ordered.

seeds.t: A Leasehold Estate Has a Finite Term and Declines in Value as the Ground Lease Expires
seeds.w: Valuing a leasehold property (improvements on leased land) the same as a fee simple — leasehold value declines as the ground lease term shortens; at expiration, land and often improvements revert to the landlord. Finite term = finite value.
seeds.c: Fee simple: perpetual ownership of land + improvements. Leasehold: use rights for lease term; value declines with remaining term; below-market ground rent increases leasehold value. Lenders typically require: loan term + 10 years ≤ remaining lease term.
seeds.s: a property valuation model that applies a cap rate to NOI without checking whether the property is on a ground lease and adjusting for the reversion; a loan underwriting model that approves a 30-year loan on a property with 25 years remaining on the ground lease.

seeds.t: Absorption Rate Measures the Pace of Leasing or Sales — Not Whether the Market Is Strong or Weak
seeds.w: Judging market health from absorption rate alone — absorption measures the pace of leasing/sales, not market strength. Low absorption + low supply = healthy; high absorption + higher new supply = oversupply. Always compare absorption to supply pipeline.
seeds.c: Absorption rate = units leased per period. Months of supply = inventory / absorption; <4 months = landlord/seller market; >6 months = tenant/buyer market. Net absorption = leased − vacated. Context: compare to new supply pipeline, not just the rate.
seeds.s: a market dashboard that displays absorption rate in isolation and labels markets as 'hot' or 'cold' purely based on the rate number; a demand model that treats high gross absorption as evidence of strong market fundamentals without netting out move-outs.

seeds.t: Land Appreciates; Improvements Depreciate and Must Be Reinvested — Separating Them Is Essential for Long-Term Analysis
seeds.w: Projecting a uniform appreciation rate across total property value — land appreciates (non-reproducible, location-driven); improvements depreciate (physical deterioration, obsolescence) and require CapEx reinvestment. Only improvements are tax-depreciable.
seeds.c: Value = land + improvements. Land: non-depreciable, appreciates with use/location; can be 50–80% of value in prime markets. Improvements: depreciate (27.5yr residential, 39yr commercial), require CapEx maintenance. Separate for tax, appraisal, and redevelopment analysis.
seeds.s: applying a single 3% appreciation rate to both land and improvements in a long-term hold model; a demolition/redevelopment analysis that fails to separate land value from improvement value.

seeds.t: Development Costs Split into Hard Costs, Soft Costs, and Land — Each Has Different Risk and Contingency Profiles
seeds.w: Applying a uniform contingency to total development cost — hard costs (construction, labour), soft costs (fees, carry, permits), and land each have different risk profiles. Hard cost contingency: 5–20%; soft cost: 3–5%; land is fixed post-acquisition.
seeds.c: TDC = land + hard costs + soft costs. Hard costs: physical construction, highest change-order risk, 5–20% contingency. Soft costs: fees, interest carry, more predictable, 3–5% contingency. Land: fixed post-acquisition. Track and report all three separately.
seeds.s: a development budget model that applies a single 10% contingency to total project cost; a construction draw schedule that doesn't distinguish between hard and soft cost categories for lender reporting.

seeds.t: Entitled Land Has Secured All Regulatory Approvals — Zoned Land Has Only the Base Designation
seeds.w: Equating zoned land with entitled land — zoning permits a class of uses; entitlement secures all project-specific approvals (environmental, utilities, design, conditional permits). Entitled land commands a premium; unentitled land carries entitlement risk and timeline uncertaint…
seeds.c: Zoning = base-use designation (necessary). Entitlement = all project-specific approvals (sufficient): rezoning/variance, subdivision, environmental review, utility capacity, design review, CUP. Model entitlement as a separate risk dimension with probability and timeline distribu…
seeds.s: a land valuation model that prices based on permitted density from zoning without discounting for unresolved entitlement risk; a development timeline that starts from 'land acquisition' to 'construction start' with no time allocated for entitlement.

seeds.t: Price-per-Square-Foot Is Only Comparable After Adjusting for Unit Mix, Location, Condition, and Time
seeds.w: Comparing raw $/sqft figures across properties without adjustment — time, location, condition, unit mix, floor level, and concessions all affect $/sqft independently of size. Unadjusted $/sqft comparisons produce systematically biased valuations.
seeds.c: $/sqft requires adjustment for: transaction date (time trend), location micro-factors (floor, view), condition/finishes, unit type mix, and seller concessions. Hedonic regression controls for all these simultaneously in AVM models.
seeds.s: an AVM that averages $/sqft of all comps in a ZIP code and applies it to a penthouse and a ground-floor studio alike; a development proforma that uses market average $/sqft for a luxury product without adjusting for quality premium.

seeds.t: Choose the right Redis data structure for your access pattern
seeds.w: storing everything as strings with JSON serialisation — loses Redis's ability to operate on data without deserialising.
seeds.c: use the right structure: Hash for objects (HGET/HSET individual fields), List for queues (LPUSH/RPOP), Set for unique membership (SADD/SISMEMBER), Sorted Set for leaderboards and time-ordered data (ZADD/ZRANGE), String for counters and simple values (INCR).
seeds.s: fetching entire serialised objects to update one field, or re-implementing sorted sets manually.

seeds.t: Set TTL at creation time — EXPIRE after SET can race
seeds.w: SET key value then EXPIRE key 3600 as two separate commands — another client may read the key between commands, or the process may crash leaving a key without expiry.
seeds.c: SET key value EX 3600 (atomic set with expiry) or SET key value PX 3600000 (milliseconds). For hash fields: use a separate expiry key or TTL on the hash itself.
seeds.s: keys persist indefinitely after a crash between SET and EXPIRE, or memory grows unboundedly.

seeds.t: Use pipelining to batch multiple commands and reduce round trips
seeds.w: sending commands one at a time in a loop — each command incurs a network round trip (typically 0.1-1ms). For 1000 commands: 100-1000ms.
seeds.c: use a pipeline to batch commands. They are sent in one network call and responses returned together. In Node: const pipeline = redis.pipeline(); pipeline.set(...); pipeline.get(...); await pipeline.exec(). Reduces 1000 round trips to 1.
seeds.s: Redis operations that should be fast are slow due to accumulated network latency.

seeds.t: Use Lua scripts or MULTI/EXEC for atomic read-modify-write
seeds.w: GET value, modify in application code, SET new value — another client may modify the key between GET and SET (race condition).
seeds.c: use INCR/DECR/INCRBY for numeric atomics. For complex read-modify-write: use EVAL with a Lua script (atomic, runs server-side) or WATCH + MULTI/EXEC for optimistic locking. Lua scripts are guaranteed atomic — no other commands run during execution.
seeds.s: counter drift under concurrent writes, or lost updates in high-traffic scenarios.

seeds.t: Use consistent hierarchical key naming with colons as separators
seeds.w: arbitrary key names like 'user123data' or 'u:123' — impossible to manage, scan, or understand.
seeds.c: use colon-separated hierarchical names: 'user:123:profile', 'session:abc123', 'cache:api:users:page:2'. This enables pattern scanning with SCAN MATCH 'user:*' and makes keys self-documenting. Include version prefixes for cache invalidation: 'v2:user:123'. Avoid very long keys (o…
seeds.s: keyspace is unnavigable, cache invalidation requires scanning with KEYS * (dangerous in production).
