seeds.t: Never use KEYS * in production — use SCAN instead
seeds.w: KEYS * or KEYS pattern — blocks Redis for the duration of the scan, which can be seconds on large keyspaces. This is a production outage waiting to happen.
seeds.c: use SCAN with a cursor and COUNT hint: SCAN 0 MATCH 'user:*' COUNT 100 — iterates in batches without blocking. Repeat until cursor returns 0. SCAN is not atomic (may miss or duplicate keys during concurrent writes) but is safe for production use.
seeds.s: Redis becomes unresponsive for seconds when KEYS is run on a large dataset.

seeds.t: Use a connection pool — creating connections per request is expensive
seeds.w: opening a new Redis connection for every request — connection setup has overhead (TCP handshake, auth) and Redis has a connection limit.
seeds.c: use a connection pool (most Redis clients have built-in pooling). Configure pool size based on concurrent request rate. In Node: ioredis and node-redis both pool by default.
seeds.s: latency spikes on first requests, or 'max clients reached' error under load.

seeds.t: Redis pub/sub messages are fire-and-forget — not persistent
seeds.w: using pub/sub for reliable message delivery — messages are lost if no subscriber is connected at the time of publish, or if a subscriber disconnects mid-message.
seeds.c: use Redis Streams (XADD/XREAD) for persistent, consumer-group-based message processing. Streams store messages, track consumer position, and support acknowledgement. Pub/sub is appropriate only for ephemeral notifications where loss is acceptable.
seeds.s: messages silently dropped when subscribers are offline or restarting.

seeds.t: Configure maxmemory-policy to control eviction behaviour
seeds.w: running Redis without a maxmemory policy — when memory is full, writes fail with OOM error.
seeds.c: set maxmemory and maxmemory-policy in redis.conf. For cache use cases: allkeys-lru (evict least recently used regardless of TTL) or allkeys-lfu (evict least frequently used). For persistent data: noeviction (fail writes rather than lose data) and manage memory explicitly. Set ma…
seeds.s: Redis OOM errors, or important data silently evicted.

seeds.t: Sorted Set scores are doubles — use timestamps carefully
seeds.w: using Unix timestamps in milliseconds as scores for very precise ordering — doubles lose integer precision above 2^53 (about 9007 trillion, safely above current timestamps).
seeds.c: millisecond timestamps are safe until year 287,396. For sub-millisecond precision: normalise scores or use a secondary sort key. ZADD key score member. ZRANGEBYSCORE key min max for range queries.
seeds.s: sort order incorrect for items with similar scores due to float precision.

seeds.t: Use SCAN to iterate keys without blocking the server
seeds.w: KEYS pattern in production — it is O(N) over all keys and blocks the single-threaded server for the entire scan duration, causing timeouts for other clients.
seeds.c: use SCAN cursor MATCH pattern COUNT 100 in a loop, starting with cursor 0 and continuing until the returned cursor is 0 again. SCAN is O(1) per call; the full iteration is O(N) but interleaved with other commands. Use HSCAN, SSCAN, ZSCAN for hashes, sets, and sorted sets.
seeds.s: Redis becomes unresponsive for seconds when an admin runs KEYS * on a large instance.

seeds.t: Use hash tags to co-locate related keys on the same slot
seeds.w: running multi-key commands (MGET, SUNION, EVAL with multiple keys) in Redis Cluster where keys live on different slots — these commands only work on keys in the same slot.
seeds.c: use hash tags to force keys to the same slot: {user:123}:profile and {user:123}:settings both hash only the part inside {}, landing on the same slot. Verify with CLUSTER KEYSLOT. Without hash tags, multi-key operations fail with CROSSSLOT errors.
seeds.s: CROSSSLOT Keys in request don't hash to the same slot error in Redis Cluster.

seeds.t: Use Streams with consumer groups for reliable message processing
seeds.w: using Pub/Sub for work queues — messages are dropped if no subscriber is connected, and there is no acknowledgement or retry mechanism.
seeds.c: use Redis Streams (XADD, XREADGROUP, XACK). Consumer groups track which messages each consumer has processed; unacknowledged messages stay in the Pending Entries List (PEL) and can be re-claimed by another consumer after a timeout. XAUTOCLAIM handles dead consumer recovery.
seeds.s: messages lost under Pub/Sub when consumers disconnect or lag behind producers.

seeds.t: Refresh TTL on access to implement sliding expiry
seeds.w: setting a TTL once and never updating it — active sessions or hot cache entries expire even while being used.
seeds.c: call EXPIRE key seconds (or EXPIREAT) each time the key is accessed to slide the expiry window forward. Use GETEX key EX seconds in Redis 6.2+ to get and reset the TTL atomically in one command. For sessions, refresh on every authenticated request to implement idle timeout.
seeds.s: active user sessions expire mid-use because TTL was set once at login and never renewed.

seeds.t: Use WATCH/MULTI/EXEC for optimistic locking, not GET then SET
seeds.w: reading a value, modifying it in application code, then writing it back with SET — another client can write between the read and write, causing a lost update.
seeds.c: use WATCH key, then MULTI/EXEC as an optimistic transaction. If the watched key changes between WATCH and EXEC, the transaction returns nil and you retry. For simple increments, INCR/INCRBY are atomic without transactions. For complex atomicity, use a Lua script executed with EV…
seeds.s: counter or balance gets corrupted under concurrent load due to read-modify-write race conditions.

seeds.t: Store serialised objects in a single key, not one key per field
seeds.w: storing each field of an object as a separate key (user:123:name, user:123:email) — requires multiple round-trips to read the full object and pollutes the keyspace.
seeds.c: use a Hash for structured objects: HSET user:123 name Alice email alice@example.com, read with HGETALL or HMGET. For full objects that are always read together, consider a single key with JSON serialised via RedisJSON or MessagePack. Use Hash for partial field access; single key…
seeds.s: reading a user profile requires 10 GET commands instead of one HGETALL.

seeds.t: Set maxmemory and eviction policy before going to production
seeds.w: running Redis without a maxmemory limit — when the host runs out of RAM, the OS starts swapping, causing dramatic latency spikes, or Redis crashes with OOM.
seeds.c: set maxmemory 2gb in redis.conf and choose an eviction policy: allkeys-lru evicts least-recently-used keys from all keys; volatile-lru only from keys with TTL. For pure cache use cases, allkeys-lru is safest. For mixed cache/session, volatile-lru or volatile-ttl.
seeds.s: Redis memory grows unbounded until the process is OOM-killed or the host starts swapping.

seeds.t: Use bitmaps for compact boolean tracking at scale
seeds.w: storing a Set of user IDs to track which users have seen a notification — a SET with 10M members uses hundreds of MB.
seeds.c: use SETBIT user_seen:notif123 userId 1 and GETBIT for O(1) per-user checks. A bitmap for 100M users costs only ~12MB. BITCOUNT counts set bits (e.g., total users who have seen the notification), BITPOS finds the first set or unset bit. Bitmaps are ideal for daily active user tra…
seeds.s: a Set tracking seen/unseen state grows to hundreds of MB when a bitmap would use kilobytes.

seeds.t: Choose AOF or RDB persistence based on durability needs
seeds.w: running Redis with no persistence configured for data you care about — a restart or crash loses all data.
seeds.c: enable RDB snapshots (save 900 1 saves after 1 change in 15min) for periodic durability, or AOF (appendonly yes) with appendfsync everysec for up to 1 second of data loss. For maximum durability, use AOF with appendfsync always (performance cost). RDB is faster to load at startu…
seeds.s: Redis restart after an outage loses hours of session or cache data that was expected to survive.

seeds.t: Use GEOADD and GEOSEARCH for proximity queries
seeds.w: storing latitude/longitude as string fields and doing proximity calculations in application code — requires loading all records and is O(N).
seeds.c: use GEOADD locations lon lat member to store geospatial data (backed by a sorted set with geohash scores), then GEOSEARCH locations FROMMEMBER point BYRADIUS 10 km ASC to find nearby members efficiently. GEODIST computes distance between two members. Redis Geo supports up to ~0.…
seeds.s: proximity search requires loading all locations from Redis and calculating distances in application code.

seeds.t: Clone is explicit; Copy happens automatically
seeds.w: passing a String to a function and then using it again — the value has been moved.
seeds.c: pass &String, or .clone() if the function needs ownership.
seeds.s: 'use of moved value' compiler error.

seeds.t: unwrap() panics on None or Err — use proper error handling
seeds.w: using .unwrap() or .expect() on Results or Options that can legitimately be None or Err — panics and crashes.
seeds.c: use pattern matching, the ? operator, .unwrap_or(), .unwrap_or_else(), .ok_or(). Reserve .unwrap() for cases you can prove are always Some/Ok, or in tests.
seeds.s: 'called unwrap() on Err' panic at runtime.

seeds.t: Cannot borrow as mutable more than once at a time
seeds.w: calling a method that borrows self mutably while another mutable borrow is active — common when iterating and modifying simultaneously.
seeds.c: collect indices or keys to modify, finish the iteration, then apply mutations.
seeds.s: 'cannot borrow X as mutable more than once at a time'.

seeds.t: ? operator only works in functions returning Result or Option
seeds.w: using ? in main() or a function returning () — ? requires the enclosing function to return a Result or Option.
seeds.c: change the signature to return Result<T, E>, or use .map_err() for type conversion. main() can return Result<(), Box<dyn Error>> to enable ? at the top level.
seeds.s: 'the ? operator can only be used in a function that returns Result or Option'.

seeds.t: Indexing a Vec panics on out-of-bounds — use .get()
seeds.w: vec[i] — panics if i is out of bounds.
seeds.c: vec.get(i) returns Option<&T>, allowing graceful handling. Use direct indexing only when you have already verified the bounds.
seeds.s: 'index out of bounds: the len is N but the index is M' panic.

seeds.t: Trait objects must be behind a pointer
seeds.w: using dyn Trait as a function parameter or struct field directly — trait objects are unsized.
seeds.c: use &dyn Trait, Box<dyn Trait>, or Arc<dyn Trait>. Box<dyn Trait> is most common for owned trait objects.
seeds.s: 'the size for values of type dyn Trait cannot be known at compilation time'.

seeds.t: Use thiserror for library errors, anyhow for application errors
seeds.w: defining error types manually with impl fmt::Display and impl Error — verbose boilerplate.
seeds.c: use thiserror for library/crate error types: #[derive(Error, Debug)] enum MyError { #[error('not found: {0}')] NotFound(String) }. For application code where error details matter less than propagation: use anyhow::Result and anyhow::bail!.
seeds.s: verbose error boilerplate, or using Box<dyn Error> everywhere losing error type information.

seeds.t: Use Cow<str> for functions that sometimes need owned strings
seeds.w: always returning String from functions that usually return borrowed data — forces allocation even when unnecessary.
seeds.c: use Cow<'a, str> which is either borrowed (&str) or owned (String). Return Cow::Borrowed(s) for no-allocation cases, Cow::Owned(s) when allocation is needed. Common for string processing: fn sanitise(s: &str) -> Cow<str> — if no change needed, return the input without allocating.
seeds.s: unnecessary heap allocations in string-processing hot paths.

seeds.t: Use RefCell for interior mutability — panics instead of compile error
seeds.w: using RefCell everywhere as a way to bypass the borrow checker — RefCell moves borrow checking to runtime and panics on violation.
seeds.c: use RefCell only when the borrow checker cannot verify safety statically (e.g. graph structures, event callbacks). For thread-safe interior mutability: use Mutex or RwLock. Call .borrow() for shared access, .borrow_mut() for exclusive — these panic if the borrow rule is violated…
seeds.s: runtime panic 'already borrowed: BorrowMutError'.

seeds.t: Iterator adapters are lazy — collect() or for loop triggers evaluation
seeds.w: calling .map(), .filter(), .flat_map() and expecting them to run immediately — iterators in Rust are lazy; adapters return new iterators without doing work.
seeds.c: terminate with .collect::<Vec<_>>(), .count(), .sum(), for elem in iter, or any consuming adapter. Without a consumer, no computation happens.
seeds.s: a chain of iterator adapters has no visible effect because it was never consumed.

seeds.t: Match guards narrow arm conditions but do not affect exhaustiveness
seeds.w: using a guard as the sole condition for an arm and omitting a default case — compiler error for non-exhaustive match.
seeds.c: add a _ wildcard arm for the remaining cases. If the guard covers all remaining cases logically but the compiler cannot verify it, add _ => unreachable!().
seeds.s: 'non-exhaustive patterns' even when match guards seem to cover everything.

seeds.t: Cannot call async functions from synchronous context without a runtime
seeds.w: calling an async function from main() or a sync function without a runtime — async functions return futures that do nothing until polled.
seeds.c: use #[tokio::main] or #[async_std::main] to set up a runtime. For calling async from sync in tests: #[tokio::test]. For calling async from a sync function inside an async context: tokio::task::spawn_blocking or runtime.block_on().
seeds.s: async function is called but nothing happens, or 'async fn used where a Future is expected' error.

seeds.t: Cold Observables Start Fresh Per Subscriber
seeds.w: Assuming an HTTP Observable is shared between multiple subscribers — each subscription triggers a new HTTP request, causing duplicate API calls.
seeds.c: Cold observables (HTTP, timer, of, from) create a new execution per subscriber. Share a single execution with shareReplay(1) for multicasting to multiple subscribers with replay of the last value, or share() for live multicast without replay. Use hot observables (Subjects, fromE…
seeds.s: three components subscribing to the same HTTP observable triggering three identical network requests.

seeds.t: Use shareReplay With refCount for Proper Memory Management
seeds.w: Using shareReplay(1) without {refCount: true} — the source Observable keeps running and the replay buffer stays alive even after all subscribers unsubscribe, causing memory leaks.
seeds.c: Use shareReplay({ bufferSize: 1, refCount: true }) so the source is unsubscribed when the subscriber count drops to zero. In Angular services, store the shared Observable as a property; do not recreate it per call. Default refCount was changed to false in RxJS 6.4 and true in 7+.
seeds.s: HTTP requests firing indefinitely in the background after all components using the shared stream have been destroyed.

seeds.t: Use switchMap to Cancel Previous Inner Subscriptions
seeds.w: Using mergeMap for user search-as-you-type — every keystroke creates a new HTTP request; responses arrive out of order and stale results overwrite current ones.
seeds.c: Use switchMap to automatically cancel the previous inner observable when a new outer value arrives: search$.pipe(switchMap(query => this.api.search(query))). Use mergeMap when all inner observables must complete (parallel uploads). Use concatMap when order and completion of each…
seeds.s: search results flickering with stale data from slow requests arriving after faster ones.

seeds.t: Choose the Right Subject Variant for the Use Case
seeds.w: Using plain Subject for everything — late subscribers miss emissions that occurred before they subscribed.
seeds.c: Subject: multicast, no replay, no initial value — for events. BehaviorSubject(initialValue): replays the latest value to new subscribers — for state. ReplaySubject(n): replays last n emissions — for buffered events. AsyncSubject: emits only the last value on complete — for one-t…
seeds.s: component subscribing to a Subject after the value was emitted receives nothing, but BehaviorSubject would have replayed it.

seeds.t: Use catchError to Recover Without Terminating the Stream
seeds.w: Letting an error propagate to the subscriber's error handler in a long-lived stream — once an Observable errors, it terminates permanently; subsequent events are never delivered.
seeds.c: Use catchError inside pipe to intercept and recover: source$.pipe(catchError(err => { logError(err); return EMPTY; })). Return EMPTY to silently swallow, or return of(fallbackValue) for a default, or rethrow with throwError(() => err) to propagate. For retrying: use retry(3) or …
seeds.s: WebSocket or polling stream stopping permanently after a single network error because the error reached the subscriber.

seeds.t: debounceTime Waits for Silence; throttleTime Limits Rate
seeds.w: Using throttleTime for search-as-you-type expecting to wait until the user stops typing — throttleTime emits immediately then suppresses for the interval, regardless of continued input.
seeds.c: debounceTime(300): waits 300ms of silence before emitting — ideal for search, autocomplete. throttleTime(300): emits the first value then ignores for 300ms — ideal for scroll/resize handlers, button spam prevention. auditTime: emits the last value at the end of each interval. sa…
seeds.s: search API called on every keystroke with throttleTime that debounceTime would have reduced to one call after typing stops.

seeds.t: combineLatest Emits on Any Source; forkJoin Waits for All to Complete
seeds.w: Using forkJoin for reactive dependent queries that should update when either source changes — forkJoin only emits once when all sources complete.
seeds.c: forkJoin([a$, b$]): waits for all to complete and emits once with the last values — ideal for parallel HTTP requests. combineLatest([a$, b$]): emits every time any source emits (after all have emitted at least once) — ideal for reactive form combinations or state derived from mu…
seeds.s: UI not updating when one of two combined filter observables changes because forkJoin was used instead of combineLatest.

seeds.t: Use tap for Side Effects Without Altering the Stream
seeds.w: Using map to perform a side effect (logging, caching) while returning the value unchanged — semantically incorrect and confusing to readers.
seeds.c: Use tap for side effects: source$.pipe(tap(v => console.log(v)), tap(v => cache.set(key, v)), map(v => transform(v))). tap receives the value, can perform any side effect, and passes the value through unchanged. Use tap(next, error, complete) for all notification types.
seeds.s: map(v => { sideEffect(v); return v; }) pattern that tap expresses more clearly and correctly.

seeds.t: Use firstValueFrom or lastValueFrom to Bridge to Promises
seeds.w: Calling .toPromise() on an Observable — deprecated in RxJS 7 and removed in RxJS 8; semantics are unclear (resolves with undefined if the stream completes empty).
seeds.c: Use firstValueFrom(obs$) to get a Promise that resolves with the first emitted value (rejects if empty). Use lastValueFrom(obs$) to get a Promise that resolves with the last emitted value before completion. Both work with async/await. Use {defaultValue} option for empty streams.
seeds.s: deprecation warnings from .toPromise() in RxJS 7, or undefined resolved value from an empty Observable.

seeds.t: Use distinctUntilChanged to Skip Repeated Emissions
seeds.w: Triggering expensive operations on every emission of a state Observable even when the value hasn't changed — components re-render or API calls fire for the same value.
seeds.c: Add distinctUntilChanged() to skip consecutive duplicates: state$.pipe(distinctUntilChanged(), switchMap(query => api.fetch(query))). Use distinctUntilKeyChanged('fieldName') for objects compared by a property. For deep equality, pass a comparator: distinctUntilChanged((a, b) =>…
seeds.s: HTTP request fired when navigating back to a page because the state Observable re-emitted the same value.

seeds.t: Use withLatestFrom to Sample a Secondary Stream
seeds.w: Using combineLatest when only one stream should drive emissions and the other provides context — combineLatest fires on changes to either stream.
seeds.c: Use withLatestFrom for 'when A fires, get the latest B': clicks$.pipe(withLatestFrom(currentUser$), map(([click, user]) => ({ click, user }))). Only clicks$ drives emissions; currentUser$ is sampled at the moment of each click. Use withLatestFrom for action + current state patte…
seeds.s: action handler firing twice when either the trigger or the context stream emits, because combineLatest was used instead of withLatestFrom.

seeds.t: Unsubscribe From Observables to Prevent Memory Leaks
seeds.w: Calling subscribe() in a component and never unsubscribing — the component is garbage-collected but the subscription keeps its reference alive and callbacks fire on destroyed components.
seeds.c: Four patterns: (1) async pipe (auto-unsubscribes). (2) takeUntilDestroyed() operator. (3) Store the Subscription and call sub.unsubscribe() in ngOnDestroy. (4) Use take(1) or first() for one-shot Observables. For multiple subscriptions, add all to a Subscription and call subscri…
seeds.s: 'cannot set property of null' errors after navigation because a subscription callback fires on a destroyed component instance.

seeds.t: Use startWith to Provide an Initial Value for combineLatest
seeds.w: Combining two streams with combineLatest but getting no emissions until both have emitted — combineLatest waits for all sources to emit at least once before emitting.
seeds.c: Add startWith(initialValue) to sources that may not emit immediately: combineLatest([filter$.pipe(startWith('')), page$.pipe(startWith(1))]). This gives combineLatest an immediate initial value to work with. Also use startWith for providing loading state: data$.pipe(startWith(nu…
seeds.s: combined observable not emitting until the user interacts with a filter, missing the initial data load.

seeds.t: Implement Exponential Backoff With retry or timer
seeds.w: Using retry(3) for network requests with no delay — immediate retries hit a failing server three more times with no recovery time.
seeds.c: Use retry({ count: 3, delay: (error, attempt) => timer(Math.pow(2, attempt) * 1000) }) for exponential backoff. Or use retryWhen (RxJS 6) with delayWhen and timer. Add a condition to only retry on specific errors (503, network errors) and rethrow others immediately. Log the atte…
seeds.s: three immediate retry requests in a row failing against a briefly-overloaded server that would have recovered with a delay.

seeds.t: Use Marble Testing for Time-Based Observable Logic
seeds.w: Testing time-based RxJS operators (debounceTime, delay, interval) with real timers and setTimeout — tests are slow, flaky, and timing-dependent.
seeds.c: Use TestScheduler from rxjs/testing: testScheduler.run(({ cold, hot, expectObservable }) => { const source = cold('--a--b|'); const result = source.pipe(map(x => x.toUpperCase())); expectObservable(result).toBe('--A--B|'); }). Marble syntax: '-' is 10ms, letters are emissions, '…
seeds.s: debounce tests using jest.useFakeTimers with complex advance patterns that marble testing handles declaratively.

seeds.t: Use scan to Accumulate State Over Time
seeds.w: Using reduce on a long-lived Observable expecting a running accumulation — reduce only emits when the source completes, making it useless for live state.
seeds.c: Use scan(accumulator, seed) which emits on every source emission with the accumulated value: clicks$.pipe(scan(count => count + 1, 0)). Ideal for building state from a stream of actions: actions$.pipe(scan(reducer, initialState)). This is the core pattern behind Redux-style stat…
seeds.s: trying to maintain a running count or state with reduce and getting no emissions until the stream completes.

seeds.t: Use finalize for Cleanup That Runs on Complete, Error, or Unsubscribe
seeds.w: Putting teardown logic only in the error handler or complete handler — doesn't run if the subscriber unsubscribes (e.g. switchMap cancellation or component destroy).
seeds.c: Use finalize(() => cleanup()) which runs after the observable terminates for any reason: completion, error, or unsubscription. Ideal for hiding loading spinners: obs$.pipe(tap(() => this.loading = true), finalize(() => this.loading = false)). Similar to try/finally semantics.
seeds.s: loading spinner stuck after navigating away from a page because finalize wasn't used and the cleanup only ran on complete.

seeds.t: Use fromEvent With Passive Listeners for Scroll and Touch
seeds.w: Creating scroll/touchmove listeners via addEventListener without the passive flag — the browser must wait for the handler to complete before scrolling, causing jank on mobile.
seeds.c: fromEvent supports event options: fromEvent(element, 'scroll', { passive: true }). Passive listeners tell the browser the handler won't call preventDefault(), enabling smoother scroll optimisation. Combine with throttleTime for scroll-position tracking.
seeds.s: jerky scrolling performance on mobile due to non-passive scroll event listeners that the browser can't optimise.

seeds.t: Use buffer and window to Batch Emissions
seeds.w: Manually accumulating emissions in a Subject and flushing on a timer — reinventing what buffer operators provide natively.
seeds.c: bufferTime(1000): collects all emissions in a 1s window and emits as array. bufferCount(10): collects 10 emissions and emits. bufferWhen(() => signal$): collects until a signal fires. windowTime/windowCount for the same but emitting inner Observables instead of arrays. Useful fo…
seeds.s: custom accumulation logic with a Subject and setInterval that bufferTime(1000) replaces in one operator.

seeds.t: Use AsyncSubject for One-Time Async Results
seeds.w: Using BehaviorSubject for a one-time initialisation result — subscribers before completion get intermediate values, and the initial value must be typed as nullable.
seeds.c: AsyncSubject emits only the last value before completion: const result$ = new AsyncSubject<Config>(); fetch().then(data => { result$.next(data); result$.complete(); }). All subscribers, even those arriving after completion, receive the final value. Useful for sharing the result …
seeds.s: nullable BehaviorSubject<Config | null> used for a config that only ever emits once and never changes.

seeds.t: Use map/flatMap on Option, Never .get
seeds.w: Calling option.get to extract an Option value — throws NoSuchElementException if the Option is None, defeating the purpose of using Option instead of null.
seeds.c: Use option.map(f), option.flatMap(f), option.getOrElse(default), or pattern match with case Some(v) / case None. Use option.fold(default)(f) for transforming with a fallback. Reserve .get only where you've already proven the value is Some via a prior check.
seeds.s: NoSuchElementException at runtime where None was unexpected, equivalent to a NullPointerException.

seeds.t: Use sealed Traits for Exhaustive Pattern Matching
seeds.w: Defining a trait with subclasses in multiple files when you want the compiler to enforce exhaustiveness — the compiler cannot warn about missing cases if subclasses can be defined anywhere.
seeds.c: Mark the trait sealed so all subclasses must be in the same file. The compiler then warns on non-exhaustive match expressions, catching missing cases at compile time. In Scala 3, use sealed or enum for algebraic data types.
seeds.s: match expression compiling without warning but throwing MatchError at runtime for an unhandled subclass.

seeds.t: Always Provide an Implicit ExecutionContext for Futures
seeds.w: Using scala.concurrent.Future without importing an ExecutionContext — compile error, or using the global ExecutionContext for all blocking I/O work in production, stalling the shared thread pool.
seeds.c: Import scala.concurrent.ExecutionContext.Implicits.global only for CPU-bound futures. For blocking I/O, create a dedicated fixed-thread-pool ExecutionContext: ExecutionContext.fromExecutorService(Executors.newFixedThreadPool(n)). In Cats Effect or ZIO, use their fiber-aware runt…
seeds.s: thread pool starvation under load because blocking database calls run on the global pool.

seeds.t: Use .copy() for Immutable Case Class Updates
seeds.w: Mutating fields on a case class instance, or creating a new instance manually specifying every field just to change one — fragile when fields are added later.
seeds.c: Use the generated .copy() method: user.copy(email = newEmail). Only the specified fields change; all others are copied from the original. This is the standard pattern for immutable updates in Scala. For deeply nested updates, use optics libraries (Monocle).
seeds.s: manual case class construction with all fields specified when .copy(field = newVal) would suffice.

seeds.t: Annotate Tail-Recursive Functions With @tailrec
seeds.w: Writing a recursive function that should be tail-recursive without the @tailrec annotation — if the recursion is accidentally non-tail-recursive, it will stack-overflow at runtime with no compile-time warning.
seeds.c: Add @tailrec from scala.annotation.tailrec to any function intended to be tail-recursive. The compiler verifies and optimises it to a loop, and errors if the tail-call condition is not met. Accumulator pattern: def loop(n: Int, acc: Int): Int.
seeds.s: StackOverflowError on large inputs for a recursive function that could have been TCO.

seeds.t: For-Comprehensions Desugar to map/flatMap/filter
seeds.w: Using nested map/flatMap chains for monadic composition when a for-comprehension would be more readable, or mixing incompatible monadic types in a single for-comprehension.
seeds.c: Use for { a <- ma; b <- mb(a) } yield f(a, b) which desugars to ma.flatMap(a => mb(a).map(b => f(a, b))). All generators in the comprehension must be the same monad type (all Option, all Future, all Either). Use monad transformers or libraries (Cats, ZIO) to compose different mo…
seeds.s: compile error 'type mismatch' mixing Option and Future generators in a single for block.

seeds.t: Use Either for Typed Error Handling, Not Exceptions
seeds.w: Throwing exceptions for expected business errors (validation failure, not found) in functional Scala code — exceptions break referential transparency and are not tracked in the type signature.
seeds.c: Return Either[Error, Result] where Left is the error case. Use .map, .flatMap, .left.map, and for-comprehensions over Either. In Cats, use EitherT for stacking Either with other effects. Reserve exceptions for truly unexpected failures.
seeds.s: business logic errors discovered only at runtime from try/catch blocks, not from the type signature.

seeds.t: Implicit Resolution Searches a Defined Scope Order
seeds.w: Defining the same typeclass instance in both the companion object and an explicit import, not understanding which one the compiler selects — leads to confusing ambiguity errors or silently using the wrong instance.
seeds.c: Scala searches for implicits in this order: local scope → enclosing scope → explicit imports → companion objects of involved types. Place default typeclass instances in companion objects (lowest priority, always available). Override with explicit imports in specific scopes. In S…
seeds.s: 'ambiguous implicit values' error or unexpected behaviour when two instances are in scope.

seeds.t: Use Value Classes for Zero-Overhead Type Safety
seeds.w: Using raw primitives (String, Int) for domain concepts like UserId or EmailAddress — no type safety, easy to confuse parameters of the same type.
seeds.c: Define value classes: case class UserId(value: Long) extends AnyVal. The compiler erases the wrapper at runtime in most contexts, so there is no allocation overhead. Value classes provide compile-time type safety for free. Limitation: value classes can't be used in generic conte…
seeds.s: swapping a userId and orderId parameter of the same type with no compile error.

seeds.t: Use s-Interpolator for Strings, f-Interpolator for Formatting
seeds.w: Using string concatenation with + for building dynamic strings, or using f-interpolation for non-formatted strings adding unnecessary format parsing.
seeds.c: Use s"Hello, $name, you have ${items.length} items" for simple interpolation. Use f"Pi is approximately $math.Pi%.4f" for printf-style formatting. Use raw"line1\nline2" to suppress escape processing. In Scala 3, use stripMargin with triple-quoted strings for multiline.
seeds.s: string concatenation expressions with many + operators that s-interpolation would make readable.

seeds.t: Default to Immutable Collections; Import Mutable Explicitly
seeds.w: Using mutable.HashMap or mutable.ArrayBuffer without importing scala.collection.mutable explicitly — Scala's default imports provide immutable collections; mutable ones require a clear intent.
seeds.c: Use immutable Map, List, Vector, and Set by default. When mutability is needed for performance (building a large collection), use mutable collections locally and return an immutable snapshot. Import scala.collection.mutable explicitly to signal intent. Prefer Vector over List fo…
seeds.s: accidentally sharing a mutable collection across threads, or unnecessary defensive copying.

seeds.t: Use Companion Object apply for Smart Constructors
seeds.w: Making case class constructors public with no validation — invalid objects can be created freely, violating invariants.
seeds.c: Make the case class constructor private and expose a smart constructor in the companion object: object Email { def apply(s: String): Option[Email] = if (isValid(s)) Some(new Email(s)) else None }. This ensures invariants are enforced at construction. Use Either[ValidationError, …
seeds.s: invalid domain objects like Email("") or Age(-5) being constructed and propagating through the system.

seeds.t: Prefer Cats Effect IO Over scala.concurrent.Future
seeds.w: Using Future for all async effects in a functional Scala application — Future is eagerly evaluated (starts immediately on creation), does not support cancellation, and is not referentially transparent.
seeds.c: Use cats.effect.IO (or ZIO) for lazy, referentially transparent effects that are only run at the 'end of the world'. IO is not started until explicitly run (.unsafeRunSync in tests, IOApp in production). IO supports cancellation, resource safety (Resource[IO, A]), and structured…
seeds.s: Futures starting on creation before they should, or inability to cancel long-running background effects.

seeds.t: Use Guard Conditions in Pattern Matches
seeds.w: Adding a nested if inside a case body when a guard condition would be cleaner, or not knowing guards exist.
seeds.c: Use if guards in case expressions: case user if user.age >= 18 => 'adult'. Guards are evaluated after the pattern matches. Multiple patterns can share a body with |: case Cat | Dog => 'pet'. Use @-bindings to capture sub-patterns: case list @ (head :: _) => (list, head).
seeds.s: nested if/else inside case bodies that could be expressed as guard conditions.

seeds.t: lazy val Is Thread-Safe but Can Deadlock
seeds.w: Using lazy val without understanding its synchronisation — lazy vals use a double-checked lock and can deadlock if two lazy vals depend on each other circularly, or if a lazy val's initialiser blocks waiting for something that needs the same lock.
seeds.c: Use lazy val for expensive one-time initialisation that is safe and non-circular. Avoid lazy val in hot paths (synchronisation overhead). For pure values, use val. For side-effectful deferred init, use cats.effect.IO or Ref.
seeds.s: application deadlock at startup from two lazy vals in mutual dependency.

seeds.t: Use Correct sbt Dependency Scopes
seeds.w: Adding test libraries like ScalaTest to the default Compile scope — they get included in the production artifact unnecessarily, bloating the jar.
seeds.c: Use % Test for test-only dependencies: libraryDependencies += "org.scalatest" %% "scalatest" % "3.2.x" % Test. Use % Provided for dependencies provided by the runtime environment (Spark, servlet containers). Use %% for Scala-version-suffixed artifacts.
seeds.s: test frameworks included in the production fat jar, or version conflicts because test libraries are in Compile scope.

seeds.t: Derive Typeclass Instances Automatically With Magnolia or Shapeless
seeds.w: Writing boilerplate typeclass instances (JSON encoders, CSV writers) by hand for every case class — tedious, error-prone when fields are added, and violates DRY.
seeds.c: Use automatic or semiautomatic derivation: import io.circe.generic.auto._ for Circe JSON codecs, or deriveEncoder[MyClass] for semiautomatic control. Shapeless-based derivation generates instances from the generic representation of case classes. Scala 3 uses derives keyword: cas…
seeds.s: 50-line hand-written JSON encoder for a 5-field case class.

seeds.t: Use Dataset and DataFrame Over RDD in Spark
seeds.w: Writing Spark jobs with the low-level RDD API — misses Catalyst query optimisation, Tungsten memory management, and requires manual serialisation.
seeds.c: Use Dataset[T] (type-safe) or DataFrame (dynamic schema) which go through Catalyst's optimiser and generate optimised bytecode. Use spark.read.parquet() and Dataset.map/filter/groupBy rather than sc.parallelize and RDD operations. Only drop to RDD for unsupported operations.
seeds.s: Spark job slower than equivalent SQL or DataFrame version because Catalyst optimisation is bypassed.

seeds.t: Prefer tell (!) Over ask (?) in Akka for Performance
seeds.w: Using the ask pattern (actor ? message) for all actor communication — ask creates a temporary actor for every request, adds a timeout mechanism, and returns a Future that must be managed carefully.
seeds.c: Use tell (actor ! message) for fire-and-forget when no response is needed. Use ask only when a response is genuinely required, and set a reasonable timeout. In Akka Typed, use the ask pattern via context.ask or the request-response pattern with a replyTo ActorRef in the message.
seeds.s: high actor overhead and Future management complexity from overuse of ask in high-throughput message paths.

seeds.t: Use collect to Apply a PartialFunction and Filter
seeds.w: Using filter followed by map to select and transform elements in two passes, when collect does both in one: list.filter(_.isInstanceOf[Foo]).map(_.asInstanceOf[Foo]).
seeds.c: Use collect with a partial function: list.collect { case foo: Foo => foo.value }. collect applies the partial function only to elements where it is defined, combining filtering and mapping. Also use in Akka actors: context.become uses PartialFunction for message handling.
seeds.s: verbose filter+map+cast chains for type-based filtering of collections.

seeds.t: Symptom Must Quote Exact Observable Output
seeds.w: Symptom says 'function behaves incorrectly' or 'returns unexpected value'
seeds.c: Symptom quotes the exact error message or wrong observable output the developer sees
seeds.s: seed never fires during lookup because symptom tokens don't match any real error output; review-seeds --report shows zero index hits for this seed.

seeds.t: Blast Radius Has a Defined Four-Point Scale
seeds.w: Marking seeds critical or high because the bug feels serious, without checking the scale definition
seeds.c: critical=data loss/security, high=build-break/crash, medium=wrong visible behavior, low=style/performance
seeds.s: all seeds inject at the same rate regardless of severity; high-blast seeds are drowned out by low-severity noise in the injection output.

seeds.t: Long Seed Content Dilutes BM25 Retrieval Score
seeds.w: Content field over 400 chars covering multiple scenarios or including background explanation
seeds.c: Content under 350 chars: one antipattern, one cause, one fix, one symptom phrase
seeds.s: seed is never injected despite the symptom matching the query; review-seeds --report shows the seed scores far below the threshold on its own symptom terms.

seeds.t: One Seed One Antipattern — Never Bundle
seeds.w: Combining multiple related antipatterns ('there are three ways this can fail') into one seed
seeds.c: One seed per antipattern; use see_also or co_inject relationships to connect related seeds
seeds.s: injected seed contains a long list of warnings only one of which is relevant; developers skip the whole seed because most of it doesn't apply to their current error.

seeds.t: Tags Are Retrieval Terms Not Topic Labels
seeds.w: Tags name the concept ('stale-closure') rather than what the developer would search for
seeds.c: Tags match symptom search terms: framework method, error keyword, observable behavior
seeds.s: 'useEffect', 'old-value', 'async', 'not-updating'. Think: 'If I had this bug and searched for help, what would I type?' Include the framework method name, the error keyword, and the observable behavior. Topic labels belong in the title or as secondary tags. Symptom: seed fires o…

seeds.t: WRONG and CORRECT Must Be Parallel in Scope
seeds.w: WRONG is a specific code snippet but CORRECT is a vague principle, or vice versa
seeds.c: WRONG and CORRECT at the same abstraction level — both code or both descriptive
seeds.s: developers read the seed but still aren't sure what to change; the seed gets 'not helpful' feedback despite addressing the right bug.

seeds.t: WRONG Section Needs the Specific Mistake Not the Class
seeds.w: WRONG says 'incorrectly using the async API' without showing what the wrong code looks like
seeds.c: WRONG shows the specific incorrect call, type, or pattern the developer would recognise in their own code
seeds.s: seed fires on relevant queries but developers report it doesn't match their situation because they can't tell if their code is the WRONG case.

seeds.t: CORRECT Section Must Be the Whole Fix Not a Partial Hint
seeds.w: CORRECT gives a partial hint ('add the second argument') without showing the complete corrected form
seeds.c: CORRECT is a complete actionable fix — names the right function, shows the required form, states the key concept
seeds.s: developer reads the seed, follows CORRECT, and still has the bug because the fix was incomplete; seed gets rejected in the drain review for under-specified correction.

seeds.t: Three to Six Tags — Zero Tags Are Invisible, Twenty Are Noisy
seeds.w: Zero tags (invisible to tag search) or 15+ tags (diluted weight, co-fires with unrelated seeds)
seeds.c: 3–6 tags: API/function name, error keyword, observable behavior — no synonyms, no stack-implicit terms
seeds.s: seed never surfaces in tag-weighted lookup; or seed fires alongside many unrelated seeds when any of its broad tags are matched.

seeds.t: Title States the Correction Not the Bug Name
seeds.w: Title names the antipattern: 'React useEffect stale closure bug'
seeds.c: Title states the correction ≤8 words: 'Add useEffect Dependency to Prevent Stale Closure'
seeds.s: seed is found and injected but the model has to read the full content to understand the corrective action; title adds no information beyond what the stack name already implies.

seeds.t: doc_reference Links to the Specific Docs Page Not the Homepage
seeds.w: doc_reference links to the library homepage or top-level docs index
seeds.c: doc_reference links to the specific page/section/anchor documenting the correct behavior
seeds.s: developers follow doc_reference and have to search within the docs site to find the relevant section; doc_reference stops being opened.

seeds.t: Version-Sensitive Seeds Need valid_through Date
seeds.w: Seed about version-specific API behavior has no valid_through date
seeds.c: Add valid_through: 'YYYY-MM-DD' to any seed whose correctness is version-dependent
seeds.s: seeds about deprecated APIs remain in the Stone and inject incorrect advice years after the API was removed; review-seeds --report shows seeds with past valid_through dates not yet archived.

seeds.t: Update Seeds When the Underlying API Changes
seeds.w: Seed unchanged after framework major version that deprecates or changes the CORRECT approach
seeds.c: After major version releases, run review-seeds --semantic and update affected seeds' CORRECT sections
seeds.s: model follows a seed's CORRECT advice and the code fails because the API no longer works that way; the seed content references an API that was removed in the current version.

seeds.t: Verify New Seeds Surface in the Symptom Index After Build
seeds.w: Adding a new seed and assuming it's retrievable without checking the symptom index
seeds.c: After build, verify the seed ID appears in api/symptom-index.json or surfaces in lookup_symptom results
seeds.s: seed is in the JSON file but never injected; lookup_symptom returns zero results for the exact symptom text from the seed.

seeds.t: Symptom Is Written From the Developer's Perspective
seeds.w: Symptom describes the internal mechanism ('fails to update internal state due to mutation')
seeds.c: Symptom describes what the developer observes: 'component does not re-render despite state update'
seeds.s: seed never fires even when the developer has the exact bug because their search terms describe their observation, not the root cause the seed uses.

seeds.t: Seed ID Must Identify the Specific Antipattern
seeds.w: ID is generic: 'bug_fix_1', 'react_issue', or 'python_error_2'
seeds.c: ID names the specific antipattern in 3–5 snake_case words: 'mutable_default_argument_python'
seeds.s: duplicate IDs cause one seed to silently overwrite another on build; relationships reference IDs that don't exist; outcome tracker can't match sessions to seeds.

seeds.t: Capture Personally First — Promote to Stack Only After Recurrence
seeds.w: Writing a new seed directly into a stack JSON file the first time a bug is encountered
seeds.c: Capture to personal-patterns.json first; promote to stack file after the antipattern recurs in a second context
seeds.s: stack files contain seeds that only apply to one specific project's architecture; drain review flags many seeds as 'too narrow for community use'.

seeds.t: Refine an Existing Seed Rather Than Creating a Near-Duplicate
seeds.w: Creating a new seed when capture_fix reports a near-duplicate with the existing one
seeds.c: Refine the existing seed's symptom, tags, or content; use force:true only if the case is genuinely distinct
seeds.s: two seeds with >50% token overlap both appearing in injection output; outcome tracker splits credit between them, preventing either from gaining enough confidence to inject aggressively.

seeds.t: source Field Has Three Valid Values With Specific Meanings
seeds.w: source field uses arbitrary value: 'mine', 'team', 'auto', 'gpt-generated'
seeds.c: source must be exactly: 'personal' | 'community' | 'grafted' — no other values
seeds.s: drain review --semantic skips the seed because source value doesn't match 'personal'; community validation pipeline rejects the PR with schema validation error.

seeds.t: Never Edit .txt Seed Files — They Are Build Artefacts
seeds.w: Editing seeds/{stack}.txt directly to fix content or typos
seeds.c: Edit seeds/{stack}.json, then run npm run build — never edit .txt files directly
seeds.s: a fix made in .txt disappears after the next build; git diff shows .txt diverging from .json; CI validation fails because .txt content doesn't match .json source.

seeds.t: Language Model Tokens Are Subwords — Token Count Is Not Word Count and Varies by Language
seeds.w: Treating tokens as equivalent to words — BPE tokens are subwords; token count ≠ word count. Non-English, code, and rare words split into more tokens. Always count tokens with the model's tokeniser, not word count.
seeds.c: BPE/WordPiece: ~1.33 tokens/word for English; 3–5× more for non-Latin scripts. Count with tiktoken or the model's tokeniser. Token count determines API cost, context window capacity, and arithmetic difficulty.
seeds.s: API request rejected for exceeding context length when word count appears to be within limits; non-English prompt consuming the full context window when the English equivalent would use half; cost estimate calculated from word count being 2× lower than the actual bill.

seeds.t: Tokenisers Split Numbers Inconsistently — Multi-Token Numbers Make Arithmetic Hard
seeds.w: Expecting reliable multi-digit arithmetic from a language model — numbers are tokenised by frequency, not by digit, creating arbitrary token boundaries that make place-value arithmetic architecturally unsupported.
seeds.c: Multi-digit numbers split at tokeniser-specific boundaries unrelated to place value. Reliable arithmetic requires code execution (tool use), not autoregressive generation. Models memorise small arithmetic facts; they do not generalise to large numbers.
seeds.s: model producing a plausible but wrong answer for 7-digit multiplication; model getting the last digit wrong consistently (the digit most likely to be a separate token boundary).

seeds.t: Autoregressive Generation Produces Exactly One Token per Forward Pass — Generation Is Inherently Sequential
seeds.w: Assuming a single forward pass generates the full response — autoregressive models produce exactly one token per forward pass. A 500-token response requires 500 sequential forward passes. Latency scales with output length.
seeds.c: One token per forward pass: sample token t from p(·|t₁..t_{t-1}), append, repeat. N tokens = N passes. KV cache makes each pass O(context) not O(N²). TTFT (prefill) and tokens/sec (decode) are separate latency dimensions.
seeds.s: a timeout set based on a fixed-duration assumption that fails on long responses; cost estimation that uses prompt token count when output token count dominates.

seeds.t: Temperature Scales Logits Before Softmax — T < 1 Sharpens, T > 1 Flattens, T = 0 Is Greedy
seeds.w: Treating temperature as an abstract creativity parameter — temperature divides logits before softmax: T < 1 sharpens (less variety), T > 1 flattens (more variety), T → 0 = greedy argmax. Applied before top-p/top-k truncation.
seeds.c: p(i) = exp(zᵢ/T) / Σ exp(zⱼ/T). T=0 → argmax (greedy, deterministic). T<1 → sharpens. T>1 → flattens. Temperature is applied first; top-p/top-k truncate afterward. Verify T=0 triggers argmax code path, not softmax(z/ε).
seeds.s: increasing temperature to get more variety but noticing occasional completely incoherent tokens (above-1 temperature amplifies low-probability tokens); using T = 0 and getting occasional non-deterministic outputs (T = 0 implemented as argmax of logits, not as softmax with ε).

seeds.t: Top-p (Nucleus) Sampling Adapts to the Sharpness of the Distribution; Top-k Does Not
seeds.w: Using fixed top-k when the distribution sharpness varies — top-k always samples from exactly k tokens regardless of confidence. Top-p adapts: at high-confidence steps it restricts to 1–3 tokens; at uncertain steps it allows more. Top-p is almost always preferable.
seeds.c: Top-p (nucleus): retain smallest set of tokens with cumulative prob ≥ p; renormalise; sample. Adapts to distribution sharpness per step. Temperature applied first; top-p truncates afterward. p = 0.9–0.95 is standard. Top-k is a fixed-width approximation.
seeds.s: top-k = 40 causing incoherent outputs when the model is confident because 39 low-probability tokens are competing with the correct one.

seeds.t: Greedy Decoding Maximises Per-Step Probability but Not Sequence Probability — Beam Search and Sampling Serve Different Goals
seeds.w: Assuming greedy decoding maximises sequence probability — greedy maximises per-step probability only; locally optimal tokens can lead to globally suboptimal sequences. Beam search approximates the most probable sequence; sampling produces diverse output.
seeds.c: Greedy: argmax per step, fast, suboptimal globally. Beam (width B): top-B partial sequences by joint probability; better for constrained tasks; can produce repetitive open-ended text. Sampling: diverse, natural; preferred for chat/creative. Match strategy to task.
seeds.s: chatbot using beam search producing robotic, repetitive responses; code generator using high-temperature sampling producing syntactically valid but logically wrong code.

seeds.t: Causal (Decoder) Attention Uses a Lower-Triangular Mask; Bidirectional (Encoder) Attends to the Full Context
seeds.w: Treating all Transformer architectures as interchangeable — causal (decoder) models use a lower-triangular mask enabling autoregressive generation; bidirectional (encoder) models attend to the full sequence, enabling deep contextual understanding but not generation.
seeds.c: Causal: attend to past only (lower-triangular mask) → autoregressive generation (GPT, LLaMA). Bidirectional: full attention → classification, embeddings (BERT). Encoder-decoder: bidirectional encoder + causal decoder with cross-attention (T5, BART).
seeds.s: using a causal decoder model for sentence-pair classification and seeing degraded performance because the first sentence's tokens never saw the second sentence; using a BERT encoder for generation and getting an error because it has no causal mask.

seeds.t: The Context Window Is a Hard Architectural Limit — Tokens Beyond It Are Not Processed, Not Summarised
seeds.w: Assuming tokens beyond the context window are compressed or summarised — they are simply not passed to the model. The context window is a hard limit. Exceeding it causes truncation (silent or error), with zero information preserved.
seeds.c: Context window C: input must be ≤ C tokens. Tokens beyond C are dropped entirely — no compression. Track cumulative token count in all pipeline stages. Long-context extensions (YaRN, RoPE scaling) extend C but with quality tradeoffs.
seeds.s: API error 'context length exceeded' when total tokens cross the limit; model appearing to ignore content from earlier in a long conversation (it was truncated, not forgotten).

seeds.t: Self-Attention Is O(n²) in Compute and Memory — Long Contexts Are Quadratically More Expensive
seeds.w: Assuming attention cost scales linearly with context length — standard self-attention is O(n²) in both compute and memory. Doubling context length quadruples the attention cost. FlashAttention achieves O(n) memory with the same O(n²) compute via tiling.
seeds.c: Self-attention: O(n²d) compute, O(n²) memory for the attention matrix. FlashAttention: O(n²d) compute, O(n) memory via SRAM tiling. Sliding-window attention: O(nw) — reduces to linear at the cost of limited context range.
seeds.s: OOM error when processing a 32k document that worked fine at 8k despite the same batch size; cost per token that grows unexpectedly at longer input lengths.

seeds.t: Most Language Models Pretrain Solely on Next-Token Prediction — Reasoning, Facts, and Style All Come From This One Objective
seeds.w: Assuming LLM capabilities require multi-task training — most capability comes from a single objective: next-token prediction on a large diverse corpus. The model learns facts, reasoning, and style implicitly. Instruction following requires separate post-training (SFT + RLHF).
seeds.c: Pretraining objective: max Σ log p(tₜ | t₁..t_{t-1}). All world knowledge, reasoning, and style emerge implicitly from predicting diverse text. Base models complete text, they do not follow instructions. Instruction following and safety require SFT + RLHF post-training.
seeds.s: a pipeline that sends user instructions directly to a base (pretrained-only) model and receives a completion-style response rather than an instruction-following answer; expecting a base model to refuse harmful requests (it has no RLHF alignment).

seeds.t: Supervised Fine-Tuning on Instruction Demonstrations Must Precede RLHF — Base Models Are Not Ready for Direct RL Training
seeds.w: Applying RLHF directly to a base pretrained model — base models produce text completions that the reward model cannot score meaningfully. SFT first establishes instruction-following behaviour; RL then refines it. SFT is not optional.
seeds.c: Post-training pipeline: (1) SFT on human-written instruction-response pairs → instruction-following baseline. (2) Reward model trained on ranked response pairs. (3) PPO (or DPO) to maximise reward while minimising KL from SFT model. Base → SFT → RLHF.
seeds.s: attempting PPO on a base model and the reward model assigning near-zero reward to all outputs because they are not instruction-following text.

seeds.t: RLHF Policies Learn to Maximise the Reward Model's Score, Not Human Preferences — Reward Hacking Is a Known Failure Mode
seeds.w: Treating RLHF alignment as permanent — the policy optimises the reward model, which is an imperfect proxy. Overoptimisation exploits RM errors (reward hacking). The KL penalty β controls the alignment-capability tradeoff; too small → hacking; too large → no gain.
seeds.c: RLHF objective: max E[r_φ(x)] − β·KL(πθ||π_SFT). RM is a proxy; overoptimisation produces reward hacking. Mitigations: iterative RM retraining, Constitutional AI, DPO. Sycophancy and excessive hedging are canonical reward-hacking failure modes.
seeds.s: RLHF-trained model producing sycophantic responses (agreement with the user always scores well on the RM); model that adds unnecessary hedges and caveats to every response (conservative hedging raises RM scores).

seeds.t: In-Context Learning Changes the Output Distribution Without Updating Any Weights — It Is Inference, Not Training
seeds.w: Describing few-shot prompting as 'training' the model — ICL updates zero weights; examples are in the context window only. No persistence across sessions. Fine-tuning updates weights permanently; ICL adapts the output distribution ephemerally.
seeds.c: ICL: examples in prompt bias the forward-pass output distribution without gradient updates. No weight changes; no persistence beyond the context window. Order and format of examples matter more than label accuracy in many settings. Fine-tuning > ICL for persistent task adaptatio…
seeds.s: expecting a model to 'remember' a correction given via in-context example in a new session; treating ICL accuracy as a ceiling for fine-tuned accuracy (fine-tuning usually exceeds ICL for the same task and data).

seeds.t: Language Model Hallucination Is Statistical Plausibility, Not Intentional Deception
seeds.w: Treating hallucination as intentional deception — models generate the most statistically plausible continuation, not the most true one. When the correct answer has low training-data probability, a plausible-but-wrong answer is generated without any internal awareness of the erro…
seeds.c: Hallucination = high-probability plausible-sounding continuation of a context where the correct answer has low training-data probability. No internal truth checker. Mitigations: RAG, tool use, self-consistency, uncertainty-expression training.
seeds.s: model confidently citing a non-existent paper; model giving a precise-sounding but wrong number with high confidence.

seeds.t: Parametric Knowledge Is Frozen at the Training Cutoff — Models Have No Access to Post-Cutoff Information Without Retrieval
seeds.w: Assuming models have access to current information — parametric knowledge is frozen at the training cutoff. Post-cutoff facts are inaccessible from weights. Provide current information via retrieval (RAG) or tool use; never rely on parametric knowledge for time-sensitive facts.
seeds.c: Parametric knowledge = frozen at training cutoff. Post-cutoff events require RAG or tool use to inject into the context window. Model can reason over contextual (in-prompt) documents but cannot update weights from them. Design all time-sensitive applications around this constrai…
seeds.s: model stating a company's CEO is someone who left that role two years ago; model describing a country's political situation using information that is no longer accurate.

seeds.t: System / User / Assistant Turns Have Distinct Semantic Roles — The System Prompt Applies to the Whole Conversation
seeds.w: Treating system/user/assistant turns as identical text fields — the system turn carries persistent, high-priority directives; user turns are the immediate request; assistant turns are prior responses. Misplacing instructions degrades how reliably they are followed.
seeds.c: System: persistent instructions applied to all turns. User: current request. Assistant: prior responses. Alternate user/assistant turns; system appears once at the start. Use the model's correct chat template (Jinja2); wrong template → degraded instruction following.
seeds.s: a system-level instruction (e.g., 'always respond in JSON') that the model follows at first but ignores later in the conversation when injected as a user turn; a pipeline that concatenates all content as a single user message and sees inconsistent instruction following.

seeds.t: Models Attend More Reliably to Content Near the Beginning and End of the Context — Important Information Should Not Be Buried in the Middle
seeds.w: Assuming equal attention reliability across all positions in a long context — models show a U-shaped performance curve: best at the beginning and end, worst in the middle. Place critical information at context boundaries, not buried in the middle.
seeds.c: Lost-in-the-middle: relevant content in the middle of a long context is attended to less reliably than content at the start or end. Mitigation: place critical chunks first or last; retrieve fewer, higher-quality chunks; repeat key instructions at the beginning and end.
seeds.s: RAG pipeline that retrieves 20 chunks and places the most relevant one in the middle, achieving lower accuracy than a pipeline that retrieves only 5 chunks but always places the most relevant one first.

seeds.t: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Improves Reasoning by Creating Additional Computation Tokens — Not by Explaining to the Model
seeds.w: Thinking CoT works by explaining the task to the model — CoT works because intermediate tokens extend the model's computation. Each generated token is a forward pass through all layers; multi-step problems need more computation than a single-token answer allows.
seeds.c: CoT creates computation: each intermediate token = one full forward pass of fixed depth. Complex reasoning decomposes into sequential steps, each feasible within the model's depth. Zero-shot 'think step by step' nearly matches few-shot CoT. CoT text ≠ faithful trace of internal …
seeds.s: a pipeline that uses a direct answer prompt for a multi-step reasoning task and sees significantly worse performance than a CoT version; interpreting a model's CoT reasoning as a reliable trace of its internal computation (it is an output, not a window into activations).

seeds.t: Solidity: Reentrancy — State Updated After External Call
seeds.w: Sending ETH or calling external contracts before updating internal state, allowing the callee to re-enter and drain funds.
seeds.c: Follow Check-Effects-Interactions: validate inputs, update all state, then make external calls. Use a reentrancy guard (ReentrancyGuard) on sensitive functions.
seeds.s: Contract drains unexpectedly; attacker calls withdraw repeatedly in a single transaction.

seeds.t: Solidity: tx.origin Used for Authentication
seeds.w: Using tx.origin to authenticate callers — it returns the original EOA, not the immediate caller, making contracts vulnerable to phishing attacks.
seeds.c: Use msg.sender for authentication. tx.origin is the transaction originator and can be a different address when called through an intermediary contract.
seeds.s: Auth passes when called through a malicious proxy contract; any contract the victim interacts with can impersonate them.

seeds.t: Solidity: Integer Overflow/Underflow Without SafeMath
seeds.w: Performing arithmetic on uint/int without overflow protection in Solidity < 0.8.0 — values silently wrap around.
seeds.c: Use Solidity 0.8.0+ (overflow protection built in) or OpenZeppelin SafeMath for older versions. Validate inputs before arithmetic.
seeds.s: Balance wraps to a huge number when subtracting more than it holds; addition silently overflows to zero.

seeds.t: Solidity: Missing Access Control on Privileged Functions
seeds.w: Leaving administrative functions (mint, pause, upgrade, transferOwnership) callable by anyone without an onlyOwner or role modifier.
seeds.c: Apply OpenZeppelin's Ownable or AccessControl. Add onlyOwner/onlyRole modifiers to all privileged functions and emit events on ownership changes.
seeds.s: Anyone calls mint() and creates unlimited tokens; contract gets drained or permanently broken.

seeds.t: Solidity: Floating Pragma Version
seeds.w: Using pragma solidity ^0.8.0 (caret) which allows compilation with any later version, potentially introducing breaking changes or vulnerabilities introduced in newer compiler versions.
seeds.c: Pin the pragma to a specific tested version: pragma solidity 0.8.21; Only upgrade after testing. Caret pragmas are acceptable during development, not in deployed contracts.
seeds.s: Contract compiles fine locally but behaves differently in production due to compiler version mismatch.

seeds.t: Solidity: Unchecked Return Value from Low-Level Call
seeds.w: Using address.call{value: amount}('') without checking the boolean return value — failed transfers silently succeed from the caller's perspective.
seeds.c: Always check the return value: (bool success, ) = addr.call{value: amt}(''); require(success, 'Transfer failed'); Or use OpenZeppelin's Address.sendValue.
seeds.s: ETH transfer fails silently; accounting is updated but funds never moved; loss of ETH.

seeds.t: Solidity: Block Timestamp Used for Critical Logic
seeds.w: Using block.timestamp for randomness, lotteries, or time-sensitive financial decisions — miners can manipulate it by ~900 seconds.
seeds.c: Use block.timestamp only for rough time windows (hours/days). For randomness use Chainlink VRF. For precise timing use block numbers with known block time.
seeds.s: Miner manipulates timestamp to win lottery or bypass time lock by a few minutes.

seeds.t: Solidity: Storage Collision in Upgradeable Contracts
seeds.w: Using delegatecall to a logic contract with storage variables that don't align with the proxy's storage layout — variables overwrite each other.
seeds.c: Use the Unstructured Storage or Transparent Proxy pattern (OpenZeppelin). Logic contracts should not declare storage variables; use a shared storage contract or ERC-1967 slots.
seeds.s: Setting a variable in the implementation reads/writes a completely different variable in the proxy; state corruption.

seeds.t: Solidity: Sensitive Operations Visible in Mempool
seeds.w: Submitting transactions with sensitive parameters (trade amounts, reveal values, bids) that can be front-run by MEV bots watching the mempool.
seeds.c: Use commit-reveal schemes for sensitive values. For DEX trades use slippage limits and deadlines. For auctions use sealed bids with deposits.
seeds.s: MEV bot sees your trade, places identical trade first with higher gas, profits from price movement at your expense.

seeds.t: Solidity: Unbounded Loop Causing Gas Exhaustion
seeds.w: Iterating over an array or mapping that grows with user input without a size limit — gas cost eventually exceeds the block gas limit.
seeds.c: Cap array sizes, use pagination via offsets, or redesign using mappings with index tracking. Move iteration off-chain and use proofs or indices on-chain.
seeds.s: Contract function reverts for all users once enough entries accumulate; contract becomes permanently unusable.

seeds.t: Solidity: Constructor Logic in Upgradeable Contracts
seeds.w: Using a constructor to set initial state in an upgradeable (proxy) contract — constructors run in the implementation context, not the proxy's storage.
seeds.c: Use an initializer function (OpenZeppelin Initializable) with the initializer modifier. Call initialize() once after deployment through the proxy.
seeds.s: Owner/admin variables are zero address in the proxy despite being set in the constructor; contract is immediately bricked.

seeds.t: Solidity: Missing Zero Address Validation
seeds.w: Accepting address parameters without checking for address(0) — accidentally setting owner/admin/token address to zero address permanently locks the contract.
seeds.c: Add require(addr != address(0), 'Zero address') before storing any critical address parameter. This is especially important for constructors and ownership transfer.
seeds.s: Owner set to zero address in constructor; contract is immediately non-administrable and funds locked forever.

seeds.t: Solidity: On-Chain Spot Price Used as Oracle
seeds.w: Reading spot price from a DEX (Uniswap pair reserves) directly in the same transaction — flash loans can manipulate it to any value.
seeds.c: Use Chainlink price feeds, Uniswap TWAP (time-weighted average price), or Maker's oracle. Never use spot reserve ratios for financial calculations.
seeds.s: Flash loan attack drains protocol by temporarily manipulating price to borrow far more than collateral is worth.

seeds.t: Solidity: State Changes Without Events
seeds.w: Making state changes (ownership transfer, parameter updates, critical operations) without emitting events — off-chain indexers and user interfaces have no way to track state history.
seeds.c: Emit events for every significant state change. Index event parameters that will be searched. Events are the only reliable way for off-chain systems to track contract state.
seeds.s: dApp shows wrong owner; audit trail missing; users can't verify what happened on-chain through standard tools.

seeds.t: Solidity: Assuming Contract ETH Balance Equals Deposits
seeds.w: Using address(this).balance to track user deposits — ETH can be force-sent via selfdestruct or coinbase, making the balance higher than tracked deposits.
seeds.c: Track deposits in a separate state variable and account for only intentional transfers. Never rely on address(this).balance for invariant checks.
seeds.s: Invariant check fails; users can't withdraw because contract balance doesn't match expectations; accounting broken.

seeds.t: Use Spring Boot's Layered Jar for Optimal Docker Caching
seeds.w: COPYing the fat jar directly into the image: COPY target/app.jar app.jar — the entire jar (dependencies + app classes) changes on every code change, invalidating the Docker layer every build.
seeds.c: Use Spring Boot's layered jar feature. In the builder stage: java -Djarmode=layertools -jar app.jar extract. This produces layers/: dependencies (rarely change), spring-boot-loader, snapshot-dependencies, application (changes on every build). COPY each layer separately so Docker…
seeds.s: 80 MB Docker image layer rebuilt from scratch on every code change because the fat jar is a single indivisible layer.

seeds.t: Let the JVM Detect Container Memory Limits Automatically
seeds.w: Setting -Xmx512m as a fixed heap in the Dockerfile — when the container memory limit is changed, the JVM doesn't know and may either use too little heap or exceed the limit and be OOMKilled.
seeds.c: Modern JVMs (8u191+, 11+) support container awareness via -XX:+UseContainerSupport (default on). Set -XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75 to use 75% of the container's memory limit as max heap — the JVM reads the cgroup memory limit and calculates accordingly. Set JAVA_OPTS or JAVA_TOOL_OPTI…
seeds.s: JVM using 256 MB of heap in a 2 GB container, or OOMKilled because -Xmx was set to 1 GB but the container limit is 512 MB.

seeds.t: Use Actuator Health Endpoint as Docker HEALTHCHECK
seeds.w: Using HEALTHCHECK CMD curl http://localhost:8080 which hits the root and may return 302 (redirect to login or Swagger), or writing a custom health endpoint.
seeds.c: Spring Boot Actuator's /actuator/health endpoint is purpose-built for this: it aggregates health indicators (DB connectivity, disk space, custom checks) and returns UP/DOWN with HTTP 200/503. HEALTHCHECK CMD curl -f http://localhost:8080/actuator/health || exit 1. Expose only he…
seeds.s: Docker health check probing / and getting 200 even when the database is down.

seeds.t: Activate Spring Profiles via Environment Variables
seeds.w: Hardcoding spring.profiles.active=prod in application.properties committed to the repository — different environments can't be configured without modifying and rebuilding the image.
seeds.c: Set the profile at runtime: ENV SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod in the Dockerfile for a production image, or docker run -e SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=staging app. Spring Boot reads SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE as an environment variable (it maps _ to . in property names). Keep application-pro…
seeds.s: separate Docker images built for each environment because the profile was baked into the image at build time.

seeds.t: Enable Spring Boot Graceful Shutdown for Docker SIGTERM
seeds.w: Leaving Spring Boot's default immediate shutdown — when Kubernetes sends SIGTERM before removing the pod from the load balancer, in-flight requests are dropped mid-processing.
seeds.c: Enable graceful shutdown in application.properties: server.shutdown=graceful and spring.lifecycle.timeout-per-shutdown-phase=30s. Spring Boot will finish processing in-flight requests before stopping (up to the timeout). Configure the Kubernetes preStop hook to add a delay befor…
seeds.s: users receiving 503 errors during pod rolling restarts because requests are cut off mid-flight.

seeds.t: Use Spring Boot Buildpacks for Zero-Config Docker Images
seeds.w: Writing a Dockerfile from scratch for every Spring Boot service — boilerplate that each team maintains slightly differently, with risk of common mistakes (running as root, no layer caching).
seeds.c: Use Spring Boot's built-in Buildpacks support: ./mvnw spring-boot:build-image -Dspring-boot.build-image.imageName=myapp:latest. This produces a production-ready OCI image using Paketo Buildpacks — includes JVM tuning, non-root user, layered jar, and memory calculator automatical…
seeds.s: five different teams with five different Dockerfiles, each with different security and optimisation choices.

seeds.t: Use Multi-Stage Builds to Separate Maven Build From Runtime
seeds.w: Running the Maven build and the application in the same Docker stage — ships the Maven cache, source code, and build tools in the production image.
seeds.c: Stage 1 (builder): FROM maven:3-eclipse-temurin-21; COPY pom.xml .; RUN mvn dependency:go-offline; COPY src ./src; RUN mvn package -DskipTests. Stage 2 (runtime): FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre-jammy; copy only the jar from builder with COPY --from=builder. The runtime image needs …
seeds.s: production Spring Boot image over 800 MB because Maven repository and source code are included.

seeds.t: Cache Maven Dependencies as a Separate Docker Layer
seeds.w: Running mvn package before copying the source code — or copying source before downloading dependencies — so every source change triggers a full dependency download.
seeds.c: Copy pom.xml first, then run mvn dependency:go-offline (or mvn install -DskipTests with an empty src) to download all dependencies into the layer cache. Then COPY src and run the full build. Docker caches the dependency download layer as long as pom.xml is unchanged. With Docker…
seeds.s: docker build downloading 200 MB of Maven dependencies on every CI run because source code was copied before the dependency download step.

seeds.t: Use a Kubernetes startupProbe for Slow JVM Startup
seeds.w: Configuring only a livenessProbe with a short initialDelaySeconds — the JVM + Spring context startup can take 30–60 seconds; the liveness probe kills the container before it finishes starting.
seeds.c: Add a startupProbe that polls the health endpoint with a generous failureThreshold: startupProbe: httpGet: path: /actuator/health; failureThreshold: 30; periodSeconds: 5. This gives 150 seconds for startup. Once the startupProbe succeeds, Kubernetes switches to the livenessProbe…
seeds.s: Spring Boot pods repeatedly crash-looping because the livenessProbe kills them during the 45-second startup phase.

seeds.t: Configure DataSource URL and Credentials via Environment Variables
seeds.w: Hardcoding spring.datasource.url=jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/mydb in application.properties — the hostname localhost doesn't resolve to the database in Docker networking; credentials are committed to the repository.
seeds.c: Use Spring Boot's environment variable binding: set SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL=jdbc:postgresql://db:5432/mydb (using the Docker service name), SPRING_DATASOURCE_USERNAME, and SPRING_DATASOURCE_PASSWORD at runtime. In Docker Compose, inject via environment: section. In Kubernetes, inj…
seeds.s: Spring Boot failing to connect to the database in Docker because the connection URL points to localhost instead of the database container's service name.

seeds.t: Run the JVM as a Non-Root User in Docker
seeds.w: Running the Spring Boot application as root inside the container — root in a container is a security risk and unnecessary; the JVM and Spring need no root privileges for standard web serving.
seeds.c: In the Dockerfile runner stage: RUN groupadd -r spring && useradd -r -g spring spring; USER spring. Paketo Buildpacks (spring-boot:build-image) create and use a non-root user automatically. Ensure application files are readable by the spring user. JVM flags and Spring configurat…
seeds.s: container security scan flagging root JVM process, or Kubernetes PodSecurityPolicy rejecting root containers.

seeds.t: Use Constructor Injection Over Field Injection
seeds.w: Using @Autowired on fields (@Autowired private UserService userService) — field injection hides dependencies, makes classes hard to unit test without a Spring context, and allows creating instances with null dependencies.
seeds.c: Declare dependencies as final fields and inject via a constructor (Lombok's @RequiredArgsConstructor generates this automatically). Constructor injection makes dependencies explicit, enables immutability, and allows plain unit testing without Spring.
seeds.s: tests requiring @SpringBootTest or Mockito's @InjectMocks just to instantiate a class, or NullPointerException when instantiating outside of Spring.

seeds.t: @Transactional Self-Invocation Bypasses the Proxy
seeds.w: Calling a @Transactional method from another method in the same class — Spring AOP uses a proxy; internal method calls bypass the proxy entirely so no transaction is started or propagated.
seeds.c: @Transactional methods must be called from outside the class through the Spring proxy. Refactor by splitting into separate Spring beans, or use AspectJ mode (proxyTargetClass is not enough — AspectJ weaving is required for self-invocation). Alternatively inject the bean into its…
seeds.s: @Transactional method not rolling back on exception when called internally.

seeds.t: Understand @Transactional Propagation Levels
seeds.w: Using the default REQUIRED propagation everywhere and being confused when an inner service method's rollback rolls back the outer transaction unexpectedly.
seeds.c: REQUIRED (default): joins an existing transaction or creates a new one. REQUIRES_NEW: always creates a new transaction, suspending the outer one (useful for audit logs that must commit even if the outer tx rolls back). NESTED: creates a savepoint. Use @Transactional(propagation=…
seeds.s: entire outer transaction rolling back because an inner service method threw an exception.

seeds.t: Fix JPA N+1 With JOIN FETCH or @EntityGraph
seeds.w: Fetching a list of entities with a @OneToMany relation and accessing the relation inside a loop — JPA fires one query to fetch the list, then one query per entity to fetch the related collection.
