seeds.c: Use JOIN FETCH in JPQL: @Query('SELECT p FROM Post p JOIN FETCH p.comments WHERE p.active = true') or annotate the repository method with @EntityGraph(attributePaths=['comments']). Use @BatchSize(size=25) as a fallback for non-query-method access. Enable spring.jpa.show-sql=true…
seeds.s: 100 entities causing 101 SQL queries visible in the query log.

seeds.t: Singleton Beans Must Not Hold Mutable Request-Scoped State
seeds.w: Storing request-specific data (current user, request ID) as instance variables in a singleton-scoped Spring bean — the same bean instance is shared across all threads and requests, causing data leakage between users.
seeds.c: Use @RequestScope or @SessionScope for beans that need per-request state. Inject request-scoped beans into singletons using Spring's scoped proxy: @Scope(value='request', proxyMode=ScopedProxyMode.TARGET_CLASS). Use ThreadLocal as a last resort for request context.
seeds.s: one user's data appearing in another user's response under concurrent load.

seeds.t: Use @ControllerAdvice for Global Exception Handling
seeds.w: Adding try-catch blocks to every controller method or duplicating error response construction across controllers — inconsistent error responses and boilerplate everywhere.
seeds.c: Create a class annotated with @RestControllerAdvice containing @ExceptionHandler methods for each exception type. These methods centralise error handling and can return consistent ProblemDetail (RFC 7807) responses. Spring Boot auto-configures BasicErrorController as a fallback.…
seeds.s: different error formats for different endpoints, or identical try-catch blocks in every controller.

seeds.t: Use Spring Profiles for Environment-Specific Configuration
seeds.w: Using if/else logic in beans to check the environment, or maintaining a single application.properties for all environments with commented-out sections.
seeds.c: Create application-dev.properties, application-prod.properties files and activate with spring.profiles.active=prod (or SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE env var). Use @Profile('prod') on beans that should only be created in specific environments. In tests, use @ActiveProfiles('test'). Prof…
seeds.s: accidentally connecting to the production database in a development environment.

seeds.t: Access Lazy-Loaded JPA Associations Within the Session
seeds.w: Fetching a JPA entity in a service, returning it to the controller, and accessing a LAZY-fetched association in the view or serialiser — the JPA session is closed by the time the view runs, throwing LazyInitializationException.
seeds.c: Initialise all needed associations within the @Transactional service method using JOIN FETCH, Hibernate.initialize(), or a DTO projection. Enable spring.jpa.open-in-view=false (default true but considered an anti-pattern) to get exceptions early rather than silently loading in t…
seeds.s: 'failed to lazily initialize a collection of role ... no session' exception in serialisation.

seeds.t: Use @Cacheable With a Cache Manager for Method-Level Caching
seeds.w: Implementing manual caching in service methods (if(cache.contains(key)) return cache.get(key)) — duplicates cache logic across methods and bypasses Spring's cache abstraction.
seeds.c: Annotate methods with @Cacheable(value='users', key='#id') and configure a CacheManager bean (CaffeineCacheManager for in-process, RedisCacheManager for distributed). Use @CacheEvict on update/delete methods, @CachePut on save methods. Enable caching with @EnableCaching on a con…
seeds.s: hand-rolled cache maps scattered across service classes, duplicating cache management logic.

seeds.t: @Async Requires @EnableAsync and a Thread Pool Configuration
seeds.w: Annotating a method with @Async but not adding @EnableAsync to a configuration class — the method runs synchronously with no error.
seeds.c: Add @EnableAsync to a @Configuration class. Configure a TaskExecutor bean to control thread pool size instead of using the default SimpleAsyncTaskExecutor (which creates a new thread per call). @Async methods must be called through the Spring proxy (same self-invocation problem …
seeds.s: @Async method executing on the calling thread because @EnableAsync was forgotten.

seeds.t: Use WebClient Instead of Deprecated RestTemplate
seeds.w: Using RestTemplate for outbound HTTP calls in new code — RestTemplate is in maintenance mode since Spring 5 and blocks the calling thread for each request.
seeds.c: Use WebClient from spring-webflux: WebClient.create().get().uri('https://api.example.com/data').retrieve().bodyToMono(Data.class). WebClient supports both synchronous (.block()) and reactive usage. Configure a shared WebClient.Builder bean with base URL, timeouts, and default he…
seeds.s: deprecation warnings on RestTemplate in new Spring Boot 3.x projects.

seeds.t: Break Circular Bean Dependencies With Refactoring
seeds.w: Using @Lazy or field injection as a quick fix for circular dependencies — these hide a design problem where two beans are too tightly coupled.
seeds.c: Break the cycle by extracting shared logic into a third bean, using events (ApplicationEventPublisher) to decouple publishers from listeners, or restructuring to remove the circular dependency. In Spring Boot 2.6+, circular dependencies are prohibited by default (spring.main.all…
seeds.s: 'The dependencies of some of the beans in the application context form a cycle' exception on startup.

seeds.t: Use Test Slice Annotations for Faster Focused Tests
seeds.w: Using @SpringBootTest for every test — loads the entire application context for each test class, making the suite slow.
seeds.c: Use test slices that load only the relevant context: @WebMvcTest for controller-layer tests (no JPA), @DataJpaTest for repository tests (no web layer), @JsonTest for JSON serialisation tests. Mock missing beans with @MockBean. Only use @SpringBootTest for true integration tests …
seeds.s: test suite taking 5 minutes because every test loads the complete application context.

seeds.t: Validate Request Bodies With @Valid and Bean Validation
seeds.w: Writing manual null/range checks at the top of every controller method — verbose and easy to miss.
seeds.c: Annotate DTO fields with Bean Validation constraints (@NotNull, @Size, @Email, @Min) and add @Valid or @Validated to the controller method parameter. Spring automatically validates and returns a 400 with errors if validation fails. Use @ControllerAdvice to customise the 400 erro…
seeds.s: repetitive if(request.getName() == null) throw new BadRequestException() in every controller method.

seeds.t: Use ApplicationEvents to Decouple Domain Side Effects
seeds.w: Calling EmailService, AuditService, and NotificationService directly in a domain service method — tight coupling, hard to test, and violates single responsibility.
seeds.c: Publish an ApplicationEvent (or custom domain event): applicationEventPublisher.publishEvent(new UserCreatedEvent(this, user)). Annotate listeners with @EventListener (synchronous) or @TransactionalEventListener(phase=AFTER_COMMIT) for events that depend on the transaction havin…
seeds.s: service method with 10 lines of side-effect calls that have nothing to do with the core domain operation.

seeds.t: @Scheduled Runs in a Single Thread by Default
seeds.w: Having multiple @Scheduled tasks and assuming they run in parallel — Spring's default TaskScheduler uses a single-threaded pool, so tasks queue behind each other.
seeds.c: Configure a ThreadPoolTaskScheduler bean with poolSize > 1 and set it as the Spring scheduler. Add @EnableScheduling to a @Configuration class. Use fixedDelay (measured from task completion) vs fixedRate (measured from task start, may overlap). Add @Async to individual @Schedule…
seeds.s: scheduled tasks delayed because a previous long-running task hasn't finished.

seeds.t: Secure Spring Actuator Endpoints in Production
seeds.w: Deploying Spring Boot Actuator with all endpoints exposed and no authentication — /actuator/env exposes environment variables including secrets, /actuator/heapdump dumps JVM heap.
seeds.c: Expose only necessary endpoints: management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,info,metrics. Require authentication for sensitive endpoints by configuring Spring Security to protect /actuator/**. Use management.server.port to serve actuator on a separate internal port not exp…
seeds.s: /actuator/env endpoint accessible from the internet, leaking database credentials and API keys.

seeds.t: Understand JPA Entity State to Avoid Unexpected Updates
seeds.w: Modifying an entity returned from a @Transactional method outside the transaction and being surprised that changes are (or aren't) persisted — managed entities are automatically 'dirty checked' within a session; detached entities are not.
seeds.c: Within a @Transactional method, any changes to managed entities are automatically flushed on commit — no explicit save() needed for updates. Outside a transaction, entities are detached; call repository.save(entity) to persist changes. Use @Transactional(readOnly=true) for read-…
seeds.s: accidental updates to entities whose fields were modified inside a transaction without calling save().

seeds.t: Use @ConfigurationProperties for Type-Safe External Config
seeds.w: Using @Value('${my.property.host}') for every individual property — scattered across the codebase, no validation, no IDE support for the prefix.
seeds.c: Create a @ConfigurationProperties(prefix='my.property') class with typed fields (String host, int port, Duration timeout) and annotate with @EnableConfigurationProperties or @Component. Spring binds properties with validation (@NotNull, @Min) and provides IDE autocomplete via sp…
seeds.s: dozens of @Value annotations across the codebase with no centralised view of what configuration the app requires.

seeds.t: Use @PreAuthorize for Method-Level Security
seeds.w: Checking roles manually in service methods (if (!user.hasRole('ADMIN')) throw new AccessDeniedException()) — duplicated security logic and easily forgotten.
seeds.c: Enable method security with @EnableMethodSecurity and annotate service methods with @PreAuthorize('hasRole("ADMIN")') or @PreAuthorize('hasAuthority("user:read") and #userId == authentication.principal.id'). Spring evaluates these SpEL expressions against the authenticated user …
seeds.s: role checks scattered throughout service code, risk of missing one and creating a privilege escalation vulnerability.

seeds.t: NULL comparisons require IS NULL, not = NULL
seeds.w: WHERE column = NULL — this always returns no rows because NULL = NULL evaluates to NULL (unknown), not TRUE.
seeds.c: WHERE column IS NULL or WHERE column IS NOT NULL. This applies to all comparisons: NULL != NULL is also NULL, not TRUE. For COALESCE: COALESCE(column, 'default') substitutes a default when column IS NULL.
seeds.s: query returns no rows despite NULL values existing in the column.

seeds.t: COUNT(*) counts rows, COUNT(column) skips NULLs
seeds.w: using COUNT(nullable_column) expecting total row count — it silently skips NULLs.
seeds.c: use COUNT(*) for total rows, COUNT(column) only when you intentionally want to count non-null values. SUM() also ignores NULLs — SUM(NULL) returns NULL, not 0; use COALESCE(SUM(column), 0).
seeds.s: count is lower than expected, or aggregate returns NULL instead of 0.

seeds.t: Avoid SELECT * in production queries
seeds.w: SELECT * in application queries — it fetches all columns including large blobs, wastes network bandwidth, and breaks if columns are added or reordered.
seeds.c: explicitly name the columns you need: SELECT id, name, created_at. This also allows the query planner to use covering indexes. Exception: SELECT * is fine for ad hoc exploration.
seeds.s: unexpectedly high query latency, or application breaks after schema changes.

seeds.t: Functions on indexed columns prevent index use
seeds.w: WHERE LOWER(email) = 'user@example.com' or WHERE DATE(created_at) = '2024-01-01' — wrapping an indexed column in a function forces a full table scan.
seeds.c: store data in the form you query it (lowercase email), use range queries for dates (created_at >= '2024-01-01' AND created_at < '2024-01-02'), or create a functional index if your database supports it.
seeds.s: slow query despite index existing on the column.

seeds.t: N+1 query pattern — fetch related data in one query
seeds.w: fetching a list of records then querying each one individually for related data (1 query for N records + N queries for related = N+1 queries total).
seeds.c: use JOIN to fetch related data in one query, or use WHERE id IN (...) to batch the secondary query. In ORMs: use eager loading (include/with/preload) instead of lazy loading in loops.
seeds.s: query count visible in logs grows linearly with result set size.

seeds.t: Read-modify-write sequences need transactions to be safe
seeds.w: SELECT a value, compute a new value in application code, then UPDATE — another process can modify the row between your SELECT and UPDATE causing a lost update.
seeds.c: wrap in a transaction, or use atomic SQL: UPDATE table SET count = count + 1 WHERE id = ?. For complex logic, use SELECT FOR UPDATE to lock the row during the transaction.
seeds.s: counter values incorrect under concurrent load, race condition data loss.

seeds.t: Leading wildcard in LIKE prevents index use
seeds.w: WHERE name LIKE '%smith' — a leading wildcard requires a full table scan regardless of indexing.
seeds.c: WHERE name LIKE 'smith%' can use a B-tree index on name. For full-text search needs, use your database's full-text search capabilities (PostgreSQL tsvector, MySQL FULLTEXT, SQLite FTS5) instead of LIKE '%term%'.
seeds.s: LIKE query slow despite index on the column.

seeds.t: DELETE and UPDATE without WHERE affect every row
seeds.w: DELETE FROM users or UPDATE users SET active = false — without a WHERE clause, every row is affected.
seeds.c: always write the WHERE clause first before the DELETE or UPDATE, then verify your filter returns the expected rows with a SELECT. In production: run in a transaction, verify the row count, then commit or rollback. Enable safe update mode in MySQL (sql_safe_updates = 1).
seeds.s: accidental deletion or modification of all table rows.

seeds.t: Store timestamps in UTC, convert to local time in application
seeds.w: storing timestamps in local time or without timezone info — queries across timezone boundaries produce incorrect results and DST causes ambiguous times.
seeds.c: always store timestamps as UTC (TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE in PostgreSQL, DATETIME in MySQL storing UTC values). Convert to user's local time in the application layer at display time.
seeds.s: timestamps off by hours, or records appear in wrong date when users are in different timezones.

seeds.t: EXISTS is faster than IN for large subquery results
seeds.w: WHERE id IN (SELECT user_id FROM orders) when the subquery returns millions of rows — the entire subquery result is materialised and scanned.
seeds.c: use WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders WHERE orders.user_id = users.id) — EXISTS short-circuits on the first match and never builds the full list. IN with a small static list is fine; IN with a correlated subquery is almost always better rewritten as EXISTS or a JOIN.
seeds.s: query slows dramatically as the subquery result set grows.

seeds.t: Use window functions instead of self-joins for row comparisons
seeds.w: self-joining a table to compare each row with the previous row — this is O(n²) and hard to read.
seeds.c: use window functions with OVER (PARTITION BY ... ORDER BY ...): LAG(col) to access the previous row, LEAD for the next, ROW_NUMBER for ranking, SUM(...) OVER for running totals. Window functions are computed after WHERE/GROUP BY, do not collapse rows, and are far more efficient …
seeds.s: complex self-join query that would be two lines with LAG or ROW_NUMBER.

seeds.t: HAVING filters groups; WHERE filters rows before grouping
seeds.w: SELECT user_id, COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE COUNT(*) > 5 GROUP BY user_id — aggregate functions are not allowed in WHERE because filtering happens before grouping.
seeds.c: use HAVING: SELECT user_id, COUNT(*) FROM orders GROUP BY user_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 5. Also note: filter on non-aggregated columns in WHERE (faster, reduces rows before grouping) and on aggregated results in HAVING.
seeds.s: 'aggregate functions are not allowed in WHERE' error.

seeds.t: UNION ALL is faster than UNION when duplicates are acceptable
seeds.w: using UNION when combining result sets that don't contain duplicates — UNION performs a sort and deduplication step (like SELECT DISTINCT) even when it's unnecessary.
seeds.c: use UNION ALL to simply concatenate result sets without deduplication; it's faster and uses less memory. Reserve UNION for cases where you genuinely need to remove duplicates across the combined sets.
seeds.s: query combining two non-overlapping tables is slow because UNION sorts millions of rows unnecessarily.

seeds.t: Use CTEs for readable multi-step queries, not nested subqueries
seeds.w: nesting subqueries four levels deep — each layer is hard to read, debug, and often re-executed.
seeds.c: use Common Table Expressions (WITH): WITH recent AS (SELECT ... FROM orders WHERE created > NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days'), totals AS (SELECT ... FROM recent ...) SELECT * FROM totals. CTEs name each step, making the query readable and the plan transparent. Many databases materiali…
seeds.s: a query with three or more levels of nested subqueries that takes minutes to understand.

seeds.t: TRUNCATE is faster than DELETE for clearing entire tables
seeds.w: DELETE FROM large_table with no WHERE clause to empty a table — this is fully logged row by row and acquires row locks, which is extremely slow on large tables.
seeds.c: use TRUNCATE TABLE large_table which deallocates pages in bulk, resets identity/sequence counters, and is much faster. Note: TRUNCATE cannot be used when foreign keys reference the table unless using CASCADE; DELETE respects triggers while TRUNCATE may not (DB-dependent).
seeds.s: clearing a 10M-row table with DELETE takes minutes; TRUNCATE takes milliseconds.

seeds.t: BETWEEN is inclusive on both ends — use < for exclusive ranges
seeds.w: WHERE created_at BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31' intending to exclude January 31 — BETWEEN includes both endpoints, so rows at exactly midnight on Jan 31 are included.
seeds.c: use explicit comparisons for date ranges: WHERE created_at >= '2024-01-01' AND created_at < '2024-02-01'. This avoids ambiguity, handles timestamps correctly, and makes the intent explicit.
seeds.s: off-by-one errors in date range reports, especially with timestamp columns.

seeds.t: CASE expressions replace multiple queries for conditional output
seeds.w: running separate queries for each category and combining results in application code — this multiplies round-trips and is error-prone.
seeds.c: use CASE expressions directly in SELECT: SELECT name, CASE WHEN score >= 90 THEN 'A' WHEN score >= 80 THEN 'B' ELSE 'C' END AS grade FROM students. CASE also works in ORDER BY, GROUP BY, and aggregate functions: SUM(CASE WHEN status='active' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END).
seeds.s: application code applying conditional logic to query results that could be done in SQL.

seeds.t: Update rows using values from another table with UPDATE JOIN
seeds.w: fetching rows from one table, computing values in application code, then updating another table with N separate UPDATE statements — this is slow and not atomic.
seeds.c: use a single UPDATE with a JOIN (MySQL/SQL Server) or an UPDATE...FROM (PostgreSQL): UPDATE orders SET status = s.status FROM shipments s WHERE orders.id = s.order_id. This is a single atomic statement and orders of magnitude faster than row-by-row updates from application code.
seeds.s: an 'update from another table' loop runs thousands of UPDATE statements instead of one.

seeds.t: Use COALESCE and NULLIF for clean null substitution
seeds.w: using CASE WHEN col IS NULL THEN 'default' ELSE col END for null substitution — verbose and hard to scan.
seeds.c: COALESCE(col, 'default') returns the first non-null argument; stack multiple: COALESCE(col1, col2, 'fallback'). The complement, NULLIF(col, 0), returns NULL when col equals 0, avoiding division-by-zero: value / NULLIF(denominator, 0). Use these to write cleaner, more readable SQ…
seeds.s: long CASE expressions for simple null-handling that COALESCE or NULLIF would express in one function call.

seeds.t: Enable WAL Mode for Concurrent Reads and Writes
seeds.w: Using the default journal mode (DELETE) in a multi-reader application — readers block writers and vice versa, serialising all access.
seeds.c: Enable WAL mode with PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL; set once and it persists. WAL allows multiple concurrent readers alongside one writer with no blocking. Set PRAGMA synchronous=NORMAL for a good durability/performance balance in WAL mode.
seeds.s: reads blocking writes or writes blocking reads, poor throughput from a single-file database with multiple threads.

seeds.t: Foreign Key Enforcement Is Off by Default in SQLite
seeds.w: Defining FOREIGN KEY constraints and expecting them to be enforced — SQLite parses the syntax but ignores constraints unless explicitly enabled per connection.
seeds.c: Run PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON immediately after every connection is opened. Foreign key enforcement is a per-connection setting and does not persist. Add it to your connection initialisation code or use an on-connect hook.
seeds.s: FOREIGN KEY constraints defined but orphaned child rows created with no error.

seeds.t: Batch Writes in Transactions for Orders-of-Magnitude Speedup
seeds.w: Inserting or updating rows outside a transaction in a loop — SQLite commits a transaction (including fsync) after every statement by default, making 10,000 inserts take seconds instead of milliseconds.
seeds.c: Wrap batches of writes in explicit transactions: BEGIN; ... many inserts ...; COMMIT. For bulk imports, this can improve throughput by 100–500x. Use a batch size of 1000–10000 rows between commits to balance speed and memory.
seeds.s: bulk insert of 50,000 rows taking 30+ seconds that should take under a second.

seeds.t: SQLite Uses Type Affinity, Not Strict Typing
seeds.w: Assuming column types enforce the stored data type — SQLite allows storing any value in any column regardless of the declared type, storing '2024-01-01' in an INTEGER column silently.
seeds.c: Use STRICT tables (SQLite 3.37+): CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER, name TEXT) STRICT; to enforce types. Without STRICT, use CHECK constraints to validate. Be aware that INTEGER PRIMARY KEY is special — it aliases the rowid and is the only truly enforced integer.
seeds.s: numeric column storing strings because application code passed a string instead of an int.

seeds.t: INTEGER PRIMARY KEY Is an Alias for the rowid
seeds.w: Using TEXT or BLOB as the primary key when integer lookups are the primary access pattern — non-rowid primary keys are stored as a separate B-tree index, adding overhead.
seeds.c: Declare the primary key as INTEGER PRIMARY KEY (exactly) to alias the rowid, giving O(1) lookup by primary key without a separate index. Use WITHOUT ROWID tables when the primary key is a composite or non-integer and the table is small.
seeds.s: primary key lookups slower than expected because a separate index is used instead of the rowid.

seeds.t: Always Use Parameterised Queries to Prevent SQL Injection
seeds.w: Formatting SQL strings with user input directly: f'SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = "{name}"' — classic SQL injection vulnerability and also breaks on inputs containing quotes.
seeds.c: Use parameterised queries with ? placeholders: cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = ?', (name,)). The SQLite driver handles quoting and escaping. Use named parameters (:name) for readability with many parameters.
seeds.s: queries breaking on user names containing apostrophes, or SQL injection vulnerability.

seeds.t: Use EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN to Diagnose Missing Indexes
seeds.w: Adding indexes without verifying they are used, or leaving full scans in production queries.
seeds.c: Run EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN SELECT ... to see the access strategy: 'SCAN table' means a full scan; 'SEARCH table USING INDEX' means an index is used; 'SEARCH table USING COVERING INDEX' is optimal. Add indexes on columns used in WHERE, JOIN ON, and ORDER BY. SQLite's query planner us…
seeds.s: query slow on large tables despite having an index; EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN shows SCAN.

seeds.t: Use :memory: Databases for Fast Test Isolation
seeds.w: Using a file-based database for tests — tests run slower, leave behind files, and can interfere with each other if cleanup fails.
seeds.c: Open an in-memory database with sqlite3.connect(':memory:') for test databases; it is created fresh per connection and destroyed when the connection closes, with no IO overhead. For test suites sharing a database, use a shared-cache in-memory database: 'file::memory:?cache=share…
seeds.s: test suite creating and cleaning up database files, slow disk IO during test runs.

seeds.t: SQLite Connections Are Not Thread-Safe by Default
seeds.w: Sharing a single SQLite connection across threads — the default threading mode (serialised) adds locking overhead; multi-thread mode without proper care causes corruption.
seeds.c: Use one connection per thread (or coroutine). In Python, use check_same_thread=False only with explicit locking or connection-per-thread. For async Python, use aiosqlite which wraps SQLite in a thread pool. WAL mode allows concurrent readers across connections.
seeds.s: 'database is locked' errors or silent corruption from concurrent connection use.

seeds.t: Use INSERT...ON CONFLICT for Upserts
seeds.w: Using INSERT OR REPLACE which deletes and re-inserts the row, resetting any columns not in the INSERT and losing the original rowid — can also violate foreign key constraints if child rows reference the deleted row.
seeds.c: Use the UPSERT syntax (SQLite 3.24+): INSERT INTO t (id, val) VALUES (1, 'new') ON CONFLICT(id) DO UPDATE SET val=excluded.val. This atomically inserts or updates specific columns without deleting.
seeds.s: INSERT OR REPLACE unexpectedly clearing non-specified columns or breaking foreign key references.

seeds.t: WAL Files Grow Until Checkpointed
seeds.w: Enabling WAL mode without a checkpoint strategy — the WAL file grows indefinitely with writes and is never merged back to the main database file, consuming disk space.
seeds.c: SQLite auto-checkpoints when the WAL reaches 1000 pages by default. For explicit control: PRAGMA wal_checkpoint(TRUNCATE) after large batch writes, or set PRAGMA wal_autocheckpoint=100 for more frequent automatic checkpointing. Monitor the -wal file size.
seeds.s: WAL file growing to gigabytes over time without the main database file growing correspondingly.

seeds.t: Run VACUUM to Reclaim Space After Large Deletes
seeds.w: Deleting large amounts of data and expecting the database file to shrink — SQLite marks pages as free but does not return space to the OS without a VACUUM.
seeds.c: Run VACUUM after significant data deletions; it rewrites the entire database into a new file, reclaiming free pages. Use VACUUM INTO 'compact.db' for a zero-downtime copy. Enable auto_vacuum=INCREMENTAL for continuous background reclamation.
seeds.s: database file staying at 2 GB after deleting half the data.

seeds.t: Store Dates as ISO 8601 Text or Unix Timestamps
seeds.w: Storing dates as locale-formatted strings ('01/15/2024') or as non-standard formats — SQLite's date functions only understand ISO 8601 text ('2024-01-15'), Julian day numbers, or Unix timestamps.
seeds.c: Store dates as TEXT in ISO 8601 format ('2024-01-15T14:30:00Z') or as INTEGER Unix timestamps. SQLite's built-in functions (date(), datetime(), strftime()) work correctly with these formats. Use REAL for Julian day if high sub-second precision is needed.
seeds.s: date comparison and sorting wrong because dates stored as locale strings sort lexicographically incorrectly.

seeds.t: Use ATTACH to Query Across Multiple Database Files
seeds.w: Copying data between databases via application code, or maintaining denormalised copies of shared reference data in every database.
seeds.c: Use ATTACH DATABASE 'path/to/other.db' AS other; to access multiple databases in one query: SELECT * FROM main.orders JOIN other.users ON orders.user_id = other.users.id. Detach when done. Useful for test databases, sharded data, and read-only reference databases.
seeds.s: copying lookup tables between database files instead of querying them directly via ATTACH.

seeds.t: Create Indexes on Expressions for Function-Based Queries
seeds.w: Querying WHERE lower(email) = ? with no index — the lower() function prevents standard index use, causing a full scan.
seeds.c: Create an expression index: CREATE INDEX idx_email_lower ON users(lower(email)). SQLite uses it when the query's WHERE clause exactly matches the indexed expression. Useful for case-insensitive searches and computed columns. Ensure PRAGMA case_sensitive_like=OFF for LIKE queries…
seeds.s: case-insensitive queries slow despite having an index on the column.

seeds.t: Use FTS5 for Full-Text Search in SQLite
seeds.w: Implementing full-text search with LIKE '%keyword%' — leading wildcard prevents index use, full table scan on every search.
seeds.c: Create an FTS5 virtual table: CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE docs_fts USING fts5(title, body, content=docs, content_rowid=id). Query with MATCH: SELECT * FROM docs_fts WHERE docs_fts MATCH 'keyword'. FTS5 supports phrase search, prefix search, and ranking with BM25. Rebuild the index with…
seeds.s: LIKE-based search slow on large text collections, no relevance ranking.

seeds.t: Use WITHOUT ROWID for Composite Primary Key Tables
seeds.w: Using a normal table for data keyed entirely by a composite primary key — stores the primary key data twice (once in the hidden rowid index, once in the declared primary key index).
seeds.c: Add WITHOUT ROWID to tables where the declared primary key (composite or single non-integer) is the primary access pattern: CREATE TABLE kv (key TEXT, val TEXT, PRIMARY KEY (key)) WITHOUT ROWID. Data is stored in primary key order, reducing lookups and space. Not suitable for ta…
seeds.s: composite-key table using more space and performing slower lookups than necessary.

seeds.t: Shared Cache Mode Has Significant Limitations
seeds.w: Enabling shared cache (cache=shared in the URI) expecting it to improve performance for multi-thread access — shared cache uses table-level locking instead of file-level locking, which can cause MORE contention and deadlocks.
seeds.c: Use shared cache only for in-memory databases shared across threads in the same process where it is required for sharing. For file-based databases, use WAL mode with separate connections per thread for true concurrent access.
seeds.s: deadlocks and lock timeout errors after enabling shared cache that were not present before.

seeds.t: Use JSON Functions for Querying JSON Stored in SQLite
seeds.w: Parsing JSON in application code after fetching entire rows, just to filter on a JSON field — unnecessary data transfer and slow.
seeds.c: Use SQLite's JSON1 functions (built-in since 3.38): json_extract(data, '$.status') in WHERE clauses, json_each() to expand JSON arrays into rows, json_set() for updates. Create expression indexes on json_extract() for performance: CREATE INDEX ON t(json_extract(data, '$.user_id'…
seeds.s: loading entire rows to filter on a nested JSON field that json_extract could filter in the database.

seeds.t: Use the SQLite Backup API for Online Backups
seeds.w: Copying the SQLite database file with cp or file copy while the database is in use — may copy a partially-written or inconsistent state, producing a corrupt backup.
seeds.c: Use the SQLite Backup API (available in all language bindings): in Python, sqlite3.connect('backup.db') as dest: source_conn.backup(dest). This performs an online, consistent backup without locking the database for the duration. For WAL databases, also copy the -wal and -shm fil…
seeds.s: backup database file is corrupt or missing recent writes because it was copied while writes were in progress.

seeds.t: Statistics: p-Value Interpreted as Proof of Hypothesis
seeds.w: Treating p < 0.05 as proof that the hypothesis is true, or p > 0.05 as proof it is false.
seeds.c: A p-value is the probability of observing results at least this extreme if the null hypothesis were true. It does not measure the probability the hypothesis is true, the effect size, or practical significance.
seeds.s: Study 'proves' an effect with p = 0.04, fails to replicate at p = 0.06, leading to apparent contradiction.

seeds.t: Statistics: Confidence Interval Misread as Probability Range
seeds.w: Saying 'there is a 95% probability the true parameter lies in this interval' — a frequentist CI either contains the true value or it doesn't.
seeds.c: A 95% CI means: if we repeated this procedure many times, 95% of constructed intervals would contain the true parameter. The specific interval either does or doesn't contain the true value.
seeds.s: Analyst reports '95% chance conversion rate is between 12% and 18%' and makes probability-based decisions on that claim.

seeds.t: Statistics: HARKing — Hypothesizing After Results Are Known
seeds.w: Running analysis then writing the hypothesis as if it was pre-specified — fitting hypothesis to data rather than data to hypothesis.
seeds.c: Pre-register hypotheses before data collection on OSF or AsPredicted. Distinguish confirmatory from exploratory analyses. Report all tests run, not only significant ones.
seeds.s: Published finding 'confirms hypothesis' generated after seeing the data; fails to replicate.

seeds.t: Statistics: Multiple Comparisons Without Correction
seeds.w: Running 20 statistical tests and celebrating the one at p < 0.05 — with 20 tests at α=0.05, you expect one false positive even with no real effects.
seeds.c: Apply Bonferroni correction (divide α by number of tests), or Benjamini-Hochberg for FDR control. Pre-specify the primary endpoint. Report all tests.
seeds.s: Product team runs 20 A/B variants, celebrates the one winner; effect disappears in follow-up test.

seeds.t: Statistics: Survivorship Bias in Sample Selection
seeds.w: Analyzing only entities that survived a selection process (successful startups, published studies) and drawing conclusions about all entities.
seeds.c: Identify what selection process generated your sample. Actively seek the missing data — failed startups, unpublished studies, closed funds. Frame conclusions as applying only to survivors.
seeds.s: Analysis of successful startups concludes founders should drop out; failures who also dropped out are excluded.

seeds.t: Statistics: Ignoring Base Rate in Conditional Probability
seeds.w: Interpreting a positive test result as likely to be true positive, ignoring the base rate of the condition in the population.
seeds.c: Use Bayes' theorem: P(condition|positive) = P(positive|condition) × P(condition) / P(positive). A 99% accurate test for a 1-in-10,000 condition yields ~1% true positive rate.
seeds.s: Doctor gets positive test result for rare disease and tells patient they likely have it — ignoring that 99 of 100 positives are false positives.

seeds.t: Statistics: Treating Observed Correlation as Causal
seeds.w: Concluding X causes Y because they are correlated — consistent with X→Y, Y→X, Z→{X,Y}, or coincidence.
seeds.c: Establish causation via randomized experiments, natural experiments, instrumental variables, or DAGs. Label observational findings as associations, not causes.
seeds.s: Ice cream sales correlate with drowning deaths; company reduces ice cream production to prevent drownings.

seeds.t: Statistics: Running an Underpowered Study and Accepting Null
seeds.w: Designing a study too small to detect the effect of interest, getting p > 0.05, and concluding the effect doesn't exist.
seeds.c: Calculate required sample size via power analysis before collecting data (specify effect size, α, and desired power ≥ 0.80). A null result in an underpowered study means 'couldn't detect it,' not 'doesn't exist.'
seeds.s: Small pilot study finds no effect; team concludes product change didn't work; real effect was too small to detect.

seeds.t: Statistics: Simpson's Paradox — Aggregated vs Stratified Analysis
seeds.w: Drawing conclusions from aggregated data when a confounding variable reverses the relationship within subgroups.
seeds.c: Always stratify analysis by potential confounders before aggregating. Use causal DAGs to identify conditioning variables. Report subgroup results alongside overall results.
seeds.s: Treatment appears better overall but worse for every individual patient group; aggregate hides severity-mix confounding.

seeds.t: Statistics: Statistically Significant But Practically Meaningless Effect
seeds.w: Reporting statistical significance without effect size — with large sample sizes, trivially small effects become statistically significant.
seeds.c: Always report effect size (Cohen's d, r, odds ratio) alongside p-values. With N > 10,000, almost any nonzero effect reaches p < 0.05. Distinguish statistical from practical significance.
seeds.s: Company celebrates statistically significant 0.001% conversion improvement that doesn't justify engineering cost.

seeds.t: Statistics: Ecological Fallacy — Group-Level Inference to Individuals
seeds.w: Drawing conclusions about individuals from group-level aggregate statistics — group correlations don't imply the same relationship at the individual level.
seeds.c: Be explicit about level of analysis. Individual claims require individual data. Group findings describe groups, not members. Multilevel models can analyze both simultaneously.
seeds.s: Countries with higher chocolate consumption have more Nobel prizes; concluding eating chocolate makes individuals smarter.

seeds.t: Statistics: Ignoring Regression to the Mean
seeds.w: Attributing improvement after extreme performance to an intervention when natural regression to the mean would produce the same result.
seeds.c: Control for regression with a control group or randomization. Be especially skeptical of interventions applied after unusually poor performance.
seeds.s: Sales team slumps badly; motivational talk given; performance improves; talk credited; effect was regression to mean.

seeds.t: Svelte reactivity requires assignment — mutations do not trigger updates
seeds.w: arr.push(item) or obj.property = value when arr and obj are declared with let — Svelte's compiler tracks assignments, not mutations.
seeds.c: arr = [...arr, item] or obj = { ...obj, property: value }. For arrays: arr = arr (self-assignment) after mutation also works but is confusing. This is the most common source of 'why is my UI not updating' bugs in Svelte.
seeds.s: array or object data changes but the component does not re-render.

seeds.t: $: reactive declarations rerun when dependencies change
seeds.w: putting side effects in regular variable declarations expecting them to update. $: is Svelte's equivalent of computed properties — for simple derived values, prefer $: over stores.
seeds.c: $: doubled = count * 2 — recalculates whenever count changes. $: { console.log(count) } — runs a block when dependencies change. Dependencies are detected by the Svelte compiler from what variables are read.
seeds.s: derived values are stale, or side effects only run once on mount.

seeds.t: Use $ prefix to auto-subscribe to stores in components
seeds.w: manually calling store.subscribe() in a component without unsubscribing in onDestroy — memory leak.
seeds.c: use the $ prefix: $myStore auto-subscribes and auto-unsubscribes when the component is destroyed. Outside of components (no auto-cleanup): use const unsubscribe = store.subscribe(value => { ... }); and call unsubscribe() when done.
seeds.s: memory leaks, or store subscriptions firing after component destruction.

seeds.t: Use store.update() for values that depend on the current value
seeds.w: reading $store then calling store.set($store + 1) — race condition between read and set in async contexts.
seeds.c: store.update(n => n + 1) — receives the current value atomically and sets the new one. For objects: store.update(obj => ({ ...obj, key: value })). update() is also more efficient than set() when the new value derives from the old one.
seeds.s: counter loses increments under rapid updates, or stale value used in update.

seeds.t: Use {#key} to force component recreation when identity changes
seeds.w: expecting a component to reset its internal state when a prop changes — Svelte reuses component instances for performance.
seeds.c: wrap the component in {#key uniqueValue}<MyComponent />{/key} — when uniqueValue changes, Svelte destroys and recreates the component. Common use case: resetting a form component when switching between different items.
seeds.s: component shows stale state from a previous item when the driving data changes.

seeds.t: Forward events from child components with on:eventname
seeds.w: creating a custom event in a child just to re-emit a native DOM event the parent needs.
seeds.c: use event forwarding — add on:click (without a handler) to the element or component to forward the event to the parent. The parent then handles it normally. For custom events: createEventDispatcher(); dispatch('myevent', detail). For components: on:myevent={handler}.
seeds.s: verbose boilerplate creating wrapper events for DOM events the parent could handle directly.

seeds.t: bind: creates two-way binding — use sparingly and prefer data-down/events-up
seeds.w: using bind: on child component props as a general pattern — creates implicit data flow that is hard to trace.
seeds.c: use bind: for form inputs (bind:value, bind:checked, bind:this for DOM references). For component communication, prefer passing props down and dispatching events up — it makes data flow explicit.
seeds.s: complex state bugs from implicit two-way bindings between components.

seeds.t: Add a key to {#each} blocks for correct DOM reconciliation
seeds.w: {#each items as item} without a key — Svelte reconciles by position, causing incorrect updates when items are added, removed, or reordered.
seeds.c: {#each items as item (item.id)} where item.id is a stable unique identifier. The key tells Svelte which DOM node corresponds to which item, enabling correct animations and preserving component state.
seeds.s: list animations are wrong, inputs in list items retain wrong values after reorder.

seeds.t: Browser APIs are not available during SSR — guard with browser check
seeds.w: accessing window, document, localStorage, or navigator at module level or outside onMount — these do not exist during server-side rendering and throw ReferenceError.
seeds.c: import { browser } from '$app/environment' (SvelteKit); if (browser) { localStorage.setItem(...) }. Or access inside onMount() which only runs in the browser. For conditional logic: {#if browser}<ClientOnlyComponent />{/if}.
seeds.s: 'window is not defined' during build or SSR.

seeds.t: Svelte 5 uses $state rune instead of let for reactive state
seeds.w: using let count = 0 in Svelte 5 and expecting reactivity — Svelte 5 runes require explicit reactive declarations.
seeds.c: use let count = $state(0); the $state rune makes the variable deeply reactive. For derived values, use let doubled = $derived(count * 2) instead of $: doubled = count * 2. For side effects, $effect(() => { console.log(count) }) replaces $: { }. Svelte 4 syntax still works in leg…
seeds.s: variable does not trigger re-renders after assignment in a Svelte 5 component.

seeds.t: Use setContext and getContext to share data without prop drilling
seeds.w: passing data through many levels of components via props just to reach a deeply nested child — tedious to maintain and couples every intermediate component.
seeds.c: call setContext('key', value) in a parent component and getContext('key') in any descendant — no prop threading needed. Context is not reactive by default; wrap the value in a Svelte store or $state if descendants need to react to changes. Context is scoped to the component tree…
seeds.s: a config or theme object passed through five levels of prop drilling when getContext would suffice.

seeds.t: Actions encapsulate DOM behaviour with use:action directive
seeds.w: writing lifecycle hooks to attach and detach third-party DOM libraries manually in each component — duplicated setup and teardown logic.
seeds.c: define a Svelte action as function tooltip(node, params) { /* attach */ return { update(p) { /* param change */ }, destroy() { /* cleanup */ } }; } and apply with use:tooltip={params}. Actions receive the DOM node, handle setup and cleanup, and are composable. Perfect for click-…
seeds.s: repeated addEventListener/removeEventListener boilerplate across components for the same DOM behaviour.

seeds.t: Use built-in transitions and animate for declarative motion
seeds.w: writing CSS animations manually and toggling classes in JavaScript for enter/leave effects — verbose and hard to coordinate with DOM updates.
seeds.c: use transition:fade, in:fly={{ y: 20 }}, out:scale from 'svelte/transition', and animate:flip from 'svelte/animate' for list reorder animations. Transitions trigger automatically when elements are added to or removed from the DOM. Combine with {#each} and the key block for list …
seeds.s: complex CSS class toggling to animate elements in/out when Svelte transitions would handle it declaratively.

seeds.t: derived() creates stores that auto-update from dependencies
seeds.w: manually subscribing to multiple stores, combining their values in a variable, and managing unsubscriptions — complex and error-prone.
seeds.c: use derived([store1, store2], ([$s1, $s2]) => $s1 + $s2) to create a new readable store that automatically recomputes when any input store changes. For async derived values, the callback can accept a set function: derived(source, ($src, set) => { fetch($src).then(set) }).
seeds.s: multiple manual store subscriptions combined in reactive statements that could be a single derived store.

seeds.t: bind:this is only populated after the component mounts
seeds.w: accessing a bind:this reference during module or component initialisation — the DOM node does not exist yet and the reference is undefined.
seeds.c: access bind:this references inside onMount or in event handlers that fire after mounting. In Svelte 5, use $effect to react when the reference changes. For inputs that need immediate focus, focus inside onMount: onMount(() => inputEl.focus()).
seeds.s: bind:this reference is undefined when accessed at the top level of a script block.

seeds.t: Svelte 5 uses snippets instead of slots for component content
seeds.w: using <slot> in Svelte 5 components expecting future-proof code — slots still work but snippets are the Svelte 5 way.
seeds.c: define snippets with {#snippet name(params)}...{/snippet} in the parent and accept them as props: {#if children}{@render children()}{/if}. The special children snippet receives default slot content. Named snippets replace named slots: <MyCard {header} {footer} />. Snippets are t…
seeds.s: using deprecated slots in Svelte 5 code when snippets provide better composition.

seeds.t: Any object with subscribe() qualifies as a Svelte store
seeds.w: wrapping an external observable (RxJS, custom EventEmitter) in a Svelte writable store just to use it in templates — unnecessary conversion layer.
seeds.c: any object implementing the store contract (subscribe(fn) that returns an unsubscribe function) works with the $ auto-subscription prefix and reactive declarations. Expose third-party observables as Svelte-compatible stores by implementing subscribe without the full writable/rea…
seeds.s: RxJS Observable wrapped in a writable store with manual subscription management instead of using the store contract.

seeds.t: Await blocks handle loading and error states declaratively
seeds.w: managing promise state manually with loading, data, and error variables plus a try/catch — boilerplate that every async component repeats.
seeds.c: use {#await promise} loading... {:then data} {data.name} {:catch err} {err.message} {/await}. The template re-renders as the promise progresses through pending, resolved, and rejected states. Reassign the promise variable to trigger a new fetch (e.g. on refresh).
seeds.s: async data loading handled with three useState variables and a useEffect equivalent when an await block would reduce it to three template lines.

seeds.t: Event modifiers replace common event handler boilerplate
seeds.w: calling event.preventDefault() and event.stopPropagation() manually in every handler where they're needed.
seeds.c: use Svelte event modifiers directly on the directive: on:click|preventDefault|stopPropagation={handler}. Available modifiers include preventDefault, stopPropagation, self (only fire if event target is element itself), once, passive, capture, and trusted. Modifiers chain with |. …
seeds.s: repetitive event.preventDefault() calls in handlers that could be event modifier declarations.

seeds.t: Force unwrapping crashes on nil — use guard or if let
seeds.w: using ! to force-unwrap an Optional when the value might be nil — crashes with 'unexpectedly found nil'.
seeds.c: use if let, guard let, or optional chaining (?.). Reserve ! only for values guaranteed non-nil by design (IBOutlets, values set in viewDidLoad before use).
seeds.s: EXC_BAD_INSTRUCTION crash at the force-unwrap site.

seeds.t: Capture lists prevent retain cycles in closures
seeds.w: capturing self strongly inside a closure stored by self — creates a retain cycle.
seeds.c: use [weak self] in the capture list: { [weak self] in guard let self else { return } ... }. Use [unowned self] only when self is guaranteed to outlive the closure.
seeds.s: memory leak, deinit never called.

seeds.t: UI updates must happen on the main thread
seeds.w: updating UIKit or SwiftUI state from a background thread — crashes or visual glitches.
seeds.c: annotate with @MainActor, or use await MainActor.run { ... }. In SwiftUI, @MainActor on the ViewModel ensures published property changes run on the main thread.
seeds.s: 'UI API called from background thread' warning, sporadic crashes.

seeds.t: Structs are value types — assignment copies, not references
seeds.w: expecting a struct variable to update another after mutation.
seeds.c: use structs for data models (Codable, SwiftUI state) and classes for objects with shared identity.
seeds.s: mutation to one variable does not affect the other.

seeds.t: Async functions do not automatically run on the main actor
seeds.w: assuming async functions run on the main thread — they run on a cooperative thread pool by default.
seeds.c: annotate the function or its enclosing type with @MainActor if it touches UI.
seeds.s: 'Publishing changes from background threads is not allowed' in SwiftUI.

seeds.t: Codable uses exact property names unless a decoder strategy is set
seeds.w: expecting a Codable struct with camelCase properties to decode snake_case JSON — fails silently.
seeds.c: set decoder.keyDecodingStrategy = .convertFromSnakeCase, or implement custom CodingKeys.
seeds.s: all Codable properties are nil after decoding valid JSON.

seeds.t: didSet and willSet do not fire during init
seeds.w: relying on didSet/willSet to perform initialisation side effects — property observers are not called when a property is set in an initialiser.
seeds.c: call the setup code explicitly in init(), or use a lazy property.
seeds.s: observer-based side effects (updating UI, triggering computation) do not run on first assignment.

seeds.t: Switch on enums with associated values requires pattern matching
seeds.w: if myEnum == .loading — this only works for enums without associated values.
seeds.c: use if case .loading = myEnum for enums with associated values, or switch with let bindings: case .success(let value). The == operator requires Equatable; enums with associated values are not automatically Equatable.
seeds.s: compiler error 'binary operator == cannot be applied', or comparison always false.

seeds.t: @escaping marks closures that outlive the function call
seeds.w: storing a closure parameter in a property or calling it asynchronously without marking it @escaping — compiler error.
seeds.c: add @escaping to the closure parameter type: func fetch(completion: @escaping (Result) -> Void). Non-escaping closures are called synchronously before the function returns. @escaping closures require careful capture semantics — check for retain cycles.
seeds.s: 'Escaping closure captures non-escaping parameter' compiler error.

seeds.t: Check Task.isCancelled in long async work
seeds.w: long async loops that never check for cancellation — the task runs to completion even after the caller cancels it.
seeds.c: check Task.isCancelled or call try Task.checkCancellation() at regular intervals inside loops and before heavy operations.
seeds.s: cancelled tasks continue running, wasting resources and potentially causing state inconsistency.

seeds.t: Actor-isolated properties cannot be accessed from outside without await
seeds.w: accessing an actor's properties or methods from outside the actor synchronously — compiler error.
seeds.c: use await to access actor-isolated members from an async context: await myActor.someProperty. Design actors so that cross-actor calls are infrequent to avoid performance overhead.
seeds.s: 'Expression is 'async' but is not marked with 'await'' compiler error.

seeds.t: Array subscript access crashes on out-of-bounds — use indices or first/last
seeds.w: array[i] when i might be out of bounds — crashes with 'Index out of range'.
seeds.c: check array.indices.contains(i) before subscripting, or use array.first, array.last which return Optional. For safe subscripting: extension Array { subscript(safe index: Int) -> Element? { indices.contains(index) ? self[index] : nil } }.
seeds.s: crash when accessing arrays of variable length.

seeds.t: String interpolation does not control numeric formatting — use formatted()
seeds.w: 'Value: \(someDouble)' for currency or precise decimals — produces full floating-point representation.
seeds.c: use formatted(): someDouble.formatted(.number.precision(.fractionLength(2))). For currency: amount.formatted(.currency(code: 'USD')). Or String(format: '%.2f', someDouble) for classic printf-style.
seeds.s: numbers display with 15 decimal places, or currency missing symbol.

seeds.t: NSObject subclasses require overriding both == and hash together
seeds.w: overriding == (isEqual:) on an NSObject subclass without also overriding hash — objects that compare equal must have the same hash value, or they break Set and Dictionary behaviour.
seeds.c: if you override isEqual(_:), always override var hash: Int as well. Use the same fields for both.
seeds.s: Set contains duplicates, Dictionary keys behave incorrectly after equality override.

seeds.t: Store Combine subscriptions in cancellables or they cancel immediately
seeds.w: calling .sink without storing the returned AnyCancellable — the subscription is immediately cancelled and the callback never fires.
seeds.c: store in a Set<AnyCancellable> or a property: cancellables.insert(publisher.sink { ... }). The subscription lives as long as the cancellable.
seeds.s: Combine publisher callback never fires despite data being published.

seeds.t: Types shared across concurrency boundaries must conform to Sendable
seeds.w: passing a non-Sendable class instance to a Task or across actor boundaries — compiler warning in Swift 5.7, error in strict concurrency mode.
seeds.c: make types Sendable by using value types, actors, or @unchecked Sendable with manual synchronisation.
seeds.s: 'Passing argument of non-sendable type' warnings.

seeds.t: System Design: Premature Microservices Decomposition
seeds.w: Splitting a system into microservices before the domain boundaries are understood — the result is a distributed monolith with network latency and no deployment independence.
seeds.c: Start with a modular monolith. Extract services only when you have stable, well-understood boundaries with different scaling needs, team ownership, or deployment cadence. Boundaries emerge from domain events, not guesses.
seeds.s: Services share a database and deploy together; distributed tracing needed for a simple user lookup; more complex than a monolith with none of the benefits.

seeds.t: System Design: Webhook Handler Not Idempotent
seeds.w: Processing incoming webhooks (Stripe, GitHub, Twilio) without idempotency — webhook providers retry on timeout or error, causing duplicate processing.
seeds.c: Store the webhook event ID (or signature) and check before processing. Upsert rather than insert. Respond with 200 quickly and process asynchronously. All major webhook providers guarantee at-least-once delivery.
seeds.s: Payment processed twice because Stripe retried a timeout; inventory decremented double on duplicate order webhook.

seeds.t: System Design: API Launched Without Versioning
seeds.w: Deploying a public or internal API without a versioning strategy — any breaking change forces all clients to update simultaneously or breaks them.
seeds.c: Version from day one: /v1/ in the URL or Accept: application/vnd.api+json;version=1 header. Maintain previous versions for a defined deprecation window. Document breaking vs non-breaking changes.
seeds.s: Removing a response field breaks all clients; renaming an endpoint requires a flag day migration across all consumers.

seeds.t: System Design: Synchronous Saga Defeats the Purpose
seeds.w: Implementing a saga by calling each step synchronously in sequence and waiting for all to complete — you get the complexity of distributed coordination without the fault tolerance benefit.
seeds.c: Sagas should be choreography (events trigger next step) or orchestration (saga orchestrator sends async commands). Each step runs independently; compensation runs on failure. Never block the caller during multi-step distributed transactions.
seeds.s: Service A waits for B which waits for C; a 200ms saga takes 600ms; downstream failure fails the whole chain synchronously.

seeds.t: System Design: Chatty API Interface Requiring Many Round Trips
seeds.w: Designing an API where clients need 5-10 calls to render a single screen — each round trip adds network latency and couples the client to backend data structures.
seeds.c: Design experience-oriented APIs (BFF pattern): each API endpoint returns all the data needed for one UI view. Use GraphQL or aggregating endpoints to reduce round trips.
seeds.s: Mobile app makes 8 sequential API calls to render a profile screen; 3G latency makes it take 4 seconds.

seeds.t: System Design: Caching Mutable Shared State Without Invalidation Strategy
seeds.w: Adding a cache for data that can be modified by other services or users without a cache invalidation strategy — stale data is served indefinitely.
seeds.c: Choose a cache invalidation strategy before caching: TTL-based (for tolerable staleness), write-through (update cache on write), event-based (invalidate on domain events). Cache only data whose staleness is acceptable.
seeds.s: User updates their name; other users see the old name for the cache TTL; worse if TTL is infinite.

seeds.t: System Design: No Fallback When Dependencies Are Unavailable
seeds.w: Building services that fail completely when a non-critical dependency (recommendations engine, social features, A/B test service) is unavailable.
seeds.c: Design for partial availability: return a degraded response without the optional component. Cache last-known-good responses. Use feature flags to disable non-critical features independently.
seeds.s: Recommendation engine outage takes down the product page; A/B test service slowdown makes all checkouts slow.

seeds.t: System Design: Event Sourcing Applied to Simple CRUD
seeds.w: Using event sourcing (storing state as a sequence of events, replaying for current state) for domains that are simple CRUD with no need for audit trail or temporal queries.
seeds.c: Event sourcing suits domains where history matters (financial ledgers, inventory, audit logs). For user profiles, settings, and basic data, use conventional CRUD with a simple audit log if needed.
seeds.s: Simple user profile service now requires rebuilding state from hundreds of events; team can't understand or modify anything; massive over-engineering.

seeds.t: Systems Thinking: Treating Symptoms Instead of Systemic Cause
seeds.w: Applying a fix to the symptom of a problem rather than the feedback loop causing it — the symptom returns, often stronger, because the underlying system is unchanged.
seeds.c: Map the causal loop diagram. Identify the feedback loop generating the symptom. Intervene at the loop, not the symptom. Ask: 'If this fix works, what happens to the system pressure that produced the symptom?'
seeds.s: Customer service hires more staff to handle complaint volume; complaint volume grows because root cause product issue isn't addressed.

seeds.t: Systems Thinking: Linear Thinking When System Has Delays
seeds.w: Applying more intervention when there's no immediate response, not accounting for the delay between action and effect — the intervention overshoots and creates oscillation.
seeds.c: Identify delays in the system before acting. Act in smaller increments and wait for the full effect before adjusting. Instability in goal-seeking systems is often caused by corrective actions that ignore delays.
seeds.s: Interest rates raised but inflation doesn't immediately fall; more increases applied; eventually overdone; recession results from overcorrection.

seeds.t: Systems Thinking: Confusing Stocks and Flows
seeds.w: Trying to change a stock rapidly — stocks (accumulated levels like trust, pollution, skills, debt) can only be changed at the rate of their flows.
seeds.c: Identify whether you're dealing with a stock (accumulated level) or a flow (rate of change). Stocks have inertia. To change a stock, change the inflow or outflow rate over time.
seeds.s: Trying to instantly rebuild customer trust after a breach; trust is a stock that can only rebuild through consistent positive experience over time.

seeds.t: Systems Thinking: Goodhart's Law — Metric Becomes Target
seeds.w: Setting a metric as a target causes agents to optimize for the metric rather than the underlying goal, destroying the metric's value as a measure.
seeds.c: Use multiple diverse metrics that are hard to game simultaneously. Rotate metrics. Monitor for gaming behavior. Keep the original goal visible alongside the metric.
seeds.s: Team measured on story points; story points inflated; velocity goes up, throughput doesn't; metric is now useless.

seeds.t: Systems Thinking: Shifting the Burden — Symptomatic Fix Creates Dependency
seeds.w: Applying a symptomatic fix that relieves pressure so the fundamental solution is never implemented — the symptomatic fix becomes a dependency.
seeds.c: Use symptomatic solutions only as temporary relief while investing in the fundamental fix. Make the long-term solution the priority. Watch for symptomatic fixes that reduce urgency to fix the root cause.
seeds.s: Ops team manually monitors and restarts failing service daily; 'works fine' so automated fix never prioritized; dependency on manual intervention grows.

seeds.t: Systems Thinking: Solutions That Worsen the Problem
seeds.w: Applying a fix that relieves the immediate symptom but creates a delayed side-effect that worsens the original problem — the second-order effect is stronger than the first.
seeds.c: Map second-order effects before implementing solutions. Ask 'what are the downstream consequences of this fix?' and 'what happens to the overall system when this is in place?'
seeds.s: Suppressing forest fires for decades leads to fuel accumulation; when fire occurs it's catastrophically worse; the fix worsened the eventual problem.

seeds.t: Systems Thinking: Success to the Successful — Rich Get Richer
seeds.w: Designing a system with winner-take-all feedback loops where success creates advantages that enable more success, gradually excluding others from competing.
seeds.c: Identify reinforcing feedback loops that advantage incumbents. Counter with equalizing mechanisms. Be cautious of systems with strong network effects.
seeds.s: Platform allocates more traffic to top performers; top performers get more reviews; gap widens; new entrants can't compete.

seeds.t: Systems Thinking: Pushing Hard on Low-Leverage Interventions
seeds.w: Applying massive effort to change a system's numbers (rates, constants) while ignoring high-leverage structural interventions.
seeds.c: Meadows' hierarchy: changing numbers is low leverage; changing structure (stocks, flows, feedback connections) is higher leverage; changing goals and paradigms is highest leverage.
seeds.s: Company increases sales targets 20% every quarter; structural bottleneck in support means growth triggers churn; target number isn't the real lever.

seeds.t: Systems Thinking: Goal-Seeking System Without Corrective Feedback
seeds.w: Designing a system that aims for a goal but has no feedback mechanism to detect when it's off course — the system has no way to self-correct.
seeds.c: Every goal-seeking system needs a sensor (to measure current state), a comparator (to measure gap from goal), and a corrective action mechanism. Without feedback, the system overshoots or undershoots permanently.
seeds.s: Thermostat with no temperature sensor; set to 68°F but has no way to know actual temperature; heater runs at fixed rate.

seeds.t: Systems Thinking: Applying Outdated Mental Model to Changed System
seeds.w: Using a mental model of how a system works that was accurate in the past but no longer reflects the current structure.
seeds.c: Periodically audit assumptions: 'What would need to be true for my model of this system to be wrong?' Seek disconfirming data. Update mental models when evidence of structural change appears.
seeds.s: Manager applies 2015-era management practices to 2025 distributed team; model was built for co-located work; effectiveness drops.

seeds.t: Technical Writing: README Describes Instead of Getting Users Started
seeds.w: Writing a README that describes what the project is without telling someone how to install it, run it, and see something working in under 5 minutes.
seeds.c: Structure READMEs around user goals: (1) one-line description, (2) quickstart with copy-paste commands, (3) what it does, (4) link to full docs. Get a new user working before they understand everything.
seeds.s: Developer clones repo, reads description, can't figure out how to run it, moves on.

seeds.t: Technical Writing: Vague Commit Messages
seeds.w: Writing commit messages like 'fix bug,' 'update,' or 'refactor' — no context about what broke, what changed, or why.
seeds.c: Follow conventional commits: type(scope): short summary in imperative. Body explains what changed and why. Example: 'fix(auth): correct token expiry check using local time instead of UTC.'
seeds.s: Six months later, no one can understand which commit introduced a regression.

seeds.t: Technical Writing: API Documentation Without Code Examples
seeds.w: Documenting API endpoints with parameter descriptions but no request/response examples — developers learn APIs by seeing them, not reading abstract parameter tables.
seeds.c: Every endpoint should have a complete, copy-paste-ready example: curl command, request body, and sample response. Show the happy path and at least one error case.
seeds.s: Developer can't integrate the API without trial and error; support tickets spike for basic usage questions.

seeds.t: Technical Writing: Comments That Restate What Code Does
seeds.w: Writing comments like '// increment i by 1' next to 'i++' — they add noise without information and drift out of sync with the code.
seeds.c: Comments should explain why, not what. 'What' is in the code. 'Why' is not obvious from reading it. Comment on business rules, non-obvious algorithmic choices, historical context, and known limitations.
seeds.s: Comment says 'multiply by 1.1' but code multiplies by 1.12 (tax rate changed); comment causes confusion.

seeds.t: Technical Writing: Architecture Decision Records Without Rejected Alternatives
seeds.w: Writing ADRs that document the decision made but not the alternatives considered and why they were rejected.
seeds.c: Include in every ADR: (1) context and problem, (2) decision and rationale, (3) alternatives considered and why rejected, (4) consequences. The alternatives section is the most valuable for future readers.
seeds.s: New engineer joins, disagrees with an architecture choice, spends a week re-investigating alternatives already rejected for good reasons.

seeds.t: Technical Writing: Error Messages Without Actionable Information
seeds.w: Displaying error messages like 'An error occurred' or 'Something went wrong' — the user knows something went wrong; they need to know what and what to do.
seeds.c: Error messages should contain: what happened (specific), why it happened (if known), what to do about it. Include an error code if support is needed. Never show stack traces to end users.
seeds.s: User sees 'An error occurred' and contacts support; support has no error ID to look up; 30-minute call to diagnose.

seeds.t: Technical Writing: CHANGELOG Not Updated When Code Changes
seeds.w: Making breaking changes, new features, and deprecations without updating a CHANGELOG — users have no way to understand what changed between versions.
seeds.c: Update CHANGELOG.md with every PR that changes user-visible behavior. Use Keep a Changelog format: Added, Changed, Deprecated, Removed, Fixed, Security per version. Tag releases to match.
seeds.s: Downstream library consumer upgrades, something breaks, reads CHANGELOG, it was last updated 6 months ago.

seeds.t: Technical Writing: Burying the Main Point
seeds.w: Starting a document or email with extensive background before stating what the reader needs to know or do — the main point appears at the end.
seeds.c: Lead with the conclusion. State what the reader needs to know or do in the first sentence. Then provide context and reasoning. Readers scan; if the point isn't in the first paragraph, it may be missed.
seeds.s: Email with five paragraphs of background; reader responds to first paragraph; misses action item at end.

seeds.t: Technical Writing: Outdated Documentation Left Without Deletion or Archiving
seeds.w: Leaving outdated documentation in place without marking it as deprecated — users find and follow wrong instructions.
seeds.c: When documentation is no longer correct: mark it with a deprecation banner linking to the current version, move to an 'archive' section, or delete it. Wrong documentation is worse than no documentation.
seeds.s: New developer follows a 2-year-old setup guide, configures the system incorrectly, spends hours debugging.

seeds.t: Never Commit Local tfstate to Version Control
seeds.w: Committing terraform.tfstate to git — teammates overwrite each other's state and secrets are exposed in plain text in the repo.
seeds.c: Store state in a remote backend (S3 + DynamoDB, GCS, Terraform Cloud) with state locking to prevent concurrent writes. Add *.tfstate and *.tfstate.backup to .gitignore immediately.
seeds.s: state conflicts on team merge or credentials visible in git history.

seeds.t: Never Hardcode Credentials in Provider Blocks
seeds.w: Setting access_key and secret_key inline in the provider block — credentials end up in state files and version control.
seeds.c: Use environment variables (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID), shared credentials files, or IAM instance roles; Terraform picks them up automatically. Use sensitive = true on variables that must be passed in.
seeds.s: credentials leaked in .tfstate or git history.

seeds.t: for_each Beats count for Resource Collections
seeds.w: Using count = length(var.list) to create multiple resources — removing an item from the middle renumbers indices and Terraform destroys/recreates the wrong resources.
seeds.c: Use for_each with a map or set of strings so each resource is addressed by a stable key, not an index. Convert lists with toset() when order doesn't matter.
seeds.s: terraform plan shows unexpected destroys when only adding to a list.

seeds.t: Use depends_on Only for Hidden Dependencies
seeds.w: Adding depends_on everywhere to 'be safe' — this prevents Terraform from parallelizing and forces sequential apply even when resources are independent.
seeds.c: Terraform infers dependencies from attribute references automatically; only add explicit depends_on for side effects not reflected in resource attributes (e.g., an IAM policy that must exist before a Lambda invocation).
seeds.s: unnecessarily slow applies or dependency cycles.

seeds.t: Use replace Instead of Deprecated taint Command
seeds.w: Running terraform taint resource to force recreation — the taint command is deprecated since Terraform 0.15.2.
seeds.c: Use terraform apply -replace=resource to directly target resource recreation in a single step without modifying state. For permanent markers, use lifecycle { replace_triggered_by }.
seeds.s: deprecation warnings or scripts relying on terraform taint.

seeds.t: Pin Module Sources to Specific Versions
seeds.w: Referencing a registry module without a version constraint, or using a branch name in a git source — modules update silently and break existing infrastructure.
seeds.c: Always specify version = "~> X.Y" for registry modules and use git ref tags (?ref=v1.2.3) for git sources. Lock root module providers with a .terraform.lock.hcl file committed to version control.
seeds.s: unexpected infrastructure changes after a module update.

seeds.t: Locals Are for Computed Values, Variables for Inputs
seeds.w: Putting all configuration into input variables including intermediate computed values, making callers pass redundant data.
seeds.c: Use local values (locals {}) for expressions derived from variables or resource attributes; reserve var.* for values that callers legitimately need to supply. Locals reduce repetition and are not exposed as module inputs.
seeds.s: module interfaces with many calculated inputs that could be locals.

seeds.t: Mark Sensitive Outputs to Prevent Log Exposure
seeds.w: Outputting database passwords or tokens without sensitive = true — values appear in plaintext in CI logs and terraform output.
seeds.c: Add sensitive = true to any output containing secrets; Terraform redacts the value in logs while still making it available to other modules. In Terraform Cloud, use ephemeral values or variable sets for secrets.
seeds.s: secrets visible in CI pipeline logs or terraform show output.

seeds.t: Use prevent_destroy for Critical Stateful Resources
seeds.w: Leaving databases and storage buckets unprotected — a misconfigured plan can destroy them without warning.
seeds.c: Add lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } to RDS instances, S3 buckets with data, and similar stateful resources; Terraform will error if a plan would destroy them. Pair with deletion_protection in the resource itself.
seeds.s: accidental data loss from an unreviewed terraform apply.

seeds.t: Data Sources Fetch Current State on Every Apply
seeds.w: Expecting data sources to be cached from a previous plan — they re-query the provider on every apply, so externally changed values can shift plans.
seeds.c: Understand that data source results are not stored in state long-term; if stability is needed, read the value once and store it as a local or output. Use refresh = false on targeted applies with caution.
seeds.s: plan shows changes only because a data source returned a different external value.

seeds.t: Workspaces Are Not a Full Environment Isolation Strategy
seeds.w: Using terraform workspaces as the primary way to separate dev/staging/prod environments — they share the same backend and code, making it easy to apply to the wrong environment.
seeds.c: Use separate state files per environment via different backend keys or directories, or use Terragrunt for DRY multi-environment setups. Workspaces are appropriate for ephemeral feature environments from the same codebase.
seeds.s: accidental prod apply when workspace was wrong.

seeds.t: Use moved Block to Rename Without Destroy/Recreate
seeds.w: Deleting a resource from config and adding it under a new address — Terraform destroys the old and creates a new one, causing downtime.
seeds.c: Add a moved { from = old_address; to = new_address } block to record the rename in config; Terraform updates state without touching the real resource. Remove the moved block after the apply is merged.
seeds.s: unnecessary recreation of resources during refactoring.

seeds.t: Use Provider Aliases for Multi-Region Resources
seeds.w: Declaring multiple provider blocks for the same cloud in different regions without aliases — Terraform only uses one and ignores duplicates.
seeds.c: Give each regional provider an alias (provider "aws" { region = "us-east-1" alias = "use1" }) and pass provider = aws.use1 explicitly on resources that need it. Modules accept a providers map argument.
seeds.s: resources created in the wrong region or provider duplication errors.

seeds.t: Import Block Brings Existing Resources Under Management
seeds.w: Running terraform import resource id imperatively without a plan preview — changes state without reviewing the generated config.
seeds.c: Use the import block (Terraform 1.5+) inside configuration to describe the import declaratively; run terraform plan to preview the generated config and state change before applying. Generate initial config with -generate-config-out.
seeds.s: state drift from imperative imports done without a matching config.

seeds.t: Dynamic Blocks Generate Repeated Nested Blocks
seeds.w: Duplicating nested blocks (like ingress rules or tags) manually for each variation — error-prone and unmaintainable.
seeds.c: Use dynamic blocks to iterate over a variable and generate nested blocks programmatically. The iterator attribute controls the loop variable name inside the block. Use sparingly — they reduce readability when overused.
seeds.s: many near-identical nested blocks that differ by one attribute.

seeds.t: Always Review Plan Before Apply in CI/CD
seeds.w: Running terraform apply -auto-approve in CI without a manual approval step — any code mistake deploys immediately to production.
seeds.c: Separate plan and apply stages; post the plan output as a CI artifact or PR comment for human review before triggering apply. In Terraform Cloud, use plan-and-apply run workflows with required approvals.
seeds.s: infrastructure broken by automated apply of a bad plan.

seeds.t: Module Outputs Must Be Declared to Be Consumed
seeds.w: Trying to access module.name.resource.attribute directly from a child module — Terraform does not expose internal resource attributes across module boundaries.
seeds.c: Declare an output in the child module that surfaces the value, then reference module.child.output_name in the parent. Keep module interfaces explicit and minimal.
seeds.s: 'An argument named X is not expected here' when crossing module boundaries.

seeds.t: ignore_changes Prevents Drift Detection for That Attribute
seeds.w: Adding lifecycle { ignore_changes = all } to silence plan noise — Terraform stops tracking the resource entirely, hiding real drift.
seeds.c: Ignore only specific attributes managed outside Terraform (e.g., ignore_changes = [desired_count] for ECS when an autoscaler controls it). Document why each ignore_changes entry exists.
seeds.s: real infrastructure drift missed because ignore_changes is too broad.

seeds.t: Use templatefile() Function Over Deprecated template_file
