seeds.w: Using the template_file data source for rendering templates — deprecated since Terraform 0.12 and not updated with new features.
seeds.c: Use the built-in templatefile(path, vars) function directly in resource arguments or locals; it reads the file and substitutes ${var} placeholders. Prefer templatefile over heredoc templates for maintainability.
seeds.s: deprecation warnings or template_file data source in modern Terraform code.

seeds.t: Enforce Canonical Format with terraform fmt in CI
seeds.w: Skipping formatting enforcement — style inconsistencies cause noisy diffs and review friction.
seeds.c: Run terraform fmt -recursive -check in CI to fail PRs with unformatted files; run terraform fmt locally or in a pre-commit hook to fix them. Also run terraform validate to catch config errors before plan.
seeds.s: unformatted code merged to main, inconsistent indentation across the codebase.

seeds.t: Dispose geometries, materials, and textures to prevent memory leaks
seeds.w: removing a mesh from the scene with scene.remove(mesh) — the underlying GPU resources (geometry buffers, textures, shader programs) remain in VRAM.
seeds.c: call mesh.geometry.dispose(), mesh.material.dispose(), and texture.dispose() before or after removing from the scene. For complex objects, traverse: mesh.traverse(obj => { obj.geometry?.dispose(); obj.material?.dispose() }). Use renderer.info.memory to monitor texture and geomet…
seeds.s: VRAM usage grows monotonically as objects are swapped out, eventually causing slowdowns or crashes.

seeds.t: Set pixel ratio to devicePixelRatio but cap it for performance
seeds.w: not setting the renderer pixel ratio — rendering looks blurry on high-DPI screens. Also wrong: setting it unconditionally to window.devicePixelRatio — on some devices this is 3x or 4x, causing 9-16x the fragment work.
seeds.c: renderer.setPixelRatio(Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio, 2)). Cap at 2 for a balance of sharpness and performance. Call renderer.setSize(width, height) whenever the window resizes.
seeds.s: canvas looks blurry on Retina displays, or the GPU is overloaded on high-DPI mobile devices.

seeds.t: Use requestAnimationFrame via renderer.setAnimationLoop, not setInterval
seeds.w: using setInterval or setTimeout for the render loop — these aren't synchronised with the display refresh rate and waste resources when the tab is hidden.
seeds.c: renderer.setAnimationLoop((time) => { update(time); renderer.render(scene, camera) }). This uses requestAnimationFrame internally, pauses when the tab is hidden, and synchronises with the display's VSync. Pass null to stop the loop. For XR (VR/AR), setAnimationLoop is mandatory …
seeds.s: render loop continues consuming CPU/GPU when the browser tab is not visible.

seeds.t: Share geometries and materials across multiple meshes
seeds.w: creating a new BoxGeometry and MeshStandardMaterial for each of 1000 identical boxes — 1000 separate GPU buffer uploads and shader compilations.
seeds.c: create geometry and material once, then instantiate many meshes: const geo = new BoxGeometry(); const mat = new MeshStandardMaterial({ color: 'red' }); meshes = positions.map(p => { const m = new Mesh(geo, mat); m.position.copy(p); return m; }). For hundreds of identical objects…
seeds.s: FPS drops sharply when adding many similar objects because each has unique GPU resources.

seeds.t: InstancedMesh renders thousands of objects in one draw call
seeds.w: creating thousands of separate Mesh objects for particles, foliage, or crowds — each is a separate draw call, destroying GPU performance.
seeds.c: use InstancedMesh(geometry, material, count). Set per-instance transforms with setMatrixAt(i, matrix) and call instancedMesh.instanceMatrix.needsUpdate = true. For per-instance colour, use InstancedBufferAttribute or setColorAt. Supports frustum culling at the InstancedMesh leve…
seeds.s: thousands of similar objects cause GPU draw call count in the thousands when InstancedMesh would collapse them to one.

seeds.t: Use GLTFLoader with DRACOLoader for compressed model loading
seeds.w: loading uncompressed GLTF models — large binary buffers slow download and parsing.
seeds.c: export models with Draco compression in Blender (File > Export > glTF > Draco) and load with DRACOLoader: const dracoLoader = new DRACOLoader(); dracoLoader.setDecoderPath('/draco/'); gltfLoader.setDRACOLoader(dracoLoader). Draco can reduce geometry file size by 90%. Host the Dr…
seeds.s: 3D scene has slow initial load because model files are large uncompressed geometry.

seeds.t: Use KTX2/Basis Universal textures for GPU-native compression
seeds.w: loading JPEG or PNG textures — they're decompressed to raw RGBA in VRAM, ignoring GPU texture compression.
seeds.c: use KTX2 textures with Basis Universal compression via KTX2Loader. The loader selects the correct GPU format (ASTC, BC, ETC2) for the device. GPU-compressed textures remain compressed in VRAM, reducing VRAM usage by 4-8x and improving cache efficiency. Convert textures with tokt…
seeds.s: scene with many high-res textures runs out of VRAM on mobile because textures are stored uncompressed.

seeds.t: Use layers to restrict raycasting to relevant objects only
seeds.w: raycasting against all objects in the scene on every frame including invisible or non-interactive objects — expensive O(N) BVH traversal against irrelevant geometry.
seeds.c: assign interactive objects to a specific layer: mesh.layers.set(1); and set the raycaster to match: raycaster.layers.set(1). Non-interactive objects default to layer 0 and are skipped. For large scenes, also set raycaster.near and raycaster.far to limit the ray length.
seeds.s: raycasting is slow because it tests thousands of background objects that will never be interactive.

seeds.t: Limit shadow-casting lights and use shadow map size appropriately
seeds.w: every light casting shadows with high resolution shadow maps — each shadow-casting light requires an additional render pass of the entire shadow-casting scene, multiplying GPU cost.
seeds.c: limit shadow-casting lights to 1-2 key lights (directional sun, main spot). Set shadow map resolution to match visual need: light.shadow.mapSize.set(1024, 1024) for typical use; 2048 for hero shadows. Use renderer.shadowMap.type = PCFSoftShadowMap for soft edges. Bake static sha…
seeds.s: scene FPS halves when shadows are enabled because every point light casts shadows.

seeds.t: Update shader uniforms via uniform.value, not reassignment
seeds.w: material.uniforms.time = { value: elapsed } inside the animation loop — this creates a new object every frame and breaks the reference Three.js holds internally.
seeds.c: set the uniform value in place: material.uniforms.time.value = elapsed. Define uniforms once when creating the ShaderMaterial: uniforms: { time: { value: 0 } }. For Vector3 and Color, use uniform.value.copy(newVec) or uniform.value.set(x, y, z) to avoid allocating new objects.
seeds.s: shader uniform stops updating because the uniform reference was replaced instead of mutated.

seeds.t: Use WebGLRenderTarget for off-screen rendering and post-processing
seeds.w: capturing the screen with renderer.domElement.toDataURL() for post-processing — this is synchronous, blocks the GPU pipeline, and is extremely slow.
seeds.c: render to a WebGLRenderTarget: const rt = new WebGLRenderTarget(width, height); renderer.setRenderTarget(rt); renderer.render(scene, camera); renderer.setRenderTarget(null). The render target's texture (rt.texture) can then be used as a uniform in a post-processing shader. Use E…
seeds.s: post-processing effect implemented by reading from the canvas instead of a render target causes frame drops.

seeds.t: Pool frequently created/destroyed objects to reduce GC pressure
seeds.w: creating new THREE.Vector3, Matrix4, or Quaternion inside the animation loop — each allocation adds to GC pressure and causes periodic frame drops.
seeds.c: declare reusable temporaries outside the loop: const _v = new THREE.Vector3(); const _m = new THREE.Matrix4(), then use them inside: _v.copy(a).add(b). For objects like bullets or particles, maintain a pool: reactivate dormant objects instead of creating new ones.
seeds.s: periodic frame stutters in an otherwise smooth animation caused by GC collecting thousands of per-frame Vector3 allocations.

seeds.t: Frustum culling is automatic — don't disable it unless necessary
seeds.w: setting mesh.frustumCulled = false on all objects to 'fix' objects disappearing when they shouldn't — this disables the optimisation that skips rendering off-screen objects.
seeds.c: investigate why an object is culled incorrectly — usually because its bounding sphere is wrong. Fix by calling geometry.computeBoundingSphere() after modifying vertex positions, or updating the object's matrix with updateMatrixWorld(). Disable frustumCulled only for procedurally…
seeds.s: objects flicker or disappear at screen edges because bounding sphere wasn't recomputed after geometry modification.

seeds.t: Three.js uses a right-handed Y-up coordinate system
seeds.w: importing assets from Blender expecting Z-up — Blender uses Z-up by default.
seeds.c: export GLTF from Blender with Y-up (the default GLTF exporter converts this automatically). For DCC tools that export Z-up, apply a rotation: object.rotation.x = -Math.PI / 2. Note that GLTF spec is Y-up, so a well-configured exporter handles this.
seeds.s: imported model appears lying flat on its back because the Z-up Blender coordinate space wasn't converted to Three.js's Y-up space.

seeds.t: Tight near/far clipping planes reduce z-fighting and depth precision
seeds.w: setting camera near=0.001 and far=100000 'to be safe' — the depth buffer precision is distributed logarithmically across this range, causing z-fighting where near and far objects compete for the same depth value.
seeds.c: set near and far as tight as your scene allows: if nothing is closer than 0.5 units or farther than 1000, use exactly those values. Reduce the far/near ratio (ideally below 10,000). For very large scenes, use a logarithmic depth buffer: renderer = new WebGLRenderer({ logarithmic…
seeds.s: two coplanar or nearly coplanar surfaces flicker because the depth buffer can't distinguish them.

seeds.t: Use glsl-literal or raw strings with #include chunks for custom shaders
seeds.w: writing large GLSL strings inline in JavaScript — no syntax highlighting, no GLSL error line numbers matching source, and no reuse of common chunks.
seeds.c: use the glsl-literal Vite/Webpack plugin to import .glsl files directly, or use Three.js's built-in chunk system with #include <common> in ShaderMaterial. Access Three.js shader chunks from THREE.ShaderChunk.common. For custom chunks, inject into ShaderLib: THREE.ShaderChunk.myC…
seeds.s: shader GLSL errors report wrong line numbers because the shader is concatenated from multiple string literals.

seeds.t: Use PMREMGenerator to process HDR environment maps for PBR
seeds.w: assigning a raw equirectangular HDR texture directly as scene.environment — PBR materials require a pre-filtered mipmapped radiance environment map, not a raw HDR.
seeds.c: process through PMREMGenerator: const pmrem = new PMREMGenerator(renderer); pmrem.compileEquirectangularShader(); const envMap = pmrem.fromEquirectangular(hdrTexture).texture; scene.environment = envMap; hdrTexture.dispose(); pmrem.dispose(). Use RGBELoader for .hdr files, EXRLo…
seeds.s: scene.environment is set but MeshStandardMaterial shows no reflections because the texture wasn't processed through PMREMGenerator.

seeds.t: Use morph targets for blend shapes, not bone animations
seeds.w: using skeletal bones for facial expressions or subtle mesh deformations — bones require complex rigging and are overkill for shape-based deformations.
seeds.c: use morph targets (blend shapes): define geometry.morphAttributes.position as an array of position arrays, set geometry.morphTargetsRelative = true for delta morphs. Animate with mesh.morphTargetInfluences[0] = 0.5. GLTF supports morph targets natively. Use AnimationMixer with m…
seeds.s: facial expressions implemented with bones when morph targets (blend shapes exported from Blender) would be simpler and faster.

seeds.t: Add Stats.js and renderer.info for real-time performance monitoring
seeds.w: guessing at performance issues without measurement — profiling GPU work blind.
seeds.c: add Stats.js to display FPS, frame time, and memory in a corner of the canvas during development. Monitor renderer.info.render for draw calls, triangles, and points per frame; renderer.info.memory for textures and geometries. A draw call count above 100-200 or triangle count abo…
seeds.s: developer notices slowness but cannot identify whether it's CPU, GPU, draw calls, or shader bottleneck without stats.

seeds.t: Update camera aspect and renderer size on window resize
seeds.w: setting the camera aspect ratio and renderer size only once at startup — the canvas becomes distorted or wrong resolution when the browser window is resized.
seeds.c: add a resize listener: window.addEventListener('resize', () => { camera.aspect = window.innerWidth / window.innerHeight; camera.updateProjectionMatrix(); renderer.setSize(window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight) }). Call this function once immediately to set initial values. For Re…
seeds.s: 3D scene appears stretched or uses incorrect resolution after resizing the browser window.

seeds.t: Use lil-gui or Tweakpane for live parameter tweaking in development
seeds.w: hardcoding shader uniforms, material colours, or physics parameters and reloading the page for each tweak — extremely slow iteration cycle.
seeds.c: add lil-gui (the modern successor to dat.GUI): const gui = new GUI(); gui.add(material, 'roughness', 0, 1); gui.addColor(params, 'color').onChange(v => material.color.set(v)). Bind sliders directly to uniform values, light intensity, camera fov, or any numeric parameter. Remove …
seeds.s: adjusting a material parameter requires editing source code and reloading the page when lil-gui would allow live tweaking.

seeds.t: any silently disables all type checking
seeds.w: using 'any' to silence type errors — it propagates and defeats the purpose of TypeScript.
seeds.c: use 'unknown' when the type is genuinely unknown and narrow it with type guards. Use specific types, generics, or union types instead.
seeds.s: type errors at runtime that TypeScript should have caught.

seeds.t: TypeScript types are erased at runtime
seeds.w: checking typeof x === 'MyInterface' at runtime — interface types do not exist at runtime.
seeds.c: use type guards, discriminated unions with a literal field, or runtime schema validation (Zod).
seeds.s: incorrect runtime behaviour despite correct TypeScript types.

seeds.t: Numeric enums have unsafe reverse mapping
seeds.w: relying on numeric enums — TypeScript allows any number to be assigned to a numeric enum type, making type safety illusory.
seeds.c: use string enums (enum Direction { Up = 'UP' }) or const objects with as const and typeof for exhaustive union types.
seeds.s: unexpected values passing type checks.

seeds.t: Type assertions bypass the type system — use narrowing instead
seeds.w: using 'as MyType' to silence a type error — assertions tell the compiler to trust you, bypassing all checks.
seeds.c: use type guards, conditional checks, or instanceof to narrow the type. Use 'as' only when you have information the compiler genuinely cannot infer.
seeds.s: runtime error on a value you asserted as a type that it was not.

seeds.t: Optional chaining short-circuits on null and undefined only
seeds.w: using ?. to guard against falsy values generally.
seeds.c: use ?. for null/undefined guards only.
seeds.s: unexpected undefined returned when value is 0 or empty string.

seeds.t: Use mapped types to transform existing types systematically
seeds.w: manually duplicating a type with small modifications.
seeds.c: type Readonly<T> = { readonly [K in keyof T]: T[K] }. type Partial<T> = { [K in keyof T]?: T[K] }. Use -readonly and -? modifiers to remove readonly/optional. Custom mapped types: type Nullable<T> = { [K in keyof T]: T[K] | null }.
seeds.s: types drift apart when the original changes.

seeds.t: Discriminated unions enable exhaustive type narrowing
seeds.w: using optional fields to differentiate union members — the compiler cannot narrow reliably.
seeds.c: type Result = { kind: 'success'; value: string } | { kind: 'error'; message: string }. The 'kind' literal field is the discriminant. switch (result.kind) { case 'success': ... case 'error': ... } narrows correctly. Add a default: assertNever(result) to catch unhandled cases at c…
seeds.s: type guards require complex if-chains instead of clean switch statements.

seeds.t: Use satisfies to validate a type without widening
seeds.w: const palette: Record<string, string | number[]> = ... — this widens the type and loses specific property types.
seeds.c: const palette = { red: [255,0,0], blue: '#0000ff' } satisfies Record<string, string | number[]>. palette.red is typed as number[], not string | number[].
seeds.s: type annotations cause loss of literal or tuple types on individual properties.

seeds.t: Use 'as const' to preserve literal types
seeds.w: const config = { direction: 'left' } — direction is typed as string, not 'left'.
seeds.c: const config = { direction: 'left' } as const — direction is typed as the literal 'left'. Useful for route definitions, configuration objects, and lookup tables where the specific string matters. For arrays: ['a', 'b'] as const produces readonly ['a', 'b'], enabling tuple and un…
seeds.s: string literal loses its specific type when stored in an object or array.

seeds.t: Use infer to extract types from conditional type patterns
seeds.w: using any to approximate the extracted type.
seeds.c: type ReturnType<T> = T extends (...args: any[]) => infer R ? R : never. type Awaited<T> = T extends Promise<infer U> ? U : T. Use to unwrap generic types, extract function signatures, or decompose tuple types.
seeds.s: utility types require knowing the inner type in advance rather than inferring it.

seeds.t: Interfaces merge — use this to extend third-party types
seeds.w: casting to any to add properties to external types. In .d.ts files: declare module 'mymodule' { ... } to augment a module's exports.
seeds.c: to extend Express's Request type: declare global { namespace Express { interface Request { user?: MyUser } } }. This is the standard way to augment module types without forking.
seeds.s: cannot add properties to framework types without TypeScript errors.

seeds.t: Excess property checking only applies to object literals
seeds.w: expecting TypeScript to catch extra properties in all contexts — excess property checking only applies when an object literal is directly assigned to a typed variable. Assigning via an intermediate variable bypasses the check: const extra = { name: 'a', extra: 'b' }; const perso…
seeds.c: pass object literals directly to typed parameters when you want excess property checking.
seeds.s: extra properties silently allowed when assigned via intermediate variables.

seeds.t: Configure path aliases in tsconfig and bundler together
seeds.w: configuring paths in tsconfig.json alone — TypeScript resolves types but the bundler (Webpack, Vite, Jest) handles actual file resolution and needs separate config.
seeds.c: configure paths in tsconfig.json AND the bundler config. For Vite: resolve.alias. For Webpack: resolve.alias or tsconfig-paths-webpack-plugin. For Jest: moduleNameMapper.
seeds.s: TypeScript compiles without errors but runtime fails with 'module not found', or tests fail while source works.

seeds.t: Use project references for monorepo TypeScript compilation
seeds.w: one root tsconfig including all source — type checking becomes slow and cross-package type errors are caught late.
seeds.c: set up project references: { 'references': [{ 'path': '../shared' }] } in each tsconfig. Run tsc --build (tsc -b) to build in dependency order. Each package uses composite: true and declarationMap: true.
seeds.s: slow TypeScript compilation in monorepos, or type errors only caught at build time.

seeds.t: Destroyed GameObjects are not null — use IsInstanceValid pattern
seeds.w: if (myObject != null) on a destroyed GameObject — Unity overrides == so destroyed objects appear null in that check, but the C# object still exists. Calling methods throws MissingReferenceException.
seeds.c: store component references during Awake/Start and null them in OnDestroy. Avoid finding objects every frame
seeds.s: NullReferenceException when accessing component reference set in the Inspector

seeds.t: Never call GetComponent or Find in Update
seeds.w: calling GetComponent<T>(), FindObjectOfType<T>(), or GameObject.Find() inside Update — expensive searches every frame.
seeds.c: call once in Awake or Start and cache the result.
seeds.s: frame rate drops, profiler shows excessive GC allocations.

seeds.t: Coroutines stop silently when the GameObject is disabled
seeds.w: expecting a coroutine to continue when its host GameObject is disabled — Unity stops all coroutines when the object is disabled.
seeds.c: use a persistent manager object to host long-running coroutines, or restart in OnEnable.
seeds.s: coroutine stops mid-execution without an error when the object is toggled.

seeds.t: Rigidbody manipulation must happen in FixedUpdate
seeds.w: applying forces or modifying Rigidbody velocity in Update — variable frame rate causes inconsistent physics.
seeds.c: all Rigidbody manipulation (AddForce, velocity, MovePosition) must happen in FixedUpdate. Read input in Update; apply physics in FixedUpdate.
seeds.s: jittery movement, inconsistent jump heights.

seeds.t: Use CompareTag instead of == for tag comparison
seeds.w: gameObject.tag == 'Player' — allocates a string every call.
seeds.c: gameObject.CompareTag('Player') — allocation-free. Also: misspelled tag names in == comparisons fail silently; CompareTag throws for invalid tags, catching errors earlier.
seeds.s: unnecessary GC allocations in hot paths.

seeds.t: [SerializeField] exposes private fields to the Inspector
seeds.w: making a field public just to expose it in the Inspector — breaks encapsulation.
seeds.c: keep the field private and add [SerializeField]. Use [HideInInspector] on public fields you want hidden.
seeds.s: unnecessary public fields used as a workaround.

seeds.t: Avoid allocations in hot paths — cache arrays and use pools
seeds.w: allocating new arrays, lists, or strings in Update or frequently called methods — triggers GC pauses causing frame spikes.
seeds.c: use object pooling (Unity's ObjectPool<T>), cache arrays used with Physics.RaycastAll, use StringBuilder for string building, and avoid LINQ in hot paths. Profile with the Memory Profiler.
seeds.s: periodic frame rate spikes, GC.Collect visible in profiler.

seeds.t: Use ScriptableObjects for shared data, not static variables
seeds.w: using static variables for shared game data (enemy stats, item configs) — statics persist between Play mode sessions in the editor causing hidden state bugs.
seeds.c: use ScriptableObjects which reset correctly and are editable assets. Create via [CreateAssetMenu] attribute. Static variables should only hold truly stateless utilities.
seeds.s: game behaves differently on second Play press without code changes.

seeds.t: The old Input class and new Input System are not interchangeable
seeds.w: mixing both in the same project without configuring which is active — leads to one system not receiving events.
seeds.c: choose one and stick to it. Set in Project Settings → Player → Active Input Handling. The new Input System requires the package installed and setup of InputActionAssets.
seeds.s: input not detected, or works in editor but not in build.

seeds.t: Dirty UI elements cause full Canvas rebuild — batch updates
seeds.w: updating many UI elements individually in the same frame — each change marks the Canvas dirty and triggers a rebuild.
seeds.c: disable the Canvas, make all changes, then re-enable it. Or use multiple Canvases to isolate frequently updated elements. Use Canvas.willRenderCanvases if you need to update just before render.
seeds.s: UI update causes frame spikes visible in profiler as Canvas.BuildBatch.

seeds.t: DontDestroyOnLoad creates duplicate objects on scene reload
seeds.w: calling DontDestroyOnLoad on a singleton without checking if one already exists — reloading the scene creates a second instance.
seeds.c: check for existing instances in Awake: if (Instance != null && Instance != this) { Destroy(gameObject); return; } Instance = this; DontDestroyOnLoad(gameObject).
seeds.s: two instances of a manager after scene reload, causing duplicate events or doubled effects.

seeds.t: Cache Animator parameter hashes for performance
seeds.w: animator.SetBool('IsRunning', true) using a string every frame — string hashing overhead in a hot path.
seeds.c: cache the hash once: private static readonly int IsRunningHash = Animator.StringToHash('IsRunning'); then use animator.SetBool(IsRunningHash, true). Same applies to SetFloat, SetInteger, GetBool, etc.
seeds.s: profiler shows string hashing in animation update path.

seeds.t: Use layer masks for selective raycasts — avoid hitting unintended objects
seeds.w: Physics.Raycast without a layerMask — the ray hits everything in the scene including the player's own collider.
seeds.c: define layers for categories (Player, Enemy, Ground) and pass a layerMask: int mask = LayerMask.GetMask('Ground', 'Enemy'); Physics.Raycast(origin, dir, out hit, distance, mask). Use ~LayerMask.GetMask('Player') to hit everything except the player.
seeds.s: raycast hits the casting object, or detects wrong objects.

seeds.t: Time.timeScale 0 pauses physics and deltaTime — use unscaledDeltaTime for UI
seeds.w: using Time.deltaTime for UI animations or timers during a pause (Time.timeScale = 0) — deltaTime becomes 0 and animations freeze.
seeds.c: use Time.unscaledDeltaTime for anything that should run regardless of time scale: UI tweens, pause menus, loading screens.
seeds.s: UI animations freeze when game is paused, or countdown timer stops during pause.

seeds.t: Set parent before activating instantiated prefabs to avoid double layout
seeds.w: Instantiate(prefab) then obj.transform.SetParent(parent) — the object activates in world space triggering Start() and layout calculations before being placed in the correct parent.
seeds.c: Instantiate(prefab, parent) or Instantiate(prefab, parent, false) to parent immediately. For pooled objects, deactivate before reparenting.
seeds.s: layout calculations run twice, or Start() fires with wrong parent context.

seeds.t: Always unsubscribe from C# events in OnDestroy
seeds.w: subscribing to a C# event or Unity event without unsubscribing — if the publisher outlives the subscriber, the event holds a reference preventing garbage collection and firing callbacks on a destroyed object.
seeds.c: subscribe in OnEnable, unsubscribe in OnDisable; or subscribe in Start, unsubscribe in OnDestroy. Use -= with the exact same method reference used for +=.
seeds.s: MissingReferenceException in event callback, or memory leak.

seeds.t: IL2CPP code stripping removes types accessed only by reflection
seeds.w: using reflection or serialisation (JsonUtility, Newtonsoft) to access types that are not directly referenced in code — IL2CPP strips them in a build causing MissingMethodException at runtime.
seeds.c: use link.xml to preserve types, or add [Preserve] attribute. Test builds with 'Medium' or 'High' stripping level during development, not just at release.
seeds.s: works in editor/development build, crashes in release build with missing method errors.

seeds.t: async void in MonoBehaviour swallows exceptions silently
seeds.w: async void Start() or async void Update() — exceptions inside async void are silently swallowed and cannot be awaited or caught by callers.
seeds.c: use async UniTask (UniTask package) or wrap with a try/catch inside the async void, rethrowing to a centralised error handler. For fire-and-forget: _ = DoThingAsync().ContinueWith(t => Debug.LogError(t.Exception), TaskContinuationOptions.OnlyOnFaulted).
seeds.s: exceptions in async methods disappear with no log output.

seeds.t: Shader variants are compiled per keyword combination — keep keywords minimal
seeds.w: adding many global shader keywords — build size and shader compilation time grow exponentially.
seeds.c: use local keywords (shader_feature_local) instead of global ones where possible. Audit keywords with the Shader Variant Collection tool.
seeds.s: builds are very slow, or 'shader warmup' causes hitches at runtime.

seeds.t: Verify every edit actually landed
seeds.w: Applying a patch and immediately assuming it landed without checking.
seeds.c: Read back the edited region after every patch to confirm the change is present before proceeding.
seeds.s: code appears unchanged after an apparently successful edit; test still fails for the same reason as before the patch.

seeds.t: Write complete files not partial fragments
seeds.w: Inserting a code block without reading the surrounding context first.
seeds.c: Read the full target region first, then produce a complete replacement that accounts for existing structure.
seeds.s: syntax error at the edit site; variable used before declaration; orphaned closing brace from mismatched insertion.

seeds.t: Commit working state before refactoring
seeds.w: Starting a refactor on a dirty working tree without committing or stashing first.
seeds.c: Commit or stash all working changes before beginning any structural refactor.
seeds.s: hours of work lost when a refactor degrades functionality and there is no safe point to revert to.

seeds.t: Test after each change not in batches
seeds.w: Batching multiple changes and running tests once at the end.
seeds.c: Run tests after each logical change to isolate failures immediately.
seeds.s: test failure with no clear owner; cannot determine which of five changes introduced the regression.

seeds.t: Guard null before accessing any member
seeds.w: Accessing obj.property without first checking that obj is non-null.
seeds.c: Guard with an explicit null check or safe-navigation operator before any member access on a nullable value.
seeds.s: NullPointerException, TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined, nil does not have a member named X.

seeds.t: Environment variables must exist before process starts
seeds.w: Setting an env var in one shell session and expecting a separately-started process to inherit it.
seeds.c: Set all required env vars in the same session before launching the process, or load them via a startup .env file.
seeds.s: variable reads as undefined or empty string despite being set in the terminal.

seeds.t: Await every async call or chain the promise
seeds.w: Calling an async function without await, receiving a Promise object instead of the resolved value.
seeds.c: await every async call, or explicitly chain .then()/.catch() when await is unavailable.
seeds.s: variable holds a Promise object instead of a value; operation silently completes after the code that depended on it has already run.

seeds.t: Log errors before catching and continuing
seeds.w: Catching an exception silently without logging it.
seeds.c: Log the error with context before any catch-and-continue handler so failures are always visible.
seeds.s: operation appears to succeed but produces wrong output; the actual exception is invisible in logs.

seeds.t: Integer division truncates toward zero
seeds.w: Dividing two integers and expecting a fractional result.
seeds.c: Cast at least one operand to float before dividing: (float)a / b or a / float(b).
seeds.s: result is always 0 when the dividend is smaller than the divisor; percentage calculations always return 0 or 100.

seeds.t: Never compare floats with exact equality
seeds.w: Using == to compare floating-point values after arithmetic operations.
seeds.c: Compare floats using an epsilon: abs(a - b) < epsilon, where epsilon is appropriate for the scale of the values.
seeds.s: condition that should be true is never true; loop that should terminate runs forever; 0.1 + 0.2 does not equal 0.3.

seeds.t: Off-by-one from mixed inclusive and exclusive bounds
seeds.w: Mixing inclusive and exclusive bounds in loops, missing or double-counting the last element.
seeds.c: Decide whether each bound is inclusive or exclusive and use < vs <= consistently throughout.
seeds.s: last element always skipped; fence-post error; array index out of bounds on the final iteration.

seeds.t: Inner scope assignment does not affect outer variable
seeds.w: Assigning to a name in an inner scope expecting it to mutate the outer variable.
seeds.c: Return the new value, pass by reference, or use the language's outer-scope keyword (nonlocal, global, ref).
seeds.s: variable retains its original value after a block that appeared to modify it; the change has no effect outside the inner scope.

seeds.t: Hardcoded path separators break cross-platform
seeds.w: Hardcoding '/' or '\\' in file paths instead of using the platform's path-join function.
seeds.c: Use path.join(), os.path.join(), Path(), or equivalent so the OS-correct separator is always used.
seeds.s: FileNotFoundError on Windows despite path appearing correct; code works locally but fails in CI.

seeds.t: Mutable shared state requires synchronization
seeds.w: Reading and writing shared mutable state from concurrent threads without synchronization.
seeds.c: Protect shared state with a mutex or lock, or eliminate sharing via ownership transfer or message passing.
seeds.s: value is unpredictably wrong across runs; intermittent crash; result differs between debug and release builds.

seeds.t: Side effects inside conditions cause silent bugs
seeds.w: Placing a mutating call or assignment inside an if/while condition.
seeds.c: Assign the result to a named variable before the condition so the side effect and the guard are clearly separated.
seeds.s: condition evaluates differently on re-read; mutation happens or does not happen unexpectedly when the condition short-circuits.

seeds.t: Count braces after any code insertion
seeds.w: Inserting a code block near a function boundary and moving on without verifying brace balance.
seeds.c: After any insertion touching function boundaries, count { vs } in the block to confirm balance before saving.
seeds.s: all JavaScript on the page silently stops executing; every interactive element becomes completely unresponsive while HTML and CSS still render correctly; no visible error appears on the page itself.

seeds.t: Second same-named function silently overwrites first
seeds.w: Using const _orig = fn; function fn(){ _orig(); } — hoisting makes _orig capture the wrapper, not the original.
seeds.c: Merge the extra logic directly into the single function declaration and delete the wrapper pattern entirely.
seeds.s: too much recursion / Maximum call stack size exceeded the moment the function is called; stack trace shows the same function name repeating hundreds of times.

seeds.t: path.join Does Not Sanitise User-Controlled Input Against Directory Traversal
seeds.w: path.join(BASE_DIR, userInput) path.join resolves ../ segments. path.join('/seeds', '../../../etc/passwd') resolves to '/etc/passwd'. An extension check like .endsWith('.json') does not help: '../../etc/passwd.json' passes the check and resolves outside BASE_DIR.
seeds.c: Validate the name component before joining: const SAFE = /^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+(\.json)?$/; if (!SAFE.test(userInput)) throw new Error('Invalid name'); const fp = path.join(BASE_DIR, userInput); Or use path.resolve and verify the result starts with the base directory: const fp = path.…
seeds.s: A file read or write operation accepts a name parameter; passing '../' sequences accesses files outside the intended directory; an .endsWith('.json') check is used as the sole guard.

seeds.t: execSync With Interpolated User Input Enables Shell Command Injection
seeds.w: execSync(`open "${userUrl}"`) or execSync(`git commit -m "${message}"`) Shell metacharacters in userUrl break out of the quoted context: a URL containing "; rm -rf /; echo " executes as three separate shell commands. The quote wrapping provides no protection against values that …
seeds.c: execFile('open', [userUrl]) — pass arguments as a separate array execFile never invokes a shell; each array element is passed directly to the OS as a literal argument. No shell metacharacter interpretation occurs regardless of the content. For cases where execSync is needed for …
seeds.s: A subprocess call builds its command via template literal with a variable derived from user input, a URL, a filename, or a message; any input containing quotes, semicolons, backticks, or $() executes injected commands.

seeds.t: .endsWith('.json') Is Not a Path Traversal Guard
seeds.w: if (filename.endsWith('.json')) writeFile(path.join(DIR, filename), data) The extension check only verifies the suffix. '../../.ssh/authorized_keys.json' ends with '.json' and passes the check; path.join resolves the traversal sequence and writes outside DIR.
seeds.c: Validate the full name structure, not just the suffix: const SAFE_FILE = /^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+\.json$/; if (!SAFE_FILE.test(filename)) throw new Error('Unsafe filename'); writeFile(path.join(DIR, filename), data); This regex requires: only safe characters in the base name, a literal …
seeds.s: A file operation filters by extension before using the name in a path; a filename from remote data (API listing, downloaded metadata, user input) containing ../ sequences is processed; files are written or read outside the intended directory.

seeds.t: Never Do Expensive Work in Tick Every Frame
seeds.w: Running physics queries, AI pathfinding, or iterating all actors in Tick() on every frame — performance tanks immediately with more than a handful of actors.
seeds.c: Move expensive logic to timers (GetWorldTimerManager().SetTimer), async tasks, or event-driven callbacks. For AI, use the Behavior Tree tick interval and EQS instead of custom Tick logic. Profile with Unreal Insights before optimizing.
seeds.s: frame rate drops proportional to actor count in the level.

seeds.t: Mark Replicated Properties with UPROPERTY Correctly
seeds.w: Modifying a variable on the server and expecting clients to see it without marking it Replicated — the value never reaches clients.
seeds.c: Add Replicated or ReplicatedUsing=OnRep_FunctionName to the UPROPERTY macro and implement GetLifetimeReplicatedProps with DOREPLIFETIME macros. Use ReplicatedUsing for properties that need a client-side callback on change.
seeds.s: server state changes invisible on clients in multiplayer play.

seeds.t: RPCs Must Be Called on the Owning Connection's Actor
seeds.w: Calling a Client or Server RPC on an actor not owned by the calling connection — the RPC is silently dropped with no error.
seeds.c: Client RPCs must be called on an actor owned by that client's player controller; Server RPCs must be called on the server from an actor the client owns. For non-owned actors, relay through the PlayerController.
seeds.s: RPCs silently failing in multiplayer with no log output.

seeds.t: UPROPERTY Prevents Garbage Collection of UObjects
seeds.w: Storing a UObject pointer in a raw C++ class member without UPROPERTY — the garbage collector does not see it and may collect the object mid-use, causing crashes.
seeds.c: Always mark UObject pointer members with UPROPERTY() so the GC tracks them. For non-UObject pointers (raw C++ structs), manage lifetime manually with TSharedPtr or TUniquePtr.
seeds.s: random crashes accessing a UObject that was silently garbage collected.

seeds.t: Put Logic in C++ and Polish in Blueprint
seeds.w: Writing complex game logic, loops, and data processing entirely in Blueprint — visual scripts become unmaintainable spaghetti and are significantly slower than C++ for heavy loops.
seeds.c: Implement performance-critical code, data structures, and core systems in C++; expose key functions and events as BlueprintCallable or BlueprintImplementableEvent for designers to extend. Keep Blueprint for gameplay parameters, sequencing, and content-specific polish.
seeds.s: Blueprint graphs too large to navigate, CPU overhead from pure-Blueprint game loops.

seeds.t: Use Soft References to Avoid Loading All Assets at Startup
seeds.w: Storing all asset references as UPROPERTY(EditAnywhere) hard references — Unreal loads all referenced assets into memory when the owning object is loaded, inflating memory.
seeds.c: Use TSoftObjectPtr and TSoftClassPtr for assets loaded on-demand; load them asynchronously with UAssetManager::GetStreamableManager().RequestAsyncLoad(). Hard references are fine only for assets that must always be in memory.
seeds.s: excessive memory usage or long load times because unrelated assets are loaded transitively.

seeds.t: GameInstance Persists Across Level Loads
seeds.w: Storing cross-level data in GameState or a level Actor — both are destroyed on level transition, losing the data.
seeds.c: Use UGameInstance (or a custom subclass) for data that must survive level loads: player progress, settings, session info. GameState is per-match/per-level; PlayerState replicates and persists for a player across seamless travels.
seeds.s: data reset on level transition that was expected to persist.

seeds.t: Configure Collision Channels Instead of Ignoring All
seeds.w: Setting all collision channels to Ignore on new object types to avoid unwanted hits — gameplay traces and overlaps stop working because nothing responds.
seeds.c: Define custom ECollisionChannel entries in Project Settings, set appropriate defaults for Object Type and Collision Responses, and use named presets. Use Overlap for trigger volumes and Block for physical collisions; never disable all channels blindly.
seeds.s: traces returning no hit, or characters falling through floors after collision changes.

seeds.t: Use Async Level Streaming to Avoid Hitches
seeds.w: Loading a large sublevel synchronously during gameplay — the game freezes for the duration of the load, producing a multi-second hitch.
seeds.c: Use ULevelStreamingDynamic::LoadLevelInstanceBySoftObjectPtr or the LoadStreamLevel node with bMakeVisibleAfterLoad=false; show the level once the OnLevelLoaded delegate fires. Use World Partition for open-world streaming automatically.
seeds.s: gameplay freeze when entering a new area or triggering a level load.

seeds.t: Migrate from Legacy Input to Enhanced Input System
seeds.w: Binding input with GetWorld()->GetFirstPlayerController()->InputComponent->BindAction — the legacy system is deprecated and lacks context-based input switching.
seeds.c: Use the Enhanced Input System (UEnhancedInputComponent, Input Mapping Contexts, Input Actions) introduced in UE5. Bind via UEnhancedInputComponent::BindAction and swap IMCs at runtime for different gameplay states.
seeds.s: input not working as expected in UE5, or deprecated input warnings in the output log.

seeds.t: Nanite Requires Static Meshes With Specific Settings
seeds.w: Enabling Nanite on all meshes including skeletal meshes, landscape, or meshes with vertex animation — Nanite does not support these and falls back silently or shows artifacts.
seeds.c: Enable Nanite only on static meshes without vertex animation; ensure meshes have no per-vertex color driven material logic that Nanite cannot handle. Check the Nanite visualization mode to confirm active coverage.
seeds.s: meshes rendering incorrectly or Nanite statistics showing unexpected fallback.

seeds.t: Lumen Requires Hardware Ray Tracing or Software Fallback
seeds.w: Enabling Lumen and expecting full quality on hardware without ray tracing support, or ignoring the software fallback quality degradation.
seeds.c: Configure Lumen for the target hardware: use Software Lumen (screen traces) for broader compatibility and Hardware Ray Tracing for maximum quality. Tune r.Lumen.Reflections.MaxRoughnessToTrace and scene.LightingQuality per platform. Disable Lumen on mobile targets.
seeds.s: dark or incorrect reflections, high GPU cost, or Lumen not working on target hardware.

seeds.t: Configure HLOD for World Partition Large Worlds
seeds.w: Enabling World Partition without configuring HLOD (Hierarchical LOD) — distant terrain and buildings have no simplified representation, causing GPU overdraw and pop-in at distance.
seeds.c: Set up HLOD layers in the World Partition settings; build HLODs as part of the cook/build process. Use Nanite with World Partition for geometry; combine with hierarchical instanced foliage for vegetation.
seeds.s: frame rate drops at open-world distances or visible geometry pop-in.

seeds.t: Pool Frequently Spawned Actors Instead of Destroying
seeds.w: Spawning and destroying actors every frame (projectiles, particles, effects) — frequent SpawnActor calls trigger expensive construction and GC cycles.
seeds.c: Implement an object pool: pre-spawn a set of actors, deactivate them (SetActorHiddenInGame, SetActorEnableCollision false, DisableComponentsSimulatePhysics), and reactivate on reuse. UE5 has built-in pooling support for Niagara particles.
seeds.s: frame spikes on projectile spawn/destroy, GC hitches in combat-heavy scenes.

seeds.t: Always Unbind Delegates Before Actor Destruction
seeds.w: Binding a lambda or member function to a delegate and never unbinding — if the bound object is destroyed before the delegate fires, the call crashes with a dangling pointer.
seeds.c: Store the delegate handle and call Remove(Handle) or Clear() in BeginDestroy or EndPlay. Prefer AddWeakLambda or AddUObject which automatically removes invalid bindings.
seeds.s: crash in delegate broadcast after a bound actor is destroyed.

seeds.t: Use Instanced Static Mesh for Repeated Geometry
seeds.w: Placing hundreds of identical static mesh actors separately — each actor generates its own draw call even for identical geometry.
seeds.c: Use UInstancedStaticMeshComponent (ISMC) or Hierarchical ISMC to batch all instances into one draw call. Nanite handles large numbers of unique meshes; ISMC is optimal for repeated identical objects (trees, rocks, fences).
seeds.s: draw call count in the thousands with a forest of identical trees.

seeds.t: Use GAS for Complex Ability and Attribute Systems
seeds.w: Hand-rolling character attributes, cooldowns, status effects, and ability activation with custom variables and timers — fragile, hard to network, and explodes in complexity.
seeds.c: Use the Gameplay Ability System (GAS): define Gameplay Attributes in UAttributeSet, implement abilities as UGameplayAbility classes, apply effects via UGameplayEffect. GAS handles replication, prediction, and stacking automatically.
seeds.s: custom ability systems duplicating functionality GAS provides with poor netcode.

seeds.t: Minimize Material Instruction Count for Mobile Targets
seeds.w: Using the same complex materials on mobile as on high-end PC — mobile GPU shader instruction limits cause significant slowdowns or visual artifacts.
seeds.c: Create mobile-specific material variants using quality switches (QualitySwitch node) or material instances with simplified feature sets. Use the mobile preview mode and stat gpu to measure shader cost. Bake lighting into textures for static objects.
seeds.s: GPU-bound frame rate on mobile, or materials exceeding instruction limits.

seeds.t: Use USaveGame for Persistent Player Data
seeds.w: Writing custom file I/O with FFileHelper to save game state — bypasses UE's save slot system, platform-specific storage paths, and async save support.
seeds.c: Subclass USaveGame, populate its UPROPERTY fields, and use UGameplayStatics::SaveGameToSlot / LoadGameFromSlot which handle platform-correct paths and slot management. Use AsyncSaveGameToSlot to avoid hitches.
seeds.s: save files in wrong locations on console platforms, or game-thread hitches during save.

seeds.t: Cook and Package Only Required Assets Per Platform
seeds.w: Packaging all assets for all platforms in every build — unnecessary assets inflate package size and cook time significantly.
seeds.c: Use the Asset Manager and Primary Asset Labels to explicitly control what is cooked per platform. Configure platform-specific cook settings in Project Settings > Packaging. Run 'Cook on the fly' during development to cook only what's loaded.
seeds.s: shipping package 2x larger than necessary, or platform-specific assets in the wrong build.

seeds.t: ref() for primitives, reactive() for objects
seeds.w: const { count } = reactive({ count: 0 }) — count is no longer reactive.
seeds.c: use ref() as the default. Use reactive() only when you need deep reactivity on a plain object and will not destructure it.
seeds.s: UI does not update when destructured reactive properties change.

seeds.t: computed() returns a readonly ref — do not assign to it
seeds.w: myComputed.value = newValue — computed refs are read-only by default.
seeds.c: to make a writable computed, provide both get and set: computed({ get: () => store.value, set: (v) => store.value = v }). For most cases, if you need to write to a computed value, a regular ref or a method is more appropriate.
seeds.s: 'Write operation failed: computed value is readonly' warning.

seeds.t: watch() does not track nested object changes by default
seeds.w: watching a reactive object without deep: true and expecting callbacks when nested properties change — watch() is shallow by default.
seeds.c: watch(myObject, callback, { deep: true }) for deep watching. For performance-sensitive scenarios, watch a specific nested path: watch(() => myObject.nested.value, callback).
seeds.s: watcher callback never fires when nested properties are mutated.

seeds.t: Always provide a unique :key with v-for
seeds.w: v-for without :key, or with :key='index' on lists that can change order — Vue reuses DOM nodes incorrectly causing input state bugs and broken animations.
seeds.c: :key='item.id' using a stable unique identifier from your data. The key must be unique among siblings.
seeds.s: form inputs retain wrong values after list updates, or transitions play on wrong elements.

seeds.t: Declare emits with defineEmits() in script setup
seeds.w: emitting events without declaring them — Vue 3 warns about undeclared emits and they do not benefit from type checking.
seeds.c: const emit = defineEmits(['update:modelValue', 'submit']). For TypeScript: defineEmits<{ 'update:modelValue': [value: string]; submit: [] }>() . Call with emit('submit'). Undeclared emits fall through to the root element which is usually incorrect.
seeds.s: 'Extraneous non-emits event listeners' warning, or event not received by parent.

seeds.t: Use defineAsyncComponent() for code-split components
seeds.w: importing heavy components synchronously — they are included in the main bundle.
seeds.c: const MyHeavyComponent = defineAsyncComponent(() => import('./MyHeavyComponent.vue')) to lazy-load. Wrap in Suspense with a fallback for loading state: <Suspense><template #fallback><Spinner /></template></Suspense>. Works with both Options and Composition API.
seeds.s: initial bundle too large, or component loaded before it is needed.

seeds.t: provide/inject for deep prop passing — use readonly() to prevent mutation
seeds.w: providing a ref and letting descendants mutate it directly — this breaks the single source of truth.
seeds.c: provide('key', readonly(myRef)) to allow reading but prevent mutations. Provide a mutation method alongside the data if descendants need to update it: provide('updateKey', (v) => myRef.value = v).
seeds.s: child components mutate provided state causing unpredictable updates.

seeds.t: setup() runs before component mount — no DOM access
seeds.w: accessing DOM refs or child components in setup() — the DOM does not exist yet at this point.
seeds.c: access DOM refs inside onMounted(() => { ... }) where the DOM is guaranteed to be ready. For cleanup on unmount: onUnmounted(() => { ... }). In script setup, these lifecycle hooks are imported from 'vue'.
seeds.s: ref.value is null when accessed in setup(), or child component methods unavailable.

seeds.t: Use Teleport for modals and overlays that must escape parent overflow
seeds.w: placing a modal inside a component with overflow:hidden or z-index stacking — the modal is clipped or rendered behind other content.
seeds.c: wrap the modal in <Teleport to='body'> to render it as a direct child of body regardless of where it appears in the component tree. The component remains logically in its parent for state and props but renders in the target.
seeds.s: modal clipped by parent container, or z-index conflicts.

seeds.t: <script setup> is the preferred Composition API syntax
seeds.w: using the verbose setup() function returning an object in the Options API or Composition API when <script setup> is available.
seeds.c: use <script setup> — top-level variables, functions, and imports are automatically exposed to the template without returning them. defineProps(), defineEmits(), and defineExpose() are compiler macros available without importing. This reduces boilerplate significantly. Component …
seeds.s: verbose setup() { return { ... } } pattern that <script setup> replaces with zero boilerplate.

seeds.t: Use withDefaults to declare prop default values in script setup
seeds.w: defining a prop as optional but not providing a default, causing undefined to propagate into the template — or using the Options API default: syntax inside defineProps.
seeds.c: wrap defineProps with withDefaults: const props = withDefaults(defineProps<{ count?: number }>(), { count: 0 }). This provides type-safe prop defaults in <script setup>. For complex default values (objects, arrays), provide a factory function.
seeds.s: optional prop is undefined in the template causing errors, or default values defined in a non-type-safe way.

seeds.t: Prefer Pinia over Vuex for Vue 3 state management
seeds.w: using Vuex in a new Vue 3 project — Vuex 4 works but lacks TypeScript inference, requires mutations, and is officially in maintenance mode.
seeds.c: use Pinia: define stores with defineStore('id', () => { const count = ref(0); function increment() { count.value++ }; return { count, increment } }). Pinia has full TypeScript support, no mutations, devtools integration, and direct store composition. It is the officially recomme…
seeds.s: new Vue 3 project scaffolded with Vuex adding unnecessary mutation boilerplate.

seeds.t: Use navigation guards for auth rather than component mounted hooks
seeds.w: checking auth in every component's mounted() or onMounted() and redirecting manually — the component renders first, causing a flash or partial render before redirect.
seeds.c: use Vue Router navigation guards: router.beforeEach((to, from) => { if (to.meta.requiresAuth && !isLoggedIn()) return '/login' }). Route meta fields control which routes require auth. This prevents the component from rendering at all if the guard redirects. Per-route guards use …
seeds.s: protected pages briefly flash before redirecting unauthenticated users.

seeds.t: shallowRef and shallowReactive avoid deep reactivity overhead
seeds.w: wrapping a large, deeply nested object in ref() or reactive() when only the top level ever changes — Vue recursively converts all nested properties to reactive proxies, which is expensive for large objects.
seeds.c: use shallowRef(obj) for objects where only the .value reference itself changes, or shallowReactive(obj) to make only the top level reactive. Trigger updates by reassigning the top-level reference rather than mutating deep properties.
seeds.s: reactive wrapping of a large data structure causes slow startup or excessive memory use.

seeds.t: v-memo skips subtree re-rendering when dependencies are unchanged
seeds.w: re-rendering large list items on every parent update even when the item data hasn't changed — v-for without memoisation re-renders all items when the parent state changes.
seeds.c: use v-memo='[item.id, item.selected]' on list items to skip re-rendering the subtree if neither id nor selected has changed. v-memo is Vue 3.2+ and works well with v-for for large lists. Unlike computed, v-memo works at the template level and skips vnode diffing entirely.
seeds.s: a large v-for list causes visible lag on parent updates despite individual item data being unchanged.

seeds.t: defineExpose controls what parent components can access via template refs
seeds.w: expecting parent components to freely access a child component's internal state via a template ref — in <script setup>, nothing is exposed to the parent by default.
seeds.c: use defineExpose({ methodName, propertyName }) to explicitly whitelist what parent refs can access. Without defineExpose, ref.value on a child component returns an empty proxy. This is a deliberate encapsulation improvement over the Options API where all public properties were a…
seeds.s: parent tries to call child.method() via template ref but gets undefined because the child uses <script setup> without defineExpose.

seeds.t: Use defineAsyncComponent with Suspense for lazy-loaded components
seeds.w: importing large components eagerly at the top of a file — they're included in the initial bundle even if rarely used.
seeds.c: const HeavyChart = defineAsyncComponent(() => import('./HeavyChart.vue')) to code-split the component. Wrap with <Suspense> to show a fallback during loading, or pass loadingComponent and errorComponent to defineAsyncComponent for built-in fallback handling. Set delay (ms before…
seeds.s: initial bundle includes large optional components that most users never interact with.

seeds.t: Attributes fall through to root element — disable with inheritAttrs: false
seeds.w: assuming non-prop attributes passed to a component won't affect its internal DOM — Vue automatically inherits class, style, and event listeners on the single root element.
seeds.c: when you need to control where attrs land (e.g. a wrapper div vs. an inner input), set inheritAttrs: false in the component options and manually bind v-bind='$attrs' to the correct element. In <script setup>, add a separate <script> block with inheritAttrs: false.
seeds.s: class or event handlers passed by the parent land on the wrong element inside the component.

seeds.t: watchEffect auto-tracks dependencies; watch is explicit and lazy
seeds.w: using watch when you need to re-run an effect whenever any of several reactive values change, manually listing all dependencies.
seeds.c: use watchEffect(() => { console.log(a.value, b.value) }) — it automatically tracks every reactive value accessed during execution and re-runs when any changes. Use watch for lazy execution (doesn't run immediately), access to old values, or when you need to watch a specific sour…
seeds.s: a watch with a long dependency list that watchEffect would auto-track more cleanly.

seeds.t: Use instantiateStreaming for Fastest WASM Loading
seeds.w: Fetching the WASM binary with fetch().then(r => r.arrayBuffer()) then passing the buffer to WebAssembly.instantiate() — this waits for the full download before starting compilation.
seeds.c: Use WebAssembly.instantiateStreaming(fetch('module.wasm'), imports) which pipes bytes directly to the compiler, overlapping download and compilation. Falls back gracefully: if (!WebAssembly.instantiateStreaming) use the arrayBuffer path. Ensure the server serves .wasm files with…
seeds.s: WASM module taking twice as long to start because compilation waits for the complete download.

seeds.t: WASM Memory Is a Flat Byte Array — No GC by Default
seeds.w: Expecting WebAssembly to manage memory like JavaScript — there is no garbage collector in standard WASM; allocations must be tracked and freed manually or by the language's runtime.
seeds.c: WebAssembly.Memory is a single contiguous ArrayBuffer accessible from both WASM and JS. Memory is grown with memory.grow(pages) (one page = 64 KB). Languages like C/C++ (via Emscripten) bring their own malloc/free; Rust via wasm-bindgen uses its own allocator; Go and Java WASM r…
seeds.s: memory usage growing without bound because allocations are never freed, or mystery corruption from overlapping allocations.

seeds.t: SharedArrayBuffer Requires COOP and COEP Security Headers
seeds.w: Trying to use SharedArrayBuffer (required for WASM threads) without the correct HTTP headers — browsers disable SharedArrayBuffer by default since Spectre mitigations.
seeds.c: Serve pages with these headers: Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin and Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy: require-corp. These isolate the page in its own browsing context, enabling SharedArrayBuffer and high-resolution timers. Check availability: typeof SharedArrayBuffer !== 'un…
seeds.s: SharedArrayBuffer is not defined at runtime despite the browser supporting it, because isolation headers are missing.

seeds.t: Minimise Calls Across the JS-WASM Boundary
seeds.w: Calling small WASM functions thousands of times per frame from JavaScript — each cross-boundary call has overhead (type coercion, bounds checking) that accumulates quickly for high-frequency calls.
seeds.c: Batch work inside WASM: pass arrays or buffer pointers, do the computation in WASM, read results back. Push the hot loop into WASM and expose a single function that operates on a buffer. Use typed arrays (Float32Array, Uint8Array) sharing the WASM memory ArrayBuffer for zero-cop…
seeds.s: WASM implementation slower than the pure JS equivalent because the JS-WASM call overhead dominates over the computation.

seeds.t: Only Numeric Types Cross the WASM Boundary Natively
seeds.w: Trying to pass JavaScript objects, arrays, or strings directly to a WASM function expecting them to just work — WASM functions only accept and return i32, i64, f32, f64 natively.
seeds.c: Strings and complex types must be serialised: write to WASM memory (via the memory ArrayBuffer) and pass a pointer + length. Tools like wasm-bindgen (Rust) and Emscripten generate this glue automatically. For Rust+wasm-bindgen, mark functions #[wasm_bindgen] and use JsValue for …
seeds.s: 'unexpected type' error passing a string to a raw WASM function, or garbage values because a JS object reference was treated as an integer.

seeds.t: Memory Growth Detaches Existing ArrayBuffer Views
seeds.w: Caching a TypedArray view of WASM memory (const view = new Uint8Array(memory.buffer)) and using it after a memory.grow() call — growing memory reallocates the underlying buffer, invalidating all existing views of the old buffer.
seeds.c: Re-acquire the memory view after any operation that may trigger memory growth: const view = new Uint8Array(instance.exports.memory.buffer). Alternatively, check view.buffer.byteLength before use. In Rust wasm-bindgen, this is handled automatically; in raw WASM or Emscripten, gua…
seeds.s: reading zeros or garbage after a large allocation, because the cached view points to the old (now detached) buffer.

seeds.t: WASI Uses a Capability-Based Security Model
seeds.w: Expecting a WASM module compiled for WASI to have arbitrary filesystem or network access — WASI grants no capabilities by default; each resource must be explicitly preopened or granted by the host.
seeds.c: When running WASI modules, configure the runtime to preopen directories (--dir=. in wasmtime), map environment variables, and grant network access explicitly. In code, use the WASI API (fd_read, fd_write, path_open) which are satisfied by the host runtime. WASI is designed for s…
seeds.s: WASI module failing to open a file that exists, because the directory wasn't preopened in the host runtime configuration.

seeds.t: Check WASM SIMD Support Before Using It
seeds.w: Shipping a WASM binary that unconditionally uses SIMD instructions — older browsers (pre-2021) and some WASM runtimes don't support the SIMD proposal.
seeds.c: Detect support at runtime with a feature test or ship two binaries: one with SIMD and one without. Emscripten supports -msimd128 for SIMD builds. The WASM SIMD proposal (128-bit vectors) is universally supported in modern browsers (Chrome 91+, Firefox 89+, Safari 16.4+). For Rus…
seeds.s: WASM module failing to load on an older browser or runtime with 'invalid opcode' error from an unrecognised SIMD instruction.

seeds.t: Use Asyncify to Call Async JS From Synchronous WASM
seeds.w: Trying to call an async JavaScript function (fetch, setTimeout) from synchronous WASM code and blocking on the result — WASM execution is synchronous; you cannot block the JS event loop waiting for a promise.
seeds.c: Use Emscripten's Asyncify or Binaryen's asyncify pass to transform the WASM binary so that suspension points unwind the WASM stack and resume it when the JS promise resolves. In Rust, use wasm-bindgen-futures to bridge between Rust Futures and JS Promises. Asyncify adds overhead…
seeds.s: calling a JS async function from C/C++ WASM causing a runtime error or deadlock because the event loop is blocked.

seeds.t: Strip Debug Symbols for Production WASM Builds
seeds.w: Shipping a WASM binary built with debug symbols — debug builds can be 10x larger than release builds, dramatically increasing load time.
seeds.c: Build with optimisation: emcc -O3 for Emscripten, wasm-pack build --release for Rust. Use wasm-opt from Binaryen for additional size reduction: wasm-opt -O3 -o output.wasm input.wasm. Keep a separate .wasm with DWARF debug info for development. Source maps (.wasm.map) allow brow…
seeds.s: production WASM binary 8 MB that could be 800 KB with proper release builds and wasm-opt.

seeds.t: Function Pointers in WASM Use an Indirect Call Table
seeds.w: Assuming function pointers in C compiled to WASM work like raw memory addresses — WASM uses a table of function references; raw function pointer values are indices into this table, not memory addresses.
seeds.c: Understand that CALL_INDIRECT in WASM looks up the function in the element section table. When importing function pointers from JS to WASM (passing callbacks), use the WASM table via addFunction (Emscripten) or wasm-bindgen Closure (Rust). Don't assume you can read function poin…
seeds.s: function pointer passed from JS to WASM causing a 'indirect call type mismatch' trap, because the function signature at the table index doesn't match.

seeds.t: Use WAT Text Format to Inspect and Debug WASM Modules
seeds.w: Trying to debug binary WASM (.wasm) directly — it's a binary format with no human-readable representation.
seeds.c: Convert to WebAssembly Text format (WAT) with wasm2wat (from wabt): wasm2wat module.wasm -o module.wat. WAT is readable S-expression format showing all functions, imports, exports, and instructions. Use wat2wasm to convert back. Use wasm-objdump (wabt) for a summary of sections …
seeds.s: unable to inspect what a WASM module imports/exports or debug unexpected behaviour at the WASM level.

seeds.t: Emscripten's Virtual Filesystem Is In-Memory by Default
seeds.w: Expecting Emscripten-compiled C code to access the real filesystem in a browser — browsers have no filesystem access by default; Emscripten provides a virtual in-memory FS (MEMFS).
seeds.c: Use --preload-file data@/ to bundle files into the WASM binary, accessible at runtime via the virtual FS. Use IDBFS (IndexedDB-backed) for persistent storage that survives page reloads. Use NODEFS when running under Node.js to map virtual paths to real filesystem paths. For brow…
seeds.s: fopen returning NULL in Emscripten-compiled code because the file doesn't exist in the virtual filesystem.

seeds.t: WASM Supports Multiple Return Values From Functions
seeds.w: Using output pointer parameters (int* out_a, int* out_b) to return multiple values from a WASM function, like in old C conventions — WASM natively supports multiple return values since the multivalue proposal.
seeds.c: In WAT, functions can return multiple values: (func (result i32 i32)). Languages that compile to WASM leverage this: Rust tuples, C structs (via the multi-value ABI in Clang 13+). Multivalue is universally supported in modern WASM runtimes. Output parameters still work but are l…
seeds.s: using output pointer parameters unnecessarily for simple multi-value returns when multivalue return is supported.

seeds.t: WASM Has a Fixed Call Stack Separate From Linear Memory
seeds.w: Expecting deep recursion to work the same in WASM as in native code — WASM engines have a fixed call stack (typically 64 KB–1 MB) separate from the linear memory heap; deep recursion overflows it just like native code.
seeds.c: Convert deep recursion to iteration with an explicit stack in linear memory. Use tail-call optimisation where available (tail call proposal, not universally supported). Emscripten's ASYNCIFY can convert recursive descent to iterative with coroutine suspension. Set stack size wit…
seeds.s: 'RuntimeError: call stack exhausted' from a recursive WASM function that works on native with the same input.

seeds.t: Use the Component Model for Language-Agnostic WASM Interfaces
seeds.w: Sharing WASM modules between different language runtimes using manual memory passing and opaque integer handles — fragile, non-discoverable, and error-prone.
seeds.c: Use the WASM Component Model (standardised in 2024) and WIT (WASM Interface Types) to define typed interfaces between components. Tools: wit-bindgen generates bindings for Rust, C, Python, and other languages from a .wit file. WASI 0.2 is built on the Component Model. Components…
seeds.s: passing complex data between a Rust WASM module and a JavaScript host via manual pointer arithmetic when WIT interfaces would generate the glue automatically.

seeds.t: Use wasm-pack for Rust-to-WASM Bundler Integration
seeds.w: Manually invoking rustc with the wasm32-unknown-unknown target and manually writing JS glue code — misses optimisations, missing wasm-bindgen glue, and doesn't integrate with bundlers.
seeds.c: Use wasm-pack build --target bundler (for webpack/Vite) or --target web (for direct ES module use). wasm-pack runs wasm-bindgen, wasm-opt, and generates the TypeScript definitions and JS wrapper automatically. Import in JS as: import init, { myFunction } from './pkg/my_crate'; a…
seeds.s: manually written JS glue that wasm-pack generates automatically, missing out on type safety and optimisation.

seeds.t: Use Reference Types to Pass JS Objects Into WASM
seeds.w: Trying to store JavaScript object references as integers in WASM — JS heap references are opaque GC-managed pointers; storing them as i32 indices into a manual table is fragile and requires manual lifecycle management.
seeds.c: Use the reference types proposal (universally supported in modern engines): externref allows WASM to hold opaque references to JS objects without exposing their memory representation. In Rust with wasm-bindgen, JsValue uses externref internally. Pass JS objects to WASM functions…
seeds.s: complex manual handle tables mapping integers to JS objects where externref would handle the lifetime automatically.

seeds.t: Use the WASM Exception Handling Proposal for C++ Exceptions
seeds.w: Compiling C++ code with exceptions to WASM using the old Emscripten JavaScript-based exception handling — this is very slow (each throw involves JS calls) and massively inflates binary size.
seeds.c: Use the native WASM exception handling proposal with Emscripten flag -fwasm-exceptions. This uses WASM's throw and catch instructions, which are 2–10x faster than the JS-based emulation and produce smaller binaries. Supported in Chrome 95+, Firefox 100+, Safari 15.2+. Check supp…
seeds.s: C++ code with try/catch in WASM running 5x slower than native because JS-based exception handling is used.

seeds.t: Standard WASM Memory Is Limited to 4 GB (Memory64 Extends This)
seeds.w: Expecting to allocate more than ~4 GB in a standard WASM module — the standard memory model uses 32-bit addresses, limiting the addressable space to 2^32 bytes (4 GB).
seeds.c: Standard WASM: pointers are i32, max memory is 4 GB (65,536 pages × 64 KB). For workloads exceeding 4 GB, use the Memory64 proposal which enables 64-bit addresses (i64 pointers). Memory64 is supported in V8/Chrome and Wasmtime but not universally. For most browser use cases (< 2…
seeds.s: WASM module refusing to grow memory beyond 4 GB, or allocation failing silently as addresses wrap around.

seeds.t: Missing Skip Navigation Forces Keyboard Users to Tab Through Every Menu
seeds.w: Starting the page body with a navigation menu that contains 8 or more links, with no mechanism for keyboard users to bypass it. On every page load, keyboard users must Tab through every navigation item before reaching the main content.
seeds.c: Add a skip link as the absolute first focusable element in : Skip to main content. Make it visible on focus with CSS: .skip-link:not(:focus) { position: absolute; left: -9999px; }. Ensure the main content area has id="main-cont…
seeds.s: The first Tab press on any page lands on a navigation link, not a skip link; screen reader users must navigate through the entire menu on every page change; NVDA or VoiceOver announces navigation items before any page content.

seeds.t: Missing <main> Landmark Prevents Screen Reader Landmark Navigation
seeds.w: Wrapping primary page content in a generic <div> or <section> without using semantic landmark elements. Screen reader users cannot jump directly to the main content; they must navigate linearly or guess where it starts.
seeds.c: Wrap all primary page content in a <main id="main-content"> element. Every page should have exactly one <main>. Also use <nav>, <header>, <footer>, and <aside> for their respective content areas. Landmark navigation (pressing R in NVDA, or swiping through landmarks in VoiceOver)…
seeds.s: A page has no <main> or role="main" element; screen reader landmark navigation list shows no main content landmark; keyboard users cannot use the skip link if it exists (it would point to a non-existent ID).

seeds.t: Dynamic Content Updates Without ARIA Live Regions Are Silent to Screen Readers
seeds.w: Updating page content via JavaScript (search results, notifications, form errors, loading states) without informing the accessibility tree: element.innerHTML = newResults. Screen reader users are not notified of the change and must manually navigate to find new content.
seeds.c: Add aria-live="polite" to containers that receive dynamic updates: <div id="search-results" aria-live="polite">. The container must exist in the DOM before content is inserted. For urgent errors or alerts, use role="alert" (equivalent to aria-live="assertive"). For loading state…
seeds.s: A screen reader user submits a search or form and hears no announcement of the results; new content appears visually but is not announced; error messages are shown but not read aloud.

seeds.t: outline:none Removes Focus Visibility for Keyboard Users
seeds.w: Applying outline: none or outline: 0 to interactive elements, typically to clean up the browser default blue ring: button { outline: none; } or *:focus { outline: none; }. This makes the page completely unusable for keyboard-only users, who have no visible indication of which el…
seeds.c: Never suppress the focus ring without providing an equivalent visible alternative. Use :focus-visible to provide a custom ring only for keyboard navigation: button:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid #005fcc; outline-offset: 2px; }. :focus-visible does not fire for mouse clicks, …
seeds.s: Pressing Tab on a page produces no visible indicator of which element has focus; WCAG 2.4.7 (Focus Visible) is violated; keyboard users cannot use the site reliably.

seeds.t: Placeholder Text as the Only Form Label Fails Accessibility and Usability
seeds.w: Using placeholder text as the only identifier for a form field: <input type="email" placeholder="Email address">. When a user starts typing, the placeholder disappears. Screen readers may not consistently announce placeholder text as a label. Users with cognitive disabilities ma…
seeds.c: Always associate a visible <label> element: <label for="email">Email address</label><input id="email" type="email" placeholder="e.g. jane@example.com">. The label remains visible while the placeholder provides an example. Visually-hidden labels (with a CSS clip pattern) are acce…
seeds.s: Form fields have placeholder but no <label>; screen readers announce "edit text" with no field name; partially filled forms confuse users about what each field requires.

seeds.t: "Click Here" and "Read More" Links Break Screen Reader Navigation
seeds.w: Using identical non-descriptive link text across a page: Read more repeated ten times for different posts. Screen reader users navigate by listing all links on the page; a list of ten identical "read more" links provides no information about what any of the…
seeds.c: Make every link descriptive in isolation: Read more about the Canoga Park Grand Opening. Or use aria-label for visual brevity: Read more. Image-only links must have meaningful alt …
seeds.s: Page has multiple identical anchor text strings; screen reader link list shows repeated items with no distinguishing information; WCAG 2.4.6 audit flags non-descriptive links.

seeds.t: Missing or Generic Alt Text Makes Images Inaccessible and Hurts SEO
seeds.w: (no alt attribute) or or copy-pasting the same alt text for every product image: alt="Blackberry Runtz" on a Sour Diesel product. Images without alt text cause screen readers to announce the filename; generic alt provid…
seeds.c: For informative images: alt text should describe the content and its purpose in context: alt="Sour Diesel Preroll 1g – Treehouse Cannabis". For decorative images: alt="" (empty string, not absent — this tells screen readers to skip it explicitly). For complex images (charts, inf…
seeds.s: with no alt attribute; the same alt text on multiple different product images; screen readers announce "image" or the file path; Google Image Search cannot understand the image content.

seeds.t: Skipping Heading Levels Breaks Screen Reader Document Navigation
seeds.w: Using headings for visual size rather than document structure: an h1 followed directly by h3 or h4 because "they look better". Screen reader users navigate by heading level (pressing H1, H2, H3 in NVDA). Skipped levels sound like missing content.
seeds.c: Heading levels must be sequential: h1 (once per page) → h2 (major sections) → h3 (subsections). Adjust visual size with CSS, not by choosing a different heading level. Use only one h1 per page describing the page's primary topic.
seeds.s: A page has an h1 followed by h3 with no h2 between them; WAVE accessibility tool reports "Skipped heading level"; screen reader users report the page structure feels broken.

seeds.t: CSS Animations Without prefers-reduced-motion Cause Harm to Some Users
seeds.w: Adding animations, transitions, parallax, or auto-playing effects without a reduced-motion alternative. Users with vestibular disorders, epilepsy, or motion sensitivity can experience nausea, disorientation, or seizures from certain animations.
seeds.c: Wrap all non-essential animations in a no-preference media query, or suppress them when reduce is preferred: @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { *, *::before, *::after { animation-duration: 0.01ms !important; transition-duration: 0.01ms !important; } }. Ensure any content …
seeds.s: Site has CSS animations or transitions with no @media (prefers-reduced-motion) override; macOS/iOS users with "Reduce Motion" enabled still see animations; WCAG 2.3.3 (Animation from Interactions) is violated.

seeds.t: Missing lang Attribute Causes Screen Readers to Use Wrong Language/Voice
seeds.w: <html> without a lang attribute, or a page serving French content where <html lang="en"> is set. Screen readers use the lang attribute to select the correct voice, pronunciation, and language rules.
